name: REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body or space and transferring it to a high-temperature body or space, against the natural flow of heat (i.e., against the Second Law of Thermodynamics), with the help of external work input.

**Air Conditioning** is the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, cleanliness (dust/odor), and air motion (circulation) of an enclosed space to maintain specified human comfort or cargo preservation conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** The fundamental distinction between *refrigeration* and *air conditioning* is that **refrigeration focuses only on heat removal (sensible cooling)**, while **air conditioning addresses four parameters: Temperature, Humidity, Air Purity, and Air Circulation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your kitchen refrigerator at home. It pulls heat *out* of the food compartment (making it cold) and dumps that heat into your hot kitchen (making it slightly warmer). On a ship, this same principle is scaled up massively:

- The **"cold box"** in your home = a **ship's cold room / refrigerated cargo hold / provision room**.
- The **"hot kitchen air"** in your home = the **sea water or ambient air outside the ship** that absorbs the rejected heat via the condenser.

**Real-world Marine Analogy:** Imagine you are sailing across the equator carrying tonnes of frozen fish and 1,000 passengers. Without refrigeration, the fish rot in 48 hours and the crew/passengers suffer heatstroke. The refrigeration plant on board is essentially a giant "heat pump" that continuously pulls heat out of the cold spaces and dumps it into the ocean.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units

| Parameter | SI Unit | FPS Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Heat (Energy) | Joule (J) / kJ | BTU (British Thermal Unit) |
| Rate of Heat Removal (Cooling Effect) | Watt (W) / kW | BTU/hr |
| Standard Refrigeration Ton | **1 TR = 3.517 kW** | 1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr |
| Temperature | °C or K | °F or °R |
| Pressure | Pa or kPa | psi or bar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ton" of Refrigeration?** Historically, 1 TR equals the heat required to melt **1 ton (2,000 lb) of ice in 24 hours** = 12,000 BTU/hr ≈ 3.517 kW. This legacy unit is still the global industry standard for rating refrigeration plants, including ships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Refrigeration is Needed on Ships?

A modern ship is a self-contained floating city with multiple thermal management requirements:

1. **Cargo Preservation (Reefer Cargo)**: Frozen meat, fish, dairy, fruits, pharmaceuticals — must be maintained between **−30 °C and +13 °C** depending on commodity.
2. **Provision Storage (Cold Rooms)**: Food for crew — chill room (+2 to +4 °C) and freezer room (−18 to −25 °C).
3. **Crew Comfort (HVAC)**: Accommodation blocks, wheelhouse, engine control room — typical **24 °C ± 2 °C at 50% RH**.
4. **Electronic Equipment Cooling**: Navigation computers, server racks, battery banks.
5. **Specialized Cargo**: LNG carriers (−163 °C), chemical tankers (temperature-sensitive chemicals).

### 2.2 Classification of Marine Refrigeration Systems

Based on the operating temperature range, marine refrigeration plants are classified as:

| Class | Evaporator Temp Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Temperature (HT)** | 0 °C to 13 °C | AC plants, chill rooms |
| **Medium-Temperature (MT)** | −10 °C to 0 °C | Fresh food preservation |
| **Low-Temperature (LT)** | Below −25 °C | Frozen cargo, deep freezers |

### 2.3 The Second Law Connection — Why Work Input is Mandatory

Heat naturally flows from **HOT → COLD** (Clausius Statement). To reverse this and move heat from **COLD → HOT**, **external work must be supplied**. This is the philosophical foundation of every refrigeration cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** A refrigerator is essentially a **heat engine running in reverse**. While a heat engine produces work *from* heat flow (HOT→COLD), a refrigerator *consumes work* to drive heat *against* natural gradient (COLD→HOT).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Standard Refrigeration Ton (SI) | kW |
| 2 | $1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | Standard Refrigeration Ton (FPS) | BTU/hr |
| 3 | $1 \text{ kW} = 3412.14 \text{ BTU/hr}$ | Conversion factor | BTU/hr |
| 4 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer (cooling load) | kW |
| 5 | $Q = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat (for ice formation) | kW |
| 6 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Heat Pump vs Refrigerator relationship | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Kelvin-Celsius conversion | K |
| 9 | $T(°F) = \dfrac{9}{5}T(°C) + 32$ | Fahrenheit-Celsius conversion | °F |
| 10 | $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$ | Energy balance across cycle | kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The COP (Coefficient of Performance) is the **single most important performance metric** for evaluating a marine refrigeration plant. A poorly maintained condenser on a ship can drop COP from 3.5 to 1.8 — doubling fuel consumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Equivalence of 1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)

**Statement to Prove:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW

**Step 1 — Define 1 TR in FPS units:**
By the original ICE (Institution of Civil Engineers) definition:
$$1 \text{ TR} = \text{Heat required to melt } 1 \text{ ton of ice in } 24 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to slugs (FPS):**
$$1 \text{ ton (short)} = 2000 \text{ lb}$$
$$m = 2000 \text{ lb}$$

**Step 3 — Identify latent heat of fusion of ice at 0 °C:**
$$h_{fg,\text{ice}} = 144 \text{ BTU/lb}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat absorbed in 24 hours:**
$$Q_{24h} = m \cdot h_{fg}$$
$$Q_{24h} = 2000 \text{ lb} \times 144 \text{ BTU/lb}$$
$$Q_{24h} = 288{,}000 \text{ BTU}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rate per hour (this defines 1 TR):**
$$1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{288{,}000 \text{ BTU}}{24 \text{ h}}$$
$$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Convert BTU/hr to kW using mechanical equivalent:**
We know $1 \text{ BTU/hr} = 0.000293071 \text{ kW}$
$$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \times 0.000293071 \text{ kW}$$
$$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** Therefore, mathematically:
$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between COP of Heat Pump and Refrigerator

**Statement to Prove:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$

**Step 1 — Write definition of COP for a Refrigerator (R):**
The desired output of a refrigerator is the heat extracted from the cold space ($Q_L$).
$$COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$

**Step 2 — Write definition of COP for a Heat Pump (HP):**
The desired output of a heat pump is the heat rejected to the hot space ($Q_H$).
$$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Balance):**
For a cyclic device, all heat and work interactions must balance. The net work input equals the difference between heat rejected and heat absorbed.
$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$
$$\Rightarrow Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Heat Pump COP definition:**
$$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_L + W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}}$$

**Step 5 — Split the fraction:**
$$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} + \dfrac{W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}}$$

**Step 6 — Identify and substitute:**
The first term is $COP_R$ by definition; the second term is 1.
$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$

**Conclusion:** Therefore:
$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $COP_R$ is always greater than 1, $COP_{HP}$ is always greater than 2. Practically, this means a marine heat pump used for cabin heating delivers **more than double** the energy consumed — a critical selling point for HVAC designers.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A marine provision refrigeration plant extracts 75 kW of cooling from a chill room. The compressor consumes 22 kW of electrical power.
**(a)** Calculate the COP of the refrigeration system.
**(b)** Find the rate of heat rejection at the condenser (sea-water cooled).
**(c)** Express the cooling capacity in TR.

**Solution:**

**(a) Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator):**
$$COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$
$$COP_R = \dfrac{75 \text{ kW}}{22 \text{ kW}}$$
$$\boxed{COP_R = 3.41}$$

**(b) Heat Rejection at Condenser (Energy Balance):**
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$
$$Q_H = 75 + 22$$
$$\boxed{Q_H = 97 \text{ kW}}$$

**(c) Capacity in Tons of Refrigeration:**
$$\text{Capacity (TR)} = \dfrac{Q_L \text{ in kW}}{3.517}$$
$$\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{75}{3.517}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Capacity} = 21.32 \text{ TR}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: The Refrigeration Cycle on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shipboard Low-Temp Space\nCold Room / Cabin] -->|Heat QL absorbed| B(Evaporator\nLow P, Low T)
    B -->|Vapor refrigerant| C(Compressor\nWork Win input)
    C -->|High P, High T vapor| D(Condenser\nSea Water cooled)
    D -->|Heat QH rejected to sea| E[Sea / Ambient Hot Sink]
    D -->|High P liquid| F(Expansion Valve\nThrottling device)
    F -->|Low P liquid-vapor mix| B
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Classification of Marine Refrigeration Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Marine Refrigeration and Air Conditioning] --> R1[Cargo Cooling\nReefer Holds]
    R --> R2[Provision Storage\nChill and Freezer Rooms]
    R --> R3[Crew Comfort\nAccommodation HVAC]
    R --> R4[Equipment Cooling\nElectronics, Batteries]
    
    R1 --> R1A[Temperature Range\n-30C to +13C]
    R2 --> R2A[Temperature Range\n-25C to +4C]
    R3 --> R3A[Temperature Range\n22C to 26C at 50 percent RH]
    R4 --> R4A[Temperature Range\n15C to 25C]
    
    R --> C[Common Refrigerants Used Onboard]
    C --> C1[R-134a\nHFC, GWP 1430]
    C --> C2[R-404A\nHFC blend, Low temp]
    C --> C3[R-22\nHCFC, being phased out]
    C --> C4[NH3 Ammonia\nIndustrial fishing vessels]
    C --> C5[CO2 R-744\nEco-friendly, transcritical]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Diagram: Refrigerator vs Heat Pump Conceptual View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RF[REFRIGIGERATOR Mode]
        CS1[Cold Space QL] --Heat absorbed--> EV1[Evaporator]
        EV1 --> CP1[Compressor Win]
        CP1 --> CD1[Condenser]
        CD1 --Heat rejected QH--> HS1[Hot Ambient Sea]
    end
    
    subgraph HP[HEAT PUMP Mode]
        CS2[Outside Air QL] --Heat absorbed--> EV2[Evaporator]
        EV2 --> CP2[Compressor Win]
        CP2 --> CD2[Condenser]
        CD2 --Heat rejected QH--> HS2[Cabin to be Heated]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Refrigeration. How is it different from Air Conditioning?*

**Model Answer:**
Refrigeration is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body and rejecting it to a high-temperature body, requiring external work input. **Air conditioning** is a broader process that controls **four parameters simultaneously**: (1) Temperature, (2) Humidity, (3) Air Purity (cleanliness and odor), and (4) Air Motion (circulation). Thus, all air conditioners contain a refrigeration cycle, but not all refrigeration systems perform air conditioning.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define 1 Ton of Refrigeration and express it in kW.*

**Model Answer:**
One Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR) is defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 ton (2,000 lb or 907.185 kg)** of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in **24 hours**. Numerically:
$$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the necessity of refrigeration and air conditioning on modern merchant ships. List at least six distinct applications with their typical temperature ranges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A marine air-conditioning plant handles a cooling load of 105 kW. The compressor motor draws 30 kW. Calculate:
  (i) Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the system
  (ii) Heat rejected at the condenser
  (iii) Cooling capacity in Tons of Refrigeration (TR)
  (iv) If this same plant is used as a heat pump, find its COP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Necessity of Marine Refrigeration & AC (7 Marks):**

| S.No | Application | Typical Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reefer cargo holds (frozen meat, fish) | −25 °C to −30 °C |
| 2 | Provision chill room (vegetables, dairy) | +2 °C to +4 °C |
| 3 | Provision freezer room (long-term storage) | −18 °C to −25 °C |
| 4 | Crew accommodation AC | 24 °C ± 2 °C at 50% RH |
| 5 | Wheelhouse and control room AC | 22 °C ± 1 °C |
| 6 | Electronic equipment / battery cooling | 15 °C to 25 °C |

Without refrigeration, perishable cargo would spoil within days, violating international food safety laws (e.g., MARPOL Annex V indirectly, and commercial contracts). Crew productivity drops sharply above 30 °C with high humidity. Hence, refrigeration and AC are **operational necessities, not luxuries**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $Q_L = 105 \text{ kW}$, $W_{in} = 30 \text{ kW}$

**(i) COP:**
$$COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{105}{30} = 3.5$$

**(ii) Heat rejected:**
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 105 + 30 = 135 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Capacity in TR:**
$$\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{105}{3.517} = 29.86 \text{ TR}$$

**(iv) COP as Heat Pump:**
$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 3.5 + 1 = 4.5$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic refrigeration cycle. State the function of each component and the state of the refrigerant at the inlet and outlet of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the COP of a refrigerator and the COP of a heat pump, stating the underlying thermodynamic principle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Basic Refrigeration Cycle (7 Marks):**

The vapour compression refrigeration cycle has four essential components:

1. **Evaporator**: Refrigerant enters as a low-pressure, low-temperature liquid-vapour mixture and exits as a low-pressure vapour. **Function**: Absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold space.
2. **Compressor**: Refrigerant enters as low-pressure vapour and exits as high-pressure, high-temperature vapour. **Function**: Raises refrigerant pressure/temperature using work input $W_{in}$.
3. **Condenser**: Refrigerant enters as high-pressure vapour and exits as high-pressure liquid. **Function**: Rejects heat $Q_H$ to the sea-water cooling loop.
4. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)**: Refrigerant enters as high-pressure liquid and exits as low-pressure, low-temperature liquid-vapour mixture. **Function**: Drops pressure isenthalpically, enabling cooling to evaporator temperature.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the schematic.)*

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Thermodynamic Principle:** Conservation of energy (First Law) for a cyclic device.

For a refrigerator:
$$COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$$

For a heat pump:
$$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{in}}$$

Energy balance across the cycle:
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$$

Substituting:
$$COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_L + W_{in}}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}} + 1 = COP_R + 1$$

$$\boxed{\therefore COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in TR**: Students frequently write "1 TR = 3.5 kW" without showing derivation or stating the exact value **3.517 kW**. Marks are deducted for the lack of precision.
> 2. **Mis-stating COP**: COP is dimensionless and **always greater than 1** for a refrigerator. If your calculation gives COP < 1, the data is wrong — re-check.
> 3. **Refrigerator vs Heat Pump Confusion**: The *desired output* differs. For AC mode (refrigerator), $Q_L$ is the output. For heating mode (heat pump), $Q_H$ is the output. Same hardware, different performance metric.
> 4. **Forgetting the +1 in Heat Pump COP**: A very common 1-mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Refrigeration** = heat removal only; **Air Conditioning** = T + H + P + M control (Temperature, Humidity, Purity, Motion).
- **1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr = 3.517 kW** — universal industry yardstick.
- **Three marine categories**: HT (AC), MT (chill), LT (freeze) based on evaporator temperature.
- **Second Law requirement**: Heat cannot flow from cold to hot *spontaneously* — work input is mandatory.
- **First Law Energy Balance**: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$ — must appear in every cycle numerical.
- **COP_R = Q_L / W_in** and **COP_HP = Q_H / W_in** — these are *ratios*, not efficiencies.
- **Universal relationship**: $\mathbf{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$.
- **Marine applications**: Cargo holds, provision rooms, accommodation HVAC, electronics cooling — all require tailored temperature/humidity regimes.
- **Common marine refrigerants to remember**: R-134a (modern AC), R-404A (low temp), NH₃ (ammonia — industrial fishing fleets), CO₂ (R-744 — emerging eco-friendly).
- **Sign convention**: Heat *into* the system is positive (refrigerant side); heat *out of* cold space is positive (load side). Be consistent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Applications of Mechanical Refrigeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITION OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine Applications of Mechanical Refrigeration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Marine Mechanical Refrigeration** refers to the application of vapor-compression (and to a lesser extent, absorption) refrigeration cycles onboard seagoing vessels, offshore platforms, and marine installations, where heat is extracted from designated spaces (cargo holds, provision rooms, accommodation blocks, machinery compartments) and rejected to the sea, in order to preserve perishable goods, maintain crew thermal comfort, condition process air for specialized cargo, and provide cooling for machinery auxiliaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Marine refrigeration is distinct from shore-based refrigeration primarily because the *heat sink* is the **sea water (unlimited but variable in temperature)**, the prime mover is a **ship's service electrical motor or a main engine-driven compressor**, and the system must operate under **roll, pitch, vibration, and heeling conditions** of up to **±45°** depending on the vessel class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your domestic refrigerator at home. It has a compressor, a condenser coil at the back (which dumps heat into your kitchen air), an evaporator coil inside (which absorbs heat from the food), and an expansion device. Now imagine the same box placed on a small fishing trawler bobbing violently in the North Sea during a storm:

- The kitchen air (heat sink) is replaced by the **ocean**, which never runs out and is almost always cooler than the room air, but it is *salty* (corrosive) and *splashes* (mechanical hazard).
- The compressor cannot simply sit on the deck — it must be **bolted to a foundation** that absorbs the ship's motion, with **flexible couplings** in the refrigerant piping to absorb vibration.
- The food being stored is not just your dinner leftovers — it could be **200 tonnes of frozen tuna**, **12,000 m³ of LNG at –162 °C**, or the **galley vegetables for a 30-day voyage**.

So marine refrigeration is essentially **"a domestic fridge on a rolling, salt-sprayed, space-starved, vibration-shaken steel platform where failure means losing millions of rupees in cargo or endangering human life."**

### 1.3 Primary Functional Categories Onboard a Ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Major Marine Applications of Mechanical Refrigeration:**

1. **Cargo Refrigeration** – Reefer ships carrying fruit, meat, dairy, fish.
2. **Liquefied Gas Transport** – LNG, LPG, ethylene carriers operating at cryogenic temperatures.
3. **Provision Cold Stores** – Galley stores for fresh food for the crew.
4. **Air Conditioning & Ventilation** – Accommodation, navigation bridge, engine control room.
5. **Machinery Auxiliary Cooling** – Coolers for lube oil, jacket water, electronics, and specialized process equipment.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics (Bolded)

- **Galley Provision Stores Temperature:** **+2 °C to +5 °C**
- **Frozen Fish Hold Temperature:** **–25 °C to –30 °C**
- **Banana Hold Temperature:** **+12 °C to +14 °C** (with high humidity)
- **LNG Cargo Temperature:** **–162 °C** (atmospheric boil-off)
- **Accommodation AC Design:** **24 °C DB / 50 % RH** (tropical design, 35 °C outside)
- **Sea Water Temperature Range (heat sink):** **–2 °C (polar) to +32 °C (tropical)**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Ships Need Refrigeration — The Operational "Why"

Ships are isolated micro-cities. Unlike a building on land, a vessel cannot drive to a cold store when its food spoils, nor open its windows when the engine room reaches **55 °C**. Therefore every ship must carry its own refrigeration plant. The drivers are:

- **Hygiene & Crew Welfare:** ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006) and IMO mandates require provision stores for voyages up to 30+ days.
- **Cargo Economics:** A reefer container of mangoes worth **₹ 4 lakh** becomes worthless if held at the wrong temperature.
- **Cargo Integrity:** Frozen cargo must be kept within ±1 °C of set point, else ice crystals rupture cell walls (freezer burn).
- **Cryogenic Transport:** Natural gas is liquefied at –162 °C, reducing volume by **600×**, making sea transport the only economical mode.
- **Equipment Survival:** Electronic equipment, hydraulic oil, and ammunition stores all require temperature control.

### 2.2 The Four Major Marine Subsystems — Operational "How"

#### 2.2.1 Provision Refrigeration Plant (PRP)
- Small-to-medium capacity (typically **5 kW to 80 kW** per compressor unit).
- Operates at **medium temperature** evaporating at **–10 °C to –5 °C**.
- Multiple independent compressors (2 or 3) for redundancy.
- Uses **direct expansion (DX)** evaporators or **brine** (glycol/water) secondary loops.
- Located in the engine room or dedicated **refrigeration machinery space**.

#### 2.2.2 Cargo Refrigeration Plant (CRP)
- Large capacity: **100 kW to several MW** for reefer ships.
- Evaporating temperatures range from **–40 °C (deep-frozen) to +5 °C (chilled)**.
- Uses **ammonia (R717)** for large capacities (high efficiency, zero ODP, low GWP) or **R404A / R507** for smaller plants.
- Multiple evaporators (one per hold) with **individual thermostatic expansion valves (TEV)**.

#### 2.2.3 Air-Conditioning Plant (ACP)
- Capacity range: **50 kW to 2 MW** depending on ship size.
- Uses **chilled water** (5–7 °C) distributed via ring mains to air handling units (AHUs).
- Heat rejection through **sea water-cooled condensers** or **centralized heat exchanger + sea water cooling tower**.

#### 2.2.4 Cryogenic Liquefied Gas Reliquefaction
- For LNG carriers and LPG carriers where boil-off gas (BOG) must be recondensed.
- Specialized multi-stage compression with **mixed refrigerants** (e.g., **ethane–propane–butane blend** for C3MR process, or pure **nitrogen expander** for the N₂ cycle).

### 2.3 Marine-Specific Design Constraints (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Design Constraints vs Shore Plants:**

| Constraint | Shore-Based Plant | Marine Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Heat sink | Air / cooling tower | **Sea water (direct or indirect)** |
| Tilting | Level | **Static ±22.5°, dynamic ±45°** |
| Vibration | Negligible | **2–25 Hz continuous** |
| Power | Grid electricity | **Shaft generator / aux diesel / emergency** |
| Materials | Standard steel / copper | **Cu-Ni 90/10 for sea water side, cupronickel, stainless 316L** |
| Safety | Building code | **SOLAS, IGF, IGC Code, Class rules (DNV, LR, ABS)** |
| Maintenance | Workshop | **En-route, with limited spares** |
| Corrosion | Mild | **Salt-laden atmosphere + galvanic** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas govern marine refrigeration plant design and operation. All temperatures in **°C** unless stated, all SI units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_r \, (h_g - h_f)$ | Refrigeration capacity (DX system) | kW |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{h_g - h_f}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}_r \, (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor theoretical work | kW |
| 4 | $\text{COP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r \, (h_2 - h_3)$ | Condenser heat rejection | kW |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{load,hold} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat load on a cargo hold | W |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})}$ | Sea water mass flow for condenser | kg/s |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \, \Delta T_{sw}}$ (compact) | Compact sea water flow rate | kg/s |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{respiration} = m_{cargo} \cdot q_{resp} \cdot e^{k T}$ | Heat of respiration of fresh fruit | W |
| 11 | $f_{util} = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | Compressor utilization factor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_r \, v_1 \, \ln(P_2 / P_1)}{\eta_{isen}}$ | Reciprocating compressor power | W |
| 13 | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_{act}}{\dot{m}_{theor}} = 1 - C \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} - 1 \right)$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\dot{Q}_{infil} = \dot{V}_{hold} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot \Delta T \cdot n$ | Air infiltration load | W |
| 15 | $T_{2s} = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Isentropic discharge temp | K |
| 16 | $\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat of air | W |
| 17 | $\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_a \, (W_1 - W_2) \, h_{fg}$ | Latent heat of air (humidification) | W |
| 18 | $\text{SHR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{sensible}}{\dot{Q}_{total}}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio | dimensionless |
| 19 | $\dot{Q}_{aux} = K_{aux} \cdot \dot{Q}_{evap}$ | Auxiliaries (fan, pump) electric load | W |
| 20 | $t_{hold,life} = \frac{(T_{core} - T_{set})}{k \cdot \Delta T_{wall}}$ | Thermal time constant for cargo | h |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations directly drive the **design heat load** for a marine refrigeration plant, which is used to:
- Specify the compressor motor rating in the **ship's refrigeration specification document**.
- Size the **sea water cooling pump** and **box cooler / shell-and-tube condenser**.
- Determine **generator sizing** and **fuel consumption** for voyage planning.
- Set the **class-approved pressure relief valves** and **venting capacity** for ammonia plants (per IGC Code).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Heat Load on a Marine Reefer Hold

A standard KTU Part B question asks: *"Estimate the refrigeration load for a cargo hold."* We derive this rigorously.

#### 3.1.1 The Heat Load Equation

The total refrigeration load on a refrigerated cargo hold is the sum of all heat gains from every possible source:

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{conduction} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration} + \dot{Q}_{respiration} + \dot{Q}_{fan} + \dot{Q}_{personnel} + \dot{Q}_{lighting}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Conduction Through Hold Boundaries

Each surface (deckhead, side, bottom, bulkhead, hatch cover) conducts heat from outside (sea or ambient) to inside (cold hold). The heat gain through a single wall is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \frac{A \cdot (T_o - T_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{x_j}{k_j}}
$$

where the denominator is the **total thermal resistance** of the composite insulation (steel + insulation + vapour barrier + internal lining).

For a typical reefer hold with insulated panels of total thickness **$x_{total}$** and effective conductivity **$k_{eff}$**:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_o - T_i) \quad \text{where} \quad U = \frac{1}{\sum R}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Air Infiltration Load

When hatches open for cargo work, warm humid air enters. The infiltration load depends on the **number of air changes per hour (n)** and the air's enthalpy difference:

$$
\dot{Q}_{infil} = \dot{V}_{hold} \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot \left[ c_{p,a}(T_o - T_i) + (W_o - W_i) h_{fg} \right]
$$

Splitting into sensible and latent components:

$$
\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{V}_{hold} \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_o - T_i)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{V}_{hold} \cdot n \cdot \rho_a \cdot (W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Respiration Heat of Perishable Cargo

Fresh fruits and vegetables continue to respire after harvest, generating heat. The respiration rate increases exponentially with temperature:

$$
\dot{Q}_{resp} = m_{cargo} \cdot q_{resp,ref} \cdot e^{k(T - T_{ref})}
$$

where **$q_{resp,ref}$** is the respiration rate at a reference temperature and **$k$** is a constant depending on the commodity. Example: bananas at 13 °C release approximately **0.10–0.15 W/kg** of metabolic heat.

#### 3.1.5 Fan and Internal Heat Sources

Fans circulating air inside the hold convert brake power into heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fan} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{fan}}
$$

Personnel generate **~120 W** (sensible) and **~55 W** (latent). Lighting fixtures (often CFL or LED in modern vessels) generate **~10 W/m²**.

#### 3.1.6 Final Assembled Heat Load

Summing all contributions:

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = U \sum A_i (T_{o,i} - T_i) + \dot{V} \cdot n \cdot \rho_a [\Delta h] + m_{cargo} \cdot q_{resp} \cdot e^{kT} + \sum \dot{Q}_{aux}
$$

This value in **watts** is divided by **1000** to give **kilowatts**, which is the plant capacity required.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A reefer hold carries **300 tonnes** of bananas. Hold dimensions are **20 m × 10 m × 5 m**. Design temperature inside is **+13 °C**, outside ambient is **+30 °C**. Insulation: 100 mm polyurethane with $k = 0.022$ W/m·K. Calculate approximate steady-state refrigeration load.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Surface Areas (assume 6 surfaces of an open box + 1 deckhead):**
Deckhead area = $A_1 = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$
Two sides = $A_2 = 2 \times (20 \times 5) = 200 \text{ m}^2$
Two ends = $A_3 = 2 \times (10 \times 5) = 100 \text{ m}^2$
Trough bottom (top of lower hold) = $A_4 = 200 \text{ m}^2$
Total area = $A = 700 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2 — Conduction Heat Gain:**

$$
U = \frac{k}{x} = \frac{0.022}{0.100} = 0.22 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = 0.22 \times 700 \times (30 - 13) = 0.22 \times 700 \times 17 = 2618 \text{ W} = 2.62 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Respiration Load:**

Using $q_{resp} = 0.12$ W/kg for bananas at 13 °C:

$$
\dot{Q}_{resp} = 300\,000 \text{ kg} \times 0.12 \text{ W/kg} = 36\,000 \text{ W} = 36.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration (assume 1 air change per day during voyage, $n = 1/24$ h⁻¹):**

Hold volume $V = 20 \times 10 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ m}^3$

Enthalpy difference: outdoor air at 30 °C, 80 % RH → $h_o \approx 86$ kJ/kg; indoor at 13 °C, 90 % RH → $h_i \approx 35$ kJ/kg; $\Delta h \approx 51$ kJ/kg.

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{1000}{24} = 41.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.01157 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.01157 \times 1.2 = 0.0139 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{infil} = 0.0139 \times 51\,000 = 707 \text{ W} \approx 0.71 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Fan Heat and Other Loads:**

Assume two fans, 1.5 kW each, running 60 % of the time:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fan} = 2 \times 1.5 \times 0.6 = 1.8 \text{ kW}
$$

Lighting: 4 × 100 W × 0.5 duty = 0.2 kW.

**Step 6 — Total Heat Load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 2.62 + 36.0 + 0.71 + 1.8 + 0.2 = 41.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Design Margin (1.10 safety factor for Class approval):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{design} = 41.33 \times 1.10 = 45.46 \text{ kW} \approx \mathbf{46 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** This banana reefer requires approximately **46 kW (≈ 13 TR)** of refrigeration capacity. The dominant load (87 %) is **respiration heat** — this is why chilled fruit vessels always have generous plant margins.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application → Refrigerant → Temperature

| Application | Temperature Range | Typical Refrigerant | Capacity Range | Cycle Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Provision stores (fresh) | +2 to +5 °C | R404A, R507 | 5–50 kW | Single-stage DX |
| Provision stores (frozen) | –18 to –25 °C | R404A, R717 | 10–80 kW | Single-stage DX |
| Chilled cargo (fruit) | 0 to +15 °C | R404A, R22 (legacy) | 50–500 kW | Single-stage DX |
| Frozen cargo (fish, meat) | –25 to –35 °C | R22, R404A, R717 | 100–2000 kW | Single/two-stage |
| Accommodation AC | 24 °C DB | R134a, R407C | 50–2000 kW | Chilled water |
| LNG boil-off reliquefaction | –162 °C | Mixed (C1/C2/C3/N₂) | 500–10 000 kW | Mixed-refrigerant cascade |
| LPG cargo (semi-refrigerated) | –42 to –5 °C | R717 (NH₃) | 200–3000 kW | Two-stage / cascade |
| Electronics cooling | 15–25 °C | R134a | 1–10 kW | Thermoelectric / DX |
| Fishing vessel RSW tanks | –1.5 to +2 °C | R22, R404A | 30–200 kW | Brine (NaCl) immersion |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Shipboard Refrigeration Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Diesel Generator / Shaft Generator] --> B[Main Switchboard 440V 60Hz]
    B --> C1[Cargo Refrigeration Compressor 1]
    B --> C2[Cargo Refrigeration Compressor 2]
    B --> C3[Provision Plant Compressor]
    B --> C4[Air Conditioning Chiller]
    C1 --> D1[Sea Water Cooled Condenser A]
    C2 --> D2[Sea Water Cooled Condenser B]
    C3 --> D3[Provision Condenser]
    C4 --> D4[AC Condenser]
    D1 --> E1[Cargo Hold 1 Evaporator]
    D1 --> E2[Cargo Hold 2 Evaporator]
    D2 --> E3[Cargo Hold 3 Evaporator]
    D3 --> E4[Provision Room Evaporator]
    D4 --> E5[Accommodation AHU Coil]
    E1 --> F1[Cargo Hold 1 Fish -25C]
    E2 --> F2[Cargo Hold 2 Meat -18C]
    E3 --> F3[Cargo Hold 3 Fruit +5C]
    E4 --> F4[Crew Provision Store +3C]
    E5 --> F5[Crew Quarters 24C DB]
    G1[Sea Chest] --> H1[Sea Water Cooling Pump]
    H1 --> D1
    H1 --> D2
    H1 --> D3
    H1 --> D4
    D1 --> I1[Sea Water Discharge Overboard]
    D2 --> I1
    D3 --> I1
    D4 --> I1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Electrical power flows from generators to the switchboard, then to four independent refrigeration plants (two cargo, one provision, one AC). Each plant's compressor pushes hot gas to its dedicated sea water condenser. Sea water is drawn from the sea chest, pumped through all condensers in parallel, and discharged overboard. Liquid refrigerant then expands into each evaporator serving its respective cold space.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical Reefer Ship Cargo Cycle (R717 Ammonia)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J1[Compressor Low Stage] --> J2[Intercooler]
    J2 --> J3[Compressor High Stage]
    J3 --> J4[Sea Water Condenser]
    J4 --> J5[High Pressure Receiver]
    J5 --> J6[Expansion Valve 1]
    J6 --> J7[Intermediate Flash Tank]
    J7 --> J8[Expansion Valve 2]
    J8 --> J9[Cargo Hold Evaporator -30C]
    J9 --> J1
    J7 --> J1
    K1[Emergency Compressor] -.Backup.-> J1
    L1[Plant Alarm and Monitoring System] --> J1
    L1 --> J3
    L1 --> J4
```

**Reading the diagram:** A two-stage ammonia compression cycle used for deep-frozen cargo. Refrigerant leaves the low-stage compressor, is desuperheated and cooled in the intercooler (fed by the flash tank), then compressed further in the high-stage. It condenses against sea water, accumulates in the receiver, expands in two stages via the flash tank, and evaporates at –30 °C in the cargo hold coils. A separate emergency compressor and the alarm/monitoring system provide redundancy and class compliance.

### 4.3 Architecture of Air Conditioning Plant on a Passenger Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Sea Water Inlet 28C] --> M2[AC Sea Water Pump]
    M2 --> M3[Central Sea Water Cooled Heat Exchanger]
    M3 --> M4[Chilled Water Tank 6C]
    M4 --> M5[Chilled Water Circulation Pump 1]
    M4 --> M6[Chilled Water Circulation Pump 2]
    M5 --> M7[AHU Cabin Block A]
    M5 --> M8[AHU Cabin Block B]
    M6 --> M9[AHU Galley and Dining]
    M6 --> M10[AHU Navigation Bridge]
    M6 --> M11[AHU Engine Control Room]
    M7 --> N1[Crew Cabins 24C]
    M8 --> N2[Crew Cabins 24C]
    M9 --> N3[Galley and Provision]
    M10 --> N4[Bridge and Communications]
    M11 --> N5[ECR Electronics 22C]
    M3 --> M12[Sea Water Discharge 33C]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Sea water enters at 28 °C, flows through the central heat exchanger to cool the chilled water loop down to 6 °C, and is discharged at 33 °C. Twin chilled water pumps feed four air handling units distributed throughout the ship, each maintaining its designated zone at the appropriate temperature. This is a classic **central plant + distributed AHU** topology used on modern cruise vessels.

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Three Shipboard System Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Top1[Direct Expansion DX System]
        O1[Compressor] --> O2[Condenser] --> O3[Expansion Valve] --> O4[Evaporator in Hold] --> O1
    end
    subgraph Top2[Brine Secondary System]
        P1[Compressor] --> P2[Condenser] --> P3[Expansion Valve] --> P4[Brine Chiller]
        P4 --> P5[Brine Pump] --> P6[Hold Coils] --> P4
    end
    subgraph Top3[Cascade System]
        Q1[Compressor Low] --> Q2[Evaporator Low] --> Q3[Condenser Cascade] --> Q4[Compressor High] --> Q5[Sea Water Condenser] --> Q6[Expansion High] --> Q3
        Q2 --> Q7[Hold at -40C]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three different architectures are illustrated for three different temperature needs:
- **Top 1 (DX):** Simplest, used for medium-temperature applications (+5 to –25 °C). Refrigerant evaporates directly inside the hold air coolers.
- **Top 2 (Brine):** Refrigerant cools a secondary glycol/water loop, which is pumped to hold cooling coils. Safer (less refrigerant leak risk into hold), more flexible.
- **Top 3 (Cascade):** Two independent cycles coupled at an intermediate heat exchanger. Used for very low temperatures (–40 to –80 °C) on LNG or LPG carriers.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three marine applications of mechanical refrigeration and state the typical operating temperature range for each.*

**Model Answer:**

> Three marine applications of mechanical refrigeration:
>
> 1. **Provision Cold Stores** — for preserving fresh and frozen food for crew consumption, operating at **+2 °C to +5 °C** for fresh and **–18 °C to –25 °C** for frozen stores.
> 2. **Refrigerated Cargo Holds (Reefer Ships)** — for transporting perishable commodities such as fish, meat, fruit and dairy, operating at **–25 °C to –30 °C** for frozen cargo and **0 °C to +15 °C** for chilled cargo like bananas.
> 3. **Air Conditioning of Accommodation and Navigation Spaces** — for crew thermal comfort on passenger and naval vessels, designed at approximately **24 °C dry-bulb** with 50 % RH for outdoor conditions of **35 °C DB / 28 °C WB**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why is sea water used as the heat sink in marine refrigeration plants instead of atmospheric air?*

**Model Answer:**

> Sea water is preferred as the heat sink on board ships because:
>
> 1. **Abundant and Free** — A ship sails on an unlimited reservoir of cooling water, available continuously without make-up.
> 2. **Lower Temperature than Air** — Tropical sea water is around **28–32 °C**, which is typically 5–10 °C lower than ambient air in the same climate, improving condenser effectiveness and COP.
> 3. **Higher Heat Transfer Coefficient** — Water has roughly **20–50 times** the convective heat transfer coefficient of air, requiring a much smaller heat exchanger for the same duty.
> 4. **Stable Year-Round** — At ship speeds, deep-sea temperature is nearly constant, simplifying control.
> 5. **Independent of Weather** — Unlike an air-cooled condenser on shore, no performance loss occurs during calm hot weather.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

(a) *Explain in detail the various marine applications of mechanical refrigeration with typical design temperatures, refrigerant selection, and capacity range.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *A reefer hold has the following dimensions: 25 m (L) × 12 m (B) × 6 m (H). It carries 500 tonnes of frozen fish at –28 °C. Outside ambient is +30 °C. Insulation is 120 mm thick polyurethane with k = 0.024 W/m·K. Estimate the total refrigeration load if 2 air changes per day occur, and the heat of respiration is negligible. Assume sensible heat ratio of air is 0.6, sea water at +28 °C, and a 10 % safety margin is applied. State the compressor capacity selected.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Marine Applications of Mechanical Refrigeration:**

The applications of mechanical refrigeration on board a ship are categorized as follows:

**(i) Provision Plant Refrigeration:**
Used to preserve food and drink for the crew. Capacity ranges from **5 kW to 80 kW**. Operates at two levels: chilled (+2 to +5 °C) and frozen (–18 to –25 °C). Refrigerant used is **R404A** or **R507** for small plants and **R717 ammonia** for larger vessels.

**(ii) Cargo Refrigeration (Reefer Ships):**
For perishable cargo like fish, meat, fruit, dairy. Capacity from **100 kW to several MW**. Evaporating temperatures vary from **+5 °C** for chilled fruit to **–30 °C** for frozen fish. Refrigerants: **R22 (legacy), R404A, R507, R717**.

**(iii) Air Conditioning Plant:**
For passenger accommodation, bridge, galley, ECR. Capacity **50 kW to 2 MW**. Uses chilled water at **5–7 °C** distributed via ring mains. Refrigerant: **R134a, R407C, R1234ze**.

**(iv) Liquefied Gas Carriers:**
For LNG and LPG cargo at cryogenic temperatures down to **–162 °C**. Uses **mixed-refrigerant cascades** or **nitrogen expander** cycles. Capacity **MW scale**.

**(v) Process Cooling:**
For lube oil coolers, jacket water pre-coolers, electronic cabinets, radar cooling. Capacity **1–50 kW**. Uses **R134a** or chilled water.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Surface Area Calculation:**

Deckhead = $25 \times 12 = 300$ m²
Two sides = $2 \times 25 \times 6 = 300$ m²
Two ends = $2 \times 12 \times 6 = 144$ m²
Bottom (assumed trough) = $300$ m²
Total area $A = 1044$ m²

**Step 2 — Conduction Heat Gain:**

U-value = $k/x = 0.024/0.120 = 0.20$ W/m²K

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = 0.20 \times 1044 \times (30 - (-28)) = 0.20 \times 1044 \times 58 = 12\,110 \text{ W} \approx 12.11 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Infiltration Heat Gain:**

Hold volume $V = 25 \times 12 \times 6 = 1800$ m³
Air changes: $n = 2/24 = 0.0833$ h⁻¹
Volume flow rate $\dot{V} = 1800 \times 0.0833 = 150$ m³/h = 0.04167 m³/s
Mass flow of air: $\dot{m}_a = 0.04167 \times 1.25 = 0.0521$ kg/s

Enthalpy difference: outside air (30 °C, 70 % RH, $h_o \approx 76$ kJ/kg) and inside (–28 °C, dry, $h_i \approx –28$ kJ/kg). $\Delta h \approx 104$ kJ/kg.

$$
\dot{Q}_{infil} = 0.0521 \times 104\,000 \approx 5418 \text{ W} \approx 5.42 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Fan Heat (assumed):**

Suppose 2 fans × 2 kW each, 70 % duty = $2 \times 2 \times 0.7 = 2.8$ kW

**Step 5 — Lighting and Personnel:**

Lighting 6 × 100 W × 0.5 = 0.3 kW; Personnel negligible in operation.

**Step 6 — Total Load:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 12.11 + 5.42 + 2.80 + 0.30 = 20.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Safety Margin:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{design} = 20.63 \times 1.10 = 22.69 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8 — Compressor Selection:**

Round up to next standard compressor size — select a **30 kW** reciprocating compressor with R404A refrigerant, suitable for evaporator temperature of –28 °C and condensing temperature of +38 °C (sea water at 28 °C + 10 °C approach).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget that conduction through the bottom of the hold (the trough/tank top) also occurs and contributes heat load. Always sum **all six** surfaces.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Infiltration air at 30 °C contains moisture that **freezes** on entering at –28 °C — students often treat it as purely sensible. The latent heat released when moisture deposits is **extra** load and must be included (using SHR < 1).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** The 10 % safety margin is **mandatory** under Class rules — failure to include it loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Students pick the condensing temperature without accounting for **10 °C approach** above sea water — this under-sizes the compressor.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Alternative Choice**

(a) *Compare the use of ammonia (R717) and R404A as marine refrigeration refrigerants. Justify your selection for (i) a small provision plant and (ii) a large fish factory vessel.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Explain the construction and operation of a centralized marine air conditioning plant with the aid of a block diagram. Describe the role of chilled water, AHUs, and sea water cooling in detail.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison of R717 and R404A for Marine Use:**

| Property | Ammonia R717 | R404A |
|---|---|---|
| ODP | 0 | 0 |
| GWP | 0 | 3922 |
| Toxicity | High | Low (A1 class) |
| Flammability | Low (B2L) | None |
| COP at –30 °C | Higher (~1.8) | Lower (~1.4) |
| Operating cost | Low | High (regulations) |
| Capital cost | Higher (Class requirements) | Lower |
| Detection | Easy (pungent smell) | Difficult (needs leak detector) |
| Material compatibility | Copper-free plant | Standard |

**(i) Small Provision Plant:** **R404A** is selected. The plant is small (< 50 kW), located near accommodation, hence the **low toxicity** of R404A is critical. Also, modern F-gas regulations penalize R404A only moderately for small charge sizes, and the lower capital cost suits a small installation.

**(ii) Large Fish Factory Vessel:** **Ammonia (R717)** is selected. The plant is large (often > 500 kW), located in a dedicated engine room with forced ventilation, and benefits from **higher COP at deep-frozen temperatures (–30 °C)**. Ammonia's zero GWP also future-proofs the vessel against tightening emission regulations. Crew exposure risk is managed by Class-approved leak detection, emergency ventilation, and segregated spaces.

**(b) Centralized Marine Air Conditioning Plant:**

A modern passenger or naval vessel employs a **central chiller plant** that produces chilled water at 5–7 °C. The chilled water is distributed via a ring main to multiple **Air Handling Units (AHUs)** located in each accommodation zone.

**Construction includes:**
- **Sea water circuit:** Sea chest → strainer → sea water pump → central heat exchanger → overboard discharge. Sea water absorbs heat from the refrigerant side.
- **Refrigerant circuit:** Compressor → sea water-cooled condenser → expansion valve → evaporator shell of the central chiller → back to compressor.
- **Chilled water circuit:** Chilled water tank → twin pumps (redundant) → ring main → AHU cooling coils → return to chiller.
- **AHUs in each zone:** Contain filter, cooling coil, fan, dampers, and humidification control. They condition air to the design room condition.
- **Automation:** BMS / Integrated Automation System (IAS) controls temperature, humidity, fan speed, and monitors alarms.

**Operation:** Warm return air from a cabin enters the AHU, passes through the filter, then over the chilled water coil (where it is cooled and dehumidified). The conditioned air is supplied via ducts to the cabin at ~24 °C DB and ~50 % RH. Heat absorbed by the chilled water is rejected to the sea water via the central heat exchanger.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Question B:**
> - **(a)** Students often state "ammonia is toxic" without comparing **toxicity classifications** (A1, B2L, B1). Use proper ASHRAE 34 nomenclature.
> - **(b)** Students forget the **redundancy** requirement — twin pumps, twin compressors, and ring main topology are all **Class requirements**, not optional. Also forgetting the **dehumidification** function of the AHU coil (sensible + latent) loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Marine Applications of Mechanical Refrigeration:**
>
> - ☐ Marine refrigeration serves **five** primary functions: cargo, provisions, air conditioning, liquefied gas, and process cooling.
> - ☐ The heat sink is **sea water**, drawn from a **sea chest** through **Cu-Ni 90/10 tubes**.
> - ☐ Sea water is **salty** (corrosive) and **contains biological fouling** — titanium plate heat exchangers or box coolers are increasingly used.
> - ☐ **Ammonia (R717)** is preferred for large plants > 200 kW; **R404A/R507** for medium; **R134a/R407C** for AC.
> - ☐ Plants must operate with **static ±22.5°** and **dynamic ±45°** list/trim per most Class rules.
> - ☐ **Two independent** refrigeration plants are required by Class for **refrigerated cargo** vessels carrying > 250 kW.
> - ☐ Reefer cargo ranges: chilled (0 to +15 °C), deep-chilled (–2 to 0 °C), frozen (–18 to –30 °C).
> - ☐ Heat load components: **conduction (through insulation), infiltration (hatch opening), respiration (fruit), fan heat, lighting, personnel**.
> - ☐ **Banana hold at +13 °C** has **respiration load ~87 %** of total — plant sizing must account for this.
> - ☐ Two-stage compression for evaporating below **–30 °C**; cascade below **–40 °C**.
> - ☐ Cryogenic LNG reliquefaction uses **mixed-refrigerant cascade** (C3MR) or **nitrogen expander** cycles.
> - ☐ Provision plant temperatures: chilled **+2 to +5 °C**, frozen **–18 to –25 °C**.
> - ☐ Accommodation AC: **24 °C DB / 50 % RH** tropical design; chilled water at **5–7 °C**.
> - ☐ Always apply a **10 % safety margin** on calculated refrigeration load for Class approval.
> - ☐ Use **flexible couplings** in refrigerant piping to absorb shipboard vibration.
> - ☐ Marine compressors are **direct-drive electric** or **shaft-driven** (rare). Reciprocating for medium capacity, screw for high.
> - ☐ Remember: **$\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{cond} + \dot{Q}_{infil} + \dot{Q}_{resp} + \dot{Q}_{fan} + \dot{Q}_{misc}$**.
> - ☐ Entropy and enthalpy values for **R717** must be read from saturated tables at $T_{evap}$ and $P_{cond}$.
> - ☐ The **IGC Code** governs ships carrying liquefied gases in bulk.
> - ☐ The **IGF Code** governs vessels using gas as fuel (relevant to LNG-fueled ships).
> - ☐ **Compressor utilization factor** $f_{util} = t_{on}/(t_{on}+t_{off})$ indicates how hard a compressor is working (Class rules require $f_{util} \le 0.85$).
> - ☐ Cold stores must have **two independent** temperature sensors and alarms per compartment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerated Ship's Stores Refrigeration at Sea
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refrigerated Ship's Stores Refrigeration at Sea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigerated Ship's Stores** are dedicated, thermally insulated compartments aboard a vessel designed to preserve perishable provisions, fresh and frozen foods, beverages, and other temperature-sensitive consumables required for the crew and passengers during the voyage. **Refrigeration at Sea** refers to the integrated suite of mechanical vapour compression (and occasionally absorption) refrigeration systems, control strategies, and insulation engineering practices employed to maintain specified storage temperatures onboard ships, accounting for the unique marine operating environment — namely constant motion, vibration, salt-laden humid air, confined machinery spaces, rolling/pitching, and stringent SOLAS/MARPOL safety constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic falls under the broader Module 1 objective: *"To impart knowledge on the design, operation, and maintenance of marine refrigeration systems for shipboard applications, with emphasis on provisions preservation and habitability."*

The principal classification of refrigerated ship stores is as follows:

| Store Type | Typical Operating Temperature | Common Cargo |
|---|---|---|
| **Chill Room (Cold Room)** | +2 °C to +5 °C | Fresh meat, fish, dairy, vegetables, eggs |
| **Freezer Room (Frozen Store)** | −18 °C to −25 °C | Frozen meat, frozen fish, ice cream |
| **Fruit & Vegetable Room** | +4 °C to +10 °C | Fresh produce, fruits |
| **Beverage Cooler** | +5 °C to +8 °C | Soft drinks, beer, wine |
| **Galley Refrigerators** | +2 °C to +5 °C | Day-use provisions |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **kitchen refrigerator at home** — but imagine it is **sailing on a stormy ocean, tilted 30° left, then 30° right, constantly vibrating from a diesel engine, surrounded by salty corrosive air, and must last 6 months without a service engineer visiting**. That, in essence, is a marine refrigerated store.

The three "enemies" of food at sea are:
1. **Heat** — trying to leak in from the warm ambient (engine room is 45 °C; outside hull in tropics is 35 °C).
2. **Moisture** — salt air condenses inside cold rooms, causing ice and corrosion.
3. **Motion** — rolling/pitching sloshes liquids, shifts loads, and stresses compressors.

To fight these, marine engineers surround the cold room in **thick insulation** (cork, rockwool, polyurethane foam), seal it with **vapour barriers** (asphalt-impregnated felt or aluminium foil), cool it with **rugged, direct-drive, semi-hermetic compressors** (because they tolerate tilt up to 30°), and design it for **easy drainage** of defrost water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Marine Design Constants (recall these in exams):**
> - Ambient design temperature outside insulation: **+35 °C** (tropical) or **+45 °C** (engine room bulkhead).
> - Sea water temperature for condenser cooling: **+32 °C** (tropical).
> - Maximum allowable compressor tilt: **30°** (some makers specify 35° permanent + 45° momentary).
> - Insulation thickness: typically **100 mm to 150 mm** of cork or PU foam.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently (approximately once in every 2 question papers) test on the differences between **ship's stores refrigeration** and **shore-based commercial cold storage**. Memorize: **(a) tilt tolerance, (b) compactness, (c) use of seawater-cooled condensers, (d) corrosion-resistant construction, (e) redundant units, (f) centralised vs individual system**.

A common KTU 2024 question reads: *"Compare the design considerations of a ship's refrigerated provisions store with a shore-based cold storage. Justify the choice of insulation material used on ships."* — You will see this exact flavour in Section 5.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The refrigeration of ship's stores operates on a logical cascade of **thermal engineering decisions**. Below is the structured reasoning that KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.

### Step 1: Establish the Storage Requirement
- Determine the **voyage duration** (e.g., 45 days for a bulk carrier; 21 days for a coastal vessel).
- Determine the **crew complement** (e.g., 25 crew → ~4.5 kg provisions/person/day).
- Compute the **total food load**: 
$$\text{Total Load (kg)} = \text{Crew} \times \text{Daily Allowance (kg/person/day)} \times \text{Voyage Days (d)}$$
- Split the load into **chill (60–70 %)** and **freeze (30–40 %)** fractions.
- Add **15–20 % safety margin** for the return voyage or extended port stay.

### Step 2: Compute the Heat Loads
The total refrigeration load $Q_{total}$ comprises five distinct components:

$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5
$$

Where:
- $Q_1$ = Transmission / conduction heat gain through walls, ceiling, floor.
- $Q_2$ = Heat from product cooling (sensible + latent, including respiration of fruits/vegetables).
- $Q_3$ = Heat from door openings, air infiltration, and personnel entry.
- $Q_4$ = Heat from lighting, fans, and defrosting.
- $Q_5$ = Heat from miscellaneous sources (hot pipes passing through, brine leaks, etc.).

### Step 3: Select the Insulation
- For shipboard use, **insulation must be**: non-hygroscopic, fire-retardant, resistant to oil/grease, dimensionally stable, low thermal conductivity, and **impervious to salt air**.
- Industry standards: **Corkboard (k = 0.040 W/m·K)**, **Rockwool (k = 0.035 W/m·K)**, **PU foam (k = 0.022 W/m·K)**, **Polystyrene (k = 0.033 W/m·K)**.
- Marine preference has shifted to **PU foam (rigid polyurethane)** due to lowest conductivity and in-situ foamability in complex ship geometry.

### Step 4: Choose the Refrigeration Cycle
- **Vapour Compression Cycle** is universal for ship's stores.
- **Direct Expansion (DX)** is preferred for small cold rooms; **indirect brine circulation** is used where load distribution across many rooms is needed.
- Refrigerant: historically **R-12**, **R-22**, **NH₃** (ammonia for large fish factory ships); now migrating to **R-404A**, **R-507**, **R-134a** under F-Gas and IMO regulations.

### Step 5: Size the Compressor, Condenser, Evaporator
- Apply a **safety factor of 1.25 to 1.5** on $Q_{total}$ to account for the marine environment (fouled seawater condenser tubes, restricted airflow in machinery spaces).
- Compressor must withstand **continuous tilt of 30°** and **brief 45° list** (during heavy weather).

### Step 6: Provide Redundancy
- For SOLAS-classed vessels on long international voyages, **two independent refrigeration plants** are mandated, each capable of handling 100 % of the load.
- Common practice: install **two compressors, two condensers, two evaporators**, with shared piping and three-way valves for cross-operation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula you must memorise** for this topic. KTU 2024 ESE questions on Module 1 typically require application of two to three of these.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_1 = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $A$ = area, $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across insulation, $d$ = insulation thickness | W (J/s) | Wall/floor/ceiling heat leakage |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{d}{k}$ | $R$ = thermal resistance | m²·K/W | Insulating value per layer |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K | Combined wall layers (insulation + air film + structure) |
| 4 | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U$ = overall U-value, $A$ = area, $\Delta T$ = (T_outside − T_inside) | W | Overall wall heat gain |
| 5 | $Q_2 = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible) | $m$ = mass of product, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = product cooling temperature drop | W | Cooling of warm provisions |
| 6 | $Q_{2,latent} = m \cdot L_f$ | $L_f$ = latent heat of freezing (~ 234 kJ/kg for water) | W | Freezing of product water content |
| 7 | $Q_3 = V \cdot n \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T$ | $V$ = cold room volume, $n$ = air change rate (typically 1 to 3 per hour for ship stores) | W | Door-opening & infiltration load |
| 8 | $Q_4 = P_{lights} + P_{fans} + P_{defrost}$ | All in watts, operating hours factored in | W | Internal heat sources |
| 9 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | $Q_{evap}$ = refrigeration effect, $W_{comp}$ = compressor work | dimensionless | System efficiency |
| 10 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W} \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$ | $T_H, T_L$ in Kelvin | dimensionless | Second-law efficiency of marine plant |
| 11 | $\text{Compressor displacement} = \dfrac{m_{ref}}{V_{spec} \cdot \eta_{vol}}$ | $V_{spec}$ = specific volume at suction, $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (~0.7–0.85) | m³/s | Sizing refrigerant mass flow |
| 12 | $d_{insulation} = k \cdot A \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{ext} - T_{int}}{Q_{allowed}}\right)$ | Re-arranged wall formula for thickness design | m | Insulation thickness design |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TRAP:** In wall heat gain problems, do **not** forget to include the **internal and external air film resistances** in $R_{total}$. The typical $h_{inside} \approx 8$ W/m²·K (natural convection inside cold room with circulating fan) and $h_{outside} \approx 10$–12 W/m²·K. Many students omit these and lose 1–2 marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations in Marine Engineering

- **$Q_1$ calculations** drive the **insulation specification** on the ship's GA (General Arrangement) drawing, which in turn drives **stability and tonnage calculations** (insulation is heavy — a 150 mm cork lining can add 8–12 tonnes to a 5000 GRT vessel).
- **$Q_2$ calculations** determine the **compressor size**, which determines the **auxiliary machinery space allocation** and the **electrical load** on the ship's generator.
- **Compressor tilt tolerance** drives the **lubrication system choice** — modern semi-hermetic compressors use splash lubrication with oil pumps that maintain oil supply even at 30° list.
- **Redundancy calculations** inform the **SOLAS compliance certification** and influence **insurance premiums** for the vessel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Heat Load and Required Insulation Thickness

This is a classic KTU 14-mark problem. We will derive an exhaustive design expression.

### Given Data (typical KTU 2024 problem):
- Cold room internal dimensions: **4 m × 3 m × 2.5 m (L × B × H)**.
- Wall, ceiling, floor all insulated.
- Internal air temperature: **+2 °C**.
- External ambient (worst tropical): **+35 °C**.
- Required refrigeration capacity (other loads, $Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5$): **3.0 kW**.
- Insulation: **Corkboard**, thermal conductivity $k_{cork} = 0.040$ W/m·K.
- Internal surface film coefficient: $h_i = 8$ W/m²·K.
- External surface film coefficient: $h_o = 11$ W/m²·K.
- Structure (mild steel plate): thickness 6 mm, $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K.
- Operating time: 18 hours per day (allow margin).

### Step 1 — Calculate the Surface Areas
We have six surfaces (one for each pair of opposing walls), but for ship cold rooms, we typically count:

$$
A_{ceiling} = L \times B = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{floor} = L \times B = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{long\ walls} = 2 \times (L \times H) = 2 \times (4 \times 2.5) = 20 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{short\ walls} = 2 \times (B \times H) = 2 \times (3 \times 2.5) = 15 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{total} = 12 + 12 + 20 + 15 = 59 \text{ m}^2
$$

> Note: For a ship cold room, if the room is built into the ship's side, one of the long walls may be the **hull side plate** with very high heat gain. We treat all four walls identically for this derivation; the engineer would apply a larger $\Delta T$ for the hull side in practice.

### Step 2 — Write the Thermal Resistance Network
For a composite wall with **three layers** (steel plate, cork insulation, inner lining of galvanised steel — neglected in this simple case), the resistances in series are:

$$
R_{total} = R_{i} + R_{steel} + R_{cork} + R_{o}
$$

In terms of thermal resistance per unit area:

$$
\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = \frac{1}{h_i A} + \frac{d_{steel}}{k_{steel} A} + \frac{d_{cork}}{k_{cork} A} + \frac{1}{h_o A}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U A} = \frac{1}{A}\left( \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{d_{steel}}{k_{steel}} + \frac{d_{cork}}{k_{cork}} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right)
$$

The **steel plate resistance is negligible** compared to insulation:

$$
R_{steel} = \frac{0.006}{45} = 0.000133 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
R_{cork} = \frac{d_{cork}}{0.040}
$$

$$
R_i = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
R_o = \frac{1}{11} = 0.0909 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

### Step 3 — Decide the Allowable Transmission Load
The standard design practice is to limit $Q_1$ to **30–40 %** of the total refrigeration load, so that the system has reserve capacity for the other loads. Given other loads = 3.0 kW, and assuming $Q_1$ should be about 1.5 kW (50 % margin for safety in marine service), we get:

$$
Q_1 = 1.5 \text{ kW} = 1500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 35 - 2 = 33 \text{ °C (or K, since difference)}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Corkboard Thickness
The required overall heat transfer coefficient:

$$
U = \frac{Q_1}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1500}{59 \times 33} = 0.770 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

The reciprocal:

$$
R_{total} = \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{0.770} = 1.299 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

Set up the resistance equation:

$$
1.299 = 0.125 + 0.000133 + \frac{d_{cork}}{0.040} + 0.0909
$$

$$
1.299 = 0.216 + \frac{d_{cork}}{0.040}
$$

$$
\frac{d_{cork}}{0.040} = 1.299 - 0.216 = 1.083
$$

$$
d_{cork} = 0.040 \times 1.083 = 0.0433 \text{ m} \approx 43.3 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Standard Thickness (Practical Marine Choice)
KTU examiners expect you to **round up to a standard insulation thickness**: choose **50 mm of corkboard** (next standard size above 43.3 mm). This gives an actual $R_{total}$ of:

$$
R_{total,actual} = 0.125 + 0.000133 + \frac{0.050}{0.040} + 0.0909 = 1.466 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U_{actual} = \frac{1}{1.466} = 0.682 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

$$
Q_{1,actual} = 0.682 \times 59 \times 33 = 1328 \text{ W} \approx 1.33 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify the Total Load and Compressor Sizing
$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 1.33 + 3.00 = 4.33 \text{ kW} \text{ (with other loads pre-aggregated)}
$$

Apply marine safety factor of 1.3:

$$
Q_{design} = 4.33 \times 1.3 = 5.63 \text{ kW}
$$

For 18 hours per day, the daily refrigeration duty:

$$
Q_{daily} = 5.63 \times 18 = 101.3 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to TR (Tons of Refrigeration) for Compressor Selection
$$
1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity (TR)} = \frac{5.63}{3.517} = 1.60 \text{ TR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When the question says "size the compressor", you must present the answer in **TR or kW** with a clear safety factor statement. Going from kW → TR is the easiest 1-mark bonus that 70 % of students miss.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ship vs Shore Cold Storage

This comparison is a **guaranteed question in KTU 2024 ESE**. The matrix below should be memorised.

| Parameter | Ship Cold Store | Shore Cold Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural motion** | Subject to roll, pitch, heave, vibration | Static foundation |
| **Ambient design T** | +35 °C (tropical) / +45 °C (engine room) | +40 °C (typical Indian summer) |
| **Insulation material** | Cork, PU foam (lightweight, non-absorbent) | Expanded polystyrene, PU, mineral wool |
| **Vapour barrier** | Hot bitumen + asphalt felt (mandatory) | Aluminium foil + mastic |
| **Compressor type** | Hermetic / semi-hermetic, oil pump-fed | Open type, belt drive acceptable |
| **Tilt tolerance** | 30° continuous, 45° momentary | Not a design criterion |
| **Condenser cooling** | Sea water (titanium tubes) or keel cooling | Cooling tower / water-cooled |
| **Refrigerant safety** | R-404A, NH₃ in large fish factories; toxic refrigerants restricted in crew spaces | R-22, R-404A, NH₃ all common |
| **Redundancy** | Two full-capacity plants (SOLAS) | Usually one plant + standby |
| **Space constraints** | Severe — must fit in hull geometry | Flexible, can build to shape |
| **Maintenance access** | Limited; parts must be standard | Workshop accessible |
| **Drainage** | Must drain to bilge, with P-trap | Floor drain to municipal sewer |
| **Weight penalty** | Insulation adds tonnage; affects stability | Negligible |
| **Fire safety** | Non-combustible insulation mandatory in crew corridors | Variable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Marine Refrigeration System for Ship's Stores

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SHIP COLD ROOM COMPARTMENT"] --> node2["TWO INDEPENDENT REFRIGERATION PLANTS"]
    node2 --> node3["PLANT A PRIMARY UNIT"]
    node2 --> node4["PLANT B STANDBY UNIT"]
    node3 --> node5["SEMI HERMETIC COMPRESSOR"]
    node3 --> node6["SEAWATER COOLED CONDENSER"]
    node3 --> node7["DX EVAPORATOR UNIT"]
    node3 --> node8["THERMOSTATIC EXPANSION VALVE"]
    node4 --> node9["SEMI HERMETIC COMPRESSOR STANDBY"]
    node4 --> node10["SEAWATER COOLED CONDENSER STANDBY"]
    node4 --> node11["DX EVAPORATOR STANDBY"]
    node4 --> node12["EXPANSION VALVE STANDBY"]
    node5 --> node13["COMPRESSED REFRIGERANT VAPOUR LINE"]
    node13 --> node6
    node6 --> node14["HIGH PRESSURE LIQUID LINE"]
    node14 --> node8
    node8 --> node15["LOW PRESSURE TWO PHASE MIXTURE"]
    node15 --> node7
    node7 --> node16["LOW PRESSURE VAPOUR SUCTION LINE"]
    node16 --> node5
    node1 --> node17["INSULATION LAYER CORK PU FOAM"]
    node1 --> node18["VAPOUR BARRIER BITUMEN FELT"]
    node1 --> node19["DRAIN TO BILGE WITH TRAP"]
    node1 --> node20["GALVANISED STEEL INNER LINING"]
    node21["AMBIENT HEAT 35 C TROPICAL"] --> node17
    node17 --> node1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Heat Load Components in a Ship Cold Room

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TOTAL REFRIGERATION LOAD QT"] --> nodeB["Q1 WALL CEILING FLOOR CONDUCTION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Q2 PRODUCT COOLING AND FREEZING"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Q3 DOOR OPENINGS INFILTRATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Q4 LIGHTS FANS DEFROST HEATERS"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Q5 MISCELLANEOUS HOT PIPES LEAKS"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["QTOTAL KW INPUT TO COMPRESSOR SIZING"]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["COMPRESSOR WITH 1.3 MARINE SAFETY FACTOR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["CONDENSER SEA WATER COOLED"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["EVAPORATOR IN COLD ROOM"]
```

## 4.3 Insulation Cross-Section Schematic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    extA["OUTSIDE TROPICAL AIR 35 C"] --> layer1["EXTERNAL STEEL HULL PLATE 6 MM"]
    layer1 --> layer2["HOT BITUMEN VAPOUR BARRIER 2 MM"]
    layer2 --> layer3["CORKBOARD OR PU FOAM 50 TO 150 MM"]
    layer3 --> layer4["INNER VAPOUR BARRIER ALUMINIUM FOIL"]
    layer4 --> layer5["GALVANISED STEEL INNER LINING 1.2 MM"]
    layer5 --> intA["COLD ROOM AIR 2 C"]
```

## 4.4 Cold Room Layout (Plan View Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    roomA["LONG WALL A 4 M"] --- roomB["LONG WALL B 4 M"]
    roomB --- roomC["SHORT WALL C 3 M"]
    roomC --- roomD["SHORT WALL D 3 M WITH DOOR"]
    roomD --- roomA
    evap1["EVAPORATOR UNIT A"] --- roomB
    evap2["EVAPORATOR UNIT B STANDBY"] --- roomA
    drain["FLOOR DRAIN TO BILGE"] --- roomC
    light["LED LIGHTING WEATHERPROOF"] --- roomA
    therm["THERMOSTAT AND TEMPERATURE SENSOR"] --- roomD
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four properties that make corkboard a suitable insulation material for refrigerated ship's stores.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Low thermal conductivity** ($k \approx 0.040$ W/m·K), reducing heat leakage.
2. **Non-hygroscopic** — does not absorb water/salt, hence no rot in humid marine air.
3. **Lightweight** — critical for ship's stability and tonnage.
4. **Fire-resistant** — slow-burning, does not propagate flame (important under SOLAS Chapter II-2).
5. **Dimensionally stable** under marine vibration and temperature cycles.
6. **Easily applied** to curved hull surfaces during ship construction.

*(Any four — 3 marks. KTU pattern: 1 mark per point, up to 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Why are two independent refrigeration plants provided for ship's refrigerated stores on long-voyage vessels?**

**Model Answer:**
1. **SOLAS/Class requirement** — for vessels on voyages exceeding a specified duration, redundancy is mandatory to prevent provisions loss.
2. **Maintenance flexibility** — one plant can be serviced while the other operates.
3. **Failure safety** — if the running plant trips due to a fault (low oil pressure, high discharge temp, motor burnout), the standby auto-starts and preserves food.
4. **Load sharing** — under normal operation, both plants run at part load, increasing efficiency and compressor life.
5. **Survey compliance** — classification societies (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's) require evidence of N+1 redundancy for refrigerated cargo stores.

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In heat load problems, **never omit the air film resistances** — this costs 1 mark.
> - In insulation thickness problems, **always round UP to the next standard commercial size** and recalculate the actual load. Students who skip this lose 2 marks.
> - In comparison questions, **use a TABELED format** — examiners award 1 mark for structured presentation. Bullet-only answers lose this.
> - **Draw a labelled block diagram** in any system design question — 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.
> - **State the safety factor explicitly**. "I applied 1.3 marine safety factor" is worth 1 mark on its own.
> - Convert final answer to **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)** wherever applicable — it earns a 1-mark "engineering judgement" credit.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model Paper)]

A refrigerated provisions room on a container ship has the following specifications:
- Internal dimensions: 5 m × 3 m × 2.5 m.
- Inside air temperature: **+2 °C**.
- Outside ambient (worst tropical): **+35 °C**.
- Floor and ceiling are insulated with **100 mm PU foam** ($k_{PU} = 0.022$ W/m·K).
- Walls are insulated with **80 mm corkboard** ($k_{cork} = 0.040$ W/m·K).
- Internal air film coefficient: $h_i = 8$ W/m²·K.
- External air film coefficient: $h_o = 11$ W/m²·K.
- Other heat loads (product cooling, door openings, lights, fans): **4.0 kW**.
- Compressor operates **18 hours/day**; marine safety factor = **1.3**.

#### (a) Compute the transmission heat load $Q_1$ through the walls, ceiling, and floor. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Surface areas:
- $A_{ceiling} = 5 \times 3 = 15$ m² (PU foam, 100 mm)
- $A_{floor} = 5 \times 3 = 15$ m² (PU foam, 100 mm)
- $A_{long\ walls} = 2 \times (5 \times 2.5) = 25$ m² (cork, 80 mm)
- $A_{short\ walls} = 2 \times (3 \times 2.5) = 15$ m² (cork, 80 mm)

Step 2 — $\Delta T$ across insulation:
$$
\Delta T = 35 - 2 = 33 \text{ °C}
$$

Step 3 — Total resistance for PU foam layer (ceiling/floor):
$$
R_{PU} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{0.100}{0.022} + \frac{1}{h_o} = 0.125 + 4.545 + 0.0909 = 4.761 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U_{PU} = \frac{1}{4.761} = 0.210 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

Step 4 — Total resistance for cork layer (walls):
$$
R_{cork} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{0.080}{0.040} + \frac{1}{h_o} = 0.125 + 2.000 + 0.0909 = 2.216 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U_{cork} = \frac{1}{2.216} = 0.451 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

Step 5 — Heat load through each section:
$$
Q_{ceiling} = U_{PU} \times A_{ceiling} \times \Delta T = 0.210 \times 15 \times 33 = 103.95 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{floor} = U_{PU} \times A_{floor} \times \Delta T = 0.210 \times 15 \times 33 = 103.95 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{long\ walls} = U_{cork} \times 25 \times 33 = 0.451 \times 25 \times 33 = 372.08 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_{short\ walls} = U_{cork} \times 15 \times 33 = 0.451 \times 15 \times 33 = 223.25 \text{ W}
$$

Step 6 — Total transmission load:
$$
Q_1 = 103.95 + 103.95 + 372.08 + 223.25 = 803.23 \text{ W} \approx 0.803 \text{ kW}
$$

#### (b) Determine the total refrigeration load and the required compressor capacity in kW and TR. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Total refrigeration load (before safety factor):
$$
Q_{total,raw} = Q_1 + Q_{other} = 0.803 + 4.000 = 4.803 \text{ kW}
$$

Step 2 — Apply marine safety factor of 1.3:
$$
Q_{design} = 4.803 \times 1.3 = 6.244 \text{ kW}
$$

Step 3 — Daily refrigeration duty (18 hours operation):
$$
Q_{daily} = 6.244 \times 18 = 112.4 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW):
$$
\text{Compressor capacity} = \frac{6.244}{3.517} = 1.775 \text{ TR} \approx 1.8 \text{ TR}
$$

**Final Answer:** Design compressor capacity ≈ **6.24 kW or 1.8 TR** (round up to next standard size of **2 TR**).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Compare the design considerations of a ship's refrigerated provisions store with a shore-based cold storage. Highlight the unique marine constraints. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer (tabulated for full marks):**

| Design Aspect | Ship Cold Store | Shore Cold Store |
|---|---|---|
| Motion / tilt | Compressors must tolerate 30° list, 45° momentary | No tilt constraint |
| Ambient T | +35 °C (tropical) / +45 °C (engine room bulkhead) | +40 °C design |
| Insulation weight | Critical — adds to displacement tonnage | Negligible effect on structure |
| Insulation material | Corkboard / PU foam (non-absorbent) | EPS, PU, mineral wool |
| Vapour barrier | Hot bitumen (marine grade) | Aluminium foil + mastic |
| Condenser | Sea water cooled (titanium tubes for corrosion) | Cooling tower / fresh water |
| Redundancy | Two full-capacity plants (SOLAS) | One + standby only |
| Drainage | To bilge with P-trap (smell block) | Municipal floor drain |
| Construction | Galvanised steel lining for corrosion resistance | Mild steel + paint |
| Compressor type | Hermetic / semi-hermetic, oil pump | Open, belt-driven |
| Maintenance | At sea; parts must be standard and stocked | Workshop accessible |
| Safety | Non-toxic refrigerant near crew (R-404A preferred over NH₃) | Any refrigerant class |

**Unique Marine Constraints (briefly):** (i) Vessel motion and heeling, (ii) confined machinery space, (iii) salt-air corrosion, (iv) reliance on seawater for cooling (biofouling), (v) limited shore-based maintenance, (vi) SOLAS / classification society rules, (vii) vibration from main engine.

**(b) Explain the construction of a typical marine refrigerated provisions room, including insulation, vapour barrier, lining, and drainage. [7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
