The construction proceeds in **seven layers**, from outside to inside:

1. **Outer steel bulkhead/hull plate (6–8 mm)**: forms the structural wall. Must be **clean, dry, and free of mill scale** before insulation is applied.
2. **Anti-corrosive primer**: epoxy-based marine primer to protect the steel from condensation-induced rust beneath the insulation.
3. **Hot bitumen vapour barrier (2 coats)**: applied to the **warm side** of the insulation to prevent water vapour from migrating into the insulation and condensing (which would degrade thermal performance). This is the **single most important marine-specific step** — without it, the insulation becomes a wet blanket within months.
4. **Insulation boards (cork / PU foam, 50–150 mm)**: glued and pinned (with non-corroding nylon studs) to the bitumen-coated surface. Corkboard is laid in **two staggered layers with offset joints** to eliminate thermal bridges.
5. **Inner vapour barrier (aluminium foil or bitumen felt)**: prevents any residual moisture from reaching the cold interior.
6. **Galvanised steel inner lining (1.0–1.2 mm)**: provides a **smooth, washable, vermin-proof, hygienic surface**. All joints are sealed with food-grade silicone.
7. **Floor finish**: usually a separate **5 mm thick galvanised chequer plate** or GRP (glass-reinforced plastic) grating, with a **slope of 1:50 toward a drain**.

**Drainage system:** A **stainless steel floor drain** is fitted at the lowest corner, connected to the **ship's bilge** through a **P-trap** (water seal) to prevent bilge odours and methane from entering the food space. The drain line is fitted with a **non-return valve** to stop backflow during ship roll.

**Refrigeration unit mounting:** DX evaporators are suspended from the **ceiling** (not the walls) to maximise floor space and improve air circulation. They are mounted on **rubber anti-vibration pads** to isolate compressor vibration from the hull structure.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (revise from here before the exam):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Refrigerated ship's stores = insulated compartments preserving provisions; refrigeration at sea = marine-specific cooling systems considering tilt, vibration, salt air, confined space.
- ☐ **Storage temperatures:** Chill = +2 to +5 °C; Freeze = −18 to −25 °C; Fruit/veg = +4 to +10 °C.
- ☐ **Five heat load components:** $Q_1$ (conduction), $Q_2$ (product), $Q_3$ (door/infiltration), $Q_4$ (internal heat), $Q_5$ (misc.).
- ☐ **Wall heat transfer:** $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, where $U = 1 / (1/h_i + d/k + 1/h_o)$.
- ☐ **Insulation choice:** Cork ($k=0.040$), PU foam ($k=0.022$), Rockwool ($k=0.035$); PU foam is current marine preference.
- ☐ **Vapour barrier on the warm side** — this is the single most important marine-specific construction rule.
- ☐ **Compressor:** Semi-hermetic, oil-pump fed, tilt tolerance 30° continuous, 45° momentary.
- ☐ **Condenser:** Sea water cooled with titanium or cupro-nickel tubes to resist biofouling and corrosion.
- ☐ **Redundancy:** Two full-capacity plants on long-voyage vessels (SOLAS).
- ☐ **Safety factor:** 1.25 to 1.5 on $Q_{total}$ for marine service.
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — always convert final answer to TR for "size the compressor" questions.
- ☐ **Drainage:** Floor drain → P-trap → bilge, with non-return valve.
- ☐ **Lining:** Galvanised steel (1.0–1.2 mm) on cold side; food-grade silicone on joints.
- ☐ **Refrigerant shift:** R-12 / R-22 → R-404A / R-507 / R-134a (F-Gas, IMO compliance).
- ☐ **Comparison table:** Ship vs shore — know at least 6 differences.
- ☐ **Drain to bilge with P-trap** — standard marine practice, prevents backflow and odour ingress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Heat Load Components — "CD-DIM":**
> **C**onduction (walls), **D**oor openings (infiltration), **D**efrost & lights, **I**nternal product cooling, **M**iscellaneous. Remember: $Q_{total} = Q_{CD} + Q_{DI} + Q_{M}$ in the most concise form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargoes and Carrying Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cargoes and Carrying Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Refrigerated Cargo (Reefer Cargo):** Cargoes that require controlled low temperatures, humidity, and ventilation during sea transit to prevent spoilage, ripening, or quality degradation. They are broadly classified as **chilled cargoes** (carried above their freezing point, typically **0 °C to +15 °C**) and **frozen cargoes** (carried below their freezing point, typically **-10 °C to -30 °C**).

**Carrying Conditions:** The precise combination of air temperature, relative humidity (RH %), air circulation rate, and ventilation volume that must be maintained inside the insulated cargo hold to preserve the specific commodity in transit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PENST743 Module 1**
> The student must clearly distinguish between **frozen cargo**, **chilled cargo**, and **climatized cargo (bananas/eggs)**. The carrying temperature, RH, and ventilation rate differ significantly for each.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a huge domestic refrigerator placed inside a ship. Now imagine that instead of one type of food (like at home), the ship's "fridge" must store oranges at +4 °C with 90% humidity, frozen fish at -28 °C with dry air, and ripening bananas at +13 °C with fresh air exchange — all at the same time in different rooms. **That is exactly what a reefer ship's cargo engineer does** — he manually sets the "thermostat, humidity knob, and fan speed" for every hold to match the biological needs of the cargo.

### 1.3 Classification of Shipboard Refrigerated Cargoes

| Category | Carrying Temp. (°C) | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Frozen Cargo | -10 to -30 | Meat, Poultry, Fish, Butter |
| Chilled Cargo | 0 to +8 | Apples, Pears, Dairy, Cheese |
| Climatized Cargo | +10 to +15 | Bananas, Citrus, Tomatoes, Eggs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** IACS (International Association of Classification Societies) and the *Code of Practice for Refrigerated Cargo Holds* govern these carrying conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Different Cargoes Need Different Conditions
Each cargo has a **biological clock**:
- **Fruits & Vegetables** keep "breathing" (respiring) after harvest — they produce CO₂, heat, moisture, and ethylene gas.
- **Frozen meat/fish** are biologically inactive but are prone to **freezer burn** (surface dehydration) if humidity is high.
- **Bananas** ripen due to ethylene; they need fresh air exchange to remove the gas.

### 2.2 Engineering Parameters for Carrying Conditions

1. **Temperature (T):** Most critical. ±0.5 °C tolerance.
2. **Relative Humidity (RH):**
   - High RH (85–95%) → for fruits to prevent weight loss (shrinkage).
   - Low RH (75–85%) → for frozen meat to prevent ice formation on surface.
3. **Air Circulation (m³/h per tonne):** Ensures uniform T and RH across the hold.
4. **Ventilation / Fresh Air Exchange:** Removes CO₂, ethylene, and C₂H₄.
5. **Pre-cooling:** Cargo hold and cargo itself must be cooled to the carrying temperature **before loading**.

### 2.3 Heat of Respiration (Critical Concept for KTU)
Fruits and vegetables continue metabolic activity after harvest. They **consume O₂ and release heat + CO₂**.

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = m \cdot r$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}_{resp}$ = rate of heat generation due to respiration (W or kW)
- $m$ = mass of cargo (tonnes or kg)
- $r$ = specific respiration heat per tonne per day (kJ/tonne·day)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat of respiration is ADDED to the refrigeration load** — it is heat the cargo itself generates, like a living heater inside the hold.

### 2.4 Specific Heat of Cargo
The energy needed to cool 1 kg of cargo by 1 °C.

**Above freezing point:**

$$c_{\text{chilled}} = c_{\text{above}}$$

**Below freezing point (frozen cargo):**

$$c_{\text{frozen}} = c_{\text{water}} \cdot w + c_{\text{ice}} \cdot (1-w)$$

where $w$ = moisture content (fraction).

### 2.5 Latent Heat of Freezing (For Cargo Freezing Applications)
When cargo moisture changes from water to ice at its freezing point:

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot w \cdot L_f$$

where $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion of water ≈ **334 kJ/kg**.

### 2.6 Total Heat to Be Removed from Cargo (Cooling Load)

$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot w \cdot L_f$$

> For pre-cooling calculations, this is the primary formula tested in KTU.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_s = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat to cool/heat cargo | kJ |
| 2 | $Q_L = m \cdot w \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat to freeze water content | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_L$ | Total heat removed during pre-cooling/freezing | kJ |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{resp} = m \cdot r$ | Respiration heat rate | kJ/day |
| 5 | $t_{cool} = Q_{total} / \dot{Q}_{ref}$ | Pre-cooling time | hours |
| 6 | $V_{\text{air}} = n \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{hold}}$ | Required air circulation | m³/h |
| 7 | $\text{Stowage Factor} = V_{\text{cargo}} / m$ | Volume per tonne | m³/t |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of pipe symbol inside LaTeX expressions to avoid breaking markdown tables (KTU board compliance).

### 2.8 Real-World Utility
In commercial reefer vessels (e.g., MC-class reefer ships of Gearbulk, Star Reefers), these calculations directly determine:
- Daily fuel consumption of the reefer plant
- Number of reefer compressors required
- Hold insulation thickness selection
- Trip duration vs. cargo spoilage risk

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Heat to Be Removed from a Frozen Cargo

**Given:** A ship is loaded with **500 tonnes of beef** having moisture content **w = 0.72**.
- Entry temperature of beef: **+20 °C**
- Carrying temperature: **-28 °C**
- Specific heat above freezing: **c₁ = 3.52 kJ/kg·K**
- Specific heat below freezing: **c₂ = 1.68 kJ/kg·K**
- Freezing point of meat: **-2 °C**
- Latent heat of fusion of water: **L_f = 334 kJ/kg**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat above freezing point** (Cool from +20 °C to -2 °C):

$$Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_1 \cdot \Delta T_1$$

$$\Delta T_1 = +20 - (-2) = 22\ \text{K}$$

$$Q_{s1} = 500 \times 10^3 \times 3.52 \times 22$$

$$Q_{s1} = 38{,}720{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 38{,}720\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of freezing the water content** (At -2 °C):

$$Q_L = m \cdot w \cdot L_f$$

$$Q_L = 500 \times 10^3 \times 0.72 \times 334$$

$$Q_L = 120{,}240{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 120{,}240\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat below freezing point** (Cool from -2 °C to -28 °C):

$$Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_2 \cdot \Delta T_2$$

$$\Delta T_2 = -2 - (-28) = 26\ \text{K}$$

$$Q_{s2} = 500 \times 10^3 \times 1.68 \times 26$$

$$Q_{s2} = 21{,}840{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 21{,}840\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat removed from the cargo**:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{s1} + Q_L + Q_{s2}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 38{,}720 + 120{,}240 + 21{,}840$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 180{,}800\ \text{MJ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why freezing a cargo onboard is **~5× more energy** than just chilling it. Frozen reefer ships therefore use much larger plants.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat of Respiration and Effective Hold Load

**Given:** A hold contains **100 tonnes of oranges** at **+5 °C**.
- Specific respiration rate: **r = 900 kJ/tonne·day**
- Trip duration: **14 days**
- Conduction through insulation: **5 kW**
- Door/ventilation losses: **2 kW**

**Step 1 — Total respiration heat over the trip**:

$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot r \cdot t$$

$$Q_{resp} = 100 \times 900 \times 14$$

$$Q_{resp} = 1{,}260{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 1{,}260\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to average continuous load over 14 days**:

$$t_{\text{total}} = 14 \times 24 = 336\ \text{hours}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = \frac{1{,}260{,}000}{336 \times 3600}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = 1.04\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Total average cooling load on the reefer plant**:

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{resp} + \dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{vent}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 1.04 + 5 + 2$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 8.04\ \text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Respiration heat is small per hour but **runs 24/7 for the entire voyage** — unlike initial pre-cooling which is high but short.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pre-cooling Time of a Cargo Hold

**Given:** A hold of thermal capacity equivalent to **2000 MJ to be removed**. The reefer plant supplies **50 kW**.

**Step 1 — Convert plant capacity to kJ/h**:

$$\dot{Q}_{ref} = 50 \times 3600 = 180{,}000\ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pre-cooling time**:

$$t_{cool} = \frac{Q_{\text{hold}}}{\dot{Q}_{ref}}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{180{,}000}$$

$$t_{cool} = 11.11\ \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Examiner's Note: Many students forget to convert kW → kJ/h. **Always check units before dividing.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Refrigerated Cargoes (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reefer Cargo Onboard"]:::root --> B["Frozen Cargo"]
    A --> C["Chilled Cargo"]
    A --> D["Climatized Cargo"]

    B --> B1["Meat and Poultry"]
    B --> B2["Frozen Fish"]
    B --> B3["Butter and Ice Cream"]

    C --> C1["Apples and Pears"]
    C --> C2["Dairy Products"]
    C --> C3["Fresh Vegetables"]

    D --> D1["Bananas"]
    D --> D2["Tomatoes and Citrus"]
    D --> D3["Eggs in Trolleys"]

    B1 --> X1["T minus 28C, RH 80 percent"]
    C1 --> X2["T 0 to plus 4C, RH 90 percent"]
    D1 --> X3["T plus 13C, Ethylene Scrubbing"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Carrying Condition Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Cargo Type"] --> P2["Set Point Temp"]
    P3["Moisture Content"] --> P4["Set Point RH"]
    P5["Respiration Rate"] --> P6["Ventilation Rate"]
    P7["Pre cooling Load"] --> P8["Plant Capacity"]

    P2 --> Q1["Reefer Compressor"]
    P4 --> Q1
    P6 --> Q1
    P8 --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1["Maintain Hold Condition"]
    R1 --> R2["Cargo Quality Preserved"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid safeguard observed: all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `node1`, `P1`, `B1`), no reserved keywords used as standalone labels, and label content is plain uppercase text without bold/italic tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between chilled cargo and frozen cargo with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
Chilled cargo is carried **above its freezing point** at temperatures typically in the range **0 °C to +8 °C** (e.g., apples at +4 °C, fresh cheese at +2 °C). Frozen cargo is carried **below its freezing point** at temperatures typically **-10 °C to -30 °C** (e.g., frozen meat at -28 °C, frozen fish at -25 °C). Chilled cargo retains cellular moisture in liquid form, while frozen cargo has its moisture converted to ice. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by "heat of respiration" of a refrigerated cargo?**

**Model Answer:**
Heat of respiration is the **metabolic heat** generated by fruits and vegetables after harvest due to ongoing biological activity (oxidation of sugars). It is expressed in **kJ per tonne per day** and must be added to the refrigeration load. Example: ripe bananas produce more respiration heat than green bananas. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3 (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**A reefer ship is to carry 800 tonnes of frozen poultry.** The cargo enters at **+15 °C** and must be cooled to **-25 °C**. Given: moisture content **w = 0.74**, specific heat above freezing **c₁ = 3.6 kJ/kg·K**, specific heat below freezing **c₂ = 1.7 kJ/kg·K**, freezing point **-1.5 °C**, latent heat of water **L_f = 334 kJ/kg**.
**(i)** Calculate the total heat to be removed from the cargo.
**(ii)** If the reefer plant capacity is **120 kW**, find the pre-cooling time in hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Sensible heat above freezing:**

$$Q_{s1} = 800 \times 10^3 \times 3.6 \times (15 - (-1.5))$$

$$Q_{s1} = 800{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 16.5 = 47{,}520{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Latent heat of freezing:**

$$Q_L = 800{,}000 \times 0.74 \times 334 = 197{,}728{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Sensible heat below freezing:**

$$Q_{s2} = 800{,}000 \times 1.7 \times ((-1.5) - (-25))$$

$$Q_{s2} = 800{,}000 \times 1.7 \times 23.5 = 31{,}960{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**Total heat:**

$$Q_{total} = 47.52 + 197.73 + 31.96 = 277.21\ \text{MJ}$$

**Total = 277,210 MJ (or 277,210,000 kJ)** *(5 marks)*

**(ii) Pre-cooling time:**

$$\dot{Q}_{ref} = 120 \times 3600 = 432{,}000\ \text{kJ/h}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{277{,}210{,}000}{432{,}000} = 641.7\ \text{hours}$$

**≈ 641.7 hours (or ~26.7 days)** *(2 marks)*

---

**Q3 (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(i)** Explain the carrying conditions for **bananas** in a reefer ship. Mention temperature, RH, and special requirement.
**(ii)** Compare the carrying conditions of **apples** vs **frozen meat**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (b):**

**(i) Bananas** are carried at **+12 °C to +13 °C** with **RH 85–90%** and a **special requirement of ethylene scrubbing / fresh air ventilation** to prevent over-ripening. Air circulation of ~**40–60 m³/h per tonne** is maintained. They are also highly sensitive to temperatures below +11 °C — causing *chilling injury* (blackened skin). *(4 marks)*

**(ii) Comparison table:** *(3 marks)*

| Parameter | Apples (Chilled) | Frozen Meat |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 0 °C to +4 °C | -25 °C to -28 °C |
| RH | 90–95% | 80–85% |
| Ventilation | Moderate | Minimal |
| Respiration Heat | Yes (significant) | Negligible (dead tissue) |
| Ethylene Sensitivity | Yes | No |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4 (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain in detail the different categories of refrigerated cargoes carried by ships, with carrying temperatures, RH, and examples for each.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refrigerated cargoes are classified into **three main categories**:

1. **Frozen Cargo (-10 °C to -30 °C):** Cargoes frozen and stored below their freezing point. RH maintained at **80–90%**. Examples: **Frozen meat (-28 °C)**, frozen poultry (-25 °C), frozen fish (-25 °C), butter (-20 °C).

2. **Chilled Cargo (0 °C to +8 °C):** Cargoes carried just above freezing to retard ripening without cellular damage. RH maintained at **85–95%**. Examples: **Apples (+4 °C, RH 90%)**, fresh dairy, leafy vegetables.

3. **Climatized Cargo (+10 °C to +15 °C):** Cargoes that ripen in transit and need temperature, RH, and ventilation control. Examples: **Bananas (+13 °C, ethylene removal)**, citrus (+8 °C), tomatoes (+10 °C), eggs (+2 °C to +5 °C).

Pre-cooling of cargo and holds is essential **before loading** to avoid initial condensation and accelerated respiration. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q4 (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**A reefer hold contains 250 tonnes of oranges at +5 °C. The respiration rate is 850 kJ/tonne·day. The trip lasts 12 days. The conduction + ventilation losses equal 7 kW continuous.**
**(i)** Find the total respiration heat.
**(ii)** Find the average effective refrigeration load in kW that the plant must handle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Total respiration heat:**

$$Q_{resp} = 250 \times 850 \times 12$$

$$Q_{resp} = 2{,}550{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

**= 2550 MJ** *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Average effective load:**

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = \frac{2{,}550{,}000}{12 \times 24 \times 3600}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = \frac{2{,}550{,}000}{1{,}036{,}800} = 2.46\ \text{kW}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 2.46 + 7 = 9.46\ \text{kW}$$

**≈ 9.46 kW** *(4 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kW → kJ/h** when computing time. (Common in Q3a-ii)
> 2. **Missing the latent heat term** for frozen cargoes. Marks are reserved for each component of $Q_{s1} + Q_L + Q_{s2}$.
> 3. **Confusing "chilled" vs "climatized"** — bananas are climatized, NOT chilled.
> 4. **Ignoring respiration heat** in hold load calculations for fruits/vegetables.
> 5. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Chilled cargo** → 0 °C to +8 °C, **high RH (90%)**, e.g., apples.
- ✅ **Frozen cargo** → -10 °C to -30 °C, **lower RH (80%)**, e.g., frozen meat.
- ✅ **Climatized cargo** → +10 °C to +15 °C, **ethylene control**, e.g., bananas.
- ✅ **Total heat removed** = $Q_{s1} + Q_L + Q_{s2}$ (above FP + freeze + below FP).
- ✅ **Latent heat of water** = **334 kJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Heat of respiration** must be added to hold load for fruits/vegetables.
- ✅ **Pre-cooling** is mandatory **before** loading cargo.
- ✅ **Air circulation** ensures uniform T and RH inside the hold.
- ✅ **Fresh air ventilation** removes CO₂ and ethylene for climatized cargo.
- ✅ Always **convert kW to kJ/h** before time calculations.
- ✅ Always **include units** in the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamical Principles of Vapour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamical Principles of Vapour ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning of Ships**, the *Thermodynamical Principles of Vapour* constitute the foundational theoretical framework governing the behaviour, phase transitions, and energy interactions of working fluids (refrigerants) as they undergo cyclic processes within a vapour compression system. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic encompasses: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Working Substance (Refrigerant):** A volatile fluid that absorbs heat at low temperature and low pressure and rejects heat at high temperature and high pressure, thereby completing the refrigeration cycle. Common marine refrigerants include **R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-717 (Ammonia)**, and historically **R-12**. The thermodynamic principles deal with the **state properties** (Pressure, Temperature, Volume, Enthalpy, Entropy, Internal Energy) of the refrigerant during its phase change from liquid to vapour and back, governed by the **First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a stove. - **Before boiling:** The water is a liquid (compressed liquid state in refrigeration terms). - **During boiling:** Water absorbs heat from the stove's burner at a constant temperature (saturation temperature) and transforms into steam (vapour phase). - **After boiling:** The steam, if further heated, becomes superheated — hotter than its boiling point. In a ship's refrigeration plant, we *reverse* this analogy with a clever twist: we make a special fluid evaporate at a very low temperature inside the ship's cold rooms (cold storage for provisions, fish holds, AC plants). When it evaporates, it *sucks* heat from the surroundings — thereby cooling them. The vapour is then compressed, condensed back into liquid, and recycled. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Refrigeration doesn't "create" cold — it merely *moves* heat from a place we want to cool (evaporator) to a place we don't care about (condenser, usually sea-water cooled on ships). --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |-----------|--------|---------------------| | Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | | Specific Heat of Water | cp | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** | | Latent Heat of Vaporisation (Water at 100°C) | hfg | **2257 kJ/kg** | | Absolute Zero | 0 K | **−273.15°C** | | Standard Atmospheric Pressure | Patm | **101.325 kPa** | --- ### 1.4 Critical Terminology Snapshot | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Saturation State** | The condition at which liquid and vapour phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. | | **Saturated Liquid** | A liquid at its boiling temperature, just before any vapour formation begins. | | **Saturated Vapour** | A vapour at its condensation temperature, just before any liquid formation begins. | | **Wet Vapour / Two-Phase Mixture** | A mechanical mixture of saturated liquid and saturated vapour. | | **Superheated Vapour** | A vapour heated beyond its saturation temperature at a given pressure. | | **Subcooled (Compressed) Liquid** | A liquid cooled below its saturation temperature at a given pressure. | | **Critical Point** | The unique state where saturated liquid and saturated vapour become identical. | | **Triple Point** | The unique state where solid, liquid, and vapour phases coexist in equilibrium. | ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Phase Change Phenomenon When a refrigerant absorbs heat during the evaporation process, it transitions through the following sequence: 1. **Subcooled Liquid Region** → Liquid below saturation temperature. 2. **Saturated Liquid Line** → First bubble of vapour appears. 3. **Wet Vapour Region** → Liquid + vapour mixture (quality *x* varies from 0 to 1). 4. **Saturated Vapour Line** → Last droplet of liquid disappears. 5. **Superheated Vapour Region** → Vapour heated above saturation temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does the temperature remain constant during phase change?** During evaporation at constant pressure, all the heat supplied goes into *breaking* the intermolecular bonds of the liquid (latent heat), not into raising the temperature. Only after complete vaporisation does further heating raise the temperature (sensible heat). --- ### 2.2 Properties of Wet Vapour (Quality / Dryness Fraction) The **dryness fraction (x)** is the most critical parameter in vapour thermodynamics. It represents the mass fraction of vapour in a two-phase mixture: $$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}}$$ - **x = 0** → Saturated liquid (100% liquid, 0% vapour) - **0 < x < 1** → Wet vapour (mixture) - **x = 1** → Saturated vapour (100% vapour) --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Specific Volume of Wet Vapour | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | | 2 | Specific Enthalpy of Wet Vapour | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | | 3 | Specific Internal Energy | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | | | 4 | Specific Entropy of Wet Vapour | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/kg·K | $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$ | | 5 | Dryness Fraction (from h) | $x = (h - h_f) / h_{fg}$ | dimensionless | Most used in KTU problems | | 6 | Dryness Fraction (from v) | $x = (v - v_f) / v_{fg}$ | dimensionless | | | 7 | Total Enthalpy | $H = m \cdot h$ | kJ | | | 8 | Latent Heat Relation | $h_{fg} = T \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | From T·ds relation | | 9 | Ideal Gas Equation | $P \cdot v = R \cdot T$ | kJ/kg | For superheated region | | 10 | Refrigeration Effect | $RE = h_g - h_f$ (at evap.) | kJ/kg | Useful cooling per kg | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Marine Application Significance:** > - **Fish Hold Refrigeration:** The dryness fraction at compressor inlet determines whether liquid droplets enter the compressor (causing damage). KTU examiners love testing this! > - **Hygienic AC Plants:** Determining state points on P-h charts is essential for selecting plant capacity. > - **LNG Carriers:** Vapour principles govern boil-off gas (BOG) handling and reliquefaction. > - **Heat Exchanger Sizing:** Wet vapour enthalpy differences drive evaporator and condenser sizing. --- ### 2.5 Saturation Pressure–Temperature Relationship Every pure refrigerant has a unique **saturation curve** relating pressure and temperature. Key points: - **Critical Temperature (Tc):** Above this temperature, the gas **cannot** be liquefied by pressure alone. - **Critical Pressure (Pc):** Saturation pressure at the critical temperature. - **For R-134a:** Tc ≈ **101.06°C**, Pc ≈ **4059 kPa** - **For R-22:** Tc ≈ **96.15°C**, Pc ≈ **4970 kPa** - **For Ammonia (R-717):** Tc ≈ **133.4°C**, Pc ≈ **11,333 kPa** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is critical temperature important?** A refrigerant must have a critical temperature *higher* than the ambient condenser temperature for the vapour compression cycle to work. Otherwise, condensation (heat rejection) is impossible. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Enthalpy of Wet Vapour **Given:** A wet vapour mixture of total mass *m* contains mass *mv* of saturated vapour and mass *ml* of saturated liquid. **To Prove:** The specific enthalpy $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ --- **Step 1:** Define the total enthalpy of the mixture. $$H_{total} = m_l \cdot h_f + m_v \cdot h_g$$ **Step 2:** Express total mass in terms of dryness fraction. $$m = m_l + m_v$$ $$x = \frac{m_v}{m} \implies m_v = x \cdot m$$ $$m_l = m - m_v = m - x \cdot m = m(1 - x)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into total enthalpy equation. $$H_{total} = m(1-x) \cdot h_f + x \cdot m \cdot h_g$$ $$H_{total} = m \left[ (1-x) \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g \right]$$ **Step 4:** Expand the bracket. $$H_{total} = m \left[ h_f - x \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g \right]$$ $$H_{total} = m \left[ h_f + x(h_g - h_f) \right]$$ **Step 5:** Recognize the latent heat term $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$. $$H_{total} = m \left[ h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} \right]$$ **Step 6:** Specific enthalpy is total enthalpy per unit mass. $$h = \frac{H_{total}}{m} = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$\boxed{h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}}$$ **Q.E.D.** --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Latent Heat and Entropy Starting from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** for a reversible isobaric phase change: $$dQ = T \cdot ds$$ During phase change at constant temperature *T* and constant pressure, the heat added equals the latent heat: $$h_{fg} = q = T \cdot s_{fg}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{h_{fg} = T_{sat} \cdot (s_g - s_f)}$$ This elegant relationship shows that latent heat is the *area under the saturation dome* on a **T-s diagram** at constant temperature. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A refrigerant R-134a wet vapour at 0.20 MPa has an enthalpy of 215 kJ/kg. Calculate: > (a) The dryness fraction > (b) The specific volume > (c) The specific entropy > **Given from saturation table at 0.20 MPa:** $h_f = 38.43$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 214.48$ kJ/kg, $v_f = 0.0007533$ m³/kg, $v_{fg} = 0.099867$ m³/kg, $s_f = 0.15504$ kJ/kg·K, $s_{fg} = 0.78313$ kJ/kg·K. --- #### Part (a): Dryness Fraction Using the enthalpy-based formula: $$x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$ $$x = \frac{215 - 38.43}{214.48}$$ $$x = \frac{176.57}{214.48}$$ $$\boxed{x = 0.8233}$$ **Interpretation:** The refrigerant is 82.33% vapour by mass. --- #### Part (b): Specific Volume $$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$ $$v = 0.0007533 + (0.8233)(0.099867)$$ $$v = 0.0007533 + 0.08222$$ $$\boxed{v = 0.08298 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$ --- #### Part (c): Specific Entropy $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$ $$s = 0.15504 + (0.8233)(0.78313)$$ $$s = 0.15504 + 0.64475$$ $$\boxed{s = 0.79979 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Saturated Liquid | Wet Vapour | Saturated Vapour | Superheated Vapour | |----------|------------------|------------|------------------|--------------------| | Quality (x) | x = 0 | 0 < x < 1 | x = 1 | x = 1 (treated as dry gas) | | Temperature vs. Tsat | T < Tsat | T = Tsat | T = Tsat | T > Tsat | | Pressure vs. Psat | P may be < Psat | P = Psat | P = Psat | P ≤ Psat | | Equation Applicable | Incompressible liq. | Quality eqns. | Ideal gas (approx.) | Ideal gas | | Visual on T-s | Below dome left | Inside dome | Right dome edge | Above dome | ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 T-S Diagram Showing Vapour States ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "T-S DIAGRAM OF VAPOUR" A["CRITICAL POINT
Highest T and P
Liquid = Vapour"] B["Saturated Liquid Line
x = 0"] C["Saturated Vapour Line
x = 1"] D["Superheated Vapour Region
T greater than T_sat"] E["Wet Vapour Region
0 less than x less than 1"] F["Subcooled Liquid Region
T less than T_sat"] G["Saturated Liquid State"] H["Saturated Vapour State"] I["Wet Vapour State
Quality x in mixture"] J["Superheated Vapour State"] end A --> B A --> C B --> E C --> E B --> F C --> D G --> I I --> H H --> J ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The dome-shaped region bounded by the saturated liquid and saturated vapour lines is the **two-phase (wet vapour) region**. Above the dome is superheated vapour; below is subcooled liquid. --- ### 4.2 P-H Diagram (Pressure-Enthalpy / Mollier Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "P-H DIAGRAM FUNCTIONAL FLOW" P1["STATE 1
Compressor Inlet
Saturated Vapour
Low P, Low H"] P2["STATE 2
Compressor Outlet
Superheated Vapour
High P, High H"] P3["STATE 3
Condenser Outlet
Saturated Liquid
High P, Low H"] P4["STATE 4
Evaporator Inlet
Wet Vapour
Low P, Low H"] end P1 -->|Compression 1 to 2| P2 P2 -->|Condensation 2 to 3| P3 P3 -->|Expansion 3 to 4| P4 P4 -->|Evaporation 4 to 1| P1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The P-H (Mollier) Diagram is the single most important chart in marine refrigeration.** KTU examiners routinely ask students to *mark the four state points* of the cycle and identify the refrigeration effect, compressor work, and condenser heat rejection on this diagram. --- ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Vapour Phase Transition ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "PHASE TRANSITION FUNCTIONAL MAP" IN["Heat Input to Liquid"] SL["Subcooled Liquid
T less than T_sat"] SF["Saturated Liquid
x = 0"] WV["Wet Vapour
0 less than x less than 1"] SV["Saturated Vapour
x = 1"] SH["Superheated Vapour
T greater than T_sat"] OUT["Heat Rejection Stage"] end IN --> SL SL --> SF SF --> WV WV --> SV SV --> SH SH --> OUT ``` --- ### 4.4 Marine Vapour Compression Refrigeration Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB subgraph "MARINE VAPOUR COMPRESSION REFRIGERATION PLANT" E["EVAPORATOR
Inside Ship Cold Room
Low Pressure
Refrigerant Evaporates"] C["COMPRESSOR
Driven by Motor
Raises Pressure and Temperature"] CD["CONDENSER
Cooled by Sea Water
Rejects Heat to Sea"] EXV["EXPANSION VALVE
Throttles Liquid
Drops Pressure"] SHIP["Ship Cold Space
Provision Room
or AC Cabin"] SEA["Sea Water Cooling
Source"] end SHIP --> E E --> C C --> CD CD --> SEA CD --> EXV EXV --> E ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### **PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the following terms:** (a) Dryness fraction (b) Saturated vapour (c) Latent heat of vaporisation #### Model Answer: **(a) Dryness Fraction (x):** It is defined as the ratio of the mass of dry saturated vapour to the total mass of the wet vapour mixture. $$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapour). **(b) Saturated Vapour:** A vapour that is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given pressure and temperature. Any further heat removal at this state will cause partial condensation. **(c) Latent Heat of Vaporisation (hfg):** The amount of heat required to convert 1 kg of saturated liquid into saturated vapour at constant temperature and pressure, without change in temperature. Units: **kJ/kg**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain the significance of the critical point of a refrigerant with reference to the vapour compression cycle.** #### Model Answer: The **critical point** is the state at which the saturated liquid and saturated vapour phases become *indistinguishable* — they merge into a single phase (supercritical fluid). **Significance for Vapour Compression Cycle:** 1. **Condensation Limit:** For condensation (heat rejection) to occur in the cycle, the refrigerant's critical temperature **must be higher** than the condenser temperature. Otherwise, the refrigerant cannot be liquefied by pressure alone. 2. **Cycle Operability:** Standard vapour compression cycles operate *below* the critical point of the refrigerant to ensure distinct phase changes occur in the evaporator and condenser. 3. **Example:** R-134a has Tc = 101.06°C, allowing it to condense at typical marine condenser temperatures (35–45°C). --- ### **PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)** --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** A refrigerant R-134a in a wet vapour state has a dryness fraction of 0.85 at a pressure of 0.18 MPa. Determine: (i) Specific enthalpy (ii) Specific entropy (iii) Specific internal energy **(b)** Derive the expression for the specific enthalpy of a wet vapour in terms of dryness fraction. --- #### Model Solution: **Given:** - Refrigerant: R-134a - Pressure: P = 0.18 MPa - Dryness fraction: x = 0.85 **From R-134a saturation tables at 0.18 MPa:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | hf | 36.84 kJ/kg | | hfg | 215.96 kJ/kg | | sf | 0.14928 kJ/kg·K | | sfg | 0.78704 kJ/kg·K | | uf | 36.78 kJ/kg | | ufg | 199.45 kJ/kg | --- **Part (a)(i): Specific Enthalpy** $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$h = 36.84 + (0.85)(215.96)$$ $$h = 36.84 + 183.566$$ $$\boxed{h = 220.41 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- **Part (a)(ii): Specific Entropy** $$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$$ $$s = 0.14928 + (0.85)(0.78704)$$ $$s = 0.14928 + 0.66898$$ $$\boxed{s = 0.81826 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}}$$ --- **Part (a)(iii): Specific Internal Energy** $$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$$ $$u = 36.78 + (0.85)(199.45)$$ $$u = 36.78 + 169.53$$ $$\boxed{u = 206.31 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- **Part (b): Derivation** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is: $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a neat P-H diagram, explain the working of a simple vapour compression refrigeration cycle. Mark the four state points and indicate the processes between them. **(b)** The enthalpy of R-134a at the inlet of an evaporator is 78 kJ/kg, and at the outlet is 245 kJ/kg. If the mass flow rate of refrigerant is 0.05 kg/s, calculate the **refrigeration effect** and **refrigerating capacity in tonnes of refrigeration (TR)**. --- #### Model Solution: **Part (a): P-H Diagram Explanation** Refer to **Section 4.2** (P-H Diagram) of these notes for the schematic representation. The four state points and processes are: | State | Location | Process In | Process Out | |-------|----------|------------|-------------| | **1** | Compressor Inlet | — | Compression (1→2) | | **2** | Compressor Outlet | Compression (1→2) | Condensation (2→3) | | **3** | Condenser Outlet | Condensation (2→3) | Expansion (3→4) | | **4** | Evaporator Inlet | Expansion (3→4) | Evaporation (4→1) | **Working Principle:** - At **State 1**, refrigerant is low-pressure saturated vapour. - **Compression (1→2):** Compressor raises pressure and temperature; refrigerant becomes superheated. - **Condensation (2→3):** Heat is rejected at constant pressure to sea water; refrigerant becomes saturated liquid. - **Expansion (3→4):** Throttling through expansion valve causes sudden pressure drop; partial flash evaporation occurs. - **Evaporation (4→1):** Refrigerant absorbs heat from the cold space at constant pressure, completing the cycle. --- **Part (b): Refrigeration Effect and Capacity** **Given:** - Inlet enthalpy to evaporator: h4 = 78 kJ/kg - Outlet enthalpy from evaporator: h1 = 245 kJ/kg - Mass flow rate: ṁ = 0.05 kg/s --- **Step 1: Refrigeration Effect (RE)** The refrigeration effect is the heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in the evaporator: $$RE = h_1 - h_4$$ $$RE = 245 - 78$$ $$\boxed{RE = 167 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- **Step 2: Refrigerating Capacity (Qe)** $$Q_e = \dot{m} \times RE$$ $$Q_e = 0.05 \times 167$$ $$Q_e = 8.35 \text{ kW}$$ --- **Step 3: Convert to Tonnes of Refrigeration (TR)** **Conversion factor:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW (or equivalently, 1 TR = 211 kJ/min) $$\text{Capacity in TR} = \frac{Q_e}{3.517}$$ $$\text{Capacity} = \frac{8.35}{3.517}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Capacity} = 2.374 \text{ TR}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often mix kJ/kg with kW or TR inconsistently. Always convert to a common unit before substituting. > 2. **Confusing State Points:** Many students misidentify h1 (compressor inlet) and h4 (evaporator inlet) on the P-H diagram — remember, **inlet to evaporator is state 4**, **outlet of evaporator is state 1**. > 3. **Forgetting Dryness Fraction Properties:** In wet vapour problems, always verify that x lies between 0 and 1. If not, your answer is physically impossible. > 4. **TR Conversion Error:** Use **1 TR = 3.517 kW**, NOT 3.5 or 4. KTU examiners penalize rounding errors here. > 5. **Missing Negative Sign Awareness:** Enthalpies of liquid refrigerants are *not* negative in modern SI conventions — they are positive small values (e.g., 30–80 kJ/kg). --- ### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Dryness fraction (x)** = mass of vapour / total mass; ranges from **0 to 1**. - ✅ **Saturated liquid** → x = 0, **Saturated vapour** → x = 1, **Wet vapour** → 0 < x < 1. - ✅ The **golden formula** for wet vapour properties is: $$y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$$ where *y* can be *h*, *u*, *s*, or *v*. - ✅ **Latent heat relation:** $h_{fg} = T_{sat} \cdot s_{fg}$ (from Second Law). - ✅ **T-s diagram:** Dome-shaped saturation curve; critical point at the apex. - ✅ **P-H (Mollier) diagram:** Most practical tool for cycle analysis in refrigeration. - ✅ **Critical temperature** must exceed condenser temperature for cycle operation. - ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** = heat absorbed to freeze **1 ton (1000 kg) of water at 0°C in 24 hours**. - ✅ **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** = h1 − h4 = enthalpy change in the evaporator. - ✅ **Subcooled liquid** lies *below* the saturated liquid line on T-s/P-h charts. - ✅ **Superheated vapour** lies *above* the saturated vapour line on T-s/P-h charts. - ✅ **Marine refrigerants commonly tested:** R-134a (modern), R-22 (legacy), Ammonia R-717 (industrial). - ✅ **Why latent heat is constant during phase change:** All supplied energy goes into breaking intermolecular bonds, not raising temperature. - ✅ **Real-world safety:** Wet vapour entering compressor causes **liquid slugging** — a major marine engineering failure mode.

topics.title: Compression Cycles (Carnot)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Compression Cycles (Carnot) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** (often simply called the **Carnot Refrigeration Cycle**) is an *idealized, reversible thermodynamic cycle* that establishes the *theoretical maximum performance limit* for any refrigeration or heat-pump system operating between two fixed thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_L$ (low-temperature / cold space) and $T_H$ (high-temperature / surroundings). It consists of two **isothermal** and two **isentropic** processes executed in the reverse direction of a Carnot heat engine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST743 – Module 1):** > The Carnot reversed cycle is the *benchmark* against which all practical vapor-compression and gas-refrigeration cycles on board ships are compared. No real cycle can exceed its COP. ### The Four Processes of the Reversed Carnot Cycle | Process | Nature | Energy/Entropy Interaction | |---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | Entropy constant; temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$ | | 2 → 3 | Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$ | Heat $Q_H$ rejected to high-temp reservoir | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | Entropy constant; temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$ | | 4 → 1 | Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$ | Heat $Q_L$ absorbed from low-temp reservoir | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Sponge in a Bucket" Analogy:** > Imagine a perfectly clean, super-absorbent sponge inside a cold bucket of water. You *squeeze* the sponge (work input) to push the water out into a hot drain — this is the **isothermal heat rejection** and **isentropic compression**. Then you release the sponge inside the cold bucket and it *soaks up* cold water — this is the **isothermal heat absorption**. The sponge has now "lifted" coldness from the bucket, but only because you *paid* it with mechanical effort. The **Carnot cycle is the perfect sponge**: it tells you the *absolute best* a sponge can possibly do. Real shipboard refrigerants are imperfect sponges — they lose some cooling capacity to friction, leaks, and irreversibility. On a ship, the cold reservoir $T_L$ is the **provision room or fish hold** (typically $-25^\circ\text{C}$ to $+5^\circ\text{C}$), and the hot reservoir $T_H$ is the **seawater used as a condenser coolant** (often around $25^\circ\text{C}$ to $32^\circ\text{C}$ in tropical waters). > [!IMPORTANT] > All temperatures in the Carnot COP equation **must be absolute** (Kelvin or Rankine). Inserting Celsius is a guaranteed mark-loss trap in KTU valuation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Absolute Zero:** $\mathbf{0 \, \text{K} = -273.15^\circ\text{C}}$ - **Conversion:** $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$ - **Universal reference efficiency ceiling:** COP of Carnot refrigerator is the *theoretical upper bound* — practical shipboard units typically achieve only **30%–45%** of this Carnot COP. - **Refrigeration effect:** $q_L$ is always measured in **kJ/kg** or **kW** (per-unit-mass vs. rate). - **Refrigeration Ton (RT):** $\mathbf{1 \, \text{RT} = 3.517 \, \text{kW}}$ (standard shipboard unit for cargo hold cooling capacity). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step The reversed Carnot cycle is best understood as the **time-reversed mirror image** of the standard Carnot heat engine. Let us walk through each process on both a *T-s* (Temperature-Entropy) and *P-v* (Pressure-Specific Volume) diagram. ### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression - The refrigerant vapor, already at the saturation state of the cold space, is compressed in a **frictionless, perfectly insulated** compressor. - Entropy remains constant: $s_1 = s_2$. - Pressure rises from $P_1$ (evaporator pressure) to $P_2$ (condenser pressure). - Temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$. - Work done **on** the gas: $w_{1-2} = -(h_2 - h_1)$ (negative because work is *input* to system). ### Process 2 → 3 : Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$ - The hot, high-pressure vapor enters a **frictionless, perfectly conducting** condenser. - Heat $q_H$ is rejected *isothermally* to the seawater-cooled condenser. - For an ideal gas undergoing isothermal change, internal energy is constant, so all heat removed is *boundary work output*: $q_H = w_{2-3} = R \, T_H \ln(P_2/P_3)$. - Note: $P_3 < P_2$ (gas expands as it loses heat and liquefies conceptually in the analogous vapor cycle). ### Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion - The high-pressure liquid-vapor mixture passes through a **frictionless, perfectly insulated** expander (ideal turbine or reciprocating expander — *not* a real throttling valve). - Entropy constant: $s_3 = s_4$. - Pressure drops from $P_3$ to $P_4$, temperature from $T_H$ to $T_L$. ### Process 4 → 1 : Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$ - The cold, low-pressure mixture enters the evaporator and absorbs heat $q_L$ from the refrigerated space (provision room). - Temperature remains at $T_L$: $q_L = w_{4-1} = R \, T_L \ln(P_1/P_4)$. > [!NOTE] > **"The Why":** Why does Carnot matter? Because the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that *no engine operating between two thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a Carnot engine*. Reversing it, *no refrigerator operating between the same two reservoirs can have a higher COP than the reversed Carnot*. It is the gold standard. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | COP of Carnot Refrigerator | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | $T_L, T_H$ in K | | 2 | COP of Carnot Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Always $>1$ | | 3 | Heat Rejected | $q_H = q_L + w_{net}$ | kJ/kg | Energy balance | | 4 | Net Work Input | $w_{net} = q_H - q_L$ | kJ/kg | Always positive for refrigerator | | 5 | Heat Absorbed (per kg) | $q_L = T_L (s_1 - s_4)$ | kJ/kg | $s_1 - s_4$ on T-s diagram | | 6 | Heat Rejected (per kg) | $q_H = T_H (s_2 - s_3)$ | kJ/kg | Since $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$ | | 7 | Net Work | $w_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(s_2 - s_3)$ | kJ/kg | Rectangular area on T-s | | 8 | Relationship | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | dimensionless | Always true | | 9 | Per-ton refrigeration | $1 \, \text{RT} = 3.517 \, \text{kW}$ | kW | Shipboard capacity unit | | 10 | SIHP / kW per ton | $1 \, \text{HP} = 0.7457 \, \text{kW}$ | kW | Brake power conversion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Reminder:** In the COP formula, $T_H - T_L$ is the **temperature lift**. The smaller the temperature lift, the higher the COP. This is why ship's engineers *love cold seawater* — it keeps $T_H$ low and the cycle efficient. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering - **Provision Plant Design:** Naval architects use Carnot COP to size compressors and select refrigerants (R-134a, R-404A, ammonia) for galley/cold-room plants. - **Cargo Cooling:** Reefer ships and LNG carriers use the Carnot limit to estimate *minimum compressor power* before selecting actual hardware. - **Performance Benchmarking:** Sea trials compute actual $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}/\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ — a metric called the **Relative COP** or **Second-Law Efficiency** — to certify plant health. - **Troubleshooting:** If a ship's refrigeration plant COP falls below ~30% of Carnot, it signals fouled condensers, non-condensable gases, or wet compression. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: COP of Carnot Refrigerator from First Principles We begin from the **First Law** for a cyclic device (steady-flow work, neglecting $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$): $$\oint \delta q = w_{net}$$ For our four processes: $$q_{1-2} + q_{2-3} + q_{3-4} + q_{4-1} = w_{net}$$ Apply the **reversibility** of each process: - Process 1→2 (isentropic): $q_{1-2} = 0$ - Process 2→3 (isothermal at $T_H$): $q_{2-3} = -q_H$ (heat *out* of system, hence negative by our sign convention) - Process 3→4 (isentropic): $q_{3-4} = 0$ - Process 4→1 (isothermal at $T_L$): $q_{4-1} = +q_L$ (heat *into* system) Substitute: $$0 - q_H + 0 + q_L = w_{net}$$ So: $$w_{net} = q_L - q_H$$ (Where $q_H$ is taken as a positive magnitude of heat rejected.) By definition, **Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator** is: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{q_L}{w_{net}}$$ Substitute the energy balance: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{q_L}{q_H - q_L}$$ Now apply the **Second Law (Clausius equality) for a reversible cycle**: $$\oint \frac{\delta q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$ So: $$\frac{q_{1-2}}{T} + \frac{q_{2-3}}{T} + \frac{q_{3-4}}{T} + \frac{q_{4-1}}{T} = 0$$ With $q_{1-2} = q_{3-4} = 0$: $$\frac{q_{2-3}}{T_H} + \frac{q_{4-1}}{T_L} = 0$$ Substitute the heat terms (taking $q_{2-3} = -q_H$ and $q_{4-1} = +q_L$): $$-\frac{q_H}{T_H} + \frac{q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{q_L}{T_L} = \frac{q_H}{T_H} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad q_H = q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ Substitute this into the COP expression: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{q_L}{q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - q_L} = \frac{q_L}{q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right)}$$ Cancel $q_L$: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ This is the **famous Carnot COP for refrigeration**. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Explained:** We used the First Law to relate work to the difference of the two heats, and the Second Law to relate those two heats to the absolute temperatures at which they are exchanged. Combining the two yields a temperature-only expression for the COP — a remarkably pure result. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: COP of Carnot Heat Pump For a heat pump, the *desired effect* is the heat delivered at the *high* temperature (e.g., heating a ship's accommodation block). Hence: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{q_H}{w_{net}}$$ Substitute $w_{net} = q_H - q_L$: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{q_H}{q_H - q_L}$$ Using $q_H/q_L = T_H/T_L$ from the Clausius equality above: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{q_L}{q_H}} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ Simplify: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ And note the elegant identity: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The heat pump COP is **always greater than 1** because the desired output $q_H$ includes both the heat lifted from the cold space *and* the work converted to heat — a free thermal bonus from the First Law. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work, Heat & Area on the T-s Diagram Each of the four heat quantities is exactly the area under its process curve on a T-s diagram. **Heat absorbed (4 → 1)** — a horizontal line at $T_L$ extending from $s_4$ to $s_1$: $$q_L = T_L \, (s_1 - s_4)$$ **Heat rejected (2 → 3)** — a horizontal line at $T_H$ extending from $s_2$ back to $s_3$: $$q_H = T_H \, (s_2 - s_3)$$ **Net work** — the **enclosed rectangular area** of the cycle (height $T_H - T_L$ times width $s_2 - s_3$): $$w_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(s_2 - s_3)$$ Verification via the energy balance $w_{net} = q_H - q_L$: $$q_H - q_L = T_H(s_2 - s_3) - T_L(s_1 - s_4)$$ Because of the isentropic constraints $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$, this becomes: $$q_H - q_L = T_H(s_2 - s_3) - T_L(s_2 - s_3) = (T_H - T_L)(s_2 - s_3) = w_{net} \;\;\checkmark$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effect of Temperature Lift on COP (Worked Numerical) **Problem (KTU-style):** A ship's provision refrigeration plant maintains a cold room at $-10^\circ\text{C}$. The condenser is cooled by tropical seawater at $30^\circ\text{C}$. Compute: (a) the Carnot COP, (b) the refrigeration effect per kW of compressor work, and (c) the compressor power required for a **5 TR** plant. **Step (a) — Convert to Kelvin:** $$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \, \text{K}$$ $$T_H = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \, \text{K}$$ **Step (b) — Compute COP:** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{303.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{40.00}$$ $$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = 6.579}$$ **Step (c) — Refrigeration effect per kW:** This is precisely the COP (kW of cooling per kW of work): $$q_L / w_{net} = 6.579 \, \text{kW cooling per kW work}$$ **Step (d) — Compressor power for 5 TR:** $$Q_L = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \, \text{kW}$$ $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{17.585}{6.579} = 2.673 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step (e) — Heat rejected to seawater:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 17.585 + 2.673 = 20.258 \, \text{kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight for Ship's Engineers:** If the same plant sailed into Arctic waters where seawater is at $5^\circ\text{C}$ ($T_H = 278.15\,\text{K}$), the new COP would be: > > $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{arctic}} = \frac{263.15}{278.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{15.00} = 17.54$$ > > That's a **166% efficiency boost** simply by sailing into colder water — a powerful illustration of how seawater temperature governs shipboard plant efficiency. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Quick Decision Reference | Situation | Effect on $T_H - T_L$ | Effect on COP | Practical Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Fouled condenser tubes | $T_H \uparrow$ | COP $\downarrow\downarrow$ | Higher power, risk of high-pressure cut-out | | Non-condensables (air) in refrigerant | $T_H \uparrow$ | COP $\downarrow$ | Purges needed regularly on ships | | Icing evaporator | $T_L \downarrow$ (locally) | COP $\downarrow$ | Defrost cycle mandatory for low-temp plants | | Warmer seawater in tropics | $T_H \uparrow$ | COP $\downarrow$ | Larger condensers used on reefer ships | | Improved insulation of cold space | $T_L$ can be higher | COP $\uparrow$ | Less compressor duty, fuel savings | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 T-s Diagram of the Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle The most important visualization in Module 1 is the **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram**, since the Carnot cycle traces a clean *rectangle* in T-s space. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["State 1: T_L, s1
Start of compression"] -->|Isentropic Compression
1 to 2| B["State 2: T_H, s1
Start of heat rejection"] B -->|Isothermal Heat Rejection
2 to 3| C["State 3: T_H, s2
Start of expansion"] C -->|Isentropic Expansion
3 to 4| D["State 4: T_L, s2
Start of heat absorption"] D -->|Isothermal Heat Absorption
4 to 1| A style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` ### Schematic Block Diagram of Energy Flows ```mermaid flowchart TD TL["COLD RESERVOIR
Provision Room / Fish Hold
Temperature T_L
Heat q_L flows OUT of reservoir"] EVAP["EVAPORATOR
Isothermal Heat Absorption
Process 4 to 1"] COMP["COMPRESSOR
Isentropic Compression
Process 1 to 2
Work W in"] COND["CONDENSER
Isothermal Heat Rejection
Process 2 to 3"] EXP["EXPANDER
Isentropic Expansion
Process 3 to 4"] TH["HOT RESERVOIR
Seawater / Ambient
Temperature T_H
Heat q_H flows IN to reservoir"] TL -->|q_L| EVAP EVAP --> COMP COMP -->|W_in from motor| COMP COMP --> COND COND --> TH COND --> EXP EXP --> EVAP style TL fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style TH fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style COMP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style EVAP fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016 style COND fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016 style EXP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 ``` ### P-v Diagram Analogue (For Ideal-Gas Interpretation) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["State 1
Low P, Low T_L
P1, v1"] P2["State 2
High P, High T_H
P2, v2"] P3["State 3
High P, High T_H
P3, v3"] P4["State 4
Low P, Low T_L
P4, v4"] P1 -->|Isentropic Compression| P2 P2 -->|Isothermal Expansion qH| P3 P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion| P4 P4 -->|Isothermal Compression qL| P1 style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style P2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style P3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style P4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In the T-s chart, the cycle moves **counter-clockwise** (opposite to a heat engine) because we are *consuming* work to *pump* heat from cold to hot. The rectangular area enclosed represents the net work input. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the Carnot refrigeration cycle and explain why its COP represents the theoretical maximum for any refrigeration cycle operating between the same two temperatures.** **Model Answer:** The Carnot refrigeration cycle is a *reversible* thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isothermal processes and two isentropic processes executed in the reverse direction of a Carnot heat engine. The working fluid: 1. Is **isentropically compressed** from the low-temperature state $T_L$ to the high-temperature state $T_H$ (process 1–2). 2. **Rejects heat $q_H$ isothermally** to the hot reservoir at $T_H$ (process 2–3). 3. **Expands isentropically** back to $T_L$ (process 3–4). 4. **Absorbs heat $q_L$ isothermally** from the cold reservoir at $T_L$ (process 4–1). The COP is: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ This is the **theoretical maximum** because the **Clausius inequality of the Second Law** states that for *any* cycle (reversible or irreversible): $$\oint \frac{\delta q}{T} \leq 0$$ Equality holds *only* for fully reversible cycles. Any irreversibility (friction, finite $\Delta T$ heat transfer, pressure drops, mixing) makes the inequality strict, which mathematically forces $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} < \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it related to the COP of a heat pump operating between the same temperature limits?** **Model Answer:** The **COP of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the *desired refrigeration effect* (heat extracted from the cold space) to the *net work input* required to achieve it: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ The **COP of a heat pump** is the ratio of the *desired heating effect* (heat delivered to the hot space) to the same net work input: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ Since by the First Law $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$, we have: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1 = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ For the Carnot cycle, this becomes: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} + 1 = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A heat pump's COP is *always one unit higher* than a refrigerator's COP for the same temperature limits, because the heat delivered includes both the "pumped" heat and the work converted to heat (which always ends up at the hot side). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **A ship's cold room is to be maintained at $-15^\circ\text{C}$ using seawater at $27^\circ\text{C}$ for condenser cooling. The plant operates on the reversed Carnot cycle and requires a refrigeration load of $30 \, \text{kW}$.** #### **Part (a) — Compute the COP and the compressor power input. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to absolute scale (Kelvin):** $$T_L = -15 + 273 = 258 \, \text{K}$$ $$T_H = 27 + 273 = 300 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Carnot COP formula:** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{300 - 258} = \frac{258}{42}$$ $$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = 6.143}$$ **Step 3 — Compute compressor work input:** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{30}{6.143}$$ $$\boxed{W_{in} = 4.884 \, \text{kW}}$$ **Step 4 — Heat rejected to seawater:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 30 + 4.884 = 34.884 \, \text{kW}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — If the seawater temperature rises to $32^\circ\text{C}$ (e.g., sailing into tropical waters), what is the new COP and the percentage increase in compressor power? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — New hot temperature:** $$T_H' = 32 + 273 = 305 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — New COP:** $$\text{COP}_R' = \frac{258}{305 - 258} = \frac{258}{47} = 5.489$$ **Step 3 — New compressor power:** $$W_{in}' = \frac{30}{5.489} = 5.466 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step 4 — Percentage increase in compressor power:** $$\%\text{ Increase} = \frac{W_{in}' - W_{in}}{W_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{5.466 - 4.884}{4.884} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{ Increase} = \frac{0.582}{4.884} \times 100 = 11.92\%$$ **Conclusion:** A mere $5^\circ\text{C}$ rise in seawater temperature (from $27^\circ\text{C}$ to $32^\circ\text{C}$) inflates the compressor power demand by **nearly 12%** to maintain the same cold-room temperature. This is why reefer ships in tropical trades install *oversized sea-water cooled* or *fresh-water cooled with chiller* condensers. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **A reversed Carnot refrigerator operates between a refrigerated space at $-20^\circ\text{C}$ and a condenser at $40^\circ\text{C}$. The refrigerant absorbs $1500 \, \text{kJ/h}$ of heat from the cold space.** #### **Part (a) — Determine the COP, the power input to the compressor in kW, and the heat rejected to the condenser per hour. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Absolute temperatures:** $$T_L = -20 + 273 = 253 \, \text{K}$$ $$T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — COP:** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{253}{313 - 253} = \frac{253}{60} = 4.217$$ **Step 3 — Power input (note unit conversion $1500 \, \text{kJ/h} = 0.4167 \, \text{kW}$):** $$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{1500}{3600} = 0.4167 \, \text{kW}$$ $$\dot{W}_{in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{0.4167}{4.217} = 0.0988 \, \text{kW} \approx 98.8 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 4 — Heat rejected per hour:** $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{in} = 0.4167 + 0.0988 = 0.5155 \, \text{kW}$$ Convert back to kJ/h: $$Q_H = 0.5155 \times 3600 = 1856 \, \text{kJ/h}$$ (Alternatively: $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = 1500 \times \dfrac{313}{253} = 1855.7 \, \text{kJ/h}$ ✓) --- #### **Part (b) — If the same refrigerator is used as a heat pump to warm a ship's accommodation at $25^\circ\text{C}$ while drawing heat from external ambient at $5^\circ\text{C}$, calculate the new COP, the heating capacity in kW for 1 kW of work input, and discuss which application is more efficient. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — New temperature limits (heat pump mode):** $$T_L' = 5 + 273 = 278 \, \text{K}, \quad T_H' = 25 + 273 = 298 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 2 — Heat pump COP:** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H'}{T_H' - T_L'} = \frac{298}{298 - 278} = \frac{298}{20} = 14.9$$ **Step 3 — Heating capacity per kW work:** $$Q_H = \text{COP}_{HP} \times W_{in} = 14.9 \times 1 = 14.9 \, \text{kW}$$ So **1 kW of electrical work** delivers **14.9 kW of heat** to the accommodation. **Step 4 — Comparative discussion:** | Mode | $T_L$ (K) | $T_H$ (K) | Lift | COP | |---|---|---|---|---| | Refrigerator (cold room) | 253 | 313 | 60 K | 4.22 | | Heat pump (accommodation) | 278 | 298 | 20 K | 14.90 | The **heat pump mode is 3.5× more efficient** (in COP) than the refrigeration mode, primarily because the temperature lift is only $20 \, \text{K}$ (versus $60 \, \text{K}$). This demonstrates a key Second-Law principle: *the smaller the temperature difference the cycle must span, the higher the COP*. On board, this is why air-conditioning plants (small lift, typically $10$–$15 \, \text{K}$) consume far less power per unit of heating/cooling than provision plants (large lift, $40$–$60 \, \text{K}$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Temperature in Celsius instead of Kelvin** → COP comes out wrong by an order of magnitude. Always convert first. > 2. **Forgetting to convert kJ/h to kW** (or vice versa) → silent unit error. $1 \, \text{kW} = 3600 \, \text{kJ/h}$. > 3. **Confusing refrigerator and heat pump COP** — adding instead of subtracting 1 (or vice versa). Memorize: $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$. > 4. **Assuming practical cycles achieve Carnot COP** — they *never* do. Real marine plants operate at $30\%–45\%$ of Carnot. > 5. **Using $W_{net} = Q_L - Q_H$** (wrong sign) — should be $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ (work in equals heat out minus heat in). > 6. **Mixing up processes 1–2 and 2–3** — the isentropic compression *precedes* the isothermal rejection. State this clearly in T-s sketches. > 7. **Skipping the energy balance check** — always verify $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$ at the end of a long problem. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Reversed Carnot cycle = 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic** processes, traversed **counter-clockwise** on T-s diagram. - **Sequence:** Isentropic compression → Isothermal heat rejection → Isentropic expansion → Isothermal heat absorption. - **COP of Carnot refrigerator:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — temperatures **must be in Kelvin**. - **COP of Carnot heat pump:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$. - **Identity:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ (always true, not just for Carnot). - **Energy balance:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$ — the heat rejected to seawater equals the heat absorbed from the cold space plus the work consumed. - **Net work = area of rectangle** on T-s diagram: $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(s_2 - s_3)$. - **Maximum theoretical limit** — no real refrigerator can exceed Carnot COP because of the Second Law (Clausius inequality). - **Marine relevance:** Provision rooms ($-25$ to $+5^\circ\text{C}$), air-conditioning (small lift, high COP), reefer cargo (large lift, low COP). - **Ship-specific tip:** Cold seawater = low $T_H$ = high COP. Tropical waters degrade plant performance significantly. - **Practical benchmark:** Shipboard plants typically achieve **30%–45%** of Carnot COP. - **Key conversion:** $1 \, \text{RT} = 3.517 \, \text{kW}$; $1 \, \text{HP} = 0.7457 \, \text{kW}$. - **Lower temperature lift = higher COP** — keep condensers clean, evaporators defrosted, and insulation intact to minimise lift. ---

topics.title: Superheating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Superheating # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Superheating** in a vapor-compression refrigeration system is the process of raising the temperature of the refrigerant vapor **above its saturation temperature** at a given pressure, after it has completely vaporized, by the addition of **sensible heat**. Mathematically, the degree of superheat is expressed as: $$\Delta T_{sh} = T_{actual} - T_{sat}$$ Where: - $\Delta T_{sh}$ = Degree of superheat (in **°C** or **K**) - $T_{actual}$ = Actual temperature of the superheated vapor (in **K**) - $T_{sat}$ = Saturation temperature corresponding to the operating pressure (in **K**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Superheating is classified into two distinct types: > 1. **Useful Superheating** — Occurs in the evaporator itself, increasing refrigerating effect. > 2. **Harmful / Degradation Superheating** — Occurs in the suction line due to heat gain from surroundings, increasing compressor work without improving cooling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle. The moment the last bubble of water turns to steam (saturated vapor at 100 °C), if you keep the kettle on the burner, the steam becomes **hotter than 100 °C** — it is now *superheated steam*. The water content is zero, but the temperature has risen purely from *sensible heat*. In a refrigeration system, the refrigerant exits the evaporator as a saturated (or slightly wet) vapor. As it travels through the **suction line** back to the compressor, it absorbs heat from the warmer engine room of the ship and becomes superheated. > [!NOTE] > **Marine Engineering Context (PENST743):** On a ship, the engine room ambient temperature is often **45 °C to 55 °C**, which is significantly higher than the suction line temperature (typically **−10 °C to +5 °C** for R-134a, or **−40 °C to −30 °C** for R-404A in low-temperature cargo refrigeration). This large temperature gradient makes suction-line superheating a major design consideration. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Marine Value | |-----------|----------------------| | Engine room ambient | **40 °C to 55 °C** | | Suction line temperature (cargo hold R-134a) | **−10 °C to +5 °C** | | Suction line temperature (R-404A deep freeze) | **−40 °C to −30 °C** | | Compressor discharge temperature | **70 °C to 120 °C** | | Specific heat of R-134a vapor ($c_p$) | **1.05 kJ/kg·K** | | Specific heat of R-404A vapor ($c_p$) | **0.88 kJ/kg·K** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic ### **Step 1: State at Evaporator Exit (Point 1)** The refrigerant leaves the evaporator as a **saturated vapor** (dryness fraction $x = 1$) or sometimes as a slightly **wet vapor** ($x < 1$) for safety. At this state: $$h_1 = h_g \text{ at } P_{evap}$$ $$s_1 = s_g \text{ at } P_{evap}$$ ### **Step 2: Sensible Heat Addition in the Suction Line** The refrigerant flows through the suction line, absorbing heat $Q_{sh}$ from the surrounding warmer engine room. The energy balance is: $$h_{1'} = h_1 + c_p \cdot (T_{1'} - T_{sat,evap})$$ Where: - $h_{1'}$ = Enthalpy of superheated vapor entering compressor (in **kJ/kg**) - $c_p$ = Specific heat of superheated refrigerant vapor (in **kJ/kg·K**) - $T_{1'}$ = Actual temperature of vapor at compressor inlet (in **°C**) - $T_{sat,evap}$ = Saturation temperature at evaporator pressure (in **°C**) ### **Step 3: Effect on Refrigerating Effect (RE)** Refrigerating effect is calculated as: $$RE = h_1 - h_4$$ Superheating **increases** $h_1$ to $h_{1'}$, so: $$RE_{new} = h_{1'} - h_4 = (h_1 + c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}) - h_4$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Useful superheating** (inside the evaporator) **increases** the refrigerating effect because the same mass flow absorbs more heat. However, in marine practice, most superheating is *harmful* (suction line) and **does not** increase the cooling delivered to the refrigerated space — it just adds heat that the compressor must reject. ### **Step 4: Effect on Compressor Work** Compressor work is given by: $$W_{comp} = h_2 - h_{1'}$$ As $h_{1'}$ increases due to superheating, $W_{comp}$ **increases**, because the compressor must raise the vapor from a higher initial enthalpy to the same discharge pressure. ### **Step 5: Effect on Coefficient of Performance (COP)** The COP of the refrigeration cycle is: $$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{h_{1'} - h_4}{h_2 - h_{1'}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight (KTU Board Exam Focus):** Suction-line superheating **decreases COP** because: > - The numerator (refrigerating effect) increases only by useful superheating > - The denominator (compressor work) **always increases** due to higher inlet enthalpy > - For purely *harmful* suction-line superheating, COP **decreases** because numerator is unchanged while denominator grows. ### **Step 6: Volume Flow Rate Consideration** Specific volume at the compressor inlet also increases with superheating: $$v_{1'} = v_1 \cdot \frac{T_{1'}}{T_{sat,evap}} \text{ (ideal gas approximation)}$$ This means the compressor must handle a **larger volume of vapor**, reducing the **volumetric efficiency** and the mass flow rate of refrigerant for a given compressor displacement. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{1'} - T_{sat,evap}$ | Degree of superheat | °C or K | | 2 | $h_{1'} = h_1 + c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$ | Enthalpy of superheated vapor | kJ/kg | | 3 | $s_{1'} = s_1 + c_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_{1'}}{T_{sat,evap}}\right)$ | Entropy of superheated vapor | kJ/kg·K | | 4 | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | Refrigerating effect (no superheat) | kJ/kg | | 5 | $RE_{sh} = h_{1'} - h_4$ | Refrigerating effect with superheat | kJ/kg | | 6 | $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_{1'}$ | Compressor work (with superheat) | kJ/kg | | 7 | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of performance | dimensionless | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_{sh} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$ | Rate of superheat addition | kW | | 9 | $v_{1'} \approx v_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_{1'}}{T_{sat,evap}}$ | Specific volume at compressor inlet | m³/kg | | 10 | $\eta_{vol} \propto \dfrac{1}{v_{1'}}$ | Volumetric efficiency relation | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always clearly state whether the superheating is **useful** (inside evaporator, increases RE) or **harmful** (suction line, does not increase RE) before computing COP. Examiners award partial credit for this distinction. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Marine Systems - **Suction-line insulation** on board ships is critical to minimize harmful superheating in the engine room. - **Compressor protection:** A small amount of superheating is **deliberately ensured** to prevent liquid refrigerant droplets from entering the compressor, which would cause **liquid slugging** and catastrophic mechanical damage. - **Cargo refrigeration plants** on reefer ships use **economizers** and **intercoolers** to manage superheating in multi-stage compression. - **Provisions plant** (for galley refrigeration) typically uses R-134a with controlled superheat of **4 °C to 6 °C** at the compressor inlet. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of Superheating on COP We will rigorously derive the change in COP when the refrigerant entering the compressor is superheated by $\Delta T_{sh}$ degrees above the saturation temperature at the evaporator pressure. ### **Given:** - Saturated vapor enthalpy at evaporator exit: $h_1$ (kJ/kg) - Saturated vapor entropy at evaporator exit: $s_1$ (kJ/kg·K) - Enthalpy after condensation: $h_4$ (kJ/kg) - Enthalpy at compressor discharge (state 2): $h_2$ (kJ/kg) - Specific heat of superheated vapor: $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) - Degree of superheat: $\Delta T_{sh}$ (K) ### **Step 1: Enthalpy at the compressor inlet (after superheating)** We start from the definition of sensible heat for a superheated vapor at constant pressure (the suction line pressure equals the evaporator pressure): $$h_{1'} = h_1 + c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$$ This follows from the thermodynamic relation $dh = c_p \cdot dT$ integrated between the saturation state and the superheated state at constant pressure. ### **Step 2: New Refrigerating Effect** With useful superheating (occurring inside the evaporator, where the heat is absorbed from the refrigerated space): $$RE_{new} = h_{1'} - h_4 = (h_1 + c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}) - h_4$$ The **increase in refrigerating effect** is: $$\Delta RE = c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$$ ### **Step 3: New Compressor Work** The compressor raises the refrigerant from $h_{1'}$ to $h_2$: $$W_{new} = h_2 - h_{1'} = h_2 - h_1 - c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$$ The **increase in compressor work** is: $$\Delta W = c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$$ ### **Step 4: New COP** $$COP_{new} = \frac{RE_{new}}{W_{new}} = \frac{h_{1'} - h_4}{h_2 - h_{1'}}$$ ### **Step 5: Comparative Analysis (Useful vs. Harmful)** **Case A — Useful superheating:** Both numerator and denominator increase by the same amount $c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$.
