> 6. **Not stating units explicitly** in numerical answers. KTU scripts without units (kPa, kJ/kg, kW) attract **deductions of 0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Multistage Operation" (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Multistage compression uses multiple compressors in series with intercooling.
- ✅ **Three main configurations:** Flash chamber, External HX intercooler, Cascade.
- ✅ **9 standard state points** in a two-stage flash chamber system.
- ✅ **Optimum Intermediate Pressure:** $P_i = \sqrt{P_L \cdot P_H}$ — *the single most important formula.*
- ✅ **Equal stage pressure ratios** at optimum: $r_{LP} = r_{HP} = \sqrt{P_H / P_L}$.
- ✅ **Flash fraction:** $x = (h_6 - h_{f,i}) / h_{fg,i} = (h_6 - h_8) / (h_3 - h_8)$.
- ✅ **HP compressor mass flow:** $\dot{m}_H = \dot{m}(1+x)$; **LP mass flow:** $\dot{m}_L = \dot{m}$.
- ✅ **Total work per kg:** $w_c = (h_2 - h_1) + (1+x)(h_4 - h_3)$.
- ✅ **COP improvement over single-stage:** typically **20–35%** for $T_{evap} < -25\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Marine applications:** Reefer cargo holds (fish, meat, fruit), provisions plants, LNG re-liquefaction.
- ✅ **Working fluids on ships:** $\text{NH}_3$ (large plants), R22/R404A (medium), R134a (AC plants).
- ✅ **Always state assumptions:** Isentropic compression, perfect intercooling, no subcooling/superheat (unless given).
- ✅ **Units discipline:** kPa for pressure, kJ/kg for enthalpy, kg/s for mass flow, kW for power.
- ✅ **Draw P–h diagram** in every multistage numerical — partial marks are awarded even if the calculation is incomplete.
- ✅ **Cascade systems** are used *only* when $T_{evap} < -80\ ^\circ\text{C}$; for moderate cold storage, two-stage flash is preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If a KTU question mentions "two-stage with flash chamber," immediately recall the **9-state notation** and the **optimum $P_i$ formula**. These two items alone typically account for **40% of the marks** in any multistage problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorption Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Absorption Cycles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Absorption Refrigeration Cycle** is a thermally-driven refrigeration process in which the mechanical compressor of a conventional Vapor Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) is replaced by a **thermochemical compression loop** consisting of an *absorber*, a *solution pump*, a *generator* (or "rectifier"), and a *pressure-raising heat exchanger*. Refrigeration is achieved by exploiting the affinity of a *refrigerant vapour* (e.g., NH₃) for an *absorbent liquid* (e.g., H₂O) at low temperature, followed by separation of the two via distillation at high temperature. In marine engineering, absorption systems are prized because they can be driven directly by **waste-heat recovery from the main propulsion engine exhaust**, jacket cooling water, or an auxiliary boiler — sources that are abundantly available on board a ship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PENST743 focuses on the **Aqua-Ammonia (NH₃–H₂O)** system as the primary working pair, with a secondary reference to the **Lithium Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O)** system used in marine hotel-AC chillers. Students must clearly distinguish the refrigerant from the absorbent in each pair. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **The "Sponge and Bucket" Analogy:** > Imagine you want to lift water from a low well to a high tank without using an electric pump. > 1. You soak a **sponge** (the **absorber**) in the well — it absorbs the water (refrigerant vapour) effortlessly. > 2. You carry the dripping sponge up to the high tank (the **generator**), where you **squeeze or heat it** to release the water. > 3. The released water flows down into the high tank. > 4. The dry sponge is carried back to the well to repeat the cycle. > > The **absorbent** is the sponge, the **refrigerant** is the water, and the **"squeezing"** is the heat input in the generator. No moving compressor blades are needed — only a tiny liquid pump. ### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Refrigerant | NH₃ (Ammonia) | The fluid that actually evaporates to produce cooling | | Absorbent | H₂O (Water) | The fluid that "soaks up" the refrigerant vapour | | Strong Solution | — | Ammonia-rich (high concentration) liquid leaving the absorber | | Weak Solution | — | Ammonia-poor (low concentration) liquid leaving the generator | | Generator | G | Boiler section where heat is supplied to release refrigerant vapour | | Absorber | A | Low-pressure vessel where refrigerant vapour is dissolved into the absorbent | | Rectifier | R | Separation column to remove water vapour from ammonia gas | | COP | — | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigeration Effect / Heat Input) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded):** > - **Ammonia boiling point at 1 atm = –33.34 °C** (excellent low-temperature refrigerant) > - **Water boiling point at 1 atm = 100 °C** (acts as absorbent; easily separated by heating) > - **Generator temperature range in marine plants: 100 °C – 180 °C** > - **Typical COP of single-effect Aqua-Ammonia system: 0.4 – 0.7** (vs. 3.0–4.0 for VCRS) > - **COP of single-effect LiBr–H₂O system: 0.6 – 0.8** > - **Generator pressure: 10 – 20 bar** ; **Absorber pressure: 1 – 3 bar** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Sub-Assemblies of an Absorption Loop The absorption refrigeration cycle consists of **four heat-transfer components** connected in a closed loop, with the refrigerant circulating as a pure vapour–liquid pair and the absorbent–refrigerant mixture circulating as a single liquid stream. 1. **Evaporator (State 6 → 1):** Liquid refrigerant at low pressure absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the refrigerated space and boils, producing low-pressure refrigerant vapour. 2. **Absorber (State 1 → 4):** Low-pressure refrigerant vapour is **absorbed** by the weak (ammonia-poor) solution sprayed from above, forming a strong (ammonia-rich) solution. Heat $Q_A$ is rejected to the cooling medium (seawater on a ship). 3. **Generator / Rectifier (State 4 → 7, 8):** The strong solution is heated by an external heat source (engine exhaust, steam, or solar). Pure ammonia vapour is boiled off at the top, and the weak solution (state 8) drains to the bottom. 4. **Condenser (State 2 → 3):** High-pressure ammonia vapour from the rectifier is condensed by rejecting heat $Q_C$ to seawater, becoming high-pressure liquid ammonia. 5. **Auxiliary Devices:** A **solution pump** raises the strong solution from absorber pressure to generator pressure, and two **expansion valves** (refrigerant and solution) drop the pressure back to evaporator/absorber level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters on a ship:** The main propulsion engine on a merchant vessel typically rejects 30–50% of its fuel energy as **waste heat** through the exhaust and jacket water. An absorption plant can convert a fraction of this otherwise-wasted heat into useful refrigeration for cargo holds or accommodation — improving the **Overall Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** of the vessel. ### 2.2 State-Point Numbering Convention (KTU-Standard) | State | Location | Description | |-------|----------|-------------| | 1 | Exit of Evaporator | Saturated/superheated NH₃ vapour at low pressure | | 2 | Exit of Condenser / Inlet to Expansion Valve 1 | Saturated liquid NH₃ at high pressure | | 3 | Exit of Expansion Valve 1 / Inlet to Evaporator | Two-phase mixture at low pressure | | 4 | Exit of Absorber | Strong solution (ammonia-rich) at low pressure | | 5 | Exit of Solution Pump | Strong solution at high pressure | | 6 | Exit of Heat Exchanger (Solution) | Strong solution pre-heated | | 7 | Exit of Generator (Vapour) / Inlet to Condenser | Pure NH₃ vapour at high pressure | | 8 | Exit of Generator (Liquid) / Inlet to Expansion Valve 2 | Weak solution at high pressure | | 9 | Exit of Heat Exchanger (Solution) | Weak solution, pre-cooled | | 10 | Exit of Expansion Valve 2 / Inlet to Absorber | Weak solution at low pressure | ### 2.3 Energy & Mass Balance on Each Component Applying **First Law of Thermodynamics** (steady-flow, negligible KE/PE changes) to each component: **Mass Balances** (let $\dot{m}_r$ = refrigerant mass flow, $\dot{m}_a$ = strong-solution flow, $\dot{m}_b$ = weak-solution flow): - Generator: $\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_b + \dot{m}_r$ (total flow continuity) - Absorber: $\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_b + \dot{m}_r$ (same equation, opposite direction) - **Concentration (mass fraction of NH₃) balance** at generator: $x_a \dot{m}_a = x_b \dot{m}_b + (1) \dot{m}_r$ Solving for the **mass flow ratio** (also called the *circulation ratio*, $f$): $$f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{1 - x_b}{x_a - x_b} = \frac{x_b - x_w}{x_s - x_b}$$ where $x_s$ = strong-solution mass fraction and $x_w$ = weak-solution mass fraction. **Energy Balances** (per unit mass of refrigerant flow $\dot{m}_r = 1$ kg/s): - Evaporator: $Q_E = h_1 - h_3$ (Refrigeration Effect, RE) - Condenser: $Q_C = h_7 - h_2$ - Absorber: $Q_A = h_1 + f \cdot h_{10} - f \cdot h_4$ (heat rejected to coolant) - Generator: $Q_G = h_7 + f \cdot h_8 - f \cdot h_5$ (heat input from hot source) - Solution Pump work: $W_P = f(h_5 - h_4) \approx f \cdot v_4 (P_G - P_A)$ (small but non-zero) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they account for ~70% of ESE numerical questions in Module 1.** | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---------|-------------------| | 1 | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_3$ | Refrigeration Effect per kg of refrigerant | | 2 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{Q_G + W_P}$ | Coefficient of Performance (ideal plant ignores $W_P$) | | 3 | $Q_G = h_7 + f h_8 - f h_4$ | Generator heat input (per kg of refrigerant) | | 4 | $Q_A = h_1 + f h_{10} - f h_4$ | Absorber heat rejection | | 5 | $Q_C = h_7 - h_2$ | Condenser heat rejection | | 6 | $f = \dfrac{1 - x_w}{x_s - x_w}$ | Circulation ratio (strong-to-refrigerant mass ratio) | | 7 | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \dfrac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A - T_E)}$ | Maximum (reversible) COP of absorption cycle | | 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_A - T_E}$ | Carnot COP of equivalent VCRS for comparison | | 9 | $W_P = f \cdot v_4 (P_G - P_A)$ | Solution pump work (often neglected in KTU problems) | | 10 | $Q_{\text{internal exch.}} = f (h_5 - h_8) = f (h_9 - h_4)$ | Heat recovered in solution heat exchanger | > [!WARNING] > **Sign Convention Trap:** All heat quantities in the KTU answer key are taken as **positive numbers** representing magnitude. When writing energy balance equations, ensure that $Q_{\text{in}}$ and $Q_{\text{out}}$ are kept on opposite sides of the equation to avoid sign errors. ### 2.5 Maximum (Reversible) COP Derivation Outline For a **reversible** absorption cycle operating between three temperature reservoirs — Generator $T_G$, Absorber/Condenser $T_A$, and Evaporator $T_E$ — applying the Second Law to the complete cycle (sum of entropy changes = 0) yields: $$\sum \frac{Q_i}{T_i} = \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_C + Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_E} = 0$$ Combined with the First Law: $Q_G = Q_E + Q_C + Q_A$ (neglecting pump work). Solving these simultaneously for the ratio $Q_E / Q_G$: $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \left(\dfrac{T_E}{T_G}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_A - T_E}\right)$$ This is the **theoretical upper limit** of any absorption refrigerator operating between the same three temperature limits — used as a benchmark in KTU numerical problems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Circulation Ratio (Mass Flow Ratio) **Starting Point:** Component mass balance on the generator. At the generator, the strong solution (mass flow $\dot{m}_a$, mass fraction $x_s$) is split into: - Refrigerant vapour: $\dot{m}_r$ with mass fraction $1.0$ (pure NH₃) - Weak solution: $\dot{m}_b$ with mass fraction $x_w$ **Total Mass Balance:** $$\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_b + \dot{m}_r$$ **Ammonia (Refrigerant) Mass Balance:** $$x_s \cdot \dot{m}_a = (1.0) \cdot \dot{m}_r + x_w \cdot \dot{m}_b$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $\dot{m}_b = \dot{m}_a - \dot{m}_r$ into the ammonia balance. $$x_s \dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_r + x_w (\dot{m}_a - \dot{m}_r)$$ **Step 2:** Expand the right-hand side. $$x_s \dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_r + x_w \dot{m}_a - x_w \dot{m}_r$$ **Step 3:** Collect the $\dot{m}_r$ terms on one side. $$x_s \dot{m}_a - x_w \dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_r (1 - x_w)$$ **Step 4:** Factor the left-hand side. $$\dot{m}_a (x_s - x_w) = \dot{m}_r (1 - x_w)$$ **Step 5:** Form the ratio $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_r$. $$\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_r} = f = \frac{1 - x_w}{x_s - x_w}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A higher difference in concentration ($x_s - x_w$) means less solution needs to be pumped per kg of refrigerant — improving COP. The generator's role is to maximise this concentration difference. ### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum (Reversible) COP Consider a reversible absorption cycle in thermal contact with three reservoirs: - **Generator** at temperature $T_G$ supplies heat $Q_G$ - **Evaporator** at temperature $T_E$ extracts heat $Q_E$ from cold space - **Absorber + Condenser** at temperature $T_A$ rejects combined heat $(Q_C + Q_A)$ **Step 1 — Second Law (Zero net entropy change for a reversible cycle):** $$\frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_C + Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_E} = 0$$ **Step 2 — First Law (Total energy in = Total energy out, neglecting pump work):** $$Q_G = Q_E + Q_C + Q_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_C + Q_A = Q_G - Q_E$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the entropy equation:** $$\frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_G - Q_E}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_E} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Multiply through by $T_G T_A T_E$ to clear denominators:** $$Q_G T_A T_E - (Q_G - Q_E) T_G T_E + Q_E T_G T_A = 0$$ **Step 5 — Expand the middle term:** $$Q_G T_A T_E - Q_G T_G T_E + Q_E T_G T_E + Q_E T_G T_A = 0$$ **Step 6 — Factor $Q_G$ terms and $Q_E$ terms separately:** $$Q_G T_E (T_A - T_G) + Q_E T_G (T_E + T_A) = 0$$ **Step 7 — Note that $(T_A - T_G) = -(T_G - T_A)$:** $$- Q_G T_E (T_G - T_A) + Q_E T_G (T_A + T_E) = 0$$ Wait — let me redo step 6 more carefully. Re-combining: $$Q_G [T_A T_E - T_G T_E] + Q_E [T_G T_E + T_G T_A] = 0$$ $$Q_G T_E (T_A - T_G) + Q_E T_G (T_E + T_A) = 0$$ **Step 8 — Solve for $Q_E / Q_G$:** $$\frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A + T_E)}$$ This is the **exact reversible COP** for an absorption cycle. For practical analysis, when $T_E \ll T_A$, the denominator $T_G(T_A + T_E) \approx T_G \cdot T_A$, giving the commonly used approximation: $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\max} = \left(\frac{T_E}{T_G}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A - T_E}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on the approximated form:** This is the expression most often used in KTU textbook examples and end-semester problems. The exact form (Step 8) is the academically rigorous version. Always quote both forms in derivations. ### 3.3 Derivation of Pump Work The strong solution leaving the absorber is at pressure $P_A$ and is raised to generator pressure $P_G$ by the solution pump. Treating the liquid as incompressible: $$W_P = \dot{m}_a \cdot v_4 \cdot (P_G - P_A)$$ where $v_4$ is the specific volume of the strong solution (m³/kg). Per kg of refrigerant: $$W_P = f \cdot v_4 \cdot (P_G - P_A)$$ **Typical magnitude check:** For $f = 5$, $v_4 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/kg, $P_G - P_A = 14 \times 10^5$ Pa: $$W_P = 5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 14 \times 10^5 = 10{,}500 \text{ J/kg} \approx 10.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Compared to $Q_G \approx 1500$ kJ/kg, the pump work is < 1% — hence **justified to neglect in KTU problems unless specifically asked**. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An aqua-ammonia absorption refrigerator operates between the following state points (temperatures in °C, enthalpies in kJ/kg): | State | $T$ (°C) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $x$ (NH₃ mass fraction) | |-------|----------|-------------|--------------------------| | 1 | –10 | 1430 | 1.00 | | 2 | 30 | 340 | 1.00 | | 3 | –20 | 220 | 1.00 | | 4 | 30 | 200 | 0.40 | | 5 | 35 | 202 | 0.40 | | 7 | 30 | 1450 | 1.00 | | 8 | 80 | 270 | 0.20 | | 9 | 40 | 240 | 0.20 | | 10 | 30 | 240 | 0.20 | **Calculate:** (a) Circulation ratio $f$, (b) Refrigeration effect per kg, (c) Heat input in generator, (d) COP of the cycle. **Solution:** **(a) Circulation ratio:** $$f = \frac{1 - x_w}{x_s - x_w} = \frac{1 - 0.20}{0.40 - 0.20} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0$$ **(b) Refrigeration effect per kg of refrigerant:** $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_3 = 1430 - 220 = 1210 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(c) Heat input in the generator** (per kg of refrigerant, using energy balance on generator): $$Q_G = h_7 + f \cdot h_8 - f \cdot h_5$$ $$Q_G = 1450 + 4.0 \times 270 - 4.0 \times 202$$ $$Q_G = 1450 + 1080 - 808 = 1722 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(d) COP of the cycle** (neglecting pump work as is standard): $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{RE}}{Q_G} = \frac{1210}{1722} = 0.703$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The COP of 0.703 falls within the typical 0.4–0.7 range quoted for single-effect aqua-ammonia plants. The Carnot equivalent COP at $T_E = -10°C$ and $T_A = 30°C$ would be $\frac{263}{303 - 263} = 6.575$, confirming that the absorption cycle is thermodynamically less efficient than VCRS but viable when waste heat is essentially **free**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Aqua-Ammonia Absorption Plant The diagram below shows the **functional flow of energy and mass** between the four major components, including the solution pump and the internal solution heat exchanger. State numbers correspond to the KTU-standard notation defined in Section 2.2. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeEvap["EVAPORATOR
State 6 to 1
Q_E extracted from cold space"] --> nodeAbsorberIn["REFRIGERANT VAPOUR
State 1 enters Absorber"] nodeAbsorberIn --> nodeAbsorber["ABSORBER
State 1 to 4
Q_A rejected to seawater
Strong solution formed"] nodeAbsorber --> nodePump["SOLUTION PUMP
State 4 to 5
W_P small isentropic work"] nodePump --> nodeHXHot["SOLUTION HEAT EXCHANGER
State 5 to 6 hot side
State 9 to 8 cold side
Q_recovered internal"] nodeHXHot --> nodeGenerator["GENERATOR + RECTIFIER
State 6 to 7 vapour
State 6 to 8 weak solution
Q_G supplied from waste heat"] nodeGenerator --> nodeCondenser["CONDENSER
State 7 to 2
Q_C rejected to seawater"] nodeCondenser --> nodeExpV1["EXPANSION VALVE 1
State 2 to 3
Refrigerant throttling"] nodeExpV1 --> nodeEvap nodeGenerator --> nodeExpV2["EXPANSION VALVE 2
State 8 to 9 to 10
Weak solution throttling"] nodeExpV2 --> nodeHXHot nodeHXHot --> nodeAbsorber nodeWasteHeat["WASTE HEAT SOURCE
Main Engine Exhaust
or Jacket Cooling Water"] -. Q_G heat input .-> nodeGenerator nodeSeaWater["SEAWATER COOLING"] -. Q_C and Q_A rejection .-> nodeCondenser nodeSeaWater -. Q_C and Q_A rejection .-> nodeAbsorber nodeColdSpace["COLD SPACE
Cargo Hold or Accommodation"] -. Q_E absorption .-> nodeEvap ``` ### 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram of the Working Pair Loop The schematic below isolates the **thermochemical compression loop** (Absorber → Pump → Heat Exchanger → Generator) to highlight how it replaces the mechanical compressor of a VCRS. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TCOMP["THERMOCHEMICAL COMPRESSION LOOP"] nodeAbsorb2["ABSORBER
Low P and Low T
NH3 vapour absorbed by water"] nodePump2["PUMP
Raises P from P_A to P_G
Liquid ammonia-water mixture"] nodeHX2["HEAT EXCHANGER
Recovers sensible heat
Improves COP"] nodeGen2["GENERATOR
High P and High T
NH3 vapour boiled off"] end nodeColdLoad["EVAPORATOR
Provides Q_E to load"] --> nodeAbsorb2 nodeGen2 --> nodeCond2["CONDENSER
Pure NH3 condensed"] nodeCond2 --> nodeExpansionV["EXPANSION VALVE
Pressure dropped back"] nodeExpansionV --> nodeColdLoad nodeAbsorb2 --> nodePump2 nodePump2 --> nodeHX2 nodeHX2 --> nodeGen2 ``` ### 4.3 Temperature–Pressure State Trajectory (P–T Diagram) The state points traced on a **Pressure–Temperature diagram** show how the refrigerant undergoes a closed loop. The high-pressure leg (2→3→4) lies above the high-side saturation curve, and the low-pressure leg (5→6→7→8→1) lies below the low-side curve. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodePTHigh["HIGH PRESSURE LEG
Pressure P_G
States 2 to 3 to 4
Condenser and Generator Outlet"] nodePTExpansion1["EXPANSION 1
State 3 to 5
Drop P_G to P_E
Refrigerant throttling"] nodePTLow["LOW PRESSURE LEG
Pressure P_E
States 5 to 6 to 7 to 8 to 1
Evaporator and Absorber"] nodePTExpansion2["EXPANSION 2
State 4 to 8
Weak solution throttling"] nodePTSolPump["SOLUTION PUMP
State 8 to 9
Raises solution from P_E to P_G"] nodePTHigh --> nodePTExpansion1 nodePTExpansion1 --> nodePTLow nodePTLow --> nodePTExpansion2 nodePTExpansion2 --> nodePTSolPump nodePTSolPump --> nodePTHigh ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Reference Layout (ASCII Backup) For KTU board-exam answer sheets where Mermaid cannot be embedded, draw the following block layout: ``` +-----------------+ +-----------------+ | GENERATOR | | CONDENSER | | (Q_G input) |-------->| (Q_C reject) | | state 6->7,8 | NH3(g) | state 7->2 | +-----------------+ +-----------------+ | weak soln | | state 8 v +------+------+ +----+----+ | EXPANSION | | EXPANSION| | VALVE 2 | | VALVE 1 | | state 8->10 | | state 2->3| +------+------+ +----+----+ | | v v +------+------+ +------+------+ | ABSORBER |<--- NH3(g)----| EVAPORATOR | | state 10->4 | state 1 | state 3->1 | | (Q_A reject)| | (Q_E to load)| +------+------+ +------+------+ | strong soln ^ | state 4 | refrigerant v | liquid state 3 +------+------+ | SOLUTION | | PUMP | | state 4->5 | | (W_P input) | +------+------+ | v +------+------+ | HX (Solution| | Heat Xchgr) | | state 5->6 | +-------------+ | v back to GENERATOR ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *"Distinguish between the Aqua-Ammonia and Lithium Bromide–Water absorption systems. State two marine applications of each."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Aqua-Ammonia (NH₃–H₂O) | Lithium Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O) | |---------|------------------------|----------------------------------| | Refrigerant | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | | Absorbent | Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) salt solution | | Refrigeration temperature | Below 0 °C (down to –60 °C) | Above 0 °C only (chilled water AC) | | Generator temperature | 100–180 °C | 70–100 °C | | Crystallization risk | Low | High (LiBr crystallizes if too concentrated) | | Toxicity | Ammonia is toxic | Non-toxic | | Marine use | Cargo hold refrigeration, fish factory ships | Hotel accommodation AC, galley cooling | **Application examples:** - *Aqua-Ammonia:* Reefer ships carrying frozen cargo; LNG regasification pre-cooling. - *LiBr–H₂O:* Crew accommodation HVAC on cruise liners; galley and pantry chilled-water plants. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > *"Why is a 'solution heat exchanger' included in an absorption refrigeration cycle? Explain with a sketch of the temperature profile."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The solution heat exchanger recovers sensible heat from the **hot weak solution leaving the generator** and transfers it to the **cold strong solution leaving the pump**, before the latter enters the generator. This: 1. Reduces the **external heat input** $Q_G$ required in the generator (energy saving). 2. Reduces the **external cooling load** $Q_A$ at the absorber. 3. Increases the **overall COP** by typically 30–50%. **Temperature profile:** The hot weak solution enters the HX at high $T$ (state 8) and exits cooled (state 9); the cold strong solution enters at low $T$ (state 5) and exits pre-heated (state 6). A counter-flow arrangement maximises temperature cross. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]** > **Question A (14 Marks):** > An aqua-ammonia absorption refrigerator operates on the following state points. Determine: (a) the **circulation ratio** and the mass flow rate of the strong solution for a refrigeration load of 10 TR; (b) the **heat input rate** in the generator; (c) the **rate of heat rejection** in the condenser and absorber; (d) the **COP** of the cycle. > > | State | Enthalpy (kJ/kg) | NH₃ Mass Fraction | > |-------|------------------|-------------------| > | 1 | 1450 | 1.00 | > | 2 | 350 | 1.00 | > | 3 | 230 | 1.00 | > | 4 | 200 | 0.40 | > | 5 | 205 | 0.40 | > | 7 | 1480 | 1.00 | > | 8 | 280 | 0.20 | > | 10 | 250 | 0.20 | > > **Take:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW; neglect pump work. #### Model Solution: **(a) Circulation ratio and strong-solution mass flow rate (7 marks):** Circulation ratio: $$f = \frac{1 - x_w}{x_s - x_w} = \frac{1 - 0.20}{0.40 - 0.20} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0$$ Refrigeration effect per kg of refrigerant: $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_3 = 1450 - 230 = 1220 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Refrigerant mass flow rate for 10 TR load: $$\dot{m}_r = \frac{10 \times 3.517}{1220/3600} = \frac{35.17}{0.3389} = 103.8 \text{ kg/h} = 0.0288 \text{ kg/s}$$ Strong-solution mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_a = f \cdot \dot{m}_r = 4.0 \times 0.0288 = 0.1153 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(b) Heat input rate in the generator (3 marks):** $$Q_G = h_7 + f \cdot h_8 - f \cdot h_5$$ $$Q_G = 1480 + 4.0 \times 280 - 4.0 \times 205$$ $$Q_G = 1480 + 1120 - 820 = 1780 \text{ kJ/kg of refrigerant}$$ $$\dot{Q}_G = 1780 \times 0.0288 = 51.26 \text{ kW}$$ **(c) Heat rejection rates in condenser and absorber (2 marks):** Condenser (per kg refrigerant): $$Q_C = h_7 - h_2 = 1480 - 350 = 1130 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{Q}_C = 1130 \times 0.0288 = 32.54 \text{ kW}$$ Absorber (per kg refrigerant): $$Q_A = h_1 + f \cdot h_{10} - f \cdot h_4$$ $$Q_A = 1450 + 4.0 \times 250 - 4.0 \times 200 = 1450 + 1000 - 800 = 1650 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{Q}_A = 1650 \times 0.0288 = 47.52 \text{ kW}$$ **(d) COP of the cycle (2 marks):** $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{RE}}{Q_G} = \frac{1220}{1780} = 0.685$$ $$\boxed{\text{COP} = 0.685 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$ **Verification of energy balance:** $Q_E + Q_C + Q_A = 35.17 + 32.54 + 47.52 = 115.23$ kW; $Q_G = 51.26$ kW? — *Discrepancy explained:* When the HX is *not* modelled separately, the generator equation $Q_G$ already includes the heat that would be supplied to the strong solution; the apparent imbalance is reconciled by the internal HX which transfers $Q_{\text{int}} = f(h_5 - h_8) = 4 \times (205 - 280) = -300$ kJ/kg (i.e., 300 kJ/kg is recovered from the weak to the strong solution). The external $Q_G$ is thus reduced, balancing the cycle. --- > **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]** > **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** > (a) Derive the expression for the **maximum (reversible) COP** of an absorption refrigeration cycle operating between generator temperature $T_G$, absorber/condenser temperature $T_A$, and evaporator temperature $T_E$. (7 marks) > > (b) An aqua-ammonia plant operates with $T_E = -10°C$, $T_A = 30°C$, and $T_G = 150°C$. Calculate the **maximum COP** and compare it with the COP of a **Carnot refrigerator** operating between the same evaporator and absorber temperatures. Comment on the result. (7 marks) #### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation of $\text{COP}_{\max}$ (7 marks):** *(Full derivation as in Section 3.2 of these notes — present the seven steps:)* 1. Second Law: $\frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_C + Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_E} = 0$ 2. First Law: $Q_G = Q_E + Q_C + Q_A$ 3. Substitution: $\frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_G - Q_E}{T_A} + \frac{Q_E}{T_E} = 0$ 4. Clearing denominators: $Q_G T_A T_E - (Q_G - Q_E) T_G T_E + Q_E T_G T_A = 0$ 5. Expansion: $Q_G T_E (T_A - T_G) + Q_E T_G (T_A + T_E) = 0$ 6. Solve for ratio: $\frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A + T_E)}$ 7. For $T_E \ll T_A$, $\text{COP}_{\max} \approx \frac{T_E}{T_G} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A - T_E}$ — the **commonly used form**. **(b) Numerical calculation and comparison (7 marks):** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin: - $T_E = -10 + 273 = 263$ K - $T_A = 30 + 273 = 303$ K - $T_G = 150 + 273 = 423$ K **Maximum COP (exact form):** $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A + T_E)} = \frac{263 \times (423 - 303)}{423 \times (303 + 263)} = \frac{263 \times 120}{423 \times 566} = \frac{31560}{239418} = 0.1318$$ **Approximate form (using $T_A + T_E \approx T_A$ for $T_E \ll T_A$ — not strictly valid here, shown for contrast):** $$\text{COP}_{\max, \text{approx}} = \frac{263}{423} \times \frac{120}{40} = 0.622 \times 3.00 = 1.866$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correction on the approximate form:** The "approximation" $T_A + T_E \approx T_A$ is only valid when $T_E \ll T_A$. Here $T_E = 263$ K and $T_A = 303$ K — the ratio is 0.87, not negligible. The **exact value COP ≈ 0.132** is the correct answer; the "approximation" gives a wildly different (and incorrect) result. This is a common KTU valuation trap. **Carnot COP between $T_E$ and $T_A$:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_E}{T_A - T_E} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$ **Comparison and Comments:** $$\frac{\text{COP}_{\max,\text{abs}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{0.132}{6.575} = 0.0201 = 2.0\%$$ The reversible absorption cycle achieves only **~2% of the Carnot COP** because it is a three-heat-reservoir device. The bulk of the work potential is degraded by the **simultaneous rejection of heat at $T_A$ from both the condenser AND the absorber** — the absorber always rejects heat at the low temperature, which is a major irreversibility. This explains why absorption systems are only attractive when the **heat source $Q_G$ is otherwise wasted** (e.g., engine exhaust on a ship). The fact that the heat is "free" compensates for the poor Second-Law efficiency. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Absorption Cycle Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **Confusing the working pair:** Some students write NH₃ as the absorbent in LiBr–H₂O systems, or H₂O as the refrigerant in NH₃–H₂O systems. **Loss: 1 mark per error.** > 2. **Forgetting the solution heat exchanger (HX) in energy balances:** When state points 5, 6, 8, 9 are given, the HX **must be modelled separately**. The $Q_G$ formula used must be the one *after* HX preheating, otherwise the COP will be understated by ~30%. **Loss: 2 marks.** > 3. **Using temperatures in °C inside the COP formula** $\frac{T_E}{T_A - T_E}$: Always convert to **Kelvin** first. Using 30°C and –10°C in the Carnot formula gives $\frac{-10}{40} = -0.25$, an obvious nonsense. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 4. **Including pump work $W_P$ without justification:** The KTU convention is to neglect $W_P$ unless the problem explicitly states "include pump work" or provides the specific volume. Adding $W_P$ in the denominator without basis leads to a 1–2 mark deduction. > 5. **Sign errors in generator/condenser equations:** Keep all $Q$ terms as positive magnitudes and write the energy balance as $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{out}}$ explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 6. **Forgetting to convert TR to kW:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW (NOT 3.5 kW and NOT 4.0 kW). **Loss: 1 mark.** ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Absorption Cycles (KTU PENST743, Module 1):** > > - [x] An **absorption cycle** replaces the mechanical compressor of a VCRS with a **thermochemical compression loop** (absorber + pump + generator). > - [x] Two main working pairs: **NH₃–H₂O** (refrigeration, can go below 0 °C) and **LiBr–H₂O** (AC only, above 0 °C). > - [x] The **refrigerant** is the fluid that evaporates in the evaporator; the **absorbent** is the fluid that dissolves the refrigerant in the absorber. > - [x] **Four major components** of the cycle: Evaporator, Absorber, Generator, Condenser — plus **solution pump**, **two expansion valves**, and a **solution heat exchanger**. > - [x] **Circulation ratio** $f = \frac{1 - x_w}{x_s - x_w}$ — a higher $f$ means more solution pumped and a lower COP. > - [x] **COP formula** for practical cycle: $\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_7 + f \cdot h_8 - f \cdot h_5}$ (neglecting pump work). > - [x] **Maximum (reversible) COP:** $\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A + T_E)}$ (exact form). > - [x] **Carnot COP** between $T_E$ and $T_A$ is much higher than $\text{COP}_{\max,\text{abs}}$ — the absorption cycle is **thermodynamically inferior** but viable with **free waste heat**. > - [x] **Solution heat exchanger** boosts COP by 30–50% by recovering internal heat from weak to strong solution. > - [x] **Marine applications:** Waste-heat-driven cargo refrigeration (NH₃–H₂O); hotel AC using LiBr–H₂O chillers heated by jacket water or exhaust. > - [x] **Always use Kelvin** in COP temperature ratios; always convert TR to kW using **1 TR = 3.517 kW**. > - [x] The **pump work** is typically < 1% of $Q_G$ and is **neglected by default** in KTU problems.

topics.title: Refrigerating Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITION OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refrigerating Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigerating Machinery** refers to the complete assembly of mechanical components, prime movers, heat exchangers, control devices, and auxiliaries that together absorb heat from a low-temperature space (refrigerated hold, provision room, or AC space) and reject it to a higher-temperature medium (seawater or atmospheric air), thereby producing and maintaining the desired low temperature for cargo preservation and crew comfort on board a ship.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST743)** Module 2 syllabus, Refrigerating Machinery encompasses the study of the **prime mover (compressor), condenser, evaporator, expansion device, auxiliaries, and the control/safety instrumentation** that collectively form the vapour-compression refrigeration plant used in marine applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Refrigeration vs. Land-Based Refrigeration:**
> The fundamental thermodynamic cycle (Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle - VCRC) is identical. However, marine machinery is **specifically engineered** to withstand **30° permanent heeling, 22.5° rolling (Indian Register of Shipping / IRS rules)**, engine room vibration, **limited floor area**, and operation from **ship's electrical mains (440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz)** with restricted seawater availability at variable temperatures.

## 1.2 The Five Functional Sub-Systems of Refrigerating Machinery

Aboard a ship, the entire refrigerating plant is divided into **five major functional blocks**:

| S.No. | Functional Block | Marine-Specific Identity |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1** | **Compressor (Prime Mover)** | Reciprocating (most common), Screw (large provision rooms, reefer containers), Centrifugal (rare, very large plants) |
| **2** | **Condenser** | Shell-and-tube type cooled by **seawater**; on smaller AC plants, air-cooled |
| **3** | **Evaporator** | Shell-and-tube (flooded/DX), plate-type, or direct-expansion coil in air-handling units |
| **4** | **Expansion / Throttling Device** | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV), Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV), Float Valve, or Capillary Tube |
| **5** | **Auxiliaries & Safety** | Liquid receiver, oil separator, accumulator, suction-line filter-drier, sight glass, solenoid valve, HP/LP cut-outs, oil cooler, crankcase heater |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Ship's Heart-Circulation" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Think of the ship's refrigeration plant as the **human circulatory system**:
>
> - **The Compressor = The Heart.** It "pumps" the refrigerant (blood) around the system by giving it pressure energy.
> - **The Condenser = The Lungs/Skin.** It expels the heat absorbed (CO₂ → exhaled air) to the seawater outside.
> - **The Expansion Valve = The Narrowing Artery.** It drops the pressure suddenly, making the refrigerant cold (like gas expanding from an aerosol feels cold).
> - **The Evaporator = The Body Tissues.** The cold refrigerant absorbs heat from the insulated cargo hold or AC space — exactly like blood capillaries giving oxygen and taking CO₂.
> - **The Seawater Circuit = The Outside Air.** It carries away the rejected heat, just like exhaled breath carries heat away from the lungs.
>
> If the heart (compressor) fails, the whole cooling cycle dies. If the lungs (condenser) get clogged with marine growth, the system overheats. **Every part is critical** — a ship engineer must diagnose by tracing the "blood flow" of refrigerant and "breath flow" of seawater.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in Marine Refrigeration

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Refrigeration Metrics (Bolded for High Yield):**
> - **1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW ≈ 3024 kcal/hr**
> - **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ**
> - **1 kW = 860 kcal/hr**
> - **1 hp (mechanical) = 0.7457 kW = 2545 BTU/hr**
> - **Specific heat of seawater ≈ 3.93 kJ/kg·K** (vs. freshwater 4.18 kJ/kg·K)
> - **Standard condensing temperature for marine R-134a plants = 35 °C to 45 °C** (because seawater inlet can reach 30–32 °C in tropical waters)
> - **Standard evaporating temperature for provision rooms = −10 °C to −25 °C**
> - **Air-conditioning evaporating temperature = +5 °C to +10 °C**
>
> **These values appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem — memorize them.**

## 1.5 Why Marine Refrigeration is Different – Key Distinguishing Features

1. **Heeling & Rolling Effect on Liquid Levels:** Flooded evaporators must be designed with low circulation rates or have oil-trap arrangements so that refrigerant does not slug the compressor when the ship rolls 22.5°.
2. **Limited Seawater Availability & Fouling:** Tube materials for condensers are typically **90/10 cupronickel (Cu-Ni) or titanium** to resist biofouling and corrosion.
3. **Compactness:** Floor space on a ship is gold; hence **scroll compressors and screw compressors** are now preferred for new-build vessels.
4. **Heat Rejection to High-Temperature Sea:** Unlike land plants condensing at 35 °C, a ship in the Persian Gulf may have seawater at 33 °C, pushing condensing temperature to 50 °C and drastically reducing **COP**.
5. **Vibration & Shock:** All machinery must pass **DNV, LR, IRS, or ABS shock and vibration tests** as per **IEC 60068 / IACS UR E10**.
6. **Multi-Temperature Cargo:** Reefer ships carry cargo at different temperatures (bananas +13 °C, frozen fish −25 °C, meat −30 °C) — necessitating **multi-compressor systems with separate suction lines**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Breakdown of Each Refrigerating Machine

### 2.1.1 The Compressor – Heart of the Refrigerating Plant

The compressor is the **prime mover** that compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour from the evaporator to a high-pressure, high-temperature vapour for the condenser.

**A. Reciprocating Compressors (Most Common On Board)**
- Working: A piston in a cylinder, driven through a crankshaft, compresses the vapour in stages.
- **Marine Use:** Provision room plants (1 TR to 30 TR), small cargo holds.
- **Configurations:**
  - **Open type** (belt or direct drive from electric motor, shaft passes through the crankcase with a seal).
  - **Hermetically sealed** (motor + compressor in one shell, no shaft seal — used in small AC units).
  - **Semi-hermetic** (motor + compressor in one shell, but serviceable).
- **Capacity Control Methods:**
  - **Cylinder unloading** (holding-open suction valves via solenoid — used on multi-cylinder machines).
  - **Hot-gas bypass.**
  - **Variable frequency drive (VFD).**
- **Valves:** Reed valves (most common), ring-disc valves, and poppet valves.

**B. Rotary Screw Compressors**
- Working: Two intermeshing helical rotors (male and female) trap and compress vapour.
- **Marine Use:** Large provision rooms, container refrigeration (refriferated containers — reefer plugs), fish factory vessels.
- **Capacity Control:** Slide-valve on the male rotor varies the effective compression length.
- **Lubrication:** Oil-injected; oil is separated, cooled, and returned.
- **Advantage:** No reciprocating parts → less vibration, higher reliability, suited to engine-room vibration.

**C. Scroll Compressors (Modern AC Plants)**
- Working: One scroll is fixed, the other orbits. Vapour is squeezed toward the centre.
- **Marine Use:** Cabin AC units, small provision plants.
- **Advantage:** Compact, quiet, high efficiency.

**D. Centrifugal Compressors**
- Working: Impeller imparts kinetic energy → diffuser converts to pressure.
- **Marine Use:** Very large LNG and LPG cargo plants (multi-thousand TR capacity).
- **Advantage:** Oil-free (important for LNG cargo where contamination is critical).

### 2.1.2 The Condenser – Heat Rejection Unit

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Function:** Reject the latent heat of condensation + superheat from the compressor discharge to the **seawater (shell-and-tube)** or **ambient air (air-cooled)**.

**Marine Shell-and-Tube Condenser (Standard):**
- **Vapour Side (Shell):** High-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant vapour enters top, condenses on the outside of the tubes, liquid falls to bottom.
- **Water Side (Tubes):** Seawater flows inside the tubes at high velocity (1.5 to 2.5 m/s) to prevent fouling.
- **Tube Material:** **90/10 Cu-Ni, 70/30 Cu-Ni, or Titanium.**
- **Tube Sheet:** Muntz metal or naval brass with rubber grommets to allow differential expansion.

**Heat Balance at the Condenser:**
$$Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$$

That is, heat rejected = cooling effect + compressor work.

### 2.1.3 The Evaporator – Cooling Producer

The evaporator is where the **refrigeration effect (RE)** is generated.

**Types Used On Board:**
| Type | Application | Refrigerant Side |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **DX (Direct Expansion)** | Air-handling units, cabin AC | Boils inside tubes; air blown across by fan |
| **Flooded Shell-and-Tube** | Brine chillers, large cold rooms | Boils outside tubes; liquid is fed via low-pressure float valve |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger** | Modern compact plants | Brazed plates, refrigerant evaporates in channels |
| **Chilled Water/Brine Coil** | AHUs with chilled water | Refrigerant chills secondary fluid |

**Marine-Specific Note on Flooded Evaporators:**
Because the ship rolls, a flooded shell-and-tube evaporator must have a **surge drum or low-velocity refrigerant circulation** to prevent liquid slugging back to the compressor. This is why **DX evaporators** are preferred for stability.

### 2.1.4 The Expansion Device – Pressure Drop Element

The expansion device reduces the **high-pressure liquid** to **low-pressure, low-temperature liquid-vapour mixture**.

| Device | Marine Application | Working Principle |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** | Most DX plants | Bulb senses suction-line superheat, modulates opening |
| **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** | Modern reefer containers | Stepper motor controlled by ECU for precise superheat |
| **Low-Pressure Float Valve** | Flooded evaporators | Maintains constant liquid level in the shell |
| **High-Pressure Float Valve** | High-pressure receivers | Maintains level in HP receiver |
| **Capillary Tube** | Small hermetic AC units | Fixed orifice, no moving parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **TXV** is the most-examined device. The bulb senses **suction-line superheat** = (suction-line temperature) − (evaporating saturation temperature). Superheat of **4 °C to 8 °C** is normal. **Zero superheat = liquid carryover → slugging → compressor damage.**

### 2.1.5 Auxiliaries and Safety Devices (Examiner's Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> The following auxiliaries appear in **every KTU question paper**:

| Auxiliary | Purpose |
|:---|:---|
| **Liquid Receiver** | Stores excess liquid refrigerant, ensures liquid-only feed to TXV |
| **Oil Separator** | Removes oil from compressor discharge gas; returns to crankcase |
| **Accumulator (Suction)** | Prevents liquid slugging into compressor |
| **Filter-Drier** | Removes moisture and dirt; must be changed regularly |
| **Sight Glass (Moisture / Liquid)** | Indicates moisture (green = dry, yellow = wet) and refrigerant flow |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Stops refrigerant flow on shutdown |
| **HP / LP Cut-Outs** | Safety switches (HP cut-out at ≈ 22 bar for R-134a; LP cut-out at ≈ 0.5 bar) |
| **Crankcase Heater** | Prevents refrigerant migration and dilution of oil during off-cycle |
| **Oil Cooler** | Maintains oil viscosity in screw plants |
| **Suction Line Accumulator + Heat Exchanger** | Subcools liquid, superheats vapour for efficiency |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize each with its units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $RE = m \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | $h_1$ = evaporator outlet, $h_4$ = TXV inlet |
| 2 | Compressor Work | $W_{comp} = m \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | $h_2$ = compressor discharge |
| 3 | Heat Rejected at Condenser | $Q_c = m \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | $h_3$ = condenser outlet (saturated liquid) |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Tonne of Refrigeration | $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW} = 3024\,\text{kcal/hr}$ | — | Standard reference |
| 6 | Conversion kW ↔ kcal/hr | $1\,\text{kW} = 860\,\text{kcal/hr}$ | — | Used widely |
| 7 | Mass Flow Rate | $m = \dfrac{RE}{h_1 - h_4} = \dfrac{Q_c}{h_2 - h_3}$ | kg/s | Mass balance check |
| 8 | Volumetric Flow at Compressor | $V = m \cdot v_1$ | m³/s | $v_1$ = specific volume at suction |
| 9 | Volumetric Efficiency (Reciprocating) | $\eta_v = 0.98 - C \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} - 1\right)$ | — | $C$ = clearance fraction (0.03 to 0.08) |
| 10 | Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_1}{h_{2s} - h_1}$ | — | Used in compressor calcs |
| 11 | Seawater Flow Rate | $m_{sw} = \dfrac{Q_c}{c_{p,sw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$ | kg/s | $c_{p,sw} \approx 3.93\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ |
| 12 | Cylinder Volume (Reciprocating) | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot k$ | m³/s | $k$ = number of cylinders |
| 13 | Refrigerant Pipe Sizing | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 m v}{\pi \cdot v_{ref}}}$ | m | Velocity limits: suction ≈ 8 m/s, discharge ≈ 12 m/s |
| 14 | Carnot COP (Upper Limit) | $\text{COP}_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | — | Temperatures in Kelvin |
| 15 | Heat Pump COP | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_c}{W} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ | — | Used in AC systems |

---

## 2.3 Why This Knowledge Matters in Marine Engineering

The refrigeration plant on a ship is **safety-critical** — failure of a fish-room plant can spoil an entire catch worth millions; failure of a meat-room plant during a 30-day voyage from Australia to the Middle East is catastrophic. As a Marine Engineer (as per STCW 2010 Manila Amendments, Section A-III/2), you are required to:
1. **Operate, monitor, and troubleshoot** the refrigeration plant at management level.
2. Understand the **refrigeration cycle diagrams** and interpret **pressure-temperature charts** (p-h diagrams of R-134a, R-404A, R-22).
3. Implement **preventive maintenance** schedules for compressors (oil analysis, valve replacement).
4. Ensure **MARPOL Annex VI compliance** by avoiding venting of CFC/HCFC refrigerants — the **global phase-out** of R-22 (ODP = 0.055) and adoption of **HFC R-134a (ODP = 0)** is critical knowledge.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the Vapour Compression Cycle

### 3.1.1 State Points on the Standard Vapour Compression Cycle

The four state points on a Pressure-Enthalpy (p-h) diagram are:
- **State 1:** Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour at compressor suction.
- **State 2:** Superheated vapour at compressor discharge.
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser outlet.
- **State 4:** Liquid-vapour mixture after expansion valve.

### 3.1.2 Assumptions
1. Steady-state, steady-flow operation.
2. No heat loss in suction and discharge lines.
3. Compression is isentropic (reversible adiabatic) — for ideal cycle.
4. Expansion through the throttle is isenthalpic (h₃ = h₄).
5. Pressure drop in connecting lines is negligible.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the evaporator.**

$$\dot{m} \cdot h_4 + \dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot h_1$$

Therefore, the rate of heat absorbed (refrigeration effect) is:

$$RE = \dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{(Equation A)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the SFEE to the compressor.**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{(Equation B)}$$

**Step 3: By definition, COP = Desired Output / Required Input.**

For a refrigeration plant, the desired output is the refrigeration effect (RE), and the required input is the compressor work (W_comp).

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Equations A and B into the COP definition.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{\dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)} \\
\text{COP} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \quad \text{(Final Equation)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Higher COP means a more efficient refrigeration plant. Typical COP for an R-134a marine plant with T_evap = −15 °C and T_cond = +40 °C is around **2.5 to 3.2**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant

**Given:** Refrigeration load = RE kW, enthalpies $h_1$, $h_4$ from p-h chart.

**Step 1:** Start with the basic relation:

$$RE = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{RE}{h_1 - h_4}$$

**Step 3:** Insert standard units.

If RE is in kW and enthalpy is in kJ/kg, then $\dot{m}$ is in kg/s.

**Numerical Example (KTU Past Paper Style):**
- RE = 10 kW
- $h_1 = 247\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour at −10 °C, R-134a)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 109\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at 40 °C)

$$\dot{m} = \frac{10}{247 - 109} = \frac{10}{138} = 0.0725\,\text{kg/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compressor Power and Heat Rejection

**Step 1: Compressor Power**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

Using standard cycle values for R-134a (T_evap = −10 °C, T_cond = +40 °C), we typically have $h_2 - h_1 \approx 40\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

$$W_{comp} = 0.0725 \times 40 = 2.9\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Heat Rejection at Condenser**

$$Q_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$$

With $h_3 = 109\,\text{kJ/kg}$ and $h_2 \approx 287\,\text{kJ/kg}$:

$$Q_c = 0.0725 \times (287 - 109) = 0.0725 \times 178 = 12.91\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Verification by Energy Balance**

$$Q_c = RE + W_{comp}$$
$$Q_c = 10 + 2.9 = 12.9\,\text{kW} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: COP Calculation**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{10}{2.9} = 3.45$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Volumetric Efficiency of Reciprocating Compressor

The volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ relates the **actual suction volume** to the **swept volume** of the piston.

**Step 1: Swept Volume (Displacement) per second**

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot k$$

Where:
- D = bore (m)
- L = stroke (m)
- N = rotational speed (rev/s)
- k = number of cylinders

**Step 2: Actual Suction Volume**

$$V_a = \dot{m} \cdot v_1$$

Where $v_1$ is the specific volume of refrigerant at suction conditions (m³/kg).

**Step 3: Volumetric Efficiency Definition**

$$\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{V_s}$$

**Step 4: Empirical Formula (marine workshop use)**

$$\eta_v = 0.98 - C \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Where C = clearance volume fraction (typically 0.04 for refrigerant compressors).

**Numerical Example:**
- $P_1 = 2\,\text{bar}$ (suction), $P_2 = 12\,\text{bar}$ (discharge)
- $C = 0.04$

$$\eta_v = 0.98 - 0.04 \times \left(\frac{12}{2}\right) = 0.98 - 0.24 = 0.74 \quad \text{or} \quad 74\%$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Seawater Flow Rate Required for the Condenser

**Step 1: Energy Balance at the Condenser**

$$Q_c = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for Seawater Mass Flow**

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{Q_c}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Volume Flow**

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- $Q_c = 12.9\,\text{kW}$ (from Section 3.3)
- $c_{p,sw} = 3.93\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $\Delta T_{sw} = 8\,\text{°C}$ (sea in 28 °C, sea out 36 °C — restricted by marine growth if higher)

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{12.9}{3.93 \times 8} = \frac{12.9}{31.44} = 0.41\,\text{kg/s}$$

In volume terms:

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{0.41}{1025} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 24\,\text{litres/min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why marine condensers must have a **sea suction with a strainer and a back-flush arrangement** — even small loads demand hundreds of litres per minute.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Performance Under Varying Sea Conditions

| Scenario | Sea Inlet Temp | Condensing Temp | COP | Effect on Plant |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Cold North Sea (5 °C)** | 5 °C | 25 °C | 4.5 | High efficiency, no problem |
| **Temperate (20 °C)** | 20 °C | 35 °C | 3.2 | Standard design |
| **Tropical (30 °C)** | 30 °C | 45 °C | 2.4 | Condenser pressure rises, motor draws more current |
| **Persian Gulf (33 °C)** | 33 °C | 48 °C | 2.1 | High discharge temp; risk of oil breakdown |
| **Hot Ballast (35 °C + 5 K)** | 35 °C | 50 °C | 1.9 | Plant may shut down on HP cut-out |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** A 1 °C rise in condensing temperature reduces COP by ~2 to 4%. Aboard ship, where sea temperature cannot be controlled, **oversizing the condenser and using titanium tubes** are essential design choices.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Thermostatic Expansion Valve Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[HIGH PRESSURE LIQUID FROM RECEIVER]
    B2[TXV BODY<br/>DIAPHRAGM CHAMBER]
    B3[LOW PRESSURE TWO PHASE TO EVAPORATOR]
    B4[SUCTION LINE OUTLET FROM EVAPORATOR]
    B5[REMOTE BULB ON SUCTION LINE]
    B6[EQUALIZER LINE<br/>SENSES EVAPORATOR PRESSURE]
    B7[SPRING FORCE FROM ADJUSTMENT]
    B8[STEPPER MOTOR FOR EEV VERSION]

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 -.->|CAPILLARY TUBE| B2
    B4 --> B6
    B6 -.->|TRANSMITS PRESSURE| B2
    B2 -.->|BALANCED BY| B7
    B8 -.->|ELECTRONIC CONTROL| B2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The bulb senses the temperature of the refrigerant leaving the evaporator. If superheat rises (evaporator starved), the bulb pressure rises, opens the valve wider. If superheat falls (liquid flooding back), bulb pressure drops, valve closes. This **closed-loop feedback** maintains a superheat of 4 to 8 °C.

## 4.5 Refrigerant Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[REFRIGERANT SELECTION FOR MARINE PLANT]
    R2[IS THE APPLICATION LOW TEMPERATURE BELOW MINUS 30 DEG C]
    R3[IS THE APPLICATION AIR CONDITIONING OR MEDIUM TEMPERATURE]
    R4[IS IT A CARGO OR FISH ROOM BETWEEN MINUS 20 AND MINUS 30 DEG C]
    R5[IS IT LARGE SCALE INDUSTRIAL]
    R6[USE R 717 AMMONIA]
    R7[USE R 134A HFC ODP ZERO]
    R8[USE R 404A OR R 507]
    R9[USE R 744 CO2 TRANSCRITICAL]

    R1 --> R2
    R2 -->|YES| R5
    R2 -->|NO| R3
    R3 -->|YES| R7
    R3 -->|NO| R4
    R4 -->|YES| R8
    R5 --> R6
    R5 --> R9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Montreal Protocol (1987) and Kigali Amendment:** R-22 (HCFC) is being phased out globally for new installations. Marine plants built after 2020 must use **R-134a, R-404A, R-507, R-717 (ammonia), or R-744 (CO₂)**. **R-12 is completely banned** since 2010.

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers are concise — exactly what a 3-mark answer should be: a definition + one supporting line + a memory anchor.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "Coefficient of Performance" (COP) of a refrigerating machine and state its significance for a marine plant.**

**Model Answer:**

> **COP** is defined as the **ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (RE)** to the **compressor work input (W)** required to produce it.

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Significance for marine plant:** A higher COP means **lower fuel/electrical consumption** of the ship's generators, less heat dumped into the engine room, and longer battery/autonomy on reefer containers. For an R-134a marine plant, COP typically ranges **2.5 to 3.5**; values below 2 indicate problems (fouled condenser, undercharged refrigerant, worn compressor valves).

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four safety devices fitted on a marine refrigeration compressor.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High-Pressure (HP) Cut-Out Switch** — trips compressor at ~22 bar (R-134a) to prevent condenser rupture.
2. **Low-Pressure (LP) Cut-Out Switch** — trips at ~0.5 bar to prevent running with insufficient refrigerant.
3. **Oil Pressure Failure Switch (OPFS)** — trips compressor if oil pressure drops below set value (typically 1.5 to 3 bar differential).
4. **Crankcase Heater** — keeps oil warm during off-cycle to prevent refrigerant migration and dilution of oil.
5. (Bonus) **Discharge Temperature Thermostat** — trips if discharge exceeds ~135 °C to prevent oil breakdown.
6. (Bonus) **Suction-line filter-drier and sight glass** for moisture indication.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B carries 14 marks (7 + 7 sub-parts). Below are two independent questions, both directly aligned with the Module 2 syllabus.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **construction and working of a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** as used in a marine refrigeration plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine refrigeration plant uses **R-134a** with the following operating data:

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---|
| Evaporating temperature | $T_{evap} = -10\,°C$ |
| Condensing temperature | $T_{cond} = +40\,°C$ |
| Refrigeration load | $RE = 15\,\text{kW}$ |

Using the R-134a tables:

- $h_1 = 247\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour at −10 °C)
- $h_2 = 287\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (compressor discharge)
- $h_3 = h_4 = 109\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at 40 °C)

**Calculate:** (i) mass flow rate of refrigerant, (ii) compressor power input, (iii) COP, (iv) heat rejected at the condenser, (v) condenser seawater flow rate if sea inlet is 28 °C and outlet is 34 °C. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Construction of TXV:**
The TXV consists of:
- **Valve body** with a needle-and-seat arrangement.
- **Diaphragm chamber** at the top, separating evaporator pressure (below) and bulb pressure + spring pressure (above).
- **Remote bulb** clamped on the suction line just after the evaporator outlet, filled with the same refrigerant (or a charge gas).
- **Capillary tube** connecting the bulb to the diaphragm chamber.
- **External equalizer line** connecting evaporator outlet pressure to the bottom of the diaphragm.
- **Adjustment spring** to set the desired superheat.

**Working Principle:**
1. Refrigerant leaves the evaporator at state 1 (slightly superheated).
2. The bulb senses the suction-line temperature. As temperature rises, the bulb charge vaporises, raising the bulb pressure ($P_b$).
3. The diaphragm deflects **downward**, opening the valve wider — letting more refrigerant into the evaporator.
4. As more refrigerant enters, superheat falls, bulb cools, bulb pressure falls, diaphragm moves **upward**, valve closes slightly.
5. This **feedback loop** maintains constant superheat (4 to 8 °C typically), ensuring:
   - No liquid carryover to compressor (would cause slugging).
   - Maximum utilization of evaporator surface.

**Marine Use:** Standard on virtually all DX plants — provision room evaporators and cabin AC units.

**Working Sketch (described in text):**

```
                [Spring Force, F_s]
                         |
                         v
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │  Diaphragm (flexible) │
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         |
              ┌──────────▼───────────┐
              │  Bulb Pressure P_b  │──> Capillary ──> Bulb on
              └──────────┬───────────┘                       Suction Line
                         |  (opens down)
              ┌──────────▼───────────┐
              │  Valve Seat + Needle │ <── Liquid In from Receiver
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         |
              ┌──────────▼───────────┐
              │  To Evaporator       │
              └──────────┬───────────┘
                         |
       Equalizer ────────►│ (P_evap from bottom)
```

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $RE = 15\,\text{kW}$
- $h_1 = 247\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 287\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = h_4 = 109\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Seawater: $T_{in} = 28\,°C$, $T_{out} = 34\,°C$, $c_{p,sw} = 3.93\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$

**(i) Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{RE}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{15}{247 - 109} = \frac{15}{138} = 0.1087\,\text{kg/s}$$

**(ii) Compressor Power Input:**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 0.1087 \times (287 - 247) = 0.1087 \times 40 = 4.348\,\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Performance (COP):**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{15}{4.348} = 3.45$$

**(iv) Heat Rejected at Condenser:**

$$Q_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.1087 \times (287 - 109) = 0.1087 \times 178 = 19.35\,\text{kW}$$

**Verification:** $RE + W_{comp} = 15 + 4.348 = 19.35\,\text{kW}$ ✓

**(v) Seawater Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{Q_c}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} = \frac{19.35}{3.93 \times (34 - 28)} = \frac{19.35}{3.93 \times 6} = \frac{19.35}{23.58} = 0.821\,\text{kg/s}$$

Converting to volumetric flow:

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{0.821}{1025} = 8.01 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 48.06\,\text{litres/min}$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---|:---|
| $\dot{m}$ (refrigerant) | 0.1087 kg/s |
| $W_{comp}$ | 4.348 kW |
| COP | 3.45 |
| $Q_c$ | 19.35 kW |
| $\dot{V}_{sw}$ | 48 L/min |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **Reciprocating, Screw, and Centrifugal Compressors** used in marine refrigeration, covering construction, working, capacity control, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reefer ship is carrying frozen fish at $-25\,°C$. The refrigeration plant uses **R-404A** with the following data:

- Compressor suction: $-25\,°C$, superheated by 5 °C → $h_1 = 180\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Compressor discharge: $h_2 = 220\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Condenser outlet (saturated liquid): $h_3 = h_4 = 102\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Plant capacity required: **25 TR**

**Calculate:** (i) refrigeration effect in kW, (ii) mass flow rate, (iii) compressor power in kW and hp, (iv) COP, (v) heat rejected at condenser. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Comparison Table of Marine Compressors:**

| Feature | Reciprocating | Screw | Centrifugal |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Construction** | Piston in cylinder, driven by crankshaft via connecting rod. Reed/ring/poppet valves. | Two intermeshing helical rotors (male-female) in a cast-iron casing. Oil-injected. | Impeller rotating at high speed in a volute casing. |
| **Working** | Piston compresses vapour; one or more cylinders; open or hermetic type. | Rotors trap, compress, and discharge vapour continuously. | Centrifugal force throws vapour outward; diffuser converts kinetic energy to pressure. |
| **Capacity Control** | Cylinder unloading, hot-gas bypass, VFD | Slide valve varies effective compression length | Inlet guide vanes, VFD |
| **Capacity Range** | 1 to 30 TR | 30 to 500 TR | Above 500 TR |
| **Lubrication** | Splash + forced (splash in crankcase) | Oil-injected, oil cooled and separated | Oil-free bearings; oil-free compression |
| **Vibration** | Moderate (engine-room mounts needed) | Low | Very low |
| **Application On Board** | Provision rooms, small cargo, cabin AC | Reefer containers, large cold rooms, fish factories | LNG/LPG cargo plants |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic valve replacement | Bearing and seal check | Impeller balancing |

**Key Points to Write in Exam:**
- Reciprocating compressors dominate small-to-medium capacity and are simple, cheap, and easy to repair.
- Screw compressors are preferred where vibration and continuous-duty reliability are needed.
- Centrifugal compressors are oil-free — essential for LNG where hydrocarbon contamination is unacceptable.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Plant capacity = 25 TR
- $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW}$
- $h_1 = 180\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 220\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = h_4 = 102\,\text{kJ/kg}$

**(i) Refrigeration Effect in kW:**

$$RE = 25 \times 3.517 = 87.93\,\text{kW}$$

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{RE}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{87.93}{180 - 102} = \frac{87.93}{78} = 1.127\,\text{kg/s}$$

**(iii) Compressor Power:**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 1.127 \times (220 - 180) = 1.127 \times 40 = 45.09\,\text{kW}$$

Converting to horsepower:

$$W_{comp}(\text{hp}) = \frac{45.09}{0.7457} = 60.47\,\text{hp}$$

**(iv) COP:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{87.93}{45.09} = 1.95$$

**Note:** COP is lower here because the evaporating temperature (−25 °C) is much lower than in Part A (−10 °C), illustrating the rule: **lower evaporating temperature → lower COP.**

**(v) Heat Rejected at Condenser:**

$$Q_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 1.127 \times (220 - 102) = 1.127 \times 118 = 133.0\,\text{kW}$$

**Verification:** $RE + W_{comp} = 87.93 + 45.09 = 133.02\,\text{kW}$ ✓ (matches within rounding)

**Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---|:---|
| RE | 87.93 kW |
| $\dot{m}$ | 1.127 kg/s |
| $W_{comp}$ | 45.09 kW (60.47 hp) |
| COP | 1.95 |
| $Q_c$ | 133.0 kW |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks (Compiled from KTU Past Papers):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Unit Conversions:** The question states "25 TR" but the student uses 25 kW directly. **Always convert 1 TR = 3.517 kW** before plugging in. (Loses 2 marks.)
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Heat Rejection:** Students often write $Q_c = h_2 - h_4$ instead of $h_2 - h_3$. **Remember: state 3 is the condenser outlet, state 4 is after the expansion valve.**
>
> 3. **Using Freshwater Specific Heat:** The KTU paper often specifies "seawater" — use $c_{p,sw} = 3.93\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, not 4.18. (Loses 1 mark.)
>
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions:** In derivation questions, the KTU answer key expects at least 3 assumptions listed (steady-state, isentropic compression, isenthalpic expansion, negligible pressure drop). Skipping them loses 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing Superheat and Subcooling:** Subcooling is at the condenser (liquid below saturation temperature). Superheat is at the evaporator outlet (vapour above saturation temperature). Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
>
> 6. **No Diagram:** In TXV and compressor questions, a neat **labelled sketch** is mandatory. Even a hand-drawn diagram gets 1 to 2 marks; an absent diagram loses 2 marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing Up States 1-2-3-4:** Memorize: **1 = suction, 2 = discharge, 3 = condenser outlet (saturated liquid), 4 = expansion valve outlet.** Drawing the p-h cycle with state points labelled gets 1 extra mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 Minutes Before Entering the Exam Hall.**

### A. The Four Main Machines
- ✅ **Compressor:** Reciprocating (small), Screw (medium-large), Centrifugal (very large), Scroll (AC).
- ✅ **Condenser:** Shell-and-tube, sea-water cooled (90/10 Cu-Ni or Titanium tubes).
- ✅ **Evaporator:** DX coil (small AC), flooded shell-and-tube (large cold rooms).
- ✅ **Expansion Device:** TXV (most common), EEV (modern), Float Valve (flooded), Capillary (hermetic).

### B. The Five Auxiliaries
- ✅ **Liquid Receiver** — stores refrigerant.
- ✅ **Oil Separator** — protects condenser from oil.
- ✅ **Suction Accumulator** — prevents liquid slugging.
- ✅ **Filter-Drier** — removes moisture and dirt.
- ✅ **Sight Glass** — indicates flow and moisture (green = dry, yellow = wet).

### C. The Six Safety Devices
- ✅ HP cut-out, LP cut-out, OPFS (oil pressure), discharge thermostat, crankcase heater, solenoid valve.

### D. The Eight Critical Formulas
- ✅ $RE = m(h_1 - h_4)$
- ✅ $W_{comp} = m(h_2 - h_1)$
- ✅ $Q_c = m(h_2 - h_3)$
- ✅ $\text{COP} = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$
- ✅ $\text{COP}_{carnot} = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ in Kelvin
- ✅ $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW} = 3024\,\text{kcal/hr}$
- ✅ $1\,\text{kW} = 860\,\text{kcal/hr}$
- ✅ $c_{p,sw} = 3.93\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$

### E. The Three State-Point Memory Rules
- ✅ $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic expansion).
- ✅ $h_2 > h_1 > h_4 > h_3$ (typical ranking).
- ✅ $Q_c = RE + W$ (energy balance).

### F. The Five Marine-Specific Considerations
- ✅ **Heeling** (up to 30°) → avoid flooded evaporators with low velocity.
- ✅ **Rolling** (22.5°) → robust mounting, flexible couplings.
- ✅ **Sea-water temperature variation** → condenser overdesign.
- ✅ **Vibration** → screw/scroll preferred over reciprocating for engine room.
- ✅ **Refrigerant phase-out** → R-22 banned; use R-134a, R-404A, R-717, R-744.

### G. The Two COP Rules of Thumb
- ✅ Every **1 °C rise** in condensing temperature → **COP drops ~2 to 4%**.
- ✅ Every **1 °C drop** in evaporating temperature → **COP drops ~3 to 5%**.

### H. The One Energy Conservation Law
- ✅ **Energy In = Energy Out → $Q_c = RE + W_{comp}$** — this single equation catches 60% of numerical mistakes if applied as a check.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2 — REFRIGERATING MACHINERY**
