topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Principal Components" in Shipboard Refrigeration & AC?

In a marine refrigeration or air-conditioning plant, the **principal components** are the *primary functional hardware elements* that physically execute the four fundamental processes of the vapour-compression refrigeration cycle (VCRC):

1. **Compression** → Compressor
2. **Condensation** → Condenser
3. **Expansion** → Throttling / Expansion Device
4. **Evaporation** → Evaporator

In addition, the system relies on a set of **auxiliary / secondary components** (receivers, driers, strainers, accumulators, sight glasses, oil separators, solenoid valves, etc.) that ensure safe, reliable, and continuous operation — particularly important at sea where ambient conditions, motion, and saltwater exposure impose severe demands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 – Principal Components):**
> *"The principal components of a shipboard refrigeration and air-conditioning system comprise the compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion device, and essential auxiliaries such as receivers, driers, strainers, accumulators, oil separators, and safety/control devices, each selected for compactness, vibration tolerance, and corrosion resistance demanded by marine service."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Bloodstream of a Refrigeration Plant"

Imagine the human **circulatory system**:

| Human Body | Refrigeration System |
|---|---|
| Heart | **Compressor** (pumps refrigerant, provides pressure) |
| Lungs | **Condenser** (rejects heat to outside) |
| Capillaries | **Expansion Valve** (drops pressure, cools the fluid) |
| Tissue cells | **Evaporator** (absorbs heat from cold space) |
| Blood (medium) | **Refrigerant** (carries heat) |

Just as blood must be clean and free of clots, refrigerant must pass through **driers, strainers, and accumulators**. The body's pressure-relief valves mirror the **safety cut-outs, HP/LP switches, and relief valves** of a marine plant.

In a ship, the condenser is usually **sea-water cooled** (instead of air-cooled on land) because ships sail through abundant cool seawater — a marine-specific twist on the analogy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for B.Tech Students:** The *same four* principal components exist in a domestic refrigerator, a building AC, and a ship's provision plant — only the **construction materials, sizes, control philosophy, and auxiliaries differ** due to the harsh marine environment.

---

## 1.3 Why Marine Plants Are Different — Design Drivers

| Driver | Effect on Component Selection |
|---|---|
| Ship motion (rolling, pitching) | Compressors mounted on **resilient pads**; welded piping; anti-slug suction accumulators |
| Salt-laden air & seawater | Use of **cupro-nickel (Cu-Ni 70/30) tubes** in condensers; epoxy-coated frames |
| Space constraint | Compact **semi-hermetic / hermetic compressors**; plate-type heat exchangers |
| Vibration | **Spring-flex couplings**, anti-vibration mounts |
| Classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) | Redundant safety devices, dual pressure relief, oil-pressure cut-outs |
| Limited power | Energy-efficient **screw compressors** for large plants |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Marine Value |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated suction temperature | T_evap | **−40 °C to +5 °C** (AC plants ~5–10 °C; cold rooms ~−25 °C; fish rooms ~−35 °C) |
| Condensing temperature | T_cond | **+35 °C to +45 °C** (tropical sea-water cooled) |
| Approach temperature | ΔT_app | **3–5 °C** |
| Sea-water inlet temperature | T_sw,in | **≤ 32 °C** (tropical) |
| Sea-water outlet temperature | T_sw,out | **≤ 36 °C** |
| Refrigerant glide (zeotropes) | ΔT_glide | **5–8 °C** (R-407C) |
| 1 ton of refrigeration (TR) | 1 TR | **3.517 kW** ≈ **12,000 BTU/h** |
| Latent heat of fusion of water | L_f | **334 kJ/kg** |
| Specific heat of water | c_p,w | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** |

---

## 1.5 At a Glance — The Five Principal Functional Blocks

```
1.  COMPRESSOR        →  Raises refrigerant pressure & temperature
2.  CONDENSER         →  Rejects heat to seawater / air
3.  EXPANSION DEVICE  →  Drops pressure; creates cold low-pressure liquid
4.  EVAPORATOR        →  Absorbs heat from cooled space / load
5.  AUXILIARIES       →  Clean, dry, safe, controlled refrigerant flow
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** **C-C-E-E-A** = **C**ompressor → **C**ondenser → **E**xpansion → **E**vaporator → **A**uxiliaries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Component 1 — The Compressor

### 2.1.1 Function & Role
The compressor is the **"heart"** of the VCRC. It draws low-pressure vapour from the evaporator and compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour so that condensation can occur at an ambient higher than the cooled space temperature.

### 2.1.2 Types Used in Marine Service

| Type | Capacity Range | Typical Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hermetic / Semi-hermetic reciprocating** | 0.5 – 50 kW | Cabin AC, small provision rooms |
| **Open reciprocating** | 5 – 200 kW | Older cargo refrigeration |
| **Scroll (hermetic)** | 3 – 60 kW | Modern cabin AC (low noise) |
| **Screw (single & twin)** | 50 kW – 5 MW | Large provision plants, reefer containers |
| **Centrifugal** | > 500 kW | LNG, large cruise-ship chillers |
| **Rotary (vane, screw-type)** | 1 – 30 kW | Small chilled-water plants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Favourite:** **Hermetic & semi-hermetic compressors** dominate because their sealed motor eliminates the shaft seal — the *single most common leak point* at sea.

### 2.1.3 Theoretical Work of Compression

For an **isentropic** compression in an open system (steady-flow, adiabatic, reversible):

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]
$$

where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy of refrigerant at compressor inlet (saturated/slightly superheated vapour)
- $h_2$ = enthalpy at compressor outlet (superheated vapour, $s_2 = s_1$ for isentropic)

Actual compressor work (accounting for isentropic efficiency $\eta_{is}$):

$$
W_c = \frac{\dot{m} \, (h_2 - h_1)}{\eta_{is}}
$$

### 2.1.4 Volumetric Efficiency

$$
\eta_v = \frac{\dot{m} \, v_1}{V_s}
$$

Where:
- $v_1$ = specific volume at suction (m³/kg)
- $V_s$ = swept volume per second = $\dfrac{\pi D^2 L \, N \, n}{4 \, 60}$ (m³/s)

**Standard marine value:** $\eta_v \approx 0.70 - 0.85$

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — The Condenser

### 2.2.1 Function & Role
Rejects the **latent heat of condensation plus any superheat** from the high-pressure refrigerant to an external medium (seawater, freshwater, or air).

### 2.2.2 Types in Marine Service

| Type | Coolant | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Shell-and-tube (Cu-Ni tubes)** | Seawater (tube-side) | Most common |
| **Shell-and-coil** | Seawater | Small plants |
| **Plate heat exchanger (PHE)** | Freshwater (intermediate loop) | Modern modular plants |
| **Air-cooled finned** | Forced ambient air | Auxiliary AC, galley refrigeration |
| **Evaporative (rare)** | Air + water spray | Large cold stores (uncommon at sea) |

### 2.2.3 Heat Rejection Equation

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r \, (h_2 - h_3) = \dot{U} \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}
$$

### 2.2.4 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

For a condenser (counter-flow refrigerant-to-seawater):
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{r,hot} - T_{sw,out}$  *(refrigerant inlet vs seawater outlet)*
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{r,cold} - T_{sw,in}$ *(refrigerant outlet vs seawater inlet)*

### 2.2.5 Seawater Side Heat Balance

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})
$$

Typical $c_{p,sw} = \textbf{3.99 kJ/kg·K}$ (at 4 % salinity)

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — The Evaporator

### 2.3.1 Function & Role
The evaporator absorbs heat $\dot{Q}_{L}$ from the cold space (provision room, cabin air, chilled water, etc.) by boiling the low-pressure liquid refrigerant.

### 2.3.2 Types

| Type | Air/Medium Cooled | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct expansion (DX) finned coil** | Forced air | Cabin AC, cold rooms |
| **Flooded (shell-and-tube)** | Brine / water | Large reefer plants, chilled water |
| **Plate evaporator** | Liquid (glycol, brine) | Modular chillers |
| **Bare-tube coil in air** | Natural convection | Small galley fridges |

### 2.3.3 Refrigeration Effect

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_r \, (h_1 - h_4)
$$

Where $h_4$ is enthalpy after throttling (isenthalpic), so $h_4 = h_3$ from condenser.

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — The Expansion Device

### 2.4.1 Function & Role
Drops the high-pressure liquid refrigerant from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure **isenthalpically**, producing a cold, low-quality two-phase mixture.

### 2.4.2 Types

| Type | Control | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV / TXV)** | Modulating | Most common |
| **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)** | Constant pressure | Older plants |
| **Float Valve** (high/low side) | Liquid level | Flooded evaporators |
| **Capillary tube** | Fixed orifice | Small hermetic units |
| **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** | Microprocessor | Modern plants, variable load |

### 2.4.2.1 TXV Working Principle
The TXV maintains a **constant superheat** at the evaporator outlet by balancing three forces:

$$
F_{bulb} + F_{spring} = F_{evap} + F_{atm}
$$

- $F_{bulb}$: bulb pressure from refrigerant saturation in sensing bulb
- $F_{spring}$: adjustable spring setting (superheat setpoint)
- $F_{evap}$: evaporator saturation pressure
- $F_{atm}$: atmospheric / equaliser pressure

If load increases → suction superheat rises → bulb pressure rises → valve opens more → more refrigerant flows.

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — Auxiliaries

| Auxiliary | Function | Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Receiver** | Stores excess liquid refrigerant, prevents flood-back | After condenser |
| **Filter Drier** | Removes moisture + acids + debris | Liquid line, before TXV |
| **Strainer / Filter** | Mechanical debris removal | Suction & liquid lines |
| **Sight Glass / Moisture Indicator** | Visual refrigerant quality check | Liquid line |
| **Suction Accumulator** | Prevents liquid slugging at compressor | Between evaporator & compressor |
| **Oil Separator** | Returns oil to compressor crankcase | Compressor discharge |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Stops liquid flow on shutdown | Liquid line |
| **LP / HP Cut-out Switch** | Safety trip on low/high pressure | Suction/Discharge |
| **Oil Pressure Switch** | Protects against lubrication failure | Crankcase |
| **Pressure Relief Valve** | Over-pressure protection | Receiver, condenser |
| **King Valve / Service Valve** | Isolation for maintenance | Suction & discharge |
| **Heat Exchanger (suction–liquid)** | Improves cycle COP | Liquid & suction lines |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE / internal tests.** Units in **SI** unless noted.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | Compressor work per kg | Compressor analysis |
| 2 | $\eta_{is} = (h_2 - h_1)/(h_{2s} - h_1)$ | Isentropic efficiency | Compressor perf. |
| 3 | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | Refrigeration effect | Evaporator |
| 4 | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | Heat rejected | Condenser |
| 5 | $\text{COP} = q_L / w_c$ | Coefficient of performance | Cycle |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_r q_L$ | Cooling capacity | Sizing |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_r q_H$ | Heat rejection | Sizing |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{sw} = \dot{m}_{sw} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Seawater heat pickup | Condenser |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | LMTD | HX sizing |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Overall heat transfer | All HX |
| 11 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/h}$ | Capacity unit | Unit conversion |
| 12 | $V_s = (\pi D^2 L N n)/(4 \times 60)$ | Swept volume | Reciprocating comp. |
| 13 | $\eta_v = \dot{m} v_1 / V_s$ | Volumetric efficiency | Reciprocating comp. |
| 14 | $h_4 = h_3$ | Throttling isenthalpic | TXV |
| 15 | $\dot{V}_{sw} = \dot{Q}_{cond}/[\rho_{sw} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})]$ | Seawater flow | Condenser design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** $c_p$ of **seawater (3.99 kJ/kg·K)** with $c_p$ of **freshwater (4.186 kJ/kg·K)** — a 5 % error in exam numerical answers!

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Power Generation:** HVAC on offshore platforms uses the *same* principal components, scaled up.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Reefer ships carrying fish/meat use flooded ammonia evaporators (similar principal components, different refrigerant).
- **Cruise Industry:** Centrifugal chillers + plate heat exchangers cool thousands of cabins.
- **Naval Defence:** Classified shock-tested hermetic compressors for warships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section derives the **core mathematical relations** linking the principal components to cycle performance. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly, as required for full marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigeration Effect $q_L$ and Compressor Work $w_c$ from the T–s / P–h Diagram

### Given
A vapour-compression refrigeration cycle operates between:
- Evaporator pressure $P_{evap}$, temperature $T_{evap}$
- Condenser pressure $P_{cond}$, temperature $T_{cond}$

The four state points are:

| State | Condition |
|---|---|
| 1 | Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour at compressor inlet |
| 2 | Superheated vapour at compressor outlet (after isentropic compression $s_2 = s_1$) |
| 3 | Saturated liquid at condenser outlet |
| 4 | Two-phase mixture after throttling ($h_4 = h_3$) |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Throttling process (3 → 4):**

The expansion valve is modelled as a **steady-flow adiabatic device** with no work and negligible KE/PE change. The steady-flow energy equation reduces to:

$$
h_3 + \cancel{\frac{V_3^2}{2}} + \cancel{g z_3} + \cancel{q} = h_4 + \cancel{\frac{V_4^2}{2}} + \cancel{g z_4} + \cancel{w}
$$

$$
\therefore h_4 = h_3
$$

**Step 2 — Refrigeration effect in the evaporator (4 → 1):**

Applying the steady-flow energy equation to the evaporator (no work, $q$ enters the refrigerant):

$$
q_L = h_1 - h_4
$$

But $h_4 = h_3$, therefore:

$$
\boxed{q_L = h_1 - h_3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compressor work (1 → 2):**

For an adiabatic compressor with negligible KE/PE change:

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

For an **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) process:

$$
s_2 = s_1
$$

So $h_{2s}$ is read from the $P\text{-}h$ chart at $P_{cond}$ and entropy equal to $s_1$.

The **isentropic efficiency** is defined as the ratio of *ideal* to *actual* work:

$$
\eta_{is} = \frac{w_{c,\,ideal}}{w_{c,\,actual}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2,\,actual} - h_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat rejected in the condenser (2 → 3):**

Energy balance on the condenser:

$$
q_H = h_2 - h_3
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Performance (COP):**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}}
$$

Also, by First Law on the cycle:

$$
q_H = q_L + w_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_c = q_H - q_L
$$

Therefore an alternative expression is:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{q_L}{q_H - q_L}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient & Area of a Marine Condenser

### Given
A shell-and-tube condenser uses seawater (tube-side) to condense R-134a (shell-side). Heat to be rejected $\dot{Q}_{cond}$ is known from the cycle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Seawater-side energy balance:**

The seawater enters at $T_{sw,i}$ and leaves at $T_{sw,o}$, picking up $\dot{Q}_{cond}$:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,o} - T_{sw,i})
$$

Solving for required seawater mass flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,o} - T_{sw,i})}
$$

**Step 2 — LMTD for the condenser (counter-flow):**

The refrigerant enters as superheated vapour at $T_2$, desuperheats, condenses at $T_{sat,cond}$, then subcools to $T_3$. The seawater warms from $T_{sw,i}$ to $T_{sw,o}$.

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_2 - T_{sw,o}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_3 - T_{sw,i}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3 — Required heat transfer area:**

From the overall heat transfer relation:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = U \, A \, F \, \Delta T_{lmtd}
$$

where $F$ is the LMTD correction factor (typically $0.75 - 0.95$ for a shell-and-tube HX with multiple passes).

Solving for area:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{U \, F \, \Delta T_{lmtd}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical overall U values:**

| HX | $U$ (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|
| Shell-and-tube condenser (seawater / R-134a) | 800 – 1500 |
| Plate HX condenser | 3000 – 5500 |
| DX finned coil evaporator (air side) | 30 – 60 |
| Flooded shell-and-tube evaporator | 500 – 1000 |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometric swept volume per cylinder:**

For a cylinder of bore $D$, stroke $L$, rotating at $N$ rpm with $n$ cylinders:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L \, N \, n}{4 \times 60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical suction mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{th} = \frac{V_s}{v_1}
$$

where $v_1$ is specific volume of refrigerant at suction conditions.

**Step 3 — Actual suction mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{act} = \eta_v \, \dot{m}_{th} = \eta_v \, \frac{V_s}{v_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $\eta_v$:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_{act} \, v_1}{V_s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Semi-empirical expression (commonly used in KTU numericals):**

$$
\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}\right)^{1/k}
$$

where:
- $C$ = clearance ratio (typically $0.04 - 0.08$ for marine compressors)
- $k$ = specific heat ratio of refrigerant vapour ($\approx 1.13$ for R-134a)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem
A shipboard refrigeration plant uses **R-134a** and operates between:
- Evaporator temperature $T_{evap} = -10^\circ\text{C}$
- Condenser temperature $T_{cond} = +40^\circ\text{C}$

State points (read from $P\text{-}h$ chart):
- $h_1 = 392 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour at −10 °C)
- $h_{2s} = 425 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (isentropic compression to 40 °C)
- $h_3 = 256 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at +40 °C)
- $h_2 = 432 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (actual outlet, given isentropic efficiency 0.85)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 256 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (throttling)

The plant provides a cooling load of **$\dot{Q}_L = 50 \text{ kW}$**. Seawater enters the condenser at $28^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $34^\circ\text{C}$.

**Find:** (a) Refrigerating effect per kg (b) COP (c) Compressor power (d) Seawater mass flow required.

### Solution

**(a) Refrigeration effect:**

$$
q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 392 - 256 = 136 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(b) Actual compressor work:**

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 432 - 392 = 40 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Check isentropic efficiency:

$$
\eta_{is} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{425 - 392}{432 - 392} = \frac{33}{40} = 0.825
$$

(The slight discrepancy with stated 0.85 is due to $P\text{-}h$ chart reading error — acceptable in exam.)

**(c) COP:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{136}{40} = 3.4
$$

**(d) Refrigerant mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L} = \frac{50}{136} = 0.3676 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(e) Compressor power input:**

$$
\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_r \, w_c = 0.3676 \times 40 = 14.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**(f) Heat rejected at condenser:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r \, (h_2 - h_3) = 0.3676 \times (432 - 256) = 0.3676 \times 176 = 64.7 \text{ kW}
$$

Check: $\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_c = 50 + 14.7 = 64.7 \text{ kW}$ ✓

**(g) Seawater mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw}(T_{sw,o} - T_{sw,i})} = \frac{64.7}{3.99 \times (34 - 28)} = \frac{64.7}{23.94} = 2.703 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Convert to volume flow (seawater density $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$):

$$
\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{2.703}{1025} \times 3600 \approx 9.49 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component-wise Cross-Check

| Component | Input Energy | Output Energy | Energy In – Out | Loss / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressor** | $h_1$ (low) | $h_2$ (high) | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | $\eta_{is} \approx 0.7 - 0.85$ |
| **Condenser** | $h_2$ | $h_3$ | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | Rejected to seawater |
| **Expansion Valve** | $h_3$ | $h_4 = h_3$ | $0$ | Isenthalpic (adiabatic) |
| **Evaporator** | $h_4$ | $h_1$ | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | Absorbed from load |
| **Cycle Net** | – | – | $q_H = q_L + w_c$ | First Law check |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Principal Components of a Marine Vapour-Compression Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA[Compressor<br/>Raises P and T]
    condA[Condenser<br/>Sea-water cooled<br/>Cu-Ni tubes]
    rxvA[Expansion Valve<br/>TXV]
    evapA[Evaporator<br/>DX coil]
    accA[Suction Accumulator<br/>Anti-slug]
    rcvA[Liquid Receiver<br/>Storage]
    drierA[Filter Drier<br/>Moisture removal]
    sgA[Oil Separator<br/>Returns oil]
    svA[Solenoid Valve<br/>Shutoff]
    swA[Sea Water Circuit<br/>Through condenser]
    loadA[Cold Space<br/>Provision room or cabin]

    compA -->|Discharge<br/>sup. vapour| sgA
    sgA -->|Oil returns| compA
    sgA -->|Clean vapour| condA
    condA -->|Liquid| rcvA
    rcvA --> drierA
    drierA --> sgA
    sgA -->|Liquid| rxvA
    rxvA -->|Two-phase| evapA
    evapA -->|Low P vapour| accA
    accA -->|Dry vapour| compA

    swA -.->|Cooling| condA
    evapA -.->|Cools| loadA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = refrigerant flow path. Dotted arrows = thermal/cooling effect. The compressor is fed back to itself via the suction line (closed loop).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Component Function Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Refrigerant at<br/>Low Pressure Vapour]
    block1[COMPRESSOR<br/>Function: Increase Pressure<br/>Type: Hermetic / Screw<br/>Marine feature: Resilient mount]
    block2[OIL SEPARATOR<br/>Function: Recover oil<br/>Marine feature: Coalescing type]
    block3[CONDENSER<br/>Function: Reject Heat<br/>Type: Shell-and-tube<br/>Marine feature: Cu-Ni tubes, seawater]
    block4[RECEIVER<br/>Function: Store liquid refrigerant]
    block5[FILTER DRIER<br/>Function: Remove moisture + acid<br/>Marine feature: Replaceable core]
    block6[SIGHT GLASS<br/>Function: Visual check<br/>Marine feature: Moisture indicator]
    block7[EXPANSION VALVE<br/>Function: Drop pressure<br/>Type: TXV<br/>Marine feature: Balanced port]
    block8[EVAPORATOR<br/>Function: Absorb Heat<br/>Type: DX coil / Flooded<br/>Marine feature: Stainless casing]
    block9[ACCUMULATOR<br/>Function: Prevent liquid slug<br/>Marine feature: Anti-siphon design]
    endA[Back to Compressor Suction]

    startA --> block1 --> block2 --> block3 --> block4 --> block5 --> block6 --> block7 --> block8 --> block9 --> endA
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — TXV Internal Force Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bulbP[Bulb at<br/>evaporator outlet]
    diaphragmP[Diaphragm<br/>Moves vertically]
    springP[Spring<br/>Set superheat]
    valveP[Valve Pin<br/>Opens or closes orifice]
    evapInP[Liquid from<br/>condenser]
    evapOutP[Two-phase into<br/>evaporator]

    bulbP -->|F_bulb| diaphragmP
    springP -->|F_spring| diaphragmP
    diaphragmP --> valveP
    evapInP --> valveP
    valveP --> evapOutP
```

**Force balance:** $F_{bulb} + F_{spring} \uparrow$ vs $F_{evap} + F_{atm} \downarrow$

---

## 4.4 Comparative Layout — Compressor Selection on Ships

```mermaid

    med[50 kW to 500 kW]

    qA --> small --> scroll
    qA --> med --> screw
    qA --> large --> centri
```

## 4.5 Marine Condenser Cross-Section (Block Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    swOutA[Sea-water OUT<br/>Top outlet]
    shellB[Liquid R-134a<br/>collects at BOTTOM]

    shellA --> tubesA
    tubesA --> shellB
    swInA --> tubesA --> swOutA
    bafflesA -.-> tubesA
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List the principal components of a marine vapour-compression refrigeration plant and state the function of each in one line."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each)

1. **Compressor** — Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour to high-pressure, high-temperature vapour *(1 m)*
2. **Condenser** — Rejects latent heat of condensation plus superheat to seawater *(1 m)*
3. **Evaporator** — Absorbs heat from the cooled space by boiling low-pressure refrigerant *(1 m)*

*(Optionally, expansion valve + auxiliaries for a fuller answer.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why are hermetic compressors preferred over open-type compressors for shipboard refrigeration plants? Give any two reasons."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer

1. **No shaft seal leakage** — Hermetic motors eliminate the rotating shaft seal, which is the most common leak point in marine plants exposed to ship motion and vibration *(1½ m)*
2. **Compact, low noise, oil-cooled motor** — Suction-cooled semi-hermetic units have quieter operation and smaller footprint suitable for engine-room-adjacent spaces *(1½ m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE format: Each Part-B question has internal choice. Both options A and B are presented below — the examiner shows **either A or B**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Adapted]**

**"With neat sketches explain the construction and working of a hermetic reciprocating compressor used in a shipboard refrigeration plant. Compare it with an open-type compressor."**

#### (a) Construction & Working of Hermetic Reciprocating Compressor — *(7 marks)*

**Construction:**

1. **Hermetically sealed steel shell** — encloses both motor and compressor; refrigerant gas cools the motor windings directly *(1 m)*
2. **Motor** — single-phase or 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor mounted vertically on a common shaft with the crankshaft *(1 m)*
3. **Crankshaft, connecting rod, piston** — the reciprocating assembly. Usually 1–4 cylinders in marine units *(1 m)*
4. **Valve plate** — contains suction (low-side) and discharge (high-side) reed or disc valves *(1 m)*
5. **Oil pump** — splash or pressure lubrication via an internal oil sump *(1 m)*
6. **Terminal box** — external electrical connection through sealed glass-metal bushings *(1 m)*

**Working (cycle):**

Suction stroke: low-pressure vapour enters cylinder as piston moves down; discharge stroke: compressed vapour forced through discharge valve into condenser. The cycle repeats at 1450/2900 rpm. Suction gas absorbs motor heat on its way to suction, improving cycle COP *(1 m)*

#### (b) Comparison with Open-Type Reciprocating Compressor — *(7 marks)*

| Feature | Hermetic | Open-Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Motor location** | Inside refrigerant shell | Separate external motor on bracket |
| **Shaft seal** | None | Required (most leak-prone part) |
| **Cooling** | Suction gas (motor-cooled) | Air-cooled or water-cooled motor |
| **Maintenance** | Whole unit replaced if motor fails | Motor accessible, field-serviceable |
| **Marine suitability** | Excellent (no leaks) | Moderate (seal wear due to motion) |
| **Cost** | Lower initially | Lower long-term |
| **Noise** | Lower | Higher |

*(7 comparison points × 1 m each = 7 m)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students often confuse **"semi-hermetic"** with **"fully hermetic"**. **Fully hermetic** = sealed for life, no service access. **Semi-hermetic** = bolted shell, motor serviceable. Both are marine-suitable; only the *open* type has shaft seal.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Adapted]**

**"Explain the construction and working of (i) a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) and (ii) a suction-line accumulator as fitted on a marine refrigeration plant. Sketch and label each."**

#### (a) Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) — *(7 marks)*

**Construction (4 m):**

1. **Sensing bulb** — clamped on suction line just after evaporator outlet; charged with same refrigerant or an inert gas + refrigerant mixture
2. **Diaphragm** — transmits bulb pressure against the spring
3. **Adjustable spring** — sets the desired superheat (typically 4–8 K)
4. **Valve seat & pin** — modulates refrigerant flow to evaporator
5. **External equaliser line** — connected to evaporator outlet (in "internally equalised" versions the equalisation is internal)

**Working (3 m):**

- Increased load → higher superheat → warmer bulb → higher bulb pressure → diaphragm lifts → valve opens more → more refrigerant flows
- Decreased load → opposite action
- Steady state: $F_{bulb} + F_{spring} = F_{evap} + F_{atm}$ (1 m)

**Sketch:** (Block diagram in Section 4.3 may be referenced)

#### (b) Suction-Line Accumulator — *(7 marks)*

**Construction (3 m):**

1. **Vertical cylindrical shell** — sized for 50–100 % of system refrigerant charge
2. **Inlet (top, tangential or perforated distributor)** — wet refrigerant from evaporator enters here
3. **U-bend or dip pipe** — dry vapour exits at top, liquid trapped at bottom
4. **Float-orifice or baffle** — separates liquid from vapour
5. **Bottom drain port** — returns trapped liquid slowly to compressor crankcase via capillary tube (or suction heat exchanger)

**Working (4 m):**

- Acts as a **liquid-vapour separator**: prevents liquid slug from reaching compressor suction *(2 m)*
- Especially vital on **screw compressors** during off-cycle pump-down and on **screw / reciprocating compressors** during start-up after standby *(1 m)*
- Maintains **dry suction** for compressor safety *(1 m)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — TXV Equalisation:**
> - **Internally equalised TXV** uses only evaporator pressure → poor performance with high pressure drop in evaporator.
> - **Externally equalised TXV** uses a sensing line at evaporator outlet → correct superheat maintained.
> In exams, **always specify the type**; this is a common 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Principal Components**

✅ **Five Functional Blocks:** Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator, Auxiliaries.

✅ **Compressor:**
- Hermetic preferred (no shaft seal).
- $w_c = h_2 - h_1$; $\eta_{is} = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_{2,actual} - h_1)$
- $\eta_v = 0.7 - 0.85$ marine typical.

✅ **Condenser:**
- Shell-and-tube with **Cu-Ni 70/30 tubes** for seawater.
- $q_H = h_2 - h_3$; $U = 800 - 1500$ W/m²·K.
- Seawater outlet ≤ 36 °C.

✅ **Evaporator:**
- DX coil for cabin AC, flooded for large plants.
- $q_L = h_1 - h_3$ (since $h_4 = h_3$).

✅ **Expansion Valve:**
- TXV = thermostatic, maintains superheat.
- Force balance: $F_{bulb} + F_{spring} = F_{evap} + F_{atm}$.
- Internal vs external equalisation matters.

✅ **Auxiliaries:**
- **Receiver** stores liquid; **Drier** removes moisture; **Accumulator** prevents slugging; **Oil Separator** returns oil; **Sight Glass** shows moisture.

✅ **Cycle Performance:**
- $\text{COP} = q_L / w_c = (h_1 - h_3)/(h_2 - h_1)$
- First Law check: $q_H = q_L + w_c$
- 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/h.

✅ **Marine-Specific Points:**
- Sea-water cooling (abundant at sea).
- Cupro-nickel condenser tubes.
- Resilient mounts for compressors.
- Classification society (DNV/Lloyd's) compliance.

✅ **Exam-Ready Constants:**
- $c_{p,sw} = 3.99$ kJ/kg·K
- $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025$ kg/m³
- Sea-water temp rise in condenser ≈ 5–8 °C.

✅ **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$ in isenthalpic throttling.
- Using freshwater $c_p$ instead of seawater $c_p$.
- Confusing "hermetic" with "open" or "semi-hermetic".
- Ignoring the **approach temperature** at seawater outlet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Compressors # Module 2 – Compressors (Marine Refrigeration Perspective) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Refrigeration Compressor)** > A refrigeration compressor is a **positive-displacement or dynamic (rotodynamic) machine** that converts low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapor drawn from the evaporator into high-pressure, high-temperature vapor by performing **mechanical work on the fluid**, thereby enabling the vapor to reject heat in the condenser. In marine applications, the compressor is the **prime mover of the vapor-compression refrigeration cycle (VCRC)** and represents the largest single power consumer (typically **60–80 %** of the total refrigeration plant load) on board a vessel. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a bicycle pump. When you push the handle down, you compress a fixed slug of air into a smaller space, raising its pressure so it can flow into a tire. A refrigeration compressor does the same thing — but continuously, with a special gas (refrigerant) that **boils and condenses** at convenient temperatures, and does this in a closed loop thousands of times per hour. On a ship, this is what chills your provision room, freezes your fish, or cools the bridge air conditioning. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 focus):** > Construction, working principle, performance characteristics, capacity control, and suitability of: > 1. Reciprocating (hermetic, semi-hermetic, open) > 2. Rotary (screw / scroll / vane) > 3. Centrifugal > 4. Compressor selection for marine plants (ammonia, HFC, HFO blends) ### Why the Compressor Matters on a Ship - **Space constraint:** Compact, vertically-mounted, vibration-tolerant designs are preferred. - **Tilt & roll:** Compressors must operate through list angles of up to **22.5°** (SOLAS/marine class rules) without losing suction. - **Saline atmosphere:** Salt-laden air mandates **hermetic or semi-hermetic** enclosures to protect the motor windings. - **Power source:** Driven by **electric motor**, **main engine PTO**, or **hydraulic motor**, with redundancy requirements for cargo holds. | Term | Standard Metric (SI) | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Volumetric efficiency** | **η_v = 70 – 90 %** | Measure of actual vs. swept volume flow | | **Isentropic (isenthalpic) efficiency** | **η_s = 60 – 80 %** | Measure of thermodynamic idealization | | **Compressor ratio** | **r_p = p_cond / p_evap** | Typically **2.5 – 8** for marine R-22/R-404A plants | | **Refrigeration effect** | **RE = h_g1 − h_f4 (kJ/kg)** | Useful cooling per kg of refrigerant | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Refrigeration Compressors ``` REFRIGERATION COMPRESSORS | ------------------------------------------------ | | POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT DYNAMIC (ROTO-DYNAMIC) (Constant mass trapped per cycle) (Continuous flow, momentum change) | | -------------- ------------------------ | | | | RECIPROCATING ROTARY AXIAL FLOW CENTRIFUGAL | | | --- -------- Used in large capacity (>500 TR) | | | e.g., LNG carrier reliquefaction | SCREW VANE / | SCROLL Open / Semi-hermetic / Hermetic ``` ### 2.2 Reciprocating Compressor — Working Steps A reciprocating compressor completes a cycle in **4 strokes (2 revolutions)** for a single-acting cylinder: 1. **Suction stroke (0° → 180°):** Low-pressure vapor from evaporator fills the cylinder as piston moves down. Pressure ≈ **p_evap**. 2. **Compression stroke (180° → 360°):** Piston moves up, compresses trapped vapor. Both valves closed. Pressure rises from **p_evap** to **p_cond**. 3. **Discharge stroke (start at 360°):** Once cylinder pressure ≥ p_cond, **discharge valve** opens. Vapor is forced out into condenser. 4. **End of stroke & valve reset:** At TDC piston nearly top, residual **clearance volume** `V_c` traps high-pressure vapor, which **re-expands** on next suction — this is the *clearance loss*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clearance volume is the single most important design parameter** governing volumetric efficiency, and is the reason KTU problems almost always test it. ### 2.3 Rotary Compressors (Marine-Favoured) - **Screw (Helical Rotary):** Two intermeshing rotors (male + female) with lubricant injected for sealing. Capacities: **50 – 1500 kW**. Standard for **fish-room and reefer container plants**. - **Scroll:** One fixed scroll + one orbiting scroll. Continuous compression, very low vibration, suitable for **AC plant on passenger vessels**. - **Vane (Sliding-vane):** Used rarely in marine R-717 (ammonia) plants. ### 2.4 Centrifugal Compressor - **Dynamic machine** — adds kinetic energy via impeller, then converts to pressure in a diffuser. - Used only in **very large capacity** shipboard plants (e.g., **LNG, large reefers > 500 TR**). - **Surge** and **stone-wall** phenomena are critical operating limits. ### 2.5 Hermetic / Semi-Hermetic / Open — Marine Context | Type | Motor Location | Leak Risk | Maintenance | Marine Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Open** | External, shaft seal | High | Easy | Engine-room R-717 (NH₃) plants | | **Semi-hermetic** | Internal, bolted cover | Low | Moderate | Provision plant AC, reefer containers | | **Hermetic** | Welded shell, no seal | Negligible | Replace unit | Domestic fridges, cabin AC | > [!NOTE] > **Marine-Class Rule:** A **standby compressor** of equal or higher capacity must be available for any cargo cooling system serving perishable goods (e.g., IACS UR F series). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Swept (displacement) volume** | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, D^{2}\, L\, N\, n_c$ | m³/s | $n_c$ = number of cylinders | | 2 | **Volumetric efficiency (with clearance)** | $\eta_v = 1 + c - c\,\left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n}$ | — | $c = V_c / V_s$ clearance ratio, $n \approx 1.3$ (polytropic) | | 3 | **Mass flow rate** | $\dot m = \dfrac{\eta_v\, V_s}{v_s}$ | kg/s | $v_s$ = specific volume at suction | | 4 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $IP = \dfrac{\dot m \,(h_{2s} - h_1)}{\eta_{mech}\,\eta_s}$ | kW | $h_{2s}$ = isentropic discharge enthalpy | | 5 | **COP of cycle** | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}}$ | — | Used in Part B 14-mark questions | | 6 | **Pressure ratio** | $r_p = \dfrac{p_{cond}}{p_{evap}}$ | — | Marine R-404A plant ≈ 4–6 | | 7 | **Isentropic work (per kg)** | $w_s = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, p_1 v_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | For polytropic compression | | 8 | **Screw compressor η_v** | $\eta_v \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ | — | Higher than reciprocating | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Pitfall:** Do **not** use the pipe `|` symbol for ratios like `p_d / p_s` inside markdown tables. Always use `\dfrac{}{}` to avoid breaking the table grid. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fuel savings:** Modern **screw compressors with VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** cut marine plant power by 20–30 %. - **Eco regulations:** IMO MARPOL Annex VI + Kigali Amendment push ship operators toward **low-GWP refrigerants** (R-1234yf, NH₃, CO₂) → **trans-critical CO₂** and **screw-oil-free** compressors are emerging choices. - **Diagnostic data:** Modern compressors include **IoT vibration sensors** to predict bearing failure 200+ hours in advance. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance (Reciprocating Compressor) This is the single most important derivation KTU 2024 Scheme asks in 14-mark problems. **Let:** - $V_s$ = swept volume per cycle - $V_c$ = clearance volume - $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ = total cylinder volume at BDC (start of suction) - $p_1 = p_s$ = suction pressure - $p_2 = p_d$ = discharge pressure - $n$ = polytropic index (1.25–1.35 for real refrigerant vapor; **1.3** is a common KTU assumption) - $V_3 = V_c$ (clearance at TDC after discharge) - $V_4$ = re-expanded clearance volume at start of next suction **Step 1 — Compression of clearance gas from 3 → 2 (discharge):** $$ p_1 V_3^{\,n} = p_2 V_c^{\,n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_3 = V_c $$ Clearance volume remains the same physically. The trapped gas goes from $(V_c,\, p_2)$ to $(V_3,\, p_1)$ during re-expansion. **Step 2 — Re-expansion of clearance gas from 3 → 4 (suction side):** $$ p_2 V_c^{\,n} = p_1 V_4^{\,n} $$ $$ V_4 = V_c \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Actual volume of fresh vapor drawn in:** $$ V_{actual} = V_1 - V_4 = (V_s + V_c) - V_c \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 4 — Divide by swept volume to get volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_s} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c - V_c\,(p_2/p_1)^{1/n}}{V_s} $$ $$ \eta_v = 1 + \dfrac{V_c}{V_s} - \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ Defining clearance ratio $c = V_c / V_s$: $$ \boxed{\;\eta_v = 1 + c - c \left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n}\;} $$ This is the **KTU textbook standard expression**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limiting checks (good answer-writing practice):** > - If $c = 0$ (no clearance), $\eta_v = 1$ — physically correct. > - As $p_d / p_s \to 1$ (small ratio), $\eta_v \to 1$ — also correct. > - As $p_d / p_s$ increases, the term $(p_d/p_s)^{1/n}$ rises and $\eta_v$ falls — matches reality. ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU sub-part):** A single-cylinder, single-acting ammonia compressor has: $D = 0.10\,$m, $L = 0.10\,$m, clearance ratio $c = 0.04$, speed $N = 600\,$rpm, suction condition $-10\,^{\circ}$C dry saturated, condensing at $+30\,^{\circ}$C, polytropic index $n = 1.30$. Find: (i) swept volume, (ii) volumetric efficiency, (iii) mass flow, (iv) theoretical power. **Ammonia properties (from KTU-approved tables):** - $p_s$ at $-10\,^{\circ}$C ≈ **2.910 bar** → $v_s$ ≈ **0.4184 m³/kg** - $p_d$ at $+30\,^{\circ}$C ≈ **11.67 bar** **Step (i) — Swept volume per second:** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, D^{2}\, L\, \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(0.10)^{2}\,(0.10)\,(10) $$ $$ V_s = 0.007854\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ **Step (ii) — Volumetric efficiency:** $$ r_p = \dfrac{p_d}{p_s} = \dfrac{11.67}{2.910} = 4.011 $$ $$ \left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n} = (4.011)^{1/1.30} = (4.011)^{0.7692} $$ $$ \ln(4.011) = 1.3886 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.7692 \times 1.3886 = 1.0684 $$ $$ \exp(1.0684) = 2.911 $$ $$ \eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 2.911 = 1.04 - 0.1164 = 0.9236 $$ $$ \eta_v \approx 92.4\ \% $$ **Step (iii) — Mass flow rate:** $$ \dot m = \dfrac{\eta_v\, V_s}{v_s} = \dfrac{0.9236 \times 0.007854}{0.4184} $$ $$ \dot m = 0.01734\ \text{kg/s} \quad (\approx 62.4\ \text{kg/h}) $$ **Step (iv) — Theoretical (isentropic) power:** First, ideal discharge enthalpy (assume isentropic, kJ/kg): From ammonia superheat table at $p_d = 11.67\,$bar, $s_{2s} = s_1$: Take $h_1$ (sat. vapor at $-10\,^{\circ}$C) ≈ **1432.0 kJ/kg** $s_1$ ≈ **5.471 kJ/kg·K** At $p_d = 11.67\,$bar, $s_{2s} = 5.471$ → $h_{2s}$ ≈ **1640 kJ/kg** (interpolated; typical KTU value ≈ 1635–1645). $$ w_s = h_{2s} - h_1 = 1640 - 1432 = 208\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ P_{th} = \dot m \cdot w_s = 0.01734 \times 208 = 3.607\ \text{kW} $$ $$ \boxed{\;V_s = 0.00785\ \text{m}^3/\text{s},\quad \eta_v = 92.4\%,\quad \dot m = 0.0173\ \text{kg}/\text{s},\quad P_{th} = 3.61\ \text{kW}\;} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Isentropic Work per kg (for Ideal Gas Approximation) Useful when KTU question specifies an air-compressor analogy or uses R-12 / R-134a as ideal gas: For closed-system polytropic process: $$ w = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p\, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p_1 V_1^{n}\, V^{-n}\, dV $$ $$ w = \dfrac{p_2 V_2 - p_1 V_1}{1 - n} $$ Using $p_1 V_1 = R T_1$ and $V_2 / V_1 = (p_1/p_2)^{1/n}$: $$ w = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, R\, T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right] $$ For isentropic ($n = k = c_p / c_v$): $$ \boxed{\;w_s = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\, R\, T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]\;} $$ This is the KTU 14-mark "compressor work" formula in Mechanical/RAC courses. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Reciprocating Compressor — P-V Diagram (Block Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BDC Point 1
P1 = Ps
V1 = Vs + Vc] -->|Suction stroke
constant pressure| B[Point 4
P4 = Ps
V4 = Vc x Pd/Ps power 1/n] B -->|Re-expansion of
clearance gas| C[TDC Point 3
P3 = Pd
V3 = Vc] C -->|Compression stroke
P x V power n constant| D[Point 2
P2 = Pd
V2 = Vc] D -->|Discharge stroke
constant pressure| A style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Screw Compressor — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUCT [Suction Side] S1[Suction Port] --> S2[Rotors Inlet Chamber] end subgraph CORE [Compression Core] MR[Male Rotor
4-6 lobes] -. mesh .-> FR[Female Rotor
6-8 flutes] S2 --> MR MR --> O1[Oil Injection Port
seal + cool] O1 --> MR end subgraph DISCH [Discharge Side] MR --> D1[Discharge Port
axial end] FR --> D1 D1 --> D2[Slide Valve for
Capacity Control] end style MR fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style FR fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660066,color:#fff style O1 fill:#66ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style D2 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Marine Refrigeration Plant — Compressor Position in Vapour Compression Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR EV[Evaporator
low p, low T] -->|Saturated vapor
state 1| COMP[Compressor
state 1 to 2
W comp in] COMP -->|Superheated vapor
state 2| CD[Condenser
state 2 to 3] CD -->|Subcooled liquid
state 3| EXV[Expansion Valve
state 3 to 4] EXV -->|Two-phase mixture
state 4| EV style COMP fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style EV fill:#66ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style CD fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style EXV fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Capacity Control Methods (Reciprocating Compressor) ```mermaid flowchart TB CC[Capacity Control] --> C1[1. On-Off Cycling
simple, small plant] CC --> C2[2. Hot Gas Bypass
constant load, 0 percent turndown] CC --> C3[3. Unloader Suction Valve
open at part load, 50-100 percent] CC --> C4[4. Cylinder Unloading
deactivate cylinders, 33/66/100] CC --> C5[5. Variable Speed
VFD or hydraulic drive, smooth turndown] style C1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C3 fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C4 fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#660066,color:#fff style C5 fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `MR`, `C5`). > - No reserved keywords used as standalone node names. > - Labels are raw uppercase/lowercase plain text — no `**bold**` or HTML tables inside the quoted labels. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A (3 Marks each — Short Answer / Concept) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the term "volumetric efficiency" of a reciprocating refrigeration compressor. Mention the typical range for marine plants.* **Model Answer:** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual volume of refrigerant vapor drawn into the cylinder per cycle to the swept (displacement) volume of the compressor. $$ \eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_s} = 1 + c - c\left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n} $$ For marine refrigeration plants, $\eta_v$ typically ranges between **70 % and 90 %**, depending on pressure ratio, clearance ratio, and refrigerant used. Higher values (~95 %) are achievable in modern oil-injected screw compressors. *(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, range 1)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three differences between open-type and hermetic compressors, with marine suitability comment.* **Model Answer:** | S.No | Open-type | Hermetic | |---|---|---| | 1 | Motor external, shaft passes through seal | Motor sealed inside refrigerant-filled shell | | 2 | Risk of refrigerant leakage via seal | Negligible leakage (no shaft seal) | | 3 | Easy on-board maintenance | Must replace entire unit if motor fails | | 4 | Preferred for **R-717 (ammonia)** engine-room plants | Preferred for **HFC** cabin / provision AC | *(3 marks: three valid differences + 1 marine context)* --- ### Part B (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice) > **Instructions for ESE (per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** With a neat P-V diagram, explain the working of a single-acting reciprocating refrigeration compressor. Derive the expression for volumetric efficiency in terms of clearance ratio and pressure ratio. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A marine R-134a reciprocating compressor has: Bore $D = 75\,$mm, Stroke $L = 65\,$mm, Clearance ratio $c = 0.05$, Speed $N = 900\,$rpm, Suction condition = $-5\,^{\circ}$C (sat. vapor), Condensing temperature = $+40\,^{\circ}$C, Polytropic index $n = 1.30$. Take $v_1 = 0.0757\,$m³/kg, $h_1 = 247.2\,$kJ/kg, $h_{2s} = 277.1\,$kJ/kg, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$. Compute: (i) swept volume, (ii) $\eta_v$, (iii) $\dot m$, (iv) indicated power. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A:** **(a) Working + P-V diagram — 7 marks** - **Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the P-V diagram in Section 4.1 above. Clearly label points 1, 2, 3, 4 and the four processes: suction, compression, discharge, re-expansion. - **Explanation (2 marks):** Mention the role of suction and discharge valves, the trapping of clearance gas, and the start of a new suction stroke. - **Derivation (2 marks):** Use the derivation in Section 3.1 above to obtain: $$ \eta_v = 1 + c - c \left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n} $$ **(b) Numerical — 7 marks** **Step (i) — Swept volume (1 mark):** $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, D^{2}\, L\, \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(0.075)^{2}\,(0.065)\,(15) $$ $$ V_s = 4.305 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ **Step (ii) — Volumetric efficiency (2 marks):** Pressure ratio (from R-134a saturation tables): - $p_s$ at $-5\,^{\circ}$C ≈ **2.439 bar** - $p_d$ at $+40\,^{\circ}$C ≈ **10.16 bar** $$ r_p = \dfrac{10.16}{2.439} = 4.166 $$ $$ (r_p)^{1/n} = (4.166)^{1/1.30} = (4.166)^{0.7692} $$ $$ \ln(4.166) = 1.4269 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.7692 \times 1.4269 = 1.0976 $$ $$ \exp(1.0976) = 2.998 \approx 3.00 $$ $$ \eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times 3.00 = 1.05 - 0.15 = 0.90 = 90\ \% $$ **Step (iii) — Mass flow (2 marks):** $$ \dot m = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot V_s}{v_1} = \dfrac{0.90 \times 4.305 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0757} $$ $$ \dot m = 0.0512\ \text{kg/s} \approx 184.2\ \text{kg/h} $$ **Step (iv) — Indicated power (2 marks):** $$ w_s = h_{2s} - h_1 = 277.1 - 247.2 = 29.9\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ $$ P_{th} = \dot m \cdot w_s = 0.0512 \times 29.9 = 1.531\ \text{kW} $$ $$ P_{ip} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{1.531}{0.85} = 1.80\ \text{kW} $$ **Final boxed answer:** $$ \boxed{V_s = 4.30 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s},\ \eta_v = 90\%,\ \dot m = 0.0512\ \text{kg}/\text{s},\ P_{ip} = 1.80\ \text{kW}} $$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Compare the construction and performance of **reciprocating, screw, and centrifugal** compressors suitable for marine refrigeration plants. Mention at least three parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an **oil-injected twin-screw compressor**. Discuss the role of the **slide valve** in capacity control. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B:** **(a) Comparative table — 7 marks** | Parameter | Reciprocating | Screw | Centrifugal | |---|---|---|---| | **Capacity range** | 1 – 200 kW | 50 – 1500 kW | > 500 kW (large) | | **Pressure ratio** | 2.5 – 8 | 3 – 10 | Up to 30 (multistage) | | **η_v** | 70 – 90 % | 85 – 95 % | not applicable (dynamic) | | **η_isentropic** | 60 – 75 % | 70 – 80 % | 75 – 85 % | | **Capacity control** | On-off, unloader, bypass | Slide valve, VFD | Inlet guide vane, VFD | | **Suitability on ship** | Small AC, provision plant | Fish room, reefer containers | LNG reliquefaction, big cargo | | **Vibration** | High (pulsating) | Low | Very low | *(7 marks: full table 5, marine suitability 2)* **(b) Screw compressor — 7 marks** - **Sketch (3 marks):** Refer to Mermaid block 4.2. Label male rotor, female rotor, suction port, discharge port, oil injection line. - **Working (2 marks):** Refrigerant vapour enters the suction port, gets trapped between meshing rotor lobes, is conveyed axially, and is progressively compressed as the trapped volume shrinks. Oil is injected for sealing, cooling, and lubrication, then separated and returned to the oil reservoir. - **Slide valve (2 marks):** The slide valve sits on the casing above the rotors. By moving it axially, the effective built-in pressure ratio is changed, which **varies the displaced volume per revolution**, giving smooth 10–100 % capacity control without altering the speed. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 2 Compressors):** > 1. **Forgetting clearance volume:** Many students assume $\eta_v = 1$. This is **wrong** — KTU always expects the clearance term. > 2. **Wrong polytropic index:** KTU 2024 problems often quote $n = 1.30$. If you use $n = 1.0$ (isothermal) or $n = k = 1.4$ (isentropic) you lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Mixing volumetric and isentropic efficiency:** These are **not** the same. State both clearly. > 4. **Skipping the sketch:** A P-V diagram or screw-compressor block diagram is worth at least 2 marks by itself. Always draw one. > 5. **Forgetting units:** $V_s$ must be in **m³/s**, not m³/min. Convert $N$ from rpm by dividing by 60. > 6. **Marine context missing:** For "open vs hermetic" or "screw vs reciprocating" questions, always add a one-line marine-suitability note. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Compressor is the **largest power consumer** in a marine VCRC plant. - ✅ Classification: **Positive displacement (reciprocating, screw, scroll, vane)** vs **Dynamic (centrifugal, axial)**. - ✅ **Clearance volume** → clearance ratio $c = V_c / V_s$ → directly reduces $\eta_v$. - ✅ Master formula: $$ \eta_v = 1 + c - c\left(\dfrac{p_d}{p_s}\right)^{1/n} $$ - ✅ **Polytropic index** for refrigerant vapour: $n = 1.25$ to $1.35$ (use **1.30** unless stated). - ✅ **Swept volume per second** for a single-acting reciprocating compressor: $$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2}\,L\,N/60 $$ - ✅ **Mass flow** uses suction specific volume $v_s$, not discharge $v_d$. - ✅ **Indicated power** divides theoretical power by **mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$. - ✅ **Hermetic** is for HFC cabin AC; **Open** is for NH₃ engine-room plants. - ✅ **Screw compressor capacity control:** Slide valve + variable speed (VFD) for fine turndown. - ✅ **Centrifugal:** surge, stone-wall, polytropic head, no clearance concept. - ✅ **Marine rules:** Standby compressor mandatory for cargo refrigeration (IACS UR F). - ✅ Always show the **P-V diagram** and label all four points 1, 2, 3, 4 in reciprocating-compressor derivations.

topics.title: Condensers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Condensers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Condenser** in a marine vapour-compression refrigeration system is a **shell-and-tube (or plate-type) heat exchanger** whose primary function is to **reject the latent heat of vaporisation plus the superheat absorbed in the evaporator and the work of compression** to a secondary cooling medium, thereby condensing the high-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant vapour into a saturated (or subcooled) liquid. The heat rejected is termed the **Heat Rejection Factor (HRF)** of the condenser.

In the **P-h (Pressure-Enthalpy) diagram** of a marine refrigeration cycle, the condenser spans the thermodynamic states from **point 2 (compressor discharge / superheated vapour)** to **point 3 (saturated liquid / subcooled liquid)** at the constant **condensing pressure $P_c$** (or **condensing temperature $T_c$**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "A condenser is a heat-rejecting heat exchanger in which the refrigerant, after compression, gives up its latent heat to a colder cooling medium (seawater, freshwater, or air) and changes phase from vapour to liquid at essentially constant pressure."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Sweating Cold Drink" in the Tropics**
> Imagine pulling an ice-cold glass of water out of a freezer on a hot, humid day in Kochi harbour. Almost instantly, the outer surface of the glass becomes wet — water droplets form. Why? Because the cold glass **absorbs heat from the warm surrounding air**, the air near the glass cools below its dew point, and water vapour in the air **condenses** into liquid droplets on the glass. The glass is doing exactly what a condenser does — **removing heat from a fluid and causing a phase change (vapour → liquid)**. In a marine condenser, the warm refrigerant vapour inside the tubes is the "warm humid air", and the cold seawater flowing past the tubes is the "cool evening breeze" that absorbs the heat. The refrigerant vapour "sweats" itself into a liquid, dumping its hidden (latent) heat into the seawater.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Marine Metrics

- **Latent heat of vaporisation of R-134a** at $40^\circ C$: $h_{fg} \approx 162.2\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Latent heat of vaporisation of R-404A** at $40^\circ C$: $h_{fg} \approx 148.0\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Latent heat of vaporisation of Ammonia (R-717)** at $40^\circ C$: $h_{fg} \approx 992.0\ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Standard marine seawater inlet temperature** to central cooler: $28^\circ C$ to $32^\circ C$ (tropical waters)
- **Typical condensing temperature** for marine A/C: $35^\circ C$ to $45^\circ C$
- **Minimum approach temperature** in marine condensers: **$5^\circ C$**
- **Specific heat of seawater** (35‰ salinity): $c_{p,sw} \approx 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- **Specific heat of freshwater**: $c_{p,fw} = 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- **Design fouling factor** for seawater-side: $0.0001$ to $0.0002\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$

---

## 1.4 Role of the Condenser in the Marine Vapour Compression Cycle

| Function | Thermodynamic Purpose | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Latent Heat Rejection** | Remove $h_{fg}$ of refrigerant | Vapour → Liquid phase change |
| **Superheat Removal** | Cool vapour from $T_{2}$ to $T_{sat}$ at $P_c$ | Saturated vapour at inlet of desuperheater zone |
| **Subcooling** (optional) | Cool liquid below $T_{sat}$ | Improved refrigeration effect ($h_f - h_4$) |
| **Pressure Maintenance** | Holds high side at $P_c$ | Enables compressor to push refrigerant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The condenser is the "heat sink" of the cycle.** Without an effective condenser, the high-side pressure would drift upward, the compressor work would increase, and the **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** would crash.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Process Inside the Condenser

The refrigerant undergoes a **constant-pressure heat rejection process** (assuming negligible pressure drop, $\Delta P \approx 0$ across the condenser). The internal zones are:

1. **Desuperheater Zone** (State 2 → State 2′):
   Superheated refrigerant vapour is cooled from discharge temperature $T_2$ down to the **saturation temperature** $T_{sat}$ at the condensing pressure $P_c$.
   - Heat rejected (sensible): $q_{sup} = c_{p,v} \cdot (T_2 - T_{sat})$

2. **Condensing Zone** (State 2′ → State 3′):
   Saturated vapour at $T_{sat}, P_c$ releases **latent heat** $h_{fg}$ and condenses into saturated liquid.
   - Heat rejected (latent): $q_{lat} = h_{fg}$ — this is the **dominant** term (≈ 80–90 % of total condenser duty).

3. **Subcooling Zone** (State 3′ → State 3):
   Saturated liquid is further cooled below $T_{sat}$ to provide **subcooling** $\Delta T_{sub}$.
   - Heat rejected (sensible): $q_{sub} = c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$

### Total Condenser Heat Rejection Rate

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_r$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant (kg/s)
- $h_2$ = specific enthalpy at compressor discharge (kJ/kg)
- $h_3$ = specific enthalpy at condenser exit (kJ/kg, subcooled)

---

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

| Step | Why this happens | How it happens physically |
|---|---|---|
| **Desuperheat** | Compressor discharge vapour is superheated (≈ 20–30 K above $T_{sat}$) to ensure no liquid droplets enter the compressor (which would cause slugging). | Vapour transfers **sensible heat** through tube wall to cooling medium. Temperature drops at constant pressure. |
| **Latent Condensation** | At $P_c$, the saturation temperature is fixed. Vapour must shed latent heat to drop into the two-phase region. | Phase change releases **$h_{fg}$**. Temperature remains constant. Massive heat flux occurs here. |
| **Subcooling** | Increases refrigeration effect by lowering $h_3$ below $h_f$ at $P_c$. Improves cycle COP. | Liquid (incompressible) transfers **sensible heat**. Only a few degrees of subcooling (typically 3–5 K) is provided. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on Condensers.

### A. Energy Balance Formulas

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$ | Refrigerant-side heat duty | kW |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{cw} \, c_{p,cw} (T_{cw,out} - T_{cw,in})$ | Cooling medium-side heat duty | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,cw} \, \Delta T_{cw}}$ | Required cooling medium flow | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}$ | Energy balance of cycle | kW |

### B. Heat Exchanger Sizing Formulas (LMTD Method)

| # | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ = heat transfer area (m²) |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1$ = hot-end temperature difference, $\Delta T_2$ = cold-end difference |
| 7 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ (parallel) | Hot fluid out vs cold fluid in |
| 8 | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ (parallel) | Hot fluid in vs cold fluid out |
| 9 | $F_t = $ LMTD correction factor (for multi-pass) | Always $\le 1$; from charts for shell-and-tube |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = U \, A \, F_t \, \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Corrected LMTD equation |

### C. Effectiveness (ε) – NTU Method

| # | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 11 | $C_{min} = (\dot{m} \, c_p)_{min}$ ; $C_{max} = (\dot{m} \, c_p)_{max}$ | Heat capacity rates |
| 12 | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Heat capacity ratio, $\le 1$ |
| 13 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{\dot{Q}_{max,possible}} = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Effectiveness (if $C_{min}$ is hot side) |
| 14 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units |
| 15 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\bigl[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\bigr]}{1 - C_r \exp\bigl[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\bigr]}$ | Counterflow |
| 16 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\bigl[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)\bigr]$ | Parallel flow |

### D. Marine-Specific Subcooling & Approach

| # | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 17 | $\Delta T_{approach} = T_{cond} - T_{cw,out}$ | Minimum approach at hot end (design $\approx 5$ K) |
| 18 | $\Delta T_{sub} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,exit}$ | Subcooling (typically 3–5 K) |
| 19 | $R_f = \dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} - \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}}$ | Fouling factor (m²K/W) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

| Marine System | Why the Condenser is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Provision Room A/C** | Maintains $T_{cw,in} \le 32^\circ C$ to ensure food preservation at $0^\circ C$ to $4^\circ C$. |
| **Accommodation A/C** | Tropical voyages with $T_{amb} \ge 45^\circ C$ push $T_{cond}$ up — condenser must be oversized. |
| **Reefer Container Plant** | High latent loads from fresh produce; condenser must reject moisture-laden thermal load. |
| **Central Sea-Water Cooling (SWC)** | All condensers draw from a common SWC loop; common failures impact multiple condensers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **In warships and LNG carriers**, condensers must also meet **vibration, shock, and noise (MIL-SPEC / IMO Tier III)** standards. Plate heat exchangers are often used instead of shell-and-tube to reduce weight and footprint.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Condenser Heat Rejection from Energy Balance on Refrigerant Stream

We apply the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** across the control volume of the condenser. Neglecting changes in kinetic and potential energy:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \dot{m}_r \left[ h_3 - h_2 + \dfrac{V_3^2 - V_2^2}{2} + g(z_3 - z_2) \right]$$

**Step 1:** No shaft work crosses the condenser boundary ($\dot{W}_{cv} = 0$).

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy change is negligible ($\Delta KE \approx 0$).

**Step 3:** Potential energy change is negligible ($\Delta PE \approx 0$).

**Step 4:** Substituting, we obtain:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$$

This is the **fundamental energy equation** for the marine condenser. The sign convention here is that $\dot{Q}_{cond}$ is **rejected** (positive magnitude on the seawater side, negative sign if we consider the refrigerant losing heat).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Seawater Flow Rate from Energy Balance

Apply SFEE on the seawater (cooling water) side:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})$$

Solving for $\dot{m}_{sw}$:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** A marine R-134a chiller has a refrigeration capacity of $\dot{Q}_{evap} = 120\ \text{kW}$. Compressor work input is $\dot{W}_{comp} = 30\ \text{kW}$. The refrigerant enters the condenser at $h_2 = 280\ \text{kJ/kg}$ and leaves as subcooled liquid at $h_3 = 110\ \text{kJ/kg}$. Seawater enters at $28^\circ C$ and leaves at $36^\circ C$. $c_{p,sw} = 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Find:
> (i) Condenser heat duty $\dot{Q}_{cond}$
> (ii) Mass flow rate of refrigerant $\dot{m}_r$
> (iii) Required seawater mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{sw}$

**Solution:**

**Step (i):** By first law for the cycle, condenser duty equals evaporator load plus compressor work:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = 120 + 30 = 150\ \text{kW}$$

**Step (ii):** Using the refrigerant-side energy equation:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$$

$$\dot{m}_r = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{h_2 - h_3} = \dfrac{150}{280 - 110} = \dfrac{150}{170}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_r = 0.882\ \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step (iii):** Using the cooling-side energy equation:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \, (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})} = \dfrac{150}{3.99 \times (36 - 28)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{150}{3.99 \times 8} = \dfrac{150}{31.92}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{sw} = 4.70\ \text{kg/s}}$$

In volumetric terms (seawater density $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{4.70}{1025} = 4.585 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 16.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: LMTD for a Marine Counterflow Condenser

**Given:** Hot fluid (refrigerant) enters at $T_{h,in} = T_{sat} = 40^\circ C$ (saturated condensing), leaves at $T_{h,out} = 35^\circ C$ (subcooled). Cold fluid (seawater) enters at $T_{c,in} = 28^\circ C$, leaves at $T_{c,out} = 36^\circ C$.

**Step 1:** Identify temperature differences at both ends.

At the **hot end** (where hot fluid enters):

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 40 - 36 = 4^\circ C$$

At the **cold end** (where hot fluid leaves):

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 35 - 28 = 7^\circ C$$

**Step 2:** Apply the LMTD formula.

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{4 - 7}{\ln(4 / 7)} = \dfrac{-3}{-0.5596}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lmtd} = 5.36^\circ C}$$

**Note:** The LMTD is always taken as a positive magnitude. The negative signs in numerator and denominator cancel.

**Step 3:** Required heat transfer area.

If $U = 1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $\dot{Q}_{cond} = 150\ \text{kW} = 150{,}000\ \text{W}$:

$$A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \dfrac{150{,}000}{1500 \times 5.36}$$

$$\boxed{A = 18.66\ \text{m}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effectiveness–NTU Relation for a Counterflow Condenser

**Given:** Same marine condenser as above. Determine $\varepsilon$ and NTU.

**Step 1:** Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$.

- Refrigerant side: $C_r = \dot{m}_r \, c_{p,r} = 0.882 \times 1.8 = 1.588\ \text{kW/K}$
- Seawater side: $C_{sw} = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_{p,sw} = 4.70 \times 3.99 = 18.75\ \text{kW/K}$

Therefore $C_{min} = 1.588\ \text{kW/K}$ (refrigerant side) and $C_{max} = 18.75\ \text{kW/K}$ (seawater side).

**Step 2:** Heat capacity ratio.

$$C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{1.588}{18.75} = 0.0847$$

**Step 3:** Maximum possible heat transfer (limited by $C_{min}$).

$$\dot{Q}_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 1.588 \times (40 - 28) = 19.06\ \text{kW}$$

Wait — the actual $\dot{Q}_{cond} = 150\ \text{kW}$. This exceeds $\dot{Q}_{max}$ because of the **phase change** (latent heat), which breaks the simple $C_{min}$ limitation. For condensers with phase change, we use the **LMTD method** rather than the standard ε-NTU method (which assumes single-phase sensible heat on both sides).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The standard ε-NTU formulas above apply to **single-phase sensible heat exchangers**. For condensers (with latent heat), use the **LMTD method** plus a **fouling factor** correction. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Condenser Type Comparison

| Feature | Shell-and-Tube (S&T) | Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) | Air-Cooled (Forced Draft) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer coefficient $U$** | 1000–2500 W/m²K | 3000–7000 W/m²K | 30–60 W/m²K |
| **Footprint** | Large | Compact (1/4 to 1/6 of S&T) | Large (fans needed) |
| **Marine suitability** | Excellent (seawater in tubes) | Good (gasket life a concern) | Poor (clogged by salt spray) |
| **Seawater-side material** | Cupronickel (Cu-Ni 90/10 or 70/30) | Titanium plates | N/A (air side) |
| **Cleanability** | Excellent (brushable tubes) | Moderate (CIP required) | Easy (air side) |
| **Fouling resistance** | High (robust design) | Low (narrow channels) | N/A |
| **Vibration tolerance** | Excellent | Moderate (gasket fatigue) | Good |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High initial | Low initial, high operating (fans) |
| **Typical use on ships** | Provision plant, reefer | Accommodation A/C | Emergency / small plants |

---

## 3.6 Subcooling Impact on Cycle Performance

The refrigeration effect with subcooling is:

$$RE = h_1 - h_4$$

Without subcooling: $h_4 = h_{f@P_c}$.

With subcooling $\Delta T_{sub}$: $h_4 = h_{f@P_c} - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$.

**Improvement in COP:**

$$\Delta COP = \dfrac{c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}}{\dot{W}_{comp} / \dot{m}_r}$$

For $\Delta T_{sub} = 5\ \text{K}$, $c_{p,l,R134a} = 1.5\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\dot{W}_{comp}/\dot{m}_r = 34\ \text{kJ/kg}$:

$$\Delta COP = \dfrac{1.5 \times 5}{34} = 0.221 \quad \text{(i.e.,} \approx 22\%\ \text{improvement)}$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Condenser in Marine Vapour Compression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Discharge] -->|Superheated Vapour h2 T2| B[Desuperheater Zone]
    B -->|Saturated Vapour h2a Tsat| C[Condensing Zone Phase Change]
    C -->|Saturated Liquid h3a Tsat| D[Subcooler Zone]
    D -->|Subcooled Liquid h3 T3| E[Expansion Valve Inlet]
    F[Seawater Inlet Tcwin] -->|Counterflow Path| G[Tube Side of Condenser]
    G -->|Heated Seawater Tcwout| H[Seawater Discharge to Sea Chest]
    I[Fouling Layer Rf] -->|Thermal Resistance| G
    J[Overall U value UA] -->|Drives Heat Transfer| G
```

**Node-Key (read with the diagram above):**
- `A` → Compressor outlet (high-pressure superheated refrigerant vapour)
- `B` → Sensible cooling zone (temperature drops, no phase change)
- `C` → Latent condensation zone (temperature constant, massive heat flux)
- `D` → Subcooling zone (further temperature drop of liquid)
- `E` → Outlet to expansion device
- `F to H` → Cooling seawater loop (counterflow)
- `I, J` → Real-world non-idealities (fouling and finite $U$)

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — P-h Cycle Showing Condenser

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[State 1: Saturated Vapour Evap Exit] -->|Isentropic Compression h1 to h2| P2[State 2: Superheated Vapour Comp Discharge]
    P2 -->|Constant Pressure Heat Rejection h2 to h3| P3[State 3: Subcooled Liquid Cond Exit]
    P3 -->|Throttling h3 to h4| P4[State 4: Two Phase Mix Expansion Exit]
    P4 -->|Constant Pressure Heat Absorption h4 to h1| P1
    subgraph CONDENSER_REGION
      P2
      P3
    end
    subgraph EVAPORATOR_REGION
      P4
      P1
    end
```

**Explanation of P-h regions:**
- The **condenser region** spans states 2 → 3 along an **isobar** (constant pressure $P_c$).
- The horizontal segment represents **latent heat rejection** at constant temperature and pressure.
- The downward-right diagonal segments represent **sensible cooling** (desuperheat and subcooling).

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Condenser Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Select Marine Condenser] --> S1{Heat Duty Qcond kW}
    S1 -->|Q below 50| S2{Seawater Available?}
    S1 -->|Q 50 to 500| S3{Compactness Required?}
    S1 -->|Q above 500| S4{New Build or Retrofit?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S5A[Use Brazed Plate HX]
    S2 -->|No| S5B[Use Air Cooled with Marine Coated Coils]
    S3 -->|Yes| S6A[Use Gasketed Plate HX Titanium]
    S3 -->|No| S6B[Use Shell and Tube Cu Ni]
    S4 -->|New Build| S7A[Use Shell and Tube Cu Ni 90 10]

```
