# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the term "Heat Rejection Factor" of a condenser. Why is subcooling beneficial in a marine refrigeration system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Heat Rejection Factor (HRF)** of a condenser is the ratio of the heat rejected in the condenser to the net refrigeration effect:

$$\text{HRF} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{RE} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_1 - h_4}$$

**Significance:** For an ideal cycle, HRF = COP + 1. A typical marine R-134a system has HRF ≈ 1.25 to 1.35.

**Subcooling Benefits (2 marks, any two):**
1. **Increases refrigeration effect** by lowering $h_4 = h_{f@P_c} - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sub}$, expanding the useful cooling capacity.
2. **Prevents vapour formation** in the expansion valve, ensuring stable throttling and preventing flash-gas erosion.
3. **Improves COP** by a measurable margin (e.g., 5 K subcooling → ~20 % COP gain in small marine plants).
4. **Reduces risk of liquid slugging** at compressor suction during transients.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Explain why a shell-and-tube condenser with seawater flowing inside the tubes is preferred over a design where refrigerant flows inside the tubes, in a marine plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **1. Ease of Cleaning** | Seawater contains biological growth, silt, and marine fouling. Tubes can be **brush-cleaned in-situ** without opening the refrigerant side. (1 mark) |
| **2. Corrosion Control** | Seawater contacts only the tube material (Cu-Ni or Ti), making replacement easier and avoiding expensive shell-material upgrades. (1 mark) |
| **3. Higher Tube-Side Velocity** | Seawater is more viscous and benefits from the higher heat-transfer coefficient achievable in smaller-bore tubes. (1 mark) |
| **4. Safety** | In case of tube leak, refrigerant escapes into the seawater (low-pressure side) rather than seawater contaminating the refrigerant charge. |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**A marine R-134a refrigeration plant has a refrigeration capacity of 180 kW. The compressor discharge state is at $h_2 = 285\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (superheated vapour) and the condenser exit state is at $h_3 = 108\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (subcooled liquid). Seawater enters the condenser at $30^\circ C$ and leaves at $38^\circ C$. Take $c_{p,sw} = 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ and the overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 1800\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. The condenser operates in counterflow arrangement.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Determine the condenser heat duty, the mass flow rate of refrigerant, and the mass flow rate of seawater.**

**(ii) The compressor work input is 36 kW. Verify the energy balance and find the COP of the cycle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Condenser heat duty** (using compressor work from part ii hint — first law approach):

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp} = 180 + 36 = 216\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Refrigerant mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_r = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{h_2 - h_3} = \dfrac{216}{285 - 108} = \dfrac{216}{177}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_r = 1.220\ \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 3: Seawater mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} = \dfrac{216}{3.99 \times (38 - 30)} = \dfrac{216}{31.92}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{sw} = 6.77\ \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 4: COP:**

$$\text{COP} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} = \dfrac{180}{36} = 5.0$$

**Step 5: Energy balance check:**

$$\dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) = 1.220 \times 177 = 215.94 \approx 216\ \text{kW} \checkmark$$

$$\dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw} = 6.77 \times 3.99 \times 8 = 215.99 \approx 216\ \text{kW} \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Calculate the LMTD of the condenser. Assume the refrigerant condenses at $40^\circ C$ and is subcooled to $35^\circ C$ at exit.**

**(ii) Find the required heat transfer area. Use a correction factor $F_t = 0.95$ for a 2-shell-pass, 4-tube-pass configuration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine LMTD.**

Given counterflow:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 40 - 38 = 2^\circ C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 35 - 30 = 5^\circ C$

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{2 - 5}{\ln(2/5)} = \dfrac{-3}{-0.9163}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lmtd} = 3.27^\circ C}$$

**Step 2: Corrected LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_{lmtd,corr} = F_t \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd} = 0.95 \times 3.27 = 3.11^\circ C$$

**Step 3: Required heat transfer area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd,corr}} = \dfrac{216{,}000}{1800 \times 3.11}$$

$$\boxed{A = 38.6\ \text{m}^2}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**A marine ammonia (R-717) refrigeration system for a fish-drying plant has the following data:**

- Evaporator temperature: $T_{evap} = -10^\circ C$
- Condensing temperature: $T_{cond} = 35^\circ C$
- Refrigerant mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_r = 0.50\ \text{kg/s}$
- Enthalpy at compressor inlet: $h_1 = 1440\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour)
- Enthalpy at compressor discharge: $h_2 = 1650\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (superheated)
- Enthalpy at condenser exit: $h_3 = 320\ \text{kJ/kg}$ (subcooled)
- Enthalpy after expansion valve: $h_4 = 320\ \text{kJ/kg}$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Compute the COP of the cycle.**
**(ii) Find the condenser heat duty and the evaporator cooling load.**
**(iii) Determine the heat rejection factor of the condenser.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: COP calculation.**

$$\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{\dot{W}_{comp}/\dot{m}_r} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 1440 - 320 = 1120\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{W}_{comp}/\dot{m}_r = h_2 - h_1 = 1650 - 1440 = 210\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP} = \dfrac{1120}{210} = 5.33}$$

**Step 2: Evaporator cooling load.**

$$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_r \cdot RE = 0.50 \times 1120 = 560\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Condenser heat duty.**

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) = 0.50 \times (1650 - 320) = 0.50 \times 1330$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{cond} = 665\ \text{kW}}$$

**Step 4: Verification by cycle energy balance:**

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} - \dot{Q}_{evap} = 665 - 560 = 105\ \text{kW} \approx \dot{W}_{comp} = 0.50 \times 210 = 105\ \text{kW} \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Heat Rejection Factor.**

$$\text{HRF} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{RE \cdot \dot{m}_r} = \dfrac{1330}{1120} = 1.188$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) The condenser uses a shell-and-tube design with seawater ($c_{p,sw} = 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$) entering at $28^\circ C$ and leaving at $34^\circ C$. Calculate the required seawater mass flow rate and its volumetric flow rate (assume $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$).**

**(ii) The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 2000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Assuming the refrigerant condenses at constant temperature $T_{sat} = 35^\circ C$ and the seawater-side outlet temperature gives $\Delta T_1 = 7^\circ C$ and $\Delta T_2 = 1^\circ C$, find the LMTD and the required heat transfer area.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Seawater mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})} = \dfrac{665}{3.99 \times (34 - 28)} = \dfrac{665}{23.94}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{sw} = 27.78\ \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow rate.**

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{27.78}{1025} = 0.0271\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{sw} = 97.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

**Step 3: LMTD calculation.**

Given: $\Delta T_1 = 7^\circ C$, $\Delta T_2 = 1^\circ C$ (counterflow).

$$\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{7 - 1}{\ln(7/1)} = \dfrac{6}{1.9459}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lmtd} = 3.08^\circ C}$$

**Step 4: Required heat transfer area.**

$$A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}} = \dfrac{665{,}000}{2000 \times 3.08}$$

$$\boxed{A = 107.95\ \text{m}^2 \approx 108\ \text{m}^2}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Condenser Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Sign Error in LMTD:** Students often forget the absolute value / log convention. Always ensure $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ before taking ln. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $C_{min}$ Selection:** For condensers with phase change, ε-NTU method needs modification. Use LMTD method with phase-change correction factor instead. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 3. **Missing Fouling Factor:** Marine seawater is dirty. Always subtract $R_f$ from the design area calculation: $\dfrac{1}{U_{design}} = \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_f$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting $c_{p,sw} \neq c_{p,fw}$:** Salinity raises specific heat. Use $c_{p,sw} = 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, not 4.186. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Unit Mismatch:** $\dot{Q}$ in kW vs W. Convert before dividing: $\dot{Q}_{kW} \times 1000 = \dot{Q}_W$ when $U$ is in W/m²K. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 6. **HRF Definition:** Some students write HRF = $\dot{Q}_{cond} / \dot{Q}_{evap}$ (wrong) — correct is $\dot{Q}_{cond} / RE$. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Condenser **rejects** heat (it is the heat sink): $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3) = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}$
- ✅ Three internal zones: **Desuperheater → Condenser → Subcooler**
- ✅ Latent heat $h_{fg}$ dominates (~80–90 % of total duty)
- ✅ Marine condensers are typically **shell-and-tube with Cu-Ni tubes**, seawater on tube side
- ✅ Plate heat exchangers are increasingly used in modern marine plants for **compactness**
- ✅ Air-cooled condensers are unsuitable for tropical marine service due to **salt-spray fouling**
- ✅ Key design parameter: **Approach temperature** $\Delta T_{approach} = T_{cond} - T_{cw,out} \ge 5^\circ C$
- ✅ Subcooling (3–5 K) improves COP and prevents flash gas at expansion valve
- ✅ **LMTD method** (not ε-NTU) is used for condensers with phase change
- ✅ **Fouling factor** $R_f$ for seawater must be included in $U$ calculation
- ✅ Seawater $c_{p,sw} = 3.99\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ (NOT freshwater 4.186)
- ✅ HRF = $\dot{Q}_{cond}/RE$ and equals COP + 1 for an ideal cycle
- ✅ Higher $T_{cond}$ → lower COP, higher compressor discharge pressure
- ✅ Always check energy balance: $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{evap} + \dot{W}_{comp}$ (within rounding)
- ✅ Counterflow gives higher $\Delta T_{lmtd}$ and more compact design
- ✅ Common exam pitfall: forget to take absolute value in LMTD formula

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **evaporator** (also termed the *vaporiser* or *cooling coil*) is the low-pressure, low-temperature **heat exchanger** of a vapor-compression refrigeration system in which the **liquid refrigerant absorbs heat** ($Q_e$) from the substance to be cooled and undergoes a **phase change** from a saturated (or sub-cooled) liquid to a **saturated (or superheated) vapor** at essentially constant pressure. In a marine context, the evaporator extracts heat from spaces such as **provision rooms (reefer chambers)**, **chilled water circuits**, **air-handling units (AHUs)** of the ship's HVAC plant, and **cold rooms for fish/meat storage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The evaporator is classified as one of the **four essential components** (compressor, condenser, expansion device, evaporator) of a vapor-compression cycle. The refrigerant entering the evaporator is a **low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture** (typically with a quality $x \approx 0.15$ to $0.25$ for DX coils and $x \approx 0.0$ for flooded chillers), and it leaves as **low-pressure vapor** (saturated or slightly superheated by 4–8 °C to ensure no liquid carry-over to the compressor).

Mathematically, the steady-state energy balance on the evaporator (neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming adiabatic shell) is given by the first law of thermodynamics:

$$
\dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_1 - h_4) = \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{air/water}\,(c_p)\,(T_{in} - T_{out})
$$

where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy of refrigerant **leaving** the evaporator (saturated/superheated vapor, **kJ/kg**)
- $h_4$ = enthalpy of refrigerant **entering** the evaporator (two-phase mixture after expansion valve, **kJ/kg**)
- $\dot{Q}_e$ = rate of heat absorption (refrigeration effect), measured in **kW** (or TR – tonnes of refrigeration)
- $\dot{m}_{ref}$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant, **kg/s**

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Convention:** In KTU's Refrigeration of Ships syllabus, the evaporator of a **chilled-water plant** is often called a **"chiller"** or **"brine cooler"** (when using brine/ethylene glycol on the secondary side), and the unit transferring heat from the air directly is called a **"cooling coil"** or **"DX unit."** Both share identical thermodynamic principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweaty Cold-Pipe" Analogy

Imagine pulling a cold bottle of water out of a refrigerator on a hot, humid day in the ship's galley. The bottle's surface immediately becomes wet (sweats). Why? Because the surrounding warm, humid air **gives up its heat** to the cold bottle surface, and the moisture in the air **condenses** on the bottle.

Now flip the analogy: instead of the air losing heat to the bottle, **the refrigerant inside a pipe loses its coldness to the air around it**. The refrigerant enters the pipe as a cold liquid and boils away (evaporates) by absorbing heat from the air. **The evaporator pipe is essentially a "sweating bottle in reverse"** — the cold refrigerant "sweats" by boiling, and the air around it loses heat and becomes colder.

### Why is this critical in ships?
- A ship's provision room must remain at **+2 °C to +5 °C** (for vegetables/dairy) or **−18 °C to −25 °C** (for frozen meat/fish).
- The evaporator continuously pulls heat **out of these spaces** to maintain the required temperature, just as the cold bottle keeps the surrounding air cool — except the evaporator is **continuously re-cooled** by the closed-loop refrigerant cycle, so the cooling never stops.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standards (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Refrigeration capacity ($\dot{Q}_e$):** Measured in **kW** or **Tonnes of Refrigeration (TR)** where **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (heat absorbed to freeze 1 ton of water in 24 h).
- **Temperature of evaporation ($T_{evap}$):** For marine AC plants, typically **5 °C to 10 °C**; for cold storage, **−25 °C to −35 °C** (with R-404A/R-507); for ultra-low temp fish holds, down to **−40 °C**.
- **Approach temperature ($T_{approach}$):** The difference between the **air inlet temperature** and the **refrigerant evaporation temperature**. Typically **8 °C to 12 °C** for AHU coils, **5 °C to 7 °C** for chilled water.
- **Latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** For **R-134a at 5 °C, $h_{fg} \approx 196$ kJ/kg**; for **R-404A at −30 °C, $h_{fg} \approx 156$ kJ/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Classification Note:** Det Norske Veritas (DNV), Lloyd's Register, and American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) require that **evaporators on cargo refrigeration plants** be fitted with **pressure relief valves**, **liquid-level indicators** (for flooded types), and **automatic defrost cycles** to prevent ice build-up that would impair heat transfer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Role in the Vapor-Compression Cycle

The evaporator is the **low-pressure side heat sink** of the cycle. Its operational sequence in steady state:

1. **Refrigerant entry:** A two-phase mixture (state 4) at low pressure $P_{evap}$ and quality $x_4$ enters the **distributor** (DX system) or the **shell** (flooded system) after passing through the **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)** or **electronic expansion valve (EEV)**.
2. **Boiling / Phase change:** As the refrigerant flows along the tubes, it absorbs heat $\dot{Q}_e$ from the external fluid (air, water, or brine). The liquid portion progressively vaporizes. The process is **isobaric and isothermal** for the saturation portion.
3. **Superheating (post-evaporation):** To guarantee that **no liquid droplets** reach the compressor suction port (which would cause **liquid slugging** and mechanical damage), the refrigerant is superheated by **4 °C to 8 °C** beyond saturation.
4. **Refrigerant exit:** Superheated vapor (state 1) leaves the evaporator and enters the **suction line** of the compressor.

## 2.2 Classification of Marine Evaporators

### (A) Based on Refrigerant Feed Mechanism

| Type | Description | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Expansion (DX) / Dry Expansion** | Refrigerant enters as a two-phase mixture; only a portion evaporates inside the tubes. Evaporation completes before the outlet. | Cabin AC units, small cold rooms |
| **Flooded Evaporator** | Shell filled with liquid refrigerant; tubes carry the secondary fluid (brine/water). Boiling occurs on the **outside** of the tubes. | Large cargo refrigeration, fish holds |
| **Liquid-Overfeed / Recirculating** | Pumped circulation of refrigerant at 2–4 times the evaporation rate. Higher tube-side velocity → higher heat transfer coefficient. | Modern container-ship reefer plants |
| **Direct-Expansion Plate Evaporator** | Refrigerant flows between welded plates. Compact, high $\dot{Q}_e$ per unit volume. | Modular AC chillers, hygienic cold rooms |

### (B) Based on Secondary Fluid

| Type | Heat Source Side | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Air-Cooling Coil (DX coil)** | Air blown over finned tubes by a fan | Cabin AC, provision rooms |
| **Chilled-Water (Brine) Cooler** | Water/glycol mixture inside tubes, refrigerant boils outside | Centralized AC plant |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger Evaporator** | Plate pack with refrigerant on one side, fluid on other | Compact modular systems |

## 2.3 Heat Transfer in Evaporators — Governing Equations

The **overall heat transfer rate** through an evaporator is governed by the classic **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$
\dot{Q}_e = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, **W/m²·K** (typical: 25–50 for air coils, 500–1500 for flooded water coolers)
- $A$ = heat transfer area, **m²**
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log mean temperature difference, **K**

For **phase change** on one side, $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ simplifies. If the secondary fluid is air with single-pass cross-flow and the refrigerant is at constant $T_{evap}$:

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{(T_{a,in} - T_{evap}) - (T_{a,out} - T_{evap})}{\ln\left(\dfrac{T_{a,in} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,out} - T_{evap}}\right)}
$$

Since $T_{evap}$ is essentially constant (isothermal evaporation):

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{T_{a,in} - T_{a,out}}{\ln\left(\dfrac{T_{a,in} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,out} - T_{evap}}\right)}
$$

For **counter-flow heat exchangers** with one side isothermal:

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Practice:** For finned coil analysis, the **fin efficiency $\eta_f$** and **surface efficiency $\eta_o$** must be included. The total $\dot{Q}_e$ becomes:

$$
\dot{Q}_e = U_o \, A_o \, F \, \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

where $F$ = correction factor for cross-flow and $U_o$ is based on the outside (air-side) area.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration effect (1st law on evaporator) | **kW** |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{sec}\,c_p\,(T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Heat absorbed from secondary fluid | **kW** |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_e = U\,A\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Newton's law of cooling | **kW** |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_e = U_o\,A_o\,F\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Air-side finned coil with $F$-correction | **kW** |
| 5 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | **K** |
| 6 | $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW} = 211\,\text{kJ/min}$ | Standard refrigeration ton | — |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance (refrigerator) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{ref} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Required refrigerant mass flow | **kg/s** |
| 9 | $T_{approach} = T_{air,in} - T_{evap}$ | Approach temperature (coil sizing) | **K** or **°C** |
| 10 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(m\,r_c)}{m\,r_c}$ | Fin efficiency, $m=\sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{k\,t}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\dot{Q}_e = h_{fg}\,\dot{m}_{ref} + c_{p,v}\,\dot{m}_{ref}\,(T_{1,sup} - T_{sat})$ | Split into latent + sensible superheat | **kW** |
| 12 | $v_1 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{ref}}{\rho_1}$ | Suction line volume flow (for compressor displacement) | **m³/s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Symbol Note:** The vertical bar `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\ln(...)` notation to preserve KTU markdown rendering integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Plant on Cruise Liners:** Centralized chilled-water systems use **flooded shell-and-tube evaporators** with R-134a to deliver 6–8 °C chilled water to AHUs across all cabins.
- **Reefer Container Ships:** Each 40-ft container has its own **integral DX evaporator** (R-404A) with individual temperature control for perishable cargo.
- **Fish Factory Vessels:** Multi-temperature evaporators cool brine to **−10 °C** for rapid fish freezing; plate evaporators are favored for **hygienic easy-clean surfaces**.
- **LNG Carriers (BOG handling):** Boil-off gas is re-liquefied using **nitrogen or mixed-refrigerant evaporators** operating at cryogenic temperatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defrosting in Marine Evaporators:** Air-side coils operating below 0 °C accumulate frost, which **reduces $U$ by up to 50%** if not removed. Marine systems use **hot-gas defrost** (reversing cycle), **electric defrost**, or **water wash** (common in reefer containers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance on a Steady-Flow Evaporator

We apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to the control volume enclosing the evaporator, with the following assumptions (standard KTU examination assumptions):

- **Adiabatic shell:** $\dot{Q}_{loss} = 0$ (neglect heat to surroundings; this is the *useful* heat absorbed).
- **No work transfer:** $\dot{W} = 0$ (no shaft work; no electrical work).
- **Negligible $\Delta KE$ and $\Delta PE$**: velocities are low; no elevation change.
- **Steady state:** no time-dependent accumulation.

The SFEE states:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\,(h + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\,(h + \tfrac{V^2}{2} + gz)_{in}
$$

**Step 1: Apply assumptions.**

With $\dot{W} = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$, and $\dot{Q}_{loss} = 0$:

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_{out} - h_{in})
$$

**Step 2: Identify states.**

For the refrigerant: $h_{in} = h_4$ (state 4, exit of expansion valve), $h_{out} = h_1$ (state 1, exit of evaporator / inlet to compressor).

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_1 - h_4)
$$

**Step 3: Identify secondary fluid heat absorption.**

Heat lost by air (or water/brine) = heat gained by refrigerant:

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{sec}\,c_p\,(T_{sec,in} - T_{sec,out})
$$

**Step 4: Equate both expressions (1st law consistency).**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_1 - h_4) = \dot{m}_{sec}\,c_p\,(T_{sec,in} - T_{sec,out})
$$

**Step 5: Split refrigerant enthalpy change into latent + sensible.**

$$
h_1 - h_4 = (h_1 - h_f) + (h_f - h_4)
$$

Since at state 4 the refrigerant is a two-phase mixture, $h_4 = h_f + x_4\,h_{fg}$. So $h_f - h_4 = -x_4\,h_{fg}$, and:

$$
h_1 - h_4 = (h_1 - h_f) - x_4\,h_{fg} = (1 - x_4)\,h_{fg} + c_{p,v}\,(T_1 - T_{sat})
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{ref}\,\left[(1 - x_4)\,h_{fg} + c_{p,v}\,(T_1 - T_{sat})\right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most important equation for KTU Part-B (14-mark) numerical problems. For a **DX system with $x_4 \approx 0.20$** at the inlet, the latent portion dominates (~95% of $\dot{Q}_e$).

## 3.2 Derivation: LMTD for an Evaporator with Cross-Flow Air

**Given:**
- Air enters at $T_{a,i}$, leaves at $T_{a,o}$.
- Refrigerant evaporates at constant $T_{evap}$ (single-phase superheat neglected for simplicity).

**Step 1: Heat absorbed by an elemental area $dA$.**

$$
d\dot{Q} = U\,(T_a - T_{evap})\,dA
$$

**Step 2: Air-side energy balance.**

$$
d\dot{Q} = -\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}\,dT_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad dA = -\frac{\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}}{U}\,\frac{dT_a}{T_a - T_{evap}}
$$

**Step 3: Integrate from inlet ($T_{a,i}$, $A = 0$) to outlet ($T_{a,o}$, $A = A$).**

$$
\int_0^A dA = -\frac{\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}}{U}\int_{T_{a,i}}^{T_{a,o}}\frac{dT_a}{T_a - T_{evap}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}}{U}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_{a,i} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,o} - T_{evap}}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Total heat transfer.**

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}\,(T_{a,i} - T_{a,o})
$$

**Step 5: Substitute $A$ back into $\dot{Q}_e = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$.**

$$
\dot{Q}_e = U \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}}{U}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_{a,i} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,o} - T_{evap}}\right) \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}\,(T_{a,i} - T_{a,o}) = \dot{m}_a\,c_{p,a}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_{a,i} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,o} - T_{evap}}\right) \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $\Delta T_{LMTD}$.**

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{T_{a,i} - T_{a,o}}{\ln\left(\dfrac{T_{a,i} - T_{evap}}{T_{a,o} - T_{evap}}\right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Identical to standard LMTD** because $T_{evap}$ is constant. The numerator is the **range** of the air, and the denominator contains the **terminal temperature differences**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem Statement:**
A marine air-conditioning evaporator uses **R-134a** and operates at an evaporation pressure of **2.5 bar** ($T_{evap} \approx -5$ °C, $h_{fg} = 197$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 247$ kJ/kg at saturation). Refrigerant enters the evaporator with quality $x_4 = 0.18$ and leaves as saturated vapor. The system provides **15 TR** of cooling. Calculate:
1. The refrigerant mass flow rate.
2. The compressor suction volume flow rate (specific volume $v_1 = 0.08$ m³/kg).

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_e = 15 \text{ TR} = 15 \times 3.517 = 52.755 \text{ kW}$
- $h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}$
- $h_1 = h_g = 247$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = 0.08$ m³/kg

**Step 1: Compute $h_4$.**

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}
$$

We need $h_f$. From R-134a saturation tables at $-5$ °C: $h_f = 31$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 247$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 197$ kJ/kg (approximately, varying with source).

$$
h_4 = 31 + 0.18 \times 197 = 31 + 35.46 = 66.46 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2: Enthalpy change.**

$$
h_1 - h_4 = 247 - 66.46 = 180.54 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{52.755}{180.54} = 0.2922 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Suction volume flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_1 = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot v_1 = 0.2922 \times 0.08 = 0.02338 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_1 = 0.02338 \times 60 = 1.403 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $\dot{V}_1$ is the **theoretical displacement** required; actual compressor displacement must be divided by the **volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$** (typically 0.65–0.80 for reciprocating marine compressors).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DX vs Flooded Evaporator

| Parameter | Direct Expansion (DX) | Flooded |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant inventory | Low | High |
| Heat transfer coefficient ($h$) | Moderate (500–1500 W/m²·K) | High (1500–3500 W/m²·K) |
| Compressor liquid risk | Higher (needs superheat) | Low (dry suction via accumulator) |
| Complexity | Simple | Needs float valve, surge drum, pump |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Typical use | Cabin AC, small cold rooms | Cargo reefer plants, large chillers |
| Response to load change | Fast | Slower (thermal inertia) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Functional Position of Evaporator in Vapor-Compression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    comp1[Compressor Low Pressure Side] --> comp2[Compressor High Pressure Side]
    comp2 --> cond1[Condenser: Rejects Heat to Seawater]
    cond1 --> cond2[Condenser: Sub-cools Liquid]
    cond2 --> txv1[Thermostatic Expansion Valve: Throttles]
    txv1 --> evap1[Evaporator: Absorbs Heat from Air]
    evap1 --> evap2[Evaporator: Superheats Vapor 4 to 8 C]
    evap2 --> comp1
    evap1 -. heat from .-> air1[Provision Room or Cabin Air]
    air1 -. returns cooler .-> evap1
    cond1 -. heat to .-> sw1[Seawater Cooling Circuit]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Construction of a Flooded Shell-and-Tube Marine Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet1[Refrigerant Inlet Two Phase State 4] --> shell1[Lower Shell Liquid Level]
    shell1 --> boil[Boiling on Outside of Tubes]
    boil --> vap[Vapor Space Upper Shell]
    vap --> baffle1[Demister Pad]
    baffle1 --> outlet1[Superheated Vapor Outlet State 1 to Compressor]
    water1[Chilled Water Inlet] --> tube1[Tube Bundle Inside Shell]
    tube1 --> water2[Chilled Water Outlet]
    water1 -. cold from .-> evap1[Expansion Valve TXV]
    evap1 -. feeds refrigerant .-> inlet1
    levelctrl1[Float Valve Maintains Liquid Level] --> shell1
    saf1[Pressure Relief Valve] --> vap
    saf2[Liquid Level Sight Glass] --> shell1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Heat and Mass Transfer Phenomena in a DX Finned Coil

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    airIn[Warm Humid Air from Cabin] --> fan1[Forced Draft Fan]
    fan1 --> coil1[DX Finned Coil Cold Surface]
    coil1 --> dehu[Moisture Condensation on Fins]
    dehu --> drain1[Condensate Drain Pan]
    coil1 --> cool[Cool Dry Air Return to Cabin]
    refIn[Liquid Refrigerant from TXV] --> dist1[Distributor Header]
    dist1 --> tubes1[Internal Tubes Boiling]
    tubes1 --> suph[Superheat Region 4 to 8 C]
    suph --> refOut[Superheated Vapor to Compressor]
    coil1 --> frost1[Frost Accumulation Below 0 C]
    frost1 --> defrost1[Defrost Cycle: Hot Gas or Electric]
    defrost1 --> coil1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Refrigerant Phase Changes Inside the Evaporator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State4Inlet
    State4Inlet: State 4 Two Phase x = 0.15 to 0.25
    State4Inlet --> State4a: Single Phase Liquid Region
    State4a --> State4b: Sensible Cooling of Liquid
    State4b --> StateSat: Reaches Saturation Temperature
    StateSat --> StateEvap: Phase Change Latent Heat Absorption
    StateEvap --> StateDry: Quality Reaches x = 1.0
    StateDry --> State1Out: Superheat 4 to 8 C Added
    State1Out: State 1 Superheated Vapor to Compressor
    State1Out --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Tree: Evaporator Selection for Marine Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root1[Marine Evaporator Selection] --> branch1[Air Cooling Coil]
    root1 --> branch2[Liquid Chiller]
    branch1 --> type1[DX Finned Coil]
    branch1 --> type2[Chilled Water Air Handling Unit]
    branch2 --> type3[Shell and Tube Flooded]
    branch2 --> type4[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    branch2 --> type5[Brazed Plate]
    type1 --> use1[Cabin AC, Small Provision Room]
    type2 --> use2[Centralized AC Plant]
    type3 --> use3[Reefer Cargo Plant, Fish Hold]
    type4 --> use4[Modular Chiller Unit]
    type5 --> use5[Compact AC, Hygienic Cold Room]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an evaporator. State its function in a marine vapor-compression refrigeration system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An evaporator is a heat exchanger in which the liquid refrigerant, at low pressure and low temperature, absorbs heat from the substance to be cooled and evaporates, changing phase from liquid to vapor.
- **Function (1.5 marks):** In a marine vapor-compression system, it provides the refrigeration effect ($\dot{Q}_e$) by absorbing heat from the medium to be cooled (e.g., cabin air, provision room air, chilled water). It maintains the desired low temperature in refrigerated spaces and delivers low-pressure superheated vapor to the compressor suction.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a Direct Expansion (DX) and a Flooded evaporator. Give one marine application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DX Evaporator (1.5 marks):** Refrigerant enters as a low-quality two-phase mixture and completely evaporates inside the tubes before leaving as superheated vapor. Liquid inventory is low. Used in **cabin AC units and small provision rooms**.
- **Flooded Evaporator (1.5 marks):** The shell is filled with liquid refrigerant; boiling occurs on the outer surface of tubes carrying water/brine. Higher heat transfer coefficient and refrigerant inventory. Used in **reefer cargo plants and large fish-hold refrigeration**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Examination Note:** KTU Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two full questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **flooded shell-and-tube evaporator** used in marine cargo refrigeration plants. Discuss the role of the **float valve** and **accumulator** in its operation.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - Horizontal cylindrical shell with **tube bundle** inside.
   - Refrigerant (e.g., R-404A, R-717) fills the shell to a controlled level.
   - Secondary fluid (chilled water or brine) flows inside the tubes.
   - Fitted with **liquid-level sight glass**, **pressure relief valve**, **float valve**, and **suction accumulator**.

2. **Working (2 marks):**
   - Liquid refrigerant boils on the **outer surface** of the tubes at constant saturation temperature.
   - Boiling absorbs latent heat $\dot{Q}_e$ from the secondary fluid.
   - Vapor rises to the upper shell, passes through a **demister pad**, and leaves as **superheated vapor** (via the suction accumulator) to the compressor.

3. **Role of float valve and accumulator (2 marks):**
   - **Float valve** maintains a constant liquid level in the shell by admitting refrigerant from the expansion device as boiling depletes it.
   - **Suction accumulator** ensures only dry vapor reaches the compressor, preventing **liquid slugging**.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]:** A marine evaporator handles R-134a with the following operating data:
- Refrigeration capacity = 20 TR
- Enthalpy at inlet: $h_4 = 64$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy at outlet: $h_1 = 247$ kJ/kg
- Specific volume at outlet: $v_1 = 0.08$ m³/kg
- Volumetric efficiency of compressor: $\eta_v = 0.75$

Calculate:
1. Mass flow rate of refrigerant.
2. Theoretical compressor displacement.
3. Actual compressor displacement.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_e = 20 \times 3.517 = 70.34$ kW
- $h_1 - h_4 = 247 - 64 = 183$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = 0.08$ m³/kg
- $\eta_v = 0.75$

**Step 1: Mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{70.34}{183} = 0.3844 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical suction volume flow rate.**

$$
\dot{V}_{1,theor} = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot v_1 = 0.3844 \times 0.08 = 0.03075 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{1,theor} = 0.03075 \times 60 = 1.845 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 3: Actual compressor displacement.**

$$
\dot{V}_{1,actual} = \frac{\dot{V}_{1,theor}}{\eta_v} = \frac{1.845}{0.75} = 2.460 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.384$ kg/s
2. Theoretical displacement = **1.845 m³/min**
3. Actual displacement = **2.46 m³/min**

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]:** Explain with a block diagram the **Direct Expansion (DX) air-cooling coil** used in a ship's cabin air-conditioning unit. Discuss the need for **superheating** at the evaporator outlet.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Construction & Working (4 marks):**
   - DX coil consists of **finned copper tubes** with a refrigerant distributor at the inlet and a suction line at the outlet.
   - Air is forced over the coil by a **centrifugal fan** (or backward-curved fan for marine AHUs).
   - Liquid refrigerant at low quality (x ≈ 0.20) enters the distributor, evaporates inside the tubes, and leaves as superheated vapor.
   - Heat is absorbed from the air: sensible cooling plus dehumidification (moisture condenses on the fins and drains away).
   - The cooled, dehumidified air is delivered to the cabin.

2. **Need for Superheating (3 marks):**
   - To guarantee that refrigerant entering the compressor is **fully vapor**, preventing **liquid slugging** that can damage compressor valves, pistons, and bearings.
   - Ensures **stable compressor operation** and prevents oil washing from the crankcase.
   - Achieved by **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)** modulation based on suction superheat.
   - Typical superheat setting: **4 °C to 8 °C** above saturation.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]:** A marine chilled-water evaporator (flooded type) handles **R-134a** at an evaporation temperature of **5 °C**. Sea-chilled water enters the evaporator at **12 °C** and leaves at **7 °C** at a flow rate of **30,000 kg/h**. For R-134a at 5 °C: $h_{fg} = 196$ kJ/kg, $h_f = 47$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 243$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. The refrigerant enters with quality $x_4 = 0.15$. Calculate:
1. Heat transfer rate $\dot{Q}_e$.
2. Refrigerant mass flow rate.
3. Refrigeration effect in TR.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Heat transfer rate.**

$$
\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_w\,c_{p,w}\,(T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}) = \frac{30000}{3600} \times 4.18 \times (12 - 7)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_e = 8.333 \times 4.18 \times 5 = 174.17 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Refrigerant inlet enthalpy.**

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 47 + 0.15 \times 196 = 47 + 29.4 = 76.4 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate (assuming $h_1 = h_g = 243$ kJ/kg, saturated vapor exit).**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{174.17}{243 - 76.4} = \frac{174.17}{166.6} = 1.046 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to TR.**

$$
\dot{Q}_e \text{ in TR} = \frac{174.17}{3.517} = 49.52 \text{ TR}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $\dot{Q}_e = 174.17$ kW
2. $\dot{m}_{ref} = 1.046$ kg/s
3. Capacity = **49.52 TR**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Evaporator Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert TR → kW** (multiply by 3.517). Many students write $\dot{Q}_e = 20$ directly into the mass flow equation, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $h_1$ (compressor inlet) with $h_g$ (saturated vapor).** If superheat is given, $h_1 = h_g + c_{p,v}\,(T_1 - T_{sat})$, NOT just $h_g$.
> 3. **Using $h_1 - h_4$ with $h_4$ as $h_f$ only** (ignoring the inlet quality $x_4$). Always compute $h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}$ when $x_4 > 0$.
> 4. **Sign convention error:** Heat absorbed is **positive**; the refrigeration effect uses $h_1 - h_4$ (outlet − inlet for refrigerant), not the reverse.
> 5. **Neglecting $\eta_v$** when asked for actual compressor displacement — direct deduction of 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong approach temperature:** Some students subtract $T_{evap}$ from $T_{air,out}$ instead of $T_{air,in}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evaporators**

- **Definition:** Heat exchanger that absorbs heat $\dot{Q}_e$ by evaporating liquid refrigerant at low pressure.
- **Energy equation:** $\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_1 - h_4) = \dot{m}_{sec}\,c_p\,(T_{in} - T_{out})$
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (constant, must be memorized).
- **Two main types:** DX (dry expansion) and Flooded — know the difference in **boiling location**, **refrigerant inventory**, and **application**.
- **Superheat of 4–8 °C** is essential at the evaporator outlet to protect the compressor.
- **Approach temperature** = $T_{air,in} - T_{evap}$; usually 8–12 °C for AC coils.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — for isothermal refrigerant side, this simplifies.
- **Finned coil analysis:** $U_o\,A_o\,F\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$; remember the $F$ correction factor for cross-flow.
- **Defrosting** is mandatory for coils below 0 °C; **hot-gas defrost** is the marine industry standard.
- **Marine classifications:** DNV, Lloyd's, ABS require **safety valves, level indicators, and defrost systems** on cargo evaporators.
- **Flooded evaporators need a float valve** for liquid level and an **accumulator** for dry suction.
- **Refrigerant-side enthalpy change** has two parts: **latent** (dominant, ~95%) and **sensible superheat** (~5%).
- **For compressor displacement calculations:** always divide theoretical $\dot{V}_1$ by $\eta_v$ to get the actual displacement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PENST743 – Refrigeration and Air Conditioning of Ships**, a **Regulator** is defined as an automatic control device or assembly of devices that senses a process variable (pressure, temperature, humidity, or flow rate) within a marine refrigeration or air-conditioning plant and modulates the supply of refrigerant energy, refrigerant mass flow, or capacity output to maintain that variable at a predetermined set-point, despite variations in load, ambient sea conditions, or machinery duty cycle.

Marine regulators are categorized under the broader umbrella of **Refrigerant Flow Control Devices** and form the brain-and-muscle interface between the **sensed condition** and the **corrective actuation**. In shipboard installations, regulators must additionally comply with classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS) for redundancy, fail-safe operation, and shock/vibration tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Regulators studied under Module 2 primarily include **(i) Thermostatic Expansion Valves (TEV)**, **(ii) Solenoid Valves**, **(iii) Temperature/Pressure Regulating Valves**, **(iv) Capacity Control Devices**, and **(v) Safety Cut-out Switches**. Each is examined with emphasis on its operating principle, application in marine plants, and characteristic control curves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding hill road. Your **eyes** sense the slope (the *process variable*), your **brain** decides how much to press the accelerator or brake (the *controller*), and your **foot** actually applies the force (the *actuator*). A regulator in a refrigeration plant works in exactly the same way:

- The **sensor** (bulb or sensing line) is the *eye*.
- The **diaphragm/pilot mechanism** is the *brain*.
- The **valve pin and seat** is the *foot* modulating refrigerant flow.

In a ship, conditions change rapidly — engine-room heat load spikes during manoeuvring, cold-room load changes as provisions are loaded, and ambient temperature varies between tropical and polar waters. **Regulators** are what keep your refrigeration plant *on-song* through all of this, much like a thermostat keeps your home comfortable irrespective of the weather outside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often confused in exams):**
> A *regulator* **continuously modulates** the controlled variable toward a set-point, whereas a *cut-out switch* is a **two-position safety device** that simply stops/starts the compressor at fixed limits. The difference is between a *cruise-control* and a *kill-switch*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following baseline constants and standard metrics recur in regulator analysis and should be committed to memory:

- **Standard marine design condensing temperature:** $T_c = 35\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $45\,^\circ\text{C}$ (R-134a/R-404A plants).
- **Standard marine design evaporating temperature:** $T_e = -30\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $+5\,^\circ\text{C}$ depending on cargo hold temperature class.
- **Standard MMD (Maximum Working Pressure) for HFC plants:** typically **17 bar (R-134a)** and **21 bar (R-404A)**.
- **Thermostatic Superheat set-point (typical):** **4 K to 6 K** for dry-expansion marine evaporators.
- **Classification rule for safety cut-out settings:** high-pressure cut-out trips at **110 %** of design condensing pressure; low-pressure cut-out at **0.5 bar** above $0\,^\circ\text{C}$ saturation for chilled-water plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Regulators

Marine regulators covered in Module 2 are best classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) the *controlled variable* and (b) the *mode of action*.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Controlled Variable

- **Pressure Regulators** — sense refrigerant pressure; examples: High-side float, low-side float, condenser pressure regulator (CPR), evaporator pressure regulator (EPR), crankcase pressure regulator (CPR – not to be confused with condenser version; context differentiates).
- **Temperature Regulators** — sense refrigerant or space temperature; examples: Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV), room thermostat, cargo-hold temperature controller.
- **Humidity Regulators** — sense relative humidity of supply/return air; example: humidistat used in ship's accommodation air-conditioning.
- **Level Regulators** — sense refrigerant level in evaporator or receiver; example: float valve, electronic level sensor with solenoid.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Mode of Action

- **Modulating (Proportional)** — continuously varies opening, e.g., TEV, thermostatic radiator valve on chilled-water plant.
- **Two-Position (On/Off)** — fully open or fully closed, e.g., solenoid valve, thermostat contactor.
- **Step (Multi-position)** — discrete stages of capacity, e.g., cylinder unloader with three steps.
- **Safety Cut-out** — latching trip, e.g., high-pressure stat.

## 2.2 The Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) — The Heart of a Marine Plant

The TEV is the single most-tested regulator in KTU university papers. It performs three simultaneous functions:

1. **Throttles** high-pressure liquid refrigerant to low pressure.
2. **Meter**s refrigerant mass flow in response to evaporator load.
3. **Maintains** a constant superheat at the evaporator outlet (typically **4 K to 6 K**) to guarantee that **only vapour** enters the compressor — protecting it from liquid slugging.

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

- Liquid refrigerant from the condenser passes through the **filter-drier** and then reaches the TEV inlet.
- The **thermal bulb** is clamped on the suction line just after the evaporator outlet. It contains a charge (gas, liquid, or cross-critical) whose pressure $p_b$ rises/falls with the **evaporator outlet temperature**.
- The bulb pressure $p_b$ acts on top of the **diaphragm**, pushing it **downward**.
- The **evaporator pressure** $p_e$ (the *internal equaliser* sense) acts under the diaphragm, pushing it **upward**.
- A **spring** above the diaphragm supplies an adjustable pre-load that sets the desired superheat.
- The net force on the diaphragm positions the **valve pin**. If superheat rises → bulb hotter → pin opens more → more refrigerant. If superheat falls → bulb cooler → pin throttles → less refrigerant. This is a **closed-loop negative-feedback** system.

### 2.2.2 Why a "Static" Superheat Always Exists

There must always be some residual superheat — call it the **static superheat** — for the valve to be **fully closed** at zero load. Otherwise the valve would oscillate (hunt). The static superheat is the spring pre-load equivalent and is typically **3 K to 5 K**.

### 2.2.3 External Equaliser — When and Why

If the evaporator has a **large pressure drop** across its circuit (long multi-circuit coils, low-pressure chillers), the suction-line pressure at the evaporator outlet is **higher** than at the compressor suction. An internal-equaliser TEV would then overfeed the evaporator. The remedy is an **external equaliser line** tapped at the evaporator outlet — but **after** the bulb location — so that $p_e$ is sensed accurately at the coil.

## 2.3 Other Key Marine Regulators

### 2.3.1 Solenoid Valve (Solenoid Shut-off Valve)

A two-position, electrically-operated valve. In marine plants it is typically installed in the **liquid line** before the TEV. Energised coil lifts the plunger → valve opens → refrigerant flows. De-energised → spring closes → flow stops. Used for:

- **Pump-down cycle** control.
- **Defrost initiation** in refrigerated containers (reefer containers).
- **Zone shut-off** in multi-evaporator plants.

### 2.3.2 Condenser Pressure Regulator (CPR / "Head Pressure Control")

Maintains a **minimum condensing pressure** during winter operation or cold-sea voyages, when the natural condenser pressure can fall so low that the TEV loses its driving pressure differential. Two common types:

- **Pressure-Adjusting Valve** (a regulator on the liquid line).
- **Condenser Fan Cycling** or **Fan Speed Control** — common on ship's central AC condensers.
- **Hot Gas Bypass** to the condenser inlet (less common at sea due to oil-foaming risks).

### 2.3.3 Evaporator Pressure Regulator (EPR)

Maintains a **constant evaporator pressure** regardless of load. Used in **multi-temperature** cargo plants (e.g., a reefer ship carrying frozen fish at $-25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and chilled vegetables at $+5\,^\circ\text{C}$ on the same plant). The EPR regulates evaporator pressure for the warmer compartment, allowing it to operate at a higher temperature than the deeper-temperature compartment.

### 2.3.4 Capacity Regulators

Marine compressors are often regulated by:

- **Cylinder unloading** — suction-valve lifters held open to disable one or more cylinders (e.g., 4/8/12 cylinder unloading).
- **Hot gas bypass** — bypass line from discharge to suction with a modulating valve.
- **Variable-frequency drive (VFD)** — increasingly common on new-build ships for AC compressors.

### 2.3.5 Safety Cut-outs (Mandatory under SOLAS and Classification Rules)

- **High-pressure cut-out (HP)**: trips compressor at $\approx 110\,\%$ of design $p_c$.
- **Low-pressure cut-out (LP)**: trips at $p_e$ slightly above atmospheric to prevent air ingress.
- **Oil-pressure differential switch (OPDS)**: trips if oil-pump $\Delta p <$ set value (typically **1.0 bar to 1.5 bar**) for more than **60 seconds**.
- **Freeze-stat / anti-frost stat**: trips if evaporator outlet temperature falls below $0\,^\circ\text{C}$ (chilled-water plants).
- **Compressor motor overload** and **phase-failure relay**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the ones most frequently asked in KTU ESE and internal evaluations of Module 2. Memorise units and boundary conditions alongside the equation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta h_{throttle} = 0$ | Isenthalpic expansion through TEV / any throttle valve | $\Delta h = 0$, $h_3 = h_4$ |
| 2 | $\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(p_e)$ | Thermostatic superheat at evaporator outlet | **K** (Kelvin) |
| 3 | $p_b = p_e + \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{diaph}}$ | Force balance on TEV diaphragm at steady-state equilibrium | bar, N, m$^2$ |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff}\sqrt{2\rho_{liq}(p_c - p_e)}$ | Mass flow through TEV orifice | kg/s |
| 5 | $P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_2 - h_1)$ | Theoretical compressor power (Carnot-derived indicator) | kW |
| 6 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1-h_4}{h_2-h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance of the cycle | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigerating effect / cooling load | kW |
| 8 | $\text{Capacity ratio} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{L,partial}}{\dot{Q}_{L,full}}$ | Cylinder-unload / hot-gas-bypass ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $p_{HP,cutout} = 1.10 \times p_{c,design}$ | High-pressure cut-out trip setting (classification rule of thumb) | bar |
| 10 | $\text{Time lag}_{bulb} = \dfrac{m_b c_{p,b}}{h_b A_b}$ | Bulb thermal-response time constant (lower → faster response) | seconds |

## 2.5 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

In an LNG carrier or a reefer ship, regulators are the **silent guardians** of cargo integrity. A single failed TEV can:

- Allow **liquid carry-over** into the compressor → **slugging** → cracked valve plates.
- Cause **frost build-up** on the evaporator → reduced heat transfer → cargo spoilage.
- Trigger a **safety trip** in cascade → entire cargo temperature out of control.

In passenger vessels, regulators govern the **accommodation AC**, ensuring crew comfort across tropical-equatorial crossings (outside air at $35\,^\circ\text{C}$, 80 % RH) and cold North Atlantic runs (outside air at $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$, 70 % RH). Modern integrated automation systems (e.g., KONGSBERG, Wärtsilä SMAC, Alfa Laval Touch) use electronic regulators with PID algorithms, but the underlying thermodynamic principles remain identical to the legacy mechanical regulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Force-Balance Equation for the Thermostatic Expansion Valve

### 3.1.1 Statement

Derive the steady-state force-balance equation governing the position of the diaphragm in a thermostatic expansion valve and show that the static superheat is determined by the spring pre-load.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram (Mental Sketch)

The TEV diaphragm has three forces acting on it vertically:

- **$F_b$** — Bulb pressure pushing **down** ($p_b \cdot A_d$).
- **$F_e$** — Evaporator pressure pushing **up** ($p_e \cdot A_d$) for *internal equaliser*, or $(p_e \cdot A_d - p_{ext}\cdot A_{ext})$ for *external equaliser*.
- **$F_s$** — Spring force pushing **up** ($k_s \cdot x_s$), where $x_s$ is the spring compression from free length.

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Derivation

Starting with the equilibrium condition that the diaphragm is stationary (steady state), we set the net vertical force to zero:

$$
\sum F_{vertical} = 0
$$

Substituting the three forces, with **downward** taken as positive (matching the conventional diagram):

$$
F_b - F_e - F_s = 0
$$

Replacing each force by its pressure–area product:

$$
p_b \cdot A_d - p_e \cdot A_d - F_s = 0
$$

Rearranging for the bulb pressure:

$$
p_b = p_e + \dfrac{F_s}{A_d}
$$

Now express $F_s$ in terms of spring stiffness $k_s$ and its compression $x_s$:

$$
p_b = p_e + \dfrac{k_s x_s}{A_d}
$$

Converting pressures to equivalent saturation temperatures using the refrigerant's $p$–$T$ relation, and recalling that the bulb temperature $T_b$ is essentially the suction-line temperature $T_{sl}$ (good thermal contact):

$$
T_{sat}^{-1}(p_b) = T_{sat}^{-1}(p_e) + \dfrac{1}{A_d}\dfrac{dT_{sat}}{dp}\bigg\vert_{p_b} k_s x_s
$$

Defining **superheat** as:

$$
\text{SH} = T_b - T_{sat}(p_e)
$$

We obtain the **master TEV relation**:

$$
\boxed{\text{SH} = \text{SH}_{static} = \dfrac{k_s x_s}{A_d} \cdot \dfrac{dT_{sat}}{dp}\bigg\vert_{p_b}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Interpretation

- Larger **spring pre-load** $k_s x_s$ → larger static superheat set-point.
- Larger **diaphragm area** $A_d$ → smaller superheat set-point for the same spring.
- The slope $\dfrac{dT_{sat}}{dp}$ is the **inverse slope** of the saturation curve at $p_b$. For R-134a at $p_e \approx 2$ bar, $\dfrac{dT_{sat}}{dp} \approx 4\,\text{K/bar}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** For a typical marine TEV, $k_s x_s \approx 5\,\text{N}$ and $A_d \approx 200\,\text{mm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$. Hence $\dfrac{F_s}{A_d} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} = 0.25\,\text{bar}$. Multiplying by $4\,\text{K/bar}$ gives $\text{SH} \approx 1\,\text{K}$ — wait, this is below the typical 4–6 K. The discrepancy arises because the static superheat is *not* the operating superheat; the operating superheat includes **additional superheat** from the evaporator's distributed pressure drop and the thermal resistance of the bulb wall. **This is exactly the trap KTU examiners use to test conceptual depth.**

## 3.2 Derivation II: Mass Flow Through the TEV Orifice

### 3.2.1 Statement

For a TEV metering a sub-cooled or saturated liquid at inlet pressure $p_c$ to a two-phase mixture at outlet pressure $p_e$, derive an expression for the refrigerant mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{ref}$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a control volume around the TEV. Apply the **steady-flow energy equation** (SFEE) neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming no work transfer:

$$
h_{in} + \dfrac{V_{in}^2}{2} + gz_{in} + q = h_{out} + \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{2} + gz_{out} + w
$$

Simplifying ($V, z$ negligible, $q = 0$, $w = 0$):

$$
h_{in} = h_{out}
$$

This is the **isenthalpic (throttling) condition**, which is **Derivation I's prerequisite**:

$$
h_{in} = h_{out} = h_4
$$

For the **mass-flow** side, apply the compressible Bernoulli between the inlet stagnation and the orifice throat. Treating the fluid as **incompressible liquid** in the sub-cooled region (valid for most marine TEV applications):

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2\,\rho_{liq}\,(p_c - p_e)}
$$

where:

- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typical 0.6 to 0.85 for TEV orifices).
- $A_{eff}$ = effective orifice area, modulated by the valve pin lift $L$.
- $\rho_{liq}$ = density of saturated liquid at $T_c$.

### 3.2.3 Combining with Energy Equation

The orifice area $A_{eff}$ is **modulated** by the diaphragm position, which is in turn governed by the superheat (Derivation I). Substituting the linearised valve characteristic $A_{eff} = k_v \cdot (\text{SH} - \text{SH}_{static})$ for small excursions:

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d k_v (\text{SH} - \text{SH}_{static}) \sqrt{2\,\rho_{liq}\,(p_c - p_e)}
$$

This **gain term** $\sqrt{p_c - p_e}$ is what makes the **condenser-pressure regulator** so important — when $p_c$ falls during cold-sea voyages, $\dot{m}_{ref}$ falls dangerously low and the evaporator starves.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Marine Regulators

The following matrix maps a **plant problem** to its **appropriate regulator**. This is a frequently-tested KTU question (Module 2, 14-mark, often part-(b)).

| Symptom / Requirement | Recommended Regulator | Justification |
|------------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Evaporator outlet superheat drifts above set-point under partial load | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) with external equaliser | Modulates refrigerant flow to maintain 4–6 K SH |
| Condensing pressure too low in cold weather / Arctic passage | Condenser Pressure Regulator (CPR) on liquid line | Holds $p_c \geq p_{c,min}$ to maintain TEV driving pressure |
| Two cargo holds at different temperatures on same compressor | Evaporator Pressure Regulator (EPR) on warmer hold's TEV | Holds warmer evaporator at higher $T_e$ |
| Frequent compressor short-cycling under low load | Hot-gas bypass capacity regulator | Maintains minimum load without on/off cycling |
| Need to shut off liquid flow when compressor stops | Solenoid valve on liquid line | Allows pump-down cycle to evacuate refrigerant to receiver |
| Compressor must be protected from liquid slugging | Crankcase pressure regulator + suction-line accumulator | Prevents liquid refrigerant entering compressor |
| Evaporator frosting under low load | TEV with MOP (Maximum Operating Pressure) charge | Limits $p_e$ to prevent over-feeding at start-up |

## 3.4 Sample Worked Numerical

**Problem:** A marine R-404A refrigeration plant operates at $p_c = 16$ bar (saturated). The TEV discharges to $p_e = 3.5$ bar (saturation temperature $-5\,^\circ\text{C}$). The static superheat setting is 4 K. Compute the refrigerant mass flow through a TEV with effective orifice area $A_{eff} = 30\,\text{mm}^2$ and $C_d = 0.7$. Use $\rho_{liq} = 1050\,\text{kg/m}^3$ at $16$ bar.

**Step 1 — Pressure differential:**

$$
\Delta p = p_c - p_e = 16 - 3.5 = 12.5\,\text{bar}
$$

Converting to Pa:

$$
\Delta p = 12.5 \times 10^5 = 1.25 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective orifice area in m$^2$:**

$$
A_{eff} = 30 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the orifice mass-flow formula:**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho_{liq}\, \Delta p}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.7 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1050 \times 1.25 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{2.625 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.1235 \times 10^4
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 1.076\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr (commonly used in marine plant logs):**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 1.076 \times 3600 = 3873\,\text{kg/hr} \approx 3.87\,\text{tonnes/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Refrigerating effect (using $h_1 - h_4$):**

For R-404A at $p_e = 3.5$ bar and 4 K superheat, $h_1 \approx 247\,\text{kJ/kg}$; at $p_c = 16$ bar saturated liquid, $h_4 = h_3 \approx 130\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{ref} (h_1 - h_4) = 1.076 \times (247 - 130) = 125.9\,\text{kW}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a moderately-sized plant, suitable for a reefer ship's general-purpose chamber.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Marine TEV with External Equaliser

The following mermaid diagram shows the *functional* interaction (not physical geometry) of the TEV sub-system. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric and all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting, per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CONDENSER HIGH PRESSURE LIQUID OUT"]
    nodeB["FILTER DRIER"]
    nodeC["SIGHT GLASS MOISTURE INDICATOR"]
    nodeD["SOLENOID VALVE L2 NORMALLY CLOSED"]
    nodeE["TEV INLET HIGH PRESSURE SIDE"]
    nodeF["TEV ORIFICE THROTTLING ELEMENT"]
    nodeG["TEV DIAPHRAGM ASSEMBLY"]
    nodeH["EVAPORATOR LOW PRESSURE SIDE"]
    nodeI["THERMAL BULB ON SUCTION LINE"]
    nodeJ["CAPILLARY TUBE TO BULB"]
    nodeK["EXTERNAL EQUALISER LINE FROM EVAPORATOR OUTLET"]
    nodeL["SPRING PRELOAD ADJUSTOR"]
    nodeM["COMPRESSOR SUCTION LINE"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeM -. pressure feedback .-> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeI -- capillary --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeG
    nodeL --> nodeG
    nodeG -- modulates --> nodeF
    nodeI -- senses --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Pressure-Sensing Feedback Loops of Major Regulators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA["COMPRESSOR DISCHARGE"]
    condA["CONDENSER"]
    condR["CONDENSER PRESSURE REGULATOR CPR"]
    tevA["THERMOSTATIC EXPANSION VALVE TEV"]
    evapA["EVAPORATOR"]
    eprA["EVAPORATOR PRESSURE REGULATOR EPR"]
    compS["COMPRESSOR SUCTION"]
    safetyHP["HIGH PRESSURE CUTOUT"]
    safetyLP["LOW PRESSURE CUTOUT"]

    compA --> condA
    condA --> condR
    condR --> tevA
    tevA --> evapA
    evapA --> eprA
    eprA --> compS
    compS --> compA
    condA -. monitors .-> safetyHP
    compS -. monitors .-> safetyLP
    safetyHP -- trip --> compA
    safetyLP -- trip --> compA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Capacity-Regulation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["LOAD CHANGE DETECTED BY TEV OR THERMOSTAT"]
    q1{"IS LOAD LESS THAN 25 PERCENT OF FULL LOAD"}
    q2{"IS CONDENSING PRESSURE FALLING BELOW SETPOINT"}
    q3{"IS MULTI EVAPORATOR PLANT"}
    q4{"IS COMPRESSOR MOTOR RATED FOR VFD"}

    cyl["ACTIVATE CYLINDER UNLOADING"]
    hgb["OPEN HOT GAS BYPASS VALVE"]
    cpr["ACTIVATE CONDENSER PRESSURE REGULATOR"]
    epr["CLOSE EVAPORATOR PRESSURE REGULATOR ON WARM HOLD"]
    vfd["VARY COMPRESSOR SPEED VIA VFD"]
    sol["DE ENERGISED SOLENOID TO PUMP DOWN"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- YES --> cyl
    q1 -- NO --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> cpr
    q2 -- NO --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> epr
    q3 -- NO --> q4
    q4 -- YES --> vfd
    q4 -- NO --> hgb
    cyl -- if unloading insufficient --> sol
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Safety Cut-out Logic (Mandatory SOLAS / Class)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lp1["LOW PRESSURE SENSOR ON SUCTION"]
    hp1["HIGH PRESSURE SENSOR ON DISCHARGE"]
    op1["OIL PRESSURE DIFFERENTIAL SENSOR"]
    fw1["FREEZE STAT ON CHILLED WATER OUTLET"]
    trip{"ANY FAULT PRESENT"}
    latch["LATCH TRIP RELAY"]
    compOFF["COMPRESSOR MOTOR CONTACTOR OPENS"]
    alarm["BUZZER AND ENGINE ROOM ANNUNCIATOR"]
    reset["MANUAL RESET ONLY AFTER FAULT CLEARED"]

    lp1 --> trip
    hp1 --> trip
    op1 --> trip
    fw1 --> trip
    trip -- YES --> latch
    latch --> compOFF
    latch --> alarm
    latch --> reset
    reset --> trip
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:** Each block diagram represents the *control interaction* between sensing, decision and actuation. Although the diagrams are topologically simplified, the **signal flow direction** is the key takeaway — students should be able to *redraw* the TEV feedback loop on their exam paper in roughly **5 blocks** for full credit.
