# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define a Thermostatic Expansion Valve and state its three primary functions in a marine refrigeration plant."**

**Model Answer:**
A thermostatic expansion valve (TEV) is a **modulating refrigerant flow control device** that automatically meters the flow of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator in response to the **superheat** at the evaporator outlet. Its three primary functions are:

1. To **throttle** high-pressure liquid refrigerant from condensing pressure to evaporator pressure (isenthalpic expansion).
2. To **meter** refrigerant mass flow in proportion to the evaporator load.
3. To **maintain a constant superheat** (typically 4 K to 6 K) at the evaporator outlet, ensuring only dry vapour enters the compressor and protecting it against liquid slugging.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between a Condenser Pressure Regulator (CPR) and an Evaporator Pressure Regulator (EPR). Mention one marine application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Condenser Pressure Regulator (CPR) | Evaporator Pressure Regulator (EPR) |
|--------|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Controlled variable | Condensing pressure $p_c$ | Evaporating pressure $p_e$ |
| Typical location | Liquid line after condenser | Suction line of one evaporator in a multi-evap plant |
| Purpose | Holds $p_c \geq p_{c,min}$ in cold ambient | Holds one evaporator at higher $T_e$ when other evaporators are at lower $T_e$ |
| Marine application | Sea-passage through Arctic / North Atlantic winter | Reefer ship carrying mixed chilled and frozen cargo |
| Mode | Modulating | Modulating |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module-2 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, the End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module-2 question is typically the 14-mark **internal-choice** question. **Attempt either Question A or Question B.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a thermostatic expansion valve (TEV)** used in marine refrigeration plants. Derive the steady-state force-balance equation and show how the static superheat is set by the spring pre-load.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Construction** — describe the four main parts: (i) body with inlet/outlet and equaliser port, (ii) diaphragm assembly, (iii) spring and adjustor, (iv) thermal bulb and capillary. Label all components clearly.

2. **Working** — explain the closed-loop negative-feedback logic:
   - High superheat → bulb hot → $p_b$ high → diaphragm pushes down → valve opens → more refrigerant → superheat falls.
   - Low superheat → bulb cold → $p_b$ low → diaphragm rises → valve closes → less refrigerant → superheat rises.

3. **Force-balance derivation** — write the equilibrium equation:

$$
p_b A_d = p_e A_d + k_s x_s
$$

and rearrange to get:

$$
\text{SH}_{static} = \dfrac{k_s x_s}{A_d} \cdot \dfrac{dT_{sat}}{dp}\bigg\vert_{p_b}
$$

4. **Discuss external equaliser** — when the evaporator has significant internal pressure drop, use external equaliser; otherwise internal equaliser suffices.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(b)** A marine R-134a refrigeration plant has a design condensing temperature of $40\,^\circ\text{C}$ and an evaporating temperature of $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$. The TEV orifice has $C_d = 0.75$ and effective area $25\,\text{mm}^2$. Determine:

1. The refrigerant mass flow rate in kg/s.
2. The refrigerating effect if $h_1 = 244\,\text{kJ/kg}$ at evaporator outlet (with 5 K SH) and $h_4 = 116\,\text{kJ/kg}$.
3. The compressor theoretical power if $h_2 = 268\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find saturation pressures from R-134a tables:**

At $40\,^\circ\text{C}$: $p_c \approx 10.16\,\text{bar}$.
At $-10\,^\circ\text{C}$: $p_e \approx 2.01\,\text{bar}$.

**Step 2 — Pressure differential:**

$$
\Delta p = 10.16 - 2.01 = 8.15\,\text{bar} = 8.15 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Density of saturated liquid at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$:**

For R-134a, $\rho_{liq} \approx 1180\,\text{kg/m}^3$ at $40\,^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho_{liq} \Delta p}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.75 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1180 \times 8.15 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 1.875 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{1.9234 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = 1.875 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.3857 \times 10^4 = 0.8224\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Refrigerating effect:**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_1 - h_4) = 0.8224 \times (244 - 116) = 0.8224 \times 128 = 105.3\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Compressor power:**

$$
P_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref}(h_2 - h_1) = 0.8224 \times (268 - 244) = 0.8224 \times 24 = 19.74\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — COP (for completeness):**

$$
COP = \dfrac{105.3}{19.74} = 5.33
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(a)** Describe the **various capacity control methods** used for marine refrigeration compressors. Compare **cylinder unloading** and **hot-gas bypass** with respect to part-load efficiency.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **List** the methods: (i) cylinder unloading (suction-valve lifters), (ii) hot-gas bypass, (iii) variable-frequency drive, (iv) on–off cycling (least preferred on ships due to motor heating), (v) multiple compressors with sequencing.

2. **Cylinder Unloading:**
   - Mechanism: a solenoid holds suction valves open, disabling compression in those cylinders.
   - Steps: typically $1/2$, $1/3$, $2/3$ unloading stages.
   - Efficiency: **high** at part load because unloaded cylinders take no power (mechanically decoupled).
   - Limitation: discrete steps; cannot smoothly vary.

3. **Hot-Gas Bypass:**
   - Mechanism: a modulating valve bypasses discharge gas directly to suction.
   - Efficiency: **poor** because the compressor still consumes full power but produces only a fraction of useful cooling.
   - Use: only when load falls below the minimum cylinder-unload step.

4. **Comparison table:**

| Aspect | Cylinder Unloading | Hot-Gas Bypass |
|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| Part-load efficiency | High | Low |
| Smoothness | Stepped | Continuous |
| Cost | Moderate (extra solenoids, unloaders) | Low |
| Risk of oil foaming | Negligible | Possible |
| Suitability for marine | Excellent | Use only below min-unload point |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the function of the **safety cut-out switches** in a marine refrigeration plant. State the typical set-points for HP, LP, and oil-pressure differential switches under classification society rules.

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Block diagram** showing sensors → trip relay → compressor contactor → annunciator. (Refer to the mermaid diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)

2. **Sensors and typical settings:**

| Cut-out | Sensor Location | Typical Trip Setting | Manual Reset? |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| High Pressure (HP) | Compressor discharge | 110 % of design $p_c$ | Yes (latching) |
| Low Pressure (LP) | Compressor suction | 0.2 bar above 0 °C saturation | Yes (latching) |
| Oil Pressure Differential | Across oil pump | $\Delta p \geq 1.0$–$1.5\,\text{bar}$ for $\geq 60\,\text{s}$ | Yes (latching) |
| Freeze-stat | Chilled-water outlet | $\leq 1.5\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Auto or manual |
| Motor Overload | Compressor windings | 110 % of FLC | Auto-reset |

3. **Discuss the latching logic** — once a trip occurs, the compressor cannot restart until the fault is cleared AND the operator manually resets.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> The **single most common error** in regulator questions is to **omit the steady-state force balance derivation** in a TEV question. Students frequently *describe* the working but forget to write $p_b A_d = p_e A_d + k_s x_s$. This alone costs 3 of 7 marks in part (a) of a 14-mark question. A second common error is **mixing up the direction of superheat correction** — stating that the valve opens when superheat is *low* (it actually opens when superheat is *high*). Memorise the loop: **high SH → more refrigerant; low SH → less refrigerant.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **regulator** is a *modulating* control device — distinct from a *cut-out* which is two-position.
- The **TEV** is the central regulator in any marine vapour-compression plant; it does **throttling + metering + superheat control**.
- The **steady-state force balance** on a TEV diaphragm yields $p_b A_d = p_e A_d + k_s x_s$, giving the static superheat set-point.
- The **mass flow** through the TEV orifice is $\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho_{liq} (p_c - p_e)}$.
- An **external equaliser** is mandatory whenever the evaporator has appreciable internal pressure drop.
- The **CPR** prevents over-low $p_c$ in cold weather; the **EPR** allows multi-temperature operation.
- **Capacity control** in marine compressors is best done by **cylinder unloading**; hot-gas bypass is a last resort.
- **Safety cut-outs** are mandated by classification societies and SOLAS; settings are HP at $1.10 \times p_{c,design}$, LP at $\approx 0.5\,\text{bar}$, oil $\Delta p$ trip at 60-second time delay.
- **Solenoid valves** on the liquid line enable pump-down cycles and are used as remote shut-offs.
- The **bulb charge** can be gas, liquid, or cross-critical (adsorption); the choice affects the MOP (Maximum Operating Pressure) behaviour.
- A **failing TEV** exhibits symptoms: (i) low suction superheat, (ii) liquid flood-back, (iii) compressor wet compression, (iv) reduced cooling capacity, (v) eventual LP cut-out trip.
- **Marine-specific rules**: regulators must be **shock-tested**, **vibration-resistant**, and **fail-safe** in case of capillary tube fracture (bulb charge lost → valve closes fully).
- **Static superheat $\neq$ operating superheat** — the operating superheat always exceeds the static due to bulb thermal lag and evaporator internal pressure drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Pre-Exam Mantra for Module 2 Regulators:**
> *"Regulator senses → Controller compares to set-point → Actuator modulates → Process variable returns to set-point."* Trace this loop for *every* regulator type the night before the exam and you cannot go wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerant Properties - Demands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refrigerant Properties - Demands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **refrigerant** is the operating fluid used in a refrigeration cycle that undergoes a phase change (typically evaporation and condensation) to absorb heat from a low-temperature region (the refrigerated space) and reject it to a high-temperature region (the surroundings or sea-water cooled condenser on board a ship).

The **demands on a refrigerant** are the comprehensive set of thermodynamic, chemical, physical, environmental, and safety properties that an *ideal* refrigerant must satisfy in order to ensure safe, efficient, economic, and ecologically responsible operation of a marine vapour compression refrigeration plant (VCRP) or any other refrigeration system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *Demands* does NOT mean "high demand / popularity". In KTU Refrigeration Engineering vocabulary, *Demands* refers to the **set of desirable properties** (also called *desirable characteristics*) that a refrigerant should possess to be classified as ideal for a given application.

### 1.2 Classification of Refrigerant Demands

The demands placed on a refrigerant fall under five broad categories:

1. **Thermodynamic Demands** — properties affecting the cycle COP and capacity.
2. **Chemical Demands** — stability, inertness, non-corrosiveness.
3. **Physical Demands** — boiling/freezing points, density, viscosity.
4. **Safety Demands** — non-toxicity, non-flammability, low pressure.
5. **Environmental Demands** — Zero ODP, Low GWP, Short Atmospheric Life.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engine Oil" Intuition

Imagine selecting the **engine oil** for a marine diesel engine:

- The oil must lubricate well (thermodynamic performance),
- It must not corrode the cylinder walls (chemical stability),
- It must flow easily at low temperatures (low pour point),
- It must not catch fire easily (fire safety), and
- It must not poison the workshop crew (non-toxicity).

Similarly, a refrigerant is the **"blood"** of a refrigeration system, and every property acts like a vital health parameter. A deficiency in any one demand (e.g., flammability in R-290 propane, or high GWP in R-134a) makes the refrigerant unsuitable for *that specific marine application*, even if it scores well on other properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters on Ships:** Unlike land-based plants, marine refrigeration systems operate in **vibration-prone, salt-laden, confined engine-room spaces** with a permanent crew onboard. Hence the demands on the refrigerant are *more stringent* than for domestic refrigeration — leak detection, low toxicity, and non-flammability are absolute necessities on passenger vessels covered by SOLAS and IGF Code.

---

### 1.4 Summary Hook — Why Study Refrigerant Demands?

Before selecting R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-744 (CO₂), R-717 (ammonia), or any newer natural refrigerant for a ship's provision plant, air-conditioning unit, or refrigerated cargo hold, the marine engineer must justify the choice against the **ideal-refrigerant demands table** given below. KTU examiners frequently test these demands directly because they form the *first design filter* in any real refrigeration project.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Demands (Performance-Driven)

These demands dictate the **size of the compressor, condenser, evaporator, and the COP** of the cycle.

| # | Demand | Engineering Reason |
|---|--------|--------------------|
| T1 | **Low boiling point (saturation temperature)** at evaporator pressure, well below the desired cold-space temperature (e.g., for fish storage at −30 °C the refrigerant should boil at about −35 °C). | Allows evaporator pressure to remain above atmospheric → leakage is outward, not inward (no moisture ingress). |
| T2 | **High latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$)**. | Reduces the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ for a given duty, lowering compressor size and cost. |
| T3 | **High critical temperature ($T_{cr}$)**, well above the maximum condensing temperature (≈ 45 °C in tropical seas). | Ensures condensation can occur at moderate pressures, not by gas cooling only. |
| T4 | **Moderately high condensing pressure**, but well below the design pressure of standard mild-steel vessels. | Avoids heavy, expensive condenser and evaporator shells. |
| T5 | **Low specific volume of saturated vapour ($v_g$)** at compressor suction conditions. | Reduces theoretical compressor displacement (cylinder swept volume) per kW of refrigeration. |
| T6 | **Low specific heat of liquid ($c_{pl}$)** and **high specific heat of vapour ($c_{pv}$)**. | Reduces flash gas at throttling, improving refrigerating effect. |
| T7 | **Low freezing point** (well below evaporator temperature). | Prevents solidification inside the evaporator tubes. |

### 2.2 Chemical Demands

| # | Demand | Reason |
|---|--------|--------|
| C1 | Chemical **stability** at high compressor-discharge temperatures (≈ 90–120 °C). | Avoids breakdown, sludge formation, and acid generation. |
| C2 | **Non-corrosive** to copper, steel, aluminium, and seal materials. | Extends life of heat exchangers and compressor valves. |
| C3 | **Miscibility with lubricating oil** (or carefully engineered immiscibility for oil-return designs). | Ensures oil circulates back to the crankcase, not slugging the evaporator. |
| C4 | **No reaction with moisture** (or formation of non-corrosive hydrates only). | Critical on ships where humidity is high. |

### 2.3 Physical & Practical Demands

| # | Demand | Reason |
|---|--------|--------|
| P1 | **High thermal conductivity** of liquid. | Improves heat-transfer coefficients in plate/freezer evaporators. |
| P2 | **Low viscosity** of both liquid and vapour. | Reduces pumping power and pressure drops. |
| P3 | **High dielectric strength** (for hermetic compressors in chillers). | Prevents motor winding short-circuits. |
| P4 | **Easy leak detection** by portable halide torch, electronic sniffer, or UV dye. | Mandatory on ships per SOLAS Ch. II-2. |
| P5 | **Low cost** and **ready availability** at all ports of call. | Operational economics. |

### 2.4 Safety Demands

| # | Demand | Reason |
|---|--------|--------|
| S1 | **Non-toxic** to crew and to refrigerated cargo (foodstuffs). | SOLAS and IMO require safe refrigerant classes. |
| S2 | **Non-flammable** and **non-explosive** when mixed with air. | Prevents fire hazard in machinery spaces. |
| S3 | **Low pressure ratio** across compressor (low discharge pressure). | Reduces mechanical stress, bearing wear, noise. |

### 2.5 Environmental Demands (Post-Montreal Protocol)

| # | Demand | Reason |
|---|--------|--------|
| E1 | **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 0)**. | CFCs and HCFCs are banned; HCFCs are being phased out. |
| E2 | **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP < 150 ideally, < 700 acceptable for HFCs)**. | Compliance with IMO MEPC, EU F-Gas, Kigali Amendment. |
| E3 | **Short atmospheric lifetime**. | Reduces long-term climate impact. |
| E4 | **Zero or low Toxicity to marine life** when discharged. | Important when refrigerant leaks at sea. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Of all demands, the *environmental triad* (ODP, GWP, Atmospheric Life) is the most heavily weighted in 2024 Scheme questions because India acceded to the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol and the IMO is enforcing carbon-intensity reduction on shipping.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — How Refrigerant Properties Enter Cycle Equations

Let $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ denote the enthalpies at compressor inlet, compressor outlet, condenser outlet, and evaporator inlet respectively, in a standard vapour compression cycle.

$$
\text{Refrigerating Effect per kg} \quad RE = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}
$$

$$
\text{Compressor Work per kg} \quad W_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}
$$

$$
\text{Coefficient of Performance} \quad COP = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}
$$

$$
\text{Heat Rejection Ratio} \quad HRR = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_1 - h_4} = 1 + \frac{1}{COP}
$$

$$
\text{Compressor Mass Flow Rate} \quad \dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4} \quad \text{[kg/s]}
$$

$$
\text{Theoretical Cylinder Displacement Volume} \quad V_d = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}
$$

$$
\text{Isentropic Discharge Temperature} \quad T_2 = T_1 \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}
$$

$$
\text{Isentropic Compression Work (ideal gas approx.)} \quad w_c = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, R\, T_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
\text{Volumetric Refrigerating Capacity} \quad Q_v = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{v_1} \quad \text{[kJ/m}^3\text{]}
$$

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Demands

- **Marine Provision Plant (Chiller on Cruiser):** Choose refrigerant with high $Q_v$ so compressor displacement is small — important in space-constrained engine rooms.
- **Reefer Container on Cargo Ship:** Choose refrigerant with low GWP (e.g., R-744 CO₂ in transcritical cycle) to meet EEDI / EEXI / CII regulations.
- **Fish-Hold Refrigeration (Brine + Direct Expansion):** Choose refrigerant with very low boiling point (e.g., R-404A, R-507A) for sub-zero evaporator temperatures.
- **Ship's Air-Conditioning Plant:** Choose R-134a or R-1234yf — moderate pressures, non-flammable, zero ODP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the key *analytical links* between refrigerant properties and the *performance numbers* a marine engineer reports to the Chief Engineer. These derivations are the most asked derivations in KTU ESE Module-2 questions.

### 3.1 Derivation — Why High Latent Heat Demands Reduce Compressor Size

Consider a refrigeration duty of $Q_e$ kW. By definition of refrigerating effect per unit mass:

$$
Q_e = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{Q_e}{RE}
$$

The theoretical volumetric displacement of the compressor is:

$$
V_d = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 = \frac{Q_e \cdot v_1}{RE}
$$

If we compare two refrigerants A and B giving the same duty:

$$
\frac{V_{d,A}}{V_{d,B}} = \frac{v_{1,A}/RE_A}{v_{1,B}/RE_B} = \left(\frac{v_{1,A}}{v_{1,B}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{RE_B}{RE_A}\right)
$$

The displacement ratio is governed by the ratio $v_1/RE$. A high $RE$ (high latent heat) drives this ratio down, hence **smaller compressor** for the same duty.

#### Worked Numerical Illustration

Suppose the evaporator conditions are $-15 \text{ °C}$ and the condenser is at $30 \text{ °C}$.

**For R-12:** $RE \approx 115$ kJ/kg, $v_1 \approx 0.075$ m³/kg.

$$
V_d = \frac{Q_e \cdot 0.075}{115} = 6.52 \times 10^{-4} \cdot Q_e \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{kW·s}
$$

**For R-22:** $RE \approx 165$ kJ/kg, $v_1 \approx 0.077$ m³/kg.

$$
V_d = \frac{Q_e \cdot 0.077}{165} = 4.67 \times 10^{-4} \cdot Q_e \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{kW·s}
$$

The displacement ratio R-22 / R-12 is:

$$
\frac{V_{d,R22}}{V_{d,R12}} = \frac{4.67}{6.52} \approx 0.716
$$

So for the same duty, an R-22 compressor needs only about **71.6 %** of the swept volume of an R-12 compressor. This is a **28.4 % reduction** in compressor size — a direct consequence of the *higher latent heat demand* of R-22.

### 3.2 Derivation — Why High Critical Temperature is a Demand

For condensation to occur at the condenser pressure $P_c$, the saturation temperature corresponding to $P_c$ must equal the condensing temperature $T_c$, and $T_c$ must be below the critical temperature $T_{cr}$:

$$
T_c < T_{cr}
$$

If $T_{cr} < T_c$, the refrigerant cannot condense at all — it remains a supercritical fluid, and the cycle must be transcritical (like CO₂ R-744 in marine heat pumps). Thus for *subcritical* cycles the demand is:

$$
T_{cr} \gg T_{\text{condenser,max}} \approx 45\text{–}55 \text{ °C}
$$

For R-22, $T_{cr} = 96.1 \text{ °C}$. For R-134a, $T_{cr} = 101.1 \text{ °C}$. For ammonia R-717, $T_{cr} = 133.4 \text{ °C}$ (best in class).

### 3.3 Derivation — Pressure-Ratio Effect on COP

Starting from the ideal vapour compression cycle, with isentropic compression:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

The compressor work per kg is:

$$
w_c = c_{p,v}(T_2 - T_1) = c_{p,v}\, T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

Let the *fixed* evaporating temperature be $T_e$ and the *variable* condensing temperature be $T_c$. Then the pressure ratio is:

$$
r_p = \frac{P_c}{P_e} \approx \left(\frac{T_c}{T_e}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}
$$

So:

$$
w_c = c_{p,v}\, T_e \left[\left(\frac{T_c}{T_e}\right) - 1\right] = c_{p,v}\,(T_c - T_e)
$$

Substituting back into the COP:

$$
COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{c_{p,v}(T_c - T_e)}
$$

This shows that **a refrigerant with high $c_{p,v}$ increases compressor work for the same condensing/evaporating spread, lowering COP.** This is *why* low specific heat of vapour is listed as a thermodynamic demand.

### 3.4 Derivation — Relationship between ODP, GWP and Atmospheric Lifetime

The *Ozone Depletion Potential* (ODP) is normalized to R-11 (ODP = 1):

$$
ODP_X = \frac{\Delta O_{3,X}}{\Delta O_{3,R11}}
$$

The *Global Warming Potential* (GWP) is integrated radiative forcing over a time horizon $H$ (typically 100 years), normalized to CO₂:

$$
GWP_X = \frac{\int_0^H a_X(t)\, dt}{\int_0^H a_{CO_2}(t)\, dt}
$$

where $a_X(t)$ is the instantaneous radiative forcing per unit mass of substance $X$ remaining in the atmosphere. Refrigerants with **chlorine** (CFC, HCFC) catalyse ozone destruction via Cl radicals; refrigerants with **C–F bonds** absorb strongly in the atmospheric window, contributing to GWP. This is the *physical justification* for demanding *zero ODP* and *low GWP*.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property vs Refrigerant Choice

The table below maps each demand to the *best-suited* and *worst-suited* common refrigerant for marine use.

| Demand | Best Refrigerant | Worst Refrigerant | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| High latent heat | R-717 (NH₃) | R-12 | NH₃ $h_{fg}$ = 1368 kJ/kg |
| Low boiling point | R-13, R-503 | R-114 | R-13 boils at −81 °C |
| High $T_{cr}$ | R-717 (133 °C) | R-13 (28.9 °C) | NH₃ condenses easily |
| Low ODP | R-134a, R-744, R-290 | R-12, R-22 | Cl-free refrigerants |
| Low GWP | R-744 (GWP=1), R-1234yf | R-404A (GWP=3922) | Natural refrigerants win |
| Non-flammable | R-134a, R-22 | R-290, R-1270 | Hydrocarbons are flammable |
| Non-toxic | R-134a, R-22 | R-717 (NH₃) | Ammonia pungent/toxic |
| Low cost | R-22, R-134a | R-1234yf, R-744 | Newer refrigerants costly |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Used Below:**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions (test *Remember / Understand* levels).
> - Part B: 14-mark analytical questions with **internal choice** (test *Apply / Analyse / Evaluate*).
> - Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

List any **six desirable thermodynamic properties** of an ideal refrigerant used in marine vapour compression plants.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure — so evaporator operates at pressure above atmospheric, preventing air/moisture ingress.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$)** — reduces refrigerant mass flow rate per kW of refrigeration.
3. **High critical temperature** — well above the maximum condensing temperature (45 °C) to ensure condensation at moderate pressures.
4. **Moderately high condensing pressure** — but low enough to use standard mild-steel vessels.
5. **Low specific volume of saturated vapour** at compressor suction — reduces theoretical compressor displacement.
6. **Low specific heat of vapour** — minimizes compression work, raising COP.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Use bullet points and underline keywords. KTU examiners award ½ mark per valid point.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

Why are **CFC and HCFC refrigerants** no longer preferred for new marine refrigeration plants? Justify with reference to **ODP, GWP, and atmospheric lifetime**.

**Model Answer:**

CFCs (e.g., R-12) and HCFCs (e.g., R-22) contain **chlorine atoms** which dissociate in the stratosphere and catalytically destroy ozone, giving them high **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 1 for R-11, ODP = 0.055 for R-22)**. They also have high **Global Warming Potential** (GWP₂₂ = 1810, GWP₁₂ = 10900) and long atmospheric lifetimes (R-12: 100 years, R-22: 12 years). Under the **Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment, and IMO MEPC regulations**, their production is being phased out, making them legally and environmentally unacceptable for new shipboard plants. Hence, **HFCs (R-134a) and natural refrigerants (R-744 CO₂, R-717 NH₃, R-290 propane)** are preferred replacements.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question A** OR **Question B** — Attempt any **ONE** full question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **thermodynamic, chemical, physical, safety, and environmental demands** that an ideal refrigerant should satisfy for marine applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a marine provision plant, the following data is given:

- Refrigerant: R-134a
- Evaporating temperature: $T_e = -10 \text{ °C}$
- Condensing temperature: $T_c = 40 \text{ °C}$
- Refrigerating capacity: $Q_e = 30 \text{ kW}$
- Enthalpy at compressor inlet: $h_1 = 392 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Enthalpy at compressor outlet: $h_2 = 430 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Enthalpy at condenser exit: $h_3 = 240 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Specific volume at compressor inlet: $v_1 = 0.075 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg}$

Calculate:
(i) Refrigerating effect per kg.
(ii) Compressor work per kg.
(iii) COP of the cycle.
(iv) Mass flow rate of refrigerant.
(v) Theoretical compressor displacement volume per second. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Outline:**

The examiner expects a structured answer covering:

- **Thermodynamic demands (3 marks):** Low b.p., high $h_{fg}$, high $T_{cr}$, moderate $P_c$, low $v_g$, low $c_{pv}$, low freezing point — each with a *one-line justification*.
- **Chemical demands (1 mark):** Stability, non-corrosiveness, oil miscibility.
- **Physical/Safety demands (1 mark):** Non-toxic, non-flammable, high dielectric strength.
- **Environmental demands (2 marks):** ODP = 0, GWP < 700, short atmospheric life, marine-safe discharge.

*(Students should structure the answer with clear sub-headings and bullet points for high marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $h_1 = 392$ kJ/kg
- $h_2 = 430$ kJ/kg
- $h_3 = 240$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = 0.075$ m³/kg
- $Q_e = 30$ kW

**Step 1 — Find $h_4$.** Since the throttling process is isenthalpic:

$$
h_4 = h_3 = 240 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Refrigerating Effect per kg:**

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392 - 240 = 152 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compressor work per kg:**

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 430 - 392 = 38 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — COP of the cycle:**

$$
COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{152}{38} = 4.00
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate of refrigerant:**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{30}{152} = 0.1974 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Theoretical compressor displacement volume per second:**

$$
V_d = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 = 0.1974 \times 0.075 = 0.01480 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

> $RE = 152$ kJ/kg, $w_c = 38$ kJ/kg, $COP = 4.00$, $\dot{m} = 0.1974$ kg/s, $V_d = 0.01480$ m³/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **classification of refrigerants** used on board ships. Discuss the suitability of **R-717 (ammonia)** as a refrigerant for large fish-hold refrigeration plants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine air-conditioning plant using R-134a operates between $T_e = 5 \text{ °C}$ and $T_c = 45 \text{ °C}$. The compressor has isentropic efficiency $\eta_c = 0.85$ and volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.90$. Given $h_1 = 405$ kJ/kg, $h_2^{ideal} = 425$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 250$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.060$ m³/kg, and refrigerating load $Q_e = 50$ kW:

Calculate:
(i) Actual compressor work per kg.
(ii) Mass flow rate of refrigerant.
(iii) Theoretical COP and actual COP.
(iv) Compressor swept volume per second. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Sketch / classification hierarchy:

```
Refrigerants
├── Primary Refrigerants (used directly, e.g., R-12, R-134a, NH₃, CO₂)
└── Secondary Refrigerants (used to transport cold, e.g., brine, glycol)
```

Further classification of *primary* refrigerants:

- **CFCs** — R-11, R-12 (banned)
- **HCFCs** — R-22 (phasing out)
- **HFCs** — R-134a, R-404A, R-507A
- **HFOs / Natural** — R-1234yf, R-744 (CO₂), R-717 (NH₃), R-290 (propane)

**Suitability of R-717 Ammonia for Fish-Hold:**

- **Advantages (5 points):** High $h_{fg} = 1368$ kJ/kg → low mass flow; high $T_{cr} = 133$ °C → easy condensation; ODP = 0; GWP = 0; low cost; copper-free system prevents stress corrosion; very high latent heat gives high COP.
- **Disadvantages (2 points):** Toxic at 0.5 % v/v; pungent smell at very low concentrations (good for leak detection but bad for crew); incompatible with copper alloys.

*Verdict:* Excellent for large fish-hold refrigeration on trawlers when safety systems (leak detectors, ventilation, alarms) are in place.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\eta_c = 0.85$, $\eta_v = 0.90$, $v_1 = 0.060$ m³/kg, $Q_e = 50$ kW, $h_1 = 405$, $h_2^{ideal} = 425$, $h_3 = 250$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Isentropic compressor work:**

$$
w_c^{ideal} = h_2^{ideal} - h_1 = 425 - 405 = 20 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual compressor work:**

$$
w_c^{actual} = \frac{w_c^{ideal}}{\eta_c} = \frac{20}{0.85} = 23.53 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpies and refrigerating effect:**

$$
h_4 = h_3 = 250 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 405 - 250 = 155 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{50}{155} = 0.3226 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical COP:**

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{RE}{w_c^{ideal}} = \frac{155}{20} = 7.75
$$

**Step 6 — Actual COP:**

$$
COP_{actual} = \frac{RE}{w_c^{actual}} = \frac{155}{23.53} = 6.587
$$

**Step 7 — Compressor swept volume per second:**

$$
V_d = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_v} = \frac{0.3226 \times 0.060}{0.90} = 0.02150 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

> $w_c^{actual} = 23.53$ kJ/kg, $\dot{m} = 0.3226$ kg/s, $COP_{ideal} = 7.75$, $COP_{actual} = 6.59$, $V_d = 0.02150$ m³/s.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic — KTU Examiner's Note:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "demand" with "popularity":** Many students write "R-134a is in high demand" — wrong. *Demand* means *desirable property requirement*.
> 2. **Mixing up ODP and GWP:** ODP is about **ozone**, GWP is about **climate**. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for swapping.
> 3. **Forgetting the throttling assumption:** In Part (b), $h_4 = h_3$ only for an isenthalpic throttle. Writing $h_4 = h_3 - x \cdot h_{fg}$ shows confusion between throttling and the wet-vapour state — penalty: 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong units on $V_d$:** Always state in **m³/s** or **m³/h**, never in litres without conversion.
> 5. **Skipping the volumetric efficiency** in part (b) of Q-B: $V_d = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 / \eta_v$, not just $\dot{m} \cdot v_1$.
> 6. **Not mentioning marine-specific safety:** On ships, fire in machinery spaces is a SOLAS hazard — always prefer **non-flammable** refrigerants unless safety systems are installed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Refrigerant Properties: Demands**

- **Definition:** *Demands* = the set of desirable properties an ideal refrigerant must possess.
- **Five categories:** Thermodynamic, Chemical, Physical, Safety, Environmental.
- **Key thermodynamic demands:** low b.p., high $h_{fg}$, high $T_{cr}$, moderate $P_c$, low $v_g$, low $c_{pv}$.
- **Key chemical demand:** Non-corrosive to copper and steel; stable at compressor discharge temperatures.
- **Key safety demand:** Non-toxic, non-flammable; mandatory for passenger ships.
- **Key environmental demand:** ODP = 0 (post-Montreal Protocol), GWP < 700 (IMO targets).
- **Why high $h_{fg}$ matters:** Reduces $\dot{m}$ and hence compressor size — quantified by $V_d = Q_e \cdot v_1 / RE$.
- **Why high $T_{cr}$ matters:** Allows condensation to occur at moderate subcritical pressures.
- **Why low $c_{pv}$ matters:** Reduces compression work, raising $COP = RE / w_c$.
- **Best refrigerant per demand:** R-717 for $h_{fg}$, R-134a for safety, R-744 for environment.
- **Marine-specific:** Vibration, salt corrosion, confined spaces, SOLAS rules make *safety* demands especially strict.
- **Remember $h_4 = h_3$** for isenthalpic throttling — this is the single most-used equation in cycle problems.
- **Final tie-breaker:** Among refrigerants with equal COP, **select the one with lower GWP and ODP = 0** to comply with Kigali Amendment and IMO MEPC 2024 carbon-intensity rules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Choice

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of marine **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC)** systems, the term **"Choice"** (sometimes phrased in textbooks as *"Selection of Refrigerant"* or *"Choice of Refrigeration System"*) refers to the **systematic engineering evaluation and selection** of the most suitable **working fluid (refrigerant)** and **refrigeration cycle** for a given shipboard application — be it **crew accommodation air-conditioning, provision (food) preservation plants, cargo refrigeration holds, or reefer containers**.

Formally, per KTU 2024 syllabus notation, *"Choice"* encapsulates the **decision-making framework** that balances **thermodynamic performance**, **mechanical safety**, **environmental compliance**, **marine-classification rules**, and **economic viability** when specifying a refrigerant–system pair for a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the course PENST743, Module 2 lays the foundation for evaluating refrigerants on the basis of: (i) thermodynamic properties, (ii) safety classification (ASHRAE 34), (iii) environmental indices — **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**, and (iv) compatibility with marine class rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **ship doctor** choosing a *medicine* for a patient (the refrigeration application):

| Ship Refrigeration Analogy | Real-Life Parallel |
|---|---|
| Selecting refrigerant | A doctor selecting a medicine |
| Vessel type & application | Patient's age, weight, illness |
| ODP/GWP regulations | Government drug-safety rules |
| Toxicity / flammability rating | Side-effect profile of drug |
| COP & refrigeration effect | Drug's potency per unit dose |
| Compressor size & cost | Pill size & price |

Just as a doctor avoids giving aspirin to a child with viral fever (wrong choice), a marine engineer **must not** install an **Ammonia (R-717) plant in a passenger cabin** (toxicity hazard) nor an **R-12 unit in a 2024-built reefer ship** (Montreal Protocol ban). The "Choice" is therefore a **multi-criteria, regulated engineering decision**, not a single-best-answer problem.

## 1.3 Governing Standards & Constants

- **ASHRAE Standard 34** — Refrigerant Safety Group Classification (e.g., A1, A2L, B2L)
- **Montreal Protocol (1987)** — Phase-out of CFCs / HCFCs
- **Kigali Amendment (2016)** — Phase-down of HFCs
- **IMO MARPOL Annex VI** — Marine pollution prevention (refrigerant release limits)
- **Standard ODP of R-11 (CFC-11) = 1.0** (reference benchmark)
- **Standard GWP of CO₂ = 1.0** (reference benchmark, 100-year horizon)
- **NBP (Normal Boiling Point) reference** for classification at **101.325 kPa**
- **1 ton of refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW** of cooling effect

> [!NOTE]
> Always quote refrigerant safety group and ODP/GWP with the **ASHRAE 34 / IPCC AR6 reference values**. Numerical values may be revised by newer assessments — cite the standard year in your answer script.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Choice" Problem — Structured Engineering Logic

The selection of a refrigerant–system combination on a ship proceeds through the following **five logical filters**, applied in order:

### Filter 1 — **Thermodynamic Performance**
The fluid must deliver a high **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** and adequate **refrigeration effect per kg (q_L)**.
- Low **specific volume of suction vapor (v_g at evaporator T)** → smaller compressor displacement.
- Moderate **latent heat of vaporization (h_fg)** → high refrigeration effect per cycle.
- Low **discharge temperature** after isentropic compression → no need for liquid/suction cooling.

### Filter 2 — **Chemical Stability & Compatibility**
- Must be **chemically stable** across the operating temperature window (typically **−40 °C to +60 °C** on ships).
- Non-corrosive to copper, steel, aluminium, and elastomeric seals.
- **Miscible with lubricating oil** (mineral or POE) to ensure compressor lubrication.

### Filter 3 — **Safety Classification (ASHRAE 34)**
Each refrigerant is tagged with **two letters and a number**:
- **Letter 1** → Toxicity: **A (low)** or **B (high)**
- **Letter 2** → Flammability: **1 (no flame propagation)**, **2L (mild)**, **2 (moderate)**, **3 (highly flammable)**
- Example: **R-134a = A1**, **R-1234yf = A2L**, **R-717 (Ammonia) = B2L**

### Filter 4 — **Environmental Compliance**
- **ODP must be 0** for new installations (CFCs and HCFCs banned).
- **GWP** — IMO and EU F-Gas Regulation push for **GWP < 150** in marine MAC units; **< 700** acceptable in some reefer applications.
- **Low Total Equivalent Warming Impact (TEWI)** preferred.

### Filter 5 — **Operational & Economic Suitability**
- Reasonable **suction/discharge pressures** (avoid deep vacuum → air ingress; avoid extreme high → heavy vessels).
- Easy **leak detection** (large molecules preferred).
- Affordable, globally available, with established **reclaim/recycle infrastructure** at ports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine-class caveat:** Most classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register) restrict use of **Group A3 (e.g., Propane R-290)** refrigerants in **machinery spaces** of passenger ships, but permit them in **cargo-only vessels** under strict ventilation and gas-detection provisions.

## 2.2 Choice of Refrigeration *System* for Ships

While the "Choice" topic often centres on the refrigerant, KTU also expects students to justify the **refrigeration cycle** chosen. The dominant options on board are:

| System Type | Typical Marine Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vapour Compression (VCRS)** | Cabin AC, provision rooms, reefer containers | High COP, compact, mature technology | Uses HFCs/HFOs — GWP concerns; oil return issues |
| **Absorption (LiBr–H₂O for AC; NH₃–H₂O for cold storage)** | Exhaust-heat-driven AC on LNG carriers, waste-heat reefer systems | No compressor (vibration-free), uses waste heat | Bulky, low COP (~0.6–0.7), crystallisation risk |
| **Steam-jet (ejector) refrigeration** | Historically used on cargo ships | No moving parts in refrigerant loop | Very low COP, needs high-pressure steam |
| **Cryogenic / Cascade** | LNG cargo boil-off gas reliquefaction | Enables BOG reliquefaction | High capital cost, two-stage complexity |

For **modern merchant vessels**, the **VCRS dominates** by a wide margin — hence the emphasis in KTU on **choice of refrigerant within a VCR system**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Symbol / Equation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigeration effect per kg | $q_L = h_g(1) - h_f(4)$ | kJ/kg |
| 2 | Compressor work per kg | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Heat rejected at condenser per kg | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance (actual) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{q_L}{w_c}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | COP of reversed Carnot cycle | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Theoretical volumetric refrigeration capacity | $q_v = \dfrac{h_g(1) - h_f(4)}{v_{g1}}$ | kJ/m³ |
| 7 | Theoretical compressor displacement | $V_d = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_{g1}}{\eta_v}$ | m³/s |
| 8 | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_L}{q_L}$ | kg/s |
| 9 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Discharge temperature (isentropic) | $T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}}$ | K |
| 11 | TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact) | $\text{TEWI} = GWP \cdot L \cdot n + \alpha \cdot E_{\text{annual}}$ | kg CO₂-eq |
| 12 | Refrigeration tonnage (1 TR) | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU script:** when comparing two refrigerants on COP, always hold the **same evaporator and condenser temperatures** — otherwise the comparison is invalid.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Reefer ship design:** Choosing **R-404A → R-449A (GWP ≈ 392)** reduces TEWI by ~60 % over a 20-year ship life.
- **LNG carrier propulsion:** CO₂ (R-744) used in some reliquefaction loops because of its low GWP and compatibility with multi-component mixtures.
- **Naval vessels:** R-134a historically used; now shifting to **R-1234ze(E)** (GWP ≈ 6) for stealth acoustic signatures.
- **Yacht-class comfort AC:** R-410A replaced by **R-32 (GWP = 675)** in newer builds.
- **Regulatory driver:** EU Ship Recycling Regulation and IMO MEPC push toward **GWP < 150** in marine HVAC by 2030.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COP and Refrigeration Effect for the Standard Vapour Compression Cycle

### 3.1.1 State Points

We adopt the standard four-state numbering used in KTU textbooks for the **ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle (VCRS)**:

- **State 1:** Saturated/superheated vapor leaving the evaporator (compressor inlet)
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor leaving the compressor (isentropic compression)
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser
- **State 4:** Two-phase mixture after throttling (isenthalpic expansion)

### 3.1.2 Mass and Energy Balances on Each Component

**Evaporator (steady-flow, negligible KE/PE):**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}\,\bigl(h_{g1} - h_{f4}\bigr)
$$

Since throttling is isenthalpic: $h_{f4} = h_{f3}$.

Therefore:

$$
q_L = h_{g1} - h_{f3}
$$

**Compressor (steady-flow, adiabatic, isentropic in ideal cycle):**

$$
\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}\,\bigl(h_2 - h_1\bigr)
$$

Therefore:

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Condenser (steady-flow):**

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}\,\bigl(h_2 - h_{f3}\bigr)
$$

Therefore:

$$
q_H = h_2 - h_{f3}
$$

### 3.1.3 Coefficient of Performance

By definition, COP is the ratio of desired refrigeration effect to the work input:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c}
$$

Substituting the expressions derived above:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{h_{g1} - h_{f3}}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

**Useful equivalent form using enthalpies only:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{h_{g1} - h_{f3}}{h_2 - h_{g1}}
$$

> This expression is the one most often quoted in KTU 14-mark derivations.

### 3.1.4 Reversed Carnot COP for Reference

For a perfectly reversible cycle operating between $T_L$ (evaporator) and $T_H$ (condenser), absolute temperatures in **Kelvin**:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

The **Second-Law efficiency** of any real cycle is then:

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Volumetric Refrigeration Capacity — Critical for Compressor Sizing

The **refrigeration effect per unit volume of suction vapor** determines the physical size of the compressor:

$$
q_v = \frac{q_L}{v_{g1}} = \frac{h_{g1} - h_{f3}}{v_{g1}}
$$

Units: **kJ/m³**. A high $q_v$ ⇒ smaller compressor for the same duty.

The **mass flow rate** required for a given refrigeration load $Q_L$ (in kW or TR) is:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_L}{q_L}
$$

The **compressor displacement volume** (theoretical swept volume per second):

$$
V_d = \dot{m} \cdot v_{g1} = \frac{Q_L \cdot v_{g1}}{h_{g1} - h_{f3}}
$$

With volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ (typically **0.65–0.85** for reciprocating compressors):

$$
V_{\text{actual}} = \frac{V_d}{\eta_v}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Comparing Refrigerants (Analytical Matrix)

> **KTU-style worked problem:** A shipboard provision room requires **Q_L = 10 TR** of cooling, with **T_evap = −10 °C** and **T_cond = +40 °C**. Compare **R-134a** and **R-404A** on the basis of: (i) refrigeration effect, (ii) compressor work, (iii) COP, and (iv) compressor displacement.

### Given Property Data (from standard refrigerant tables)

| Property (at given T) | R-134a | R-404A |
|---|---|---|
| $h_{f3}$ at +40 °C (kJ/kg) | 256.41 | 271.71 |
| $h_{g1}$ at −10 °C (kJ/kg) | 392.32 | 397.32 |
| $v_{g1}$ at −10 °C (m³/kg) | 0.0993 | 0.0629 |
| $h_2$ (after isentropic compression to 40 °C) | 430.20 | 440.00 |

*(Property values illustrative for academic use; actual tables may differ slightly — always refer to ASHRAE Handbook.)*

### Step 1 — Refrigeration Effect $q_L$

For **R-134a:**

$$
q_L^{(\text{R134a})} = h_{g1} - h_{f3} = 392.32 - 256.41 = 135.91 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

For **R-404A:**

$$
q_L^{(\text{R404A})} = h_{g1} - h_{f3} = 397.32 - 271.71 = 125.61 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Compressor Work $w_c$

For **R-134a:**

$$
w_c^{(\text{R134a})} = h_2 - h_1 = 430.20 - 392.32 = 37.88 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

For **R-404A:**

$$
w_c^{(\text{R404A})} = h_2 - h_1 = 440.00 - 397.32 = 42.68 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Coefficient of Performance

$$
\text{COP}^{(\text{R134a})} = \frac{135.91}{37.88} = 3.588
$$

$$
\text{COP}^{(\text{R404A})} = \frac{125.61}{42.68} = 2.943
$$

### Step 4 — Compressor Displacement

Convert refrigeration duty to kW: $\,Q_L = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17 \text{ kW}$.

**R-134a:**

$$
\dot{m}^{(\text{R134a})} = \frac{35.17}{135.91} = 0.2588 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
V_d^{(\text{R134a})} = 0.2588 \times 0.0993 = 0.02570 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 92.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**R-404A:**

$$
\dot{m}^{(\text{R404A})} = \frac{35.17}{125.61} = 0.2800 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
V_d^{(\text{R404A})} = 0.2800 \times 0.0629 = 0.01761 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 63.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

### Step 5 — Comparative Summary Table

| Performance Metric | R-134a | R-404A | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration effect $q_L$ (kJ/kg) | 135.91 | 125.61 | R-134a (higher) |
| Compressor work $w_c$ (kJ/kg) | 37.88 | 42.68 | R-134a (lower) |
| COP | **3.588** | **2.943** | **R-134a (≈ 22 % better)** |
| Compressor displacement (m³/hr) | 92.5 | 63.4 | R-404A (smaller) |

### 3.2.1 Engineering Conclusion

- **R-134a wins on COP** ⇒ lower running cost, less shaft power, lower fuel consumption in ship auxiliary engines.
- **R-404A wins on volumetric capacity** ⇒ smaller compressor swept volume for the same duty (advantageous in retrofit/space-constrained engine rooms).
- **However, GWP(R-134a) = 1430**, **GWP(R-404A) = 3922** — both unacceptable for forward-looking EU/IMO regulations.
- **Decision:** A modern ship would adopt **R-449A (GWP ≈ 392, COP ≈ 3.5)** or **R-1234ze(E) (GWP ≈ 6)** — combining thermodynamic performance comparable to R-134a with environmental compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU lesson:** A "Choice" problem is **never** decided on COP alone. The selection matrix must include **environmental, safety, regulatory, and operational** dimensions alongside thermodynamic performance. Marks are awarded for showing this **multi-criteria reasoning**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Refrigerant Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **multi-criteria decision logic** that a marine engineer applies when "choosing" a refrigerant for a shipboard system. Each node is purely alphanumeric; no markdown formatting is embedded inside labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Refrigerant Selection for Marine RAC]) --> q1{Is ODP = 0 required?}
    q1 -- No, CFC HCFC legacy --> reject1[Reject: R11 R12 R22 R502]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Application type?}
    reject1 --> q2
    q2 -- Cabin AC MAC --> branch1[Crew Comfort AC Branch]
    q2 -- Provision Cold Room --> branch2[Provision Plant Branch]
    q2 -- Reefer Cargo Hold --> branch3[Reefer Cargo Branch]
    q2 -- LNG BOG Reliquefaction --> branch4[Cryogenic LNG Branch]

    branch1 --> crit1{GWP limit?}
    crit1 -- GWP less than 150 --> recA1[R1234ze E or R1234yf A2L]
    crit1 -- GWP less than 700 --> recA2[R32 or R410A alternative]
    crit1 -- Legacy acceptable --> recA3[R134a A1 high GWP]

    branch2 --> crit2{Temperature range?}
    crit2 -- Medium temp 0 to 5 C --> recB1[R449A or R404A replacement blend]
    crit2 -- Low temp minus 25 to minus 40 C --> recB2[R404A or R507 cascade stage 1]
    crit2 -- Fresh produce high humidity --> recB3[NH3 R717 B2L with PSLV]

    branch3 --> crit3{Capacity and crew safety?}
    crit3 -- Direct expansion --> recC1[CO2 R744 transcritical cascade stage 2]
    crit3 -- Central plant --> recC2[R744 or R717 with machinery space class rules]

    branch4 --> crit4{Boil off gas reliquefaction?}
    crit4 -- Mixed refrigerant --> recD1[MR LNG mixed blend proprietary]
    crit4 -- Nitrogen expander --> recD2[N2 Brayton cycle reverse]

    recA1 --> finalCheck[Final Verification Step]
    recA2 --> finalCheck
    recA3 --> finalCheck
    recB1 --> finalCheck
    recB2 --> finalCheck
    recB3 --> finalCheck
    recC1 --> finalCheck
    recC2 --> finalCheck
    recD1 --> finalCheck
    recD2 --> finalCheck

    finalCheck --> v1{Toxicity and flammability within class limit?}
    v1 -- Yes --> v2{Cost and availability at home port?}
    v1 -- No --> reject2[Reject and return to branch selection]
    v2 -- Yes --> approved([Approved Refrigerant for Ship Installation])
    v2 -- No --> reject3[Reject select backup option]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Marine VCRS Choice Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode([Ship Specification Document]) --> procA[Vessel Type Analysis]
    procA --> procB[Load Estimation kW TR]
    procB --> procC[Refrigerant Thermodynamic Score]
    procC --> procD[Environmental ODP GWP Score]
    procD --> procE[Safety ASHRAE 34 Score]
    procE --> procF[Regulatory IMO EU Flag Score]
    procF --> procG[Economic Capex Opex Score]
    procG --> weight[Weighted Multi Criteria Decision Matrix]
    weight --> outputNode([Selected Refrigerant and Cycle Combination])
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Choice of Cycle on Board (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appStart([Shipboard Application]) --> tc1{Cooling Load Profile}
    tc1 -- Steady large load --> vcrs[Vapour Compression VCRS]
    tc1 -- Waste heat available --> abs[Absorption System]
    tc1 -- Steam readily available --> ejector[Steam Jet Ejector]
    tc1 -- Cryogenic LNG --> cascade[Cascade System]

    vcrs --> vcrsDetail[Reciprocating Screw or Scroll Compressor with HFC HFO or HC refrigerant]
    abs --> absDetail[LiBr H2O for AC above 5 C OR NH3 H2O for subzero]
    ejector --> ejectorDetail[High pressure boiler steam 6 to 10 bar ejector]
    cascade --> cascadeDetail[Two stage R744 low stage R717 or R134a high stage]

    vcrsDetail --> install([Install on Ship])
    absDetail --> install
    ejectorDetail --> install
    cascadeDetail --> install
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> List **four desirable properties** of an ideal refrigerant for a marine air-conditioning plant, and state one **undesirable property** that must always be avoided.

**Model Answer:**

Four desirable properties:

1. **Low boiling point** at the desired evaporator temperature → ensures sufficient pressure above atmospheric (avoids air ingress).
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** → reduces mass flow rate of refrigerant for a given duty.
3. **Low ODP (ideally 0) and low GWP** → compliance with Montreal Protocol / Kigali Amendment.
4. **Chemical stability, non-corrosive, non-toxic, and non-flammable** → ASHRAE 34 Group **A1** preferred for occupied spaces.

Undesirable property to always avoid: **High toxicity** (ASHRAE Group B) in **passenger or crew spaces**, due to risk of leakage in confined shipboard compartments.

> *(3 marks: 2 marks for four properties + 1 mark for the undesirable property correctly justified.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> What is meant by the **ASHRAE safety classification A2L**? Give **one marine-relevant example** of a refrigerant carrying this classification.

**Model Answer:**

- **A** → **Low toxicity** (the refrigerant is non-toxic or only mildly toxic at normal concentrations).
- **2L** → **Mild flammability**, with a slow flame propagation velocity (L = "lower flammability" / "burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s").
- Combined, **A2L** denotes a refrigerant that is **safe to handle** but has a **limited, controlled flammability risk** that can be managed with leak detection and ventilation.

**Marine-relevant example:** **R-1234ze(E)** — an HFO refrigerant with **GWP ≈ 6**, **ODP = 0**, used in modern naval and commercial marine chillers as a low-GWP substitute for R-134a.

> *(3 marks: 2 marks classification + 1 mark correct example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** For Module 2, students must answer **ONE full 14-mark question** from an internal choice of **two** (Question A or Question B). Each question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

> **(a)** With a **neat labelled schematic**, explain the working of a **single-stage vapour compression refrigeration system** used for ship accommodation air-conditioning. List the function of each component. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shipboard reefer plant uses **R-134a** with evaporator temperature **−15 °C** and condenser temperature **+35 °C**. The refrigerant is **dry-saturated** at compressor inlet. Using refrigerant property tables, compute:
> 1. **Refrigeration effect** per kg of refrigerant (kJ/kg)
> 2. **Compressor work** per kg (kJ/kg)
> 3. **COP** of the cycle
> 4. **Mass flow rate** required for a **20 TR** load
>    **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Schematic description (block-by-block answer):**

1. **Compressor** — Draws low-pressure vapor from the evaporator, compresses it isentropically to high pressure and temperature. Function: **raises refrigerant pressure and temperature.**
2. **Condenser** — High-pressure superheated vapor rejects heat to sea-water cooling medium (via shell-and-tube or plate heat exchanger). Function: **rejects $q_H$ to surroundings; condenses vapor to liquid.**
3. **Expansion valve (throttle)** — Reduces pressure of saturated liquid from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure. Function: **isenthalpic pressure drop; creates two-phase mixture.**
4. **Evaporator** — Low-pressure two-phase mixture absorbs heat from the conditioned space (cabin air). Function: **provides refrigeration effect $q_L$.**

**Working fluid cycle:** Vapor → Compressed vapor → Liquid → Two-phase → Vapor (closed loop).

**Operating conditions on board:** Suction pressure typically **2–4 bar**, discharge **10–15 bar** for R-134a; seawater cooling at **32 °C inlet, 36 °C outlet**.

> *(Mark split: 2 marks schematic + 2 marks component functions + 1 mark cycle description + 2 marks marine-specific operating data.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Enthalpy values from R-134a saturation tables:**

- At **T_evap = −15 °C** (saturated vapor): $h_{g1} = 389.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $v_{g1} = 0.1066 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- At **T_cond = +35 °C** (saturated liquid): $h_{f3} = 249.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $p_{cond} \approx 8.78 \text{ bar (abs)}$
- At compressor inlet pressure ≈ 1.64 bar (abs), $T_1 = -15\,°C$

**Step 2 — Isentropic compression to condenser pressure:**

Use superheated R-134a tables at $p = 8.78 \text{ bar}$ and entropy equal to that at state 1 ($s_{g}$ at −15 °C ≈ 0.9557 kJ/kg·K). Interpolating: **$h_2 \approx 425.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$**.

**Step 3 — Refrigeration effect:**

$$
q_L = h_{g1} - h_{f3} = 389.16 - 249.66 = 139.50 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compressor work:**

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.45 - 389.16 = 36.29 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — COP:**

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{139.50}{36.29} = 3.844
$$

**Step 6 — Mass flow rate:**

Convert load: $\,Q_L = 20 \times 3.517 = 70.34 \text{ kW}$.

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{Q_L}{q_L} = \frac{70.34}{139.50} = 0.5043 \text{ kg/s}
$$

---

#### **Part B Question A — Final Answer Table**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Refrigeration effect $q_L$ | **139.50 kJ/kg** |
| Compressor work $w_c$ | **36.29 kJ/kg** |
| COP | **3.844** |
| Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ | **0.504 kg/s** |

> *(Mark split: 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 marks for the four sub-parts respectively.)*

---

### **Question B** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

> **(a)** Define **ODP** and **GWP**. Why is **R-22** no longer installed in new shipboard plants? Suggest **two suitable HFC/HFO replacements** for R-22 with their classifications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **factors that govern the choice of refrigerant** for a marine provision refrigeration plant. State the **ASHRAE classification** and **GWP** of at least two refrigerants commonly used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** A relative index that compares the ozone-destroying ability of a refrigerant to **R-11 (CFC-11)** which is assigned **ODP = 1.0**. A higher ODP ⇒ greater harm to the stratospheric ozone layer.

**GWP (Global Warming Potential):** A relative index that compares the radiative forcing impact of 1 kg of refrigerant over **100 years** to that of 1 kg of **CO₂** which is assigned **GWP = 1.0**.

**Why R-22 is no longer installed:**
- R-22 is an **HCFC** with **ODP = 0.055** and **GWP ≈ 1810**.
- Under the **Montreal Protocol**, HCFCs are being phased out for developing and developed nations — complete phase-out of R-22 in new equipment by **2020 (developing) / 2025 (developing nations, Kigali-aligned)**.
- For ships, classification societies and EU regulations prohibit new installations of HCFCs.

**Two suitable HFC/HFO replacements:**

| Replacement | Type | ASHRAE 34 Class | ODP | GWP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-410A** | HFC blend | A1 | 0 | 2088 |
| **R-407C** | HFC blend | A1 | 0 | 1774 |
| **R-1234ze(E)** | HFO | A2L | 0 | ≈ 6 |

(Note: R-410A and R-407C are transitional; for long-term marine compliance, **R-1234ze(E)** or **R-32** are preferred.)

> *(Mark split: 1 mark ODP definition + 1 mark GWP definition + 3 marks R-22 phase-out justification + 2 marks replacements with classification.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Factors governing the choice of refrigerant for a marine provision plant:**

1. **Thermodynamic suitability** — Evaporator temperature ≈ −5 °C to +5 °C; refrigerant should have boiling point 5–10 °C **below** evaporator temperature to maintain positive suction pressure.
2. **Safety in occupied galley/mess spaces** — Low toxicity (**ASHRAE A**) preferred; flammability controlled (**A1 or A2L**).
3. **Environmental impact** — **ODP = 0** mandatory; **GWP < 150** preferred (F-Gas Regulation, IMO MEPC guidelines).
4. **Oil return and material compatibility** — Must be **miscible with POE oil** (modern synthetic lubricants) and compatible with copper, brass, and elastomeric seals used in marine heat exchangers.
5. **Leak detectability and reclaim infrastructure** — Refrigerant should be detectable with standard halogen leak detectors, with **port-side reclaim availability** at major hubs (Singapore, Rotterdam, Cochin).
6. **Compactness and weight** — Marine engine rooms have **space and displacement constraints**; high volumetric refrigeration capacity is preferred.
7. **Cost and global availability** — Refrigants with multi-source manufacturing (R-134a, R-449A) are preferred over proprietary single-source HFOs.

**Two commonly used refrigerants on marine provision plants with classification:**

| Refrigerant | ASHRAE Class | ODP | GWP | Typical Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-449A** | A1 | 0 | 1397 | Modern provision rooms (R-404A replacement) |
| **R-134a** | A1 | 0 | 1430 | Older provision plants and cabin AC |
| **R-744 (CO₂)** | A1 | 0 | 1 | Cascade systems, low-temp cargo |
| **R-717 (Ammonia)** | B2L | 0 | 0 | Industrial fishing vessels, large reefer holds |

> *(Mark split: 5 marks for seven factors (≈1 each) + 2 marks for correctly tabulated refrigerant classifications.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfalls — How students LOSE marks on "Choice" questions:**
>
> 1. **Choosing refrigerant on COP alone** — A high COP alone does not justify selection. Always present the **multi-criteria decision** (COP + ODP + GWP + safety + cost). **(Lose up to 2 marks)**
> 2. **Skipping saturation tables** — Always quote property values from a **named reference** (ASHRAE Handbook, NIST REFPROP). Fabricating values costs **full marks** for the numerical part.
> 3. **Confusing ODP and GWP** — ODP = ozone depletion; GWP = climate warming. Mixing them up loses **1–2 marks** even if numbers are correct.
> 4. **Ignoring marine-specific rules** — A generic RAC answer (no mention of marine class rules, sea-water cooling, vibration, space) is treated as incomplete. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical** — Always write **kJ/kg, kW, kg/s** with values. Bare numbers without units are penalised.
> 6. **Missing the safety group letter** — Forgetting to write **A1 / A2L / B2L** for a refrigerant loses **1 mark** in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **No mention of Kigali Amendment / IMO MARPOL** — A "Choice" answer without regulatory citation is incomplete in the 2024 scheme.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final things to remember before the KTU 2024 exam:**

- ☐ **"Choice" = Multi-criteria selection** of refrigerant + refrigeration cycle for a shipboard application.
- ☐ Five filters: **Thermodynamic → Chemical → Safety → Environmental → Economic**.
- ☐ **ODP of CFC-11 = 1.0**; **GWP of CO₂ = 1.0** are the standard reference benchmarks.
- ☐ **ASHRAE 34** safety classification: **Letter 1** = toxicity (A/B); **Letter 2** = flammability (1/2L/2/3).
- ☐ **CFCs and HCFCs banned** in new marine installations — current refrigerants must have **ODP = 0**.
- ☐ **HFCs (R-134a, R-404A)** are being phased down under **Kigali Amendment (2016)**.
- ☐ **HFOs (R-1234ze(E), R-1234yf)** are the next-generation low-GWP replacements (**GWP < 10**).
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — always use this conversion in numerical problems.
- ☐ Key formulas: $q_L = h_{g1} - h_{f3}$; $w_c = h_2 - h_1$; $\text{COP} = q_L / w_c$; $\dot{m} = Q_L / q_L$.
- ☐ **R-717 (Ammonia)** is **B2L** — excellent COP, but used only in **non-occupied machinery spaces** due to toxicity.
- ☐ **R-744 (CO₂)** is gaining importance in **transcritical cascade systems** for reefer ships due to **GWP = 1**.
- ☐ **VCRS is the dominant choice** for marine RAC — absorption is secondary, used only with waste-heat availability.
- ☐ Always present a **comparison table** with COP, GWP, ODP, and safety class in "Choice" answers — examiner rewards structured presentation.
- ☐ Cite at least **one regulatory framework** (Montreal Protocol / Kigali / IMO MARPOL) in every full-length answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Shipboard Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems** is defined as the integrated set of engineering controls, regulatory compliance measures, design provisions, and operational protocols implemented to prevent harm to personnel, vessel, cargo, and environment from the inherent hazards of refrigerants (toxicity, flammability, pressure, temperature) and ancillary equipment, in accordance with the **SOLAS Convention (Chapter II-2)**, **MARPOL Annex VI**, **ISO 5149**, **ASHRAE Standard 15**, and classification society rules (**DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS**).

In marine engineering practice, shipboard refrigeration systems pose **multifaceted hazards** because they operate in confined spaces, often while the vessel is rolling, pitching, and exposed to saline atmosphere. The safety discipline therefore combines **inherent safe design**, **protective devices**, **operational discipline**, and **emergency preparedness**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiler Room of the Sea" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Refrigerated Compressor Room is like the Galley of a Cruise Ship**

Imagine a busy ship's galley where chefs use LPG burners (like a refrigeration plant). Just as the galley has:
- **Gas detectors** (leak alarms) → equivalent to refrigerant detectors
- **Automatic shut-off solenoid valves** → equivalent to compressor safety cut-outs
- **Ventilation hoods** (mechanical extraction) → equivalent to machinery room ventilation
- **Fire suppression (CO₂ flooding)** → equivalent to emergency discharge systems
- **Bilingual safety placards** → equivalent to IMO/MSDS labelling

The chef cannot rely on "common sense" alone — the system is engineered so that **failure of any single human or mechanical action cannot, by itself, cause catastrophe**. This is the foundation of **inherent safety** in marine refrigeration.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Shipboard Refrigeration Safety

| Pillar | Primary Concern | Typical Devices |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Toxicological** | Asphyxiation, poisoning | Leak detectors, SCBA, ventilation |
| **Flammability** | Fire, explosion | Deflagration vents, inerting, ATEX equipment |
| **Pressure** | Rupture, projectile | Relief valves, rupture discs |
| **Thermal** | Frostbite, cryogenic burns | PPE, insulation, low-pressure alarms |
| **Environmental** | ODP/GWP, ozone, GHG | Leak-tight systems, recovery units |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics (Bold)

- **TLV / OEL (Threshold Limit Value / Occupational Exposure Limit)** for common refrigerants:
  * **R-717 (Ammonia): 25 ppm (8-hr TWA)**
  * **R-134a: 1000 ppm**
  * **R-404A: 1000 ppm**
  * **R-744 (CO₂): 5000 ppm**
  * **R-1234yf: 500 ppm**
- **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** for hydrocarbons ≈ **0.038 kg/m³**
- **Standard reference for ODP** = **CFC-11 (= 1.0)**
- **Standard reference for GWP** = **CO₂ (= 1.0) over a 100-year horizon**
- **SOLAS mandated minimum oxygen** in occupied spaces = **≥ 19.5 % by volume**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ASHRAE Standard 34 — Refrigerant Safety Classification

The international standard **ASHRAE 34 / ISO 817** classifies every refrigerant into a **two-letter safety group**:

$$\text{Safety Group} = \underbrace{\text{Letter A or B}}_{\text{Toxicity}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Digit 1, 2, or 3}}_{\text{Flammability}}$$

**Toxicity Sub-group (Letter):**
- **Letter A** → Lower Toxicity → **OEL > 400 ppm**
- **Letter B** → Higher Toxicity → **OEL ≤ 400 ppm**

**Flammability Sub-group (Digit):**
- **Digit 1** → No Flame Propagation (non-flammable, flame velocity ≤ 10 cm/s)
- **Digit 2** → Lower Flammability (**LFL > 0.10 kg/m³** AND heat of combustion < 19 MJ/kg)
- **Digit 3** → Higher Flammability (**LFL ≤ 0.10 kg/m³** OR heat of combustion ≥ 19 MJ/kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Marine Refrigerants and Their Groups**
> - **R-134a → A1** (low toxicity, non-flammable) → most common in ship AC & provision cooling
> - **R-404A → A1** → widely used in fish-hold plants
> - **R-717 (Ammonia) → B2L** (toxic, mildly flammable) → common in fish factory vessels
> - **R-744 (CO₂) → A1** (low toxicity, non-flammable, high pressure ≈ 120 bar)
> - **R-1234yf → A2L** (mildly flammable, low GWP) → emerging in reefer containers

## 2.2 Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) and Global Warming Potential (GWP)

**ODP** measures the destructive impact of a refrigerant on the stratospheric ozone layer, referenced to CFC-11:

$$\boxed{\text{ODP}_i \;=\; \frac{\text{Ozone destroyed per unit mass of refrigerant } i}{\text{Ozone destroyed per unit mass of CFC-11}}}$$

**GWP** measures the integrated radiative forcing over a chosen time horizon (typically **100 years**):

$$\boxed{\text{GWP}_i \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{TH} a_i \cdot c_i(t)\, dt}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot c_{CO_2}(t)\, dt}}$$

where:
- $a_i$ = radiative efficiency (W m⁻² ppb⁻¹)
- $c_i(t)$ = atmospheric concentration perturbation (kg)
- $TH$ = time horizon = **100 years**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters at sea:** The **Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol (2016)** and **EU F-Gas Regulation** progressively ban high-GWP refrigerants. From **January 1, 2030**, the **MARPOL Annex VI** prohibits new installations of refrigerants with **GWP > 150** on ships, except in specified exceptions. Ship engineers are therefore required to migrate to **R-1234yf (GWP < 1)** or natural refrigerants (**R-744, R-717, R-290**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\text{ODP}_i = \dfrac{\Delta O_{3,i}}{\Delta O_{3,R-11}}$ | Ratio of ozone destruction | Dimensionless | Compare refrigerant environmental hazard |
| 2 | $\text{GWP}_i = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} F_i\, dt}{\int_0^{TH} F_{CO_2}\, dt}$ | Integrated radiative forcing | Dimensionless | Climate impact comparison |
| 3 | $E_{CO_2,eq} = m_{leak}\times \text{GWP}_i$ | Equivalent CO₂ mass | kg CO₂-eq | Annual refrigerant emission accounting |
| 4 | $Q_{vent} = \dfrac{G}{c_{lim}\cdot \rho_{air}}$ | Ventilation flow rate | m³/s | Refrigerant dilution in machinery room |
| 5 | $A_{orifice} = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot P_{set}}$ | Relief valve area | mm² | Sizing pressure relief device |
| 6 | $T_{discharge} = T_{suction}\cdot \left(\dfrac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}}$ | Discharge temperature | K | Predict thermal runaway, oil breakdown |
| 7 | $\text{PR}_{max} = \dfrac{P_{d,max}}{P_{s,min}}$ | Max pressure ratio | Dimensionless | Compressor safety limit |
| 8 | $n_{critical} = 1 + \dfrac{\ln(\pi_1)}{\ln(\text{PR})}$ | Polytropic index | – | Predict compression work limit |
| 9 | $\rho_{gas} = \dfrac{P\,M}{R_u\,T}$ | Ideal gas density | kg/m³ | Leak rate estimation |
| 10 | $h_{fg}(T) \approx h_{fg}(T_{ref})\cdot \left(\dfrac{1-T_r}{1-T_{r,ref}}\right)^{0.38}$ | Latent heat approx. | kJ/kg | Two-phase refrigerant mass release |

> *Note: $\pi_1$ denotes the critical (cut-off) pressure ratio for a positive-displacement compressor; values vary from 3 to 8 depending on refrigerant and design.*

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility of These Equations

In a **shipboard fish-hold refrigeration plant** with **R-717 (Ammonia)**, the safety officer must:

1. Compute **maximum credible leak inventory** ($m_{ref}$ in the largest vessel section).
2. Convert to **ppm equivalent** via the room volume formula.
3. Determine **required ventilation** $Q_{vent}$ such that after one complete air change, $c_{room} < \text{OEL}/2$.
4. Size **relief valves** using equation (5) above, certified to **PED (2014/68/EU)** or **ASME Section VIII**.
5. File the **TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)** logbook annually as per **EU MRV Regulation 2015/757**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Ventilation Air Quantity for Safe Refrigerant Dilution

**Statement:** When a fixed mass $G$ (kg) of refrigerant is instantaneously released into a closed machinery room, the minimum volumetric flow of fresh air $Q$ (m³/s) required to dilute the refrigerant concentration to a fraction $c_{lim}$ (ppm/10⁶) below the OEL is:

$$\boxed{\;Q_{min} \;=\; \frac{G}{c_{lim}\cdot \rho_{air}\cdot t_{dilute}}\;}$$

### Full Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Definition of mass concentration:**
The mass of refrigerant released into a room of volume $V$ produces an average concentration:

$$C \;=\; \frac{G}{V} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumetric ppm:**
Since ppm by volume for a gas/gas mixture at low concentration equals mole fraction × 10⁶:

$$c_{ppm} \;=\; \frac{n_{ref}}{n_{air}+n_{ref}} \times 10^6 \;\approx\; \frac{n_{ref}}{n_{air}}\times 10^6 \;\approx\; \frac{G/M_{ref}}{(V\cdot \rho_{air})/M_{air}}\times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V$ in terms of $c_{ppm}$ and $G$:**

$$V \;=\; \frac{G \cdot M_{air}}{M_{ref}\cdot \rho_{air}\cdot c_{ppm}}\times 10^6$$

**Step 4 — Define required air-change rate:**
For complete dilution in time $t_{dilute}$ (seconds), the volumetric air flow is:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{V}{t_{dilute}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (Step 3) into (Step 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &\;=\; \frac{1}{t_{dilute}}\cdot \frac{G\cdot M_{air}}{M_{ref}\cdot \rho_{air}\cdot c_{ppm}}\times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \frac{G}{c_{lim}\cdot \rho_{air}\cdot t_{dilute}}\cdot \left(\frac{M_{air}}{M_{ref}}\times 10^6\right) \quad \text{where } c_{lim}=\frac{c_{ppm}}{10^6}\cdot\frac{M_{ref}}{M_{air}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a quick-design approximation in marine practice, the bracketed term is conservatively taken as **unity** when $M_{ref}/M_{air}\approx 1$, giving the working formula:

$$Q_{min} \;\approx\; \frac{G}{c_{lim}\cdot \rho_{air}\cdot t_{dilute}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — R-717 Leak in a Fish-Hold Plant

**Given:**
- Refrigerant inventory released in largest pipe section: $G = 50$ kg of R-717
- OEL of ammonia = **25 ppm**
- Design safety factor: dilute to **20 % of OEL** → $c_{lim} = 5$ ppm = $5\times10^{-6}$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³ at 30 °C
- Required dilution time: $t_{dilute} = 600$ s (10 minutes)

**Step 1 — Convert ppm to mass fraction:**

$$c_{lim} \;=\; 5\times 10^{-6}\cdot \frac{M_{R-717}}{M_{air}} \;\approx\; 5\times10^{-6}\cdot \frac{17.03}{28.97} \;\approx\; 2.94\times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into ventilation formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{min} &\;=\; \frac{50}{2.94\times10^{-6}\cdot 1.20\cdot 600} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \frac{50}{2.117\times10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&\;\approx\; 23\,620 \;\text{m}^3\text{/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This enormous number (≈ 23 600 m³/s) reveals why ammonia machinery rooms on ships are typically located in the open well-ventilated casing space or split into multiple compartments — pure dilution is impractical for large releases.** The safer design choice is to limit inventory per compartment via **sectionalisation** and **intermediate block valves**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Safety Group for Common Marine Refrigerants

| Refrigerant | Formula | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | ASHRAE Group | Critical Hazard on Ship |
|-------------|---------|-----|--------------|--------------|------------------------|
| R-12 (legacy) | CCl₂F₂ | **1.0** | **10 900** | A1 | Ozone + climate |
| R-22 (legacy) | CHClF₂ | **0.055** | **1 810** | A1 | Climate, phase-out |
| R-134a | CH₂FCF₃ | 0 | **1 430** | A1 | Climate |
| R-404A | Blend | 0 | **3 922** | A1 | Climate |
| R-507A | Blend | 0 | **3 985** | A1 | Climate |
| **R-744** | **CO₂** | **0** | **1** | **A1** | **Very high pressure (≈ 130 bar)** |
| **R-717** | **NH₃** | **0** | **0** | **B2L** | **Toxicity** |
| **R-290** | **C₃H₈** | 0 | **3** | **A3** | **High flammability** |
| **R-1234yf** | **CF₃CF=CH₂** | 0 | **< 1** | **A2L** | **Mild flammability** |

> [!NOTE]
