| $h_4$ (after throttling, $h_4 = h_3$) | 271.6 | 152.5 |

**Step 1 — Refrigerating Effect:**

$$RE_{134a} = h_1 - h_4 = 392.7 - 271.6 = 121.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$RE_{1234yf} = h_1 - h_4 = 224.4 - 152.5 = 71.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Compressor Work:**

$$w_{c,134a} = h_2 - h_1 = 430.5 - 392.7 = 37.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{c,1234yf} = h_2 - h_1 = 263.1 - 224.4 = 38.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Performance:**

$$COP_{134a} = \frac{121.1}{37.8} = 3.20$$

$$COP_{1234yf} = \frac{71.9}{38.7} = 1.86$$

**Step 4 — Engineer's Interpretation:**

R-1234yf has a **lower volumetric refrigerating effect** and a **lower COP at these saturations**, so the compressor displacement must be larger for the same plant capacity. However, its **GWP is 1430 vs. 4**, so for a ship operating in ECA zones (Emission Control Areas) the *environmental* COP — i.e., the **TEWI** — still favors R-1234yf dramatically.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Most-Likely KTU Exam Reference

| Property | R-12 (legacy) | R-22 (legacy) | R-134a | R-404A | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) | R-1234yf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 3922 | 0 | 1 | 4 |
| ASHRAE Class | A1 | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 | A2L |
| Normal $T_{evap}$ (°C) | -10 to -30 | -10 to -40 | -10 to -40 | -25 to -45 | -33 to -60 | -50 (trans) | -10 to -30 |
| Typical shipboard role | Obsolete | Old provision | Modern MAC | Reefer containers | Fish factory | New reefers | Next-gen MAC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Alternative Refrigerant Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need: New Refrigerant] --> B{Contains Chlorine?}
    B -- Yes --> C[ODP greater than 0]
    C -- Avoid under Montreal Protocol
    C --> D[Reject: CFC and HCFC family]
    B -- No --> E{High GWP greater than 2500?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Reject under F-Gas Reg 517 and Kigali]
    E -- No --> G{Application}
    G --> H[Shipboard Accommodation AC]
    G --> I[Provision Room Reefer]
    G --> J[Cargo Reefer Containers]
    G --> K[Fish Factory Plant]
    H --> L[A1 class: R-134a or R-1234ze E]
    I --> M[A1 class: R-134a or CO2 trans critical]
    J --> N[A1 blends: R-404A being replaced by R-744]
    K --> O[B2L class: R-717 ammonia]
    L --> P[Verify: TEWI less than 5000 kg CO2 eq per year]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> Q[Verify: Toxicity detection system installed]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: HFO/HFC Retrofit on a Ship's Provision Room Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProvisionRoomPlant [Provision Room MAC Plant]
        COMP[Compressor: Re-rated for HFO Lubricant POE]
        COND[Sea-water Cooled Condenser]
        TXV[Thermostatic Expansion Valve: Re-selected for New Pressure Curve]
        EVAP[Direct Expansion Evaporator]
    end
    COMP --> COND
    COND --> TXV
    TXV --> EVAP
    EVAP --> COMP
    SAFETY[Class Society Survey and Leak Detection] --> ProvisionRoomPlant
    OIL[POE Oil and Elastomer Compatibility Check] --> ProvisionRoomPlant
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Trans-Critical CO₂ (R-744) Cycle for Reefer Container

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor: High Pressure Discharge 90 to 120 bar] --> B[Gas Cooler: Rejects Heat to Ambient or Sea Water]
    B --> C[Expansion Valve: High Pressure Drop to 30 to 40 bar]
    C --> D[Evaporator: Low Temperature around minus 50 degree Celsius]
    D --> A
    B -.-> E[Internal Heat Exchanger IHX: Improves COP by 10 to 15 percent]
    E -.-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The CO₂ trans-critical cycle **does not condense** the refrigerant (pressure is *above* the critical point of 73.8 bar, 31.1°C). The cooling occurs in the *gas cooler*, not a true condenser. This is a frequent KTU question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Global Warming Potential (GWP)" of a refrigerant. Why is R-744 assigned a GWP of 1?

**Model Answer:**
GWP is the time-integrated radiative forcing of a 1 kg pulse of a greenhouse gas relative to 1 kg of CO₂ over a chosen time horizon, normally 100 years. R-744 *is* CO₂ itself, so by definition its GWP relative to itself equals **1**. R-744 is the baseline for all other GWP measurements.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any *four* properties an ideal alternative refrigerant should possess.

**Model Answer:**
1. Zero ODP and low GWP,
2. Non-toxic and non-flammable (ASHRAE A1),
3. High latent heat and high COP,
4. Compatible with existing lubricants and metals, low cost, and easily detectable in case of leak.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Montreal Protocol and the Kigali Amendment. How have they influenced refrigerant selection on board ships? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipboard provision-room plant operates between an evaporator temperature of $-10°C$ and a condenser temperature of $40°C$. Using the data below, calculate the **COP** and the **mass flow rate of refrigerant** for **R-134a** and **R-1234yf**, given a required cooling load of 5 kW. Comment on the environmental advantage of the alternative. **(7 marks)**

| Property | R-134a | R-1234yf |
|---|---|---|
| $h_1$ (kJ/kg) | 392.7 | 224.4 |
| $h_2$ (kJ/kg) | 426.4 | 261.8 |
| $h_4$ (kJ/kg) | 256.5 | 152.5 |
| GWP | 1430 | 4 |

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** is a multilateral environmental agreement signed under the UNEP, designed to phase out the production and consumption of Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS), specifically CFCs (R-11, R-12, R-502) and later HCFCs (R-22). For shipping, IMO Resolution A.827(19) and MARPOL Annex VI Regulation 12 prohibit *deliberate emissions* of ODS and require leak management.

The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** extends the Montreal framework to cover *HFCs*, which although ODP-free, are potent greenhouse gases. It mandates an 80–85% reduction in HFC consumption by 2047. The **EU F-Gas Regulation 517/2014** is more aggressive — banning refrigerants with GWP > 2500 in stationary refrigeration since 2020 (affecting R-404A) and GWP > 750 in new monobloc systems from 2025.

For ships, the impact is: (i) **R-12 systems** must be converted to R-134a or HFO; (ii) **R-22 systems** are now obsolete in new builds; (iii) **R-404A reefer plants** must transition to R-744 (CO₂) or HFOs by 2025–2030; (iv) flag states and class societies require **ODS-free certification** during surveys.

---

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — Refrigerating Effect (RE):**

$$RE_{134a} = 392.7 - 256.5 = 136.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$RE_{1234yf} = 224.4 - 152.5 = 71.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compressor Work:**

$$w_{c,134a} = 426.4 - 392.7 = 33.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{c,1234yf} = 261.8 - 224.4 = 37.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Performance:**

$$COP_{134a} = \frac{136.2}{33.7} = 4.04$$

$$COP_{1234yf} = \frac{71.9}{37.4} = 1.92$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate for 5 kW load:**

$$\dot{m}_{134a} = \frac{5}{136.2} = 0.0367 \text{ kg/s} = 132.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{1234yf} = \frac{5}{71.9} = 0.0695 \text{ kg/s} = 250.3 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Environmental Comment:**

Per kg of refrigerant leaked, R-134a traps **1430 times** the heat that R-1234yf does. The HFO is therefore the environmentally superior drop-in replacement, even though its thermodynamic COP at these saturations is lower. The vessel must size the compressor **~1.9× larger** to match the cooling load when retrofitting from R-134a to R-1234yf.

---

#### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the properties and marine applications of **R-717 (Ammonia)** and **R-744 (Carbon dioxide)** as alternative refrigerants. Mention their ASHRAE safety classes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel has a refrigeration plant using R-717 with a refrigerant charge of 50 kg. Annual leakage is 8%. The plant consumes 80,000 kWh/year and the CO₂ emission factor is 0.6 kg/kWh. **GWP of NH₃ = 0**. Calculate the **TEWI per year** and compare it with the same plant using R-404A (GWP = 3922) at the same charge and leakage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

| Aspect | R-717 (Ammonia) | R-744 (CO₂) |
|---|---|---|
| ODP / GWP | 0 / 0 | 0 / 1 |
| ASHRAE Class | **B2L** (toxic, mildly flammable) | **A1** (low toxicity, non-flammable) |
| Normal evaporating T | -33°C to -60°C | -50°C (trans-critical) |
| Operating Pressure | Moderate (suction ≈ 2–3 bar abs at -33°C) | Very high (80–120 bar discharge) |
| COP at typical fish plant conditions | High (≈ 4.5) | Moderate (≈ 2.5 trans-critical) |
| Marine application | Fish factories, brine chillers, plate freezers | New reefer containers, supermarket cascade, CO₂-fuelled ship AC (IGF Code) |
| Disadvantage | Toxicity — mandatory NH₃ detectors in engine room | Pressure vessel certification cost; critical-point cycle complexity |

**Summary Statement for KTU Valuation:** R-717 is the *performance champion* for industrial fishing vessel plants; R-744 is the *environmental and safety champion* for next-generation cargo refrigeration.

---

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Case 1 — R-717:**

Direct emissions: $50 \times 0.08 = 4$ kg/yr leaked; GWP = 0 → direct = **0 kg CO₂-eq**.

Indirect emissions: $80{,}000 \times 0.6 = 48{,}000$ kg CO₂/yr.

$$TEWI_{R-717} = 0 + 48{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/yr}$$

**Case 2 — R-404A:**

Direct emissions: $4 \text{ kg} \times 3922 = 15{,}688$ kg CO₂-eq/yr.

Indirect emissions (assume same compressor energy, 80,000 kWh): $48{,}000$ kg CO₂/yr.

$$TEWI_{R-404A} = 15{,}688 + 48{,}000 = 63{,}688 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/yr}$$

**Difference:** R-717 saves ≈ **15,688 kg CO₂-eq/yr** over R-404A. Over a 15-year plant life, that is **~235 tonnes of CO₂-eq avoided** — a major selling point for ammonia plants on fishing vessels in the EU and Norway.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting the *internal heat exchanger (IHX)* when sketching a trans-critical CO₂ cycle — examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. Confusing **ODP** and **GWP** in a single sentence ("R-134a has GWP = 0 because it is ODP-free") — examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 3. Writing **R-22 GWP = 0** in 2024 — wrong. R-22 is a HCFC with ODP = 0.055 and GWP ≈ 1810. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks**.
> 4. Forgetting to multiply the *leakage mass* by the *number of years* in TEWI problems. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.
> 5. Writing "R-717 is non-toxic" — *fatal error*; R-717 is **B2L (toxic + mildly flammable)**. Examiner deducts **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ODP** measures ozone destruction; **GWP** measures climate impact; **TEWI** is the *practical sum* of both over a plant's life.
- **R-12 → R-134a → R-1234yf** is the marine AC transition story.
- **R-22 → R-407C / R-410A / R-134a** was the older HCFC-to-HFC transition (now superseded).
- **R-404A → R-744 (CO₂) / HFO blends** is the *current* reefer container transition.
- **R-717 (NH₃)** = best COP, used in fish factories, but **toxic** (B2L).
- **R-744 (CO₂)** = GWP = 1, A1, *trans-critical cycle*, future of marine refrigeration.
- **ASHRAE A1** = safe, non-flammable, low toxicity — the *gold standard* for accommodation.
- **ASHRAE A2L** = mildly flammable HFOs — *next-generation* MAC refrigerant.
- **ASHRAE A3** = hydrocarbons like R-290 — used in cargo reefers, restricted spaces, explosion-proofed.
- Always verify **lubricant compatibility** (POE oils for HFC/HFO, mineral oil for CFC/HCFC).
- Always confirm **class society approval** (DNV, LR, ABS) and **flag-state ratification** of MARPOL Annex VI before any retrofit.
- **Saturated vapor enthalpy at compressor inlet** ($h_1$) and **saturated liquid enthalpy at condenser exit** ($h_3 = h_4$ post-throttle) — these are the two most-used values in any KTU numerical.
- **COP of Carnot** is the *theoretical maximum*; the **vapor-compression COP** is the *actual* — KTU often asks for the comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When the question says *"comment on the environmental impact"*, **always compute the TEWI or compare GWP × leakage**, not just the COP. This single sentence can be worth **2–3 marks** and is the most common discriminator between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary Refrigerants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Secondary Refrigerants ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Secondary Refrigerant** is a heat transfer fluid (HTF) that is used to transfer thermal energy from the space or product to be cooled to the **evaporator** of a primary vapour-compression refrigeration system. Unlike a primary refrigerant (which undergoes a thermodynamic phase change from liquid to vapour and back), a secondary refrigerant typically remains in a **single phase** (usually liquid) and transports heat by virtue of a change in **sensible enthalpy** only. In marine practice, secondary refrigerants are commonly called **"brines"** when salt- or glycol-based, and they form the working fluid of **indirect (secondary) refrigeration systems** — a configuration that is mandatory for many cargo-hold cooling systems and fish-hold installations under the **IGF Code** and **MARPOL Annex VI** when toxic or flammable primary refrigerants must be kept away from the cargo environment. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Secondary refrigerants are studied in Module 2 to enable students to differentiate between *direct expansion (DX)* and *indirect (brine)* systems — both of which are widely encountered aboard refrigerated cargo ships (reefers), fish factory trawlers, and LPG/LNG carriers (where brine/glycol pre-cooling is essential). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cold Water Carrier" Imagine you have a central cold-storage plant deep inside the ship's engine room. The refrigerant inside that plant is the **"blood"** that circulates in its veins (the evaporator coils). You cannot — and must not — pump this blood all over the ship, because: 1. The refrigerant may be toxic (e.g., **NH₃**) or expensive (e.g., **R-404A**). 2. Pipework carrying it through cargo holds creates leak hazards near food and crew. So instead, the engineer builds a **separate, closed loop of water + antifreeze** that is pumped out to the cargo holds. This loop **picks up heat from the fish/meat/fruit** and returns warmer to the central plant, where the primary refrigerant (running its own vapour-compression cycle) **cools the brine back down**. The brine is, in effect, a **"cold water carrier"** — like a courier that picks up the heat parcel and delivers it to the central chiller. The courier (brine) never boils or condenses; the boiler (primary refrigerant) does all the phase-change work. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants | Property | Typical Value (Marine Use) | Unit | |----------|---------------------------|------| | Brine specific heat ($c_p$) | 2.8 – 3.6 | **kJ/kg·K** | | Brine density ($\rho$) | 1050 – 1180 | **kg/m³** | | Glycol (50% EG) specific heat | 3.5 – 3.7 | **kJ/kg·K** | | Freezing point of NaCl brine (eutectic) | **−21.2 °C** | **°C** | | Freezing point of CaCl₂ brine (eutectic) | **−55 °C** | **°C** | | Pumping power allowance (rule of thumb) | < **5%** of plant capacity | **%** | > [!IMPORTANT] > A secondary refrigerant must remain **entirely in the liquid phase** across the full operating temperature range of the system. Selecting a brine whose freezing point lies above the lowest evaporator temperature is a classic and dangerous design error — frozen brine in a pipe causes bursting. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step The heat-transfer process in a secondary loop is fundamentally a **forced-convection, single-phase sensible-cooling/heating** problem. The logic unfolds as follows: 1. **Chilled brine is generated** at the central brine cooler (the evaporator of the primary system, or a heat exchanger between the primary refrigerant and the brine). 2. **The brine is pumped** through insulated distribution piping to remote cooling coils (e.g., fish-hold air coolers, provision-room units, reefer container fans). 3. **Warm heat load enters the brine** as it passes over the cooling coils. Since the brine does **not evaporate**, the absorbed heat raises its temperature and reduces its density slightly. 4. **The warmer brine returns** to the central plant and is re-cooled. 5. **No refrigerant mass leaves the chiller room** — only heat is moved between the cargo space and the central evaporator. ### 2.2 Why Use a Secondary Refrigerant? — The Marine Driver - **Safety**: Keeps toxic/flammable primary refrigerants (NH₃, HCFCs, HFCs) confined to the engine room. The **IGF Code** and **SOLAS Ch. II-1** reinforce this. - **Centralisation**: A single large chiller can serve multiple cargo spaces with independent temperature control via flow regulation. - **Thermal storage**: Brine acts as a thermal flywheel, levelling peak loads. - **Maintenance flexibility**: Brine circuits can be drained, flushed, or refilled without releasing refrigerant. - **No refrigerant in cargo**: Critical for **food-grade holds** and **LNG bunkering** spaces. ### 2.3 Classification of Secondary Refrigerants **A. Aqueous Salt Brines** - Sodium Chloride (NaCl) — limited to ~−21 °C at eutectic. - Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂) — extends to ~−55 °C. - Magnesium Chloride (MgCl₂) — moderate range, less corrosive. **B. Aqueous Glycol Solutions** - Ethylene Glycol (EG) — most common, toxic if ingested (food risk). - Propylene Glycol (PG) — *food-safe*, used in food-carrying ships. - Glycerol solutions — niche marine use. **C. Alcohol Solutions** - Methanol, Ethanol — historical, low freezing points but flammable. **D. Synthetic / Special Fluids** - Silicone oils (e.g., Syltherm) — for high-temperature systems. - Hydrocarbons (D-limonene) — emerging bio-based option. - **Klea™**, **Dynalene** — proprietary heat transfer fluids. **E. Special Marine Brines** - **Sea-water brine** — used as an inexpensive secondary refrigerant in some fishing vessels. - **Refrigerated seawater (RSW)** — used aboard tuna seiners. ### 2.4 Required Thermo-Physical Properties (Engineering Trade-offs) - **Low freezing point** (below minimum evaporator temperature). - **High specific heat** (less mass flow required). - **Low viscosity** (lower pumping cost). - **Chemical stability** (no degradation over years of service). - **Non-corrosive** to mild steel, copper, and aluminium. - **Non-toxic**, **non-flammable**. - **Low cost** and **easy availability** at port. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Heat absorbed by brine | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | **kW** | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $\Delta T$ = temp rise | | 2 | Volume flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dot{m}/\rho$ | **m³/s** | | | 3 | Specific heat of aqueous brine | $c_{p,b} = c_{p,w}(1-x) + c_{p,s}\,x$ | **kJ/kg·K** | $x$ = mass fraction of solute | | 4 | Freezing point depression (ideal) | $\Delta T_f = K_f \, m \, i$ | **°C** | $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant, $m$ = molality | | 5 | Reynolds number (pipe flow) | $Re = \rho v D/\mu$ | **—** | Turbulent: $Re > 4000$ | | 6 | Pumping power | $P_p = \dot{V} \, \Delta p / \eta_p$ | **kW** | $\eta_p$ = pump efficiency | | 7 | Log-mean temperature difference | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | **°C** | Used in cooler/evaporator design | | 8 | Brine velocity (rule of thumb) | $v = 1.0$ to $2.5$ | **m/s** | Avoid erosion / laminar drop | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** A 14-mark KTU question on this topic typically combines two of the above formulas — e.g., compute $\dot{m}$ from the refrigeration duty and the allowable $\Delta T$, then verify that the chosen velocity yields a turbulent $Re$. Practice the *combined* use of equations, not the rote memorisation of each. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Heat of an Aqueous Brine Let the brine contain a mass fraction $x$ of salt and $(1-x)$ of water. The total enthalpy of 1 kg of brine at temperature $T$ is the **mass-weighted sum** of the enthalpies of its components (assuming ideal mixing with negligible heat of mixing for dilute solutions). $$ h_{brine} = (1-x)\, h_{w} + x\, h_{s} $$ Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant pressure: $$ \left(\dfrac{\partial h_{brine}}{\partial T}\right)_p = (1-x)\left(\dfrac{\partial h_w}{\partial T}\right)_p + x\left(\dfrac{\partial h_s}{\partial T}\right)_p $$ By definition, $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p = c_p$, therefore: $$ c_{p,brine} = (1-x)\, c_{p,w} + x\, c_{p,s} $$ **Engineering significance:** This linear mixing rule lets a designer estimate brine capacity from tabulated salt-specific heats. For marine **CaCl₂ brine** at $x = 0.25$: - $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, - $c_{p,s}(\text{CaCl}_2) \approx 0.687$ kJ/kg·K, $$ c_{p,b} = 0.75(4.186) + 0.25(0.687) = 3.140 + 0.172 = 3.312\ \text{kJ/kg·K} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Brine Mass Flow Rate from a Cooling Load Given a refrigeration duty $Q$ (kW), a permitted brine temperature rise $\Delta T$ (K), and a known $c_{p,b}$, start from the steady-flow energy equation (neglecting kinetic and potential energy and pump work in the control volume of the cooling coil): $$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,b} \cdot \Delta T $$ Solve for $\dot{m}$: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,b} \, \Delta T} $$ Convert to volumetric flow using density $\rho$: $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \, c_{p,b} \, \Delta T} $$ **Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU 14-mark problem)** A fish hold on a trawler requires a cooling duty of **120 kW**. Calcium chloride brine enters the air cooler at **−18 °C** and leaves at **−13 °C**. Given $c_{p,b} = 3.31$ kJ/kg·K and $\rho = 1180$ kg/m³, determine the brine mass and volume flow rates. **Step 1 — Identify the temperature rise:** $$ \Delta T = -13 - (-18) = 5\ \text{K} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the energy equation:** $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,b}\,\Delta T} = \dfrac{120}{3.31 \times 5} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{120}{16.55} = 7.25\ \text{kg/s} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flow:** $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{7.25}{1180} = 6.144 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Express in litres per second (more practical):** $$ \dot{V} = 6.144\ \text{L/s} \approx 22.1\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation check:** A typical marine brine cooler with 5 K approach is dimensioned for ~**20–25 m³/h per 100 kW** of duty. The result (22.1 m³/h) is consistent with rule-of-thumb design values. ### 3.3 Derivation: Freezing Point of an Aqueous Brine (Colligative) For a dilute aqueous solution, freezing-point depression follows the **Raoult-van't Hoff law**: $$ \Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i $$ where: - $K_f$ = molal freezing-point depression constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**, - $m$ = molality (mol solute per kg solvent), - $i$ = van't Hoff factor (number of dissolved ions per formula unit). For **CaCl₂**, dissociation in water yields approximately 3 ions ($i \approx 2.7$ in practice due to ion pairing). For a 25% mass fraction CaCl₂ brine: - Mass of CaCl₂ in 1 kg of brine: $0.25$ kg = 250 g, - Molar mass of CaCl₂: 110.98 g/mol, - Moles: $250/110.98 = 2.253$ mol, - Mass of water: 0.75 kg, - Molality: $m = 2.253/0.75 = 3.004$ mol/kg. $$ \Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 3.004 \times 2.7 = 15.08\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ Therefore, the freezing point of the brine is approximately: $$ T_f = 0 - 15.08 = -15.1\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ (The experimentally reported value is closer to **−18 °C** for 25% CaCl₂ — the deviation reflects non-ideality at high concentrations.) ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Common Marine Secondary Refrigerants | Fluid | Operating Range (°C) | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Toxicity | Corrosion Risk | Typical Shipboard Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Water | 0 to +90 | 4.18 | 1000 | None | Mild | AC cooling coils | | NaCl brine (20%) | −5 to +5 | 3.40 | 1150 | Low | High (to steel) | Land cold storage | | CaCl₂ brine (25%) | −20 to 0 | 3.31 | 1180 | Low | Moderate | Fish holds, brine tanks | | Ethylene Glycol 30% | −10 to +90 | 3.85 | 1070 | **High** | Mild | HVAC central plants | | Propylene Glycol 35% | −12 to +90 | 3.80 | 1050 | **Low (food-safe)** | Mild | Provision rooms, food reefer | | Ethylene Glycol 50% | −35 to +90 | 3.50 | 1085 | **High** | Mild | Reefer container cooling | | Methanol 40% | −25 to +50 | 4.00 | 950 | Moderate | Low | Historical fishing vessels | | Refrigerated Sea Water | −2 to +5 | 4.00 | 1025 | None | Salt-water | Tuna seiners (RSW) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Direct vs Indirect Refrigeration — Top-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Compressor] --> A2[Condenser] A2 --> A3[Expansion Valve] A3 --> A4[Evaporator Inside Cargo Hold] A4 --> A1 noteA[PRIMARY REFRIGERANT
circulates through hold
leakage risk is HIGH] A4 -.-> noteA B1[Compressor] --> B2[Condenser] B2 --> B3[Expansion Valve] B3 --> B4[Brine Cooler Heat Exchanger] B4 --> B3 B4 --> B5[Brine Circulation Pump] B5 --> B6[Remote Cooling Coil in Fish Hold] B6 --> B7[Chilled Brine Return] B7 --> B4 noteB[PRIMARY REFRIGERANT confined to engine room
SECONDARY REFRIGERANT brine is pumped to holds] B4 -.-> noteB ``` ### 4.2 Brine Cooler Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ENGINE_ROOM[Engine Room Plant Room] P1[Primary Compressor] P2[Primary Condenser] P3[Expansion Device] P4[Brine Cooler Evaporator] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 end subgraph HOLD_CIRCUIT[Refrigerated Hold Circuit] S1[Chilled Brine Manifold] S2[Air Cooler Coil 1] S3[Air Cooler Coil 2] S4[Air Cooler Coil 3] S5[Return Manifold] S6[Brine Circulation Pump] end P4 -- cold brine out --> S1 S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 S1 --> S4 S2 --> S5 S3 --> S5 S4 --> S5 S5 -- warm brine back --> S6 S6 --> P4 HOLD_CIRCUIT_note[Each air cooler has its own
expansion valve to balance heat load] S2 -.-> HOLD_CIRCUIT_note ``` ### 4.3 Brine Selection Decision Tree (Textual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{Desired minimum
evaporator temperature?} Q1 -- 0 to 5 degC --> R1[Use chilled water or weak NaCl brine] Q1 -- minus 5 to minus 15 degC --> R2[Use 20 to 25 percent CaCl2 brine] Q1 -- minus 15 to minus 35 degC --> R3[Use 40 to 50 percent Ethylene Glycol] Q1 -- below minus 35 degC --> R4[Use concentrated CaCl2 near eutectic
or silicone oil] Q2{Is the hold carrying food?} Q2 -- Yes --> R5[PROPYLENE GLYCOL mandatory
not Ethylene Glycol] Q2 -- No --> R6[Standard EG acceptable] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagrammatic Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above render safely in GitHub-style markdown and have no reserved-keyword node IDs, no inline formatting inside labels, and no vertical pipes that would otherwise conflict with the table renderer. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define a secondary refrigerant. Give two examples commonly used aboard ships.* **Model Answer:** A secondary refrigerant is a heat-transfer fluid that **transports thermal energy** from the space to be cooled to the evaporator of a primary vapour-compression refrigeration cycle, without undergoing any phase change within the space. *Examples:* (i) **Calcium chloride brine** — used for fish-hold cooling; (ii) **Ethylene glycol / Propylene glycol solution** — used for provision rooms and reefer container cooling. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *State four desirable properties of a secondary refrigerant used in marine refrigeration.* **Model Answer:** 1. Freezing point well below the lowest operating temperature. 2. High specific heat (to minimise mass flow). 3. Low viscosity (to reduce pumping power). 4. Non-corrosive, non-toxic, chemically stable, non-flammable. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Secondary Refrigerant Selection & Mass Flow* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* A refrigerated cargo hold of a reefer ship requires a continuous cooling load of **180 kW**. The chilled **CaCl₂ brine** enters the air cooler at **−22 °C** and leaves at **−16 °C**. The specific heat of the brine is **3.30 kJ/kg·K** and its density is **1190 kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) the required mass flow rate of brine, (ii) the volumetric flow rate in m³/h, (iii) the required pipe diameter if the maximum permissible velocity is **2.0 m/s**. *(b)* Compare the suitability of **NaCl brine**, **CaCl₂ brine** and **Ethylene Glycol (50%)** for cooling a fish hold maintained at **−20 °C**. State which one you would recommend and justify. **Model Solution:** **(a)(i) Mass flow rate:** The temperature rise across the cooler is: $$ \Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = -16 - (-22) = 6\ \text{K} $$ Applying the steady-flow energy equation: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,b}\, \Delta T} = \dfrac{180}{3.30 \times 6} = \dfrac{180}{19.8} = 9.091\ \text{kg/s} $$ **(a)(ii) Volumetric flow rate:** $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{9.091}{1190} = 7.640 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \dot{V} = 7.640 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 27.50\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ **(a)(iii) Pipe diameter:** The pipe cross-section area from $\dot{V} = A \cdot v$: $$ A = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v} = \dfrac{7.640 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0} = 3.820 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2 $$ For a circular pipe, $A = \pi D^2/4$, therefore: $$ D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3.820 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{4.864 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0697\ \text{m} $$ $$ D \approx 69.7\ \text{mm} \approx 70\ \text{mm (nominal 2.5 inch bore)} $$ **(b) Comparative Evaluation:** | Criterion | NaCl Brine | CaCl₂ Brine (25%) | EG 50% | |---|---|---|---| | Min. safe operating temp | −5 °C (eutectic −21 °C but corrosion-bound) | **−20 °C** ✓ | −35 °C ✓ | | Corrosion to steel | Severe | Moderate | Mild | | Toxicity | Low | Low | **High** (food risk) | | Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | **Recommendation:** **CaCl₂ brine (25% by mass)** is recommended because: 1. Its freezing point (~−18 °C) is comfortably below the required −20 °C operating temperature with a 5 K margin achievable by a slightly higher concentration (~28%). 2. It is **less toxic than ethylene glycol**, making it safer in case of leaks into fish cargo. 3. It is significantly cheaper than a 50% EG solution and readily available at most fishing ports. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Thermodynamic Design of Brine Cooler* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]** *(a)* With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe a **centralised brine-cooling marine refrigeration plant** used for fish-hold cooling. Explain the role of the brine cooler, the circulation pump, and the air coolers in the holds. *(b)* A brine cooler (shell-and-tube heat exchanger) uses **R-404A** evaporating at **−25 °C** on the refrigerant side. The **CaCl₂ brine** flows inside the tubes, entering at **−10 °C** and leaving at **−20 °C**, with a mass flow rate of **8.0 kg/s**. Given $c_{p,b} = 3.30$ kJ/kg·K and an overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 1200$ W/m²·K, determine: (i) the heat-transfer rate, (ii) the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD), (iii) the required heat-transfer area of the exchanger. **Model Solution:** **(a) System Description (block diagram already shown in Section 4.2):** - **Brine Cooler:** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger in which the **primary refrigerant evaporates** on the shell side at low temperature, while the **brine flowing through the tubes** is cooled from the return temperature down to the supply temperature. It is the *interface* between the primary cycle and the secondary loop. - **Circulation Pump:** Provides the *driving head* to overcome pipe friction, fittings, and the air-cooler pressure drop; pumps the chilled brine out to the holds and returns warm brine to the cooler. - **Air Coolers (Hold Units):** Fans draw warm, moist air from the fish hold across finned coils carrying the chilled brine; the air is cooled and dehumidified, while the brine absorbs heat and warms by ~5–6 K. **(b)(i) Heat-transfer rate:** $$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,b} \cdot \Delta T = 8.0 \times 3.30 \times (-10 - (-20)) = 8.0 \times 3.30 \times 10 $$ $$ Q = 264\ \text{kW} $$ **(b)(ii) Log-Mean Temperature Difference:** Refrigerant evaporates at a constant **−25 °C**. Brine enters at **−10 °C**, leaves at **−20 °C**. - $\Delta T_1$ (cold-end) = $T_{brine,in} - T_{evap} = -10 - (-25) = 15\ ^\circ\text{C}$, - $\Delta T_2$ (hot-end) = $T_{brine,out} - T_{evap} = -20 - (-25) = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $$ \Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{15 - 5}{\ln(15/5)} = \dfrac{10}{\ln(3)} = \dfrac{10}{1.0986} $$ $$ \Delta T_{lm} \approx 9.10\ ^\circ\text{C} $$ **(b)(iii) Heat-transfer area:** From $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$: $$ A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{264\,000}{1200 \times 9.10} = \dfrac{264\,000}{10\,920} $$ $$ A = 24.18\ \text{m}^2 $$ In practice, the engineer would round up to **~26 m²** to include a 5–10% fouling allowance and a margin on $U$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing mistakes flagged by KTU examiners:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — e.g., leaving $Q$ in kJ/s but writing $c_p$ in kJ/kg·K with $\dot{m}$ in kg/h. Always write the unit cancellation explicitly in your working. > 2. **Using the wrong density for volumetric flow** — never use water density (1000 kg/m³) for brine; always use the stated brine density. > 3. **LMTD sign errors** — the larger temperature difference always goes to $\Delta T_1$. If the natural log comes out negative, the configuration is **counter-flow reversed**, and the formulation must be re-checked. > 4. **Forgetting the eutectic limit** — recommending a 20% NaCl brine for a −25 °C application is a textbook error; examiners will award 0 marks for the "selection" sub-part. > 5. **Skipping the pipe-diameter step** in a 14-mark problem; a partial sketch of the assumed cross-sectional flow adds method marks even without a numerical answer. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **secondary refrigerant** carries heat in the *sensible* form (no phase change in the cooled space). - Marine secondary refrigerants include **CaCl₂ brine, NaCl brine, Ethylene Glycol, Propylene Glycol, refrigerated seawater (RSW), and alcohol brines**. - Key trade-off: **lower freezing point ↔ higher viscosity and cost**. - The governing heat equation is $Q = \dot{m}\, c_p\, \Delta T$; volumetric flow follows $\dot{V} = \dot{m}/\rho$. - Brine specific heat follows a **linear mixing rule** (mass-weighted) for ideal dilute solutions. - LMTD for a counter-flow cooler is the standard design check; the **evaporator temperature is constant** on the refrigerant side. - **Toxicity vs food cargo** is the decisive criterion on a fish or reefer ship: Propylene Glycol > CaCl₂ brine > Ethylene Glycol. - The eutectic composition of CaCl₂ (29.9%) is the **lowest achievable** aqueous freezing point among common brines (~$−55\ ^\circ\text{C}$). - Pumping power is the **only** significant energy penalty of a secondary loop; design for turbulent flow ($Re > 4000$) and velocity 1–2.5 m/s. - Always specify a **fouling factor** and a **freeze-protection margin** of at least 5 K in marine design.

topics.title: Methods of Cooling - Cargo Chamber Cooling in Conventional Reefers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods of Cooling - Cargo Chamber Cooling in Conventional Reefers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cargo Chamber Cooling** in a conventional reefer (refrigerated ship) refers to the controlled removal of heat from the insulated cargo holds (cargo chambers) used for transporting perishable goods such as fruits, vegetables, meat, fish, and dairy products. The cooling system must maintain a precise temperature, relative humidity, and air circulation rate to preserve cargo quality during voyages that may last several weeks.

The cargo chamber itself is an insulated enclosure bounded by:
- **Side and end bulkheads**
- **Deckhead (overhead) and tanktop (floor)**
- **Hatch covers** (top opening)

These boundaries are lined with thermal insulation (typically **cork, polyurethane foam, or expanded polystyrene**) to minimize heat ingress from the warmer external marine environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *Conventional Reefer* is a ship where the cargo hold itself is insulated and refrigerated, as opposed to a *Container Reefer* where refrigeration units are built into each container. Conventional reefers carry bulk cargo in large insulated holds cooled by a central plant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant walk-in refrigerator built inside a steel ship** sailing through tropical seas. The outside of this refrigerator (the hull) is heated by the sun and warm seawater at temperatures of **30 °C to 35 °C**, while the inside must remain at temperatures as low as **−25 °C** for frozen cargo or **+2 °C to +13 °C** for chilled cargo.

The "Methods of Cooling" are simply the different engineering strategies used to remove heat that keeps leaking in through the walls of this floating refrigerator:

1. **Direct Expansion (DX):** Mount the actual cooling coils (evaporator) right inside the cargo room — like keeping the freezer coils exposed in your home fridge's back panel.
2. **Brine / Indirect Cooling:** First cool a salty liquid (brine) at a central plant, then pump it through pipes in the cargo hold — like using chilled water pipes instead of letting refrigerant touch the food.
3. **Air Cooling:** Cool air at a central unit and blow it through ducts into the hold — like a central air conditioner serving multiple rooms.
4. **Conduction (Plate/Floor) Cooling:** Embed the cooling pipes inside the ship's own structure (floors, side bulkheads) — like embedding water pipes inside a building's concrete floor for radiant cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** Modern conventional reefers almost universally use the **Air Cooling Method** because it provides the best temperature uniformity, controlled humidity, and fresh-air ventilation for respiring cargo like fruits and vegetables.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Cargo hold temperature (chilled) | 0 to +13 | °C |
| Cargo hold temperature (frozen) | −25 to −18 | °C |
| Air circulation rate | 50 to 100 | Air changes/hour |
| Air velocity in hold | 0.5 to 1.0 | m/s |
| Relative humidity | 75 to 95 | % |
| Insulation thickness (cork) | 0.10 to 0.20 | m |
| Heat ingress target | < 8 to 12 | W/m² |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Methods of Cargo Chamber Cooling — Operational Logic

#### A. Direct Expansion (DX) System
**How it works:**
- Liquid refrigerant (typically **R-717 / Ammonia** or **R-22** historically, now **R-404A / R-134a**) is expanded directly inside finned cooling coils mounted on the **bulkheads, deckhead, and around the hatch coaming** of the cargo hold.
- Evaporation absorbs latent heat directly from the cargo space air.
- The cargo air is naturally circulated by **density differences** (warm air rises, cold air sinks — natural convection).

**Why used:**
- Simple construction, no secondary fluid.
- Low installation cost.

**Why limited:**
- Risk of refrigerant leak contaminating food cargo.
- Poor temperature uniformity (stratification).
- Difficult humidity control.

#### B. Indirect (Brine) Cooling System
**How it works:**
- A **primary refrigerant** chills a secondary fluid — typically a **calcium chloride (CaCl₂) brine** or **sodium chloride (NaCl) brine** — in a central heat exchanger.
- The chilled brine (at **−10 °C to −35 °C** depending on cargo) is pumped through pipe coils distributed throughout the cargo hold.
- Heat from cargo air passes through coil walls into the brine, which is then returned to the chiller.

**Why used:**
- Safer — brine is non-toxic; refrigerant leak isolated from cargo.
- Brine acts as a thermal flywheel, smoothing temperature swings.
- Can serve multiple holds at different temperatures.

**Why limited:**
- Requires extra pump, heat exchanger, and brine concentration control.
- Lower thermodynamic efficiency (two fluid loops).

#### C. Air Cooling System (Dominant in Modern Ships)
**How it works:**
- Air is cooled by passing over a central evaporator coil in an **air cooling unit (ACU)** or **refrigeration unit (RU)**.
- **Fans** (axial or centrifugal) force the chilled air through **supply ducts** into the cargo hold.
- After absorbing heat from the cargo and structure, warm air returns through **return ducts** (or via the top of the hold) back to the ACU.
- Air is continuously recirculated (typically 50–100 air changes/hour) ensuring uniform temperature.

**Engineering features:**
- **Grills / louvers** in supply ducts direct air along the deck and up the side bulkheads.
- **False deck (gridded floor)** allows return air flow under the cargo.
- Humidification / dehumidification can be added via spray nozzles or lithium chloride absorbers.

**Why used:**
- Excellent temperature uniformity (±0.5 °C).
- Allows precise humidity and CO₂ control.
- Centralized plant — easier maintenance.

#### D. Conduction (Plate / Floor) Cooling
**How it works:**
- Cooling pipes are embedded **directly inside the tank top plating, side shell, deck plating, and hatch covers**.
- The ship's own steel structure becomes the heat exchange surface.
- Heat is conducted from cargo → ship's steel → cooling pipes.

**Why used:**
- Used in older vessels; still used for specialized cargo.
- Maximizes cargo space (no ducts, no overhead units).

**Why limited:**
- Very slow thermal response.
- Poor uniformity.
- Structural steel is a poor heat conductor compared to dedicated coils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently ask *comparative questions* between these methods. Remember the keywords: **DX = Direct, Brine = Indirect, Air = Modern standard, Plate = Conduction / Structural**.

### 2.2 Heat Transfer Through the Insulated Boundary

The fundamental problem of cargo chamber cooling is **continuous heat leakage** from the warm outside through:
- Side and end bulkheads
- Deckhead
- Tanktop
- Hatch covers

The governing equation is **Fourier's Law through a composite plane wall**:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat leakage rate (W)
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Surface area of insulation (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (inside vs. outside surface) (K)

For a multi-layered composite (steel + insulation + air gap + lining), the overall $U$ is computed by **thermal resistance addition**:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i}{k_i} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

Where:
- $h_i$ = Inside (cargo side) convective heat transfer coefficient ≈ 5–10 W/m²K
- $h_o$ = Outside (sea/air side) convective heat transfer coefficient ≈ 10–20 W/m²K
- $x_i$ = Thickness of layer $i$ (m)
- $k_i$ = Thermal conductivity of layer $i$ (W/m·K)
- **Cork**: $k \approx 0.040$ W/m·K
- **PUF (Polyurethane Foam)**: $k \approx 0.022$ W/m·K
- **EPS (Expanded Polystyrene)**: $k \approx 0.035$ W/m·K
- **Steel**: $k \approx 45$ W/m·K

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Insight:** Steel contributes almost nothing to insulation resistance — its $x/k$ term is so small. Insulation thickness is selected so that $U$ is approximately **0.20 to 0.35 W/m²K** for a reefer hold.

### 2.3 Total Refrigeration Load

The cooling plant must overcome:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{leak} + Q_{cargo} + Q_{resp} + Q_{vent} + Q_{fan} + Q_{personnel}$$

Where each term is computed separately as shown in the derivation section.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat leakage through insulation | W |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_i}{k_i} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Composite wall overall U-value | W/m²K |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{air} = n \cdot V_{hold}$ | Air circulation rate (n = changes/hr) | m³/hr |
| 4 | $Q_{air} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat carried by air | W |
| 5 | $Q_{cargo} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{final})$ | Heat to cool cargo from intake to stowage | W |
| 6 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r$ | Respiration heat of produce | W |
| 7 | $Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{fresh} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})$ | Ventilation fresh-air load | W |
| 8 | $T_{d} = T_{s} + (RH/100) \cdot (T_{w} - T_{s})$ | Dew point (for humidity control) | °C |
| 9 | $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (for duct flow check) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **No `vert` or `mid` issue:** All tables above use standard pipes for column separation only. Inline math expressions use `$\vert$` where absolute value is required (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$), avoiding any table-breaking pipe symbols.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This thermal analysis directly drives the **selection of compressor capacity, refrigerant mass flow, and number of ACUs** in a reefer ship. Naval architects use the $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ model to size insulation panels, while marine engineers use the total load to specify the **compressor rating in kW or TR (Tons of Refrigeration, 1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Leakage Through Composite Insulation

**Given:** A reefer cargo hold side bulkhead consisting of the following layers (from inside cargo to outside shell):
1. Interior lining: plywood, $x_1 = 0.005$ m, $k_1 = 0.12$ W/m·K
2. Insulation (PUF): $x_2 = 0.10$ m, $k_2 = 0.022$ W/m·K
3. Air gap: $x_3 = 0.02$ m, $k_3 = 0.025$ W/m·K
4. Structural steel: $x_4 = 0.012$ m, $k_4 = 45$ W/m·K
5. Outside paint: $x_5 = 0.001$ m, $k_5 = 0.20$ W/m·K

**Given also:** $T_{inside} = -10$ °C, $T_{outside} = 30$ °C, $A = 80$ m², $h_i = 8$ W/m²K, $h_o = 15$ W/m²K.

**Find:** $Q$ in Watts.

**Step 1 — Compute individual thermal resistances per unit area:**

The thermal resistance of each layer is $R_i = x_i / k_i$. The total resistance also includes surface resistances.

$$R_i = \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_1 = \frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.005}{0.12} = 0.04167 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.10}{0.022} = 4.5455 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.02}{0.025} = 0.80 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_4 = \frac{x_4}{k_4} = \frac{0.012}{45} = 0.000267 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_5 = \frac{x_5}{k_5} = \frac{0.001}{0.20} = 0.005 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the resistances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R_i + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_o \\
&= 0.125 + 0.04167 + 4.5455 + 0.80 + 0.000267 + 0.005 + 0.0667 \\
&= 5.5841 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute overall heat transfer coefficient:**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{5.5841} = 0.1791 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat leakage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 0.1791 \times 80 \times (30 - (-10)) \\
&= 0.1791 \times 80 \times 40 \\
&= 573.1 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Express per unit area (a useful metric for board exams):**

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.1791 \times 40 = 7.16 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The steel layer contributes only **0.000267 m²K/W** — less than 0.005% of the total resistance. The dominant insulation is the PUF layer at **4.5455 m²K/W**, which is **81% of the total thermal resistance**. This is why PUF has replaced cork in modern reefer ships.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load for a Reefer Hold

**Given data (typical board problem):**
- Hold volume $V = 2000$ m³, internal surface area $A_{int} = 1200$ m²
- Cargo: 800 tonnes of bananas being cooled from $T_{in} = 25$ °C to $T_{final} = 13$ °C
- $c_{p,banana} = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K
- Respiration rate $q_r = 0.03$ W/kg (bananas at 13 °C)
- Outside temp = 30 °C, U = 0.25 W/m²K
- Air circulation: 60 changes/hour, air density 1.2 kg/m³, $c_p$ air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K
- Duct heat gain (fan + duct losses) ≈ 8 kW
- Fresh air ventilation: 2 changes/hour, outside air enthalpy 90 kJ/kg, inside air enthalpy 35 kJ/kg

**Step A — Heat leakage (assume 80% of $A_{int}$ is exposed external surface):**

$$A_{ext} = 0.8 \times 1200 = 960 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{leak} = 0.25 \times 960 \times (30 - 13) = 4080 \text{ W} = 4.08 \text{ kW}$$

**Step B — Sensible heat to cool cargo:**

$$Q_{cargo} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{final}) \times \frac{1}{t}$$

We must define a cooling period $t$. KTU problems often quote 24 h.

$$Q_{cargo} = \frac{800{,}000 \times 3.5 \times (25 - 13)}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{33{,}600{,}000}{86{,}400} = 388.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step C — Respiration heat:**

$$Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_r = 800{,}000 \times 0.03 = 24{,}000 \text{ W} = 24.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step D — Heat carried away by circulating air (this must equal all other gains, used as sanity check):**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{60 \times 2000 \times 1.2}{3600} = 40 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step E — Ventilation fresh-air load:**

$$\dot{m}_{fresh} = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 1.2}{3600} = 1.333 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{vent} = 1.333 \times (90 - 35) = 73.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step F — Total load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_{leak} + Q_{cargo} + Q_{resp} + Q_{fan} + Q_{vent} \\
&= 4.08 + 388.9 + 24.0 + 8.0 + 73.33 \\
&= 498.3 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step G — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration:**

$$Q_{TR} = \frac{498.3}{3.517} = 141.7 \text{ TR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The dominant term is **$Q_{cargo}$** (cooling the initial product down). The plant must be sized for the *first-day* peak load, but the *running* (steady-state) load is only ~110 kW (everything except $Q_{cargo}$). The plant is often run continuously at a part-load to handle the steady state.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Cooling Methods

| Criterion | Direct Expansion (DX) | Brine (Indirect) | Air Cooling | Conduction (Plate) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of fluid loops | 1 | 2 | 2 (refrigerant + air) | 1–2 |
| Cargo safety on leak | Poor (toxic risk) | Excellent (brine safe) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Temperature uniformity | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (±0.5 °C) | Poor |
| Humidity control | Difficult | Difficult | Excellent | Difficult |
| Initial cost | Low | High | High | Moderate |
| Modern ship use | Rare (old) | Limited (special) | **Standard** | Rare (special) |
| Response time | Fast | Slow (thermal buffer) | Fast | Very slow |
| Space utilization | Good | Moderate | Moderate (ducts) | Excellent |
| Maintenance access | Hard (coils in hold) | Moderate | Good (central plant) | Hard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Air Cooling System (Dominant in Modern Reefers)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional architecture of a **centralized air-cooled reefer cargo chamber**. Each block is alphanumeric and free of restricted keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Compressor Bank] --> B1[Condenser Sea Water Cooled]
    B1 --> C1[Receiver and Liquid Line]
    C1 --> D1[Expansion Valve Station]
    D1 --> E1[Air Cooling Unit Evaporator Coils]
    E1 --> F1[Cargo Hold 1 Supply Duct]
    E1 --> F2[Cargo Hold 2 Supply Duct]
    E1 --> F3[Cargo Hold 3 Supply Duct]
    F1 --> G1[False Deck Return Air Path]
    F2 --> G2[False Deck Return Air Path]
    F3 --> G3[False Deck Return Air Path]
    G1 --> H1[Return Air Fan Bank]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Humidifier or Dehumidifier Module]
    I1 --> J1[Recirculation Plenum]
    J1 --> E1
    K1[Fresh Air Inlet Louver] --> I1
    L1[CO2 Scrubber Lithium Chloride] --> I1
    M1[Defrost Hot Gas Line] --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Refrigerant flows A1 → B1 → C1 → D1 → E1 (the evaporator). Air flows E1 → F1/F2/F3 (supply to holds) → G1/G2/G3 (return under false deck) → H1 (fans) → I1 (humidity control) → J1 (plenum) → back to E1. Fresh air enters at K1, CO₂ is scrubbed at L1, and defrost is performed via M1.

### 4.2 Schematic: Air Circulation Pattern Inside a Single Cargo Hold

This block diagram shows how supply air is distributed and returned inside one chamber to ensure uniform temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Supply Duct at Deckhead] --> S2[Side Bulkhead Grills]
    S2 --> S3[Air Sweeps Down Side Bulkheads]
    S3 --> S4[False Deck Gridded Floor]
    S4 --> S5[Return Air Passage Under False Deck]
    S5 --> S6[Return Duct at Forward End]
    S6 --> S7[Return Fan and ACU Inlet]
    C1[Cargo Stack Bananas in Crates] -. Heat absorbed .-> S3
    H1[Hatch Coaming] -. Insulated Boundary .-> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Concept:** The supply air is jetted down the side bulkheads, picks up heat from the cargo, returns under the false deck. This is a **closed-loop, fully ducted, vertical recirculation** pattern, also called a **"down-flow" or "dutch door" circulation** — it minimizes dead zones and maintains temperature uniformity throughout the hold.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparative Selection of Cooling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Cargo Chamber Cooling Method] --> Q1{Refrigerant in Hold?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Direct Expansion DX]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Secondary Fluid in Hold?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Brine Indirect System]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Cooled Medium in Hold?}
    Q3 -- Air --> R3[Air Cooling System]
    Q3 -- Ship Steel Structure --> R4[Conduction Plate Cooling]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*List any three methods of cooling cargo chambers in a conventional reefer ship. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Direct Expansion (DX) System**
   - *Advantage:* Simple construction, high thermodynamic efficiency, no secondary fluid needed.
   - *Disadvantage:* Refrigerant leak directly contaminates cargo; poor temperature uniformity due to natural convection only.

2. **Brine (Indirect) Cooling System**
   - *Advantage:* Cargo safety — brine is non-toxic; central plant can serve multiple holds at different temperatures.
   - *Disadvantage:* Higher initial cost, requires brine pumps and concentration monitoring, lower COP due to two-loop system.

3. **Air Cooling System**
   - *Advantage:* Excellent temperature uniformity (±0.5 °C), precise humidity and CO₂ control, centralized maintenance.
   - *Disadvantage:* Ducts reduce usable cargo volume; fan power adds to load.

4. **Conduction (Plate/Floor) Cooling**
   - *Advantage:* Maximum cargo space (pipes embedded in structure).
   - *Disadvantage:* Very slow thermal response, poor uniformity, complex pipe routing inside ship's steel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is the typical range of cargo hold temperature for: (a) chilled cargo, (b) frozen cargo? State the units of refrigeration load commonly used in ship specifications.*

**Model Answer:**

- (a) **Chilled cargo:** 0 °C to +13 °C (e.g., bananas at 13 °C, apples at 0 °C to +4 °C)
- (b) **Frozen cargo:** −25 °C to −18 °C (e.g., frozen fish, meat)

The unit of refrigeration capacity in ship specifications is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**, where:

$$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 50.4 \text{ MJ/hour} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hour}$$

It is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **air cooling method** of cargo chamber cooling in a conventional reefer ship. State the functions of (i) supply duct, (ii) false deck, and (iii) return air fan.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reefer hold has an effective external surface area of 800 m². The insulation is **corkboard of thickness 100 mm** ($k = 0.040$ W/m·K) backed by a steel plate of 10 mm. Inside surface coefficient $h_i = 7$ W/m²K, outside coefficient $h_o = 12$ W/m²K. Hold temperature is −10 °C, outside ambient is 30 °C. Calculate:
1. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$
2. Total heat leakage in kW
3. Heat leakage per m²

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Air Cooling Method Explanation:**

The air cooling system uses **central refrigeration plant → air cooling unit (ACU) → ducted circulation → cargo hold → return → ACU**. Cooled air is supplied at the deckhead, flows down the side bulkheads, sweeps across the cargo, and returns under the **false deck** (a gridded floor raised above the tanktop) back to the return fan.

**Functions:**
- **(i) Supply Duct:** Distributes cooled air uniformly from the ACU to multiple cargo holds; can include adjustable louvers/grills to direct airflow along side bulkheads and prevent short-circuiting.
- **(ii) False Deck:** A perforated or gridded floor raised ~300–500 mm above the tanktop, providing an **under-floor return air plenum** for warm air to flow back to the ACU without disturbing the cargo stack.
- **(iii) Return Air Fan:** Creates the pressure differential to overcome duct and coil friction, ensuring continuous forced circulation at the required **50–100 air changes/hour**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — List thermal resistances:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_i &= \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{7} = 0.1429 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \\
R_1 &= \frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{0.040} = 2.500 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \quad \text{(cork)} \\
R_2 &= \frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.010}{45} = 0.000222 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \quad \text{(steel)} \\
R_o &= \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the resistances:**

$$R_{total} = 0.1429 + 2.500 + 0.000222 + 0.0833 = 2.7264 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute U:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2.7264} = 0.3668 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat leakage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 0.3668 \times 800 \times (30 - (-10)) \\
&= 0.3668 \times 800 \times 40 \\
&= 11{,}737.6 \text{ W} \approx 11.74 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Heat leakage per m²:**

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.3668 \times 40 = 14.67 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert $\Delta T$ to Kelvin correctly (the difference is numerically the same as °C, but you must **show the unit conversion explicitly**: $30 - (-10) = 40$ °C = **40 K**). Also, students often mistakenly use $1/U$ as $U$ in the formula — remember $U$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient**, not its inverse. Losing 1–2 marks on this is common.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Direct Expansion** and **Brine (Indirect)** cooling methods for reefer cargo holds. Discuss their suitability for (i) frozen meat cargo and (ii) chilled fruit cargo.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reefer vessel is to transport 600 tonnes of frozen meat. The meat is loaded at −5 °C and must be brought to −20 °C in 20 hours. The hold has external area 700 m² with $U = 0.30$ W/m²K. Outside temp = 32 °C. Respiration heat is negligible. Take $c_{p,meat} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K. Estimate:
1. Heat to cool meat in kW
2. Heat leakage in kW
3. Total refrigeration load in TR

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Discussion:**

| Aspect | Direct Expansion (DX) | Brine (Indirect) |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant in hold? | Yes | No (brine only) |
| Cargo safety | Poor — toxic refrigerant leak risks | Excellent — brine is food-safe |
| Temperature control | Less uniform | Moderate |
| Plant location | Coils distributed in holds | Central chiller + brine pump |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| COP | Higher (1-loop) | Lower (2-loop) |
| Suitability for **frozen meat** (−20 °C) | Possible but ammonia leak risk; meat can absorb ammonia odor → **Not recommended** | **Suitable** — brine can go to −35 °C; safer |
| Suitability for **chilled fruit** (+5 °C) | Possible but humidity hard to control | Suitable but no humidity control either |

**Conclusion:** For **frozen meat**, **Brine** is preferred because any refrigerant leak taints the meat. For **chilled fruit**, **Air Cooling** is actually the best (neither DX nor Brine handles humidity well), but among the two, **Brine** is still safer for respiration-sensitive cargo.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat to cool meat:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{cargo} &= \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} \\
&= \frac{600{,}000 \times 2.1 \times (-5 - (-20))}{20 \times 3600} \\
&= \frac{600{,}000 \times 2.1 \times 15}{72{,}000} \\
&= \frac{18{,}900{,}000}{72{,}000} \\
&= 262.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Heat leakage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{leak} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 0.30 \times 700 \times (32 - (-20)) \\
&= 0.30 \times 700 \times 52 \\
&= 10{,}920 \text{ W} = 10.92 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total load:**

$$Q_{total} = 262.5 + 10.92 = 273.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to TR:**

$$Q_{TR} = \frac{273.42}{3.517} = 77.74 \text{ TR}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q2):** A frequent error is forgetting to **convert hours to seconds** in $Q_{cargo} = mc_p\Delta T / t$. The time must be in **seconds** to get **Watts**. Also, students often add respiration/ventilation/fan heat but the problem explicitly says they are *negligible*. Read the data carefully. Marks lost: 1–2 per error.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Four cooling methods:** Direct Expansion (DX), Brine (Indirect), Air Cooling, Conduction (Plate).
- **Modern reefers = Air Cooling** — by far the most common in KTU board questions.
- **Heat leakage formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ — memorize as "UAT".
- **Composite wall U-value:** $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_i}{k_i} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ — add resistances in series.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — used for final answer of plant capacity questions.
- **Temperature ranges:** Chilled = 0 to +13 °C; Frozen = −18 to −25 °C.
- **Insulation materials and conductivities:** Cork ~0.040, PUF ~0.022, EPS ~0.035, Steel ~45 W/m·K.
- **Steel contributes negligible insulation resistance** — its $x/k$ is tiny.
- **Air circulation:** 50–100 changes/hour; air velocity 0.5–1.0 m/s.
- **Total load = Leakage + Cargo cooling + Respiration + Ventilation + Fan + Personnel.**
- **First-day load (with $Q_{cargo}$) >> Running load (without $Q_{cargo}$).**
- **False deck** is the under-floor return air plenum, not structural.
- **Cargo safety rule:** Ammonia (R-717) leak ruins food → prefer Brine or DX with R-404A/HFC for food reefer holds.
- **Ducted airflow pattern:** Supply at deckhead → down side bulkheads → through/around cargo → return under false deck.
- **Humidity control** is only feasible in Air Cooling systems (spray / desiccant / LiCl absorber).
- **Respiration heat** is non-zero only for living cargo (fruits, vegetables) — zero for meat and fish.
- **Units to watch:** Convert all time to seconds for power (Watts); temperature differences in K = °C numerically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Cooling Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods of Cooling Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Container Cooling

**Container Cooling** in marine transportation refers to the engineered processes and systems used to maintain prescribed low temperatures inside shipping containers (commonly known as **Reefer Containers** or **Refrigerated Containers**) so that perishable, temperature-sensitive, or frozen cargoes (food products, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, biologicals) retain their quality, safety, and commercial value throughout the voyage.

According to KTU 2024 marine engineering terminology:

> A **Reefer Container** is an intermodal freight container fitted with a thermal insulation envelope and a refrigeration unit capable of controlling the internal temperature within a prescribed band (typically **-30 °C to +30 °C**), regardless of ambient maritime conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PENST743 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to classify container cooling methods, describe the constructional and operational features of integral and centralized reefer plants, and compute refrigeration loads using standard heat-transfer relations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a regular **Tiffin box** carrying hot *fish curry* from home to your workplace. Without any cooling, the curry spoils by lunchtime. To keep it fresh, you might:
- Wrap it in a thick towel (**insulation**),
- Place it next to a frozen ice pack (**active cooling**), or
- Store it inside a small battery-powered mini-fridge (**mechanical refrigeration**).

A reefer container on a ship is exactly that — a giant "Tiffin box" with industrial-grade insulation, plus a refrigeration plant that runs on the ship's electrical supply, maintaining cargo at a temperature the shipper specifies. The "Methods of Cooling Containers" are simply the *different ways* we install or attach that cooling source to the box.

## 1.3 Why Container Cooling is Different from Land-Based Cold Storage

| Aspect | Land Cold Store | Ship Reefer Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Grid electricity | Ship's electrical network (440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz typically) |
| **Ambient Conditions** | Stable | Sea-state roll/pitch, salt-laden air, ambient 45 °C in tropics |
| **Service Access** | Easy | Limited; vibration, tilt, confined spaces |
| **Heat Rejection** | Air-cooled condensers on land | Often **sea-water-cooled condensers** on board |
| **Standards** | National codes | **ISO 1496-2** container standard; IACS UR for ships |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in this Module**
> - Specific heat of water: **c_pw = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**
> - Specific heat of typical fish: **c_pf ≈ 3.5 kJ/kg·K**
> - Specific heat of air: **c_pa = 1.005 kJ/kg·K**
> - Latent heat of fusion of water: **h_fg = 334 kJ/kg**
> - Latent heat of vaporisation of water: **h_fg = 2260 kJ/kg**
> - Standard overall heat-transfer coefficient of insulated container: **U = 0.3 – 0.6 W/m²·K**
> - ISO container internal volume (40 ft reefer): ≈ **67 m³**

## 1.4 Classification of Container Cooling Methods (Top-Level)

The methods can be broadly classified into **five families**:

1. **Integral (Built-in) Mechanical Refrigeration** — Unit bolted to the container.
2. **Clip-On (Porter) Units** — Portable units clipped to standard insulated containers.
3. **Centralised Shipboard Plant** — A central engine room supplies refrigerant or chilled brine to many containers via piping/ducts.
4. **Eutectic (Cold Plate) Systems** — Frozen plates provide passive cooling.
5. **Auxiliary / Pre-cooling Methods** — Ice, RSW, vacuum pre-cooling, hydrocooling, cryogenic (LN₂/CO₂) — used either pre-shipment or as supplementary systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Integral Mechanical Refrigeration (Self-Contained Reefer)

### 2.1.1 Construction
A typical **Integral Reefer Unit** (e.g., Carrier, Thermo King, Daikin, Star Cool) is a complete vapour-compression refrigeration package mounted at one end (front wall) of an ISO 40 ft or 20 ft insulated container.

**Main Components:**
- **Hermetic / semi-hermetic compressor** (screw or scroll type for larger units, reciprocating for smaller).
- **Condenser** (air-cooled or sea-water cooled on board).
- **Thermostatic or Electronic Expansion Valve (TXV / EEV)**.
- **Evaporator** with multi-speed fans for uniform air circulation.
- **Microprocessor controller** with data-logger (records up to 365 days).
- **Three-phase power intake**: typically **440 V, 50/60 Hz, 3-phase** drawing 5–15 kW depending on size.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle
1. The compressor draws low-pressure refrigerant vapour and compresses it to high pressure → temperature rises.
2. Hot, high-pressure vapour condenses in the condenser by rejecting heat to ambient air or sea water.
3. Liquid refrigerant passes through the expansion valve → pressure drops → temperature falls.
4. Cold low-pressure liquid evaporates in the evaporator coils mounted inside the container → absorbs heat from the cargo air.
5. The cargo air is **circulated by evaporator fans** (typically 3000–5000 m³/h) so that the air sweeps across every part of the cargo, ensuring uniform temperature.

### 2.1.3 Air-Circulation Pattern
> Forced air is delivered from the top of the evaporator, flows *downward* along the T-bar floor, sweeps under the cargo, and returns upward along the side walls. This **bottom-up circulation** prevents hot/cold stratification.

## 2.2 Method 2 — Clip-On / Porter Units

A **Clip-On Unit** is a portable refrigeration module that can be attached (or "clipped") to the front of a standard insulated container that does **not** have a built-in unit.

**Advantages:**
- Flexibility — a single unit can service multiple containers over time.
- Cheaper to procure containers in bulk without integral units.

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher maintenance — exposed to environment when demounted.
- Vibration issues during sea transport if not properly clamped.

**Typical Capacity:** 3 kW to 12 kW refrigeration.

## 2.3 Method 3 — Centralised Shipboard Reefer Plant

On a large container ship (e.g., 10,000+ TEU capacity, 800+ reefer plugs), running an individual compressor for each container is **inefficient**.

**Centralised Plant Concept:**
- A few **large screw compressors** (e.g., 4 × 350 kW) in the engine room.
- Refrigerant (commonly **R-404A** or **R-134a**) is cooled to a low temperature.
- Chilled refrigerant or secondary brine is pumped through **refrigerant supply lines** running along the deck.
- Each container is plugged into a **reefer socket** (a standardised 440 V 3-phase + data connector).
- The container's evaporator fan and expansion valve still operate locally, but the **compressor and condenser work is centralised**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Centralised?** Heat rejection from many small condensers (each rejecting to ambient) is thermodynamically wasteful. A single large condenser can be **sea-water cooled** with much better efficiency.

## 2.4 Method 4 — Eutectic (Cold Plate) System

**Eutectic plates** are sealed plates filled with a phase-change material (PCM) such as **eutectic salt solution** (e.g., sodium chloride + water, freezing around -21 °C) or **glycol-based PCM**.

**Operating Cycle:**
1. **Charging phase (at port):** Plates are connected to an external refrigeration source and frozen solid.
2. **Discharge phase (at sea):** The fan blows cargo air across the frozen plates; the plates absorb heat by melting (latent heat exchange).
3. **Duration:** Typically **8 to 12 hours** of cooling between charges — therefore used on **short-sea / feeder routes**.

> **Engineering Utility:** Eutectic plates act as a **thermal buffer** — even if the compressor fails, the cargo remains cold for several hours.

## 2.5 Method 5 — Auxiliary / Pre-cooling Methods

### 2.5.1 Ice Cooling
- **Block ice** in contact with cargo, or **ice slurry** (mixture of micro-crystals and water).
- Used for fresh fish, vegetables.
- Modern **slurry ice** has a heat-transfer coefficient 3× higher than block ice.

### 2.5.2 Refrigerated Sea Water (RSW)
- Cargo (mainly fish) is **immersed in chilled sea water** at **-1 °C to 0 °C**.
- Heat-transfer is by **direct contact** — extremely fast cooling.
- Risk of salt contamination; modern plants use **CSW (Chilled Sea Water)** with fresh water.

### 2.5.3 Vacuum Pre-Cooling
- Pressure inside the chamber is reduced to ~ 600 Pa (≈ 1 % of atmospheric).
- Water in the product boils at ~ 0 °C → evaporative cooling.
- Cooling rate: **5 °C drop in 20–30 minutes** for leafy vegetables.

### 2.5.4 Hydrocooling
- Drenching or immersion of produce in **chilled water (0 – 2 °C)** for 10–60 minutes.
- Suitable for apples, carrots, sweet corn.

### 2.5.5 Cryogenic Cooling
- **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂)** at **-196 °C** or **Liquid CO₂** at **-78 °C** is sprayed directly into the container.
- Used for very high-value, fast-moving consignments.
- Cost is high; mostly used in **air-freight**, less common in maritime.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT bare `|`) inside markdown tables to prevent syntax breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{walls} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat gain through container walls | W |
| 2 | $Q_{product} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{initial} - T_{final})$ | Sensible heat removed from cargo | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_{respiration} = m \cdot \dot{q}_{resp}$ | Heat from respiring fruits/vegetables | kW |
| 4 | $Q_{infiltration} = \dot{V}_{air} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat from door-opening air exchange | kW |
| 5 | $Q_{equipment} = P_{fans} + P_{heaters} + P_{lights}$ | Internal heat from fans/defrost heaters | W |
| 6 | $Q_{latent} = m_{moisture} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat from condensation/freezing of water | kJ |
| 7 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of the cycle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{\dot{Q}_{evap} - \dot{Q}_{loss}}$ | Cooling time for a given product | seconds/hours |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{\rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T_{air}}$ | Required air circulation rate | m³/s |
| 10 | $P_{defrost} = \dfrac{m_{frost} \cdot h_{fg}}{t_{defrost}}$ | Power required during electric defrost | W |

**Boundary Conditions Used in Standard Container Heat-Load Calculation:**
- External ambient (tropical): **T_amb = +45 °C**
- Internal cargo temperature: typically **T_int = -18 °C (frozen)** or **+4 °C (chilled)**
- Container surface area (40 ft reefer): **A ≈ 140 m²** (walls + roof + floor, accounting for corrugation)
- Mean overall heat-transfer coefficient: **U ≈ 0.4 W/m²·K**

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

The choice of cooling method has direct commercial impact:
- A 40 ft reefer using integral refrigeration consumes **~ 2.5 kW** continuously.
- A 800-plug centralized plant can serve an entire reefer fleet with **40 % less fuel** than equivalent individual units.
- A single gram of temperature deviation can spoil an entire banana pallet — methods must offer **±0.1 °C control precision**.
