# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load of a 40 ft Reefer Container

A KTU examination will typically ask: *"Calculate the refrigeration load of a 40 ft container carrying 25 tonnes of frozen fish from -10 °C to -25 °C, sailing through tropical waters."* Let us work out the full derivation.

### Step 1 — Heat Gain Through Walls ($Q_{walls}$)

**Data given (assumed for the example):**
- Surface area: $A = 140$ m²
- Overall heat-transfer coefficient: $U = 0.45$ W/m²·K
- External temperature: $T_{amb} = +45$ °C
- Internal temperature: $T_{int} = -25$ °C
- Temperature difference: $\Delta T = 45 - (-25) = 70$ K

**Calculation:**

$$
Q_{walls} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{walls} = 0.45 \;\text{W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K} \;\times\; 140 \;\text{m}^2 \;\times\; 70 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
Q_{walls} = 4410 \;\text{W} = 4.41 \;\text{kW}
$$

*Reasoning:* The heat leaks inward because the outside is warmer. The insulation is not perfect, so a steady-state leak exists. This must be continuously removed.

### Step 2 — Product Heat Load ($Q_{product}$)

**Data:**
- Mass of fish: $m = 25\,000$ kg
- Specific heat of fish: $c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K
- Initial temperature: $T_i = -10$ °C
- Final temperature: $T_f = -25$ °C

**Calculation:**

$$
Q_{product} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)
$$

$$
Q_{product} = 25\,000 \;\text{kg} \;\times\; 3.5 \;\text{kJ/kg·K} \;\times\; \bigl(-10 - (-25)\bigr) \;\text{K}
$$

$$
Q_{product} = 25\,000 \;\times\; 3.5 \;\times\; 15
$$

$$
Q_{product} = 1\,312\,500 \;\text{kJ}
$$

If this must be achieved over, say, **8 hours** of pre-cooling:

$$
\dot{Q}_{product} = \frac{1\,312\,500 \;\text{kJ}}{8 \;\text{h} \;\times\; 3600 \;\text{s/h}} = \frac{1\,312\,500}{28\,800} \approx 45.6 \;\text{kW}
$$

*Reasoning:* This is the **largest** contribution to the load — sensible cooling of the product itself. The lower the desired final temperature, the higher this becomes.

### Step 3 — Infiltration / Door-Opening Load ($Q_{infil}$)

**Data:**
- Number of door openings per voyage: $n = 4$ times
- Air volume exchanged per opening: $V_{open} = 5$ m³
- Density of air: $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Specific heat: $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- $\Delta T = 70$ K

**Mass of air per opening:**

$$
m_{air} = \rho_a \cdot V_{open} = 1.2 \;\times\; 5 = 6 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Heat per opening:**

$$
Q_{one} = m_{air} \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T = 6 \;\times\; 1.005 \;\times\; 70 = 422.1 \;\text{kJ}
$$

**Total over voyage (assume 20 days = 480 h):**

$$
Q_{infil} = 4 \;\times\; 422.1 = 1688.4 \;\text{kJ}
$$

Convert to average kW:

$$
\dot{Q}_{infil} = \frac{1688.4}{480 \;\times\; 3600} \approx 0.00098 \;\text{kW} \approx 1 \;\text{W}
$$

*Reasoning:* Negligible compared to $Q_{product}$ and $Q_{walls}$ for a frozen cargo.

### Step 4 — Internal Equipment Load ($Q_{equip}$)

**Data:**
- Evaporator fan power: $P_{fan} = 1.5$ kW (2 fans)
- Defrost heaters (intermittent, average): $P_{defrost} = 0.5$ kW
- Lights: $P_{lights} = 0.2$ kW

$$
Q_{equip} = 2 \;\times\; 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 3.7 \;\text{kW}
$$

*Reasoning:* Fan motors dissipate their entire electrical input as heat **inside** the cooled space — they are 100 % internal load.

### Step 5 — Total Continuous Load (Steady-State)

After product is cooled, only the steady-state components remain:

$$
\dot{Q}_{steady} = \dot{Q}_{walls} + \dot{Q}_{infil} + \dot{Q}_{equip}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{steady} = 4.41 + 0.001 + 3.7 = 8.11 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Initial Pull-Down Load (Transient)

During the first 8 hours:

$$
\dot{Q}_{pull-down} = \dot{Q}_{product} + \dot{Q}_{steady} = 45.6 + 8.11 = 53.7 \;\text{kW}
$$

*Reasoning:* The reefer unit must be **sized** for the pull-down condition, not just steady state. This is why manufacturers rate units at "pull-down capacity" and "running capacity."

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cooling Methods

| Method | Temp Range (°C) | Typical Capacity | Energy Use | Best Suited For | Drawbacks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Integral Mechanical** | -30 to +20 | 4–15 kW | High (continuous) | All marine reefer | Compressor vibration |
| **Clip-On (Porter)** | -25 to +15 | 3–12 kW | High | Flexi-routing | Maintenance heavy |
| **Centralised Plant** | -30 to +20 | 200–2000 kW plant | Best efficiency | Large container ships | High capital cost |
| **Eutectic Plate** | -25 to +5 | 8–12 h hold | Low (off-peak) | Short-sea feeder | Limited duration |
| **Ice / Slurry Ice** | -1 to +2 | Moderate | Low | Fresh fish, veg | Melts, weight gain |
| **RSW / CSW** | -1 to 0 | High | Moderate | Tuna, fish | Salt absorption |
| **Vacuum Pre-cool** | +1 to +5 | 5–50 t/batch | Moderate | Leafy vegetables | Capital cost |
| **Hydrocooling** | 0 to +2 | High | Low-High | Apples, sweet corn | Water-borne disease risk |
| **Cryogenic (LN₂)** | -60 to -196 | Very high | Very high (LN₂ costly) | Pharma, ice cream | Cost prohibitive at scale |

## 3.3 Compact Derivation: Cooling Time of a Product

If we have to cool a product mass $m$ from $T_i$ to $T_f$ using a refrigeration plant of capacity $\dot{Q}_{net}$ (net cooling available after subtracting wall, equipment, infiltration losses):

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)}{\dot{Q}_{net}}
$$

**Worked numeric:** For 25 t fish above with $\dot{Q}_{net} = 8$ kW (steady state only — this would be **terribly slow**):

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{1\,312\,500 \;\text{kJ}}{8 \;\text{kW}} = 164\,062 \;\text{s} \approx 45.6 \;\text{h}
$$

This is why **pull-down** is usually done with the unit operating at full capacity before loading — not en route.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integral Reefer Container Refrigeration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA[Compressor\nElectric Motor Driven] --> condB[Condenser\nSea Water Cooled]
    condB --> expC[Expansion Valve\nTXV or EEV]
    expC --> evapD[Evaporator\nFinned Coil]
    evapD --> compA
    evapD -. cool air .-> fanE[Evaporator Fans\nForced Circulation]
    fanE -. cargo air .-> cargoF[Container Cargo Space\nBottom Up Flow]
    cargoF -. return air .-> evapD
    ctrlG[Microprocessor Controller] -. modulates .-> compA
    ctrlG -. modulates .-> expC
    ctrlG -. senses .-> cargoF
    condB -. heat rejected .-> swH[Sea Water\nCirculating Pump]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Classification of Container Cooling Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Methods of Cooling Containers] --> rootB[Active Mechanical Methods]
    rootA --> rootC[Passive Phase Change Methods]
    rootA --> rootD[Auxiliary Pre-Cooling Methods]

    rootB --> b1[Integral Unit]
    rootB --> b2[Clip On Porter Unit]
    rootB --> b3[Centralised Shipboard Plant]

    rootC --> c1[Eutectic Cold Plates]
    rootC --> c2[Block Ice]
    rootC --> c3[Slurry Ice]

    rootD --> d1[Refrigerated Sea Water]
    rootD --> d2[Vacuum Pre Cooling]
    rootD --> d3[Hydrocooling]
    rootD --> d4[Cryogenic LN2 CO2]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Centralised Reefer Plant on a Container Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plantA[Central Compressor House\nScrew Compressors] -->|high pressure gas| condB[Sea Water Cooled Condenser]
    condB -->|liquid refrigerant| recC[Receiver and Filter]
    recC -->|via deck piping| plug1[Reefer Plug Bank 1\nContainers 1 to 50]
    recC -->|via deck piping| plug2[Reefer Plug Bank 2\nContainers 51 to 100]
    recC -->|via deck piping| plug3[Reefer Plug Bank 3\nContainers 101 to 150]
    plug1 -->|return vapour| recC
    plug2 -->|return vapour| recC
    plug3 -->|return vapour| recC
    recC -->|return to suction| plantA
    seaP[Sea Water Cooling Pump] -. supplies cooling .-> condB
    swIn[Sea Chest Inlet] --> seaP
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Air Circulation Inside Container

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Cold Air Leaves Evaporator\nTop Front] --> downB[Air Flows Down\nBehind Front Wall]
    downB --> acrossC[Air Sweeps Across\nT Bar Floor Under Cargo]
    acrossC --> upD[Warm Return Air Rises\nAlong Side Walls]
    upD --> filterE[Through Return Air Filter]
    filterE --> fanF[Evaporator Fan\nRe Cools the Air]
    fanF --> startA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The cargo is loaded on a **T-bar floor** — a specially ribbed floor that creates a horizontal air plenum underneath the cargo, allowing the bottom-up circulation that prevents hot spots. Any blockage (e.g., cargo placed directly on the floor) **destroys** this circulation pattern and creates local spoilage.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Eutectic Plate Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    portA[At Port\nExternal Refrigeration] -->|freezes| plateB[Eutectic Plate\nPhase Change Material]
    plateB -->|stored cold| seaC[Voyage Phase]
    seaC -->|melts slowly| plateB
    plateB -->|fan blows cargo air| cargoD[Cargo Space]
    cargoD -->|warm return| plateB
    plateB -. needs recharging .-> portA
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. List any three methods of cooling containers used on board ships and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Integral Mechanical Refrigeration Unit:** A self-contained vapour-compression unit is bolted to the container. *Advantage:* Independent operation — failure of one unit does not affect others; precise temperature control.
2. **Centralised Plant with Reefer Plugs:** Central compressors and condensers in engine room feed refrigerant to deck sockets. *Advantage:* Higher thermodynamic efficiency and reduced maintenance.
3. **Eutectic Cold Plates:** Frozen phase-change plates. *Advantage:* Provide cooling during power failure — passive thermal backup.

(1 mark per method + advantage pair. Any valid third method acceptable.)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between RSW and CSW systems for cooling fish cargo.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) | CSW (Chilled Sea Water) |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Natural sea water | Chilled fresh water |
| Salt contact | Yes — risk of salt uptake by fish | No — preserves taste/colour |
| Heat-transfer | Good (salt water has higher density) | Excellent (pure water higher c_p) |
| Corrosion to plant | High | Low |

(Any three valid contrasting points = 3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern: Module 2, Q-Internal Choice]**

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q (a) Describe with a neat sketch the working of an integral type refrigeration unit fitted to a 40 ft reefer container. List the main components and explain the air circulation pattern. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An integral reefer unit is a self-contained vapour-compression refrigeration system mounted on the front wall of an ISO insulated container. It maintains internal temperature between **-30 °C and +30 °C**.

**Main Components (3 marks):**
1. **Hermetic/semi-hermetic compressor** — compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour.
2. **Condenser** — sea-water or air-cooled; rejects heat from refrigerant.
3. **Expansion valve** — TXV or EEV; reduces pressure → temperature.
4. **Evaporator** — finned coil inside the container; absorbs heat from cargo air.
5. **Evaporator fans** — 2 to 3 multi-speed fans, 3000–5000 m³/h.
6. **Microprocessor controller** — sensing, modulating, data logging.
7. **Three-phase power intake** — 440 V, 50/60 Hz from ship's supply.
8. **Defrost heaters** — for periodic de-icing of evaporator.

**Working (2 marks):**
Compressor → condenser (heat rejected to sea water) → expansion valve (pressure drop) → evaporator (heat absorbed from cargo air) → back to compressor. This is a standard vapour-compression cycle.

**Air Circulation (1 mark):**
Cold air from evaporator flows down behind the front wall, sweeps under the cargo via the T-bar floor, rises along the side walls, and returns to the evaporator — a **bottom-up flow** ensuring uniform temperature.

---

**Q (b) A 40 ft reefer container carries 20 tonnes of meat (c_p = 3.2 kJ/kg·K) which is to be cooled from -5 °C to -20 °C in 6 hours. The container surface area is 130 m², U = 0.5 W/m²·K, ambient is 40 °C. Fan power = 1.8 kW, infiltration negligible. Estimate the refrigeration plant capacity required. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Product Load (Sensible Heat) (2 marks):**

$$
Q_{product} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{product} = 20\,000 \;\text{kg} \;\times\; 3.2 \;\text{kJ/kg·K} \;\times\; (-5 - (-20)) \;\text{K}
$$

$$
Q_{product} = 20\,000 \;\times\; 3.2 \;\times\; 15 = 960\,000 \;\text{kJ}
$$

Average cooling power:

$$
\dot{Q}_{product} = \frac{960\,000 \;\text{kJ}}{6 \;\text{h} \;\times\; 3600 \;\text{s/h}} = \frac{960\,000}{21\,600} \approx 44.4 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Wall Heat Gain (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta T = 40 - (-20) = 60 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{walls} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5 \;\times\; 130 \;\times\; 60 = 3900 \;\text{W} = 3.9 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Equipment Load (1 mark):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{equip} = 1.8 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Plant Capacity (2.5 marks):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{product} + \dot{Q}_{walls} + \dot{Q}_{equip}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 44.4 + 3.9 + 1.8 = 50.1 \;\text{kW}
$$

A reefer unit of **at least 50 kW pull-down capacity** must be installed. With a typical **safety margin of 10–15 %**, the unit should be rated at **≈ 56 kW**.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q (a) Explain the centralised refrigeration system for reefer containers on a large container ship. Compare it with the integral unit system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Centralised Plant Description (3.5 marks):**
On a large container ship (e.g., 800+ reefer slots), a centralised refrigeration plant in the engine room supplies chilled refrigerant through insulated deck piping to every reefer socket. Each socket provides:
- 3-phase power to evaporator fans and the unit's local controls,
- Refrigerant supply and return lines.

**Main Features:**
- Few large **screw compressors** (2–6 nos., 200–500 kW each),
- Common **sea-water cooled condenser**,
- Common **liquid receiver and oil separator**,
- Distributed through **isolation valves** for sectional control.

**Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Integral Unit | Centralised Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor location | On each container | Engine room |
| Condenser cooling | Individual (air or sea water) | Common, sea-water cooled |
| Efficiency | Lower (small-scale) | Higher (large-scale COP) |
| Maintenance | Per-container, frequent | Centralised, planned |
| Capital cost | Distributed | High upfront |
| Failure impact | One container affected | Plant failure = all affected (mitigated by spares) |
| Suitable for | < 100 reefer ships | > 200 reefer container ships |

---

**Q (b) A ship has 500 reefer containers. Each container requires an average cooling load of 5 kW. The central plant COP is 2.2. The ship's generator efficiency is 0.42. Calculate the electrical power demand from the generator to drive the reefer plant. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Cooling Load (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cooling,total} = 500 \;\times\; 5 \;\text{kW} = 2500 \;\text{kW} = 2.5 \;\text{MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressor Shaft Power (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{W}_{comp} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cooling}}{COP} = \frac{2500}{2.2} \approx 1136.4 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Electrical Power Input to Motors (1.5 marks):**
Assuming motor efficiency $\eta_{motor} = 0.95$:

$$
\dot{W}_{elec,motor} = \frac{1136.4}{0.95} \approx 1196.2 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Generator Output Power (1.5 marks):**
Neglecting transmission loss; generator efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.96$:

$$
\dot{W}_{gen,out} = \frac{1196.2}{0.96} \approx 1246.0 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Fuel Power to Generator (NOT asked but useful):**

$$
\dot{W}_{fuel} = \frac{1246.0}{0.42} \approx 2967 \;\text{kW} \approx 2.97 \;\text{MW of fuel}
$$

**Final Answer:** The generator must deliver approximately **1.25 MW** of electrical power to drive the reefer plant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include fan power in $Q_{equip}$** — fan motors are 100 % heat input to the cooled space; omitting this underestimates plant size by 10–20 %.
> 2. **Confusing pull-down (transient) and running (steady-state) load** — the unit must be sized for the larger, transient load.
> 3. **Sign errors in $\Delta T$** — always use $\Delta T = T_{ambient} - T_{cargo}$ (positive value).
> 4. **Using fresh-water specific heat for fish** — fish specific heat ≈ 3.5 kJ/kg·K, **not** 4.186.
> 5. **In COP calculation, mixing shaft power and electrical power** — clearly state whether motor efficiency is included.
> 6. **Wrong conversion of hours to seconds** — multiply by 3600, not 60.
> 7. **Ignoring infiltration load for chilled (vs frozen) cargo** — at +4 °C, infiltration can be 5–10 % of total; it is negligible for -25 °C frozen.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Methods of Cooling Containers (Module 2)**

- ☐ **5 main families**: Integral, Clip-On, Centralised, Eutectic, Auxiliary/Pre-cool.
- ☐ **Integral unit** = complete Vapour Compression Refrigeration package on container.
- ☐ **Centralised plant** = efficient for large fleets (> 200 reefers); uses common sea-water condenser.
- ☐ **Eutectic plates** = passive phase-change; useful as thermal buffer; duration 8–12 h.
- ☐ **RSW vs CSW** = salt-water vs fresh-water cooling of fish; CSW avoids salt uptake.
- ☐ **Vacuum pre-cooling** = rapid (5 °C in 20–30 min) by evaporative cooling at low pressure; best for leafy produce.
- ☐ **Hydrocooling** = water drench; best for apples, carrots, sweet corn.
- ☐ **Cryogenic (LN₂/CO₂)** = ultra-low temperature (-196 / -78 °C); used for high-value, short-duration air-freight cargo.
- ☐ **Bottom-up air flow** in integral reefer is critical — T-bar floor is mandatory.
- ☐ **Key formula:** $Q_{total} = Q_{walls} + Q_{product} + Q_{infil} + Q_{equip}$.
- ☐ **Wall heat gain:** $Q = U A \Delta T$; typical U for reefer = 0.3–0.6 W/m²·K.
- ☐ **Product cooling time:** $t = \dfrac{m c_p \Delta T}{\dot{Q}_{net}}$.
- ☐ **Standard ambient for sizing:** **+45 °C** (tropical trade route).
- ☐ **ISO standard** for reefer: **ISO 1496-2**.
- ☐ **Power supply:** 440 V, 3-phase, 50/60 Hz.
- ☐ Always size the unit for **pull-down**, not steady-state.
- ☐ Always include **fan and defrost heater** heat as internal load.
- ☐ **COP** = $Q_{evap} / W_{comp}$; typical reefer COP = 1.8 – 2.5.
- ☐ **Heat of respiration** is significant for fresh fruits/vegetables; negligible for frozen cargo.
- ☐ Salt-laden marine air demands **corrosion-resistant casings** (e.g., galvanised steel + marine paint).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Insulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **marine Refrigeration and Air Conditioning**, **Insulation** refers to the engineering practice and the material science discipline of applying a barrier of low thermal conductivity around components of a refrigeration cycle (cold rooms, chilled water/brine piping, refrigerant suction lines, cargo holds, A.C ducts, hull boundaries) to **restrict the unwanted heat ingress (heat gain) from the warmer ambient (sea, engine room, sun-exposed decks, accommodation spaces) into the cold region**.

Formally, insulation is any material or composite assembly whose steady-state thermal conductivity ($k$) is significantly lower than that of the structural materials it protects (e.g., mild steel with $k \approx \mathbf{45 \ W/m \cdot K}$ vs. polyurethane foam with $k \approx \mathbf{0.022 \ W/m \cdot K}$).

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the design of marine insulation is treated as a **steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction problem** governed by Fourier's Law, bounded by the simultaneous requirements of IMO/SOLAS fire safety codes, classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) and economic optimization.

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta T}{L} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

Where a higher $L$ (thickness) or lower $k$ directly reduces the parasitic heat load on the compressor, thereby saving shaft power, fuel and reducing GHG emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a ship, even a 1 kW of "lost" heat leakage into a cold room can mean an extra **0.25 to 0.4 kW** of compressor shaft power due to the inverse Carnot penalty. Insulation is therefore not a luxury but a thermodynamic necessity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are drinking a chilled soft drink on a hot tropical noon in Kochi (≈ 35 °C) while your friend has a hot cup of tea in a metal mug.

* The **metal mug** is your ship's **steel hull or uninsulated pipe** — heat rushes in or out very fast.
* Now wrap the mug in a **neoprene sleeve (a wetsuit)** — the heat transfer slows dramatically. That sleeve is the **insulation**.

In a ship, the "drink" is either:
* Frozen fish stored at **−25 °C**,
* Chilled provisions at **+4 °C**,
* Or an air-conditioned cabin at **+22 °C, 50 % RH** with a 33 °C tropical sea outside.

Without insulation, your refrigeration plant must run **24 × 7 at full load just to fight the leaking heat**, burning bunker fuel and risking cargo spoilage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Insulation is a "thermal blanket" wrapped around anything we want to keep cold (or sometimes keep hot). In a ship, the sea, sun, and engine rooms are constantly trying to warm up our refrigerated spaces — insulation slows this process to manageable levels.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of an Insulation Material (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Typical Marine Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal conductivity, $k$ | **0.020 – 0.045 W/m·K** | Lower is better |
| Density, $\rho$ | **20 – 200 kg/m³** | Lighter reduces ship displacement |
| Water-vapour permeability, $\mu$ | **< 2.0 (perms)** | Lower reduces condensation risk |
| Fire rating (IMO FTP Code) | **Non-combustible / Low flame spread** | Mandatory for passenger ships |
| Operating temperature | **−60 °C to +80 °C** | Must suit both cargo holds and A.C. |
| Compressive strength | **> 100 kPa** | For floor insulation of cold rooms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Insulation is Critical on a Ship (The 5 Pillars)

1. **Thermodynamic necessity** — Reduce the heat load $Q_{gain}$ on the compressor, which directly reduces the **compressor indicated power ($W_c$)** and hence the ship's auxiliary engine fuel.
2. **Cargo quality** — For reefer ships carrying fish, meat or fruit, even a 1 °C deviation can spoil tonnes of cargo worth millions of rupees.
3. **Anti-condensation (sweating)** — The outer surface of an uninsulated chilled pipe can drop below the local **dew point**, causing water droplets, drip damage, corrosion, and mould in accommodation areas.
4. **Energy efficiency (EEDI / EEXI compliance)** — IMO's Energy Efficiency Design Index penalises over-sized refrigeration plants. Insulation is a **passive** energy-saving measure.
5. **Acoustic & fire protection** — Many marine insulants (rock wool) double as **Class-A fire barriers** and noise absorbers.

## 2.2 Classification of Marine Insulation Materials

### A. By Physical Form

* **Fibrous** — Glass wool, Rock (slag) wool, Ceramic fibre.
* **Cellular / Foamed** — Polyurethane (PUR/PUF), Polyisocyanurate (PIR), Expanded Polystyrene (EPS), Extruded Polystyrene (XPS), Phenolic foam, Elastomeric (NBR/PVC) foam.
* **Granular / Cork** — Expanded cork (corkboard), Vermiculite, Perlite (now rare on ships).
* **Reflective / Multi-foil** — Aluminium foil + bubble/foam laminates (used for exhaust and steam lines).

### B. By Temperature Range (High-Yield)

| Range | Application on Ship | Preferred Material |
|---|---|---|
| **−60 °C to −25 °C** | Fish reefer holds, LNG vaporisers | Polyurethane (PUF), XPS, Polyurethane + cork composite |
| **−25 °C to 0 °C** | Cold rooms, chill rooms | PUF, PIR, EPS |
| **0 °C to +15 °C** | Provision rooms (fruit, veg) | PUF, EPS, rock wool |
| **+15 °C to +30 °C** | A.C. cabins, galley, ductwork | Fibreglass, elastomeric, phenolic foam |
| **Hot side (>100 °C)** | Steam pipes, exhaust lagging | Calcium silicate, ceramic fibre, rock wool |

> [!NOTE]
> **The marine "workhorse"** is **Polyurethane Foam (PUF / PUR)** because it offers the lowest $k$ per unit thickness and can be **foamed-in-place** to fit complex curved hulls and pipe penetrations. However, for **passenger ships**, SOLAS demands non-combustible insulants, so **rock wool** dominates.

## 2.3 Mechanism of Heat Transfer Through an Insulated Wall

For a composite insulated wall, heat is transferred by:

1. **Convection** from the warm air to the outer insulation surface ($h_o$).
2. **Conduction** through the insulation layer ($k_{ins}/L$).
3. **Conduction** through the structural steel liner and vapour barrier (if any).
4. **Convection** from the inner insulation surface to the cold air in the cold room ($h_i$).

For a simple plane wall with a single insulation layer bonded to steel, the **overall heat transfer coefficient** is:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{L_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Setting a target $U$ (e.g., 0.30 W/m²·K for a fish hold) and solving for $L_{ins}$ is the standard KTU design question. The "U-value" of the assembled wall — not just the bare $k$ of the foam — is what matters for energy accounting.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the table below. Every 14-mark question in Module 2 reduces to one or more of these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steady-state conductive heat gain | $Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{L}$ | W | Fourier's Law (plane wall) |
| 2 | Heat gain through composite wall | $Q = \dfrac{A \Delta T}{\sum (L_i / k_i)}$ | W | Resistances in series |
| 3 | Heat gain through cylinder (pipe) | $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi L k (T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | W | Critical for refrigerant/brine lines |
| 4 | Heat gain through sphere (tank) | $Q = \dfrac{4 \pi k (T_i - T_o)}{(1/r_i - 1/r_o)}$ | W | For spherical cargo tanks |
| 5 | Overall U-value | $U = 1 / \left( \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} \right)$ | W/m²·K | Includes surface films |
| 6 | R-value (thermal resistance) | $R = L/k$ | m²·K/W | American convention; inverse of U for single layer |
| 7 | Log-mean radius difference | $r_{lm} = \dfrac{r_o - r_i}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | m | Used to linearise cylindrical conduction |
| 8 | Critical radius of insulation (pipe) | $r_c = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$ | m | Below $r_c$ insulation *increases* heat loss! |
| 9 | Surface temperature (plane wall) | $T_s = T_a - \dfrac{Q}{h A}$ | °C | Used to check dew-point / condensation |
| 10 | Dew-point / condensation criterion | $T_s \ge T_{dp}$ to avoid sweating | °C | $T_{dp}$ from psychrometric chart |
| 11 | Economic thickness (incremental saving method) | $\dfrac{dC_{insulation}}}{dL} = \dfrac{dC_{energy}}}{dL}$ | ₹/m | Minimum life-cycle cost thickness |
| 12 | Compressor power saved per kW of $Q$ | $W_c = Q \left( \dfrac{T_{hot}}{T_{cold}} - 1 \right)$ | W | Inverse Carnot estimate |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown formulas above, **never write `|x|` with a vertical pipe** inside a table cell. Use `\vert` (as in $\vert T_{dp} \vert$) or split the absolute value into words. Otherwise, the table renders incorrectly.

## 2.5 Real-World Marine Engineering Utility

* **Reefer ships** carrying tuna or swordfish at −25 °C use **PUF panels of 150–200 mm** to keep the daily heat-leak below 8 kW per container.
* **LNG carriers** have a sophisticated **triple barrier** — primary stainless steel membrane, secondary insulation (perlite or foam glass), and tertiary nitrogen barrier — to limit boil-off gas (BOG) to 0.10–0.15 %/day.
* **Cruise ships** under SOLAS Chapter II-2 forbid combustible insulants in corridors and stairways; they use **non-combustible rock wool** with aluminium foil vapour barrier.
* **Naval submarines** use **elastomeric rubber** insulation (Armaflex-class) on chilled water and A.C. ducts to limit acoustic and IR signatures.
* **INS Harshit-class frigates** (Indian Navy) and commercial tankers of SCI use **PUF-cork composite** for refrigerated provision rooms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Loss Through a Composite Cylindrical Pipe Insulation (Refrigerant Suction Line)

**Scenario:** A refrigerant suction line of outside radius $r_1 = 25$ mm is wrapped with elastomeric insulation of thickness $L = 32$ mm. The refrigerant surface is at $T_i = 5$ °C, the ambient is at $T_a = 35$ °C, the conductivity of insulation $k = 0.034$ W/m·K, and the outer surface coefficient $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K. Find the heat gain per metre of pipe length.

### Step 1 — Identify geometry and radii

The bare pipe outer radius is:

$$r_1 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$$

The insulated outer radius is:

$$r_2 = r_1 + L = 0.025 + 0.032 = 0.057 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Resistances in series for a cylinder

For a long cylinder with an external film, the total thermal resistance per unit length is:

$$\frac{R_{tot}}{L_{pipe}} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o \, 2 \pi r_2}$$

**Why this form?** Because the area for conduction in a cylinder changes with radius ($A = 2 \pi r L_{pipe}$), and integrating Fourier's law radially yields a logarithmic mean area term.

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

Conduction resistance:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.057/0.025)}{2 \pi (0.034)} = \frac{\ln(2.28)}{0.2136} = \frac{0.824}{0.2136} = 3.857 \text{ m·K/W per m length}$$

Convection resistance outside:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2 \pi \times 0.057} = \frac{1}{3.581} = 0.2792 \text{ m·K/W per m length}$$

### Step 4 — Total resistance

$$R_{tot} = 3.857 + 0.2792 = 4.137 \text{ m·K/W per m}$$

### Step 5 — Heat gain per metre

$$q = \frac{T_a - T_i}{R_{tot}} = \frac{35 - 5}{4.137} = \frac{30}{4.137} = 7.25 \text{ W/m}$$

### Step 6 — Verify and interpret

Without insulation, with $r_1 = 0.025$ m exposed bare to air:

$$q_{bare} = \frac{T_a - T_i}{1/(h_o 2 \pi r_1)} = (35 - 5) \times 10 \times 2 \pi \times 0.025 = 47.1 \text{ W/m}$$

So the insulation reduces heat gain from **47.1 W/m → 7.25 W/m**, a saving of **~85 %**. ✅ Sanity check passes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $A = 2 \pi r_{avg} L$ and forget the log-mean term. For thick insulation ($r_2/r_1 > 1.5$), the log-mean form is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Economic Thickness of Insulation on a Cold Room Wall

**Scenario:** A ship's cold room wall has a steel plate ($L_{st} = 5$ mm, $k_{st} = 45$ W/m·K) lined with PUF insulation. Cold room temperature $T_c = -20$ °C, ambient $T_a = 32$ °C, $k_{ins} = 0.022$ W/m·K, electricity cost $C_e = \text{₹} 8/\text{kWh}$, plant COP = 2.5, annual operating hours = 6000 h, insulation cost $C_{ins} = \text{₹} 8000/\text{m}^3$.

### Step 1 — Express the total resistance

For a plane wall of area $A$:

$$R_{tot} = \frac{L_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{L_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

Using $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K (forced convection in cold room) and $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K (engine room air):

$$R_{tot} = \frac{0.005}{45} + \frac{L_{ins}}{0.022} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{12}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.000111 + 45.45 \, L_{ins} + 0.1111 + 0.0833$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.1945 + 45.45 \, L_{ins} \quad [\text{m}^2 \text{K/W}]$$

### Step 2 — Heat flux through the wall

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{tot}} = \frac{32 - (-20)}{0.1945 + 45.45 L_{ins}} = \frac{52}{0.1945 + 45.45 L_{ins}} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

### Step 3 — Annual energy cost (per m²)

Electrical energy consumed (input to the refrigeration plant) is $Q/\text{COP}$ per second, so for 6000 h/year:

$$C_{energy}(L) = \frac{q}{1000 \times \text{COP}} \times 6000 \times C_e = \frac{52 \times 6000 \times 8}{1000 \times 2.5 \times (0.1945 + 45.45 L_{ins})}$$

$$C_{energy}(L) = \frac{998{,}400}{0.1945 + 45.45 L_{ins}} \quad [\text{₹/m}^2 \text{ per year}]$$

### Step 4 — Annualised insulation cost

The insulation cost is paid once; annualising over a lifetime of $N = 10$ years with a discount/sinking-fund factor $a = 0.12$ (typical marine economic life):

$$C_{ins,annual}(L) = C_{ins} \times L \times a = 8000 \times L \times 0.12 = 960 \, L \quad [\text{₹/m}^2 \text{ per year}]$$

### Step 5 — Total annual cost

$$C_{total}(L) = \frac{998{,}400}{0.1945 + 45.45 L} + 960 \, L$$

### Step 6 — Differentiate and equate to zero for optimum

Differentiating with respect to $L$:

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dL} = -\frac{998{,}400 \times 45.45}{(0.1945 + 45.45 L)^2} + 960 = 0$$

Solve:

$$(0.1945 + 45.45 L)^2 = \frac{998{,}400 \times 45.45}{960}$$

$$(0.1945 + 45.45 L)^2 = 47{,}267$$

$$0.1945 + 45.45 L = 217.41$$

$$L_{opt} = \frac{217.41 - 0.1945}{45.45} = \frac{217.22}{45.45} = 4.78 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This result is **physically impossible** — the equation shows the linearised surface resistance is negligible, and the "optimum" is dominated by the cheap steel plate. The reason: our $C_e$ and $C_{ins}$ in the data, or units, are off-scale. **In practice** for ship cold rooms, the *minimum permissible* thickness is set by **condensation control (dew point)** or **fire code**, not by classical economics. The **practical economic thickness** is normally **75–150 mm** of PUF.

### Step 7 — Practical design choice: Dew-Point criterion

We must ensure the **outer surface temperature** of the insulation does not drop below the **dew point** of the ambient air.

Suppose ambient RH = 80 % at 32 °C → from psychrometric chart, $T_{dp} \approx 28.4$ °C. The outer surface temperature at steady state is:

$$T_s = T_a - q \times \frac{1}{h_o} = 32 - \frac{52}{0.1945 + 45.45 L} \times \frac{1}{12}$$

For no condensation, $T_s \ge 28.4$ °C:

$$32 - \frac{4.333}{0.1945 + 45.45 L} \ge 28.4$$

$$\frac{4.333}{0.1945 + 45.45 L} \le 3.6$$

$$0.1945 + 45.45 L \ge 1.204$$

$$L \ge \frac{1.009}{45.45} = 0.0222 \text{ m} = 22.2 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore a minimum of **~ 25 mm of PUF** is required to prevent sweating. Final design: $L = 75$ mm (standard) — comfortably satisfying condensation and providing a margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation

For a small pipe, if the outer radius of insulation $r_2$ is **less than** the critical radius $r_c$, adding insulation **increases** the heat loss. Derivation:

The total resistance for a cylinder with external film is:

$$R_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o 2 \pi r_2 L}$$

For a given $r_1$ and $L$ (so $r_2 = r_1 + L$), heat flow $Q$ is maximum (resistance minimum) when $\partial R_{tot} / \partial r_2 = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial R_{tot}}{\partial r_2} = \frac{1}{2 \pi k L r_2} - \frac{1}{h_o 2 \pi r_2^2 L} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h_o r_2^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = \frac{k}{h_o}$$

Defining $r_c = k_{ins} / h_o$, the critical radius of insulation is:

$$r_c = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For PUF ($k = 0.022$) and still air ($h_o \approx 5$ W/m²·K), $r_c = 0.0044$ m = 4.4 mm. **For all practical marine pipe diameters, $r_2 \gg r_c$, so insulation always saves energy.** The critical radius issue arises only for **insulating thin electrical wires** with low-$k$ insulants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting Insulation Thickness on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    S1 --> S2[Get RH_outside and T_dewpoint]

    S3 -->|No| S4[Set T_surface >= T_dewpoint + 2C\nSolve for L_min_condensation]
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Get electricity cost C_e and insulation cost C_i]
    S5 --> S6[Compute C_total L = C_ins + C_energy]
    S6 --> S7[dC_total / dL = 0\nSolve L_optimal]
    S7 --> S8{L_optimal greater than L_min_condensation?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Adopt L_optimal]
    S8 -->|No| S4
    S4 --> S10{Code check\nSOLAS / DNV}
    S10 --> S11[Adopt L_design = max L_optimal, L_min_cond, L_code]
    S9 --> S10
    style S0 fill:#0a6,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#ffd
    style S7 fill:#dfd
    style S11 fill:#06c,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define thermal conductivity and state three desirable properties of a marine insulation material.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal conductivity ($k$) of a material is defined as the rate of heat flow in steady state through a unit area of the material of unit thickness per unit temperature difference across it, with units **W/m·K** (or W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹). Mathematically, $Q = k A \Delta T / L$.

Three desirable properties of a marine insulation material are:

1. **Low thermal conductivity** ($k \le 0.04$ W/m·K) — to minimise parasitic heat gain.
2. **Low water-vapour permeability / closed cell structure** — to avoid moisture ingress that degrades $k$ and corrodes hull steel.
3. **Non-combustibility** (as per IMO FTP Code Part 1) and **low flame-spread** for passenger ship compliance with SOLAS Chapter II-2.

(Other valid properties: lightweight, non-toxic, vermin-resistant, high compressive strength.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a vapour barrier in marine insulation? Why is it placed on the *warm* side?**

**Model Answer:**

A vapour barrier is an essentially impermeable membrane (typically 0.05 mm aluminium foil or 0.2 mm PE film) laminated to or wrapped around the insulation layer to prevent water vapour in the warm ambient from diffusing into the insulation and condensing within it.

It is placed on the **warm side** because the **vapour pressure of water** is higher on the warm side. Without the barrier, water vapour would migrate down its partial-pressure gradient into the cold insulation, condense when it crosses the dew-point isotherm, and **saturate the insulation**, increasing its effective $k$ by up to 5× and causing corrosion of the steel beneath.

(2 supporting points: 1 mark each; definition 1 mark.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, similar pattern]

**(a) (7 marks)** A cold room of a reefer ship is maintained at $T_i = -25$ °C. The wall consists of 6 mm mild steel ($k = 45$ W/m·K) lined on the inside with polyurethane foam ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K). The outer ambient is at $T_o = 35$ °C with $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K and the inside film coefficient $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K. Find the **minimum thickness of PUF required to prevent condensation on the outer surface** when the ambient relative humidity is 80 %.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the difference between **economic thickness** and **critical radius of insulation**. Why does the critical-radius phenomenon *not* normally affect marine pipework insulated with PUF?

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Find the ambient dew point.**
Using the psychrometric relation, saturated vapour pressure at 35 °C is $p_{sat,35} \approx 5.628$ kPa. At 80 % RH, $p_v = 0.80 \times 5.628 = 4.502$ kPa. The dew point is the saturation temperature corresponding to 4.502 kPa. From steam tables, $T_{dp} \approx 31.3$ °C.

**Step 2 — Allowable outer surface temperature.**
To prevent condensation we require $T_s \ge T_{dp}$, i.e. $T_s \ge 31.3$ °C. Apply a 1 °C safety margin, so target $T_s = 32.3$ °C (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Total thermal resistance per unit area.**
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{L_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$
$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{0.006}{45} + \frac{L_{ins}}{0.022} + \frac{1}{12}$$
$$\frac{1}{U} = 0.1111 + 0.000133 + 45.45 L_{ins} + 0.0833 = 0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins}$$

**Step 4 — Express $T_s$ in terms of $L_{ins}$.**
The heat flux $q = U (T_o - T_i) = U (35 - (-25)) = 60 U$.
The outer surface temperature drop is $q/h_o$, so:
$$T_s = T_o - \frac{q}{h_o} = 35 - \frac{60 U}{12} = 35 - 5 U$$
With $U = 1 / (0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins})$:
$$T_s = 35 - \frac{5}{0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the no-condensation criterion.**
$$35 - \frac{5}{0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins}} \ge 32.3$$
$$\frac{5}{0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins}} \le 2.7$$
$$0.1946 + 45.45 L_{ins} \ge 1.852$$
$$L_{ins} \ge \frac{1.657}{45.45} = 0.0365 \text{ m} = 36.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
Adopt a standard PUF panel of **40 mm** (next commercial size up). Total heat flux:
$$U = 1 / (0.1946 + 45.45 \times 0.040) = 1 / 2.013 = 0.497 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$
$$q = 0.497 \times 60 = 29.8 \text{ W/m}^2$$
This is well within the design value of 25–35 W/m²·K for a fish reefer hold. ✅

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

* **Economic thickness** is the thickness of insulation at which the **sum of the annualised capital cost of insulation plus the annual energy cost of heat loss** is minimum. Mathematically, it is the $L$ at which $d(C_{ins} + C_{energy})/dL = 0$. (2 marks)

* **Critical radius of insulation** is the **outer radius of the insulation at which the heat loss from an insulated cylinder reaches a maximum** (i.e. resistance is minimum) before decreasing with further thickness. It is given by $r_c = k_{ins} / h_o$. (2 marks)

* **Why $r_c$ rarely matters for marine PUF pipes:** For PUF, $k = 0.022$ W/m·K. Even in **stagnant air** ($h_o = 5$ W/m²·K), $r_c = 0.022/5 = 0.0044$ m = 4.4 mm. Almost all marine pipes and ducts have an outer radius $r_1 \ge 10$ mm, and any practical insulation thickness gives $r_2 \gg r_c$, so the heat loss is monotonically decreasing with thickness — insulation always helps. (3 marks for explanation and one example)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023, similar pattern]

**(a) (7 marks)** A refrigerant suction line of outside diameter 50 mm carries R-134a at 5 °C. The pipe is insulated with 30 mm thick elastomeric foam ($k = 0.034$ W/m·K) and exposed to engine-room air at 38 °C with $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K. Calculate **(i)** the heat gain per metre of pipe and **(ii)** the temperature at the outer surface of the insulation. State whether condensation will occur if the engine-room air has a dew point of 30 °C.

**(b) (7 marks)** With the aid of a suitable block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure** to select the type of marine insulation for a given application. Discuss the role of SOLAS / IMO regulations in your answer.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**(i) Heat gain per metre:**

* $r_1 = 25$ mm = 0.025 m, $L = 30$ mm → $r_2 = 0.055$ m.
* Conduction resistance (per metre length):
$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.055/0.025)}{2 \pi (0.034)} = \frac{\ln(2.20)}{0.2136} = \frac{0.7885}{0.2136} = 3.692 \text{ m·K/W}$$
* External film resistance:
$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2 \pi \times 0.055} = \frac{1}{3.456} = 0.2894 \text{ m·K/W}$$
* Total resistance:
$$R_{tot} = 3.692 + 0.289 = 3.981 \text{ m·K/W per m}$$
* Heat gain:
$$q = \frac{38 - 5}{3.981} = \frac{33}{3.981} = 8.29 \text{ W/m}$$

**(ii) Outer surface temperature:**
The temperature drop across the outer film is:
$$\Delta T_{film} = q \times R_{conv} = 8.29 \times 0.2894 = 2.40 \text{ °C}$$
$$T_s = T_a - \Delta T_{film} = 38 - 2.40 = 35.6 \text{ °C}$$

**Condensation check:**
Ambient dew point $T_{dp} = 30$ °C. Since $T_s = 35.6$ °C $\gg T_{dp}$, **no condensation will form** on the outer surface. ✅ (2 marks for the conclusion; 1 mark for the calculation)

**Comparison with bare pipe:** Without insulation, with $h_o$ acting on bare steel at $r_1 = 0.025$ m:
$$q_{bare} = h_o \times 2 \pi r_1 \times (T_a - T_i) = 10 \times 2 \pi \times 0.025 \times 33 = 51.8 \text{ W/m}$$
So the insulation reduces heat gain by **84 %** — an enormous saving.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step-by-step selection procedure for marine insulation (with reference to block diagram in Section 4.3):**

1. **Identify the boundary conditions**: Determine the inner (cold) temperature, outer (ambient) temperature, ambient relative humidity, and corresponding dew point. (1 mark)
2. **Determine applicable codes**: Consult SOLAS Chapter II-2 (for passenger vessels, non-combustibility is mandatory), IMO FTP Code, and the rules of the classification society (e.g. DNV GL Pt.3 Ch.6, Lloyd's SSC Rules, ABS SVR). (1 mark)
3. **List candidate materials**: From the marine insulation database, list materials whose conductivity and temperature rating are compatible. For low-temperature applications (−25 °C), include PUF, PIR, XPS, expanded cork. For hot pipes, include rock wool, ceramic fibre. (1 mark)
4. **Apply dew-point / anti-sweating criterion**: For cold surfaces, calculate the minimum thickness $L_{min}$ that keeps the outer surface temperature at or above the ambient dew point, using $T_s = T_a - q/h_o$. (1 mark)
5. **Compute energy cost vs insulation cost** to determine the economic thickness $L_{opt}$, and adopt $L = \max(L_{min}, L_{opt}, L_{code})$. (1 mark)
6. **Verify fire and acoustic performance**: Confirm the chosen material has the required fire rating (e.g. Class A, low flame spread per IMO FTP Part 5) and acoustic absorption for accommodation areas. (1 mark)
7. **Vapour barrier selection**: For cold surfaces, ensure a vapour barrier (aluminium foil) is installed on the warm side to prevent moisture ingress and consequent loss of $k$. (1 mark)

**Role of SOLAS / IMO:** Under SOLAS Chapter II-2 Reg. 5.3, insulation of accommodation, service and control spaces on passenger ships carrying more than 36 passengers must be of **non-combustible material** tested per the IMO Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code. The FTP Code specifies three tests: (i) non-combustibility test, (ii) smoke and toxicity test, (iii) surface flammability test. PUF, being combustible, is therefore restricted to cold rooms, cargo holds and insulated containers; it cannot be used for A.C. ductwork in passenger corridors. Rock wool, classified as non-combustible, is the only permitted insulant in such cases. (1 mark for the SOLAS link)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks per question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the surface film resistance** ($1/h_i$ and $1/h_o$) — if the question says "steady state" and gives film coefficients, you MUST include them. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Using the wrong area** for a cylinder: writing $A = 2 \pi r_{avg} L$ instead of integrating to get $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ — for $r_o/r_i > 1.4$, the log-mean form is non-negotiable. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up "economic thickness" and "minimum thickness for no-condensation"** — the design thickness is the **maximum** of the two, not the minimum. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring units**: thermal conductivity is W/m·K, not W/m·°C numerically equivalent but **always write "K"** in units. (Loss: ½ mark)
> 5. **Omitting the vapour barrier discussion** in design questions — the examiner explicitly tests whether you understand that insulation alone, without a vapour barrier, can absorb moisture and lose effectiveness. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 6. **Writing "critical radius of insulation"** when the question asks for "economic thickness" — these are entirely different concepts. Critical radius is a geometric/heat-transfer phenomenon; economic thickness is a life-cycle cost concept.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* Insulation **reduces heat gain** $Q$ into a cold space; it is the cheapest "passive" energy-saving tool on a ship.
* The master design equation for a plane wall is $Q = A \Delta T / \sum (L_i/k_i)$.
* For a **cylinder**, use $Q = 2 \pi L k \Delta T / \ln(r_o/r_i)$ — **never** use $2 \pi r_{avg} L k \Delta T$ for thick insulation.
* **Thermal conductivity $k$** of good marine insulants lies between **0.020 and 0.045 W/m·K**.
* The **marine workhorse insulant** is **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** for cold rooms and refrigerant lines; **Rock Wool** for passenger-ship A.C. ductwork.
* The **vapour barrier** (aluminium foil) is installed on the **warm side** of the insulation to prevent moisture ingress.
* **Minimum thickness = max(L_dewpoint, L_economic, L_code)**.
* The **critical radius of insulation** $r_c = k_{ins} / h_o$ is irrelevant for marine PUF pipework because typical pipe outer radii are far larger than $r_c$.
* **Fire safety** is governed by SOLAS Chapter II-2, IMO FTP Code, and the classification society rules. Use **non-combustible** insulants in passenger accommodation.
* Units to remember: $k$ in **W/m·K**, $U$ in **W/m²·K**, $R$ in **m²·K/W**, $q$ in **W/m²** (plane) or **W/m** (cylinder), $Q$ in **W**.
* A 1 kW of heat leak into a cold room costs approximately **0.3–0.4 kW of extra compressor shaft power** (inverse Carnot factor).
* Economic thickness formula (incremental method): $dC_{insulation}/dL = dC_{energy}/dL$ gives the optimum.
* Always include **surface film resistances** unless the problem states they are negligible.
* Marine applications: reefer holds (−25 °C), provision rooms (0–8 °C), A.C. cabins (22–25 °C, 50 % RH), chilled-water pipes (5–12 °C), exhaust lines (>100 °C).
* For condensation control, $T_{surface} \ge T_{dewpoint} + 1\text{–}2$ °C safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Leakage Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Leakage Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Heat Leakage Estimation — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat Leakage** in the context of shipboard refrigeration and air conditioning is defined as the *unwanted, parasitic rate of heat transfer* (in **Watts** or **kW**) that flows from the warmer external environment into a refrigerated space, cold room, insulated pipe, or air-conditioned cabin — purely due to a temperature gradient, and *without contributing to the intended cooling function* of the system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **Total Heat Leakage Load (Q_L)** is the sum of all conductive, convective, and radiative heat inflows through the envelope of the refrigerated/air-conditioned volume, and it forms the **single largest component** of the design refrigeration load on a ship (typically **40 % – 60 %** of total compressor duty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"Heat leakage is the rate of unwanted heat ingress into a refrigerated or air-conditioned space, estimated as the product of overall heat transfer coefficient, surface area, and temperature differential between the conditioned space and its ambient surroundings."*

The governing equation in its most general form:

$$Q_L \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $U$ = **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = **Effective Heat Transfer Area** (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = **Temperature Difference** between the conditioned interior and the external ambient (K or °C)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ice-Box on a Hot Deck"

Imagine a perfectly sealed lunch box made of thin steel sitting on a ship's sun-baked steel deck at 45 °C. You place ice inside at 0 °C. The ice does **not** stay cold by itself — heat from the deck, sun, and surrounding air **leaks inward** continuously through every wall, lid, and corner. The faster the heat leaks in, the faster the ice melts.

To slow this down, you wrap the lunch box in a thick woolen blanket (this is the **insulation**). Now the heat has to travel through *steel + wool + air-gap* before reaching the ice — a much harder journey. The "rate of heat leakage" is the **Woolen Blanket Design Problem** of ship refrigeration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Realities of a Ship's Environment** (Board Favourite):
> 1. **Tropical ambient temperatures** are extreme (35 °C DB, 30 °C sea water, 50 °C engine rooms).
> 2. **Solar radiation** on unshaded deck-heads can add an equivalent temperature of **11 – 17 °C** to the design ambient.
> 3. **Vibrations and structural flexing** of the hull can crack rigid insulation, *increasing* heat leakage over the ship's life.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Revision)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Shipboard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Conductivity — Cork | $k_{cork}$ | **0.040 – 0.045** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity — PUF | $k_{PUF}$ | **0.020 – 0.024** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity — Mineral Wool | $k_{MW}$ | **0.035 – 0.040** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity — Foam Glass | $k_{FG}$ | **0.040 – 0.045** | W/m·K |
| Thermal Conductivity — Mild Steel | $k_{steel}$ | **45.0** | W/m·K |
| Internal Surface Film Coefficient | $h_i$ | **8 – 10** (still air) | W/m²·K |
| External Surface Film Coefficient | $h_o$ | **10 – 15** (engine room air) | W/m²·K |
| Sea Water Film Coefficient | $h_{sw}$ | **200 – 500** (high velocity) | W/m²·K |
| Standard Design ΔT (Provision Room) | $\Delta T$ | **40 – 45** | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these values matter in ships:** Unlike a building (where ambient is ~30 °C), a ship's refrigerated space may face an engine bulkhead at **55 °C** on one side and a sea-water chill at **5 °C** on another. The **asymmetric boundary conditions** make *uniform U-value* assumptions dangerous and examiners specifically test this.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermal Resistance Network — The "Electrical Analogy"

Heat leakage follows the same mathematics as **Ohm's Law** in electricity. Replace *current* with *heat flow (Q)*, *voltage* with *temperature difference (ΔT)*, and *resistance* with *thermal resistance (R)*:

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{total}}$$

For a **composite plane wall** of $n$ layers in series (the most common case in a ship's insulated cold-room wall — steel + insulation + steel + air film + air film):

$$Q \;=\; \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

Defining the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** $U$:

$$\dfrac{1}{U} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic (often asked as 3-mark):** *"Add resistances, not conductances."* Just like resistors in series in electricity, thermal resistances are additive in series.

## 2.2 Heat Leakage Through an Insulated Pipe (Cylindrical Geometry)

Refrigerant suction lines, liquid lines, and chilled-water pipes on board are cylindrical. The flat-wall formula **over-estimates** heat gain because the area changes with radius. The correct expression is:

$$\dfrac{Q}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \,(T_o - T_i)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h_i \, r_1} + \dfrac{1}{h_o \, r_2}}$$

where $r_1$ is the pipe's outer radius (inner radius of insulation) and $r_2$ is the outer radius of the insulation.

### Critical Radius of Insulation — A "Counter-Intuitive" Concept

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Missed Concept in KTU Papers:** Adding insulation to a *small* pipe can sometimes **increase** heat loss! This happens when the bare pipe radius is less than the **critical radius**:

$$r_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{insulation}}{h_o}$$

- If $r_{pipe} > r_{cr}$ → adding insulation **reduces** heat loss (normal case).
- If $r_{pipe} < r_{cr}$ → heat loss **first increases** to a maximum at $r_{cr}$, then decreases. Common for small electrical cables and capillary tubes.

## 2.3 Sources of Heat Leakage in a Ship (The Master Checklist)

For a typical refrigerated **provision room** on a merchant vessel, heat leakage occurs through:

1. **Boundary walls** (shell, deck-head, deck, bulkheads) — dominant.
2. **Insulated doors and hatches** — high leakage if not gasketted.
3. **Penetrations** — pipe and cable glands (often neglected, KTU trick question).
4. **Floor** if room is directly above a heated tank or engine room.
5. **Ceiling** if exposed to sun on a tropical voyage.
6. **Adjacent heated spaces** — galley, boiler room, incinerator room.
7. **Sun-load equivalent temperature** on exposed deck-heads ($T_{sol-air}$).
8. **Air infiltration** through door openings (in ships, often **positive pressure** from AC ducting).

## 2.4 Condensation Check (Dew-Point Design)

A critical shipboard design rule — *the external surface temperature $T_{so}$ must be above the ambient dew point*, otherwise condensation drips, insulation wets out, and heat leakage *skyrockets* (wet insulation's $k$ rises 5–10×):

$$T_{so} \;=\; T_i \;+\; U \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j}\right)$$

If $T_{so} < T_{dew\text{-}point,ambient}$ → **increase insulation thickness** or add a **vapour barrier**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | Total heat leakage, plane surfaces | W |
| 2 | $1/U = 1/h_i + \sum x_j/k_j + 1/h_o$ | Composite wall resistance | m²·K/W |
| 3 | $Q/L = 2\pi \Delta T \;/\; [\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(h_o r_2)]$ | Insulated pipe | W/m |
| 4 | $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$ | Critical radius of insulation | m |
| 5 | $R = x/k$ | Thermal resistance of a layer | m²·K/W |
| 6 | $T_{so} = T_i + U \Delta T (1/h_i + \sum x/k)$ | External surface temperature | °C |
| 7 | $T_{sol\text{-}air} = T_{amb} + \alpha I/h_o$ | Sun-load equivalent temp. | °C |
| 8 | $Q_{inf} = \dot{V} \rho c_p \Delta T$ | Air infiltration through doors | W |
| 9 | $Q_{respiration} = m \cdot q_r$ | Fresh-produce heat (fruits/veg) | W |
| 10 | $Q_{product} = m c_p (T_1 - T_2)/t$ | Sensible product cooling | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** A correct heat-leakage estimate determines (a) compressor selection, (b) insulation thickness, (c) vapour-barrier specification, and (d) defrost cycle frequency. A 10 % error in heat leakage on a reefer ship translates to ~50 tonnes of extra fuel per year.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flow Through a Composite Plane Wall

Consider a wall made of $n$ layers, each of thickness $x_j$ and thermal conductivity $k_j$. At steady state, the same heat $Q$ flows through every layer (continuity), but the temperature drops across each layer add up to the total $\Delta T$.

For layer $j$ (Fourier's Law, 1-D, steady state):

$$Q_j \;=\; \dfrac{k_j A (T_{j-1} - T_j)}{x_j} \quad\Rightarrow\quad T_{j-1} - T_j \;=\; \dfrac{Q \, x_j}{k_j A}$$

Summing over all $n$ layers *and* the two surface films:

$$\Delta T \;=\; Q \left[\dfrac{1}{h_i A} + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{x_j}{k_j A} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A}\right]$$

Rearranging for $Q$:

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}} \;=\; U A \Delta T\,}$$

This is the derivation examiners expect when a 14-mark question asks "derive the U-value expression for a composite wall."

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Radius of Insulation

Consider an insulated cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$ (bare pipe) covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and conductivity $k$. Total thermal resistance per unit length:

$$R_{total} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi h_o r_2}$$

Heat loss per unit length:

$$\dfrac{Q}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \Delta T}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

To find the radius $r_2$ that **maximises** $Q/L$ (worst case), differentiate $Q/L$ with respect to $r_2$ and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(Q/L)}{dr_2} \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad -\dfrac{1}{k r_2} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_2^{\,2}} \;=\; 0$$

Solving:

$$\dfrac{1}{h_o r_2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{k}\quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\,r_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o}\,}$$

This is the **critical radius**. If bare pipe radius < $r_{cr}$, adding insulation increases heat loss until $r_2 = r_{cr}$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Composite Wall of a Ship's Provision Room

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A provision cold room on a container ship has internal dimensions 4 m × 3 m × 2.5 m. The wall construction (from outside to inside) is:
- 6 mm mild steel plate ($k = 45$ W/m·K)
- 100 mm cork board ($k = 0.04$ W/m·K)
- 6 mm mild steel plate ($k = 45$ W/m·K)

Internal air: $-5$ °C. External air: $35$ °C. Surface coefficients: $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K. Determine:
1. The overall $U$-value.
2. The heat leakage through the six wall surfaces.

**Step 1 — Resistances per unit area (1 m²):**

$$R_i = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{9} = 0.1111 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{steel,1} = \dfrac{0.006}{45} = 0.000133 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{cork} = \dfrac{0.100}{0.04} = 2.5000 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{steel,2} = \dfrac{0.006}{45} = 0.000133 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Sum all resistances (KTU Board line-by-line method):**

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = 0.1111 + 0.000133 + 2.5000 + 0.000133 + 0.0833 = 2.6947 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$\boxed{\,U = \dfrac{1}{2.6947} = 0.371 \text{ W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation (mark-worthy):** The two steel plates contribute only **0.000266 m²·K/W** — i.e., **0.01 %** of the total resistance. The cork alone is **92.7 %** of the resistance. This is why shipyards quote insulation, not steel, as the "thermal envelope."

**Step 3 — Total wall area:**

$$A = 2(4 \times 3) + 2(4 \times 2.5) + 2(3 \times 2.5) = 24 + 20 + 15 = 59 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = 35 - (-5) = 40 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Heat leakage:**

$$Q_L = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.371 \times 59 \times 40 = 875.6 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{0.876 \; kW}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Insulated Refrigerant Suction Line

**Problem Statement:**
A refrigerant suction line in the engine room has:
- Pipe: 25 mm OD, 5 mm wall, mild steel ($k = 45$ W/m·K)
- Insulation: 50 mm thick PUF ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K)
- $T_{pipe} = -10$ °C, $T_{ambient} = 45$ °C
- $h_i = 1500$ W/m²·K (refrigerant side, condensing), $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K
- Length $L = 10$ m

Calculate the heat gain and the critical radius.

**Step 1 — Radii:**

$$r_{pipe,outer} = \dfrac{0.025}{2} = 0.0125 \text{ m}, \quad r_{ins,outer} = 0.0125 + 0.050 = 0.0625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Critical radius check:**

$$r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \dfrac{0.022}{10} = 0.0022 \text{ m} = 2.2 \text{ mm}$$

Since $r_{pipe,outer} = 12.5 \text{ mm} > r_{cr} = 2.2 \text{ mm}$, **adding insulation reduces heat loss** (normal regime).

**Step 3 — Resistance network (cylindrical):**

The refrigerant-side resistance $1/(h_i r_1) = 1/(1500 \times 0.0125) = 0.0533$ m·K/W.

Insulation resistance: $\ln(r_2/r_1)/k = \ln(0.0625/0.0125)/0.022 = \ln(5)/0.022 = 1.6094/0.022 = 73.16$ m·K/W.

External resistance: $1/(h_o r_2) = 1/(10 \times 0.0625) = 1.600$ m·K/W.

Total per-unit-length resistance:

$$R_{total} = 0.0533 + 73.16 + 1.600 = 74.81 \text{ m·K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Heat gain per metre:**

$$\dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{45 - (-10)}{74.81} = \dfrac{55}{74.81} = 0.735 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat gain for 10 m line:**

$$\boxed{\,Q = 0.735 \times 10 = 7.35 \text{ W}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The insulation resistance (73.16) dominates **97.8 %** of the total. Even with *perfect* insulation, the minimum heat gain would still be $\approx 7.0$ W from film coefficients — an absolute physical floor.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Geometry | Boundary | Governing Equation | Typical Shipboard Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane wall | Both sides air | $Q = UA\Delta T$ | Cold-room walls, deck-heads |
| Cylinder | Both sides air | $Q/L = 2\pi\Delta T / [\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + \dots]$ | Insulated refrigerant pipes |
| Cylinder | Inside condensation, outside air | Add $1/(h_i r_1)$ term | Suction lines, chillers |
| Plane wall with sun | $T_{sol\text{-}air}$ outside | $\Delta T$ uses $T_{sol\text{-}air}$ | Deck-heads on tropical voyages |
| Plane wall with vapour barrier | Impermeable film | $R$ of barrier $\approx 0$ | All refrigerated spaces |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B --> B1[Shell Plate to Sea Water]

    C --> C2[Lower Deck over Heated Tank]

    D --> D1[Insulated Door with Gasket]
    D --> D2[Hatch Cover on Deck]

    E --> E1[Refrigerant Pipe Glands]
    E --> E2[Electrical Cable Entries]

    F --> F1[Door Opening Frequency]
    F --> F2[Positive Pressure from AC Duct]

    B1 --> X[Heat Gain Q1]
    B2 --> X
    B3 --> X
    B4 --> X
    C1 --> X
    C2 --> X
    D1 --> X
    D2 --> X
    E1 --> X
    E2 --> X
    F1 --> X
    F2 --> X

    X --> Y[Compressor Sizing and Insulation Design]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Ambient T outside] --> R1[R outside film 1/h sub o]
    R1 --> R2[R steel plate 1 x sub 1 k sub 1]
    R2 --> R3[R insulation x sub 2 k sub 2]
    R3 --> R4[R inner steel x sub 3 k sub 3]
    R4 --> R5[R inside film 1/h sub i]
    R5 --> T2[Internal T inside]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Insulated Refrigerant Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Refrigerant at minus 10 deg C inside pipe] --> P2[Pipe wall steel k equals 45]
    P2 --> P3[Insulation PUF k equals 0 point 0 2 2]
    P3 --> P4[Outer surface film h sub o]
    P4 --> P5[Engine room air at 45 deg C]

    subgraph CRITICAL_RADIUS_CHECK
        C1[Pipe outer radius 12 point 5 mm] --> C2{Compare with r sub cr equals k over h sub o}
        C2 -->|Greater than r sub cr| C3[Insulation reduces heat loss]
        C2 -->|Less than r sub cr| C4[Insulation increases heat loss WARNING]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Heat Leakage Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Identify all surfaces and pipes] --> S2[Step 2 Determine temperatures T inside and T ambient]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 For each surface compute resistance sum]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute U value 1 over sum of R]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute Q equals U A delta T]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Check condensation at outer surface]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Sum all Q values for total heat leak]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Add product respiration personnel lights for total refrigeration load]
```
