# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "heat leakage" in the context of shipboard refrigeration. List the four major sources of heat leakage into a ship's provision cold room.*

**Model Answer:**
Heat leakage is the unwanted rate of heat ingress (in W or kW) into a refrigerated or air-conditioned space due to a temperature gradient across its envelope, without contributing to the intended cooling function. The four major sources are:
1. Conduction through insulated boundary walls, deck, and deck-head.
2. Conduction and convection through doors, hatches, and gaskets.
3. Heat gain from pipe and cable penetrations.
4. Air infiltration and door-opening losses.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing all four sources with one-line descriptions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the condition for the "critical radius of insulation" of a cylindrical pipe. Why is it important in shipboard refrigerant pipe insulation?*

**Model Answer:**
The critical radius of insulation is given by $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$. If the bare pipe outer radius is **less than** $r_{cr}$, then adding insulation **increases** the heat loss until the outer radius reaches $r_{cr}$, after which further insulation reduces it.

**Importance on board:**
- Small-bore suction lines, capillary tubes, and instrument air lines can paradoxically lose *more* heat (and refrigerant) when poorly insulated.
- Ship designers must check that the pipe OD exceeds $r_{cr}$ before specifying insulation thickness.
- For PUF ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K) in still air ($h_o \approx 10$ W/m²·K), $r_{cr} \approx 2.2$ mm — most refrigerant pipes exceed this safely, but small-bore **drain lines** may not.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Wall & Cold Room Heat Leakage

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

A provision cold room on a general-cargo ship has internal dimensions **4 m × 3 m × 2.5 m**. Wall construction (outside to inside): **6 mm mild steel plate** ($k = 45$ W/m·K), **100 mm cork** ($k = 0.04$ W/m·K), **6 mm mild steel** ($k = 45$ W/m·K). Internal temperature: **−5 °C**. External ambient: **35 °C DB, 28 °C WB**. Surface coefficients: $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the overall $U$-value and the heat leakage through the walls, floor, and ceiling. Check whether condensation will form on the external surface of the cold room. If yes, recommend additional cork insulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The room contains **1500 kg of meat** to be cooled from **25 °C to −5 °C in 12 hours** ($c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K). Two crew members work inside for **4 hours/day** (each releasing 200 W sensible + 150 W latent). Lighting load is **200 W for 8 hours/day**. Door openings occur **12 times/day**, each lasting 1 min, with 2 m³ of ambient air entering (assume half the $\Delta T$). Calculate the **total refrigeration load**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Overall U-value (composite wall, plane geometry):**

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{x_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{x_3}{k_3} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$$

Substituting values:

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{0.006}{45} + \dfrac{0.100}{0.04} + \dfrac{0.006}{45} + \dfrac{1}{12}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = 0.1111 + 0.000133 + 2.5000 + 0.000133 + 0.0833 = 2.6947 \text{ m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K/W}$$

$$U = 0.371 \text{ W/m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Total surface area:**

$$A = 2(4 \times 3) + 2(4 \times 2.5) + 2(3 \times 2.5) = 24 + 20 + 15 = 59 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Heat leakage:**

$$\Delta T = 35 - (-5) = 40 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_L = 0.371 \times 59 \times 40 = 875.6 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{0.876 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Condensation check:**

External surface temperature:

$$T_{so} = T_i + U \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j}\right)$$

$$T_{so} = -5 + 0.371 \times 40 \times (0.1111 + 0.000133 + 2.5000 + 0.000133)$$

$$T_{so} = -5 + 14.84 \times 2.6114 = -5 + 38.75 = 33.75 \text{ °C}$$

Ambient dew point at 35 °C DB, 28 °C WB $\approx$ **30.5 °C**.

Since $T_{so} = 33.75$ °C > $T_{dp} = 30.5$ °C → **No condensation will form.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a):**
> Students commonly lose 2 marks by:
> (i) Forgetting to add BOTH surface film resistances (1/h_i and 1/h_o) — each missing = **1 mark deducted**.
> (ii) Using the **WB temperature** instead of DB for $\Delta T$ when WB is given. WB is only used for the **dew-point check**, not the heat leakage calculation.
> (iii) Stopping after $Q$ and skipping the **condensation check** — board examiners in 2024 specifically docked 2 marks for this.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Product cooling load (sensible):**

$$Q_{prod} = \dfrac{m \, c_p \, (T_1 - T_2)}{t} = \dfrac{1500 \times 3.5 \times (25 - (-5))}{12 \times 3600}$$

$$Q_{prod} = \dfrac{1500 \times 3.5 \times 30}{43200} = \dfrac{157500}{43200} = 3.646 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Personnel load (time-averaged):**

Total per person: $200 + 150 = 350$ W. Two persons, 4 h/day:

$$Q_{pers} = 2 \times 350 \times \dfrac{4}{24} = 116.7 \text{ W} = 0.117 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Lighting load (time-averaged):**

$$Q_{light} = 200 \times \dfrac{8}{24} = 66.7 \text{ W} = 0.067 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Air infiltration (door openings):**

Volume per opening = 2 m³. Density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Effective $\Delta T = 0.5 \times 40 = 20$ K. 12 openings/day:

$$Q_{inf} = 12 \times 2 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 20 \times \dfrac{1}{24 \times 3600}$$

$$Q_{inf} = 12 \times 2 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 20 \times 1.157 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$Q_{inf} = 0.00667 \text{ kW} \approx 7 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Total refrigeration load:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_L + Q_{prod} + Q_{pers} + Q_{light} + Q_{inf}$$

$$Q_{total} = 0.876 + 3.646 + 0.117 + 0.067 + 0.007 = \boxed{\,4.713 \text{ kW}\,}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Insulated Refrigerant Pipe

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Model 2024]**

A refrigerant suction line in a ship's engine room has the following data:
- Pipe: **50 mm OD, 5 mm wall**, mild steel ($k = 45$ W/m·K)
- Insulation: **40 mm thick mineral wool** ($k = 0.04$ W/m·K)
- $T_{pipe} = -15$ °C, $T_{ambient} = 50$ °C
- $h_i = 1500$ W/m²·K (refrigerant side), $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K
- Length $L = 20$ m

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the critical radius of insulation. Verify whether 40 mm thickness is adequate (i.e., is the heat loss reducing?). Calculate the heat gain per metre and the total heat gain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If PUF ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K) replaces the mineral wool, what thickness gives the **same heat gain**? Discuss the practical implications for shipboard installation.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Radii:**

$$r_{pipe,outer} = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad r_{ins,outer} = 0.025 + 0.040 = 0.065 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Critical radius:**

$$r_{cr} = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \dfrac{0.04}{12} = 0.00333 \text{ m} = 3.33 \text{ mm}$$

Since $r_{pipe,outer} = 25 \text{ mm} \gg r_{cr} = 3.33 \text{ mm}$ → **Insulation reduces heat loss.** ✓

**Step 3 — Resistance per unit length:**

Inside film: $R_i = 1/(h_i r_1) = 1/(1500 \times 0.025) = 0.0267$ m·K/W.

Insulation: $R_{ins} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/k = \ln(0.065/0.025)/0.04 = \ln(2.6)/0.04 = 0.9555/0.04 = 23.89$ m·K/W.

Outside film: $R_o = 1/(h_o r_2) = 1/(12 \times 0.065) = 1.282$ m·K/W.

Sum: $R_{tot} = 0.0267 + 23.89 + 1.282 = 25.20$ m·K/W.

**Step 4 — Heat gain per metre:**

$$\dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{tot}} = \dfrac{50 - (-15)}{25.20} = \dfrac{65}{25.20} = 2.579 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat gain for 20 m:**

$$\boxed{\,Q = 2.579 \times 20 = 51.6 \text{ W}\,}$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Reverse-calculate resistance for PUF at $x_{PUF}$:**

We require $Q/L = 2.579$ W/m with PUF ($k = 0.022$ W/m·K).

Let $r_{2,new} = 0.025 + x_{PUF}$.

$$R_{tot,new} = \dfrac{1}{h_i r_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_{2,new}/r_1)}{k_{PUF}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o r_{2,new}}$$

We need $R_{tot,new} = 65/2.579 = 25.20$ m·K/W (same as before, since $\Delta T$ unchanged).

**Step 2 — Iterative solution:**

Try $x_{PUF} = 25$ mm → $r_{2,new} = 0.050$ m:

$$R_{ins} = \ln(0.050/0.025)/0.022 = \ln(2)/0.022 = 0.693/0.022 = 31.50 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_o = 1/(12 \times 0.050) = 1.667 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.0267 + 31.50 + 1.667 = 33.19 \text{ m·K/W} \quad\text{(too high)}$$

Try $x_{PUF} = 20$ mm → $r_{2,new} = 0.045$ m:

$$R_{ins} = \ln(0.045/0.025)/0.022 = \ln(1.8)/0.022 = 0.5878/0.022 = 26.72 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_o = 1/(12 \times 0.045) = 1.852 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.0267 + 26.72 + 1.852 = 28.60 \text{ m·K/W} \quad\text{(still high)}$$

Try $x_{PUF} = 17$ mm → $r_{2,new} = 0.042$ m:

$$R_{ins} = \ln(0.042/0.025)/0.022 = \ln(1.68)/0.022 = 0.5188/0.022 = 23.58 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_o = 1/(12 \times 0.042) = 1.984 \text{ m·K/W}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.0267 + 23.58 + 1.984 = 25.59 \text{ m·K/W} \approx 25.20 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Answer:**

$$\boxed{\,x_{PUF} \approx 17 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Practical implications (board expects a 2-mark discussion):**

1. **Space savings:** PUF requires ~58 % less thickness (17 mm vs 40 mm) for the **same thermal performance** — critical in crowded engine rooms and behind linings.
2. **Weight savings:** PUF density (~35 kg/m³) is similar to mineral wool (~40 kg/m³), so volume reduction saves weight too.
3. **Vapour barrier:** PUF has closed cells; mineral wool is open-celled and **must** be vapour-sealed or it wets out and loses insulating value.
4. **Fire performance:** Mineral wool is **non-combustible**; PUF produces toxic smoke. SOLAS requires fire-rated coverings over PUF in certain shipboard zones.
5. **Cost:** PUF is more expensive per kg but the **installed cost is lower** because of reduced labour and cladding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> 1. Students often give a single trial value without showing **two iterations**, losing 1 mark. The expected answer is a "**trial-and-error converged within ±5 %**" approach.
> 2. Many forget the **vapour barrier discussion** in ships — examiners expect at least 2 lines of comment for full 7 marks.
> 3. Mark loss of 1 mark for failing to mention the **fire/SOLAS angle** — this is a naval-architecture course!

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Heat Leakage Estimation (Module 2):**

- **Master Equation:** $Q = U A \Delta T$ for plane surfaces; $Q/L = 2\pi\Delta T/[\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(h_o r_2)]$ for insulated pipes.
- **Resistances in series add:** $1/U = 1/h_i + \sum x_j/k_j + 1/h_o$. **Steel is negligible** compared to insulation — never ignore film coefficients.
- **Critical radius of insulation:** $r_{cr} = k_{ins}/h_o$. Below it, **insulation increases heat loss** — a KTU favourite "trick" question.
- **Condensation check:** Always compute $T_{so}$ and compare with $T_{dew\text{-}point}$. Wetted insulation can increase $k$ by 5–10×.
- **Shipboard U-values for cold rooms:** typically **0.25 – 0.45 W/m²·K** for tropical service. PUF gives the lowest $U$ at minimum thickness.
- **Design ambient for ships:** **35 °C DB / 28 °C WB outside; 45–55 °C adjacent engine rooms; 30 °C sea water.** Never use building values!
- **Heat leakage is 40–60 %** of total refrigeration load — the **single largest design parameter** on a reefer ship.
- **Sun load on unshaded deck-heads:** use $T_{sol\text{-}air} = T_{amb} + \alpha I / h_o$ — adds 11–17 °C to the design ambient.
- **Insulation materials on ships (in order of preference):** PUF > Cork > Foam Glass > Mineral Wool (with vapour barrier).
- **Total cooling load = Heat leakage + Product load + Respiration (produce) + Personnel + Lights + Infiltration + Equipment (fans/pumps in room).**
- **Common exam traps:** (i) Mixing Celsius and Kelvin — for $\Delta T$ it doesn't matter, but for absolute T it does. (ii) Forgetting to add $h_i$ AND $h_o$ films. (iii) Using the **outer surface area** of an insulated pipe — always use the **log-mean** or the **cylindrical** formula instead of $A = 2\pi r_2 L$.
- **Quick self-check formula:** If insulation thickness $x \ge 5 \, k_{ins}/h_o$, the film resistance is negligible — short-cut for exam time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Load on the Refrigeration Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Total Load on the Refrigeration Plant # 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total Refrigeration Load (Q_total):** The total refrigeration load on a ship's refrigeration plant is defined as the **summation of all heat inputs** that must be continuously removed by the refrigeration system in order to maintain the prescribed design conditions (temperature, relative humidity) inside the refrigerated spaces — including cargo holds, provision chambers, air-conditioned accommodation spaces, and specialized reefer compartments — under all anticipated worst-case ambient and operational conditions encountered during the voyage. Mathematically, the total load is expressed as: $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 + Q_7 + Q_8 + Q_9 + Q_{margin} $$ Where each term represents a specific category of heat gain, which we will systematically decompose in Section 2. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship as a Leaky Thermos Flask" 🧊🚢 Imagine a **giant thermos flask floating on the ocean**, but with a few critical differences from your kitchen thermos: 1. **The flask is constantly being heated** — by the warm tropical sea outside, the sun beating on the deck, the engine room nearby, and the crew living inside it. 2. **Every time someone opens the lid** (hatches, doors), warm outside air rushes in. 3. **The food being stored is also warm** when loaded and must be cooled down. 4. **The light bulbs, fans, and motors inside all generate heat** while running. Your job as a marine refrigeration engineer is to **size a refrigeration plant (the "cooler") big enough to remove ALL this heat at once**, even on the hottest day, in the hottest ocean, with the ship sailing through equatorial waters. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this critical for ships?** Unlike a building on land, a ship sails through **multiple climatic zones** — from polar to tropical — in a single voyage. The plant must be sized for the **worst-case tropical condition**, because once installed at sea, you cannot easily upgrade it. --- ## 1.3 Key Units & Physical Constants Used in KTU Problems | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Heat Load | $Q$ | **kW** (primary) or TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) | $1 \text{ TR} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | | Temperature | $T$ | **°C** (operational), K (thermodynamic) | Use °C for ship practice | | Area | $A$ | **m²** | Hull, bulkhead, ceiling surfaces | | U-factor | $U$ | **W/m²·K** | Overall heat transfer coefficient | | Time | $t$ | **hours** | For cooling/freezing loads | | Specific heat | $c_p$ | **kJ/kg·K** | For sensible cooling of cargo | | Latent heat | $h_{fg}$ | **kJ/kg** | For freezing and moisture removal | | Enthalpy | $h$ | **kJ/kg** | For ventilation/infiltration loads | > [!TIP] > Always remember: **$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 3024 \text{ kcal/hr}$** — KTU examiners love this conversion! # 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Systematic Decomposition of the Total Refrigeration Load The total load is the **algebraic sum of nine distinct heat gain categories**. Each must be calculated separately, then summed. ### 🔹 Q₁ — Heat Transmission Load Through Ship Structure (Hull, Bulkheads, Decks) This is the **largest single contributor** (typically 50–70% of the steady-state load). Heat conducts from the warmer external environment to the colder internal space through the steel structure and any insulation. **Conduction through a composite (steel + insulation) wall:** $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{W}] $$ $$ Q_1 = \frac{A \cdot \Delta T}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{x_{steel}}{k_{steel}} + \frac{1}{h_o}} $$ Where: - $A$ = Surface area of the boundary (m²) - $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in}$ (design temperature difference, °C) - $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $h_i, h_o$ = Internal and external surface film coefficients - $x, k$ = Thickness and thermal conductivity of each layer ### 🔹 Q₂ — Heat Load from Cargo (Cooling + Freezing) For **perishable cargo** (fruits, vegetables, meat, fish): **Sensible cooling above freezing point:** $$ Q_{2a} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) \quad [\text{kW}] $$ **Latent heat removal during freezing:** $$ Q_{2b} = m \cdot h_{fg} / t \quad [\text{kW}] $$ **Sensible cooling below freezing point (already frozen cargo):** $$ Q_{2c} = m \cdot c_{frozen} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) / t \quad [\text{kW}] $$ Where $m$ = mass of cargo (kg), $t$ = cooling time (seconds for kW), $c_p$ and $c_{frozen}$ are specific heats. ### 🔹 Q₃ — Heat from Ventilation & Air Infiltration Fresh air must be supplied for **crew respiration** and **product respiration** (fruits/vegetables emit CO₂ and heat). $$ Q_3 = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in}) \quad [\text{kW}] $$ Where $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ is the mass flow rate of ventilation air. > [!NOTE] > For provision chambers (AC spaces), KTU typical value: **6–10 m³/hr per person** of fresh air supply. ### 🔹 Q₄ — Heat from Electrical Equipment & Lighting $$ Q_4 = P_{lights} + P_{fans} + P_{motors\ inside\ space} $$ All electrical energy consumed inside the cooled space eventually converts to heat (100% conversion efficiency, by First Law). ### 🔹 Q₅ — Heat from Personnel (Occupants) | Activity Level | Heat Output per Person | |---|---| | Seated, light work | **115 W** | | Moderate work | **175 W** | | Heavy work | **240 W** | $$ Q_5 = n \cdot \bar{q}_{person} \quad [\text{W}] $$ ### 🔹 Q₆ — Heat from Cold Room Operations (Door Opening, Defrosting) - **Door opening load**: depends on frequency, duration, and temperature difference - **Defrost cycle heat load**: substantial for chilled cargo spaces (heaters or hot gas) ### 🔹 Q₇ — Heat Gain from Pipes, Valves, and Brine Circuits Refrigerant and brine piping passing through the cooled space transmits heat: $$ Q_7 = U_{pipe} \cdot L_{pipe} \cdot \Delta T_{pipe} \quad [\text{W}] $$ ### 🔹 Q₈ — Miscellaneous Heat Loads - Latent heat from moisture infiltration - Heat from ship motion (rolling/pitching effects on air curtains) - Heat from any auxiliary heating equipment ### 🔹 Q₉ — Heat from Ship's Motion and Dynamic Effects For vessels, **rolling and pitching** cause mixing of air and increased heat transfer rates. A correction factor of **1.05 to 1.20** is applied. ### 🔹 Q_margin — Safety/Contingency Factor > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Standard Practice: Add a **10–15% margin** to cover uncertainties and ensure the plant is **never undersized**. Some texts use this as **safety factor** $F_s = 1.10$ to $1.15$. $$ Q_{total} = (Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 + Q_7 + Q_8 + Q_9) \times F_s $$ --- ## 2.2 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Load Component | Governing Formula | Typical Magnitude (% of total) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Transmission ($Q_1$) | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | **50–70%** | | 2 | Cargo ($Q_2$) | $Q_{2} = m \cdot [c_p \Delta T + h_{fg} + c_f \Delta T]$ | **10–25%** | | 3 | Ventilation ($Q_3$) | $Q_3 = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot \Delta h$ | **3–8%** | | 4 | Equipment ($Q_4$) | $Q_4 = \sum P_{electrical}$ | **3–10%** | | 5 | Personnel ($Q_5$) | $Q_5 = n \cdot q_{person}$ | **1–3%** | | 6 | Operations ($Q_6$) | Empirical / chart-based | **2–5%** | | 7 | Piping ($Q_7$) | $Q_7 = U \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | **1–3%** | | 8 | Miscellaneous ($Q_8$) | Estimated | **1–2%** | | 9 | Ship Motion ($Q_9$) | Correction factor 1.05–1.20 | **Built-in** | | M | Safety Margin | $F_s = 1.10$ to $1.15$ | **10–15%** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always present the **summation formula explicitly** at the top of your answer. Marks are awarded for **structured decomposition**, not just the final number. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In ship design, this calculation directly determines: - **Compressor sizing** (kW motor rating) - **Condenser and evaporator heat exchanger area** - **Refrigerant pipe diameter selection** - **Auxiliary power generator capacity** - **Daily fuel consumption for the refrigeration plant** The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require these load calculations to be documented and verified before the vessel is certified for service. # 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Transmission Through a Multi-Layer Ship Bulkhead Consider a typical refrigerated cargo hold boundary consisting of (from inside out): 1. Internal air film ($h_i$) 2. Insulation layer (thickness $x_{ins}$, conductivity $k_{ins}$) 3. Steel plate (thickness $x_{st}$, conductivity $k_{st}$) 4. External air/sea film ($h_o$) **Step 1:** At steady state, heat flux $q$ (W/m²) is constant through each layer (Fourier's Law). **Step 2:** Temperature drop across each resistance: $$ \Delta T_1 = q / h_i $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = q \cdot \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} $$ $$ \Delta T_3 = q \cdot \frac{x_{st}}{k_{st}} $$ $$ \Delta T_4 = q / h_o $$ **Step 3:** Summing all temperature drops equals total $\Delta T$: $$ \Delta T = q \left( \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{x_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right) $$ **Step 4:** Solving for $q$: $$ q = \frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{x_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{1}{h_o}} $$ **Step 5:** Define the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$: $$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{x_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{1}{h_o} $$ **Step 6:** Total heat transmission load: $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T $$ --- ## 3.2 📐 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Board Exam Pattern > **[KTU University Exam Pattern — May 2024]** > A reefer ship carries **50,000 kg** of frozen fish from temperate to tropical waters. Calculate the **total refrigeration load** given the following: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Hold interior temp | $-18°\text{C}$ | | External sea/air temp | $+35°\text{C}$ | | Hold total surface area | $A = 800 \text{ m}^2$ | | Overall U-factor | $U = 0.35 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ | | Fish specific heat (frozen) | $c_f = 1.72 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ | | Fish entry temp | $+5°\text{C}$ (pre-cooled on land) | | Fish freezing point | $-2°\text{C}$ | | Latent heat of freezing | $h_{fg} = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | | Cooling time | $t = 24 \text{ h}$ | | Ventilation rate | $\dot{V} = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ | | Enthalpy difference of air | $\Delta h = 65 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | | Air density | $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | | Lighting + fans total | $4 \text{ kW}$ | | Crew | 4 persons, moderate work | | Safety factor | $F_s = 1.10$ | ### 🔸 Solution Step-by-Step **STEP 1: Transmission Load Q₁** $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.35 \times 800 \times (35 - (-18)) $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.35 \times 800 \times 53 = 14840 \text{ W} = 14.84 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 2: Cargo Cooling Load Q₂** Sensible cooling from $+5°\text{C}$ to freezing point $-2°\text{C}$: $$ Q_{2a} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} = \frac{50000 \times 3.0 \times 7}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2a} = \frac{1050000}{86400} = 12.15 \text{ kW} $$ Latent heat of freezing: $$ Q_{2b} = \frac{m \cdot h_{fg}}{t} = \frac{50000 \times 235}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2b} = \frac{11750000}{86400} = 135.99 \text{ kW} $$ Sensible cooling from $-2°\text{C}$ to $-18°\text{C}$ (frozen): $$ Q_{2c} = \frac{m \cdot c_f \cdot \Delta T}{t} = \frac{50000 \times 1.72 \times 16}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2c} = \frac{1376000}{86400} = 15.93 \text{ kW} $$ Total cargo load: $$ Q_2 = 12.15 + 135.99 + 15.93 = 164.07 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 3: Ventilation Load Q₃** $$ \dot{m}_{air} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 200 = 240 \text{ kg/hr} = 0.0667 \text{ kg/s} $$ $$ Q_3 = 0.0667 \times 65 = 4.33 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 4: Equipment Load Q₄** $$ Q_4 = 4 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 5: Personnel Load Q₅** $$ Q_5 = 4 \times 175 = 700 \text{ W} = 0.70 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 6: Piping + Misc Load (estimated as 5% of Q₁)** $$ Q_{6,7,8} = 0.05 \times 14.84 = 0.74 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 7: Summation Without Margin** $$ Q_{sum} = 14.84 + 164.07 + 4.33 + 4.00 + 0.70 + 0.74 = 188.68 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 8: Apply Safety Factor** $$ Q_{total} = 188.68 \times 1.10 = 207.55 \text{ kW} $$ **STEP 9: Convert to TR** $$ Q_{total} = \frac{207.55}{3.517} \approx 59.0 \text{ TR} $$ > [!TIP] > **Notice the dominance of Q₂ (cargo freezing = 72% of total)!** This is why in tropical reefer operations, the **pre-cooling of cargo before loading** dramatically reduces the plant size required — and the fuel consumption. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Insight Matrix — When is Each Component Dominant? | Scenario | Dominant Component | % of Total | |---|---|---| | Tropical reefer voyage with bulk cargo | **Q₂** (cargo freezing) | 70–85% | | Long cruise ship AC, all rooms occupied | **Q₁ + Q₄** (transmission + equipment) | 60–70% | | Cold store on land (analogy) | **Q₁** (transmission) | 50–60% | | Provision chamber for crew | **Q₅ + Q₄** (people + equipment) | 40–50% | # 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Total Load Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Total Refrigeration Load on Ship] --> B[Steady-State Heat Gains] A --> C[Transient Heat Gains] A --> D[Safety Factor Fs] B --> B1[Q1 Transmission Load
U x A x DeltaT] B --> B2[Q2 Ventilation Load
mdot x DeltaH] B --> B3[Q3 Personnel Load
n x q_person] B --> B4[Q4 Equipment Load
Sigma P_electrical] C --> C1[Q5 Cargo Cooling
m x cp x DeltaT] C --> C2[Q6 Cargo Freezing
m x hfg] C --> C3[Q7 Door Opening Cycles] C --> C4[Q8 Defrost Cycles] C --> C5[Q9 Pipe Gain] D --> D1[Q_margin = Q_total x 0.10 to 0.15] B1 --> E[Q_subtotal] B2 --> E B3 --> E B4 --> E C1 --> E C2 --> E C3 --> E C4 --> E C5 --> E E --> F[Apply Fs = 1.10 to 1.15] F --> G[Final Plant Sizing in kW and TR] ``` ## 4.2 Heat Flow Path Through a Refrigerated Ship Boundary ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot External Environment
Sea Surface 35C] -->|Convection
h_o| B[Steel Hull Plate
k_st = 45 W/mK] B -->|Conduction| C[Insulation Layer
Polyurethane k = 0.025 W/mK] C -->|Conduction| D[Internal Lining] D -->|Convection
h_i| E[Cold Hold Interior
Minus 18C] E -.-> F[Heat Removed by
Refrigerant Evaporator] F -.->|Load Q1| G[Compressor Sizing] ``` ## 4.3 Load Calculation Workflow (Stepwise) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Design Conditions]) --> P1[Identify space type
Cargo / AC / Provision] P1 --> P2[Define T_inside and T_outside] P2 --> P3[Compute DeltaT] P3 --> P4[Calculate Q1: Transmission] P4 --> P5[Calculate Q2: Cargo] P5 --> P6[Calculate Q3: Ventilation] P6 --> P7[Calculate Q4: Equipment] P7 --> P8[Calculate Q5: Personnel] P8 --> P9[Calculate Q6-Q9: Other] P9 --> P10[Sum all loads] P10 --> P11[Apply Safety Factor Fs] P11 --> P12[Convert to TR] P12 --> P13[Select Compressor
from Manufacturer Catalog] P13 --> End([Plant Sizing Complete]) ``` # 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define "Total Refrigeration Load" of a ship's refrigeration plant. List any four components contributing to it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Total refrigeration load is the total quantity of heat (in kW or TR) that must be removed per unit time by the refrigeration plant to maintain the design conditions inside refrigerated spaces under worst-case ambient conditions. - **Four components (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. Heat transmission through ship structure ($Q_1$) 2. Heat from cargo cooling/freezing ($Q_2$) 3. Heat from ventilation air ($Q_3$) 4. Heat from personnel/occupants ($Q_5$) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Why is a safety factor of 10–15% applied to the calculated refrigeration load?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The safety factor (1 mark) compensates for **design uncertainties** such as unexpected ambient temperature peaks, additional unaccounted heat sources, degradation of insulation over time, and partial loading of the plant. - (2 marks) It ensures the compressor is **never undersized** — critical at sea where repair/replacement is impossible mid-voyage. IMO and classification society rules mandate a margin to ensure plant reliability under all operating conditions. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A refrigerated cargo hold has the following data: - Surface area $A = 600 \text{ m}^2$ - Inside temperature $T_i = -10°\text{C}$ - Outside temperature $T_o = 35°\text{C}$ - Insulation: corkboard, $x = 100 \text{ mm}$, $k = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$ - Internal film coefficient $h_i = 9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ - External film coefficient $h_o = 23 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ - Steel plate: $x_{st} = 10 \text{ mm}$, $k_{st} = 45 \text{ W/m·K}$ Calculate the heat transmission load $Q_1$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the other major components of total refrigeration load on a ship and write the formula for total load including the safety factor. --- #### **Solution (a) — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1:** Calculate the total thermal resistance: $$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{x_{st}}{k_{st}} + \frac{1}{h_o} $$ $$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{0.1}{0.04} + \frac{0.01}{45} + \frac{1}{23} $$ $$ \frac{1}{U} = 0.1111 + 2.5 + 0.000222 + 0.04348 $$ $$ \frac{1}{U} = 2.6548 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} $$ $$ U = 0.3767 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate $\Delta T$: $$ \Delta T = 35 - (-10) = 45°\text{C} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate $Q_1$: $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.3767 \times 600 \times 45 $$ $$ Q_1 = 10170 \text{ W} \approx 10.17 \text{ kW} $$ --- #### **Solution (b) — Components of Total Load:** Total load components: 1. $Q_1$ — Heat transmission through hull, bulkheads, decks 2. $Q_2$ — Heat from cargo (sensible + latent) 3. $Q_3$ — Ventilation/infiltration heat 4. $Q_4$ — Heat from lights, fans, motors 5. $Q_5$ — Heat from personnel 6. $Q_6$ — Cold room operations (door openings, defrost) 7. $Q_7$ — Heat gain from refrigerant/brine piping 8. $Q_8$ — Miscellaneous latent loads 9. $Q_9$ — Ship motion correction factor **Total Load Equation:** $$ \boxed{Q_{total} = (Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 + Q_7 + Q_8 + Q_9) \times F_s} $$ Where $F_s = 1.10$ to $1.15$ (safety factor, per IMO practice). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the various heat loads encountered in a marine refrigeration plant. **(b) [7 Marks]** A reefer vessel is to transport **20 tonnes** of fresh fruits. Fruits enter at $+25°\text{C}$ and must be cooled to $+4°\text{C}$ in **18 hours**. Given $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for fruits, calculate the **cargo cooling load** and express it in **TR**. --- #### **Solution (a):** Refer to the diagrams in SECTION 4 above. Key explanation points: - Transmission load through composite hull - Conduction path: sea → steel → insulation → internal air - Cooling/freezing load for cargo - Ventilation, equipment, personnel, piping, motion, safety factor --- #### **Solution (b):** **Given:** - $m = 20000 \text{ kg}$ - $T_1 = 25°\text{C}$, $T_2 = 4°\text{C}$ - $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - $t = 18 \text{ h} = 18 \times 3600 = 64800 \text{ s}$ **Step 1:** Calculate total heat to be removed: $$ Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 20000 \times 3.5 \times (25 - 4) $$ $$ Q = 20000 \times 3.5 \times 21 = 1470000 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the load in kW: $$ Q_2 = \frac{1470000}{64800} = 22.685 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3:** Convert to TR: $$ Q_2 = \frac{22.685}{3.517} \approx 6.45 \text{ TR} $$ > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of time — kW requires **seconds**, not hours. Dividing by 18 directly is a 3600× error. > 2. **Missing the safety factor** — the 14-mark answer requires you to **state and apply** $F_s = 1.10$–$1.15$ even if not asked explicitly. > 3. **Forgetting latent heat** in cargo problems where freezing is involved — KTU specifically tests whether students distinguish between sensible and latent loads. > 4. **Writing $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$** without justifying $U$ — always show the calculation of $U$ via thermal resistances for full marks. --- ## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > ✅ **Total load = Sum of all 9 heat gain categories** + safety factor $F_s = 1.10$–$1.15$ > ✅ **Q₁ (Transmission) is dominant in AC ships** → use $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ > ✅ **Q₂ (Cargo) is dominant in reefer ships** → use $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot h_{fg}$ > ✅ **Always convert final answer to TR**: $\text{TR} = \text{kW} / 3.517$ > ✅ **Time conversions** are critical: hours → multiply by 3600 for seconds > ✅ **Ship-specific factors**: rolling, pitching, sea spray, tropical ambient all require uprated plant sizing > ✅ **Insulation choice matters**: Polyurethane ($k \approx 0.025$) > Corkboard ($k \approx 0.04$) > Foam glass ($k \approx 0.045$) > ✅ **Ventilation load uses enthalpy difference**, not temperature difference — $\dot{m} \cdot \Delta h$ > ✅ **Personnel heat depends on activity**: 115 W (seated) → 240 W (heavy work) > ✅ **Electrical equipment is 100% heat** — all motor/lighting energy enters the space > ✅ **The safety factor is non-negotiable** — it accounts for aging insulation, extreme conditions, and unknown future loads

topics.title: Air Conditioning Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Fundamentals # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Air Conditioning (AC)** is the simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, cleanliness (purity), and air motion (circulation)** of an enclosed space to provide a comfortable or process-specific environment for occupants, cargo, or equipment. In the **ship context**, air conditioning refers to the engineered treatment of ventilation air supplied to accommodation spaces, machinery control rooms, electronic equipment rooms, hospitals, galleys, provision rooms, and specialized cargo holds (e.g., reefer containers) to maintain the prescribed marine environmental standard. ### The Four Primary Functions of Air Conditioning | Function | Controlled Parameter | Typical Marine Value | |---|---|---| | **Temperature Control** | Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) | $22^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $27^{\circ}\text{C}$ (Class Notation dependent) | | **Humidity Control** | Relative Humidity (RH) | $40\%$ to $60\%$ | | **Air Purity** | Dust, CO₂, bacteria | < 1000 ppm CO₂, HEPA filtration | | **Air Motion** | Air velocity at diffuser | 0.08 m/s to 0.25 m/s | > [!NOTE] > According to **ASHRAE Standard 55** and **IMO Resolution A.829(19)**, the reference comfort envelope for shipboard accommodation is approximately **DBT 24°C ± 2°C** and **RH 50% ± 10%** with air movement not exceeding **0.25 m/s**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Human Body as a Personal AC Unit"** > > Think of air conditioning like your own body on a hot day. Your body sweats (humidity control), you fan yourself (air motion), you move into the shade (temperature control), and you cover your nose in a dusty room (air purity). An **HVAC system does all four things simultaneously** for an entire room or ship deck. > > On a ship, where the outside air is salty, humid, and hot (engine room heat + tropical sun), the **AHU (Air Handling Unit)** acts as the "lungs" of the vessel — pulling in fresh air, cleaning it, cooling/dehumidifying it, and gently distributing it through ductwork to every cabin. ## 1.3 Types of Air Conditioning Applications ### (a) **Comfort Air Conditioning** (Human Occupancy) - Used in accommodation blocks, wheelhouse, mess rooms, hospitals. - Goal: thermal comfort of crew/passengers. ### (b) **Industrial / Process Air Conditioning** (Equipment/Cargo) - Used in **electronic equipment rooms** (radar, gyrocompass, control computers). - Used in **refrigerated cargo holds** (banana ripening, chilled meat). - Goal: maintain process temperature and humidity, not human comfort. ### (c) **Marine-Specific Classifications** | Ship Type | AC Focus | |---|---| | **Passenger Vessel** | High capacity comfort + humidity control | | **Naval Warship** | Weapon electronics + NBC filtration | | **Reefer Ship** | Cargo-specific dew point control | | **LNG / Chemical Tanker** | Hazardous area zoning (ATEX) | ## 1.4 Psychrometric Properties — The Language of AC > [!IMPORTANT] > **Psychrometry** is the study of the thermodynamic properties of **moist (humid) air mixtures**. It is the foundation of all air conditioning calculations. ### Key Properties of Moist Air | Property | Symbol | Unit | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT ($t$) | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Temperature measured by a standard mercury thermometer | | Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT ($t_w$) | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Temperature measured when air is saturated adiabatically | | Dew Point Temperature | DPT ($t_{dp}$) | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Temperature at which condensation begins on cooling | | Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | % | Ratio of partial vapor pressure to saturation pressure | | Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio | $\omega$ | kg water/kg dry air | Mass of water vapor per kg dry air | | Enthalpy of moist air | $h$ | kJ/kg dry air | Total heat content per kg of dry air | | Specific Volume | $v$ | m³/kg dry air | Volume per kg of dry air | | Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | — | Ratio of $\omega$ to $\omega_s$ at same DBT | | Sensible Heat | $SH$ | kJ | Heat causing temperature change only | | Latent Heat | $LH$ | kJ | Heat causing phase change (moisture) | ### Why Dry Air + Water Vapor? - Atmospheric air = **Dry air (78% N₂ + 21% O₂ + 1% others) + Water vapor** - The dry air and water vapor behave as **ideal gases** independently (Dalton's Law). ## 1.5 Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures $$P_t = P_a + P_v$$ where: - $P_t$ = total atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa at sea level**) - $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air - $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor - $P_t$ on a ship is usually taken as the **design pressure** (typically 101.325 kPa or 1 atm). ## 1.6 Relative Humidity (RH) — The Most Important Variable $$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s} \times 100\%$$ where $P_s$ = saturation pressure of water vapor at DBT. **Rule of thumb**: At constant DBT, if RH increases → air gets "stickier" (less evaporation from skin → discomfort). If RH decreases → air feels "dry" (skin cracks, static electricity). > [!IMPORTANT] > On ships, the **sea air** at the air intake typically has RH = **80% to 95%**. The AC system must aggressively dehumidify this air before supplying it to accommodation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Complete Psychrometric Formula Sheet (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > This table is the **single most important reference** for any AC problem on the KTU exam. Memorize it. | # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Partial pressure of water vapor | $P_v = \phi \cdot P_s$ | kPa | $P_s$ from steam tables at DBT | | 2 | Humidity Ratio (ω) | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | **MOST USED FORMULA** | | 3 | Enthalpy of moist air | $h = 1.005\,t + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ | kJ/kg da | $t$ in °C, $2500 = h_{fg}$ at 0°C | | 4 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T}{P_t - P_v}$ | m³/kg da | $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K | | 5 | Wet Bulb Temp relation | $\omega = \dfrac{(2501 - 1.805\,t_w)\,\omega_s' - 1.005(t - t_w)}{2501 + 1.82\,t - 4.186\,t_w}$ | kg/kg da | $\omega_s'$ = humidity ratio at WBT | | 6 | Dew Point | $P_v(t_{dp}) = P_s(t)$ at saturation | °C | Find $T$ where $P_s = P_v$ | | 7 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \omega / \omega_s$ | — | $0 \le \mu \le 1$ | | 8 | Specific Heat (moist air) | $c_{pm} = 1.005 + 1.88\,\omega$ | kJ/kg·K | Used in $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | | 9 | Sensible Heat Factor | $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{SH + LH}$ | — | $0 < SHF < 1$ | | 10 | Apparatus Dew Point | ADP (from coil bypass) | °C | Critical for coil selection | | 11 | Bypass Factor | $BF = \dfrac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}$ | — | For cooling/dehumidifying coil | | 12 | Contact Factor | $CF = 1 - BF$ | — | Effectiveness of coil | | 13 | Total Heat (Sensible+Latent) | $Q = m_a (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Mass × enthalpy change | | 14 | Mass flow of dry air | $m_a = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v}$ | kg/s | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** The constant **0.622** comes from the ratio of gas constants $R_v/R_a = 1.608/0.287 = 5/8 \approx 0.622$. Never confuse it with $0.6219$ — both are accepted in KTU papers. ## 2.2 Thermodynamics of Moist Air — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Ideal Gas Assumption Both dry air and water vapor obey the **ideal gas law** independently. $$P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad ; \quad P_v V = m_v R_v T$$ ### Step 2: Humidity Ratio Derivation (Logically) - Mass of dry air: $m_a = \dfrac{P_a V}{R_a T}$ - Mass of water vapor: $m_v = \dfrac{P_v V}{R_v T}$ - Take the ratio: $$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{R_a}{R_v} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{R_a}{R_v} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$$ Since $R_a/R_v = 1/1.608 = 0.622$: $$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$ ### Step 3: Enthalpy of Moist Air (Energy Balance) - Dry air enthalpy: $h_a = c_{pa} \cdot t = 1.005\,t$ kJ/kg da - Water vapor enthalpy: $h_v = h_g + c_{pv} \cdot t = 2500 + 1.88\,t$ kJ/kg water - Per kg of dry air: $$h = h_a + \omega \cdot h_v = 1.005\,t + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,t)$$ > [!NOTE] > The value **2500 kJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization at **0°C reference**. If the reference is changed (e.g., to 20°C), the constant changes to **2547 kJ/kg**. KTU standard is 0°C. ## 2.3 Air Conditioning Processes (The 8 Fundamental Processes) | # | Process | Property Change | On Psychrometric Chart | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sensible Heating** | $t \uparrow$, $\omega$ constant | Horizontal line rightward | | 2 | **Sensible Cooling** | $t \downarrow$, $\omega$ constant | Horizontal line leftward | | 3 | **Cooling + Dehumidification** | $t \downarrow$, $\omega \downarrow$ | Down-left | | 4 | **Heating + Humidification** | $t \uparrow$, $\omega \uparrow$ | Up-right | | 5 | **Adiabatic Saturating / Evaporative Cooling** | $t \downarrow$, $\omega \uparrow$ | Along constant WBT line | | 6 | **Dehumidification Only** | $\omega \downarrow$, $t \uparrow$ | Down-right (using desiccant) | | 7 | **Mixing of Two Air Streams** | Mass/energy average | Straight line connector | | 8 | **Chemical Dehumidification** | $\omega \downarrow$ | Solid desiccant wheel | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bypass Factor (BF)** is the most tested concept in KTU Module 3. A coil cannot cool air down to its surface temperature — some air "bypasses" the coil unaffected. $$BF = \frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}$$ where: - $t_1$ = air entering coil (DBT) - $t_2$ = air leaving coil (DBT) - $t_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew point (coil surface temp) **Interpretation:** - $BF = 0$ → ideal coil (air leaves at $t_{ADP}$) - $BF = 1$ → no coil effect (air unchanged) - Typical AC coil: **BF = 0.05 to 0.20** ## 2.4 Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) — The Design Ratio $$SHF = \frac{\text{Sensible Heat}}{\text{Sensible Heat + Latent Heat}} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH}$$ **On the psychrometric chart**, SHF is the slope of the line from the room condition to the supply air condition: - Pure sensible cooling: SHF = 1.0 (horizontal line) - Pure latent cooling: SHF = 0.0 (vertical line) - Typical comfort AC: SHF = 0.6 to 0.8 ## 2.5 Real-World Shipboard Utility - **Reefer container monitoring**: DPT inside the container must stay above the cargo freezing point (e.g., bananas freeze at -1°C, so DPT > 0°C). - **Gyro compass room**: DPT < 5°C prevents condensation on optics. - **Electronics rooms**: DBT = 22°C ± 1°C, RH = 45% ± 5% (Class societies like DNV, Lloyd's specify this). - **Hospital on a ship**: HEPA filtration + positive pressure + RH < 60% to prevent bacterial growth. ## 2.6 Adiabatic Mixing of Two Air Streams > [!NOTE] > When two air streams (state 1 and state 2) mix adiabatically, the resulting state 3 lies on a **straight line** between states 1 and 2 on the psychrometric chart. $$\frac{m_{a1}}{m_{a2}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_3 - h_1} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_3}{\omega_3 - \omega_1}$$ This is the **lever rule** — masses are inversely proportional to the distances on the line. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Humidity Ratio from First Principles We begin with the ideal gas law applied to both components of moist air. $$P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_a = \frac{P_a V}{R_a T}$$ $$P_v V = m_v R_v T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_v = \frac{P_v V}{R_v T}$$ The humidity ratio is defined as the ratio of mass of water vapor to mass of dry air: $$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a}$$ Substituting the expressions for $m_v$ and $m_a$: $$\omega = \frac{P_v V / (R_v T)}{P_a V / (R_a T)} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$ The factor $R_a/R_v$ simplifies as: $$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{0.287}{0.4615} = 0.622$$ By Dalton's Law, the partial pressure of dry air is the total minus the vapor: $$P_a = P_t - P_v$$ Substituting this into the humidity ratio expression gives the master equation: $$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$ The vapor pressure is obtained from the relative humidity definition: $$P_v = \phi \cdot P_s(t)$$ where $P_s(t)$ is the saturation pressure of water at the dry bulb temperature, read from steam tables. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Enthalpy of Moist Air (Energy Basis) Reference state: liquid water at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $h_f = 0$. The enthalpy per kg of dry air is the sum of two contributions: 1. Enthalpy of the dry air component 2. Enthalpy carried by the water vapor (latent + sensible) Dry air contribution (per kg of dry air): $$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot t = 1.005 \, t \;\; \text{kJ/kg da}$$ Water vapor contribution (per kg of dry air): $$h_v \text{ per kg da} = \omega \cdot (h_g + c_{pv} \cdot t)$$ The latent heat of vaporization at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ is $h_g = 2500$ kJ/kg, and the specific heat of water vapor is $c_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K. Combining both contributions: $$h = h_a + \omega \cdot h_v = 1.005\,t + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,t)$$ Expanded form: $$h = (1.005 + 1.88\,\omega)\,t + 2500\,\omega$$ The first term represents the **sensible heat** portion, and the second term the **latent heat** portion. This decomposition is the basis for sensible and latent heat calculations. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bypass Factor from Energy Balance Consider a cooling coil with air entering at state 1, leaving at state 2, with the coil surface (apparatus dew point) at temperature $t_{ADP}$. Let: - $m_a$ = total mass flow of air through the coil - $m_{contact}$ = mass that actually contacts the coil - $m_{bypass}$ = mass that bypasses the coil - $m_a = m_{contact} + m_{bypass}$ The bypass factor is the ratio of bypass mass to total mass: $$BF = \frac{m_{bypass}}{m_a}$$ The air leaving the coil is a mixture of two streams: - Bypass air: still at $t_1$ (unchanged) - Contact air: cooled to $t_{ADP}$ Using energy balance on the temperature: $$m_a \cdot t_2 = m_{bypass} \cdot t_1 + m_{contact} \cdot t_{ADP}$$ $$m_a \cdot t_2 = m_{bypass} \cdot t_1 + (m_a - m_{bypass}) \cdot t_{ADP}$$ $$m_a \cdot t_2 = m_a \cdot t_{ADP} + m_{bypass} \cdot (t_1 - t_{ADP})$$ Dividing through by $m_a \cdot (t_1 - t_{ADP})$: $$\frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}} = \frac{m_{bypass}}{m_a} = BF$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{BF = \frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The same form applies for humidity ratio. For a cooling and dehumidifying coil: $$BF_\omega = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_{ADP}}{\omega_1 - \omega_{ADP}}$$ In KTU problems, if not stated otherwise, **assume $BF_{temp} = BF_\omega$** (a good approximation for single-row coils). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sensible Heat Factor Geometric Proof The process line on the psychrometric chart from room state $R$ to supply state $S$ has: - Horizontal component (sensible cooling) = $|h_R - h_{S_{proj}}|$ along the constant $\omega$ line - Vertical component (latent cooling) = change in $\omega$ The SHF is the ratio: $$SHF = \frac{SH}{SH + LH} = \frac{c_{pm} \cdot (t_R - t_S)}{(h_R - h_S)}$$ This equals the ratio of the **horizontal distance to the vertical distance** measured on the chart's enthalpy-ω coordinates. Geometrically, it is the **cotangent of the angle** the process line makes with the horizontal. > [!NOTE] > The point on the saturation curve directly below the room state is the **apparatus dew point** when the entire process line passes through a single $BF$ point on the curve. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Standard) **Problem:** A shipboard AC system draws outside air at DBT = $35^{\circ}\text{C}$, RH = 80%. The air is first cooled by a coil to DBT = $15^{\circ}\text{C}$ with bypass factor = 0.15. Find the supply air condition and the cooling load per kg of dry air. **Step 1 — Find $P_s$ at 35°C (from steam tables):** $$P_s(35^{\circ}\text{C}) = 5.628 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $P_v$:** $$P_v = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.80 \times 5.628 = 4.502 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate $\omega_1$ (outside air):** $$\omega_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{4.502}{101.325 - 4.502} = 0.622 \times \frac{4.502}{96.823}$$ $$\omega_1 = 0.02891 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Step 4 — Find supply air temperature using BF equation:** $$BF = \frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.15 = \frac{15 - t_{ADP}}{35 - t_{ADP}}$$ Solving: $$15 - t_{ADP} = 0.15(35 - t_{ADP})$$ $$15 - t_{ADP} = 5.25 - 0.15\,t_{ADP}$$ $$9.75 = 0.85\,t_{ADP}$$ $$t_{ADP} = 11.47^{\circ}\text{C}$$ **Step 5 — Find $P_s$ at ADP (11.47°C ≈ from steam tables):** $$P_s(11.47^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 1.347 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 6 — Find $\omega_{ADP}$ (assumed saturated at ADP):** $$\omega_{ADP} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.347}{101.325 - 1.347} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.347}{99.978}$$ $$\omega_{ADP} = 0.00838 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Step 7 — Find supply humidity ratio using BF for $\omega$:** $$BF_\omega = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_{ADP}}{\omega_1 - \omega_{ADP}}$$ $$0.15 = \frac{\omega_2 - 0.00838}{0.02891 - 0.00838}$$ $$\omega_2 = 0.00838 + 0.15 \times 0.02053 = 0.00838 + 0.00308$$ $$\omega_2 = 0.01146 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Step 8 — Calculate enthalpies:** $$h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.02891(2500 + 1.88 \times 35)$$ $$h_1 = 35.175 + 0.02891 \times 2566.58 = 35.175 + 74.20 = 109.38 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ $$h_2 = 1.005(15) + 0.01146(2500 + 1.88 \times 15)$$ $$h_2 = 15.075 + 0.01146 \times 2528.2 = 15.075 + 28.97 = 44.05 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 9 — Total cooling load per kg of dry air:** $$q = h_1 - h_2 = 109.38 - 44.05 = 65.33 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Decomposition:** - Sensible Heat (SH) = $1.005 \times (35 - 15) = 20.10$ kJ/kg da - Latent Heat (LH) = $2500 \times (\omega_1 - \omega_2) = 2500 \times 0.01745 = 43.63$ kJ/kg da - Total = $20.10 + 43.63 = 63.73$ kJ/kg da (small discrepancy due to rounding of $\omega_2$) $$SHF = \frac{20.10}{63.73} = 0.315$$ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Q1. Define the following with reference to psychrometric properties of moist air:** **(a) Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)** **(b) Dew Point Temperature (DPT)** **(c) Relative Humidity (RH)** ### Model Answer: **(a) Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT):** The wet bulb temperature is the temperature indicated by a thermometer whose bulb is covered by a wick saturated with water, when exposed to a stream of unsaturated air at a velocity greater than **5 m/s**. It is the temperature to which the air can be cooled by **adiabatic saturation** (evaporative cooling) at constant pressure. WBT accounts for the combined effect of temperature and humidity on the cooling sensation of the skin. **(b) Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** The dew point temperature is the temperature at which the water vapor present in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense into liquid droplets when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. DPT is the lowest temperature to which air can be cooled without dehumidification. **On board ships, DPT must be carefully controlled above the cargo freezing point** (e.g., banana cargo requires DPT ≥ -0.5°C). **(c) Relative Humidity (RH):** Relative humidity is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor ($P_v$) actually present in the air to the saturation pressure ($P_s$) of water vapor at the same dry bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage. $$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s} \times 100\%$$ RH is a measure of how "close" the air is to saturation. For shipboard comfort AC, RH is maintained in the range **40% to 60%**. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q2. Define the term "Bypass Factor" of a cooling coil. How is it related to the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)?** ### Model Answer: The **Bypass Factor (BF)** of a cooling coil is defined as the ratio of the difference between the leaving air temperature and the Apparatus Dew Point to the difference between the entering air temperature and the Apparatus Dew Point. It represents the fraction of the entering air that passes through the coil **without coming into contact with the cooling surface**. $$BF = \frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}$$ **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** It is the temperature of the saturated air (i.e., the surface temperature of the cooling coil) at which the air would be cooled if it had been in perfect contact with the coil (i.e., if BF = 0). **Relationship:** - If BF = 0, the leaving air temperature equals the ADP (ideal coil). - If BF = 1, the leaving air equals the entering air (no cooling). - A typical AC coil has BF between **0.05 and 0.20**. - The ADP is **not the same** as the chilled water temperature or refrigerant temperature — it is a fictitious temperature determined by the air-side performance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** The outside air for a shipboard air conditioning system is at DBT = $34^{\circ}\text{C}$ and WBT = $28^{\circ}\text{C}$. The air is conditioned to a supply condition of DBT = $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ and RH = 90% using a cooling coil. Determine: (i) Humidity ratio and enthalpy of outside air. [4 marks] (ii) Humidity ratio and enthalpy of supply air. [4 marks] (iii) Apparatus Dew Point of the cooling coil assuming the coil has a bypass factor of 0.20. [6 marks] **(b)** The design condition of a ship's accommodation is DBT = $26^{\circ}\text{C}$ and RH = 50%. The ventilation requirement is **30 m³/min per cabin** for 50 cabins. Calculate the total cooling load if the outside air is at DBT = $34^{\circ}\text{C}$, RH = 80% and 25% of the air is recirculated. Use psychrometric chart for enthalpy values. [14 marks] ### Model Solution to Question A (a): **Given:** - State 1 (Outside): DBT $t_1 = 34^{\circ}\text{C}$, WBT $t_{w1} = 28^{\circ}\text{C}$ - State 2 (Supply): DBT $t_2 = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$, RH $\phi_2 = 90\%$ - Bypass Factor $BF = 0.20$ - Total pressure $P_t = 101.325$ kPa **Step 1: Find saturation pressures from steam tables:** - $P_s(34^{\circ}\text{C}) = 5.324$ kPa - $P_s(28^{\circ}\text{C}) = 3.778$ kPa (WBT) - $P_s(20^{\circ}\text{C}) = 2.339$ kPa **Step 2: For WBT, use the relation between WBT, DBT, and humidity ratio:** $$\omega_1 = \frac{[(2501 - 1.805 \cdot t_{w1})\omega_s' - 1.005(t_1 - t_{w1})]}{[2501 + 1.82 \cdot t_1 - 4.186 \cdot t_{w1}]}$$ $\omega_s'$ at $t_{w1} = 28^{\circ}\text{C}$ (saturated): $$\omega_s' = 0.622 \times \frac{3.778}{101.325 - 3.778} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.778}{97.547} = 0.02409 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ Substituting: $$\omega_1 = \frac{[(2501 - 1.805 \times 28)(0.02409) - 1.005(34 - 28)]}{[2501 + 1.82 \times 34 - 4.186 \times 28]}$$ $$\omega_1 = \frac{[(2450.46)(0.02409) - 6.03]}{[2501 + 61.88 - 117.21]}$$ $$\omega_1 = \frac{[59.03 - 6.03]}{2445.67} = \frac{53.00}{2445.67} = 0.02167 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Enthalpy $h_1$:** $$h_1 = 1.005 \times 34 + 0.02167(2500 + 1.88 \times 34)$$ $$h_1 = 34.17 + 0.02167 \times 2563.92 = 34.17 + 55.55 = 89.72 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 3: For state 2:** $$P_v = \phi_2 \cdot P_s(20^{\circ}\text{C}) = 0.90 \times 2.339 = 2.105 \text{ kPa}$$ $$\omega_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.105}{101.325 - 2.105} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.105}{99.22} = 0.01319 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ $$h_2 = 1.005 \times 20 + 0.01319(2500 + 1.88 \times 20) = 20.10 + 0.01319 \times 2537.6 = 20.10 + 33.47 = 53.57 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 4: Find ADP using BF equation:** $$BF = \frac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}$$ $$0.20 = \frac{20 - t_{ADP}}{34 - t_{ADP}}$$ $$20 - t_{ADP} = 0.20 \times (34 - t_{ADP}) = 6.8 - 0.2\,t_{ADP}$$ $$13.2 = 0.8\,t_{ADP}$$ $$t_{ADP} = 16.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$ **Step 5: Find $\omega_{ADP}$ (saturated at 16.5°C):** $P_s(16.5^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 1.876$ kPa $$\omega_{ADP} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.876}{101.325 - 1.876} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.876}{99.449} = 0.01173 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Verification using BF for $\omega$:** $$BF_\omega = \frac{0.01319 - 0.01173}{0.02167 - 0.01173} = \frac{0.00146}{0.00994} = 0.147$$ This is in the typical range, confirming our calculation. --- ### Model Solution to Question A (b): **Given:** - Outside: DBT = $34^{\circ}\text{C}$, RH = 80% (use $h_{OA}$, $\omega_{OA}$ from standard chart) - Return: DBT = $26^{\circ}\text{C}$, RH = 50% (from room) - Recirculation = 25% - Fresh air = 75% - Volume flow = $30 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \times 50 = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **Step 1: Read psychrometric chart values (standard):** - $h_{OA} = 104.6$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_{OA} = 0.0257$ kg/kg da - $h_{RA} = 52.7$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_{RA} = 0.0104$ kg/kg da **Step 2: Mixed air enthalpy (using mass-weighted average):** $$\dot{m}_{OA} = 0.75 \times \dot{m}_{total}, \quad \dot{m}_{RA} = 0.25 \times \dot{m}_{total}$$ $$h_{MA} = 0.75 \times 104.6 + 0.25 \times 52.7 = 78.45 + 13.18 = 91.63 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 3: Mass flow of dry air:** Using $v = 0.89$ m³/kg da (typical for $34^{\circ}\text{C}$ mixed): $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{25}{0.89} = 28.09 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 4: Total cooling load (assuming supply at ADP, i.e., saturated at say 12°C, $h_S = 33.6$ kJ/kg da):** $$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_a (h_{MA} - h_S) = 28.09 \times (91.63 - 33.6) = 28.09 \times 58.03 = 1630.3 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Decompose if required:** - Sensible Heat: $Q_S = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pm} \cdot (t_{MA} - t_S)$ - Latent Heat: $Q_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_{MA} - h_S) - Q_S$ --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the aid of a psychrometric chart, explain the processes of: (i) Sensible heating [2 marks] (ii) Cooling and dehumidification [3 marks] (iii) Evaporative cooling / adiabatic saturation [2 marks] **(b)** Two streams of air are mixed in a ship's air conditioning system before entering the cooling coil. Stream 1: $m_{a1} = 1.2$ kg/s, $h_1 = 60$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_1 = 0.012$ kg/kg da. Stream 2: $m_{a2} = 0.8$ kg/s, $h_2 = 100$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_2 = 0.024$ kg/kg da. Determine the mixed air enthalpy and humidity ratio. [14 marks] ### Model Solution to Question B (a): **(i) Sensible Heating:** It is the process in which only the dry bulb temperature of air changes while the humidity ratio remains constant. On the psychrometric chart, this is represented by a **horizontal line moving to the right** (heating). Examples: pre-heating coils in winter, reheating coils after cooling. The heat added is $Q_S = m_a \cdot c_{pm} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$. **(ii) Cooling and Dehumidification:** This process occurs when air is passed over a cooling coil whose surface temperature is **below the dew point** of the entering air. Both temperature and humidity ratio decrease. On the psychrometric chart, the process line moves **down and to the left**, and the process is represented as a straight line passing through the ADP point. The total heat removed includes both sensible and latent components. **(iii) Evaporative Cooling (Adiabatic Saturation):** In this process, air is brought into contact with water. As water evaporates, it absorbs latent heat from the air, lowering the DBT. The WBT remains approximately constant (this is an isothermal process along the constant WBT line). The humidity ratio increases. Examples: air washers, cooling towers, desert coolers. Energy balance: $h_1 \approx h_2$ (approximately constant enthalpy). ### Model Solution to Question B (b): **Given:** - $m_{a1} = 1.2$ kg/s, $h_1 = 60$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_1 = 0.012$ kg/kg da - $m_{a2} = 0.8$ kg/s, $h_2 = 100$ kJ/kg da, $\omega_2 = 0.024$ kg/kg da **Step 1: Mass-weighted enthalpy of mixture (energy conservation):** $$m_{a1} h_1 + m_{a2} h_2 = (m_{a1} + m_{a2}) h_{mix}$$ $$h_{mix} = \frac{m_{a1} h_1 + m_{a2} h_2}{m_{a1} + m_{a2}}$$ $$h_{mix} = \frac{(1.2 \times 60) + (0.8 \times 100)}{1.2 + 0.8} = \frac{72 + 80}{2.0} = \frac{152}{2.0} = 76 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$ **Step 2: Mass-weighted humidity ratio of mixture (mass conservation of water):** $$m_{a1} \omega_1 + m_{a2} \omega_2 = (m_{a1} + m_{a2}) \omega_{mix}$$ $$\omega_{mix} = \frac{(1.2 \times 0.012) + (0.8 \times 0.024)}{1.2 + 0.8} = \frac{0.0144 + 0.0192}{2.0} = \frac{0.0336}{2.0} = 0.0168 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ **Step 3: Verification using lever rule on psychrometric chart:** The mixed state lies on the line joining state 1 and state 2, and the lever rule gives: $$\frac{m_{a1}}{m_{a2}} = \frac{h_2 - h_{mix}}{h_{mix} - h_1} = \frac{100 - 76}{76 - 60} = \frac{24}{16} = 1.5$$ This matches $m_{a1}/m_{a2} = 1.2/0.8 = 1.5$. ✓ **Step 4: Optional — find DBT of mixed air:** $$h_{mix} = 1.005\,t_{mix} + \omega_{mix}(2500 + 1.88\,t_{mix})$$ $$76 = 1.005\,t_{mix} + 0.0168(2500 + 1.88\,t_{mix})$$ $$76 = 1.005\,t_{mix} + 42.0 + 0.0316\,t_{mix}$$ $$34.0 = 1.0366\,t_{mix}$$ $$t_{mix} = 32.8^{\circ}\text{C}$$ **Final Answer:** - $h_{mix} = 76$ kJ/kg da - $\omega_{mix} = 0.0168$ kg/kg da - $t_{mix} = 32.8^{\circ}\text{C}$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 Papers (Module 3 — AC Fundamentals):** > > 1. **Forgetting the 0.622 constant:** Students often write $\omega = P_v / P_t$ — this costs **1 full mark** in Part A. > 2. **Using $P_v$ instead of $P_s$ for dew point:** DPT requires $P_s(t_{dp}) = P_v(\text{given air})$ — not $P_v = P_s(\text{given DBT})$. > 3. **Confusing Bypass Factor with Contact Factor:** $CF = 1 - BF$. Mixing these up gives a 7-mark deduction in Part B. > 4. **Not reading BF from psychrometric chart:** In KTU problems, BF is often *implicit* — you must read ADP and use the formula. > 5. **Enthalpy reference inconsistency:** Use 0°C reference (2500 kJ/kg) unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Inconsistent references lose **2 marks**. > 6. **Mixing mass and volume flow rates:** The formula $\dot{m}_a = \dot{V} / v$ is essential. Students forget $v$ and write $\dot{m} = \dot{V}$ — instant 2-mark loss. > 7. **Sensible heat factor = 1 confusion:** SHF cannot exceed 1. If your calculation gives SHF > 1, you have used the wrong enthalpy values. > 8. **Drawing the psychrometric chart without axes labels:** Even in descriptive questions, **always label DBT, ω, h, RH lines** — the examiner awards marks for axis labels. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Conditioning Fundamentals** ### Core Concepts - ✅ Air conditioning = simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, purity, motion** - ✅ Psychrometry = study of moist air properties - ✅ Dalton's Law: $P_t = P_a + P_v$ - ✅ **RH = 100% at dew point and saturation** ### Critical Formulas (Memorize) - ✅ $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ — **Master equation of psychrometry** - ✅ $h = 1.005\,t + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ — **Master equation of enthalpy** - ✅ $BF = \dfrac{t_2 - t_{ADP}}{t_1 - t_{ADP}}$ — **Bypass Factor** - ✅ $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{SH + LH}$ — **Sensible Heat Factor** - ✅ $Q = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2)$ — **Total cooling load** - ✅ $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P_t - P_v}$ — **Specific volume** - ✅ Lever rule: $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_{mix}}{h_{mix} - h_1}$ ### Key Numbers - ✅ $0.622$ (gas constant ratio) - ✅ $1.005$ kJ/kg·K ($c_{pa}$) - ✅ $1.88$ kJ/kg·K ($c_{pv}$) - ✅ $2500$ kJ/kg ($h_{fg}$ at 0°C) - ✅ $0.287$ kJ/kg·K ($R_a$) - ✅ $R_a/R_v = 0.622$ - ✅ Ship comfort: $24 \pm 2$°C, $50 \pm 10$% RH - ✅ Ship velocity: 0.08–0.25 m/s ### Process Identification - ✅ Horizontal line right → sensible heating - ✅ Horizontal line left → sensible cooling - ✅ Down-left → cooling + dehumidification - ✅ Constant WBT line → evaporative cooling - ✅ Straight line between two points → adiabatic mixing ### Common Pitfalls Avoided - ✅ Don't confuse BF with CF - ✅ Don't use $P_v$ in place of $P_s$ - ✅ Don't forget to label axes on psychrometric charts - ✅ Don't mix up SH and LH in energy equations - ✅ Always specify enthalpy reference (0°C) ### Marine Context - ✅ Outside air is hot, humid, salty → aggressive dehumidification needed - ✅ Recirculation (typically 25–75%) saves energy - ✅ Two-stage filtration (G4 + F7/HEPA) is standard - ✅ Critical rooms (ECR, gyro, hospital) need tighter control - ✅ DPT control prevents cargo freezing in reefers

topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundational Definition of Refrigeration

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body or substance and transferring it to a high-temperature body or substance, using mechanical work as the driving input. In marine engineering, this principle is critical for preserving perishable cargo, maintaining crew comfort, and ensuring the safe operation of electronic and mechanical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST743 - Module 3):**
> The "Definitions" topic forms the lexical foundation of the entire Refrigeration and Air Conditioning discipline. Mastery of these terms is non-negotiable for KTU ESE and Continuous Evaluation questions, as nearly every numerical problem in this module begins with the correct identification and application of these definitions.

## 1.2 Foundational Definition of Air Conditioning

**Air Conditioning** is the simultaneous control of **temperature**, **humidity**, **purity (cleanliness)**, and **air motion (circulation)** within a defined enclosed space — typically the accommodation blocks, bridge, engine control room, and galleys onboard a ship.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Heat River" Analogy**
> Think of heat as a flowing river. In nature, heat *always* flows from a hot body to a cold body (the Second Law of Thermodynamics). **Refrigeration is essentially a "heat pump" that artificially reverses this natural flow** — it acts like a hydraulic pump pushing water uphill. Work (electrical energy from the ship's auxiliary generator) is the "pump" that drives the heat "uphill" from the cold refrigerated space to the warm ambient sea air or sea water (used as the heat sink in the condenser).

## 1.4 Primary Performance & Capacity Definitions

### A) Ton of Refrigeration (TR)
- **Definition:** The rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg)** of pure water at **0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.
- **SI Equivalent:** **1 TR = 3.51685 kW** (≈ 3.517 kW)
- **Older/Imperial:** 1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr

### B) Coefficient of Performance (COP)
- **Definition:** The ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (Q_e) to the work input (W).
- **Significance:** Unlike efficiency, COP can be **greater than 1** because it is not a ratio of similar quantities.

### C) Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)
- **Definition:** The ratio of cooling capacity (in BTU/hr) to the power input (in Watts).
- **Marine Relevance:** Critical when evaluating HVAC chillers serving accommodation spaces.

### D) British Thermal Unit (BTU)
- **Definition:** The quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of **1 pound of water by 1 °F**.
- **SI Conversion:** 1 BTU = **1055.056 J**

### E) Sensible Heat
- **Definition:** Heat that causes a measurable change in **temperature** of a substance *without* changing its phase.
- **Marine Application:** Cooling of return air from a cabin from 27 °C to 13 °C is sensible cooling.

### F) Latent Heat
- **Definition:** Heat that causes a change in **phase** (state) of a substance at constant temperature.
- **Marine Application:** Dehumidification of air — condensing water vapor from return air.

### G) Specific Enthalpy (h)
- **Definition:** The total heat content of a substance per unit mass (kJ/kg), measured relative to a reference state.

## 1.5 Psychrometric Definitions (Air Conditioning)

| Symbol | Term | Working Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DBT | Dry Bulb Temperature | Air temperature measured by a standard thermometer. |
| WBT | Wet Bulb Temperature | Temperature measured when air is adiabatically saturated by water. |
| DPT | Dew Point Temperature | Temperature at which water vapor begins to condense out of air. |
| RH | Relative Humidity | Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor to saturation pressure at DBT. |
| SHF | Sensible Heat Factor | Ratio of sensible heat to total heat load. |
| ACH | Air Changes per Hour | Volume of fresh air supplied per hour relative to room volume. |
| $\omega$ | Specific Humidity | Mass of water vapor per kg of dry air. |

## 1.6 System-Component Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Marine System Components**
> - **Condenser:** Rejects heat from refrigerant to the cooling medium (sea water or air).
> - **Evaporator:** Absorbs heat from the space to be refrigerated.
> - **Compressor:** Provides the mechanical work to circulate the refrigerant.
> - **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device):** Reduces refrigerant pressure, enabling evaporation.
> - **Primary Refrigerant:** The fluid that undergoes phase change (e.g., R-134a, R-404A, R-22).
> - **Secondary Refrigerant (Brine):** A liquid (e.g., glycol-water) that *transports* refrigeration from a central plant to remote spaces — common on passenger vessels.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Classification of Definitions

Definitions in this module are classified into **four distinct families** based on their engineering role:

### Family 1: Capacity Definitions (How much?)
These quantify the rate at which the system removes heat.
- Ton of Refrigeration (TR)
- Refrigeration Effect (RE) / Cooling Load (Q_e)
- Heat Load / Heat Gain (W or kW)

### Family 2: Performance Definitions (How well?)
These quantify the thermodynamic efficiency of the cycle.
- Coefficient of Performance (COP) — for refrigeration and heat pumps.
- Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)
- Heat Load Factor (HLF)

### Family 3: Property Definitions (What is it made of?)
These describe the thermodynamic state of the refrigerant and air.
- Specific Enthalpy (h)
- Specific Entropy (s)
- Specific Volume (v)
- Saturation Temperature / Pressure

### Family 4: Process Definitions (What is happening?)
These describe thermodynamic transformations.
- Superheat (SH)
- Subcooling (SC)
- Throttling (Isenthalpic expansion)
- Compression (Isentropic / Polytropic)

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Definition

### Why is "1 Ton" Defined as 3.517 kW?
The "ton" originates from the heat absorbed when one ton of ice melts in 24 hours. Engineers converted this latent heat figure into an equivalent rate of heat removal, giving us a **practical industrial unit** that survives in modern marine and HVAC specifications.

### Why does COP exceed 1?
Because COP is **not** a thermal efficiency. It is a *coefficient of performance* — a comparison between the heat moved (Q_e) and the work purchased (W). Since the refrigerant also absorbs heat from the compressor's surroundings, COP can range from 2.0 to 5.0 in marine chillers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU Part B problem on this module will draw from these expressions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ton of Refrigeration | $1\,TR = \dfrac{907.185 \times 335}{24 \times 3600}$ | kW | Equals **3.51685 kW** |
| 2 | COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{net,in}}$ | — | $Q_e = h_g - h_f$ at evaporator |
| 3 | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_h}{W_{net,in}} = COP_R + 1$ | — | $Q_h = Q_e + W$ |
| 4 | EER | $EER = \dfrac{Q_e \, [BTU/hr]}{P_{input} \, [W]}$ | BTU/Wh | $\times 0.2931$ gives COP |
| 5 | Sensible Heat | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ/hr or kW | $c_{p,air} \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K |
| 6 | Latent Heat | $Q_l = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/hr or kW | $h_{fg,water} = 2257$ kJ/kg at 100 °C |
| 7 | Refrigeration Effect | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | At evaporator inlet/outlet |
| 8 | Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant |
| 9 | Heat Rejected | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | At condenser |
| 10 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg/kg | From psychrometric chart |
| 11 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{g}}$ | — | $P_g$ at DBT |
| 12 | Sensible Heat Factor | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | — | $0 \le SHF \le 1$ |
| 13 | Dew Point | $T_{dp} = T_{sat}(P_v)$ | °C | Read from steam tables |
| 14 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Per TR for R-134a ≈ **0.099 kg/s** |
| 15 | Volumetric Capacity | $V_c = \dot{m} \cdot v_1$ | m³/s | At compressor inlet |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute values:** In the formulas above, any ratio such as $\dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ is to be read as the ratio of $P_v$ to the difference $(P_{atm} - P_v)$, NOT an absolute value. No vertical bars are used to avoid markdown parsing errors.

## 2.4 Real-World Marine Utility

- **Cargo Holds (Reefer Ships):** Capacity is quoted in **kW per hold** (older practice: TR). Modern reefer containers use **W per TEU** ratings.
- **Accommodation HVAC:** Selected using **Total Heat Load = Q_s + Q_l**, with the **SHR (Sensible Heat Ratio)** line drawn on the psychrometric chart to locate the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**.
- **Provisions Plant (Galley Stores):** Defined heat load from each compartment — meat rooms, vegetable rooms, fish rooms — added to obtain the plant capacity in **TR**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Ton of Refrigeration in SI Units

**Starting Premise:** One ton of ice (2000 lb) melts at 0 °C in 24 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of ice} \, (m) &= 2000 \, \text{lb} \\
&= 2000 \times 0.4536 \, \text{kg} \\
&= 907.185 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C:**

$$
L_f = 335 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Total heat removed in 24 hours:**

$$
Q_{24h} = m \times L_f = 907.185 \times 335 = 303{,}907 \, \text{kJ}
$$

**Convert to per-second rate (1 hour = 3600 s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \, TR &= \frac{Q_{24h}}{24 \times 3600} \, \text{kW} \\
&= \frac{303{,}907}{86{,}400} \, \text{kW} \\
&\approx 3.51685 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of Conversion:** We start with a 24-hour total, then divide by the number of seconds in 24 hours to obtain a *rate* (kW = kJ/s). This is the only correct way to convert a stored energy quantity into a power rating.

## 3.2 Derivation: COP from the First Law

Consider a vapor-compression refrigeration cycle. Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component (neglecting KE and PE changes):

**Evaporator (Q_e absorbed):**

$$
q_e = h_1 - h_4
$$

**Compressor (work input):**

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Condenser (heat rejected):**

$$
q_c = h_2 - h_3
$$

**Expansion Valve (throttling, isenthalpic):**

$$
h_3 = h_4
$$

**By the First Law (cyclic device):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_c &= q_e + w_c \\
\Rightarrow w_c &= q_c - q_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, COP of the refrigerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{q_e}{w_c} \\
&= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a Heat Pump:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{q_c}{w_c} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

**Proof of the COP relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{q_c - q_e}{w_c} \\
&= \frac{w_c}{w_c} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Definition × Application

| Definition | Quantitative Form | Marine System Application | Typical Value Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ton of Refrigeration** | $Q = m \cdot L_f / (24 \times 3600)$ | Reefer container cooling plant | 5 – 30 TR per container |
| **COP (VCR cycle)** | $COP_R = Q_e / W$ | Provision plant (R-134a) | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| **SHR (Sensible Heat Ratio)** | $SHR = Q_s / Q_t$ | Accommodation HVAC sizing | 0.6 – 0.8 (cabins) |
| **Specific Humidity** | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_a - P_v)$ | Humidity control in wheelhouse | 0.008 – 0.018 kg/kg |
| **ACH** | $ACH = \dot{V}_{fresh} / V_{room}$ | Engine room ventilation | 20 – 30 ACH |
| **Superheat** | $SH = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}$ | Compressor safety margin | 4 – 8 K |
| **Subcooling** | $SC = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out}$ | Cycle efficiency gain | 3 – 5 K |
| **Dew Point** | $T_{dp}$ from $P_v$ | Anti-condensation in bridge | T < 18 °C |

## 3.4 Derivation: EER ↔ COP Conversion

By definition:

$$
EER = \frac{Q_e \, [\text{BTU/hr}]}{P_{input} \, [\text{W}]}
$$

Converting $Q_e$ to watts: $1 \, \text{BTU/hr} = 0.29307 \, \text{W}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_e \, [\text{BTU/hr}]}{P \, [\text{W}]} &= \frac{Q_e \, [\text{BTU/hr}] \times 0.29307 \, \text{W/(BTU/hr)}}{P \, [\text{W}]} \\
&= 0.29307 \times \frac{Q_e}{P} \\
&= 0.29307 \times COP
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
EER = 0.29307 \times COP \quad \text{or} \quad COP = \frac{EER}{0.29307}
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A marine provision refrigeration plant has a refrigeration capacity of **10 TR**. The compressor consumes **8 kW** of shaft power. The condenser rejects heat to sea water at **30 °C**. Calculate:
1. The COP of the system
2. The heat rejected at the condenser in kW
3. The EER

**Solution:**

**Given:** Capacity = 10 TR, $W_c = 8$ kW

**Step 1: Convert capacity to kW.**

$$
Q_e = 10 \times 3.51685 = 35.17 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2: COP.**

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{35.17}{8} = 4.40
$$

**Step 3: Heat rejected at condenser.**

$$
Q_c = Q_e + W_c = 35.17 + 8 = 43.17 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4: EER.**

$$
EER = 0.29307 \times 4.40 = 1.29 \, \text{BTU/Wh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
