> **Examiner Insight:** Most students lose marks by either (a) forgetting to convert TR to kW, or (b) computing $Q_c$ as $Q_e - W_c$ (sign error). The condenser ALWAYS rejects the sum of the useful refrigeration effect AND the work supplied.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Definition Hierarchy in Module 3

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Module 3 Definitions] --> B[Capacity Quantities]
    A --> C[Performance Quantities]
    A --> D[Property Quantities]
    A --> E[Process Quantities]
    A --> F[Psychrometric Quantities]

    B --> B1[Ton of Refrigeration TR]
    B --> B2[Refrigeration Effect RE]
    B --> B3[Heat Load Qe]

    C --> C1[COP Refrigerator]
    C --> C2[COP Heat Pump]
    C --> C3[Energy Efficiency Ratio EER]

    D --> D1[Specific Enthalpy h]
    D --> D2[Specific Entropy s]
    D --> D3[Specific Volume v]

    E --> E1[Superheat SH]
    E --> E2[Subcooling SC]
    E --> E3[Throttling]

    F --> F1[Dry Bulb Temperature DBT]
    F --> F2[Wet Bulb Temperature WBT]
    F --> F3[Dew Point Temperature DPT]
    F --> F4[Relative Humidity RH]
    F --> F5[Sensible Heat Factor SHF]

    B1 --> G[Marine Application]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G

    G --> H[Reefer Cargo Hold]
    G --> I[Accommodation HVAC]
    G --> J[Provision Plant]
    G --> K[Engine Room Cooling]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Vapour Compression Cycle with Labelled Definitions

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Compressor] -- Wc shaft work --> B[Condenser]
    B -- Qc heat rejected to sea --> C[Expansion Valve]
    C -- h3 equals h4 --> D[Evaporator]
    D -- Qe refrigeration effect --> A

    A2[State 1 superheated vapor] --> A
    B2[State 2 superheated vapor] --> B
    C2[State 3 saturated liquid] --> C
    D2[State 4 liquid vapor mix] --> D

    E[Key Definitions] --> E1[TR equals 3.517 kW]
    E --> E2[COP equals Qe divided by Wc]
    E --> E3[EER equals 0.293 times COP]
    E --> E4[SHR equals Qs over Qt]
    E --> E5[SH equals T1 minus Tsat]
    E --> E6[SC equals Tsat minus T3]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow: From Definition to Shipboard Use

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Define Capacity in TR] --> S2[Convert to kW]
    S2 --> S3[Calculate Mass Flow Rate]
    S3 --> S4[Select Compressor Size]
    S4 --> S5[Determine Condenser Heat Rejection]
    S5 --> S6[Select Sea Water Pump Flow]
    S6 --> S7[Final Plant Rating]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Psychrometric Process Identification

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Outside Air DBT and WBT] --> P2[Sensible Heating or Cooling]
    P2 --> P3[Humidification or Dehumidification]
    P3 --> P4[Supply Air State at ADP]
    P4 --> P5[Room Conditions after Load]
    P5 --> P6[Plot on Psychrometric Chart]
    P6 --> P7[Read SHF and ADP values]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?**
> Since the topic is the *lexical* definitions of refrigeration (rather than the physical construction of a compressor), a functional architecture diagram more accurately captures how the definitions interrelate, are grouped by purpose, and connect to shipboard systems. Physical drawings of compressors, condensers, and evaporators are introduced in **Module 2** and **Module 4** of PENST743.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Ton of Refrigeration" (TR). State its SI equivalent and explain why this unit is still used in marine industry specifications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Ton of Refrigeration is defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg) of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.
- **SI Equivalent (1 mark):** **1 TR = 3.51685 kW** (approximately 3.517 kW).
- **Marine Relevance (1 mark):** The TR unit survives in marine specifications because it offers a **practical, intuitive benchmark** for cargo cooling capacity (e.g., "a 20 TR provision plant") and aligns with older engineer training and classification society documentation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from a heat pump COP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **COP_R (1.5 marks):** $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{net,in}}$ — the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect to the work input.
- **COP_HP (1.5 marks):** $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_h}{W_{net,in}} = COP_R + 1$, where $Q_h = Q_e + W$. The difference arises because a heat pump's *useful* output is the heat rejected at the condenser, while a refrigerator's useful output is the heat absorbed at the evaporator.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with their SI units:
**(i)** Sensible Heat **(ii)** Latent Heat **(iii)** Specific Enthalpy
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine Vapour Compression Refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The enthalpy values at key state points are:
$h_1 = 240$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 270$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 110$ kJ/kg, $h_4 = 110$ kJ/kg.
The mass flow rate of refrigerant is **0.2 kg/s**. Calculate:
**(i)** Refrigeration Effect (RE) in kW
**(ii)** Compressor Work in kW
**(iii)** COP of the refrigerator
**(iv)** Heat rejected at the condenser in kW.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Choice A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

- **(i) Sensible Heat (2 marks):** Heat that produces a measurable change in temperature of a substance without any change in phase. Formula: $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$, **unit: kJ (or kW for rate)**. Example: cooling return air from 27 °C to 13 °C.

- **(ii) Latent Heat (2 marks):** Heat that causes a change in phase (state) of a substance at constant temperature and pressure. Formula: $Q_l = m \cdot h_{fg}$, **unit: kJ**. Example: condensation of water vapour in the dehumidifier coil.

- **(iii) Specific Enthalpy (3 marks):** The total heat content of a substance per unit mass, measured relative to a reference state (typically 0 °C for water, or saturated liquid at 0 °C for refrigerants). **Unit: kJ/kg**. It is a key state point function used in determining refrigeration effect, compressor work, and condenser load.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** $h_1 = 240$, $h_2 = 270$, $h_3 = 110$, $h_4 = 110$ kJ/kg; $\dot{m} = 0.2$ kg/s

**(i) Refrigeration Effect:**

$$
RE = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4) = 0.2 \times (240 - 110) = 0.2 \times 130 = 26 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Compressor Work:**

$$
W_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) = 0.2 \times (270 - 240) = 0.2 \times 30 = 6 \, \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) COP:**

$$
COP_R = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{26}{6} \approx 4.33
$$

**(iv) Heat Rejected at Condenser:**

$$
Q_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) = 0.2 \times (270 - 110) = 0.2 \times 160 = 32 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Verification (First Law):** $Q_c = RE + W_c = 26 + 6 = 32$ kW ✓

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Define the following psychrometric terms:
**(i)** Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) **(ii)** Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) **(iii)** Dew Point Temperature (DPT) **(iv)** Relative Humidity (RH)
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship accommodation block has the following air-conditioning load:
- Sensible Heat Load, $Q_s = 18$ kW
- Latent Heat Load, $Q_l = 6$ kW
- Room conditions: DBT = 27 °C, RH = 60%
- Sea-water cooling available at 28 °C

Calculate:
**(i)** Total Heat Load in kW and TR
**(ii)** Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)
**(iii)** Mass of dry air to be circulated if a temperature drop of 10 °C is desired across the cooling coil. (Take $c_p$ of air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K)
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Choice B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

- **(i) Dry Bulb Temperature (1.5 marks):** The temperature of air as measured by a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer freely exposed to the air, with **no radiation shielding** against moisture. **Unit: °C or K.**

- **(ii) Wet Bulb Temperature (1.5 marks):** The temperature registered by a thermometer whose bulb is covered by a wick soaked in water, exposed to a stream of air. The WBT is **always lower than DBT** except at saturation. **Unit: °C.**

- **(iii) Dew Point Temperature (2 marks):** The temperature at which the water vapour present in air begins to condense when cooled at constant pressure and constant moisture content. It is a direct indicator of moisture load. **Unit: °C.**

- **(iv) Relative Humidity (2 marks):** The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in the air ($P_v$) to the saturation pressure of water vapour at the DBT ($P_g$). Expressed as a percentage. **$\phi = P_v / P_g \times 100$%, dimensionless.**

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Total Heat Load:**

$$
Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = 18 + 6 = 24 \, \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_t \, \text{in TR} = \frac{24}{3.51685} \approx 6.82 \, \text{TR}
$$

**(ii) Sensible Heat Factor:**

$$
SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{18}{24} = 0.75
$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate of dry air:**

Using sensible heat equation:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{18}{1.005 \times 10} = \frac{18}{10.05} \approx 1.79 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Sign error in Q_c:** Many students compute $Q_c = Q_e - W_c$, but the correct relation is $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$ because the condenser rejects **both** the useful cooling effect **and** the work put in.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion:** Always convert TR to kW (× 3.517) before mixing units in a single calculation.
> 3. **Using WBT in place of DBT:** WBT is an *empirical* property of the air-water mixture, not a direct substitute for DBT in sensible-heat calculations.
> 4. **Confusing SHF with SHR:** SHF is defined on the **room sensible/total** load ratio; it determines the slope of the Room Condition Line on the psychrometric chart. Do not confuse with the RSHF (Room Sensible Heat Factor) which is the same in standard textbooks.
> 5. **Skipping the First-Law check:** Always verify $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$ at the end of any cycle calculation — it is a guaranteed full-mark clincher.
> 6. **Improper throttling assumption:** In an ideal VCR cycle, $h_3 = h_4$. Forgetting this drops 1–2 marks instantly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Definitions: Key Takeaways**

- **Capacity vs Performance:** TR, Q_e are *capacity* units; COP, EER are *performance* metrics.
- **1 TR = 3.51685 kW** — memorize this conversion factor; it appears in almost every KTU numerical.
- **COP_R = Q_e / W** and **COP_HP = Q_c / W = COP_R + 1**.
- **EER = 0.29307 × COP**.
- **First Law check:** $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$ — must always hold.
- **Psychrometric trio:** DBT > WBT ≥ DPT, with equality only at RH = 100%.
- **SHR = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)**, used to find ADP on the psychrometric chart.
- **Superheat = T_actual − T_sat** at compressor inlet; **Subcooling = T_sat − T_liquid_out**.
- **Throttling process:** $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic) — the foundation of the expansion device.
- **Marine-specific:** TR for cargo; kW for chillers; ACH for engine room ventilation; EER for cabin AC ratings.
- **COP > 1 is normal** — students should NOT mark such answers as wrong; this is the most common conceptual error in viva-voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometric Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychrometric Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Psychrometric Chart** (also called the **Mollier Diagram** or **Hygrometric Chart**) is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air at a constant pressure (usually standard atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa** or **1 atm**). It is the fundamental tool used in shipboard air-conditioning design, cargo ventilation analysis, and refrigerated transport engineering.

The chart plots the relationship between:
- **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** — horizontal axis
- **Moisture/Humidity content (Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio)** — vertical axis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A psychrometric chart is a graphical plot of the **moist air properties** (DBT, WBT, DPT, RH, Specific Humidity, Enthalpy, and Specific Volume) at a fixed total pressure, used to visualize and solve problems involving **moist air mixtures, conditioning processes, and load calculations** essential in shipboard HVAC and refrigeration systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Simple Plain English)

Imagine you have a glass of **cold water on a hot, humid day**. The outside of the glass becomes wet (water droplets form). Why? Because the warm, moist air around the glass cools down, and when it can no longer hold all its water vapor, the excess condenses.

Now imagine **"writing"** every possible state of that air — dry, moist, hot, cold, just-saturated — on a single huge graph. That graph is the **Psychrometric Chart**. It is essentially a "**map of air**" where:
- **Moving right** = getting **hotter** (temperature increases)
- **Moving up** = getting **more humid** (more water vapor)
- **The top curved line** = the **saturation line** (air cannot hold any more water — like the glass of cold water!)
- **The bottom straight line** = **bone-dry air** (zero moisture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Engineering Insight:** On a ship, the Chief Engineer uses this "map" to determine whether cargo holds need **ventilation, dehumidification, or heating** to prevent cargo damage (e.g., condensation on steel coils, sweat on tea chests, mold on grain). For the accommodation block, it is used to design the **AC plant** so that crew comfort is maintained in tropical seas.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | **101.325 kPa** (1.01325 bar) |
| Sea Level Reference Pressure | **1 atm** |
| Reference Enthalpy (h) | **0 kJ/kg dry air at 0°C DBT** |
| Specific Heat of Dry Air (cp,a) | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| Specific Heat of Water Vapor (cp,v) | **1.88 kJ/kg·K** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (hfg at 0°C) | **2501 kJ/kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties Plotted on the Psychrometric Chart (The "Six Lines")

The psychrometric chart has **six (6) families of lines** that the KTU examiner will expect you to identify, label, and use. These are the **HIGH-YIELD** marks for any 3-mark question.

#### 1. Constant Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) Lines — **Vertical Lines**
- DBT is the temperature of air measured by an ordinary thermometer with a dry bulb.
- These lines run **vertically** (parallel to the Y-axis).
- Each line is labeled at the saturation curve.

#### 2. Constant Specific Humidity (ω or W) Lines — **Horizontal Lines**
- Specific humidity (also called **humidity ratio**) is the mass of water vapor per kg of dry air.
- These lines run **horizontally** (parallel to the X-axis).
- Units: **kg water vapor / kg dry air**.

#### 3. Constant Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) Lines — **Inclined Lines (≈ 45° to the left)**
- WBT is the temperature measured by a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a wet wick.
- These lines are also called **psychrometric lines** or **constant enthalpy lines (approx.)** because WBT ≈ WBT lines approximate constant enthalpy on a Mollier chart.

#### 4. Constant Relative Humidity (RH or φ) Lines — **Curved Lines**
- RH = (Partial pressure of water vapor / Saturation pressure at DBT) × 100%
- These curve from the lower-left to the upper-right, all originating at the bottom.
- The **outermost curve = 100% RH = Saturation Line**.

#### 5. Constant Enthalpy (h) Lines — **Inclined Lines**
- Enthalpy is the total energy content of moist air per kg of dry air.
- These lines coincide closely with **WBT lines** on the Mollier-type psychrometric chart.

#### 6. Constant Specific Volume (v) Lines — **Inclined Lines (steeper than enthalpy lines)**
- Specific volume is the volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air plus its associated water vapor.
- Units: **m³/kg dry air**.

### 2.2 Definition of Each Property (In-Depth)

**Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT, °C):** The actual air temperature measured by a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer, shielded from radiation and moisture.

**Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT, °C):** The temperature a parcel of air would have if it were cooled **adiabatically to saturation** by evaporation of water into it.

**Dew Point Temperature (DPT, °C):** The temperature at which the air becomes **saturated** (RH = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio.

**Specific Humidity (ω, kg/kg dry air):**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{p - p_v}
$$

where $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor, $p$ = total pressure.

**Relative Humidity (RH or φ, %):**

$$
\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_s} \times 100
$$

where $p_s$ = saturation pressure of water vapor at the DBT.

**Enthalpy of Moist Air (h, kJ/kg dry air):**

$$
h = c_{p,a} \cdot T + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot T)
$$

**Specific Volume (v, m³/kg dry air):**

$$
v = \frac{R_a \cdot T}{p - p_v} = \frac{R_a \cdot T}{p_a}
$$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Cargo Ventilation Systems:** Determining whether outside air will cause **"sweat"** on cargo surfaces.
- **Accommodation AC Plant Sizing:** Calculating the **sensible heat factor (SHF)** and **apparatus dew point (ADP)**.
- **Reefer Container Monitoring:** Analyzing air conditions inside refrigerated containers.
- **Engine Room Ventilation:** Designing supply/return air systems to maintain **< 35°C** in engine control rooms.
- **Grain Carriers:** Avoiding moisture migration in bulk cargo holds.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD — Memorize!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE ALERT:** No vertical pipe `|` is used in this table. The absolute values are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| # | Property / Formula | Equation | Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{p_v}{p - p_v}$ | kg/kg da | $\omega = 0$ for dry air |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_s}$ (as fraction) | — | $\phi = 1$ at saturation |
| 3 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | — | $\mu = 1$ at saturation |
| 4 | DBT–DPT Relation | $p_v(T_{dpt}) = p_s(T_{dpt})$ | — | $T_{dpt} \leq T_{db}$ |
| 5 | Enthalpy | $h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | kJ/kg da | Reference: $h = 0$ at $T = 0$°C, $\omega = 0$ |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{p - p_v}$ | m³/kg da | At $T = 0$°C, $v \approx 0.773$ m³/kg da |
| 7 | Specific Heat of Moist Air | $c_{pm} = c_{pa} + \omega \cdot c_{pv}$ | kJ/kg·K | $c_{pa} = 1.005$, $c_{pv} = 1.88$ |
| 8 | SHF | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | — | $0 \leq SHF \leq 1$ |
| 9 | SHF in terms of ADP | $SHF = \dfrac{T_{db1} - T_{ADP}}{T_{db1} - T_{db2}}$ | — | Used in coil selection |
| 10 | Bypass Factor (BF) | $BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | — | $0 \leq BF \leq 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Humidity Formula (Humidity Ratio)

**Starting Point:** A mixture of **dry air (a)** and **water vapor (v)** occupies a total volume $V$ at total pressure $p$ and temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures:**

By Dalton's Law, the total pressure equals the sum of the partial pressures:

$$
p = p_a + p_v
$$

where:
- $p_a$ = partial pressure of dry air (kPa)
- $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor (kPa)

**Step 2 — Express Each Partial Pressure Using the Ideal Gas Law:**

For an ideal gas, $pV = mRT$. Rearranging for the partial pressure:

$$
p_a = \frac{m_a R_a T}{V}
$$

$$
p_v = \frac{m_v R_v T}{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Mass Ratio of Vapor to Dry Air:**

Specific humidity $\omega$ is defined as the mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air:

$$
\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide the Two Partial Pressure Equations:**

$$
\frac{p_v}{p_a} = \frac{m_v R_v T / V}{m_a R_a T / V} = \frac{m_v R_v}{m_a R_a}
$$

The temperature $T$ and volume $V$ cancel out — a key insight!

**Step 5 — Substitute the Universal Gas Constants:**

$R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K and $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K.

$$
\frac{R_v}{R_a} = \frac{0.4615}{0.287} = 1.6078 \approx 1.608
$$

But to get the **molecular weight ratio** (which is what we want for the mass ratio), we use:

$$
\frac{R_v}{R_a} = \frac{M_a}{M_v} = \frac{28.97}{18.02} = 1.6078
$$

So:

$$
\frac{p_v}{p_a} = \omega \cdot \frac{M_a}{M_v}
$$

**Step 6 — Express $p_a$ in Terms of Total Pressure:**

From Step 1: $p_a = p - p_v$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{p_v}{p - p_v} = \omega \cdot \frac{M_a}{M_v}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\omega$:**

$$
\omega = \frac{M_v}{M_a} \cdot \frac{p_v}{p - p_v}
$$

Since $M_v / M_a = 18.02 / 28.97 = 0.622$:

$$
\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{p - p_v}}
$$

**This is the standard psychrometric formula on the chart.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of Enthalpy of Moist Air

**Step 1 — Define the Reference State:** Enthalpy is set to **zero** at 0°C for both dry air and liquid water (which is in the saturated state at 0°C).

**Step 2 — Express the Enthalpy of Each Component:**

- Enthalpy of dry air at temperature $T$: $h_a = c_{p,a} \cdot T$
- Enthalpy of water vapor (per kg of water vapor): $h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot T$
  - where $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg is the latent heat at 0°C, and we then add the superheat from 0°C to $T$.

**Step 3 — Combine on a Per-kg-of-Dry-Air Basis:**

The mixture contains 1 kg of dry air and $\omega$ kg of water vapor. Total enthalpy per kg dry air:

$$
h = 1 \cdot c_{p,a} T + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} T)
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in Standard Values:**

$$
h = 1.005 T + \omega (2501 + 1.88 T)
$$

$$
\boxed{h = (1.005 + 1.88\omega)T + 2501\,\omega \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air]}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The first term is the **sensible heat** of the moist air mixture; the second term is the **latent heat** carried by the water vapor. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Psychrometric Processes on the Chart

| Process | Description | Direction on Chart | Property Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heating** | Sensible heating of air (e.g., heater coil in winter) | Horizontal line to the right | $\omega$ constant, RH ↓ |
| **Cooling** | Sensible cooling (e.g., cooling coil above dew point) | Horizontal line to the left | $\omega$ constant, RH ↑ |
| **Cooling & Dehumidification** | Air cooled below DPT (e.g., AC coil) | Downward-left, then horizontal left | $\omega$ ↓, h ↓ |
| **Adiabatic Humidification** | Water spray in air (e.g., air washer) | Along WBT line (≈ constant h) | h constant, $\omega$ ↑ |
| **Chemical Dehumidification** | Solid/liquid absorbent | Vertical line downward | DBT constant, $\omega$ ↓ |
| **Mixing of Two Air Streams** | Recirculated + fresh air | Straight line between two state points | $h$, $\omega$ linear in mass fractions |
| **Evaporative Cooling** | Spray water into air | Approx. along WBT line | WBT constant |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU Sub-Question):**

In a ship's accommodation AC system, air enters the cooling coil at **30°C DBT** and **22°C WBT**. The coil has a bypass factor of **0.20**, and the apparatus dew point is **12°C**. Find:
- (i) The exit DBT, WBT, and relative humidity
- (ii) The heat removed per kg of dry air
- (iii) The amount of water condensed per kg of dry air

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 30$°C (DBT entering coil)
- $T_{wbt,1} = 22$°C
- $BF = 0.20$
- $T_{ADP} = 12$°C

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Exit State:**

Using the bypass factor definition (this is the foundational psychrometric formula on a cooling coil):

$$
BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

Rearranging for the exit DBT $T_2$:

$$
T_2 = T_{ADP} + BF \times (T_1 - T_{ADP})
$$

$$
T_2 = 12 + 0.20 \times (30 - 12) = 12 + 0.20 \times 18 = 12 + 3.6
$$

$$
\boxed{T_2 = 15.6\ \text{°C}}
$$

**Finding $T_{wbt,2}$:**

Since cooling coils are **close to the WBT lines** (approximately constant WBT = ADP for the saturated portion), and using the property that WBT at the exit can be read off by considering that mixing on the chart gives a state lying on the line between the ADP point and the inlet point:

$$
T_{wbt,2} = T_{ADP} + BF \times (T_{wbt,1} - T_{ADP}) = 12 + 0.20 \times (22 - 12) = 12 + 2.0
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{wbt,2} = 14.0\ \text{°C}}
$$

**Relative Humidity at Exit:**

From the chart, reading at $T_2 = 15.6$°C and the state line, $RH_2 \approx 90\%$.

**Part (ii) — Heat Removed per kg of Dry Air:**

Enthalpy at inlet (using $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$):

First, find $\omega_1$ from the chart at 30°C DBT / 22°C WBT → $\omega_1 \approx 0.0132$ kg/kg da.

$$
h_1 = 1.005(30) + 0.0132 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 30) = 30.15 + 0.0132 \times 2557.4
$$

$$
h_1 = 30.15 + 33.76 = 63.91\ \text{kJ/kg da}
$$

At exit (15.6°C / 14°C WBT), reading from the chart: $h_2 \approx 39.4$ kJ/kg da.

$$
q_{cooling} = h_1 - h_2 = 63.91 - 39.4
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{cooling} = 24.51\ \text{kJ/kg da}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Water Condensed:**

Assuming air leaves the coil **saturated** at the ADP for the part that contacts the coil:

- $\omega_1 = 0.0132$ kg/kg da
- $\omega_2 = 0.0106$ kg/kg da (from chart at 15.6°C / 14°C WBT)

$$
\Delta\omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2 = 0.0132 - 0.0106
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta\omega = 0.0026\ \text{kg water/kg dry air}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight:** In air conditioning, the heat removed consists of **sensible heat (DBT change)** plus **latent heat (moisture removal)**. The ratio of these is the **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**, which is the single most important parameter for selecting a cooling coil on a ship.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Six Lines on a Psychrometric Chart

The following block-level Mermaid diagram maps the **logical structure of lines** on a typical psychrometric chart. This is a schematic representation of the relationships — not a literal drawing of the chart (which is impossible in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CHART["PSYCHROMETRIC CHART"]:::root

    CHART --> DBTTYPE["CONSTANT DBT LINES (Vertical)"]:::group
    CHART --> WBTTYPE["CONSTANT WBT LINES (Inclined ~45 deg)"]:::group
    CHART --> DPTTYPE["CONSTANT DPT LINES (Horizontal)"]:::group
    CHART --> RHTYPE["CONSTANT RH LINES (Curved)"]:::group
    CHART --> ENTHALPY["CONSTANT ENTHALPY LINES (Inclined)"]:::group
    CHART --> VOL["CONSTANT SPECIFIC VOLUME LINES (Steeper Inclined)"]:::group

    DBTTYPE --> DBT1["Reading: Air Temperature"]:::leaf
    WBTTYPE --> WBT1["Reading: WBT measured by wet wick"]:::leaf
    DPTTYPE --> DPT1["Reading: Saturation temperature"]:::leaf
    RHTYPE --> RH100["100 percent Saturation Line (Outer curve)"]:::leaf
    RHTYPE --> RH50["50 percent RH (Innermost)"]:::leaf
    ENTHALPY --> H1["Reading: Total heat content kJ per kg da"]:::leaf
    VOL --> V1["Reading: Volume per kg dry air"]:::leaf

    RH100 --> SWEAT["Forms CONDENSATION on cold cargo (marine sweat problem)"]:::warning

    classDef root fill:#0b3d91,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef group fill:#f4a300,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef warning fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Shipboard AC Plant Using Psychrometric Chart

The following diagram shows the **functional flow** of air through a typical marine accommodation AC unit and identifies where on the psychrometric chart each state point is located.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OUTAIR["OUTSIDE AIR\nDBT 32 C, RH 75 percent\nState Point O on chart"]:::inlet

    MIX["MIXING CHAMBER\n(Fresh + Return Air)\nState Point M"]:::mix

    FILTER["FILTER + PREHEATER\n(Sensible heating)\nHorizontal line to the right"]:::process

    COOLCOIL["COOLING COIL\n(Cool + Dehumidify)\nLine M to C (down and left)"]:::cool

    HEATER["REHEATER\n(Sensible heating if needed)\nHorizontal line to the right"]:::process

    FAN["FAN + DUCT TO CABIN\nState Point S (Supply)"]:::fan

    CABIN["ACCOMMODATION CABIN\nHeat and moisture gains\nLine S to R (up and right)"]:::cabin

    EXHAUST["EXHAUST + RECIRCULATION\nState Point R"]:::outlet

    OUTAIR -->|"Omega constant"| MIX
    MIX -->|"DBT rises"| FILTER
    FILTER -->|"h and omega fall"| COOLCOIL
    COOLCOIL -->|"Optional sensible heat"| HEATER
    HEATER -->|"Through fan"| FAN
    FAN -->|"Room sensible + latent load"| CABIN
    CABIN -->|"To outside or return"| EXHAUST
    EXHAUST -.->|"Partial return"| MIX

    classDef inlet fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#00bfff,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fan fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef cabin fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef outlet fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Standard Psychrometric Process (Reading the Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Start with DBT = 35 C and WBT = 25 C\n(Locate state point on chart)"]:::root
    STEP1["Step 1: Move UP vertically to saturation line\nto read DPT (here DPT = 19 C)"]:::step
    STEP2["Step 2: Move along RH lines to read RH\n(here RH = 47 percent)"]:::step
    STEP3["Step 3: Read horizontal line to right\nto get specific humidity (omega = 0.0146 kg per kg)"]:::step
    STEP4["Step 4: Follow WBT line to enthalpy scale\nto get h = 72.5 kJ per kg da"]:::step
    STEP5["Step 5: Read specific volume from inclined line\nto get v = 0.892 m cubed per kg da"]:::step

    START --> STEP1
    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5

    STEP1 -.- NOTE1["DPT is the temperature below which\nCONDENSATION will occur\nCritical for shipboard cargo sweat analysis"]:::note
    STEP2 -.- NOTE2["As air is heated, RH drops\nImportant for winter heating coils"]:::note

    classDef root fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#fffacd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms with reference to the psychrometric chart:
- (i) Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)
- (ii) Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)
- (iii) Dew Point Temperature (DPT)

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT):** It is the temperature of moist air measured by a standard thermometer whose bulb is kept dry and shielded from radiation. It is plotted on the **X-axis** of the psychrometric chart as **vertical constant-DBT lines**.

(ii) **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT):** It is the temperature measured by a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a wick moistened with water, exposed to a stream of air. It represents the **adiabatic saturation temperature** of air. WBT lines are **inclined lines** on the chart.

(iii) **Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** It is the temperature at which moist air becomes **saturated (RH = 100%)** when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. On the chart, DPT is found by following the **horizontal constant-ω line** from the state point to the **saturation curve**. [3 Marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

List the **six families of lines** present on a psychrometric chart and state the property that each represents.

**Model Answer:**

The six families of lines on a psychrometric chart are:

| Sl. No. | Line Family | Property Represented |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical lines | Constant **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** |
| 2 | Horizontal lines | Constant **Specific Humidity (ω)** |
| 3 | Inclined (≈ 45° left) lines | Constant **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)** |
| 4 | Curved lines | Constant **Relative Humidity (RH)** |
| 5 | Inclined lines (≈ WBT lines) | Constant **Enthalpy (h)** |
| 6 | Steeper inclined lines | Constant **Specific Volume (v)** |

[3 Marks]

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and salient features of a psychrometric chart**. Explain the significance of the **saturation curve** and the **constant-WBT lines**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at a shipboard accommodation is at **35°C DBT** and **50% RH**. It is first cooled to **15°C DBT** (sensible cooling only, no moisture removal) and then heated to **25°C DBT** for supply to the cabin. Using the psychrometric chart, determine:
- (i) Specific humidity (ω) of the air
- (ii) Enthalpy at each state
- (iii) RH after heating
- (iv) DPT of the original air

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction & Salient Features [7 Marks]:**

The psychrometric chart is a graph drawn on a 2-D plane at **constant total pressure (1 atm)**.

**Axes:**
- **X-axis:** Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) in °C, ranging typically from **0°C to 50°C**
- **Y-axis:** Specific Humidity (ω) in **kg water vapor per kg dry air**, ranging from 0 to ~0.030

**Six Families of Lines (as tabulated above).** The **saturation curve** (RH = 100%) forms the upper boundary, sloping upward from left to right. Below the saturation curve lies the **unsaturated region** where normal AC processes occur.

**Significance of the Saturation Curve:**
- Represents the **state at which condensation begins**.
- All DPT readings are taken on this curve.
- On ships, the **dew point of the cargo hold air** vs. the **cargo surface temperature** determines if **"ship sweat"** or **"cargo sweat"** will occur.

**Significance of Constant-WBT Lines:**
- WBT lines approximate **constant-enthalpy lines**.
- They help in determining the **sensible and latent heat** components of the cooling load.
- Air moving along a WBT line represents **adiabatic saturation** (e.g., air washer on ship).

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- State 1: $T_1 = 35$°C, $RH_1 = 50\%$
- State 2: $T_2 = 15$°C (after sensible cooling, $\omega$ constant)
- State 3: $T_3 = 25$°C (after sensible heating, $\omega$ constant)
- Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find partial pressure of vapor at state 1:**

Saturation pressure at 35°C from steam tables: $p_{s,1} = 5.628$ kPa.

$$
p_{v,1} = \phi_1 \times p_{s,1} = 0.50 \times 5.628 = 2.814\ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific humidity at state 1 (and 2 and 3, since ω is constant):**

$$
\omega_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{p_{v,1}}{p - p_{v,1}} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.814}{101.325 - 2.814}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{2.814}{98.511} = 0.622 \times 0.02857
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = \omega_2 = \omega_3 = 0.01778\ \text{kg/kg da}}
$$

**(i) Specific humidity = 0.01778 kg/kg da** ✓

**Step 3 — Enthalpy at each state:**

Using $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$:

At State 1 (T = 35°C):
$$
h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.01778 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 35) = 35.175 + 0.01778 \times 2566.8
$$

$$
h_1 = 35.175 + 45.64
$$

$$
\boxed{h_1 = 80.81\ \text{kJ/kg da}}
$$

At State 2 (T = 15°C):
$$
h_2 = 1.005(15) + 0.01778 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 15) = 15.075 + 0.01778 \times 2529.2
$$

$$
h_2 = 15.075 + 44.97
$$

$$
\boxed{h_2 = 60.05\ \text{kJ/kg da}}
$$

At State 3 (T = 25°C):
$$
h_3 = 1.005(25) + 0.01778 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 0.01778 \times 2548
$$

$$
h_3 = 25.125 + 45.30
$$

$$
\boxed{h_3 = 70.43\ \text{kJ/kg da}}
$$

**(ii) Enthalpies: $h_1 = 80.81$, $h_2 = 60.05$, $h_3 = 70.43$ kJ/kg da** ✓

**Step 4 — RH at state 3:**

Saturation pressure at 25°C: $p_{s,3} = 3.169$ kPa.

Partial pressure of vapor at state 3 (same as state 1):
$$
p_{v,3} = 2.814\ \text{kPa}
$$

$$
\phi_3 = \frac{p_{v,3}}{p_{s,3}} = \frac{2.814}{3.169}
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi_3 = 88.8\%}
$$

**(iii) RH at state 3 = 88.8%** ✓

**Step 4 — Dew Point Temperature of original air:**

DPT is the saturation temperature corresponding to $p_{v,1} = 2.814$ kPa.

From steam tables: At what temperature is $p_s = 2.814$ kPa? Between 22°C ($p_s = 2.645$ kPa) and 24°C ($p_s = 2.985$ kPa).

Interpolating:
$$
T_{dpt} = 22 + \frac{2.814 - 2.645}{2.985 - 2.645} \times 2 = 22 + \frac{0.169}{0.340} \times 2
$$

$$
T_{dpt} = 22 + 0.994
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{dpt} \approx 23.0\ \text{°C}}
$$

**(iv) DPT of original air ≈ 23°C** ✓

**[Total: 7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the terms **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** and **Bypass Factor (BF)**. With the help of the psychrometric chart, show how the **bypass factor** affects the performance of a cooling coil used in a ship's accommodation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A marine cargo hold contains air at **30°C DBT** and **80% RH**. The cargo surface temperature is **20°C**. Using the psychrometric chart and steam tables, determine:
- (i) Whether **sweat (condensation)** will form on the cargo
- (ii) Dew point temperature of the hold air
- (iii) Specific humidity of the air
- (iv) If the air is to be dehumidified to a DPT of **15°C** by cooling, what is the minimum cooling required per kg of dry air?

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) SHF and BF [7 Marks]:**

**Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** It is the ratio of the **sensible heat** to the **total heat** (sensible + latent) removed by the cooling coil:

$$
SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}
$$

- $Q_s$ = sensible heat (temperature reduction)
- $Q_l$ = latent heat (moisture condensation)

For a ship in the **tropics**, SHF is typically **0.6 to 0.8** (sensible dominant). For a ship in the **humid monsoon trade**, SHF may be **0.5 to 0.7** (more latent).

**Bypass Factor (BF):** A cooling coil is not 100% effective — some air **bypasses** the coil surface without being cooled. BF is defined as:

$$
BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

- $T_1$ = air DBT entering the coil
- $T_2$ = air DBT leaving the coil
- $T_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew point (the temperature of the coil surface)

**Effect on Performance:** A **lower BF** (e.g., 0.10) means the coil is more effective — it cools the air closer to the ADP. A **higher BF** (e.g., 0.30) means less contact time, larger coil needed. On a ship, **BF = 0.10–0.25** is typical for AC coils.

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 30$°C, $RH_1 = 80\%$
- Cargo surface temperature: $T_{cargo} = 20$°C
- $T_{dpt, target} = 15$°C

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of vapor in hold:**

Saturation pressure at 30°C: $p_{s,1} = 4.246$ kPa.

$$
p_{v,1} = 0.80 \times 4.246 = 3.397\ \text{kPa}
$$

**(i) Will sweat form?**

DPT is the temperature at which air becomes saturated with its current moisture content. We find DPT by looking up the saturation temperature corresponding to $p_v = 3.397$ kPa.

From steam tables: At 26°C, $p_s = 3.363$ kPa. So DPT ≈ 26°C.

Since $T_{cargo} = 20$°C $< T_{dpt} = 26$°C, the cargo surface is **colder than the dew point**. Therefore, **YES, sweat (condensation) will form** on the cargo. This is a classic **"ship sweat"** problem.

**(ii) DPT ≈ 26°C** ✓

**Step 2 — Specific humidity of hold air:**

$$
\omega_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{3.397}{101.325 - 3.397} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.397}{97.928}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 0.622 \times 0.03469
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = 0.02158\ \text{kg/kg da}}
$$

**(iii) Specific humidity = 0.02158 kg/kg da** ✓

**Step 3 — Minimum cooling required to dehumidify to DPT = 15°C:**

For minimum cooling, the air is cooled to the **ADP = target DPT = 15°C** with zero bypass factor. At ADP, the air is **saturated** at 15°C.

Saturation pressure at 15°C: $p_{s,ADP} = 1.705$ kPa.

$$
\omega_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.705}{101.325 - 1.705} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.705}{99.620} = 0.01065\ \text{kg/kg da}
$$

Enthalpy at state 1:
$$
h_1 = 1.005(30) + 0.02158 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 30)
$$
$$
h_1 = 30.15 + 0.02158 \times 2557.4 = 30.15 + 55.19 = 85.34\ \text{kJ/kg da}
$$

Enthalpy at state 2 (saturated at 15°C):
$$
h_2 = 1.005(15) + 0.01065 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 15)
$$
$$
h_2 = 15.075 + 0.01065 \times 2529.2 = 15.075 + 26.94 = 42.01\ \text{kJ/kg da}
$$

Minimum heat to be removed:
$$
q_{min} = h_1 - h_2 = 85.34 - 42.01
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{min} = 43.33\ \text{kJ/kg da}}
$$

**(iv) Minimum cooling required = 43.33 kJ per kg of dry air** ✓

**[Total: 7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** when using the ideal gas law for specific volume — **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing DBT, WBT, and DPT** — they are three different temperatures! DBT is highest, DPT is lowest, WBT is in between. Mixing them up = **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Not using the reference state** (0°C) for enthalpy — using T in °C is correct ONLY because the reference is 0°C. Do NOT use 273 K. **−2 marks**.
> 4. **In SHF/BF problems, drawing the psychrometric process** is mandatory — full marks require the diagram. Without it, expect **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 5. **In cargo sweat problems**, students forget to compare $T_{cargo}$ with $T_{dpt}$ — both quantities must be calculated. Missing the comparison = **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Incorrect use of 0.622** — this is the ratio $M_v / M_a$ and is constant only for air-water vapor mixtures. **−1 mark** if units are mixed up.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⭐ EXAM-DAY RAPID REVISION — Memorize Before Entering the Hall**

- [ ] A **psychrometric chart** is drawn at **constant pressure** (1 atm / 101.325 kPa).
- [ ] **DBT lines = vertical.** **Specific humidity (ω) lines = horizontal.** **WBT lines = inclined at ~45°.** **RH lines = curved.** **Enthalpy lines ≈ WBT lines.**
- [ ] The **saturation curve = 100% RH** is the **upper boundary** of the chart.
- [ ] **DPT is read by following a horizontal line** (constant ω) from the state point to the saturation curve and then dropping down to the DBT axis.
- [ ] **Enthalpy reference** is $h = 0$ at $T = 0$°C and $\omega = 0$ (dry air at 0°C).
- [ ] The specific humidity formula: $\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{p_v}{p - p_v}$
- [ ] The enthalpy formula: $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$ — in **°C**, not K.
- [ ] The **bypass factor (BF)** formula: $BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$.
- [ ] The **sensible heat factor (SHF)** formula: $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$.
- [ ] **Ship sweat** forms when **cargo surface temperature < DPT of air**.
- [ ] **Cargo sweat** forms when **cargo surface temperature < outside DPT** and outside air enters the hold.
- [ ] **Heating** = horizontal line to the right (ω constant).
- [ ] **Cooling** = horizontal line to the left (ω constant, sensible only).
- [ ] **Cooling & Dehumidification** = line moves down and left, ω decreases, h decreases.
- [ ] **Adiabatic humidification (air washer)** = motion along WBT line (h ≈ constant).
- [ ] On ships, the psychrometric chart is used for **AC plant design, cargo ventilation decisions, and reefer container management**.
- [ ] Standard constants: $c_{p,a} = 1.005$, $c_{p,v} = 1.88$, $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning (Heating)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning (Heating)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Conditioning (Heating)** in the context of marine HVAC systems refers to the controlled addition of thermal energy to the supply air stream — and consequently to the conditioned spaces (cabins, mess rooms, wheelhouse, engine control rooms, galleys, passages) — in order to maintain the **dry-bulb temperature** of the ventilated air at a level prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Marine HVAC Design Code** and aligned with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels** for Outcome-Based Education (OBE).

In thermodynamic rigor, heating of air is the *sensible* increase of enthalpy of a moist air parcel, while the moisture content (humidity ratio, $w$) remains unchanged. The process is therefore represented on the **psychrometric chart** as an *almost horizontal* line moving to the right (increasing dry-bulb temperature, constant humidity ratio).

Mathematically, the heating process across a heating coil can be expressed as:

$$
q_s \;=\; m_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{db,out} - T_{db,in}) \;=\; \dot{V} \, \rho_a \, c_{p,a} \, \Delta T_{db}
$$

where $q_s$ is the sensible heat added (kW), $m_a$ is the mass flow rate of dry air (kg/s), $c_{p,a}$ is the specific heat of moist air at constant pressure (**≈ 1.02 kJ/kg·K** at standard conditions), and $\Delta T_{db}$ is the temperature rise across the coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *“Air Conditioning (Heating) is the process of supplying heated air to a conditioned space to compensate for envelope heat losses, ventilation heat losses, and internal heat deficits, such that the resultant space temperature lies within the marine comfort band of 20 °C to 26 °C DB (typically 22 °C ± 1 °C for accommodation spaces).”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a cold winter morning in the North Sea. A sailor steps out of his cabin into a steel corridor. The steel bulkhead feels like ice, and the air is biting. Now imagine an invisible, gentle warm current blowing along that corridor — coming from a hidden finned coil mounted just above the door. That coil has steam or hot water running through its copper tubes, and a fan is pushing air across the hot fins. The air warms up, becomes buoyant, and quietly blankets the corridor.

**That, in essence, is shipboard air-conditioning heating.** The “heating coil” is just like the *radiator* in your home, except:

- It blows air *across* the hot surfaces (forced convection) instead of relying on natural convection alone.
- It is fed by *low-pressure steam* from an oil-fired boiler, *engine jacket cooling water*, or *electrical resistance elements* — whichever is most efficient for that particular vessel.
- It is **never** allowed to overheat the air beyond safe limits (typically a maximum air-off temperature of **45 °C** to avoid scorching and duct degradation).

The ship’s central heating system is essentially the *lungs* of the accommodation block — it inhales cold return air, warms it, and exhales a comfortable supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics for Marine Heating:**
> - Cabin comfort DB temperature: **22 °C ± 1 °C**
> - Minimum outside air supply: **≈ 8 L/s per person** (IMO/ILO)
> - Maximum air-off coil temperature: **≈ 45 °C** (to prevent duct damage)
> - Heating coil face velocity: **≈ 2.5 to 3.0 m/s**
> - Heating fluid (steam) pressure: **typically 0.2 to 0.5 MPa gauge**
> - Heating fluid (hot water) supply temperature: **≈ 80 °C to 90 °C**

## 1.3 Why Heating is Critical Onboard Ships

1. **Crew welfare and morale** in cold North Atlantic, Baltic, or polar routes.
2. **Condensation prevention** on cold steel bulkheads and deckheads (causes corrosion and electrical faults).
3. **Instrument protection** in wheelhouse, chart room, and engine control room.
4. **Cargo protection** for certain cargoes (e.g., provisions, electronics, fresh produce).
5. **Regulatory compliance** with **MLC 2006** (Maritime Labour Convention) and **ILO Convention 92**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick mnemonic for KTU Viva:** When asked *“Why do ships need heating even in the tropics?”* — answer: **C**ondensation, **C**omfort, **C**orrosion control, **C**argo safety — the four C’s.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Heating Systems

Shipboard heating systems can be classified along several axes. KTU examiners frequently ask for these classifications, so memorizing the matrix below guarantees at least 3 to 4 marks in Part A.

### 2.1.1 By Heat Source / Carrier Fluid

| No. | Type | Heat Source | Typical Application | Pros | Cons |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Steam heating** | Auxiliary boiler steam | Large passenger/cargo ships | High heat capacity, fast response | Boiler dependency, piping losses |
| 2 | **Hot-water heating** | Engine jacket cooling water + calorifier | Most modern motor vessels | Energy-efficient, recycles waste heat | Slower response, pump dependency |
| 3 | **Electric heating** | Resistive elements (NiCr wire) | Small craft, standby, local zones | Precise control, simple | High electrical load |
| 4 | **Diesel/oil-fired unit heaters** | Direct combustion of MDO | Engine rooms, workshops | Independent of boiler | Combustion exhaust handling |
| 5 | **Heat-pump heating** | Reverse-cycle refrigerant cycle | New-generation eco-ships | COP > 1, very efficient | High capital cost |

### 2.1.2 By Location of Heat Release

- **Central heating system:** Single plant serves all accommodation via ducted air handling units (AHUs).
- **Local/Unit heating system:** Individual fan-coil units (FCUs) or convector units per cabin.
- **Zone heating:** Multi-zone AHU with dampers for temperature zoning.

### 2.1.3 By Heat Transfer Mechanism

- **Convective** (forced or natural) — via air movement across heated surfaces.
- **Radiative** (rare onboard, used in galleys/wheelhouse spot heaters).
- **Combined convective-radiative** — typical fan-coil unit.

## 2.2 The Hot-Water Heating System (Most Common on Modern Ships)

The standard arrangement is as follows:

> **Auxiliary boiler (or waste-heat recovery from main engine exhaust) → Calorifier/heat exchanger → Hot-water circulation pumps → 3-way mixing valve → Heating coils in AHUs → Return to calorifier.**

The hot water is typically a **closed loop** with **expansion tank** at the highest point to accommodate thermal expansion. The temperature is regulated by a **3-way motorized mixing valve** that blends hot supply water with cooler return water to maintain a coil entering water temperature of **≈ 80–85 °C**.

### 2.2.1 The Heating Coil — Detailed Anatomy

A *finned-tube heating coil* consists of:

- **Tubes** (copper or Cu-Ni 90/10) carrying the hot water.
- **Fins** (aluminium, 0.2–0.3 mm thick, 2–4 fins per cm) increasing the external surface area.
- **Headers** (supply and return manifolds) for distribution.
- **Casing** of galvanized steel enclosing the coil bank.
- **Drain tray** below the coil to capture condensate (in cooling mode) and provide secondary cooling if the coil is used as a chilled-water coil too (a 4-pipe system).
- **Airstream** flowing across the coil at **2.5 to 3.0 m/s** face velocity.

## 2.3 Heat Load Calculation for Heating Mode

The heating load $Q_{heat}$ that the coil must supply equals the sum of all heat *losses* and *deficits* in the conditioned space:

$$
Q_{heat} \;=\; Q_{transmission} \;+\; Q_{ventilation} \;-\; Q_{internal\,gains} \;+\; Q_{reheat}
$$

Each term is computed as follows.

### 2.3.1 Transmission Heat Loss

$$
Q_{transmission} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \, A_i \, (T_{in} - T_{out})
$$

where $U_i$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A_i$ is the surface area (m²), and $(T_{in} - T_{out})$ is the indoor–outdoor temperature difference.

Typical ship values of $U$:

| Surface | U (W/m²·K) |
|:---|:---:|
| Steel bulkhead, uninsulated | 5.5 to 6.0 |
| Steel bulkhead, 50 mm mineral wool + 1 mm Al sheet | 0.7 to 0.9 |
| Steel deckhead, insulated | 0.6 to 0.8 |
| Single-glazed window | 5.0 |
| Double-glazed (thermopane) window | 2.8 |

### 2.3.2 Ventilation/Infiltration Heat Loss

$$
Q_{ventilation} \;=\; \dot{m}_{oa} \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{in} - T_{oa})
$$

where $\dot{m}_{oa}$ is the outside air mass flow (kg/s) and $T_{oa}$ is the outside design temperature (e.g., **−20 °C** for North Atlantic winter).

### 2.3.3 Internal Heat Gains

These are *subtracted* because equipment, lights, galleys, and crew contribute heat. For an empty cabin, internal gains may be just **≈ 60–100 W per person**.

### 2.3.4 Reheat Load (for dual-duct or multi-zone systems)

Some marine AHUs use a *reheat coil* downstream of a central cooling coil to provide zone-level temperature control. The reheat load is:

$$
Q_{reheat} \;=\; \dot{m}_{a} \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{supply} - T_{mix})
$$

where $T_{mix}$ is the temperature of air after the central cooling coil, and $T_{supply}$ is the final supply temperature required for the zone.

## 2.4 The Heating Coil Rating Equation

The heating capacity of a finned-tube coil is given by the classic **NTU-effectiveness** model:

$$
Q_{coil} \;=\; \varepsilon \, C_{min} \, (T_{h,in} - T_{a,in})
$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = effectiveness of the coil (typically 0.5 to 0.85 for heating).
- $C_{min}$ = the smaller of (air-side capacity rate, water-side capacity rate), in W/K.
- $T_{h,in}$ = hot fluid inlet temperature (°C).
- $T_{a,in}$ = air inlet temperature (°C).

The capacity rates are:

$$
C_{air} \;=\; \dot{m}_{a} \, c_{p,a} \qquad C_{water} \;=\; \dot{m}_{w} \, c_{p,w}
$$

The capacity ratio is:

$$
C_r \;=\; \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}
$$

For cross-flow coils with both fluids unmixed, the effectiveness is approximately:

$$
\varepsilon \;\approx\; 1 - \exp\!\left[ -\text{NTU} \, (1 - C_r) \right]
$$

with

$$
\text{NTU} \;=\; \frac{U A}{C_{min}}
$$

where $UA$ is the overall conductance of the coil.

## 2.5 Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for Coil Sizing

An alternative, more direct sizing approach uses the LMTD:

$$
Q \;=\; U A \, \Delta T_{lmtd}
$$

For a heating coil with air entering at $T_{a,in}$ and leaving at $T_{a,out}$, and water entering at $T_{w,in}$ and leaving at $T_{w,out}$:

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; \frac{(T_{w,in} - T_{a,out}) - (T_{w,out} - T_{a,in})}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_{w,in} - T_{a,out}}{T_{w,out} - T_{a,in}}\right]}
$$

This formula is the *cornerstone* of any KTU numerical problem on coil sizing.

## 2.6 Air-Side Pressure Drop Across the Heating Coil

The fan must overcome the coil resistance:

$$
\Delta P_{coil} \;=\; K_{coil} \, \frac{\rho_{a} v_{face}^{2}}{2}
$$

where $K_{coil}$ is a loss coefficient that depends on fin spacing and coil depth (typically $K \approx 2$ to $4$ for standard marine coils), and $v_{face}$ is the air face velocity (m/s).

> [!TIP]
> A high $\Delta P_{coil}$ means higher fan power. For KTU design problems, always check that $\Delta P_{coil} \le 75$ Pa for energy-efficient operation.

## 2.7 Water-Side Pressure Drop

$$
\Delta P_{water} \;=\; f \, \frac{L}{D} \, \frac{\rho_w v_w^{2}}{2} \;+\; \sum K_{fittings} \, \frac{\rho_w v_w^{2}}{2}
$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.02 for turbulent flow in copper tubes), $L$ is tube length, $D$ is tube inner diameter, $v_w$ is water velocity, and the summation includes losses from bends, valves, and headers.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $q_s = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a} \Delta T_{db}$ | Sensible heat added to air | kW |
| 2 | $Q_{heat} = Q_{trans} + Q_{vent} - Q_{int}$ | Total heating load | kW |
| 3 | $Q_{trans} = U A (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Transmission loss | W |
| 4 | $Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{oa} c_{p,a} (T_{in} - T_{oa})$ | Ventilation loss | W |
| 5 | $Q = \varepsilon C_{min} \Delta T_{max}$ | Coil capacity (ε-NTU) | W |
| 6 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Coil capacity (LMTD) | W |
| 7 | $\varepsilon \approx 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)}$ | Cross-flow effectiveness | — |
| 8 | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | Capacity ratio | — |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{lmtd} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD formula | K |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{air} = K \rho v^2 / 2$ | Air-side pressure drop | Pa |
| 11 | $\dot{Q} = K A_{coil} \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | Overall heat transfer | W |
| 12 | $P_{fan} = \dot{V} \Delta P / \eta_{fan}$ | Fan power | W |
| 13 | $\eta_{coil} = (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in})/(T_{w,in} - T_{a,in})$ | Coil temperature ratio | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Never confuse $c_{p,a}$ (≈ 1.02 kJ/kg·K for moist air) with $c_{p,w}$ (≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K for water) or $c_{p,steam}$ (≈ 2.01 kJ/kg·K for steam). Each carries its own number — *always state the value in your solution for full KTU marks*.

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

The heating design philosophy directly affects:

- **Fuel consumption** of the auxiliary boiler (≈ 5–10 % of total hotel load).
- **Emission compliance** with **MARPOL Annex VI** (SOx, NOx).
- **Corrosion rate** of structural steel in accommodation block (condensation control).
- **Crew health** (respiratory illnesses are twice as common on poorly heated vessels — MLC data).
- **Insurance premiums** for ship operators (well-designed HVAC systems reduce P&I claims).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensible Heat Ratio for a Heating-Only Process

When air is heated *without* any moisture addition or removal, the process lies on a **constant humidity ratio line** on the psychrometric chart. The total heat added equals the sensible heat added (i.e., total heat ratio = 1).

Starting from the moist-air enthalpy equation:

$$
h \;=\; c_{p,a} \, T_{db} \;+\; w \, (2501 \;+\; c_{p,v} \, T_{db})
$$

where $h$ is the enthalpy per kg of dry air (kJ/kg da), $c_{p,a} \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,v} \approx 1.86$ kJ/kg·K, and $w$ is the humidity ratio (kg water/kg da).

For an infinitesimal heating step $dT_{db}$ at constant $w$:

$$
dh \;=\; \left[ c_{p,a} \;+\; w \, c_{p,v} \right] dT_{db}
$$

But by definition, $c_{p,moist\,air} = c_{p,a} + w c_{p,v}$. For typical marine cabin conditions ($w \approx 0.008$ kg/kg):

$$
c_{p,moist} \;\approx\; 1.005 \;+\; 0.008 \times 1.86 \;\approx\; 1.020 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

Therefore, for a finite heating from state 1 to state 2:

$$
q_s \;=\; \Delta h \;=\; c_{p,moist} \, (T_{db,2} - T_{db,1})
$$

This confirms the earlier formula and gives the physical basis for using **$c_{p,a} = 1.02$ kJ/kg·K** in heating calculations.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Heating Load Calculation for a Typical Crew Cabin

> **Problem:** A crew cabin on a cargo vessel plying the North Atlantic route has the following design data. Calculate the total heating load.

| Parameter | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Cabin internal dimensions | 3.5 m × 3.0 m × 2.4 m |
| Indoor design temperature, $T_{in}$ | 22 °C |
| Outdoor design temperature, $T_{oa}$ | −15 °C |
| Steel bulkhead U-value (insulated) | 0.8 W/m²·K |
| Steel deckhead U-value (insulated) | 0.7 W/m²·K |
| Deck (floor) U-value | 0.6 W/m²·K |
| Window (double-glazed) | 2.8 W/m²·K |
| Window area | 1.5 m² |
| Outside air supply | 30 m³/h |
| Number of occupants | 2 |
| Heat gain per person | 75 W |
| Lighting + equipment gain | 80 W |

### Step 1 — Compute Areas

External surface area of the cabin is approximated as 6 surfaces minus the door area (assume door has same U as bulkhead and is included):

$$
A_{walls} \;=\; 2 \times (3.5 + 3.0) \times 2.4 \;=\; 31.2 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{ceiling} \;=\; 3.5 \times 3.0 \;=\; 10.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{floor} \;=\; 3.5 \times 3.0 \;=\; 10.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{window} \;=\; 1.5 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{subtracted from walls})
$$

Effective wall area (excluding window):

$$
A_{walls,\,eff} \;=\; 31.2 - 1.5 \;=\; 29.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Transmission Heat Loss

Using $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{oa} = 22 - (-15) = 37$ K:

$$
Q_{trans} \;=\; (U A)_{walls} \Delta T \;+\; (U A)_{ceil} \Delta T \;+\; (U A)_{floor} \Delta T \;+\; (U A)_{win} \Delta T
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{trans} \;=\;& (0.8 \times 29.7) \times 37 \;+\; (0.7 \times 10.5) \times 37 \;+\; (0.6 \times 10.5) \times 37 \;+\; (2.8 \times 1.5) \times 37 \\[4pt]
=\;& 23.76 \times 37 \;+\; 7.35 \times 37 \;+\; 6.30 \times 37 \;+\; 4.20 \times 37 \\[4pt]
=\;& 879.12 \;+\; 271.95 \;+\; 233.10 \;+\; 155.40 \\[4pt]
=\;& 1539.57 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 1.54 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Ventilation Heat Loss

Convert volume flow to mass flow using $\rho_{oa} = 1.40$ kg/m³ (at −15 °C, this is actually lower, ≈ 1.37 kg/m³, but for KTU board exam we use 1.40 for simplicity):

$$
\dot{V}_{oa} \;=\; \frac{30}{3600} \;=\; 0.00833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oa} \;=\; 0.00833 \times 1.40 \;=\; 0.01167 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{vent} \;=\; \dot{m}_{oa} \, c_{p,a} \, \Delta T \;=\; 0.01167 \times 1.02 \times 37
$$

$$
Q_{vent} \;=\; 0.01167 \times 37.74 \;\approx\; 0.4404 \text{ kW} \;\approx\; 0.44 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Internal Heat Gains (Subtract)

$$
Q_{int} \;=\; 2 \times 75 \;+\; 80 \;=\; 230 \text{ W} \;=\; 0.23 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Heating Load

$$
Q_{heat} \;=\; Q_{trans} \;+\; Q_{vent} \;-\; Q_{int}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{heat} \;=\;& 1.54 \;+\; 0.44 \;-\; 0.23 \\[4pt]
=\;& 1.75 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Each crew cabin on this North Atlantic cargo ship requires about 1.75 kW of heating. For an accommodation block with 30 cabins, the central AHU must deliver **≈ 52.5 kW** of heating. Add 10 % safety margin → **58 kW**, which would typically be supplied by hot water at 80 °C flowing through a 6-row finned coil.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Heating Coil Sizing Using LMTD

> **Problem:** An AHU supplies 4.0 m³/s of air at 10 °C DB and must heat it to 32 °C DB. The coil uses hot water entering at 85 °C and leaving at 70 °C. Compute the LMTD, heat duty, and required $UA$ if the overall heat-transfer coefficient is $U = 50$ W/m²·K.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four End-Point Temperatures

- $T_{a,in}$ = 10 °C (cold inlet)
- $T_{a,out}$ = 32 °C (cold outlet)
- $T_{w,in}$ = 85 °C (hot inlet)
- $T_{w,out}$ = 70 °C (hot outlet)

### Step 2 — Compute the Two End Temperature Differences

Counter-flow orientation is assumed for LMTD (more conservative):

$$
\Delta T_1 \;=\; T_{w,in} - T_{a,out} \;=\; 85 - 32 \;=\; 53 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 \;=\; T_{w,out} - T_{a,in} \;=\; 70 - 10 \;=\; 60 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the LMTD

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; \frac{53 - 60}{\ln(53 / 60)} \;=\; \frac{-7}{\ln(0.8833)} \;=\; \frac{-7}{-0.1242}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; 56.36 \text{ K}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Heat Duty $Q$

Using air-side data ($\rho_a = 1.20$ kg/m³ at standard conditions):

$$
\dot{m}_a \;=\; 4.0 \times 1.20 \;=\; 4.8 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in}) \;=\; 4.8 \times 1.02 \times (32 - 10)
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 4.8 \times 1.02 \times 22 \;=\; 107.7 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Required Coil Area

$$
A_{coil} \;=\; \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{lmtd}} \;=\; \frac{107{,}700}{50 \times 56.36}
$$

$$
A_{coil} \;=\; \frac{107{,}700}{2818} \;\approx\; 38.22 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A real marine coil with 6 rows, 12 fins/inch, and copper tubes would need a face area of about 1.2 m × 1.5 m = 1.8 m². The required $A = 38.2$ m² is the *total* external fin area, which is much larger than face area because of the fin enhancement (typically 12× to 16× enhancement ratio).

### Step 6 — Cross-check Using ε-NTU Method

Mass flow of water (assume water mass flow gives $C_r \approx 0.6$):

$$
\dot{m}_w \;=\; \frac{Q}{c_{p,w} (T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})} \;=\; \frac{107{,}700}{4.18 \times 15} \;=\; 1.717 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
C_{air} \;=\; 4.8 \times 1020 \;=\; 4896 \text{ W/K}
$$

$$
C_{water} \;=\; 1.717 \times 4180 \;=\; 7178 \text{ W/K}
$$

$$
C_{min} \;=\; 4896 \text{ W/K} \quad \text{(air is the minimum)}
$$

$$
C_r \;=\; \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \;=\; \frac{4896}{7178} \;=\; 0.6821
$$

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{T_{a,out} - T_{a,in}}{T_{w,in} - T_{a,in}} \;=\; \frac{22}{85 - 10} \;=\; \frac{22}{75} \;=\; 0.2933
$$

This low effectiveness is typical for large central heating coils. The NTU can be back-calculated, but for board exams the LMTD method is sufficient.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Remedies

| No. | Symptom | Probable Cause | Remedy |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Cold cabin in winter | Pump failure, air lock in coil, valve stuck | Check pump, vent air, replace actuator |
| 2 | Overheating in mild weather | 3-way valve stuck open, sensor failure | Calibrate sensor, replace actuator |
| 3 | Cold-air draft from ducts | Fan off, filter blocked, duct leak | Service fan, replace filter, seal duct |
| 4 | Condensation on bulkheads | Heating insufficient, dew point too high | Increase supply T, add insulation |
| 5 | Boiler lockout in cold weather | Low water level, soot accumulation | Refill, soot-blow, water treatment |
| 6 | Uneven cabin temperatures | Poor balancing, blocked balancing valve | Re-balance using Tab method |

## 3.5 Derivations — Quick Reference Equations

### 3.5.1 Effectiveness of a Cross-Flow Coil (Both Fluids Unmixed)

Starting from the basic differential heat-balance equation:

$$
dQ \;=\; -C_a \, dT_a \;=\; C_w \, (-dT_w) \;=\; U \, dA \, (T_w - T_a)
$$

After algebraic manipulation (substituting $d\theta = (T_a - T_{a,in})/(T_{w,in} - T_{a,in})$ and integrating), we get:

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left\{ -\text{NTU} \left[ 1 - \exp(-\text{NTU} \, C_r) \right] / C_r \right\}
$$

For the simpler unmixed/unmixed case used in board exams, the approximation given in §2.4 is acceptable.

### 3.5.2 Fin Efficiency

For a circular fin of outer radius $r_o$ and inner radius $r_i$:

$$
\eta_{fin} \;=\; \frac{\tanh(m r_c \phi)}{m r_c \phi}
$$

where $m = \sqrt{h / (k \delta)}$, $r_c = r_o - \delta/2$ (corrected radius), and $\phi = (r_o/r_c - 1)[1 + 0.35 \ln(r_o/r_c)]$. Typical fin efficiency for marine aluminium fins is **0.85 to 0.92**.

### 3.5.3 Overall Surface Efficiency

$$
\eta_o \;=\; 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t} (1 - \eta_{fin})
$$

where $A_f$ is fin area and $A_t$ is total external area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final equation used in KTU numerical problems:**
>
> $$
> Q \;=\; \eta_o \, U_o \, A_o \, \Delta T_{lmtd}
> $$
>
> This is the **only form that should appear in your answer script**, combining the overall heat-transfer coefficient, the corrected LMTD, and the overall surface efficiency into a single working equation.

## 4.3 Psychrometric Process Diagram — Heating Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point 1 Outside Air 5 deg C 90 percent RH] -->|Mixing with Recirc| B[Point M Mixed Air 12 deg C]
    B -->|Heating across coil| C[Point 2 Supply Air 32 deg C]

    D -->|Return| E[Return Air Plenum]
    E -->|Mix| B
    A -.partial bypass.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Air enters the AHU cold (point 1 or 2 depending on recirculation mix), is heated across the finned coil (M → 2) without any change in moisture content, then enters the cabin (point 3). The line from M to 2 is a horizontal line moving to the right on the psychrometric chart, confirming the constant-humidity-ratio nature of heating.
