# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the term “sensible heating” as applied to marine air-conditioning. Sketch the process on a psychrometric chart and write the governing equation.*

**Model Answer:**

Sensible heating is the process of raising the dry-bulb temperature of moist air *without* changing its moisture content (humidity ratio $w$ remains constant). On the psychrometric chart, the process is a horizontal line moving to the right.

Governing equation:

$$
q_s \;=\; \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,moist} \, (T_{db,2} - T_{db,1}) \quad \text{where } c_{p,moist} \approx 1.02 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*List any four sources of heat supply commonly used for shipboard heating systems and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Auxiliary boiler steam** — high heat capacity, fast response.
2. **Engine jacket cooling water via calorifier** — recycles waste heat, fuel-efficient.
3. **Electric resistance heaters** — precise control, no combustion exhaust.
4. **Diesel-fired unit heaters** — independent of central plant, suitable for engine rooms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** *A 30-cabin accommodation block on a North Atlantic cargo ship has the following data. Calculate the total heating load for the entire block.*

- Indoor design temperature: 22 °C
- Outdoor design temperature: −20 °C
- Each cabin transmission loss (envelope + window): 1.4 kW
- Outside air supply per cabin: 35 m³/h
- Internal gains per cabin: 200 W
- $\rho_{oa}$ = 1.40 kg/m³; $c_{p,a}$ = 1.02 kJ/kg·K

**(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Transmission loss per cabin (given):**
$Q_{trans,cabin} = 1.4 \text{ kW}$

**Step 2 — Ventilation loss per cabin:**

$$
\dot{V}_{oa} \;=\; \frac{35}{3600} \;=\; 0.00972 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{oa} \;=\; 0.00972 \times 1.40 \;=\; 0.01361 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 22 - (-20) \;=\; 42 \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q_{vent,cabin} \;=\; 0.01361 \times 1.02 \times 42 \;=\; 0.583 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Net load per cabin:**

$$
Q_{net,cabin} \;=\; 1.4 \;+\; 0.583 \;-\; 0.200 \;=\; 1.783 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Total for 30 cabins:**

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; 30 \times 1.783 \;=\; 53.5 \text{ kW}
$$

Add 10 % safety margin:

$$
Q_{design} \;=\; 53.5 \times 1.10 \;\approx\; 58.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total heating load ≈ **58.8 kW**.

**(b)** *A heating coil supplied with hot water at 85 °C and returning at 70 °C is used in the AHU. The coil must deliver 58.8 kW while heating 4.5 m³/s of air from 5 °C to 30 °C DB. Calculate the LMTD and the required coil area if $U = 55$ W/m²·K. State whether the chosen capacity is realistic.* **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_1 \;=\; 85 - 30 \;=\; 55 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 \;=\; 70 - 5 \;=\; 65 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; \frac{55 - 65}{\ln(55/65)} \;=\; \frac{-10}{\ln(0.8462)} \;=\; \frac{-10}{-0.1671}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; 59.85 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute required coil area:**

$$
A_{coil} \;=\; \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{lmtd}} \;=\; \frac{58{,}800}{55 \times 59.85}
$$

$$
A_{coil} \;=\; \frac{58{,}800}{3291.75} \;\approx\; 17.86 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Reality check:**

For a face area of 1.5 m × 1.5 m = 2.25 m² and 6 rows of fins, the total external surface area is typically 16–20 m². So the chosen coil is *just* adequate; a 7-row coil would provide a 15 % margin.

**Final Answer:** LMTD ≈ 59.85 K; coil area ≈ 17.86 m²; marginally adequate, recommend 7-row coil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a hot-water central heating system used on board ships. List the major components and state the function of each.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

A hot-water central heating system consists of:

1. **Heat source** — auxiliary boiler or waste-heat recovery unit providing primary energy.
2. **Calorifier** — indirect heat exchanger transferring heat to the closed water loop without contamination.
3. **Circulation pumps** — primary and standby, providing forced circulation.
4. **Expansion tank** — accommodates thermal expansion of water.
5. **3-way motorized mixing valve** — modulates supply water temperature by blending hot supply with cool return.
6. **Heating coils in AHUs** — finned-tube heat exchangers transferring heat to the air stream.
7. **Piping** — supply and return headers, balancing valves, strainers.
8. **Controls** — temperature sensors, controllers (PID), actuators.
9. **Safety devices** — pressure relief valves, air vents, low-water cutoff.

Working: water is heated in calorifier → pumped through supply header → modulated by 3-way valve → enters AHU coils → transfers heat to air → returns cooler to calorifier.

**(b)** *A marine AHU has a heating coil with the following data:*  
*Air flow: 3.5 m³/s, air heated from 8 °C to 28 °C DB.*  
*Hot water enters at 90 °C and leaves at 65 °C.*  
*Overall heat-transfer coefficient: U = 48 W/m²·K.*  
*Compute: (i) heat duty, (ii) LMTD, (iii) coil area, (iv) water mass flow rate, (v) air-side pressure drop if $K = 2.8$ and face area = 1.4 m².* **(7 Marks)**

**(i) Heat duty:**

$$
\dot{m}_a \;=\; 3.5 \times 1.20 \;=\; 4.2 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 4.2 \times 1.02 \times (28 - 8) \;=\; 4.2 \times 1.02 \times 20 \;=\; 85.68 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_1 \;=\; 90 - 28 \;=\; 62 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 \;=\; 65 - 8 \;=\; 57 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; \frac{62 - 57}{\ln(62/57)} \;=\; \frac{5}{\ln(1.0877)} \;=\; \frac{5}{0.08412}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lmtd} \;=\; 59.44 \text{ K}
$$

**(iii) Coil area:**

$$
A_{coil} \;=\; \frac{85{,}680}{48 \times 59.44} \;=\; \frac{85{,}680}{2853.1} \;\approx\; 30.03 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(iv) Water mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_w \;=\; \frac{Q}{c_{p,w} \, \Delta T_w} \;=\; \frac{85{,}680}{4.18 \times 25} \;=\; 0.8199 \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 0.82 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(v) Air-side pressure drop:**

Face velocity:

$$
v_{face} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{1.4} \;=\; 2.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; K \times \frac{\rho_a v^2}{2} \;=\; 2.8 \times \frac{1.20 \times 2.5^2}{2} \;=\; 2.8 \times \frac{7.5}{2} \;=\; 2.8 \times 3.75
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; 10.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

This is well below the 75 Pa design limit → coil selection is energy-efficient.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board):**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign in heat-loss equation** — Students often compute $T_{oa} - T_{in}$ instead of $T_{in} - T_{oa}$, giving a *negative* heating load. Always state the design direction.
> 2. **Using $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ instead of 1.02** — KTU accepts either, but the answer changes by ~1.5 %. Always write which value you used.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract internal gains** — many students omit $-Q_{int}$, leading to an oversized system.
> 4. **Confusing transmission loss with total load** — Question A.1(a) of every KTU paper asks for *total* load, not transmission alone.
> 5. **Mixing up steam and water enthalpy** — in steam-heated systems, use $h_{fg} \approx 2100$ kJ/kg at 0.2 MPa, not $c_p \Delta T$.
> 6. **LMTD sign errors** — keep $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ or take absolute values; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for inconsistent sign convention.
> 7. **Skipping units** — every numerical answer without units loses 1 mark automatically.
> 8. **Ignoring safety factor** — KTU expects a 10–15 % safety margin on the final design load.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Heating is a *sensible-only* process at constant humidity ratio; on the psychrometric chart it is a horizontal line moving right.
- **Standard cabin temperature:** 22 °C ± 1 °C DB, 50 % ± 5 % RH.
- **Most common system on modern ships:** hot-water central heating via calorifier heated by engine jacket water or auxiliary boiler.
- **Key heat sources:** boiler steam, jacket cooling water, electric heaters, waste-heat recovery, heat pumps.
- **Heat-load formula:** $Q_{heat} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{ventilation} - Q_{internal\,gains}$.
- **Transmission equation:** $Q_{trans} = U A \Delta T$.
- **Ventilation equation:** $Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{oa} c_{p,a} \Delta T$.
- **Coil-rating equations:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ (preferred) or $Q = \varepsilon C_{min} \Delta T_{max}$ (alternative).
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- **Standard coil design data:** face velocity 2.5–3.0 m/s, air-off max 45 °C, water supply 80–90 °C, water return 60–70 °C.
- **Air-side pressure drop:** should not exceed ~75 Pa for energy efficiency.
- **Always subtract internal gains** from total heating requirement.
- **Apply 10–15 % safety margin** to the final design load.
- **Maximum air-off temperature** limited to **45 °C** to prevent duct damage.
- **Hot-water loop** must include expansion tank, safety relief, and air vents.
- **3-way mixing valve** is the primary control element for modulating heating capacity.
- **Fin efficiency** $\eta_{fin} \approx 0.85$–$0.92$ for marine aluminium fins.
- **Overall surface efficiency** $\eta_o = 1 - (A_f/A_t)(1 - \eta_{fin})$.
- **Regulations:** MLC 2006, IMO Habitability, ILO Convention 92, SOLAS Ch. II-2 fire safety for heaters.
- **Realistic coil area:** for a typical marine AHU (~3–5 m³/s), coil external area is 15–40 m².
- **Combustion safety:** diesel-fired unit heaters must have automatic flame-failure shutoff.
- **Insulation:** minimum 50 mm mineral wool with aluminium cladding on accommodation bulkheads.
- **Common failure modes:** pump failure, valve stuck, sensor drift, filter blockage, air lock.
- **Energy savings:** always prefer waste-heat recovery over boiler-fed heating if available.
- **Quick numerical sanity check:** typical cabin heating load = 1.5–2.0 kW in cold climates.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Hook:** If you remember only three equations, remember these:
> 1. $Q = \dot{m}_a c_p \Delta T$
> 2. $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd}$
> 3. $\Delta T_{lmtd} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$
>
> These three equations alone will solve 80 % of KTU heating numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 – Heat Sources in Shipboard Refrigeration & Air Conditioning

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Sources** on a ship refer to the **aggregate of all thermal energy inputs** (sensible and latent) that must be **rejected by the refrigeration and air-conditioning plant** in order to maintain the prescribed *dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, and air quality* within the conditioned spaces (accommodation blocks, wheelhouse, ECR, galleys, refrigerated cargo chambers, etc.) as stipulated by classification societies and IMO standards.

In the context of **PENST743 (Refrigeration and Air Conditioning of Ships)**, the heat load calculation forms the foundational step in **plant sizing** — without an accurate heat-source inventory, the compressor, condenser, evaporator, and ductwork cannot be correctly specified. Every refrigeration engineer, therefore, must first **identify, quantify, and classify** every contributing heat source before proceeding to equipment selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology:**
> - **Sensible Heat (SH):** Heat that changes the *temperature* of air without changing its moisture content.
> - **Latent Heat (LH):** Heat that changes the *moisture content* (phase change of water vapour) at constant temperature.
> - **Total Heat (TH) = SH + LH** — measured in **kW** or **TR (Tonnes of Refrigeration, where 1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweating Ship" Story

Imagine you are locked inside a glass-walled cabin on a hot summer afternoon. Sunlight streams in through the roof and one wall. Three electric bulbs burn overhead. A small kitchen stove operates in one corner. Four people sit inside, breathing and perspiring. Fresh air is pumped in from outside (which is also hot and humid).

Even if the cabin had **no mechanical refrigeration**, the temperature inside would continuously rise because heat keeps *entering* the cabin from multiple directions simultaneously:

| Real-World Element | Shipboard Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sunlight through glass | Solar gain through deckhead, hull plating, and windows |
| Electric bulbs | Lighting circuits (fluorescent, LED, halogen) |
| Stove heat | Galley cooking equipment, ovens, dishwashers |
| People breathing/sweating | Crew occupants (sensible + latent body heat) |
| Hot outside air infiltration | Ventilation air & door-opening losses |
| Walls getting warm | Heat conduction through ship's steel bulkheads |

> The **refrigeration plant** is essentially the device that **"mops the floor"** of all this entering heat — it cannot reduce *only one* source; it must handle the **sum total** of all heat sources.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are critical for heat-load calculations and are **frequently tested in KTU examinations**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in Heat-Load Calculations**
> - **Specific heat of air, cₚ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K (sensible heat calculation)**
> - **Specific heat of water, cₚw = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**
> - **Latent heat of vaporisation of water, h_fg = 2501 kJ/kg (at 0 °C)**
> - **Density of standard air, ρ = 1.20 kg/m³ (at 20 °C, 101.325 kPa)**
> - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr**
> - **Solar constant (outside atmosphere) ≈ 1.367 kW/m²**
> - **Mean transmittance of ship window glass ≈ 0.84 (for direct beam), 0.91 (for diffuse)**
> - **Body heat — sensible: ~60–70 W per person (light work); latent: ~55–70 W per person**
> - **U-value (overall heat-transfer coefficient) of typical ship bulkhead ≈ 1.5–4.0 W/m²·K**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Heat Sources — The KTU Taxonomy

For KTU 2024 scheme examinations, heat sources are systematically classified into **two broad categories** with several sub-categories:

### A. External Heat Sources (from outside the conditioned space)
1. **Heat conduction** through ship structure (hull, decks, bulkheads exposed to sea/sun)
2. **Solar radiation** through transparent and semi-transparent surfaces
3. **Ventilation / Infiltration air** (fresh air makeup)
4. **Re-radiation** from adjacent unconditioned spaces

### B. Internal Heat Sources (generated inside the conditioned space)
1. **Occupants** (sensible + latent body heat)
2. **Lighting** (fluorescent, incandescent, LED with drivers)
3. **Electrical equipment** (motors, servers, instruments, radars in wheelhouse)
4. **Galley & Pantry equipment** (cooking ranges, ovens, dishwashers)
5. **Steam and hot-fluid piping** passing through the space
6. **Refrigerated cargo heat in-leak** (for reefer ships)
7. **Miscellaneous** (fan motor heat in ductwork, cleaning steam, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> The student is expected to be able to **(i) enumerate all sources**, **(ii) compute the heat contribution** of each, and **(iii) sum them up to arrive at the total refrigeration tonnage** required for the shipboard HVAC plant.

---

## 1.5 Why Heat-Source Identification Matters in Marine Practice

In shipboard practice, heat sources are not "static" — they vary dramatically based on:

- **Latitude of vessel operation** (tropical passages = massive solar gain; polar passages = conduction dominant)
- **Time of day** (peak solar load occurs between 11:00 and 15:00 local time)
- **Hull colour** (dark hulls absorb up to 90 % of incident radiation; light/aluminium paints reflect more)
- **Number of crew and passengers** (warships may have 200–300 personnel; passenger ships 1000+)
- **Operational mode** (galley "full cooking" vs. "stand-by")

> A wrong heat-load estimate of just **10 %** can result in an undersized plant — leading to **compressor overloading, high condensing pressures, frequent cut-outs, and uncomfortable conditions**. This is why KTU examiners place strong emphasis on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Heat-Transfer Fundamentals Behind Each Source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All heat-source calculations in Module 3 are rooted in **three modes of heat transfer**: **Conduction, Convection, and Radiation**. Every formula in the cheat-sheet below can be derived from one of these three fundamental modes.

### 2.1.1 Conduction Through Ship Structure

Heat flows from the warmer side (sea/sun-exposed shell) to the cooler side (interior) through a **composite wall** — typically: outer steel plate → insulation → inner lining.

The rate of heat transfer is governed by **Fourier's Law**:

$$q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{1000} \quad \text{(in kW)}$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = surface area (**m²**)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across the structure (**K or °C**)
- The factor 1000 converts **W → kW**

> [!NOTE]
> The **overall U-value** for a composite wall is computed as:
> $$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_k}{k_k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$
> where $h_i$ and $h_o$ are the **inside and outside surface-film coefficients**, $x_k$ is the thickness of layer $k$, and $k_k$ is its thermal conductivity.

### 2.1.2 Radiation Through Transparent Surfaces

Solar radiation is the **largest single contributor** in tropical waters. It is split into:

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Direct beam radiation** | Sun's rays directly incident on the surface |
| **Diffuse sky radiation** | Scattered radiation from sky and clouds |
| **Reflected (ground) radiation** | Radiation bounced off sea surface |

For shipboard calculations, **solar heat gain through windows** is given by:

$$Q_{solar} = A_{glass} \times SHGF \times SC \times CLF$$

where:
- $SHGF$ = **Solar Heat Gain Factor** (W/m²), depends on latitude, orientation, month
- $SC$ = **Shading Coefficient** of the glazing
- $CLF$ = **Cooling Load Factor** (accounts for thermal storage lag in the structure)

> For walls and opaque deckheads exposed to sun, **simplified equivalent temperature-difference (ETD)** method is used:
> $$Q_{wall} = A \cdot U \cdot \Delta T_{eq}$$
> where $\Delta T_{eq}$ is the **equivalent temperature difference** (sum of outdoor–indoor dry-bulb difference and solar radiation equivalent).

### 2.1.3 Convection from Ventilation Air

$$Q_{vent} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \dot{m} \cdot (W_o - W_i) \cdot h_{fg}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of outside air (**kg/s**)
- $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between outdoor and indoor air
- $W_o, W_i$ = humidity ratios (kg water / kg dry air) outside and inside
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation (≈ 2501 kJ/kg)

> Simplification — using volumetric flow rate $V$ in m³/s:
> $$Q_{vent} = 1.2 \cdot V \cdot [1.005 \cdot \Delta T + 2501 \cdot \Delta W] \;\;(\text{kW})$$

### 2.1.4 Internal Heat Generation

**(a) Occupants:** Use ASHRAE tables; values depend on activity level.

| Activity | Sensible Heat (W/person) | Latent Heat (W/person) | Total (W/person) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seated, quiet (cabin) | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| Light work (engine room) | 70 | 80 | 150 |
| Heavy work (galley) | 90 | 180 | 270 |

$$Q_{occ} = n \cdot q_{person} \quad (\text{W})$$

**(b) Lighting:** For fluorescent fittings, **all electrical input becomes heat** in the conditioned space.

$$Q_{light} = P_{light} \times F_{use} \times F_{ballast}$$

- $F_{use}$ = usage factor (0.4–1.0)
- $F_{ballast}$ = ballast factor (≈ 1.2 for fluorescent; ≈ 1.0 for LED)

**(c) Electric Motors & Equipment:**

$$Q_{motor} = P_{rated} \times \eta \times (1 - \eta)$$

For motors **inside** the conditioned space, all losses appear as heat; for motors **outside**, only the shaft-work portion is excluded.

**(d) Galley Equipment:** Manufacturers publish heat-emission data. Typical values:

| Appliance | Heat Output (W) |
|---|---|
| Electric range (per burner) | 1500–2000 |
| Convection oven | 1500–3000 |
| Deep fryer | 1500–2500 |
| Dishwasher | 1500–2500 |
| Refrigerator (heat rejected to room) | 200–500 |
| Coffee urn | 750–1500 |

---

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for Module 3 (Heat Sources). Memorise these — they appear in **at least one 14-mark Part B question** every KTU exam cycle.

| # | Heat Source | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction through structure | $Q = U A \Delta T \,/\, 1000$ (kW) | $U$: W/m²·K, $A$: m², $\Delta T$: K |
| 2 | Composite wall U-value | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_k}{k_k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | $h$: W/m²·K, $x$: m, $k$: W/m·K |
| 3 | Solar through windows | $Q = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$ | $A$: m², others dimensionless/W/m² |
| 4 | Solar on walls/decks (ETD) | $Q = A \cdot U \cdot \Delta T_{eq}$ | All in SI |
| 5 | Ventilation (sensible + latent) | $Q = 1.2 V [1.005 \Delta T + 2501 \Delta W]$ | $V$: m³/s, $\Delta W$: kg/kg |
| 6 | Occupants | $Q = n \cdot q_{person}$ | $n$: number, $q$: W/person |
| 7 | Lighting | $Q = P \cdot F_{use} \cdot F_{ballast}$ | $P$: W, $F$: dimensionless |
| 8 | Motor in conditioned space | $Q = P \cdot (1 - \eta)$ | $P$: W, $\eta$: efficiency |
| 9 | Pipe / steam line | $Q = U_{pipe} \cdot A_{pipe} \cdot \Delta T$ | All SI |
| 10 | Total heat load | $Q_{total} = \sum Q_i + Q_{safety\,margin}$ | Add 5–10 % safety factor |
| 11 | Plant capacity (TR) | $TR = \frac{Q_{total}\,\text{(kW)}}{3.517}$ | — |
| 12 | Air-mass flow for given load | $\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{sens}}{c_p \Delta T}$ | $\dot{m}$: kg/s |

> **Pitfall Note:** Never confuse **W (watts)** with **kW**. Always carry units in intermediate steps — KTU examiners often deduct a full mark for missing unit conversions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The heat-source calculation method discussed here is the **exact same methodology** used by:

1. **Marine HVAC designers** (Wärtsilä, ABB, Alfa Laval, Carrier Marine) for specifying chillers and air-handling units (AHUs) on cruise ships, naval vessels, and offshore platforms.
2. **DNV / Lloyd's Register / ABS** approved plant-sizing calculations for SOLAS-compliant accommodation ventilation.
3. **ASHRAE Handbook (Marine Applications)** as the global reference for shipboard cooling-load estimation.
4. **Classification society energy-efficiency indices (EEDI, EEXI)** — accurate heat-load data feeds into the ship's overall energy balance.

> The student who masters this module essentially masters the **first half** of any refrigeration-plant design problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Conduction Through Composite Bulkhead

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
A ship's accommodation bulkhead has the following layered construction, from outside (sea-side, ambient = 35 °C, surface coefficient $h_o$ = 23 W/m²·K) to inside (cabin side, ambient = 24 °C, surface coefficient $h_i$ = 9 W/m²·K):

| Layer | Material | Thickness $x$ (mm) | Thermal conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Steel plate | 8 | 45 |
| 2 | Mineral wool insulation | 50 | 0.04 |
| 3 | Aluminium sheet (inner lining) | 3 | 205 |

The bulkhead has area $A$ = 30 m². Calculate the **heat conduction rate** $Q$ in kW.

### Step 1 — Compute Resistances per Unit Area

The thermal resistance of each layer $R_k$ is given by:

$$R_k = \frac{x_k}{k_k}$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{0.008}{45} = 1.778 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_2 &= \frac{0.050}{0.04} = 1.250 \;\;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_3 &= \frac{0.003}{205} = 1.463 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Add Surface-Film Resistances

The outside and inside air-films have resistances $1/h_o$ and $1/h_i$ respectively:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{i,film} &= \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{9} = 0.1111 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_{o,film} &= \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{23} = 0.04348 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Resistance and U-value

$$R_{total} = R_{i,film} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{o,film}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 0.1111 + 1.778\times 10^{-4} + 1.250 + 1.463\times 10^{-5} + 0.04348 \\
&= 1.40478 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the overall heat-transfer coefficient is:

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.40478} = 0.7119 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The mineral-wool insulation layer dominates the thermal resistance — it accounts for ~89 % of $R_{total}$. The steel plate is so conductive that it contributes negligibly. This is why **proper insulation thickness** is the single most effective way to reduce conduction heat gain.

### Step 4 — Apply Fourier's Law to Compute Heat Transfer Rate

The temperature difference across the bulkhead is:

$$\Delta T = 35 - 24 = 11 \;\; \text{K}$$

Heat transfer rate:

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{1000}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.7119 \times 30 \times 11}{1000}$$

$$Q = \frac{234.93}{1000} = 0.2349 \;\; \text{kW} \approx 235 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Result:** Heat conduction through the bulkhead = **0.235 kW** = **0.067 TR**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Ventilation / Infiltration Heat Load

**Problem Setup:**
An accommodation block on a ship receives **1500 m³/h** of fresh outside air. Outside conditions: 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT. Inside design conditions: 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH. Compute the total heat load due to ventilation.

### Step 1 — Determine Humidity Ratios

From psychrometric chart (or formulas):
- Outside air at 35 °C DBT / 28 °C WBT: $W_o = 0.0206$ kg/kg
- Inside air at 24 °C DBT / 50 % RH: $W_i = 0.0094$ kg/kg

### Step 2 — Convert Volume Flow to Mass Flow

Volumetric flow rate:

$$V = \frac{1500}{3600} = 0.4167 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Mass flow rate (using ρ = 1.20 kg/m³):

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot V = 1.20 \times 0.4167 = 0.500 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Sensible Heat Component

$$Q_{sens} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sens} = 0.500 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 24)$$

$$Q_{sens} = 0.500 \times 1.005 \times 11 = 5.528 \;\; \text{kW}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Latent Heat Component

$$Q_{lat} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$$

$$Q_{lat} = 0.500 \times 2501 \times (0.0206 - 0.0094)$$

$$Q_{lat} = 0.500 \times 2501 \times 0.0112 = 14.006 \;\; \text{kW}$$

### Step 5 — Total Ventilation Heat Load

$$Q_{vent} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 5.528 + 14.006 = 19.534 \;\; \text{kW}$$

In tonnes of refrigeration:

$$TR = \frac{19.534}{3.517} = 5.55 \;\; \text{TR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Latent heat dominates (≈ 72 % of total ventilation load) in tropical, humid conditions. This is why dehumidification (via chilled-water coils or desiccant wheels) is just as important as sensible cooling on ships operating in tropical waters.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Solar Heat Gain Through a Wheelhouse Window

**Problem Setup:**
A ship's wheelhouse has a **single forward window** of area 4 m². Latitude of operation: 15 °N (tropical). The window faces forward (orientation = vertical, sun-facing). For a vertical surface at 15 °N latitude in summer at solar noon, $SHGF$ ≈ 320 W/m². The glass is single-pane with $SC = 0.95$. The CLF (cooling load factor, accounting for thermal storage in the surrounding structure) is 0.65.

### Step 1 — Apply the Solar Gain Formula

$$Q_{solar} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$$

$$Q_{solar} = 4 \times 320 \times 0.95 \times 0.65$$

$$Q_{solar} = 790.4 \;\; \text{W} \approx 0.79 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> **Result:** A single 4 m² window contributes 0.79 kW (= 0.225 TR) of solar heat — purely *sensible* (the sun's radiation does not directly add moisture, though window surface heating re-radiates to indoor surfaces).

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Combined Heat-Load Summary for an Accommodation Block

A typical KTU 14-mark question requires the student to compute a **complete heat-load inventory**. The following table summarises a representative calculation for a **single officer's cabin** on a ship operating in tropical waters:

| # | Heat Source | Magnitude | kW |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction through external bulkhead | (from §3.1) | 0.235 |
| 2 | Conduction through deckhead (sun-exposed) | $A$=12 m², $U$=0.6, $\Delta T_{eq}$=25 K | 0.180 |
| 3 | Conduction through deck (below sun deck) | $A$=10 m², $U$=0.5, $\Delta T$=12 K | 0.060 |
| 4 | Solar gain through one porthole (0.5 m²) | 0.5 × 320 × 0.95 × 0.65 | 0.099 |
| 5 | Ventilation air (30 m³/h per person) | (proportional to §3.2) | 0.390 |
| 6 | Occupant (1 person, seated) | 100 W | 0.100 |
| 7 | Lighting (2 × 40 W fluorescent) | $2 \times 40 \times 1.0 \times 1.2$ | 0.096 |
| 8 | Small fan motor (50 W) | $50 \times (1 - 0.6)$ | 0.020 |
| 9 | TV / laptop (100 W) | All to room | 0.100 |
| | **Subtotal** | | **1.280** |
| 10 | Safety factor (10 %) | $1.280 \times 0.10$ | 0.128 |
| | **Total Design Load** | | **1.408 kW** |

Plant capacity required for this single cabin:

$$TR = \frac{1.408}{3.517} = 0.40 \;\; \text{TR}$$

> A typical **300-person accommodation block** would then require roughly $300 \times 0.40 = 120$ TR (plus 30–50 % for galley, mess rooms, public spaces, and re-radiation losses) — a plant of **~180 TR** is reasonable for a mid-sized naval vessel or small passenger ship.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Heat Sources by Space Type

The following matrix summarises which heat sources dominate in different shipboard spaces:

| Heat Source | Cabin | Galley | ECR / Wheelhouse | Engine Room Office | Reefer Chamber |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction through walls | M | M | M | M | H |
| Solar through windows | M | M | H | L | L |
| Ventilation | M | H | M | M | L |
| Occupants | M | M | L | L | L |
| Lighting | L | M | M | M | L |
| Equipment / motors | L | L | H | M | H |
| Cooking | — | H | — | — | — |
| Steam/hot pipes | L | M | M | H | — |
| Cargo heat in-leak | — | — | — | — | H |

*(Legend: H = High dominance, M = Medium, L = Low)*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Heat-Source Aggregation

The following **block-level functional flow** maps how individual heat sources flow into the **total refrigeration load** and onwards to the **plant specification**. This diagram is a **board-exam-grade schematic** that students should reproduce during valuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Heat Sources] --> A1[Conduction through Hull / Bulkhead / Deck]
    A --> A2[Solar Radiation through Windows]
    A --> A3[Ventilation / Infiltration Air]
    A --> A4[Re-radiation from Adjacent Spaces]

    B[Internal Heat Sources] --> B1[Occupants - SH plus LH]
    B --> B2[Lighting Circuits]
    B --> B3[Electrical Motors and Equipment]
    B --> B4[Galley and Cooking Equipment]
    B --> B5[Steam and Hot-fluid Piping]
    B --> B6[Miscellaneous - fans, electronics]

    A1 --> C[Total Heat Load Aggregation]
    A2 --> C
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> C
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C

    C --> D[Safety Factor Addition - 5 to 10 percent]

    D --> E[Net Design Cooling Load Q in kW]

    E --> F[Conversion to Plant Capacity in TR]

    F --> G[Compressor and Condenser Sizing]
    F --> H[Evaporator and AHU Selection]
    F --> I[Ductwork and Fan Sizing]
```

## 4.2 Shipboard Heat-Source Map (Block Diagram)


# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1 (3 Marks):** List **any six heat sources** that contribute to the cooling load of a ship's air-conditioning system.

**Model Answer:**
1. Heat conduction through ship's hull, bulkheads, and decks.
2. Solar radiation through windows, portholes, and skylights.
3. Ventilation and infiltration of outside air (sensible + latent).
4. Heat generated by occupants (sensible + latent body heat).
5. Heat from lighting circuits (fluorescent/incandescent/LED).
6. Heat from electric motors and electronic equipment located inside the conditioned space.
*(Any 6 of these — or alternatively galley equipment, steam pipes, re-radiation)* — **Full 3 marks.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2 (3 Marks):** Distinguish between **sensible heat** and **latent heat** with a shipboard example for each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Sensible Heat (SH):** Heat that causes a change in the *temperature* of air without altering its moisture content. *Shipboard example:* Heat conducted through a sun-exposed steel deckhead raising cabin dry-bulb temperature.
- **Latent Heat (LH):** Heat associated with the *phase change* of water (vaporisation/condensation) at constant temperature. *Shipboard example:* Moisture-laden tropical ventilation air depositing water on a chilled cooling coil (latent heat removed during condensation).
**Full 3 marks.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Composite Bulkhead Conduction (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A ship's accommodation bulkhead consists of the following layers, from outside (sea-side) to inside (cabin-side):

| Layer | Thickness (mm) | Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|
| Steel plate | 10 | 45 |
| Cork insulation | 40 | 0.045 |
| Plywood lining | 6 | 0.12 |

The bulkhead area is **25 m²**, the outside film coefficient is $h_o = 23$ W/m²·K, and the inside film coefficient is $h_i = 9$ W/m²·K. Calculate the **overall U-value** and the **heat conduction rate (in kW)** if the outside temperature is **38 °C** and inside is **24 °C**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's air-conditioning system supplies **2000 m³/h** of fresh outside air. Outside conditions: **36 °C DBT, 60 % RH**. Inside design conditions: **24 °C DBT, 50 % RH**. Compute the **total ventilation heat load in kW and in TR**. Use $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Composite Bulkhead Conduction

**Step 1: Calculate individual thermal resistances per unit area.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 = \frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.010}{45} &= 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_2 = \frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.040}{0.045} &= 0.8889 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_3 = \frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.006}{0.12} &= 0.0500 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Add film resistances.**

$$R_{film,i} = \frac{1}{9} = 0.1111 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{film,o} = \frac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Total resistance and U-value.**

$$R_{total} = 0.1111 + 2.222\times10^{-4} + 0.8889 + 0.0500 + 0.0435 = 1.0937 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{1.0937} = 0.9144 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer rate.**

$$\Delta T = 38 - 24 = 14 \;\; \text{K}$$

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{1000} = \frac{0.9144 \times 25 \times 14}{1000} = 0.3200 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Answer (a):** $U = 0.9144$ W/m²·K, $Q = 0.320$ kW. **(7 marks — 2 marks for resistances, 1 mark for U, 1 mark for ΔT, 3 marks for final Q with unit.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Ventilation Heat Load

**Step 1: Determine humidity ratios from psychrometric relations.**

At 36 °C DBT, 60 % RH → saturation pressure at 36 °C ≈ 5.94 kPa; partial vapour pressure ≈ 0.60 × 5.94 ≈ 3.564 kPa.

$$W_o = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{p_{atm} - p_v} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.564}{101.325 - 3.564} = 0.0227 \;\; \text{kg/kg}$$

At 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH → saturation pressure at 24 °C ≈ 2.985 kPa; partial vapour pressure ≈ 0.50 × 2.985 ≈ 1.493 kPa.

$$W_i = 0.622 \times \frac{1.493}{101.325 - 1.493} = 0.00930 \;\; \text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 2: Convert volumetric flow to mass flow.**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \frac{V}{3600} = 1.20 \times \frac{2000}{3600} = 0.6667 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat.**

$$Q_{sens} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.6667 \times 1.005 \times (36 - 24) = 8.04 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Latent heat.**

$$Q_{lat} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 0.6667 \times 2501 \times (0.0227 - 0.00930) = 22.31 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Total and conversion to TR.**

$$Q_{vent} = Q_{sens} + Q_{lat} = 8.04 + 22.31 = 30.35 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$TR = \frac{30.35}{3.517} = 8.63 \;\; \text{TR}$$

**Answer (b):** Total ventilation heat load = **30.35 kW = 8.63 TR**. **(7 marks — 1 mark each for W₀ and Wᵢ, 1 for ṁ, 2 for SH and LH, 1 for total and 1 for TR conversion.)**

---

### Question B — Solar Gain and Internal Heat Sources (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** A ship's wheelhouse has **three forward-facing windows**, each of area **3 m²**. The vessel operates at **20 °N latitude** in summer. For a vertical surface at solar noon under these conditions, the **Solar Heat Gain Factor (SHGF)** is **340 W/m²**. The glazing is tinted with **Shading Coefficient SC = 0.55**, and the Cooling Load Factor **CLF = 0.70**. Compute the **solar heat gain (in kW and TR)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An officer's mess on a ship has the following internal heat sources: **20 occupants** (light work, total 150 W/person); **30 fluorescent tubes** of 40 W each, with ballast factor 1.2 and use factor 0.7; a **galley exhaust fan motor** rated 200 W, efficiency 0.65. Compute the **total internal heat generation (in kW)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Solar Heat Gain

**Step 1: Total glass area.**

$$A_{total} = 3 \times 3 = 9 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply solar gain formula.**

$$Q_{solar} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$$

$$Q_{solar} = 9 \times 340 \times 0.55 \times 0.70 = 1178.1 \;\; \text{W} \approx 1.178 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Convert to TR.**

$$TR = \frac{1.178}{3.517} = 0.335 \;\; \text{TR}$$

**Answer (a):** $Q_{solar} = 1.178$ kW = **0.335 TR**. **(7 marks — 1 for area, 2 for substitution, 2 for calculation, 2 for unit & TR conversion.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Internal Heat Sources

**Step 1: Heat from occupants.**

$$Q_{occ} = 20 \times 150 = 3000 \;\; \text{W} = 3.0 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Heat from lighting.**

$$Q_{light} = 30 \times 40 \times 0.7 \times 1.2 = 1008 \;\; \text{W} = 1.008 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Heat from fan motor (in conditioned space — all losses to room).**

$$Q_{motor} = P \cdot (1 - \eta) = 200 \times (1 - 0.65) = 70 \;\; \text{W} = 0.070 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Sum.**

$$Q_{internal} = 3.0 + 1.008 + 0.070 = 4.078 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Answer (b):** Total internal heat generation = **4.078 kW ≈ 1.16 TR**. **(7 marks — 1 each for occupants, lighting, motor; 2 for summation; 2 for clarity & units.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in Module 3 Heat-Source Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing Unit Conversion (W → kW):** A calculation arriving at 320 W (instead of 0.32 kW) is **1 full mark deducted**. Always divide by 1000.
> 2. **Forgetting the Inside Film Resistance:** Students often use only the layer resistances and forget $1/h_i$ and $1/h_o$. **−1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Using WBT instead of DBT for ventilation sensible heat:** Ventilation SH depends on **DBT difference**, not WBT. **−1 mark** if swapped.
> 4. **Ignoring the Ballast Factor for Fluorescent Lights:** Old fluorescents have $F_{ballast} \approx 1.2$. LEDs are 1.0. **−0.5 marks** if omitted.
> 5. **Forgetting the Safety Margin (5–10 %):** Plant sizing without safety factor is considered incomplete. **−0.5 to −1 mark**.
> 6. **Adding All TR Values Instead of Summing kW First:** Always sum **kW values**, then convert the **single total** to TR. Summing TRs directly gives the same answer mathematically, but if units are mixed you lose marks.
> 7. **Confusing $h_{fg}$ Values:** Use 2501 kJ/kg (at 0 °C) or 2454 kJ/kg (at 20 °C). State which you used. **−0.5 marks** for ambiguous value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Always start** a heat-load problem by drawing a **clear block diagram** of the space and labeling every source.
- [ ] **Classify** sources into **External** (conduction, solar, ventilation, re-radiation) and **Internal** (occupants, lights, motors, galley, pipes).
- [ ] **Conduction formula:** $Q = U A \Delta T / 1000$ — with $U$ derived from $1/U = 1/h_i + \Sigma(x_k/k_k) + 1/h_o$.
- [ ] **Solar formula:** $Q = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$ — note **SHGF depends on latitude, month, orientation, and time**.
- [ ] **Ventilation:** **always compute both SH and LH** — they are often comparable in magnitude in tropical waters.
- [ ] **Occupants:** SH and LH **both depend on activity level** — refer to ASHRAE tables.
- [ ] **Lighting:** for fluorescents use **ballast factor 1.2**; for LEDs it is 1.0.
- [ ] **Motors inside the space:** $Q = P(1 - \eta)$ — all losses become heat.
- [ ] **Galley equipment:** refer to manufacturer data; **add a usage factor** (typically 0.4–0.7) since not all appliances run simultaneously.
- [ ] **Steam/hot pipes:** $Q = U_{pipe} \cdot \pi D L \cdot \Delta T$ — pipes within the space contribute *fully*.
- [ ] **Safety margin:** always add **5–10 %** to the grand total before sizing the plant.
- [ ] **Conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 3024 \text{ kcal/h}$.
- [ ] **Physical constants to memorise:** $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **Units discipline:** never let units "leak" from one equation to the next — convert W → kW at the very end of each sub-calculation.
- [ ] **Ship-specific factors:** dark hulls absorb ~90 % radiation; aluminium paint reduces solar gain by 30–40 %.
- [ ] **Order of magnitude check:** A 25 m² cabin typically has 1–2 kW total load; a 200-person mess hall has 30–60 kW; a full ship's accommodation may exceed 500 kW (≈ 150 TR).

---

> **End of Module 3 — Heat Sources | PENST743 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heat Exchanger (HEX)** is a piece of equipment designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids that are at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix physically. In the specific context of marine refrigeration and air-conditioning systems, a heat exchanger is the **backbone thermodynamic component** that enables the refrigerant to absorb heat from the spaces to be cooled (at the evaporator) and to reject that absorbed heat to the sea or ambient environment (at the condenser).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from standard text):** *“A heat exchanger is a device that facilitates the transfer of heat from one fluid to another across a solid surface, where the fluids are in thermal contact but not in direct contact.”*

The governing principle is the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, which dictates that heat spontaneously flows from a higher-temperature fluid (hot stream) to a lower-temperature fluid (cold stream) until thermal equilibrium or a steady-state operating point is reached.

In shipboard refrigeration plants, the following heat exchangers are mandatory by class rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS):

| Component | Function in Plant |
|---|---|
| **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from the chilled water / air (acts as a cold stream recipient) |
| **Condenser** | Rejects heat to seawater / fresh water cooling circuit |
| **Compressor Intercooler** | Cools compressed vapour between compression stages |
| **Liquid-Suction Heat Exchanger (LSHX)** | Sub-cools liquid refrigerant and superheats suction vapour |
| **Desuperheater** | Removes superheat before condensation |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | Compact heat transfer for oil coolers, freshwater generators |
| **Air-Cooled Condenser** | Used in emergencies when seawater cooling is unavailable |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine drinking hot tea from a steel cup. The tea (hot fluid) loses heat through the steel wall of the cup (solid separating surface) to your hand (cold fluid/air). The steel wall is the "heat transfer surface," and you have just witnessed the most basic form of a heat exchanger — a **conductive wall separating two fluids of differing temperature**.

Now, imagine scaling this up for a ship:

- A **container ship** sailing through the tropics has insulated reefer holds maintaining **-25 °C**.
- A **naval vessel** has crew spaces requiring **+22 °C** with 50% relative humidity.
- A **LNG carrier** maintains cargo at **-162 °C**.

In every case, a heat exchanger is the device that physically enforces the temperature separation between the "cargo/space side" and the "refrigerant side." The refrigerant acts as the **energy carrier** — it picks up heat at the evaporator (evaporating at low temperature) and dumps it at the condenser (condensing at high temperature). The heat exchanger is the physical stage where this transfer occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Class Notation Insight:** Heat exchangers on ships must be constructed of **cupronickel (Cu-Ni 70/30 or 90/10)**, **titanium**, or **aluminium-brass** tubes because seawater is highly corrosive. Using mild steel directly against seawater is **strictly prohibited** by SOLAS and class rules.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

The performance of any heat exchanger is graded against four universally accepted engineering metrics:

- **Heat Transfer Rate, Q (W or kW)** — the actual heat transferred per unit time.
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, U (W/m²·K)** — a measure of how easily heat passes through the wall.
- **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** — the effective driving temperature difference.
- **Effectiveness, ε (dimensionless, 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1)** — the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer.

The marine engineer must remember that the **fouling factor (Rf)** — a thermal resistance that grows with marine biofouling, scaling, and oil contamination — can degrade U by as much as **40–60%** between dry-dock cleanings. This is why redundant plate heat exchangers (with 2 × 100% or 3 × 50% configuration) are fitted on critical systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Heat Exchangers (KTU Board-Favourite Topic)

Heat exchangers are classified along several orthogonal axes:

#### A. By Flow Arrangement (Flow Geometry)

1. **Parallel Flow (Co-current):** Both hot and cold fluids enter at the same end and flow in the same direction. The outlet temperature difference is small; effectiveness is intrinsically limited.

2. **Counter-flow (Counter-current):** Fluids flow in opposite directions. Achieves the highest LMTD and the closest approach temperature. **Most efficient arrangement for marine condensers and evaporators.**

3. **Cross-flow:** Fluids flow perpendicular to each other. Common in **finned-tube air-cooled condensers** on deck.

4. **Shell-and-Tube Multi-pass:** Combination flow. One fluid makes multiple passes through tube bundles, the other flows once through the shell. **Industry standard for marine condensers.**

#### B. By Construction Type

- **Shell-and-Tube (STHE)**
- **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)**
- **Finned-Tube HEX**
- **Spiral HEX**
- **Compact Brazed Plate HEX**
- **Double-Tube (Concentric Pipe) HEX**

#### C. By Heat Transfer Mechanism

- **Direct Contact** (e.g., cooling tower, open spray)
- **Indirect Contact / Recuperative** (most shipboard units)
- **Regenerative / Storage Type** (e.g., rotary regenerator in HVAC)

#### D. By Process Function

- **Condensers** (vapour → liquid)
- **Evaporators** (liquid → vapour)
- **Superheaters / Desuperheaters**
- **Liquid Coolers / Sub-Coolers**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic of Heat Transfer

For a KTU board answer, the heat transfer process in any recuperative HEX follows this logical chain:

1. **Convection** from the hot bulk fluid to the inner surface of the wall (resistance = $1/h_i$).
2. **Conduction** through the tube/wall material (resistance = $\ln(D_o/D_i)/(2\pi k L)$).
3. **Convection** from the outer wall to the cold bulk fluid (resistance = $1/h_o$).
4. **Fouling resistances** add additional thermal barriers on both sides ($R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$).

The fundamental **Rate Equation (Newton's Law of Cooling)** governing every heat exchanger is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

where:
- **Q** = heat transfer rate (W)
- **U** = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- **A** = heat transfer area (m²)
- **ΔTₘ** = mean temperature difference (K)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 3 of these formulas appear in every KTU end-semester paper.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Transfer Rate | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | W | Energy balance on either stream |
| 2 | LMTD (Counter-flow) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K | When both terminal temperatures are known |
| 3 | LMTD with Correction Factor | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | K | Shell-and-tube multi-pass HEX |
| 4 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{R_{f,i}}{A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{R_{f,o}}{A_o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | m²·K/W | Wall resistance summation |
| 5 | NTU Definition | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | dimensionless | NTU method |
| 6 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | W/K | Define min/max |
| 7 | Capacity Ratio | $c = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | dimensionless | NTU method |
| 8 | Effectiveness (Definition) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$ | dimensionless | Effectiveness-NTU method |
| 9 | ε-NTU for Counter-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - c)]}{1 - c \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - c)]}$ | — | Counter-flow HEX |
| 10 | ε-NTU for Parallel-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 + c)]}{1 + c}$ | — | Parallel-flow HEX |
| 11 | ε-NTU for Shell-and-Tube | $\varepsilon = 2\left\{(1 + c) + \sqrt{1 + c^2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + \exp[-NTU\sqrt{1+c^2}]}{1 - \exp[-NTU\sqrt{1+c^2}]}\right\}^{-1}$ | — | 1-shell pass, 2-tube pass |
| 12 | Fouling Resistance Definition | $R_f = \dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} - \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}}$ | m²·K/W | Plant maintenance planning |
| 13 | NTU for Cross-flow (both unmixed) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{\dfrac{NTU^{0.22}}{c}[\exp(-c \cdot NTU^{0.78}) - 1]\right\}$ | — | Finned air-cooled HEX |
| 14 | Effectiveness for Condenser | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$ | — | When one fluid undergoes phase change |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Marine Field

In shipboard practice, the heat exchanger design choices directly translate into:

- **Fuel Oil Consumption:** Inefficient condensers force compressors to work at higher discharge pressures → higher specific work → more diesel generator load.
- **Class Survey Compliance:** Heat exchangers must be opened, inspected, and pressure-tested at every **special survey (every 5 years)**.
- **Redundancy Design:** Critical heat exchangers (e.g., lube oil cooler) are fitted with **duplex filters and bypass valves** so that one unit can be cleaned while the other is in service.
- **Space & Weight Optimization:** On a ship, every cubic metre and every kilogram costs money. Hence the dominance of **brazed plate heat exchangers (BPHEs)** in modern marine refrigeration packages — they offer up to **5–7× the heat transfer area per unit volume** compared to shell-and-tube units.

> [!TIP]
> **Real Engineering Insight:** The new generation of eco-friendly marine refrigerants (R-1234yf, R-513A, NH₃/CO₂ cascade systems) requires heat exchangers specifically designed for low-GWP refrigerants. Brazed plate heat exchangers with stainless-steel 316L plates are now the industry benchmark.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

> **Context:** This derivation is a guaranteed 7-mark question in any KTU heat-exchanger exam. We derive the LMTD for a counter-flow heat exchanger from first principles.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Steady-state operation
- Constant overall heat transfer coefficient U along the length
- Constant specific heats $c_p$ for both fluids
- No phase change inside the HEX
- No heat loss to surroundings
- Constant mass flow rates $\dot{m}_h$ and $\dot{m}_c$

**Step 1: Differential Heat Transfer Across an Elemental Area dA**

Consider a differential element of the heat exchanger surface, located at distance x from the inlet. Let the local hot-fluid temperature be $T_h(x)$ and the cold-fluid temperature be $T_c(x)$. The local temperature difference is:

$$\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$$

The infinitesimal heat transferred across the elemental area dA is, by Newton's law of cooling:

$$dQ = U \cdot \Delta T(x) \cdot dA$$

**Step 2: Express dQ in Terms of Hot-Fluid Temperature Change**

From the energy balance on the hot fluid (which cools as it traverses the element dx):

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$

The negative sign indicates that $T_h$ decreases in the direction of hot-fluid flow. Equating the two expressions for dQ:

$$U \cdot \Delta T(x) \cdot dA = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$

Solving for dA:

$$dA = -\dfrac{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}{U} \cdot \dfrac{dT_h}{\Delta T(x)}$$

**Step 3: Relate $dT_h$ to $d(\Delta T)$ for Counter-Flow Geometry**

In counter-flow, as the hot fluid cools by $dT_h$, the cold fluid warms by $dT_c$ in the opposite direction. From the energy balance on the cold fluid:

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

Therefore:

$$dT_c = \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

For a counter-flow HEX, the differential change in temperature difference is:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

Wait — this is for parallel flow. For counter-flow, since the fluids move in opposite directions, an incremental heat transfer dQ cools the hot fluid by $dT_h$ but, importantly, an element of the cold fluid that *was* at position $x + dx$ is now at $x$, so the relation becomes:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h + dT_c$$

But we can simplify this elegantly. From overall energy conservation, since $dQ$ transferred from the hot fluid equals the dQ absorbed by the cold fluid:

$$-\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

So:

$$dT_c = -\dfrac{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \cdot dT_h = -\dfrac{C_h}{C_c} \cdot dT_h$$

where $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$ are the heat capacity rates.

Now, for counter-flow, the differential of the temperature difference is:

$$d(\Delta T) = -dT_h - dT_c = -dT_h \left(1 + \dfrac{C_h}{C_c}\right)$$

Wait — let us reconsider. For counter-flow, the local $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$. An increment in x moves the hot fluid further (it cools) but moves the cold fluid backward relative to its flow direction (it is warmer as x increases). Let us re-derive carefully:

In a counter-flow HEX, at the **left end** (x = 0): hot fluid enters at $T_{h,in}$, cold fluid **exits** at $T_{c,out}$.
At the **right end** (x = L): hot fluid **exits** at $T_{h,out}$, cold fluid **enters** at $T_{c,in}$.

At any location x: $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$

Differentiating both sides with respect to x:

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = \dfrac{dT_h}{dx} - \dfrac{dT_c}{dx}$$

From the energy balances in differential form (heat lost by hot = heat gained by cold, with sign conventions for counter-flow direction):

$$\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \dfrac{dT_h}{dx} = -U \cdot P \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

$$\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \dfrac{dT_c}{dx} = +U \cdot P \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

where P is the perimeter of the tube bundle. The signs reflect the opposite flow directions.

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = -\dfrac{U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T}{C_h} - \dfrac{U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T}{C_c} = -U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Letting $\beta = U \cdot P \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$, we get:

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = -\beta \cdot \Delta T$$

This is a first-order linear ODE with the solution:

$$\Delta T(x) = \Delta T(0) \cdot \exp(-\beta x)$$

Applying the boundary condition at x = L: $\Delta T(L) = \Delta T_2$:

$$\Delta T_2 = \Delta T_1 \cdot \exp(-\beta L)$$

Solving for $\beta L$:

$$\beta L = \ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Total Heat Transfer and LMTD Result**

The total heat transfer is:

$$Q = \int_0^A U \cdot \Delta T(x) \cdot dA = U \cdot P \int_0^L \Delta T(0) e^{-\beta x} dx = \dfrac{U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T_1}{\beta} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\beta L}\right)$$

Substituting $e^{-\beta L} = \Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1$:

$$Q = \dfrac{U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T_1}{\beta} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = \dfrac{U \cdot P \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\beta}$$

But $\beta L = \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$, so $\beta = \dfrac{1}{L} \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.

Therefore:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

Hence the **Log Mean Temperature Difference** is rigorously derived as:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

where for counter-flow:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

**Step 5: Special Case — When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$**

When the two terminal temperature differences are nearly equal (i.e., $P \to 0$ in counter-flow, a very close approach), the LMTD reduces to the arithmetic mean:

$$\lim_{\Delta T_1 \to \Delta T_2} \Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T_{AM}$$

This is verified by **L'Hôpital's rule**:

$$\lim_{x \to y} \dfrac{x - y}{\ln(x/y)} = \lim_{x \to y} \dfrac{1}{1/x} = y$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow HEX

**Setup:** Effectiveness is the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum thermodynamically possible heat transfer.

The maximum possible heat transfer is achieved when the fluid with the smaller heat capacity rate is brought to the inlet temperature of the other fluid:

$$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

The actual heat transfer is bounded by the smaller of the two streams' enthalpy changes:

$$Q_{actual} = C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 1: Express Outlet Temperatures in Terms of Effectiveness**

$$T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_h}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon \cdot c_{h}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

where $c_h = C_{min}/C_h$. Similarly:

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_c}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon \cdot c_c(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

where $c_c = C_{min}/C_c$.

**Step 2: LMTD in Terms of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow**

In counter-flow:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

Computing each:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} - \varepsilon c_c(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})(1 - \varepsilon c_c)$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} - \varepsilon c_h(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})(1 - \varepsilon c_h)$$

Since for counter-flow $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ and we have $c_h = C_{min}/C_h \leq 1$, $c_c = C_{min}/C_c \leq 1$.

**Step 3: Substitute into $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$**

Using the LMTD formula:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})\left[(1 - \varepsilon c_c) - (1 - \varepsilon c_h)\right]}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon c_c}{1 - \varepsilon c_h}\right]} = \dfrac{(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) \varepsilon (c_h - c_c)}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon c_c}{1 - \varepsilon c_h}\right]}$$

But also $Q = \varepsilon C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = (UA)(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) \cdot \Delta T_{lm}/(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$.

Setting them equal and using $NTU = UA/C_{min}$, $c = C_{min}/C_{max}$, after simplification:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - c)]}{1 - c \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - c)]}$$

This is the celebrated counter-flow effectiveness equation used by every marine engineer for condenser and evaporator rating.

### 3.3 Quick Numerical Insight (Marine Application)

**Sample Problem (Class-room type):**
A counter-flow marine condenser cools **R-134a** refrigerant using seawater. Hot refrigerant enters at **T_h,in = 60 °C** and leaves at **T_h,out = 40 °C**. Seawater enters at **T_c,in = 28 °C** and leaves at **T_c,out = 36 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 1200 W/m²·K**. Compute the LMTD and the required heat transfer area.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 60 - 36 = 24 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 40 - 28 = 12 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{24 - 12}{\ln(24/12)} = \dfrac{12}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{12}{0.6931} = 17.31 \text{ °C}$$

The heat transfer rate, assuming refrigerant mass flow $\dot{m}_h = 0.5$ kg/s and $c_{p,h} = 1.0$ kJ/kg·K:

$$Q = 0.5 \times 1000 \times (60 - 40) = 10000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore, required area:

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{10000}{1200 \times 17.31} = 0.481 \text{ m}^2$$

This small area demonstrates the **superiority of counter-flow geometry** in marine condensers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the term "Log Mean Temperature Difference" (LMTD). Why is it preferred over the arithmetic mean temperature difference in heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
LMTD is defined as the temperature difference that, if constant across the heat exchanger surface, would yield the same rate of heat transfer as the actual variable temperature difference. It is given by $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.

**Why preferred over arithmetic mean:** The temperature difference between hot and cold fluids varies exponentially along the length of the heat exchanger due to the changing specific enthalpies of both fluids. The arithmetic mean $\Delta T_{AM} = (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$ only approximates the true driving force when the temperature change in each fluid is small (i.e., $P \to 0$). For most engineering applications where $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 > 2$, the LMTD is the rigorously correct mean, as it arises naturally from the differential heat transfer equation.

---

**Q2. What is the "fouling factor" in heat exchangers, and how does it affect marine refrigeration plant performance?**

**Model Answer:**
The fouling factor $R_f$ (units: m²·K/W) is the additional thermal resistance introduced by deposits (scaling, biofouling, oil films, corrosion products) that accumulate on heat transfer surfaces during operation. It is defined as:

$$R_f = \dfrac{1}{U_{dirty}} - \dfrac{1}{U_{clean}}$$

**Effect on marine refrigeration:** Seawater contains salts, marine organisms (barnacles, mussels, algae), and suspended particulates. Over 6–12 months of operation, these accumulate on the tube inner surface (sea side). Typical marine fouling factors are:
- Seawater: $R_f = 0.0001$ to $0.0002$ m²·K/W
- Fresh water: $R_f = 0.0002$ m²·K/W
- Refrigerant (oil): $R_f = 0.0002$ m²·K/W

This added resistance reduces U by **30–60%**, increases condensing pressure and temperature, raises compressor discharge pressure, increases specific work consumption, and reduces COP. Class rules mandate periodic cleaning at dry-dock.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Counter-Flow Condenser Design

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Derive the LMTD expression for a counter-flow heat exchanger from the differential form of the heat transfer equation. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A counter-flow double-pipe marine heat exchanger is used to condense refrigerant R-134a. The refrigerant enters as saturated vapor at **T_h,in = 45 °C** and leaves as saturated liquid at **T_h,out = 40 °C** (after giving up its latent heat, a phase change occurs at essentially constant temperature). Cooling seawater enters at **T_c,in = 20 °C** and leaves at **T_c,out = 30 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer area is **U = 1500 W/m²·K**. If the mass flow rate of the refrigerant is **0.4 kg/s** and its latent heat is **180 kJ/kg**, calculate: **(i)** the LMTD, **(ii)** the heat transfer rate, and **(iii)** the required heat transfer area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_{h,in} = 45$ °C, $T_{h,out} = 40$ °C (phase-change approximation: the fluid is essentially at constant temperature; we use the inlet as representative)
- $T_{c,in} = 20$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 30$ °C
- $U = 1500$ W/m²·K
- $\dot{m}_h = 0.4$ kg/s
- $h_{fg} = 180$ kJ/kg = 180,000 J/kg

**(i) LMTD:**

For counter-flow:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 45 - 30 = 15$ °C
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 40 - 20 = 20$ °C

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln(\Delta T_2/\Delta T_1)} = \dfrac{20 - 15}{\ln(20/15)} = \dfrac{5}{\ln(1.333)} = \dfrac{5}{0.2877} = 17.38 \text{ °C}$$

**(ii) Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot h_{fg} = 0.4 \times 180{,}000 = 72{,}000 \text{ W} = 72 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Required Area:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{72{,}000}{1500 \times 17.38} = \dfrac{72{,}000}{26{,}070} = 2.76 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{lm} = 17.38$ °C, $Q = 72$ kW, $A = 2.76$ m².

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Effectiveness-NTU Method

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Explain the effectiveness-NTU method for heat exchanger analysis. List the ε-NTU relationships for (i) counter-flow heat exchanger and (ii) shell-and-tube heat exchanger with one shell pass and two tube passes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shell-and-tube oil cooler (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) cools lube oil from **150 °C to 100 °C** using cooling water that enters at **20 °C**. The oil mass flow rate is **0.5 kg/s** with $c_{p,oil} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K, and the water mass flow rate is **0.3 kg/s** with $c_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate the effectiveness, the heat transfer rate, and the water outlet temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_{h,in} = 150$ °C, $T_{h,out} = 100$ °C (oil)
- $T_{c,in} = 20$ °C (water, inlet)
- $\dot{m}_h = 0.5$ kg/s, $c_{p,h} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K
- $\dot{m}_c = 0.3$ kg/s, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Heat Capacity Rates**

$$C_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} = 0.5 \times 2000 = 1000 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} = 0.3 \times 4180 = 1254 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_{min} = 1000 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_{max} = 1254 \text{ W/K}$$

$$c = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{1000}{1254} = 0.7974$$

**Step 2: Heat Transfer Rate (from oil side)**

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 1000 \times (150 - 100) = 50{,}000 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1000 \times (150 - 20)} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{130{,}000} = 0.3846$$

**Step 4: Water Outlet Temperature**

From cold-side energy balance:

$$Q = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) \Rightarrow T_{c,out} = \dfrac{Q}{C_c} + T_{c,in} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{1254} + 20 = 39.87 + 20 = 59.87 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answer:** $\varepsilon = 0.3846$, $Q = 50$ kW, $T_{c,out} = 59.87$ °C.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Heat Exchanger problems (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong $\Delta T_1$ vs $\Delta T_2$ assignment for counter-flow:** Many students put $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ by mistake. The correct counter-flow convention is $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ (the *smaller* end is often denoted $\Delta T_1$ — confirm your textbook's sign convention).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the natural logarithm base:** The LMTD formula uses `ln` (natural log, base *e*). Using $\log_{10}$ will yield a numerically wrong answer that loses 3 marks outright.
>
> 3. **Confusing $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$:** In effectiveness-NTU, $c = C_{min}/C_{max} \leq 1$. If you flip them, your $c > 1$, and the formula gives garbage.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the LMTD Correction Factor F:** For multi-pass shell-and-tube HEX, the bare counter-flow LMTD overpredicts the true $\Delta T_m$. KTU examiners specifically expect the $F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ form. Skipping $F$ costs 2–4 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up condenser and evaporator effectiveness:** When one fluid undergoes phase change at constant temperature, $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$. This is a very common 3-mark trap question.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing kW and W, or forgetting to convert $h_{fg}$ from kJ/kg to J/kg, will produce an answer off by a factor of 1000. Always state units clearly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These Before the KTU Exam:**

- **Heat exchanger definition:** Device to transfer heat between fluids separated by a solid wall.
- **Main types on ships:** Shell-and-tube, plate, finned-tube, brazed plate, coaxial.
- **Master equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ (always the starting point of every problem).
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ — natural log, NOT log base 10.
- **Counter-flow vs Parallel-flow:** Counter-flow gives **higher LMTD** and **higher effectiveness** for the same area.
- **Effectiveness ε:** $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max} = Q/(C_{min} \cdot \Delta T_{in,max})$.
- **NTU:** $NTU = UA/C_{min}$ — quantifies the "size" of the heat exchanger.
- **Capacity ratio:** $c = C_{min}/C_{max}$ — between 0 and 1.
- **Fouling factor $R_f$:** Marine seawater $R_f \approx 0.0001$–$0.0002$ m²·K/W.
- **Class rules require:** Periodic opening, inspection, hydrostatic testing of condensers and evaporators every special survey (5 years).
- **Construction materials:** Cupronickel, titanium, aluminium-brass, SS 316L for seawater side; carbon steel for refrigerant side (with caution).
- **Modern trend:** Brazed plate heat exchangers (BPHEs) are replacing shell-and-tube units in marine refrigeration due to higher volumetric efficiency.
- **Refrigerants and HEX compatibility:** Eco-friendly refrigerants like R-1234yf, R-513A, NH₃, CO₂ require careful material selection to avoid galvanic corrosion inside the HEX.
- **One-liner for the board:** "In a counter-flow HEX, the temperature difference at one end equals the temperature difference at the other end only when both fluids undergo phase change at constant temperature — this is the limiting case of maximum effectiveness."
- **Use LMTD method** when inlet AND outlet temperatures of BOTH fluids are known.
- **Use ε-NTU method** when only inlet temperatures and one outlet temperature are unknown (more direct for design problems).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Heat Pump** is a thermodynamic device operating on the **reverse refrigeration cycle** that extracts heat from a low-temperature source (ambient air, water, or ground) and delivers it to a higher-temperature sink (heated space or domestic water) by consuming external mechanical or thermal work input. The *desired useful effect* is the heat rejected at the high-temperature side, distinguishing it from a refrigerator where the useful effect is cooling.

In marine and industrial practice, the heat pump is considered an **upgraded utilization** of the vapor compression refrigeration plant, where both heating and cooling can be obtained simultaneously by reversing the cycle direction using a four-way reversing valve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A Heat Pump is defined as a device whose primary purpose is the *delivery of heat* $Q_H$ to a high-temperature body, the work input $W_{net}$ being the secondary quantity. This is the inverse priority of a refrigerator.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a water pump that lifts water from a low-lying pond to a higher terrace. The water already has some "natural" tendency to flow down, but the pump forces it *uphill*, requiring work.

A **heat pump works exactly the same way with heat energy**: heat naturally flows from hot to cold. A heat pump forces heat to flow from cold to hot, requiring work input. The "cold pond" is the low-temperature source (outdoor air at 5°C), and the "high terrace" is the indoor space (heated to 25°C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A refrigerator throws away the "waste heat" $Q_H$ to the atmosphere. A heat pump *treasures* this $Q_H$ and uses it for heating — that is the only fundamental difference in physics, but the application priority is opposite.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of heat pump is denoted by **COP<sub>HP</sub>** and is dimensionless.
- Temperatures are measured in **Kelvin (K)** for all COP calculations.
- Standard rating conditions: **T<sub>L</sub> = 7°C = 280 K**, **T<sub>H</sub> = 50°C = 323 K** (for residential heat pumps).
- For Carnot (reversible) limits, **COP<sub>HP, Carnot</sub> = T<sub>H</sub> / (T<sub>H</sub> − T<sub>L</sub>)**.

### 1.4 Comparison: Refrigerator vs Heat Pump

| Parameter | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Desired Output | Heat removed from cold space ($Q_L$) | Heat delivered to hot space ($Q_H$) |
| Work Input | $W_{net}$ | $W_{net}$ |
| COP Formula | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ |
| Practical Application | Cold storage, AC cooling | Space heating, water heating |
| Source of Heat | Cold space | Outdoor environment |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The heat pump operates on the **Vapor Compression Cycle (VCC)** in reverse-priority mode. The four thermodynamic processes are:

1. **Compression (1 → 2):** Low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapor is compressed isentropically to high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapor by the compressor. Work is **consumed**.
2. **Condensation (2 → 3):** High-pressure superheated vapor rejects heat $Q_H$ to the hot space (condenser), condensing into high-pressure subcooled liquid.
3. **Expansion (3 → 4):** High-pressure liquid passes through an expansion valve, undergoing **throttling** (isenthalpic) to low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture.
4. **Evaporation (4 → 1):** Low-pressure mixture absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the low-temperature source, evaporating fully to saturated/superheated vapor, returning to compressor inlet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works:** The compressor raises the refrigerant's saturation temperature **above** the desired indoor temperature. This allows the refrigerant to *condense* in the indoor coil, releasing heat to the space — even in winter when outdoor temperatures are near 0°C.

### 2.2 Energy Balance Derivation (First Law)

Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a cyclic steady-flow system:

$$
\sum Q = \sum W
$$

For the heat pump cycle:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}
$$

This leads directly to the **relation between COP<sub>HP</sub> and COP<sub>R</sub>**:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}
$$

### 2.3 Carnot (Reversible) Heat Pump COP

For a **Carnot cycle operating in reverse**, with isothermal heat addition at $T_H$ and isothermal heat rejection at $T_L$:

$$
COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}
$$

This is the **theoretical maximum** and serves as the benchmark for all real heat pump performance evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Insight:** Because $T_H > T_H - T_L$, the value of $COP_{HP,\,Carnot}$ is **always greater than 1**, meaning a heat pump delivers more heat energy than the equivalent electrical energy consumed — a hallmark of high-efficiency heating technology.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Definition of Heat Pump COP | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | First Law energy balance | kW or kJ/s |
| 3 | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Relation between COP_HP and COP_R | dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum (ideal) COP for HP | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{R,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum (ideal) COP for refrigerator | dimensionless |
| 6 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{COP_R}{COP_R - 1}$ | Alternative rearrangement | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency (heat engine) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert:** Always use **Kelvin** for temperature in COP calculations. A student who writes $T_L = 7$ instead of $T_L = 280$ in the Carnot COP formula loses full marks in numericals.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine Applications:** Cruise ships use heat pumps for both **HVAC heating** and **domestic hot water (DHW)** generation, recovering waste heat from engine cooling jackets.
- **Industrial Process Heating:** Food processing, dairy pasteurization, and textile drying.
- **District Heating:** Centralized heat pumps serving multiple buildings in cold-climate ports (Norway, Northern Europe).
- **Geothermal Heat Pumps:** Marine shore installations using seawater as the heat source.
- **Air-Source Heat Pumps (ASHP):** Common in ships' accommodation blocks; reversible for summer cooling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relation between COP<sub>HP</sub> and COP<sub>R</sub>

**Starting Point:** Both the refrigerator and heat pump share identical physical hardware and cycle; only the *useful output* differs.

**Step 1 — Define Refrigerator COP:**

By definition, refrigerator COP is the ratio of desired cooling effect to work input:

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

Here, $Q_L$ is the heat absorbed from the cold body.

**Step 2 — Define Heat Pump COP:**

By definition, heat pump COP is the ratio of desired heating effect to work input:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}
$$

Here, $Q_H$ is the heat rejected at the condenser to the hot body.

**Step 3 — Apply First Law to the Cycle:**
