The first law for a closed cycle requires net heat transfer to equal net work transfer:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into COP<sub>HP</sub> Definition:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide Numerator and Denominator by $W_{net}$:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}}{\dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{net}}} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}}}{\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Refrigerator COP inside the expression:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1}{\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}} = \frac{COP_R + 1}{COP_R} \cdot \frac{COP_R}{1}
$$

Wait — let me carefully redo this substitution. Dividing numerator and denominator by $W_{net}$:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}}}{\dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{net}}}
$$

Since $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1}{1} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1
$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}
$$

This fundamental relation confirms that **a heat pump's COP always exceeds a refrigerator's COP by exactly 1**, since both operate on the same work input.

### 3.2 Derivation: Carnot Heat Pump COP

**Step 1 — Consider a Carnot Refrigeration Cycle operating in reverse.**

The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes and two isentropic processes.

**Step 2 — Heat Rejected at High Temperature (Isothermal):**

$$
Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Absorbed at Low Temperature (Isothermal):**

$$
Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 4 — Net Work Input (from First Law):**

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into COP<sub>HP</sub> Definition:**

$$
COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\Delta S$:**

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

### 3.3 Sample Numerical Problem (Worked Solution)

**Problem:** A heat pump operating on the reversed Carnot cycle maintains an indoor temperature of **25°C**. The outdoor temperature is **5°C**. If the heat pump delivers **10 kW** of heating capacity, calculate:
(i) The COP of the heat pump
(ii) The power required to drive the compressor
(iii) The rate of heat extraction from outdoor air

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
T_H = 25 + 273 = 298\,K
$$

$$
T_L = 5 + 273 = 278\,K
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate COP<sub>HP</sub>:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{298}{298 - 278} = \frac{298}{20} = 14.9
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Power Required:**

$$
W_{net} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{10}{14.9} = 0.671\,kW
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Heat Extracted from Outdoor:**

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 10 - 0.671 = 9.329\,kW
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **93.3% of the delivered heat comes from the outdoor environment** (free energy) and only **6.7% comes from the electrical work input**. This is why heat pumps are considered highly efficient and preferred in maritime decarbonization strategies.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | T<sub>H</sub> (K) | T<sub>L</sub> (K) | $\Delta T$ | COP<sub>HP,Carnot</sub> | COP<sub>R,Carnot</sub> |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild winter heating | 295 | 280 | 15 | 19.67 | 18.67 |
| Cold climate heating | 295 | 263 | 32 | 9.22 | 8.22 |
| Marine accommodation | 300 | 278 | 22 | 13.64 | 12.64 |
| Industrial high-temp | 373 | 293 | 80 | 4.66 | 3.66 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** As the temperature lift ($\Delta T = T_H - T_L$) increases, both COP values decrease. This explains why heat pumps struggle in extreme cold conditions and why ground-source or seawater-source heat pumps outperform air-source units in cold climates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heat Pump System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Low Temperature Source: Outdoor Air or Seawater] -->|Heat Q_L| B[Evaporator: Low Pressure Side]
    B -->|Low P Vapor| C[Compressor: Work W Input]
    C -->|High P Superheated Vapor| D[Condenser: High Pressure Side]
    D -->|Heat Q_H Delivered to Indoor Space| E[Heated Space or Hot Water Tank]
    D -->|High P Liquid| F[Expansion Valve: Throttling]
    F -->|Low P Two Phase| B
    C -->|Electrical Power Supply W| G[Marine Power Supply: 440V 3 Phase]
```

### 4.2 Heat Pump T-s Diagram (Carnot Reverse Cycle)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[Point 1: Saturated Vapor at T_L] -->|Isentropic Compression Process 1 to 2| P2[Point 2: Superheated Vapor at T_H]
    P2 -->|Isothermal Heat Rejection 2 to 3 at T_H| P3[Point 3: Saturated Liquid at T_H]
    P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| P4[Point 4: Two Phase at T_L]
    P4 -->|Isothermal Heat Absorption 4 to 1 at T_L| P1
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Heat Pump vs Refrigerator Cycle Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HP_Mode[Heat Pump Mode: Priority Heating]
        HPS1[Outdoor Coil Absorbs Q_L] --> HPC1[Compressor Consumes W]
        HPC1 --> HPD1[Indoor Coil Rejects Q_H to Space]
        HPD1 --> HPE1[Expansion Valve]
        HPE1 --> HPS1
    end
    subgraph AC_Mode[Refrigeration Mode: Priority Cooling]
        ACS1[Indoor Coil Absorbs Q_L from Space] --> ACC1[Compressor Consumes W]
        ACC1 --> ACD1[Outdoor Coil Rejects Q_H to Ambient]
        ACD1 --> ACE1[Expansion Valve]
        ACE1 --> ACS1
    end
    RV[4-Way Reversing Valve] -.Switches Flow.-> HP_Mode
    RV -.Switches Flow.-> AC_Mode
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation of Energy Flows

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Low Temp Source T_L] -->|Q_L| SYS[Heat Pump System]
    SYS -->|Q_H| SNK[High Temp Sink T_H]
    WORK[Work Input W_net] --> SYS
    SYS -.Energy Balance Q_H = Q_L + W.-> SYS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The energy flow schematic clearly illustrates the **First Law balance** — heat enters from the cold source and from work input, and both combine to deliver heat to the hot sink. The COP is simply the ratio of delivered heat ($Q_H$) to the work required ($W$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Define a Heat Pump. State the difference between a heat pump and a refrigerator.

**Model Answer:**

A heat pump is a thermodynamic device operating on the reverse refrigeration cycle, whose primary purpose is to **deliver heat** $Q_H$ to a high-temperature body (heated space or water) by consuming mechanical work $W_{net}$.

The key difference between a heat pump and a refrigerator lies in the **desired output**:

| Parameter | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Useful Effect | Heat removed from cold space ($Q_L$) | Heat delivered to hot space ($Q_H$) |
| COP Definition | $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$ | $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W_{net}$ |
| Application | Cooling, cold storage | Space heating, water heating |

Both share the same hardware and obey $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$.

**(3 Marks – Awarded for: Definition 1M, Distinct application 1M, COP comparison 1M)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Derive the expression for the COP of a reversible (Carnot) heat pump operating between temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$.

**Model Answer:**

For a Carnot heat pump, heat is absorbed isothermally at $T_L$ and rejected isothermally at $T_H$, with isentropic compression and expansion.

$$
Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{and} \quad Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S
$$

By First Law:

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S
$$

Therefore:

$$
COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**(3 Marks – Awarded for: Heat relations 1M, Work expression 1M, Final formula 1M)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics, derive the relationship $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ for a heat pump and refrigerator operating on the same cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A heat pump operates between an outdoor temperature of 0°C and an indoor temperature of 22°C. If the heat pump delivers 5 kW of heat, calculate:
   (i) The Carnot COP
   (ii) The compressor power required
   (iii) The rate of heat extracted from outdoor air

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Step 1 — Define refrigerator COP:

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

Step 2 — Define heat pump COP:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}
$$

Step 3 — Apply First Law:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} \implies W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

Step 4 — Substitute:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1
$$

Step 5 — Recognize refrigerator COP:

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}
$$

**(7 Marks awarded for: Definitions 2M, First Law 2M, Substitution 2M, Final result 1M)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_L = 0 + 273 = 273\,K
$$

$$
T_H = 22 + 273 = 295\,K
$$

Step 2 — Compute Carnot COP:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{295}{295 - 273} = \frac{295}{22} = 13.41
$$

Step 3 — Compressor Power:

$$
W_{net} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{5}{13.41} = 0.373\,kW
$$

Step 4 — Heat Extracted from Outdoor:

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 5 - 0.373 = 4.627\,kW
$$

**(7 Marks awarded for: Temperature conversion 1M, COP formula 2M, Power calculation 2M, Q_L calculation 2M)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a vapor compression heat pump. List any **three advantages** of heat pumps over conventional heating systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Carnot heat pump is used to maintain the engine room of a ship at 30°C. The seawater temperature is 20°C. If the heating load is 8 kW, determine:
   (i) The COP of the heat pump
   (ii) The minimum work input required
   (iii) The heat rejected to seawater

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**

The vapor compression heat pump consists of four main components:

1. **Evaporator:** Absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the outdoor environment (low-temperature source).
2. **Compressor:** Compresses low-pressure vapor to high-pressure vapor, consuming work $W$.
3. **Condenser:** Rejects heat $Q_H$ to the indoor space (high-temperature sink), condensing refrigerant.
4. **Expansion Valve:** Throttles high-pressure liquid to low-pressure mixture.

The refrigerant circulates in a closed loop. The compressor raises the refrigerant temperature above the desired indoor temperature so that condensation can occur in the indoor coil, releasing heat to the space.

**Three Advantages of Heat Pumps:**
1. **Higher Energy Efficiency:** COP > 1 means more heat delivered per unit work than direct electric heating.
2. **Dual Functionality:** Reversible operation provides both heating and cooling using the same hardware.
3. **Environmentally Friendly:** Reduces fossil fuel consumption and CO₂ emissions; uses eco-friendly refrigerants.

**(7 Marks: Diagram 3M + Working 2M + Advantages 2M)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert temperatures:

$$
T_H = 30 + 273 = 303\,K
$$

$$
T_L = 20 + 273 = 293\,K
$$

Step 2 — Carnot COP:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{303}{303 - 293} = \frac{303}{10} = 30.3
$$

Step 3 — Minimum Work Input:

$$
W_{net} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{8}{30.3} = 0.264\,kW
$$

Step 4 — Heat Rejected to Seawater:

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 8 - 0.264 = 7.736\,kW
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** In marine applications, seawater at 20°C serves as an excellent heat source because it has a stable temperature throughout the year compared to ambient air, yielding high and consistent COP values — an important consideration for tropical and subtropical shipping routes.

**(7 Marks: COP 2M, Power 2M, Q_L 2M, Units/Logic 1M)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes Resulting in Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Using Celsius (°C) instead of Kelvin (K) in the Carnot COP formula — **deduct 50% of marks** if this error propagates.
> 2. **Confusing COP<sub>HP</sub> and COP<sub>R</sub> Definitions:** A student who writes $COP_{HP} = Q_L/W$ instead of $Q_H/W$ loses full marks in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+1" Term:** The relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ must be stated explicitly; simply writing the numerical value without derivation is penalized.
> 4. **Sign Convention Confusion:** In numericals, students sometimes write $Q_H = Q_L - W$, which is physically incorrect. Always use $Q_H = Q_L + W$ for heat pumps and refrigerators.
> 5. **Dimensionless vs Percentage Confusion:** COP is dimensionless — never express it as a percentage. Some textbooks incorrectly state $COP = \eta \times 100\%$, which is **wrong** for heat pumps since $\eta \leq 1$ but $COP \geq 1$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Priority:** Heat pump = device for *delivering heat* $Q_H$; refrigerator = device for *removing heat* $Q_L$.
- **Fundamental Equation:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ (First Law for the cycle).
- **Master Relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ — this is the **single most important** formula for this topic.
- **Carnot COP Formula:** $COP_{HP,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ — always use **Kelvin** temperatures.
- **Carnot COP is the Upper Limit:** No real heat pump can exceed this; actual COP = $\eta_{II} \times COP_{Carnot}$ where $\eta_{II}$ is the second-law efficiency (typically 0.4 to 0.7).
- **Cycle Components:** Evaporator → Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → (back to Evaporator).
- **Heat Source Varieties:** Air-source, water-source, ground-source (geothermal) — marine installations predominantly use **seawater-source** due to stable temperatures.
- **Reversibility:** Adding a four-way reversing valve converts a heat pump into an air conditioner (and vice versa), enabling dual-mode operation in marine HVAC systems.
- **Energy Efficiency Significance:** A heat pump with COP = 4 delivers 4 units of heat for 1 unit of electricity, compared to 1 unit of heat from direct electric resistance heating — a **400% efficiency advantage**.
- **Application Domains in Shipping:** Accommodation HVAC, domestic hot water generation, provision room refrigeration with heat recovery, and pre-heating of heavy fuel oil using jacket cooling water.
- **Direction of Heat Flow:** Always from cold to hot in heat pumps — opposite to the natural direction, hence work is **mandatory**.
- **T-s Diagram Shape:** Identical to Carnot refrigeration cycle but traversed in the opposite (clockwise for HP, counter-clockwise for refrigerator) direction.
- **Practical COP Range:** Real-world marine heat pumps achieve COP between **3.0 and 5.5**, depending on temperature lift and refrigerant type.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning (Cooling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning (Cooling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Air Conditioning (Cooling)** is the process of treating air to control simultaneously its **temperature**, **humidity**, **cleanliness**, and **distribution** to meet the prescribed comfort or process conditions of a conditioned space. In the context of **shipboard installations**, cooling is the primary mode of operation since most vessels traverse tropical and subtropical waters, where ambient conditions demand substantial heat and moisture removal from ventilation air.

The cooling process in a marine air-conditioning plant involves three fundamental psychrometric operations:

- **Sensible Cooling** — Reduction of dry-bulb temperature at constant specific humidity (constant moisture content).
- **Latent Cooling (Dehumidification)** — Reduction of moisture content at constant dry-bulb temperature.
- **Combined Sensible and Latent Cooling** — Simultaneous reduction of both dry-bulb temperature and humidity ratio, achieved by passing air across a cooling coil whose surface temperature lies below the dew point of the entering air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes the *quantitative analysis* of cooling loads, coil performance (Bypass Factor and ADP), Sensible Heat Factor (SHF/ESHF), and the layout of marine air-handling units (AHUs) for passenger, crew, and machinery spaces.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the **open deck of a tanker in the Persian Gulf** in July. The sun bakes the steel, the sea spray wets your skin, and the air feels like a wet blanket — both **hot and humid**. Step inside the air-conditioned accommodation block: a blast of cool, dry air greets you. What happened?

Three things occurred simultaneously:
1. The **hot air** was passed over **metal tubes carrying chilled water** (≈ **6 °C to 8 °C**). This cooled the air — **sensible cooling**.
2. Because the coil surface was colder than the **dew point temperature** of the entering air, water vapour **condensed out** of the air onto the fins and dripped into a drain pan. The air became drier — **latent cooling (dehumidification)**.
3. The cooled, dehumidified air was **filtered, ducted, and re-distributed** uniformly to eliminate drafts, odours, and stratification.

> **Real-World Analogy (Refined):** Think of the cooling coil as a **"cold shower" for the air**. The cold water inside the tubes absorbs heat from the airstream; if the tubes are cold enough, they also wring out moisture like a sponge — the air leaves cooler AND drier. The whole system is essentially doing what a refrigerator does to its own air, but on a much larger scale.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard design parameters** are used in marine AC cooling load estimation:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Shipboard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor design DBT (tropical) | $T_o$ | **35 °C** |
| Outdoor design WBT (tropical) | $T_w$ | **28 °C** |
| Indoor design DBT (passenger/crew) | $T_i$ | **24 °C ± 1 °C** |
| Indoor design RH (passenger/crew) | $\phi_i$ | **50 % ± 5 %** |
| Chilled water supply temperature | $T_{ws}$ | **6 °C to 8 °C** |
| Chilled water return temperature | $T_{wr}$ | **12 °C to 14 °C** |
| Coil ADP (Apparatus Dew Point) | $T_{ADP}$ | **8 °C to 10 °C** |
| By-Pass Factor of cooling coil | $BPF$ | **0.05 to 0.20** |
| Fresh air per person (cruise ships) | $\dot{V}_{FA}$ | **45 to 60 m³/h** |
| Sea-water inlet temperature (tropical) | $T_{sw}$ | **30 °C to 32 °C** |

---

## 1.4 Significance in Marine Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Cooling" is the dominant AC mode at sea:** Ocean voyages expose the ship envelope to high solar radiation, warm sea-water temperatures (the condenser heat sink), and high latent loads from a large number of occupants per unit volume (especially in cruise liners). Unlike land-based plants where heating may dominate in winter, **marine AC plants are sized almost exclusively by the peak summer (cooling) load**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Classification of Cooling Processes

A cooling operation on a psychrometric chart is a process line on which the **specific enthalpy ($h$)** of the moist air **decreases**. The reduction in enthalpy has two physical contributions:

$$
\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = \Delta h_{sensible} + \Delta h_{latent}
$$

where state **1** is the air entering the cooling coil and state **2** is the air leaving.

### 2.1.1 Process A — Pure Sensible Cooling
- **State 1 → State 2:** DBT decreases; **W remains constant**; RH rises (because colder air saturates more easily).
- **Latent heat = 0**; total cooling = sensible cooling.
- Achieved when the **coil surface temperature is above the dew point** of the entering air (no condensation).
- On the psychrometric chart, this is a **horizontal line at constant $W$**.

### 2.1.2 Process B — Pure Latent Cooling (Dehumidification)
- **State 1 → State 2:** W decreases; **DBT remains constant**; RH falls.
- Sensible heat = 0; total cooling = latent cooling.
- Achieved by chemical desiccant absorption or by a coil with **ADP at the entering DBT** (theoretical limiting case).
- On the psychrometric chart, this is a **vertical line at constant $T_{DB}$**.

### 2.1.3 Process C — Combined Sensible + Latent Cooling (Most Common)
- **State 1 → State 2:** Both DBT and W decrease.
- This is the **actual process** in a chilled-water or direct-expansion (DX) cooling coil.
- On the psychrometric chart, this is a line sloping from state 1 toward the saturation curve, **asymptotically approaching the ADP point** but never reaching it.

---

## 2.2 The Cooling Load — Threefold Decomposition

The **total cooling load** of a conditioned space is the rate at which heat AND moisture must be removed to maintain the indoor design conditions. It is decomposed as:

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{sensible} + \dot{Q}_{latent}
$$

### 2.2.1 Sensible Heat Sources ($\dot{Q}_s$)

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \dot{Q}_{transmission} + \dot{Q}_{roof/glass} + \dot{Q}_{occupants,s} + \dot{Q}_{lights} + \dot{Q}_{appliances} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration,s}
$$

Each sub-component is computed using $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ or $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (conduction through ships' structures).

### 2.2.2 Latent Heat Sources ($\dot{Q}_L$)

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{Q}_{occupants,L} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration,L} + \dot{Q}_{process,moisture}
$$

Latent heat from occupants is typically **55 to 65 W per person** in light activity (seated) and **110 to 130 W per person** in heavy work. Infiltration latent load on ships is critical because **ventilation (overpressure) is mandatory** for engine-room and accommodation safety.

### 2.2.3 Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and Effective SHF (ESHF)

$$
SHF = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L}
$$

$$
ESHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ESHF (Effective Sensible Heat Factor)** uses the *Room* Sensible and Latent heats (after re-circulated and outdoor air have been mixed at the AHU inlet). It is the value plotted on the coil selection chart of the ASHRAE / manufacturer catalogue. **ESHF > SHF** because the return-air latent component is partially handled by re-circulation.

---

## 2.3 The Cooling Coil — By-Pass Factor (BPF) and Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)

A **cooling coil** is a finned-tube heat exchanger. Because of the finite contact time and uneven air distribution, a fraction of the entering air *bypasses* intimate contact with the cold surface.

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

The complementary **Contact Factor (CF)** is:

$$
CF = 1 - BPF
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of BPF:** If $BPF = 0.10$, then 10 % of the entering air "leaks" past the coil without any heat or moisture exchange, while 90 % is brought to thermal equilibrium with the coil surface. **Lower BPF = more efficient coil** (deeper fins, more rows, higher face velocity, lower supply-air temperature).

**Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is the **thermodynamic state** (DBT = WBT at saturation) the air would reach if it were perfectly conditioned (BPF = 0). It is also called the **Coil Apparatus Dew Point (CADP)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations to Memorise for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_1 - T_2)$ | Sensible heat rate of air | **W** or **kW** |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_a \, (W_1 - W_2) \, h_{fg}$ | Latent heat rate of air | **W** or **kW** |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{tot} = \dot{m}_a \, (h_1 - h_2)$ | Total cooling from enthalpy drop | **W** or **kW** |
| 4 | $SHF = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L}$ | Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $ESHF = \dfrac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$ | Effective SHF (room basis) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | By-Pass Factor of coil | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CF = 1 - BPF$ | Contact Factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v_a}$ | Mass flow rate from volume flow | **kg/s** |
| 9 | $RTH = RSH + RLH$ | Room Total Heat | **W** or **kW** |
| 10 | $GTH = RTH + \dot{m}_{OA} (h_{OA} - h_{RA})$ | Grand Total Heat at coil | **W** or **kW** |
| 11 | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{tot}}{c_{p,w} (T_{w,r} - T_{w,s})}$ | Chilled-water mass flow | **kg/s** |
| 12 | $\dot{Q}_{coil} = \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, (T_{wr} - T_{ws})$ | Coil duty (water side) | **W** or **kW** |

> **CRITICAL Notation Note:** In all KTU questions, $T_1, T_2, T_3$ refer to air states: $T_1$ = entering coil (mixed air), $T_2$ = leaving coil (supply air), $T_3 = T_{ADP}$ = coil surface saturation temperature.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Cooling Analysis in Marine Engineering

1. **Sizing of Compressors and Chillers** — The total cooling load (in TR — Tonnes of Refrigeration, 1 TR ≈ **3.517 kW**) directly determines the compressor displacement and motor rating. Cruise ships of 6,000 passengers have plant capacities exceeding **5,000–7,000 TR**.
2. **Duct and Diffuser Design** — The supply-air temperature $T_2$ (after the coil) is used to size the duct cross-section to deliver the required air quantity $\dot{V}$ without drafts (NC ≤ 45 in passenger cabins).
3. **Selection of Chilled-Water Temperature** — A lower $T_{ws}$ allows a smaller air flow (and hence smaller ducts) but increases compressor work. The optimum is at $T_{ws} \approx$ **6 °C to 7 °C**.
4. **Provisions Plant Design** — Separate from comfort AC, the provision rooms (meat, vegetable, dairy) use the **same cooling principles** but with strict $T$ and $RH$ set points (typically +2 °C and 75 % RH for chilled, –18 °C for frozen).
5. **Energy Management** — The $SHF/ESHF$ of the conditioned space dictates whether reheat or face-bypass control is more energy-efficient for part-load operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sensible Heat Factor Relation

Starting from the definition of sensible and latent heat transfer of moist air across a cooling section:

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Express sensible heat rate

The sensible heat rate is the rate at which the *sensible* (temperature-related) internal energy of the moist air is reduced:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_1 - T_2)
$$

Here, $\dot{m}_a$ is the mass flow rate of **dry air** (kg/s), $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K is the specific heat of dry air, and $(T_1 - T_2)$ is the dry-bulb temperature reduction across the coil.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Express latent heat rate

The latent heat rate is the rate at which water vapour is condensed out of the air:

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_a \, (W_1 - W_2) \, h_{fg,0}
$$

Here, $W_1$ and $W_2$ are the humidity ratios (kg water vapour per kg dry air) at the inlet and outlet, and $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporisation of water at 0 °C (a standard reference used in psychrometric tables).

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Define the ratio

By definition, the **Sensible Heat Factor** is the ratio of the sensible portion to the total cooling:

$$
SHF = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L} = \frac{c_{p,a} (T_1 - T_2)}{c_{p,a} (T_1 - T_2) + (W_1 - W_2) h_{fg,0}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Substitute numerical constants for evaluation

Using $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg, the formula becomes:

$$
SHF = \frac{1.005 \, (T_1 - T_2)}{1.005 \, (T_1 - T_2) + 2501 \, (W_1 - W_2)}
$$

This is the canonical form used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the By-Pass Factor (BPF) from Energy Balance on a Cooling Coil

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Conceptual model

The actual air stream leaving the coil can be **modelled as a mixture** of two hypothetical streams:
- Stream A: a fraction $CF$ of the inlet air, fully cooled to the ADP state.
- Stream B: a fraction $BPF$ of the inlet air, bypassing without change.

Therefore:

$$
BPF + CF = 1
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Energy balance on the temperature (sensible) axis

The outlet temperature of the mixed stream is the mass-weighted average:

$$
T_2 = (BPF) \cdot T_1 + (CF) \cdot T_{ADP}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Substitute CF = 1 − BPF

$$
T_2 = (BPF) \cdot T_1 + (1 - BPF) \cdot T_{ADP}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Solve for BPF

Rearranging:

$$
T_2 - T_{ADP} = BPF (T_1 - T_{ADP})
$$

$$
\boxed{BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}}
$$

This is the **standard BPF expression** used in KTU exam questions. By analogous reasoning for the humidity ratio:

$$
BPF = \frac{W_2 - W_{ADP}}{W_1 - W_{ADP}}
$$

and the same value must result from both expressions (consistency check in the valuation).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Scheme Style

**Problem Statement (Model):**
> An accommodation block on a container ship receives **2.5 kg/s** of dry air at $T_1 = 32$ °C, $W_1 = 0.0180$ kg/kg. The cooling coil has an **ADP of 10 °C** and a **BPF of 0.15**. Determine: (a) the supply-air state, (b) the total cooling load in kW, (c) the SHF, and (d) the chilled-water flow rate for $T_{ws} = 6$ °C and $T_{wr} = 12$ °C.

### 3.3.1 Part (a) — Supply air state

Using the BPF formula on temperature:

$$
T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF (T_1 - T_{ADP}) = 10 + 0.15 \, (32 - 10) = 10 + 3.30 = 13.30 \text{ °C}
$$

For humidity ratio, $W_{ADP} = W_s(10 \, °C)$. From steam tables, $p_{ws}(10\,°C) = 1.228$ kPa, hence:

$$
W_{ADP} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.228}{101.325 - 1.228} = 0.00763 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_2 = W_{ADP} + BPF (W_1 - W_{ADP}) = 0.00763 + 0.15 \, (0.0180 - 0.00763) = 0.00763 + 0.00155 = 0.00918 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

### 3.3.2 Part (b) — Total cooling load

Enthalpies (using $h = 1.005 T + W (2501 + 1.88 T)$ in kJ/kg-dry-air):

State 1: $h_1 = 1.005(32) + 0.0180(2501 + 1.88 \times 32) = 32.16 + 0.0180 \times 2561.16 = 32.16 + 46.10 = 78.26$ kJ/kg

State 2: $h_2 = 1.005(13.30) + 0.00918(2501 + 1.88 \times 13.30) = 13.37 + 0.00918 \times 2526.00 = 13.37 + 23.19 = 36.56$ kJ/kg

Total cooling:

$$
\dot{Q}_{tot} = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = 2.5 \times (78.26 - 36.56) = 2.5 \times 41.70 = 104.25 \text{ kW}
$$

### 3.3.3 Part (c) — SHF

Sensible heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = 2.5 \times 1.005 \times (32 - 13.30) = 2.5 \times 1.005 \times 18.70 = 46.98 \text{ kW}
$$

Latent heat:

$$
\dot{Q}_L = 104.25 - 46.98 = 57.27 \text{ kW}
$$

Sensible Heat Factor:

$$
SHF = \frac{46.98}{104.25} = 0.4507 \approx 0.45
$$

### 3.3.4 Part (d) — Chilled-water flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{\dot{Q}_{tot}}{c_{p,w} (T_{wr} - T_{ws})} = \frac{104.25}{4.186 \times (12 - 6)} = \frac{104.25}{25.116} = 4.151 \text{ kg/s}
$$

In volumetric terms:

$$
\dot{V}_w = \frac{4.151}{1000} = 4.151 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 14.94 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Identification on the Psychrometric Chart

| Process Description | DBT | W | RH | h | Line on Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible cooling | ↓ | const | ↑ | ↓ | Horizontal |
| Latent cooling (dehumidification) | const | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Vertical |
| Combined cooling (coil) | ↓ | ↓ | varies | ↓ | Sloped toward ADP |
| Heating + humidification | ↑ | ↑ | varies | ↑ | Sloped upward |
| Mixing of two air streams | between | between | — | mass-weighted avg | Straight line |
| Adiabatic saturation (evaporative) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ (to 100 %) | const | Along wet-bulb line |

---

## 3.5 Energy-Saving Reheat Cycle Derivation

For high SHF applications (e.g., tropical cabins where latent load is moderate), the **reheat cycle** prevents overcooling of the supply air:

$$
\dot{Q}_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{2,reheat} - T_{2,coil})
$$

The **Grand Total Heat (GTH)** including reheat is:

$$
GTH = \dot{Q}_{cool,coil} + \dot{Q}_{reheat}
$$

This is fundamental to part-load control on board where the chilled-water temperature may not be variable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Marine AC Cooling Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Return Air from Cabin] --> B[Mixing Plenum]
    C[Fresh Air from Vent] --> B
    B --> D[Pre-Filter and Salt-Laden Air Eliminator]
    D --> E[Cooling Coil Chilled Water 6 to 8 degC]
    E --> F[Condensate Drain Pan]
    E --> G[Supply Air Plenum]
    G --> H[Supply Fan]
    H --> I[Duct Network]
    I --> J[Diffuser in Cabin]
    J --> K[Conditioned Cabin Space]
    K --> A
    L[Sea Water In 30 degC] --> M[Shell and Tube Condenser]
    N[Refrigerant Vapor from Compressor] --> M
    M --> O[Refrigerant Liquid]
    O --> P[Expansion Valve]
    P --> Q[Chilled Water Evaporator]
    Q --> R[Compressor]
    R --> N
    Q --> S[Chilled Water 6 degC to Cooling Coil]
    S --> E
    E --> T[Chilled Water Return 12 degC]
    T --> Q
```

**Functional Architecture Notes:**
- The **mixing plenum** combines return air and fresh (ventilation) air in a fixed proportion — typically 70 % return / 30 % fresh for accommodation.
- The **salt-laden air eliminator** (often a *self-cleaning filter* with electrostatic precipitator) is a marine-specific addition to prevent corrosion of the cooling coil fins.
- The **chilled-water loop** is a *secondary refrigerant system* (chilled water + R-134a/HFC primary), standard on passenger ships for safety (no refrigerant in occupied spaces).
- The **sea-water condenser** is the heat-rejection element; the cooling load ultimately goes to the sea.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Psychrometric Process Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1 Outside Air 35 C W 0.0200] --> B[State M Mixed Air 31 C W 0.0160]
    C[State R Return Air 24 C W 0.0090] --> B
    B --> D[State 2 Supply Air 13 C W 0.0092]
    E[ADP Point 10 C Wsat 0.0076] --> D
    D --> F[State 3 Room 24 C W 0.0090]
    F --> A
    F --> C
```

**Explanation of States:**
- **State 1 (OA):** Outside (tropical) air — high DBT, high W.
- **State R (RA):** Return air from cabin — design indoor condition.
- **State M:** Mixture of OA and RA, plotted on the straight line connecting 1 and R.
- **State 2:** Supply air leaving the coil, plotted on the line from M toward the ADP point.
- **ADP (State 3 on coil surface):** Saturation state of the coil surface.
- **State 3 (Room):** Indoor design condition, reached as supply air absorbs room sensible + latent loads.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Control Loops for a Marine AC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Room Temperature Sensor PT100] --> C1[PID Controller]
    S2[Room RH Sensor Capacitive] --> C1
    C1 --> V1[Chilled Water 3-Way Modulating Valve]
    V1 --> CL[Cooling Coil]
    S3[Supply Air DBT Sensor] --> C1
    S4[Chilled Water Supply Temp Sensor] --> C1
    S5[Sea Water Inlet Temp Sensor] --> C2[Compressor Capacity Controller]
    C2 --> CP[Compressor Capacity 25 to 100 percent]
    CP --> COND[Condenser]
    S6[Condenser Pressure Transducer] --> C2
    S7[Suction Pressure Transducer] --> C2
    S8[Cabin CO2 Sensor NDIR] --> C3[Fresh Air Damper Actuator]
    C3 --> FD[Fresh Air Damper]
    S9[Duct Static Pressure Sensor] --> C4[VFD for Supply Fan]
    C4 --> FAN[Supply Fan VFD]
```

**Control Philosophy:**
- **Temperature & RH** are controlled by modulating the chilled-water flow (3-way valve) — this varies the coil's effective capacity without changing supply-air quantity.
- **Compressor capacity** is controlled by the *suction pressure* (cut-in/cut-out) and the *condenser pressure* (via sea-water regulating valve or compressor unloading).
- **Fresh-air quantity** is modulated by cabin CO₂ level (typically setpoint **800 ppm**).
- **Fan energy** is saved by a *variable frequency drive (VFD)* that holds duct static pressure constant, a major energy-saving feature on modern eco-ships.

---

## 4.4 Pressure–Enthalpy Indication (Schematic Only — Coil on P-h Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Entering Air State 1] --> B[Air-side BPF Mixing Zone]
    B --> C[Leaving Air State 2]
    D[Coil Surface ADP] --> B
    E[Chilled Water In 6 C] --> F[Tube-side Heat Transfer]
    F --> G[Chilled Water Out 12 C]
    C -.Heat Q.-> F
```

This representation is the **air-side energy balance** that mirrors the chilled-water-side equation. The two must be equal at steady state:

$$
\dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{wr} - T_{ws})
$$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

### Q1. Define "Sensible Heat Factor" (SHF) of an air-conditioning system. Why is the "Effective SHF" (ESHF) used for coil selection instead of the simple SHF? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Sensible Heat Factor is defined as the ratio of the sensible heat to the total heat (sensible + latent) of the conditioned space:

$$
SHF = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L}
$$

The **Effective SHF (ESHF)** uses the *Room* sensible and latent heats, i.e.:

$$
ESHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}
$$

ESHF is used for **coil selection** because the coil handles the *mixed-air* state (return air + fresh air), not the room state. Since part of the room latent load is satisfied by re-circulated (return) air, the latent load seen by the coil is lower than the room latent load; thus ESHF > SHF. Using ESHF correctly matches the coil's process line to the actual psychrometric transformation required.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

### Q2. What is the "By-Pass Factor" (BPF) of a cooling coil? A coil has surface temperature 8 °C and handles air from 30 °C to 18 °C. Calculate BPF. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

BPF is the fraction of the entering air that *bypasses* the cooling coil without undergoing any heat or moisture exchange:

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

Substituting $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_2 = 18$ °C, $T_{ADP} = 8$ °C:

$$
BPF = \frac{18 - 8}{30 - 8} = \frac{10}{22} = 0.4545
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 3 Choice, Dec 2024 Model]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> A passenger-cabin AHU on a cruise ship handles **5.0 kg/s** of dry air entering the cooling coil at **30 °C DBT, 60 % RH**. The coil has an **ADP of 9 °C** and a **BPF of 0.18**. The chilled-water supply and return temperatures are **6 °C and 12 °C** respectively.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the supply-air condition (DBT, W, RH, enthalpy) and the total, sensible, and latent cooling loads.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Find (i) the SHF, (ii) the chilled-water mass flow rate, and (iii) the BPF expressed on a humidity-ratio basis (check consistency).

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Saturation pressure at $T_1 = 30$ °C:** $p_{ws}(30) = 4.246$ kPa.

**Step 2 — Partial vapour pressure:** $p_w = 0.60 \times 4.246 = 2.548$ kPa.

**Step 3 — Humidity ratio at inlet:**

$$
W_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = 0.01604 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy at inlet:**

$$
h_1 = 1.005(30) + 0.01604(2501 + 1.88 \times 30) = 30.15 + 0.01604 \times 2557.4 = 30.15 + 41.02 = 71.17 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — ADP saturation humidity ratio at 9 °C:** $p_{ws}(9) = 1.148$ kPa, hence:

$$
W_{ADP} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.148}{101.325 - 1.148} = 0.00713 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Supply-air DBT:**

$$
T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF (T_1 - T_{ADP}) = 9 + 0.18 (30 - 9) = 9 + 3.78 = 12.78 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 7 — Supply-air humidity ratio:**

$$
W_2 = W_{ADP} + BPF (W_1 - W_{ADP}) = 0.00713 + 0.18 (0.01604 - 0.00713) = 0.00713 + 0.001604 = 0.00873 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**Step 8 — Supply-air enthalpy:**

$$
h_2 = 1.005(12.78) + 0.00873(2501 + 1.88 \times 12.78) = 12.84 + 0.00873 \times 2525.0 = 12.84 + 22.04 = 34.88 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 9 — Supply-air RH at 12.78 °C:** $p_{ws}(12.78) \approx 1.484$ kPa, hence $p_w = \frac{0.00873 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.00873} = 1.403$ kPa, and:

$$
\phi_2 = \frac{1.403}{1.484} = 0.9454 = 94.5\%
$$

**Step 10 — Loads:**

Total: $\dot{Q}_{tot} = 5.0 \times (71.17 - 34.88) = 5.0 \times 36.29 = 181.45$ kW
Sensible: $\dot{Q}_s = 5.0 \times 1.005 \times (30 - 12.78) = 5.0 \times 1.005 \times 17.22 = 86.53$ kW
Latent: $\dot{Q}_L = 181.45 - 86.53 = 94.92$ kW

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) SHF:**

$$
SHF = \frac{86.53}{181.45} = 0.4770
$$

**(ii) Chilled-water flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{181.45}{4.186 \times (12 - 6)} = \frac{181.45}{25.116} = 7.224 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(iii) BPF on humidity-ratio basis:**

$$
BPF_W = \frac{W_2 - W_{ADP}}{W_1 - W_{ADP}} = \frac{0.00873 - 0.00713}{0.01604 - 0.00713} = \frac{0.00160}{0.00891} = 0.1796 \approx 0.18
$$

This is consistent (within rounding) with the temperature-based BPF = 0.18, confirming the coil model is internally consistent.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> An accommodation block of a naval vessel has a **Room Total Heat (RTH) of 80 kW**, comprising **RSH = 56 kW** and **RLH = 24 kW**. The return-air condition is **26 °C DBT, 50 % RH**, and outdoor air is **34 °C DBT, 70 % RH**. The ventilation requirement is **30 % of total air** to the coil. The coil's **ADP = 10 °C** and **BPF = 0.20**.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the (i) ESHF, (ii) mixed-air condition at coil inlet, and (iii) the supply-air DBT and W.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine (i) the chilled-water flow rate for $T_{ws} = 6$ °C and $T_{wr} = 13$ °C, (ii) the contact factor, and (iii) the percentage by which the coil's latent capacity exceeds the room latent load.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**(i) ESHF:**

$$
ESHF = \frac{RSH}{RTH} = \frac{56}{80} = 0.700
$$

**(ii) Mixed-air state:** Let $r$ = fraction of outdoor air = 0.30, $(1-r)$ = 0.70 return.

Mixed DBT (mass-weighted, since mass flow ratio equals volume flow ratio at approximately equal specific volumes):

$$
T_M = 0.30 (34) + 0.70 (26) = 10.2 + 18.2 = 28.4 \text{ °C}
$$

Humidity ratios:
- $W_{OA}$ at 34 °C, 70 %: $p_{ws}(34) = 5.324$ kPa, $p_w = 3.727$ kPa, $W_{OA} = 0.622 \times 3.727/(101.325 - 3.727) = 0.02377$ kg/kg
- $W_{RA}$ at 26 °C, 50 %: $p_{ws}(26) = 3.363$ kPa, $p_w = 1.682$ kPa, $W_{RA} = 0.622 \times 1.682/(101.325 - 1.682) = 0.01052$ kg/kg

$$
W_M = 0.30 (0.02377) + 0.70 (0.01052) = 0.007131 + 0.007364 = 0.01450 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**(iii) Supply-air state:**
$T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF (T_M - T_{ADP}) = 10 + 0.20(28.4 - 10) = 10 + 3.68 = 13.68$ °C

$W_{ADP}$ at 10 °C: 0.00763 kg/kg (from previous)

$W_2 = 0.00763 + 0.20(0.01450 - 0.00763) = 0.00763 + 0.001374 = 0.00900$ kg/kg

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Chilled-water flow rate:**
Enthalpies:
$h_M = 1.005(28.4) + 0.01450(2501 + 1.88 \times 28.4) = 28.54 + 0.01450 \times 2554.4 = 28.54 + 37.04 = 65.58$ kJ/kg
$h_2 = 1.005(13.68) + 0.00900(2501 + 1.88 \times 13.68) = 13.75 + 0.00900 \times 2526.7 = 13.75 + 22.74 = 36.49$ kJ/kg
$\Delta h = 29.09$ kJ/kg
$\dot{m}_a = \frac{RTH + \text{vent. correction}}{c_{p,a}(T_1 - T_2) + (W_1 - W_2) h_{fg}} = $ solved iteratively; for simplicity, use $GTH = RTH + \dot{m}_{OA}(h_{OA} - h_{RA})$:
$h_{OA} = 1.005(34) + 0.02377(2501 + 1.88 \times 34) = 34.17 + 0.02377 \times 2564.9 = 34.17 + 60.97 = 95.14$ kJ/kg
$h_{RA} = 1.005(26) + 0.01052(2501 + 1.88 \times 26) = 26.13 + 0.01052 \times 2549.9 = 26.13 + 26.82 = 52.95$ kJ/kg

Outdoor air fraction load = $0.30 \times (h_{OA} - h_{RA}) = 0.30 \times (95.14 - 52.95) = 0.30 \times 42.19 = 12.66$ kJ/kg per unit dry-air-flow fraction.

The required dry-air flow can be obtained from room energy balance; assume for the question $\dot{m}_a = 5.0$ kg/s.

Total coil load = $\dot{m}_a (h_M - h_2) = 5.0 \times (65.58 - 36.49) = 145.45$ kW.

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{145.45}{4.186 \times (13 - 6)} = \frac{145.45}{29.302} = 4.964 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Contact Factor:**

$$
CF = 1 - BPF = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 \quad \text{or} \quad 80\%
$$

**(iii) Coil latent capacity vs room latent load:**

Coil latent load = $\dot{m}_a (W_M - W_2) h_{fg} = 5.0 \times (0.01450 - 0.00900) \times 2501 = 5.0 \times 0.0055 \times 2501 = 68.78$ kW.

Room latent load = 24 kW.

Excess percentage = $\dfrac{68.78 - 24}{24} \times 100 = 186.6\%$.

> This result is **physically correct** — the coil handles latent load from *both* the outdoor air and the return air, so its latent capacity always exceeds the room's latent load by a substantial margin. This is the reason why the **ESHF approach** correctly accounts for coil sizing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cooling Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing $W$ and $W$ in vapour-pressure formulas** — Students often confuse the humidity ratio $W$ (kg/kg dry air) with specific humidity (kg/kg moist air). Always use $W = 0.622 \, p_w / (p - p_w)$ and clearly label "kg water per kg dry air".
> 2. **ADP vs Coil Surface Temperature confusion** — The ADP is the *thermodynamic saturation state*; the actual coil surface temperature is generally **slightly higher** than ADP (typically by 1–2 °C) due to non-equilibrium condensation. Use ADP for the BPF equation, not the surface temperature.
> 3. **Forgetting to use $c_p$ on the moist-air basis** — For low humidity, the dry-air $c_p$ (1.005 kJ/kg·K) is acceptable; for high-precision KTU answers, the **moist-air $c_p$** ($1.005 + 1.88 W$) should be used in enthalpy expressions.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in chilled-water calculations** — Use $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K; ensure $\dot{Q}$ is in kW and $\dot{m}_w$ comes out in kg/s.
> 5. **Treating BPF as constant for part-load** — BPF changes slightly with air velocity and coil loading. For a single steady-state design problem, assume constant. **Do not apply BPF iteratively across multiple states without explicit justification.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before KTU ESE):**

- ☐ **SHF** = sensible / total heat; **ESHF** = RSH / RTH. ESHF > SHF always.
- ☐ **BPF** = $(T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$; also valid on humidity-ratio basis.
- ☐ **CF** = $1 - BPF$ (Contact Factor).
- ☐ **ADP** is a *saturation state* on the psychrometric chart; air is cooled *toward* it but never *to* it.
- ☐ Sensible cooling is a **horizontal line** on the psychrometric chart (constant W).
- ☐ Latent cooling (dehumidification) is a **vertical line** (constant T).
- ☐ Combined cooling is a **sloped line** from inlet toward ADP, governed by BPF.
- ☐ **Total load** = $\dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2)$; **Sensible load** = $\dot{m}_a c_{p,a} (T_1 - T_2)$; **Latent load** = $\dot{m}_a (W_1 - W_2) h_{fg,0}$.
- ☐ **Marine AC** = chilled-water secondary loop + R-134a/HFC primary; sea-water condenser; salt-laden air filter mandatory.
- ☐ Chilled-water **$\Delta T$** typically 5–7 K; chilled-water **$T_{ws}$** typically 6 °C; **$T_{wr}$** typically 12–14 °C.
- ☐ For the **reheat cycle**, the chilled-water flow may be reduced at part-load and the re-heater handles trim.
- ☐ Provision rooms are **NOT** comfort AC — they use **direct-expansion** with strict set-points and high RH control.
- ☐ Always verify **BPF on both temperature and humidity-ratio** axes; consistency is part of the full-mark answer in KTU valuation.
- ☐ **Design condition** for a tropical ship OA is 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT; IA is 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH.
- ☐ The **1 TR = 3.517 kW** conversion must be used whenever a question asks for plant capacity in "tonnes of refrigeration".
- ☐ For the chilled-water mass flow: $\dot{m}_w = \dot{Q}_{tot} / [c_{p,w} (T_{wr} - T_{ws})]$.
- ☐ Air-handling unit (AHU) for marine applications: mixing box → filters → cooling coil → fan → ductwork; condensate drain must be **trapped** to prevent reverse flow under ship motion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Arrangement and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principal Arrangement and Equipment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Principal Arrangement** of a marine refrigeration and air-conditioning plant refers to the systematic, engineered layout, interconnection, and spatial disposition of the major thermodynamic, fluid-handling, and auxiliary components — compressors, condensers, evaporators, expansion devices, receivers, heat exchangers, pumps, fans, ducting, controls, and safety devices — within the envelope of a seagoing vessel, so as to deliver specified cooling/heating duty under dynamic maritime operating conditions while satisfying Classification Society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV), SOLAS, MARPOL, and the IGC/IGF Codes. **Equipment**, in this KTU context, denotes the integrated set of mechanical, thermodynamic, electrical, and control hardware that constitutes the working plant — *not* the building services accessories. The arrangement is therefore not merely "plumbing"; it is a regulated engineering deliverable governed by redundancy, separation, and failure-mode analysis. | Parameter | Typical Shipboard Value | |---|---| | **Plant COP** | 2.8 – 3.6 (modern screw plant with economizer) | | **Design ambient (tropical)** | **45 °C DB / 28 °C WB** (outside air) | | **Design accommodation** | **24–27 °C DB / 50 % RH** | | **Sea-water inlet temperature** | **32 °C** (tropical) / **15 °C** (temperate) | | **Plant location** | Engine room / dedicated refrigeration machinery room | | **Specific cooling load** | 100 – 180 W/m² (accommodation) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship as a Floating Refrigerator" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine your household refrigerator. Now strap it onto a ship that pitches 12°, rolls 30°, vibrates from a main engine, and runs in 50 °C Persian Gulf water. The same compression cycle must work, but every choice — *where* to mount the compressor, *how* to support the condenser against shock, *whether* one chiller is enough — becomes a maritime engineering decision. The principal arrangement is the **blueprint of how all the "organs" of the cold plant are wired together on a moving, vibrating, space-starved platform** that must not leak refrigerant into the engine room or accommodation. ### 1.3 The Two Functional Halves 1. **Refrigerant Side (Hermetic Loop):** Compressor → Condenser → Receiver → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → Accumulator → back to compressor. 2. **Secondary Fluid / Air Side (Heat Rejection & Distribution):** Sea-water circuit (condenser cooling), chilled-water circuit (for AHUs), ventilation ducting, and return-air paths. These two halves exchange heat through the **evaporator coil** and the **condenser coil** — the two thermal "lungs" of the plant. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Logic of a Marine Plant A marine refrigeration/AC arrangement is laid out in **four logical layers**, each with a distinct engineering purpose: ### Layer 1 — Compression & High-Pressure Side - **Compressor** (reciprocating / screw / centrifugal — screw dominates in modern ships). - **Oil separator** at discharge (98–99 % separation efficiency, returns oil to crankcase). - **Condenser** (shell-and-tube with sea-water through tubes, refrigerant on shell side — *or* plate heat exchanger with sea-water). - **Liquid receiver** (storage + surge volume + pump-out for refrigerant management). ### Layer 2 — Expansion & Low-Pressure Side - **Liquid-line filter-drier** (moisture + particulate removal; molecular sieve core). - **Solenoid valve** (electrical shut-off). - **Sight glass + moisture indicator**. - **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** or **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)**. - **Distributor** (for multi-circuit evaporators). ### Layer 3 — Evaporation & Load Side - **Evaporator / Cooling coil** (DX coil in AHU, or shell-and-tube chiller for chilled water). - **Suction-line accumulator** (protects compressor from liquid slugging). - **Suction-line filter**. - **Insulated suction line** returning to compressor suction. ### Layer 4 — Secondary Fluid Distribution - **Sea-water cooling pumps** (centrifugal, bronze or stainless impeller). - **Fresh-water (chilled-water) pumps** for central AC systems. - **Air Handling Units (AHUs)** with fans, filters (G4 + F7), heating/cooling coils, humidifiers. - **Ductwork** with grilles, dampers, and sound attenuators. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the order matters:** The arrangement is dictated by the **second law** — heat must flow from low to high temperature, with the compressor supplying the work. Any reversed component sequence is physically impossible without violating the **Clausius inequality** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$. ## 2.2 Ship-Specific Design Drivers The "principal arrangement" is not a textbook cycle — it is a **constrained optimisation** under the following maritime pressures: - **Hull motion & inclination:** Components must function at **static angles up to 22.5°** (LR/DNV for machinery) and **dynamic roll up to ±30°** — dictates use of flooded evaporators or pump-circulated systems, **not** gravity-dependent risers. - **Shock & vibration:** Compressors on **resilient rubber/metal mounts**; flexible couplings on piping; flexible ducts at AHU connections. - **Space:** Typical engine-room refrigeration plant occupies **8–25 m²** for a 200 kW plant. - **Redundancy:** SOLAS Ch. II-1 / Classification Rules require **N+1** redundancy for essential services (e.g., provision rooms). - **Refrigerant safety:** Toxicity (ammonia) and flammability (propane/R-290) restrictions dictate **machinery-room location, not accommodation**. - **Materials:** **90/10 Cu-Ni tubes**, **stainless 316L plates**, **galvanised steel ductwork** to resist salt-laden air. ## 2.3 Centralised vs Distributed Arrangement | Feature | Centralised Plant | Distributed Plant | |---|---|---| | Plant location | Engine room / dedicated refrigeration room | Multiple AHUs at each deck | | Refrigerant inventory | High (long runs) | Low (short runs) | | Maintenance | Easy (one place) | Scattered | | Failure consequence | Catastrophic (whole ship affected) | Localised | | Vibration / noise | Contained | Distributed (sleeping quarters!) | | Adopted in | Modern cruise liners, naval ships | Small vessels, ferry cabins | Modern cruise ships use a **hybrid arrangement** — central chiller plant producing chilled water (6–8 °C) at engine room level, distributed via **ring-main chilled-water piping** to AHUs on every accommodation deck. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **canonical** marine-plant relationships. Note the units carefully — examiners in KTU ESE frequently deduct marks for unit inconsistencies. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate | W | | 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat in evaporator (refrigeration effect) | W | | 3 | $\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}$ | Sea-water flow for condenser | m³/s | | 4 | $\dot{V}_{chw} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{c_{p,w} \cdot \rho_{w} \cdot \Delta T_{chw}}$ | Chilled-water flow rate | m³/s | | 5 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of performance | — | | 6 | $Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{roof} + Q_{floor} + Q_{infil} + Q_{internal}$ | Total shipboard cooling load | W | | 7 | $Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{corrected}$ | Heat through ship structure (corrected for solar & adjacent spaces) | W | | 8 | $\Delta T_{corrected} = CLTD + LM + (T_{room}-T_{out})$ | ASHRAE marine correction | K | | 9 | $W = \dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work (per kg refrigerant) | J/kg | | 10 | $q_{evap} = h_1 - h_4$ | Specific refrigeration effect | J/kg | | 11 | $\dot{m}_r = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{q_{evap}}$ | Required refrigerant mass flow | kg/s | | 12 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ | Second-law efficiency | — | > **Critical Pitfall:** Never write $\Delta T$ as $\Delta t$ — use Kelvin for *differences*, °C for *levels* (KTU strict convention). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The arrangement study is the **bridge between thermodynamics and shipyard installation**. In practice, the naval architect's General Arrangement (GA) drawing of the refrigeration machinery room must satisfy: 1. **Maintainability** — 600 mm access around compressor for cylinder-head withdrawal. 2. **Ventilation** — 25 air changes/hour for ammonia machinery rooms. 3. **Drainage** — 1:100 floor fall to bilge. 4. **Fire-fighting** — CO₂ / NOVEC flooding system readiness. 5. **Escape** — Two exits, diametrically opposite, for ammonia plants (IGC Code §11.3). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every line below is fully expanded. No "and so on" or "similarly" shortcuts. ## 3.1 Derivation — Sea-Water Flow Rate for a Marine Condenser This is the most-tested calculation in KTU Module 3 (see Section 5, Q. A-1). ### Given (typical KTU problem data): - Total refrigeration load: $Q_{evap} = 350 \text{ kW}$ - Compressor electrical input: $W_{comp} = 110 \text{ kW}$ - Sea-water inlet: $T_{sw,in} = 30 \text{ °C}$ - Sea-water outlet (allowed): $T_{sw,out} = 35 \text{ °C}$ - $c_{p,sw} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (≈ fresh-water at marine salinity) - $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ ### Step 1 — Energy balance on refrigerant cycle $$\begin{aligned} Q_{cond} &= Q_{evap} + W_{comp} \\[4pt] &= 350 + 110 \\[4pt] &= 460 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ *Logic:* **First Law** for a cyclic, steady-flow refrigeration plant — all refrigeration effect plus all compressor work must be rejected at the condenser. There is no other heat sink. ### Step 2 — Energy balance on sea-water stream $$\begin{aligned} Q_{cond} &= \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw} \\[4pt] \dot{m}_{sw} &= \frac{Q_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{460}{4.18 \times (35 - 30)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{460}{4.18 \times 5} \\[4pt] &= \frac{460}{20.90} \\[4pt] &= 22.01 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow $$\begin{aligned} \dot{V}_{sw} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{22.01}{1025} \\[4pt] &= 0.02147 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt] &= 77.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Pump sizing (auxiliary) $$\begin{aligned} P_{pump} &= \frac{\dot{V}_{sw} \cdot \Delta p_{sw}}{\eta_{pump}} \\[4pt] \Delta p_{sw} &= 150 \text{ kPa (condenser + pipework + strainer loss)} \\[4pt] P_{pump} &= \frac{0.02147 \times 150{,}000}{0.70} \\[4pt] &= 4601 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.6 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ This 4.6 kW is then added to the **total auxiliary load** of the plant, an item often forgotten by KTU candidates (see Pitfall callout in Section 5). ## 3.2 Derivation — Corrected Heat Gain Through a Ship's Steel Bulkhead A KTU favourite. Given a bulkhead separating the air-conditioned accommodation from a hot tropical deck-head zone. ### Data - Steel bulkhead thickness: $t = 8 \text{ mm}$ - Insulation (rockwool): $t_{ins} = 50 \text{ mm}$, $k_{ins} = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K}$ - Inside air: $T_i = 24 \text{ °C}$ - Outside air: $T_o = 38 \text{ °C}$, plus solar radiation: $I = 350 \text{ W/m}^2$ on outer surface - Inside surface film coefficient: $h_i = 9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ - Outside surface film coefficient: $h_o = 23 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ (ship-side, wind-affected) - Bulkhead area: $A = 18 \text{ m}^2$ - $k_{steel} = 45 \text{ W/m·K}$ ### Step 1 — Total thermal resistance (per unit area) $$\begin{aligned} R_{tot} &= \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{t_{steel}}{k_{steel}} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{9} + \frac{0.008}{45} + \frac{0.050}{0.04} + \frac{1}{23} \\[4pt] &= 0.1111 + 0.000178 + 1.2500 + 0.04348 \\[4pt] &= 1.4048 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Overall heat-transfer coefficient $$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{1}{R_{tot}} = \frac{1}{1.4048} = 0.7119 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Solar-augmented temperature difference (sol-air temperature) $$\begin{aligned} T_{sol-air} &= T_o + \frac{\alpha \cdot I}{h_o} \\[4pt] &= 38 + \frac{0.7 \times 350}{23} \\[4pt] &= 38 + 10.65 \\[4pt] &= 48.65 \text{ °C} \end{aligned}$$ where $\alpha = 0.7$ is the absorptivity of weathered ship-side steel. ### Step 4 — Heat gain through bulkhead $$\begin{aligned} Q_{wall} &= U \cdot A \cdot (T_{sol-air} - T_i) \\[4pt] &= 0.7119 \times 18 \times (48.65 - 24) \\[4pt] &= 0.7119 \times 18 \times 24.65 \\[4pt] &= 315.83 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Logical check A 0.7 mm–thin insulation **cannot** keep the bulkhead cool in the tropics — the dominant resistance is $R_{ins} = 1.25$ vs total $R_{tot} = 1.40$, so the insulation is doing $\dfrac{1.25}{1.40} \approx 89\%$ of the thermal blocking. This is exactly why KTU examiners expect a 50–75 mm rock-wool minimum. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Equipment Selection | Vessel Type | Compressor Choice | Refrigerant | Evaporator | Justification | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cruise ship | Screw (oil-injected) | R-134a | Plate heat exchanger (chilled water) | High capacity, smooth operation, efficiency | | Reefer cargo | Screw (economised) | R-22 / R-404A | Direct-expansion in air coolers | Low-temperature capability | | Naval warship | Hermetic scroll | R-134a | DX coil in AHU | Vibration tolerance, sealed for EMC | | LNG carrier | Centrifugal | Nitrogen / mixed | Brazed-plate | Inert, oil-free, classified-service | | Fishing vessel | Reciprocating (open) | R-22 / R-404A | Plate, flooded | Cheap, robust, easy repair | | Yacht | Hermetic reciprocating | R-410A | DX coil | Compact, quiet | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Typical Marine Central AC Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Sea Water Inlet
32 C Through Hull] --> B1[Sea Water Strainer
Duplex Type] B1 --> C1[Sea Water Pump
Centrifugal Bronze] C1 --> D1[Tube Side
Condenser Shell and Tube] D1 --> E1[Sea Water Outlet
37 C Overboard] R1[Refrigerant
R134a] --> M1[Compressor
Screw Oil Injected] M1 --> N1[Oil Separator
98 Percent Efficiency] N1 --> O1[Discharge Line
with Check Valve] O1 --> D1 D1 --> P1[Liquid Receiver
with Sight Glass] P1 --> Q1[Filter Drier
Molecular Sieve] Q1 --> R2[Solenoid Valve
Shut Off] R2 --> S1[Sight Glass
Moisture Indicator] S1 --> T1[Thermostatic Expansion Valve
TXV with Equaliser] T1 --> U1[Distributor
Multi Circuit] U1 --> V1[Evaporator Chilled Water
Brazed Plate] V1 --> W1[Suction Accumulator
Anti Slug] W1 --> X1[Suction Line
Insulated] X1 --> M1 V1 --> Y1[Chilled Water Pump
Stainless Centrifugal] Y1 --> Z1[AHU 1 Accommodation Deck 5] Y1 --> Z2[AHU 2 Accommodation Deck 6] Y1 --> Z3[AHU 3 Accommodation Deck 7] Z1 --> AA1[Return Chilled Water 12 C] Z2 --> AA1 Z3 --> AA1 AA1 --> V1 AB1[Control Panel
PLC with HMI] -.-> M1 AB1 -.-> T1 AB1 -.-> Y1 AB1 -.-> C1 AB1 -.-> Z1 AB1 -.-> Z2 AB1 -.-> Z3 AC1[Safety
HP LP Oil Pressure] -.-> M1 AC2[Classification
DNV LR] -.-> AB1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **left column** (A→E) is the *sea-water heat-rejection loop*. - The **centre column** (R→X) is the *refrigerant hermetic cycle* — the heart of the plant. - The **bottom row** (V→Z) is the *chilled-water distribution ring-main* to accommodation. - The **dotted lines** are *control & instrumentation*, governed by the Classification-Society-approved PLC. ## 4.2 Component Interconnection Matrix | Upstream Component | Connects to | Via | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Compressor discharge | Oil separator | Hot-gas line, vibration loop | Removes oil before condenser | | Oil separator | Condenser | Hot-gas line | Oil-free refrigerant condenses efficiently | | Condenser | Receiver | Liquid line with filter-drier | Buffers liquid refrigerant | | Receiver | TXV | Liquid line with sight glass | Metered expansion | | TXV | Evaporator distributor | Distributor tubes | Even refrigerant distribution | | Evaporator | Accumulator | Suction line | Catches un-evaporated liquid | | Accumulator | Compressor suction | Insulated suction line | Returns dry vapour only | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A-1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > *With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the **principal arrangement** of a marine central air-conditioning plant serving the accommodation block of a passenger vessel.* **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** A central AC plant on a passenger vessel typically consists of: (i) **refrigerant side** — screw compressor → oil separator → shell-and-tube sea-water-cooled condenser → liquid receiver → filter-drier/sight glass → thermostatic expansion valve → brazed-plate evaporator, with suction-line accumulator returning to compressor; (ii) **secondary side** — chilled-water pump → ring main → multiple AHUs (with filters, DX/coil, fan) on each accommodation deck, returning chilled water to the evaporator; (iii) **heat-rejection side** — sea-water pump circulating through condenser tubes with overboard discharge. All components are located in a **dedicated refrigeration machinery room** at lower deck, with PLC-based control, HP/LP cut-outs, and oil-pressure safety interlocks per SOLAS Ch. II-1 and Classification rules (DNV/LR). *(Examiner awards 1 mark for refrigerant loop, 1 mark for secondary loop, 1 mark for safety & control.)* ### Question A-2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > *Why are flexible couplings and resilient mounts used on marine refrigeration compressors?* **Model Answer:** Marine compressors experience **continuous vibration** from the main engine, propeller, and auxiliary machinery, as well as **shock loads** (slams in heavy seas up to 5g) and **roll/pitch inclinations** up to ±30°. Resilient rubber/metal mounts **(1 mark)** isolate high-frequency vibration, preventing it from being transmitted to the ship's hull and radiating as structure-borne noise into accommodation. Flexible couplings on refrigerant piping **(1 mark)** accommodate thermal expansion, hull flexure, and relative motion between the compressor and its rigid pipework — preventing fatigue failure at brazed joints. This also satisfies Classification Society noise limits (typically ≤ 65 dB(A) in cabins) **(1 mark).** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Forgetting to convert mass flow to **volumetric flow** in m³/h loses 1 mark. > - Omitting the **solar-air correction** in ship-bulkhead heat-gain calculations loses 2 marks (very common error). > - Not stating **Classification Society** basis for redundancy loses 1 mark. > - Writing **refrigerant cycle in wrong direction** (e.g., evaporator → condenser) loses 2 marks outright. > - Forgetting to add **pump power** to total plant load loses 1 mark. ### Question A (14 Marks) — Central Plant Design **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Module 3 Focus]** > *(a)* With a neat schematic, explain the **principal arrangement of a marine central air-conditioning plant** serving a 6-deck accommodation block of a cruise vessel. Discuss the role of each major component. **(7 marks)** > > *(b)* A central chiller produces 480 kW of refrigeration. The chilled water leaves the evaporator at 6 °C and returns at 12 °C. Calculate the chilled-water mass flow rate and volumetric flow rate. The compressor consumes 145 kW. Determine the **condenser heat-rejection rate**, the **COP**, and the required **sea-water flow** if sea-water enters at 30 °C and leaves at 35 °C. Take $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)** #### Solution to A(a): A central plant comprises four functional blocks arranged physically across **lower-deck machinery room** and **distributed AHUs**: **(i) Refrigerant Cycle (4 marks):** Hermetic loop of R-134a — **screw compressor (1)** compresses low-pressure vapour to high-pressure superheated vapour; passes through **oil separator (½)** to recover lubricating oil and return to crankcase; enters **shell-and-tube condenser (1)** where sea-water inside tubes removes heat; **liquid receiver (½)** stores refrigerant and provides pump-out for servicing; **filter-drier + sight glass + solenoid valve** remove moisture and indicate condition; **thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) (½)** meters refrigerant into **plate evaporator (½)** where it boils at low pressure; **suction-line accumulator (½)** prevents liquid carry-over back to compressor. **(ii) Chilled-Water Secondary Circuit (1 mark):** Evaporator chills water to 6 °C; **chilled-water pump** circulates via insulated ring-main to AHUs. **(iii) AHU Distribution (1 mark):** Each deck has **Air Handling Unit** with G4+F7 filters, chilled-water coil, fan, return-air grille. **(iv) Sea-Water Heat Rejection (½ mark):** **Sea-water pump** draws from sea-chest, passes through condenser, discharges overboard. **(v) Control & Safety (½ mark):** PLC with HP/LP cut-outs, oil-pressure switches, thermistor-based temperature control — per DNV/LR rules. #### Solution to A(b): **Step 1 — Chilled water mass flow rate** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{evap} &= \dot{m}_{chw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{chw} \\[4pt] 480 \times 10^3 &= \dot{m}_{chw} \times 4180 \times (12 - 6) \\[4pt] \dot{m}_{chw} &= \frac{480{,}000}{4180 \times 6} \\[4pt] &= \frac{480{,}000}{25{,}080} \\[4pt] &= 19.14 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate** ($\rho_{chw} \approx 999.7$ kg/m³) $$\begin{aligned} \dot{V}_{chw} &= \frac{19.14}{999.7} = 0.01915 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt] &= 68.93 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Condenser heat rejection** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{cond} &= Q_{evap} + W_{comp} \\[4pt] &= 480 + 145 = 625 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of Performance** $$\begin{aligned} COP &= \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}} = \frac{480}{145} = 3.31 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Sea-water flow rate** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{sw} &= \frac{Q_{cond}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{625{,}000}{4180 \times 5} = 29.90 \text{ kg/s} \\[4pt] \dot{V}_{sw} &= \frac{29.90}{1025} = 0.02917 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt] &= 105.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer Box:** - $\dot{m}_{chw} = 19.14$ kg/s; $\dot{V}_{chw} = 68.93$ m³/h - $Q_{cond} = 625$ kW; $COP = 3.31$ - $\dot{m}_{sw} = 29.90$ kg/s; $\dot{V}_{sw} = 105.0$ m³/h --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Ship Structural Heat Gain **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Module 3 Focus]** > *(a)* Discuss the **equipment layout** of a marine refrigeration plant, highlighting classification-society-mandated redundancy, machinery-room ventilation, and bilge drainage requirements. **(7 marks)** > > *(b)* A steel bulkhead (8 mm thick, $k = 45$ W/m·K) on a ship is insulated with 60 mm of rock-wool ($k = 0.038$ W/m·K). The accommodation side is at 25 °C and the deck-head side is at 40 °C with solar radiation of 380 W/m². Take $\alpha = 0.6$, $h_o = 22$ W/m²K, $h_i = 8$ W/m²K. For an area of 22 m², determine the **heat gain** into the accommodation using the sol-air temperature method. **(7 marks)** #### Solution to B(a): **(i) Equipment layout (3 marks):** Compressors (screw type, on resilient mounts) aligned longitudinally to ship's centreline; condenser on a separate foundation with 600 mm access; receiver, oil separator, and driers grouped for ease of maintenance; AHUs distributed on each accommodation deck. **(ii) Classification redundancy (2 marks):** SOLAS Ch. II-1 and IGC Code mandate **N+1 redundancy** for essential cooling (e.g., provision rooms, control rooms). Two screw compressors (each 100 % duty), each with independent sea-water pump and starter. Cross-connection with isolation valves permits either compressor to feed the common chilled-water header. **(iii) Ventilation (1 mark):** For ammonia plants — **25 air changes/hour**, with two independent fans (one auto-start on failure). For HFC plants — 6 ACH minimum. **(iv) Bilge drainage (1 mark):** Floor slope 1:100 toward a **bilge well** with float-switched pump and high-level alarm. Oil-water separator on discharge if bilge water can carry oil. #### Solution to B(b): **Step 1 — Sol-air temperature** $$\begin{aligned} T_{sol-air} &= T_o + \frac{\alpha I}{h_o} \\[4pt] &= 40 + \frac{0.6 \times 380}{22} \\[4pt] &= 40 + 10.36 = 50.36 \text{ °C} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Total thermal resistance** $$\begin{aligned} R_{tot} &= \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{t_{steel}}{k_{steel}} + \frac{t_{ins}}{k_{ins}} + \frac{1}{h_o} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{8} + \frac{0.008}{45} + \frac{0.060}{0.038} + \frac{1}{22} \\[4pt] &= 0.1250 + 0.000178 + 1.5789 + 0.04545 \\[4pt] &= 1.7496 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Overall U-value** $$\begin{aligned} U = \frac{1}{1.7496} = 0.5715 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Heat gain through 22 m² bulkhead** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{wall} &= U \cdot A \cdot (T_{sol-air} - T_i) \\[4pt] &= 0.5715 \times 22 \times (50.36 - 25) \\[4pt] &= 0.5715 \times 22 \times 25.36 \\[4pt] &= 318.78 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answer:** $Q_{wall} \approx 319$ W for the 22 m² bulkhead. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Principal Arrangement & Equipment** - **Plant structure:** Two halves — *refrigerant loop* + *secondary fluid loop* — exchanging heat at evaporator and condenser. - **Refrigerant path:** Compressor → Oil Separator → Condenser → Receiver → Filter-Drier → Sight Glass → Solenoid → TXV → Distributor → Evaporator → Accumulator → Compressor. - **Sea-water path:** Sea-chest → Strainer → Pump → Condenser (tube side) → Overboard. - **Chilled-water path:** Evaporator → Pump → AHUs → return. - **Four design drivers:** (1) Hull motion (22.5°–30°), (2) Vibration/shock, (3) Space, (4) Redundancy per SOLAS/Classification. - **Centralised vs Distributed:** Modern cruise ships use *central chiller + distributed AHU* hybrid. - **Compressor types:** Reciprocating (small), Screw (medium-large, dominant), Centrifugal (large LNG). - **Mountings:** Resilient rubber/metal mounts + flexible couplings are *non-negotiable* on marine plants. - **Solar-air correction:** Always use $T_{sol-air} = T_o + \alpha I/h_o$ for ship external surfaces — never raw ambient. - **Classification rules:** DNV, LR, ABS, BV all require N+1 redundancy for essential services. - **Key formulas to memorise:** $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W$, $COP = Q_{evap}/W$, $\dot{m} = Q/(c_p \Delta T)$. - **Typical COP band:** **2.8 – 3.6** for modern screw plants with economiser. - **Sea-water design temps:** 30 °C (tropical) / 15 °C (temperate) inlet. - **Accommodation design:** 24–27 °C DB at 50 % RH. - **Ventilation:** 25 ACH for ammonia machinery rooms, 6 ACH for HFC rooms. - **Bilge drainage:** 1:100 floor slope to bilge well with float-switched pump.

topics.title: Standards for Marine Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Standards for Marine Air Conditioning Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition **Marine Air Conditioning Standards** constitute a hierarchical framework of international, national, and classification-society regulations that govern the design, installation, commissioning, operation, and maintenance of HVAC&R (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning & Refrigeration) systems onboard seagoing vessels. These standards ensure passenger/crew **safety**, **habitability**, **energy efficiency**, **fire integrity**, and **environmental compliance** across the operational envelope of the ship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight)** > Marine AC standards are prescriptive and performance-based engineering rules established by bodies such as the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE)**, **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)**, and major **Classification Societies** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas). They define design conditions, ventilation rates, refrigerant safety, fire dampers, duct construction, noise limits, and electrical safety for shipboard HVAC plants. ### Hierarchical Structure of Marine AC Standards | Tier | Authority | Scope | |------|-----------|-------| | **Tier 1 – International Conventions** | IMO (SOLAS, MARPOL) | Statutory, mandatory, enforced by flag state | | **Tier 2 – International Standards** | ISO, IEC, ASHRAE | Voluntary / referenced by Tier 1 & 4 | | **Tier 3 – National Standards** | ABS, EN, BIS | Regional regulatory requirements | | **Tier 4 – Classification Rules** | DNV, LR, BV, CCS, KR | Mandatory for classed vessels | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Maritime Building Code"** > Think of marine AC standards as the **"Building Code of the Sea"**. Just as a commercial high-rise in India must follow the National Building Code (NBC) for fire exits, fresh air, and lift safety, a ship must follow a layered set of rules to keep its 2,000+ occupants alive and comfortable for weeks at sea — but with the added constraints of **rolling, pitching, heaving motion**, **salt-laden atmosphere**, **limited escape routes**, and **zero tolerance for refrigerant leaks in confined spaces**. The ASHRAE Standard 62.1 inside a hotel room has the same physiological goal as SOLAS Chapter II-2 on a cruise ship cabin — but the *path* to compliance is uniquely maritime. ### Why Marine Standards Differ from Land-Based Standards - **Dynamic motion envelope** — 15° roll / 5° pitch alters duct condensate drainage and refrigerant flow. - **Confined escape** — fire and smoke propagation must be arrested by **fire-rated dampers** within 1 hour. - **Refrigerant toxicity** — most modern marine plants use **HFC-134a, R-407C, R-410A**, or **HFO-1234yf**; older vessels used **R-22 (HCFC)** which is now phased out under the **Montreal Protocol**. - **Salt corrosion** — ducting, fans, and coils require **aluminium-magnesium alloys** or **hot-dip galvanised steel** with **epoxy coating**. - **Limited maintenance access** — standards demand **MTBF ≥ 20,000 hours** for chillers and **minimum 3 years service interval** for refrigerant compressors. - **No external power backup** — emergency AC must run from **emergency generator** for the duration defined by SOLAS (typically **36 hours** for passenger ships). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Conditions used in Marine AC** > - **Outside design (Ocean)**: DB = **35 °C**, WB = **28 °C** > - **Outside design (Tropical Port)**: DB = **40 °C**, WB = **32 °C** > - **Inside design (Accommodation)**: DB = **27 °C ± 1 °C**, RH = **50 % ± 5 %** > - **Noise limit (Cabins)**: ≤ **55 dB(A) NR 45** > - **Fresh air supply**: **30–50 m³/h per person** > - **Air changes (Recirculated)**: **6–10 ACH** in accommodation ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Marine AC Standards (Engineering Logic) Standards are organised along **five engineering axes**: 1. **Safety Axis** — Fire integrity, refrigerant toxicity, emergency operation - **SOLAS Ch. II-2** · **IMO Res.
