A.754(18)** · **ASHRAE 15** · **IEC 60092-506** 2. **Habitability Axis** — Thermal comfort, IAQ, acoustic comfort - **ISO 7547** · **ISO 8468** · **ASHRAE 62.1** · **ISO 717-1** 3. **Performance Axis** — Cooling load calculation, COP, EER - **ISO 5151** · **ASHRAE 41.1–41.9** · **ISO 13256** 4. **Construction Axis** — Materials, duct sizing, fan selection - **ISO 13347** · **SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards — Marine Adaptation** 5. **Environmental Axis** — Refrigerant GWP, ODP, leak detection - **MARPOL Annex VI** · **Kigali Amendment** · **EU F-Gas Regulation 517/2014** ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Tier - **Why IMO / SOLAS?** They are the **only legally binding** international convention under **UNCLOS**. A flag state that ignores SOLAS forfeits its right of navigation. - **Why Classification Society rules?** Insurance underwriters (P&I Clubs) will not insure an unclassed vessel; class rules convert safety philosophy into **auditable engineering specifications**. - **Why ASHRAE 15?** It is the **only globally harmonised refrigerant safety standard**, addressing toxicity (A/B class), flammability (1/2L/3), and pressure relief. - **Why ISO 7547?** It is the **dedicated marine AC standard**, providing design temperatures, ventilation rates, and test methods specific to ship accommodation spaces. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Source Standard | |---|-----------|-----------------|------|-----------------| | 1 | Sensible Heat Gain | $Q_s = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | ASHRAE Handbook | | 2 | Latent Heat Gain | $Q_l = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ | kW | ASHRAE Handbook | | 3 | Total Cooling Load | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_l$ | kW | ASHRAE | | 4 | Air-side SHF | $SHF = Q_s / Q_t$ | – | – | | 5 | Air Changes per Hour | $ACH = \dot{V} / V_{room}$ | 1/h | ISO 7547 | | 6 | Fresh Air per Person | $F_{ap} = 30 \text{ to } 50$ | m³/h | ASHRAE 62.1 | | 7 | Ventilation Rate (Diesel Engg. Room) | $\dot{V} = 15 \text{ to } 30$ | m³/h per kW | SOLAS Reg. II-1/40 | | 8 | Duct Velocity (Main) | $v \leq 10$ | m/s | SMACNA Marine | | 9 | Duct Velocity (Branch) | $v \leq 6$ | m/s | SMACNA Marine | | 10 | Sound Power Limit (Cabin) | $L_w \leq 55$ | dB(A) | ISO 717-1 | | 11 | Refrigerant Leak Rate (max) | $\dot{m}_{leak} \leq 0.1\%/\text{yr}$ | – | EN 378 / ASHRAE 15 | | 12 | Chiller COP (Marine) | $COP = 2.8 \text{ to } 3.4$ | – | ISO 13256 | | 13 | EER of Package Unit | $EER = Q_c / P_{in}$ | – | ISO 5151 | | 14 | Fire Damper Rating | $F \geq 60$ | min | SOLAS / FTP Code | | 15 | Emergency AC Endurance | $t_{emg} \geq 36$ | h | SOLAS Ch. II-1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symbol Key** (do **not** confuse) > - $\dot{V}$ → volumetric airflow in **m³/s** (SI) or **m³/h** (with constant adjusted) > - $\Delta T$ → temperature difference **°C** or **K** (numerically equal) > - $\Delta W$ → humidity ratio difference **kg water / kg dry air** > - Constant **1.21** = $\rho_{air} \cdot c_p$ at **1.013 bar, 27 °C** = **1.21 kJ/(m³·K)** > - Constant **3010** = $\rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg}$ at standard conditions ≈ **3010 kJ/m³** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not academic — they are the **design language** used by every shipyard HVAC designer in Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Hyundai Heavy Industries, and Fincantieri. A wrong $\Delta T$ by 1 °C on a **2,000-passenger cruise ship** translates to a **chiller oversizing of 200 kW** ≈ **₹1.2 crore** in equipment cost. Hence KTU emphasises these formulas. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Sensible Heat Formula $Q_s = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ The sensible heat load on a cooling coil is the energy required to lower the temperature of the air stream without changing its moisture content. **Step 1 — Start from First Principle (Energy Balance)** The energy required to cool a mass flow of air $\dot{m}$ by a temperature $\Delta T$ is: $$ Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 2 — Convert Mass Flow to Volumetric Flow** Using $\dot{m} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}$, we substitute: $$ Q_s = \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 3 — Insert Standard Marine Air Properties** At the **mean coil condition of 27 °C DB, 50 % RH, 1.01325 bar**: $$ \rho_{air} = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ **Step 4 — Combine the Constants** $$ \rho_{air} \cdot c_p = 1.18 \times 1.005 = 1.1859 \text{ kJ/(m}^3\text{·K)} \approx 1.21 $$ **Step 5 — Final Form** $$ Q_s = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T \;\;[\text{kW when } \dot{V} \text{ in m}^3/\text{s}] $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The constant **1.21** is the volumetric heat capacity of air at standard marine conditions. If $\dot{V}$ is given in **m³/h**, divide by 3600 first: $\dot{V}_{\text{m³/s}} = \dot{V}_{\text{m³/h}} / 3600$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Latent Heat Formula $Q_l = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ Latent heat load = moisture removal from the air stream by condensation on the cooling coil. **Step 1 — First-Principle Energy Balance** $$ Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Mass Flow** $$ Q_l = \rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W $$ **Step 3 — Insert Constants** At coil surface temperature of ~12 °C: $$ h_{fg} \approx 2525 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad \rho_{air} = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Combine** $$ Q_l = 1.18 \times 2525 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W = 2979.5 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W \approx 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W $$ > The constant **3010 kJ/m³** is the volumetric latent capacity of air. $\Delta W$ is in **kg water / kg dry air**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Ventilation Air Quantity from SOLAS & ISO 7547 **Step 1 — Identify occupancy and activity level from ISO 7547 Table** For a **passenger cabin (sleeping occupant)**: required fresh air = **30 m³/h per person** (ASHRAE 62.1 default). **Step 2 — Compute total ventilation airflow for n occupants** $$ \dot{V}_{vent} = n \cdot F_{ap} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass flow for duct sizing** $$ \dot{m}_{vent} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{vent} / 3600 $$ **Step 4 — Recirculated airflow to maintain ACH** $$ \dot{V}_{recirc} = ACH \cdot V_{room} - \dot{V}_{vent} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Recirculated air is **filtered, mixed with fresh air**, then re-cooled. For a typical cabin of $V_{room} = 60 \text{ m}^3$, $ACH = 8$: total airflow = $8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. If fresh air = 30 m³/h, recirculated = **450 m³/h** (≈ 94 % recirculation, typical). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Compliance Matrix — Standards vs. Engineering Parameter | Parameter | SOLAS Ch.II-2 | ISO 7547 | ASHRAE 62.1 | ASHRAE 15 | Class Rules (DNV/LR) | MARPOL | |-----------|:-------------:|:--------:|:-----------:|:---------:|:--------------------:|:------:| | Fire damper 1 h | ✓ | – | – | – | ✓ | – | | Outside design T | – | ✓ | – | – | – | – | | Inside design T/RH | – | ✓ | – | – | ✓ | – | | Fresh air per person | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – | – | – | | Refrigerant limit | – | – | – | ✓ | ✓ | – | | Emergency power | ✓ | – | – | – | ✓ | – | | Ventilation E/R | ✓ | – | – | – | – | – | | Refrigerant GWP | – | – | – | – | – | ✓ | | Noise limit (cabin) | – | ✓ | – | – | ✓ | – | | Leak detection | – | – | – | ✓ | ✓ | – | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** No single standard covers everything. The ship designer must **simultaneously satisfy** all five columns — a typical **compliance matrix** in a shipyard AC specification runs to **40+ pages**. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A crew cabin on a product tanker has volume $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$, occupied by 1 person. Outside design is 35 °C DB / 28 °C WB. Inside target is 27 °C DB / 19.5 °C WB. Find (a) cooling load, (b) ventilation airflow, (c) total supply airflow if ACH = 8. **Solution:** **(a) Humidity ratios** (from psychrometric chart / formula): $$ W_{out} = 0.0217 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad W_{in} = 0.0138 \text{ kg/kg} $$ $$ \Delta W = 0.0217 - 0.0138 = 0.0079 \text{ kg/kg} $$ $$ \Delta T = 35 - 27 = 8 \text{ °C} $$ Assume supply airflow $\dot{V} = 0.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$: $$ Q_s = 1.21 \times 0.15 \times 8 = 1.452 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_l = 3010 \times 0.15 \times 0.0079 = 3.567 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_t = 1.452 + 3.567 = 5.019 \text{ kW} $$ **(b) Ventilation airflow** (ISO 7547 / ASHRAE 62.1): $$ \dot{V}_{vent} = 1 \times 30 = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.00833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **(c) Total supply airflow for ACH = 8**: $$ \dot{V}_{total} = 8 \times 50 = 400 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.111 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \dot{V}_{recirc} = 400 - 30 = 370 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.103 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ > **Sanity check:** The latent load is **dominant** in tropical seas (≈ 71 % of total), confirming the marine design philosophy of **oversized dehumidification coils**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied All node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix; no reserved keywords are used; labels contain raw uppercase text only. ### 4.2 Figure 1 — Standards Hierarchy for Marine AC Systems ```mermaid graph TD rootA[International Conventions
IMO SOLAS MARPOL] --> b1[SOLAS Chapter II 2
Fire Safety] rootA --> b2[MARPOL Annex VI
Air Pollution Control] rootA --> b3[FTP Code
Fire Test Procedures] rootA --> b4[STCW Convention
Crew Competency] b1 --> c1[Fire Dampers 1 Hour] b1 --> c2[Means of Escape] b1 --> c3[Smoke Detection] b2 --> c5[Refrigerant GWP Limit] b2 --> c6[Ozone Depleting Substances Ban] rootD[International Standards
ISO IEC ASHRAE] --> d1[ISO 7547
Marine AC Design] rootD --> d2[ISO 8468
Bridge Layout] rootD --> d3[ASHRAE 62.1
Indoor Air Quality] rootD --> d4[ASHRAE 15
Refrigerant Safety] rootD --> d5[IEC 60092 506
Electrical Ship Install] rootD --> d6[ISO 13256
Chiller Performance] d1 --> e1[Design Temperatures] d1 --> e2[Ventilation Rates] d3 --> e3[Fresh Air per Person] d4 --> e4[Refrigerant Classification] d4 --> e5[Leak Detection Threshold] rootG[Classification Society Rules
DNV LR ABS BV] --> f1[DNV Pt 4 Ch 7
HVAC Systems] rootG --> f2[Lloyd Register
Ship Rules Ch 12] rootG --> f3[ABS SVR
Chapter 7 HVAC] rootG --> f4[BV NR 467
HVAC Rules] f1 --> g1[Materials and Corrosion] f1 --> g2[Fire Integrity Test] f1 --> g3[Emergency Power Supply] f2 --> h1[Noise and Vibration] f2 --> h2[Duct Construction Standard] f3 --> i1[Machinery Space Ventilation] f3 --> i2[Refrigeration Plant Safety] ``` ### 4.3 Figure 2 — Block Diagram of a Typical Marine Central AC Plant ```mermaid graph LR sea1[Sea Water Inlet
28 to 32 C] --> swp1[Sea Water Pump] swp1 --> cond1[Shell and Tube Condenser] cond1 --> swout1[Sea Water Outlet to Overboard] comp1[Electric Driven Screw Compressor
150 to 600 kW] --> cond1 comp1 --> evap1[Shell and Tube Evaporator
Chilled Water 6 to 8 C] evap1 --> chwp1[Chilled Water Pump Primary] chwp1 --> ahu1[AHU 1 Accommodation Block A] chwp1 --> ahu2[AHU 2 Accommodation Block B] chwp1 --> ahu3[AHU 3 Bridge and ECR] chwp1 --> ahu4[AHU 4 Galley and Laundry] evap1 --> chwp2[Chilled Water Pump Secondary] chwp2 --> fcu1[FCU Cabin 1] chwp2 --> fcu2[FCU Cabin 2] chwp2 --> fcu3[FCU Cabin 3] fcu1 --> fd1[Fire Damper 1 Hour] fcu2 --> fd2[Fire Damper 1 Hour] fcu3 --> fd3[Fire Damper 1 Hour] fd1 --> sd1[Smoke Damper] fd2 --> sd2[Smoke Damper] fd3 --> sd3[Smoke Damper] sd1 --> room1[Cabin Space] sd2 --> room2[Cabin Space] sd3 --> room3[Public Space] emg1[Emergency Generator
SOLAS 36 Hour] --> comp1 emg1 --> ahu1 emg1 --> chwp1 return1[Chilled Water Return 12 C] --> evap1 return2[Hot Water Return from Galley] --> hx1[Heat Recovery Heat Exchanger] hx1 --> reheat1[Reheat Coil in AHU] hx1 --> dh1[Domestic Hot Water Tank] ``` ### 4.4 Figure 3 — Compliance Workflow for a New-Build Vessel ```mermaid graph TD s1[Shipowner HVAC Specification] --> s2[Class Society Rule Check] s1 --> s3[SOLAS and MARPOL Statutory Check] s1 --> s4[ISO 7547 and ASHRAE 62.1 Habitability Check] s2 --> s5[Design Review by Class] s3 --> s5 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6[Approval Drawing GA] s6 --> s7[Fabrication at Shipyard] s7 --> s8[Stage Inspection by Class Surveyor] s8 --> s9[Trial and Commissioning] s9 --> s10[Sea Trials Performance Test] s10 --> s11[Class Certificate Issued] s10 --> s12[SOLAS Safe Manning and Equipment Certificate] s11 --> s13[Vessel Delivered to Owner] s12 --> s13 s13 --> s14[Annual Survey by Class] s14 --> s15[Special Survey every 5 Years] ``` ### 4.5 Figure 4 — Refrigerant Safety Classification (ASHRAE 15) ```mermaid graph TD a[ASHRAE 15 Safety Group] --> a1[Group A Lower Toxicity] a[ASHRAE 15 Safety Group] --> a2[Group B Higher Toxicity] a1 --> a3[Class A1 Non Flammable Low Tox eg R134a] a1 --> a4[Class A2L Mild Flammable Low Tox eg R1234yf] a1 --> a5[Class A3 High Flammable Low Tox eg R290 Propane] a2 --> a6[Class B1 Non Flammable High Tox eg R123] a2 --> a7[Class B2L Mild Flammable High Tox] a2 --> a8[Class B3 High Flammable High Tox] a3 --> m1[Marine Use Allowed with Limits] a4 --> m2[Marine Use Allowed with Leak Detection] a5 --> m3[Marine Use Restricted Charge Size] a6 --> m4[Marine Use Requires Indirect System] a7 --> m5[Marine Use Requires Special Approval] a8 --> m6[Marine Use Prohibited] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. List any six major international and classification-society standards that govern marine air conditioning systems.** > **Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):** > 1. **SOLAS Chapter II-2** – Fire safety and ventilation > 2. **ISO 7547** – Air conditioning and ventilation of ship accommodation spaces > 3. **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** – Ventilation for acceptable indoor air quality > 4. **ASHRAE Standard 15** – Safety standard for refrigeration systems > 5. **IEC 60092-506** – Electrical installations in ships – AC and ventilation > 6. **DNV Rules Part 4 Chapter 7** – HVAC systems (or equivalent Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV rules) --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. State the standard inside design conditions for ship accommodation and the minimum fresh-air supply rate per person as per ISO 7547 / ASHRAE 62.1.** > **Model Answer:** > - **Inside design conditions:** **DB = 27 °C ± 1 °C, RH = 50 % ± 5 %** (1 mark) > - **Fresh-air supply:** **30 m³/h per person** (sleeping occupant) up to **50 m³/h per person** (active public spaces) (1 mark) > - Reference to **ISO 7547 and ASHRAE Standard 62.1** (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Pitfall 1:** Students confuse **ASHRAE 15** (refrigerant safety) with **ASHRAE 62.1** (IAQ) — a common 1-mark loss in Part A. > - **Pitfall 2:** In Part B derivations, students forget to convert $\dot{V}$ from **m³/h to m³/s** before applying $1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ — losing 2 marks. > - **Pitfall 3:** Students omit the **fire damper rating (1 hour)** and **emergency AC endurance (36 hours)** clauses of SOLAS — a standard 2-mark loss. > - **Pitfall 4:** Forgetting the **Montreal Protocol / Kigali Amendment** reference when discussing refrigerant GWP — costs a full CO-mark in long-answer questions. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Standards Framework and Fire Safety > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain, with a neat hierarchy diagram, the **four-tier structure of marine AC standards**, citing at least one example standard from each tier. Discuss the **statutory vs. voluntary** nature of these standards. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **fire safety requirements** for marine air conditioning systems as per **SOLAS Chapter II-2** and **IMO Resolution A.754(18)**. Include the role of **fire dampers, smoke dampers, duct penetrations, and material certification** in achieving a **1-hour fire integrity rating**. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution (Question A) **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - **Tier 1 – International Conventions (2 marks):** SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL (Marine Pollution), STCW. These are **statutory** under UNCLOS, enforced by the **flag state** through the **Maritime Authority** (e.g., DG Shipping India). Failure to comply = detention by **Port State Control (PSC)**. - **Tier 2 – International Standards (2 marks):** ISO 7547, ISO 8468, ASHRAE 15, ASHRAE 62.1, IEC 60092. **Voluntary** but **mandatorily referenced** by Tier 1 and Tier 4. - **Tier 3 – National Standards (1 mark):** e.g., **BIS (India)**, **EN (Europe)**, **ANSI (USA)**. Apply when the vessel operates in that nation's waters or is built at that nation's shipyard. - **Tier 4 – Classification Society Rules (2 marks):** **DNV, Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV), ClassNK, Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**. **Mandatory for classed vessels** — insurance and port-of-call access depend on a valid class certificate. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** - **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 9 / 11** mandates that **ventilation ducts passing through fire-rated divisions** must be fitted with **automatic fire dampers** that close on **fusible link at 68 °C** or **smoke detector signal (1 mark)**. - The **fire integrity rating** is typically **A-60 (60 minutes)** for main vertical zones and **A-0 / B-0** for horizontal subdivisions (1 mark). - **IMO Res. A.754(18) — Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** prescribes the **standard fire test** where the structure is exposed to a **standard time-temperature curve** (up to 945 °C at 60 min). The duct + damper assembly must maintain **integrity (no flame passage)** and **insulation (back-face temperature rise ≤ 140 °C average, 180 °C at any point)** (2 marks). - **Material certification:** ducts in **machinery spaces** must be **steel or equivalent non-combustible** (FTP Code Annex 1, Part 1). **Aluminium** is permitted in accommodation with **fire-rated insulation** wrapping (1 mark). - **Smoke dampers** are required where **return air passes** between smoke-tight sections per **ISO 7547** and **FSS Code Ch. 9** (1 mark). - **Penetration seals** around ducts through bulkheads must use **firestop material** (e.g., **intumescent collars, mineral wool, fire-rated foam**) tested per **IMO Res. A.754(18)** (1 mark). --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Refrigerant Safety, IAQ, and Habitability > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Alternate]** **(a)** Discuss the **ASHRAE 15 refrigerant safety classification system** in detail, with a classification matrix of **toxicity (A/B) and flammability (1/2L/3)**. Indicate which classes are **permitted, restricted, or prohibited** in marine AC plants. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **2,000-passenger cruise ship** has accommodation designed for **27 °C DB / 50 % RH** with outside conditions of **35 °C DB / 28 °C WB**. The total recirculated air volume is **8,00,000 m³/h** with **30 % fresh air**. Calculate the **(i) total ventilation airflow, (ii) sensible and latent cooling loads, (iii) chiller tonnage**, and verify the design against **ASHRAE 62.1 and ISO 7547**. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution (Question B) **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - ASHRAE 15 groups refrigerants into **two safety dimensions**: - **Toxicity:** **A = lower toxicity** (OEL ≥ 400 ppm), **B = higher toxicity** (OEL < 400 ppm) (1 mark). - **Flammability:** **1 = non-flammable**, **2L = mild flammability (burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s)**, **3 = high flammability** (1 mark). - **Classification matrix** (2 marks): | Group | A1 | A2L | A3 | B1 | B2L | B3 | |-------|----|----|----|----|----|----| | Example | R-134a, R-22 | R-1234yf, R-32 | R-290 (propane) | R-123 | – | R-40 | | Marine status | Allowed | Allowed w/ leak detection | Restricted | Indirect system only | Special approval | Prohibited | - **Marine permitted:** **A1 (R-134a, R-407C, R-410A)** — most common; **A2L (R-1234yf)** — emerging low-GWP choice (1 mark). - **Marine restricted:** **A3 (R-290)** — limited to small self-contained units; charge ≤ 5 kg per compartment per **EN 378-1** (1 mark). - **Marine prohibited:** **B3 and high-charge B2L** — risk of toxic phosgene in accommodation (1 mark). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Total ventilation airflow:** $$ \dot{V}_{total} = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 222.22 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \dot{V}_{fresh} = 0.30 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 66.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ > Per person: $2{,}40{,}000 / 2000 = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ → **exceeds ASHRAE 62.1 minimum of 30–50 m³/h** ✓ **(1 mark)** **(ii) Sensible and latent loads:** From psychrometric chart at the given conditions: $$ W_{out} = 0.0217 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad W_{in} = 0.0138 \text{ kg/kg} $$ $$ \Delta W = 0.0079 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad \Delta T = 8 \text{ °C} $$ $$ Q_s = 1.21 \times 222.22 \times 8 = 2151.6 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_l = 3010 \times 222.22 \times 0.0079 = 5284.6 \text{ kW} $$ $$ Q_t = 2151.6 + 5284.6 = 7436.2 \text{ kW} \approx 7.44 \text{ MW} $$ **(1 mark for $Q_s$, 1 mark for $Q_l$)** **(iii) Chiller tonnage:** $$ 1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \text{Chiller capacity} = 7436.2 / 3.517 = 2114 \text{ TR} \approx 2115 \text{ TR} $$ **(1 mark)** — typically split into **3–4 chillers of 600 TR each** for redundancy. **Verification against ISO 7547:** - Inside DB 27 °C ± 1 °C ✓ - RH 50 % ± 5 % ✓ - Fresh air ≥ 30 m³/h/person ✓ - Chiller COP expected ≈ 2.9 (per ISO 13256) **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points — Standards for Marine AC** - ✅ **Five pillars of marine AC standards:** SOLAS, ISO 7547, ASHRAE 15, ASHRAE 62.1, Classification Rules. - ✅ **Inside design accommodation:** **27 °C DB, 50 % RH** ± **1 °C / 5 %**. - ✅ **Outside design (tropical):** **35 °C DB / 28 °C WB** ocean, **40 °C DB / 32 °C WB** port. - ✅ **Fresh air per person:** **30 m³/h (sleeping), 50 m³/h (active spaces)**. - ✅ **Air change rate:** **6–10 ACH** for accommodation. - ✅ **Fire dampers:** **1 hour (A-60)** rating, **fusible link 68 °C** per **SOLAS Ch. II-2** and **FTP Code (IMO Res. A.754(18))**. - ✅ **Refrigerant safety:** **ASHRAE 15** toxicity (A/B) + flammability (1/2L/3); marine workhorse = **A1 group (R-134a, R-407C)**; future = **A2L (R-1234yf)**. - ✅ **Latent load dominates** in tropical seas (~60–70 % of total) → oversized dehumidification coils. - ✅ **Sensible heat formula:** $Q_s = 1.21 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ (kW, $\dot{V}$ in **m³/s**). - ✅ **Latent heat formula:** $Q_l = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ (kW, $\dot{V}$ in **m³/s**). - ✅ **Emergency AC endurance:** **36 hours** from **emergency generator** for passenger ships (SOLAS). - ✅ **MARPOL Annex VI + Kigali Amendment:** restrict **GWP** of refrigerants — phase-down of HFCs by **80–85 % by 2045**. - ✅ **Noise limit in cabins:** **≤ 55 dB(A), NR 45** (ISO 717-1). - ✅ **Refrigerant leak limit:** **≤ 0.1 % per year** per **EN 378 / ASHRAE 15**. - ✅ **Chiller COP benchmark:** **2.8–3.4** for marine screw chillers (ISO 13256). - ✅ **Machinery space ventilation:** **15–30 m³/h per kW** of engine power (SOLAS Reg. II-1/40). - ✅ **Statutory vs. voluntary:** SOLAS/MARPOL = **statutory**; ISO/ASHRAE = **voluntary** but **referenced mandatorily** by class rules. - ✅ **Classification Societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, IRS)** translate IMO/ISO into **auditable engineering rules**; their certificate is **a precondition for insurance and PSC clearance**. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Quick Recall — "SIFA-R"** > **S**OLAS · **I**SO 7547 · **F**TP Code (fire) · **A**SHRAE 15 & 62.1 · **R**ules (Class Society)

topics.title: Air Flow and Air Conditioning Capacity Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Flow and Air Conditioning Capacity Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Conditioning Capacity** of a marine HVAC plant is the total rate at which heat (sensible + latent) must be removed from a conditioned space to maintain the prescribed inside design conditions against the heat gains from outside, internal loads, and ventilation air. It is expressed in **kW** (SI) or **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)**, where **1 TR = 3.516 kW**.

**Air Flow (Q)** is the mass or volumetric rate of conditioned air supplied to / extracted from a space, sized such that the air can absorb the room's sensible and latent heat loads while being warmed up (or cooled down) from its supply temperature to the room temperature.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology (PENST743 – Refrigeration and Air Conditioning of Ships), Module 4 unifies the psychrometric basis established in Module 3 with the practical **engineering sizing of ducts, grilles, fans and cooling coils** that constitute the air-side design of a ship's AC plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The student is expected to compute the **total cooling load**, the **required supply air quantity (CFM / m³/h)**, the **coil bypass factor (BF)**, and the **apparatus dew point (ADP)**. These four quantities together define the size of the cooling coil, the fan motor rating, and the duct cross-section.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the ship's accommodation block as a **large aquarium sitting under the tropical sun**. Heat keeps pouring in (sun, engines below, 200 sailors, cooking, lights, hot bulkheads). To keep the water (the cabin air) at a pleasant 24 °C, you can either:

- **Drop in a giant block of ice** (i.e., increase the *capacity* of the refrigeration plant), **OR**
- **Circulate the water past smaller ice cubes more rapidly** (i.e., increase the *air flow* so that a smaller temperature lift still removes the same heat).

Air-conditioning capacity calculations are exactly this trade-off: how much **"ice"** (refrigeration tonnage) and how much **"flow"** (CFM of air) are needed, and at what temperature the air must leave the coil so that, after mixing with bypass air and absorbing room gains, it arrives at the room at the right condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters on a ship**
> - **Weight & footprint** are precious on board. Over-sized ducts eat cargo space; over-sized plants burn more bunker fuel.
> - **Noise & vibration** limits dictate low air velocities (typically **6–9 m/s** in main trunks, **2.5–4 m/s** at grilles).
> - **Salt-laden, corrosive** outside air forces designers to take ventilation air only through dedicated treated-air units.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used in Air-Flow Calculations

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea-Level Tropical) |
|---|---|---|
| Density of air | ρ | **1.20 kg/m³** at 30 °C, 50 % RH |
| Specific heat of dry air | cp | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Specific heat of moist air | cp,m | **1.02 kJ/(kg·K)** approx |
| Latent heat of vaporisation of water | hfg | **2500 kJ/kg** at 0 °C, ≈ **2430 kJ/kg** at 25 °C |
| Specific volume of air | v | **0.85 m³/kg** (typical marine indoor) |
| Standard inside design | T_inside | **24 °C DB / 50 % RH** |
| Standard outside design (tropical) | T_outside | **35 °C DB / 28 °C WB / 70 % RH** |
| Standard outside design (temperate) | T_outside | **32 °C DB / 24 °C WB** |
| Conversion: 1 CFM | – | **0.000472 m³/s = 1.699 m³/h** |
| 1 Ton of Refrigeration | TR | **3.516 kW = 12000 BTU/h** |
| 1 BTU/h | – | **0.293 W** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Faces of Heat Load: Sensible (SH) and Latent (LH)

Every heat load in an air-conditioned space has two components:

1. **Sensible Heat (SH)** – heat that changes *temperature only* (dry-bulb). Examples: heat through ship's side shell, solar gain through skylights, light fixtures, crew body dry loss.
2. **Latent Heat (LH)** – heat that changes *moisture content only* (humidity ratio). Examples: moisture from breathing, cooking vapour, laundry drying, wet clothes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Heat Load (Q_total) = SH + LH**

## 2.2 The Air-Side Design Equation — Deriving Air Quantity

The cooling coil drops the entering air from condition **1 (mixed/return air)** to leaving condition **2 (supply air)**. The supply air then absorbs the room's load and rises back to room condition **3 (room)**.

For a steady-state room:

$$\text{Heat removed by supply air} = \text{Room heat gain}$$

### 2.2.1 Sensible Heat Equation

Mass flow of air $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) × specific heat cp × temperature rise ΔT equals the room sensible heat:

$$\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2) = SH \;\;[\text{kW}]$$

Solving for the **volumetric flow rate** using specific volume v (m³/kg):

$$Q_s = \frac{SH \times 1000 \cdot v}{c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)} \;\;\Big[\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{h}}\Big]$$

In imperial (commonly used in marine design with CFM):

$$Q_s = \frac{SH \;[\text{BTU/h}]}{1.08 \times \Delta T \;[°F]} \;\;\Big[\text{CFM}\Big]$$

where **1.08 = ρ × cp × 60** with ρ = 0.075 lb/ft³ and cp = 0.24 BTU/(lb·°F).

### 2.2.2 Latent Heat Equation

$$\dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_3 - W_2) = LH$$

In imperial:

$$Q_l = \frac{LH \;[\text{BTU/h}]}{0.68 \times \Delta W \;[\text{gr/lb}]} \;\;\Big[\text{CFM}\Big]$$

where **0.68 = ρ × hfg / 7000** with appropriate unit conversions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule (KTU-favoured):**
> The **larger** of Qs and Ql governs the supply air quantity, because the same air stream must satisfy both sensible and latent removal. Hence:
>
> $$Q_{\text{supply}} = \max(Q_s, \; Q_l)$$
>
> In tropical ship design, latent loads from 200 crew can easily make **Q_l > Q_s**, forcing a much larger air flow than pure sensible heat would suggest.

## 2.3 Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) — the Key to Coil Selection

$$\boxed{\text{SHF} = \frac{SH}{SH + LH} = \frac{SH}{Q_{\text{total}}}}$$

SHF is plotted on the **psychrometric chart as a slope through the room condition**. The line drawn from the room point at this slope intersects the saturation curve at the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — the theoretical surface temperature of an ideal cooling coil that could perfectly condition the air in one pass.

## 2.4 Bypass Factor (BF) — Real Coil Performance

A real coil cannot cool all the air to its surface temperature; a fraction slips past unchanged. **Bypass Factor** quantifies this:

$$\boxed{\text{BF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}}$$

where
- $T_1$ = coil entering DB (mixed air)
- $T_2$ = coil leaving DB (supply air)
- $T_3$ = coil surface (ADP) temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical marine coil BFs**: 4-row chilled-water coil → BF ≈ **0.10 – 0.20**; 6-row → **0.05 – 0.10**; DX coil → **0.15 – 0.30**.

## 2.5 Master Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible air quantity (SI) | $Q_s = \dfrac{SH \cdot v \cdot 1000}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | m³/h | Duct / fan sizing |
| 2 | Sensible air quantity (IP) | $Q_s = \dfrac{SH}{1.08 \cdot \Delta T}$ | CFM | American manuals |
| 3 | Latent air quantity (IP) | $Q_l = \dfrac{LH}{0.68 \cdot \Delta W}$ | CFM | Latent-dominated spaces |
| 4 | Mass flow | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_s}{v}$ | kg/s | Coil rating |
| 5 | Sensible Heat Factor | $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{SH+LH}$ | – | Coil selection |
| 6 | Bypass Factor | $BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$ | – | Coil performance |
| 7 | Coil surface (ADP) temp | $T_{ADP} = T_1 - \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{1 - BF}$ | °C | Coil rating |
| 8 | Total capacity | $Q = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2)$ | kW | Plant TR |
| 9 | Equivalent duct dia. | $D_e = 1.30 \cdot \dfrac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ | m | Rectangular ducts |
| 10 | Fan power (ideal) | $P = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | kW | Fan selection |
| 11 | Mixed air temp | $T_m = \dfrac{\dot{V}_r T_r + \dot{V}_o T_o}{\dot{V}_r + \dot{V}_o}$ | °C | Energy recovery |
| 12 | Fresh air per person | $Q_{fa} = 30 \cdot N$ | m³/h | IASO / ILO |
| 13 | Cooling load per person (sensible) | $SH_p \approx 60 + 14v$ | W | Tropical |
| 14 | Cooling load per person (latent) | $LH_p \approx 70$ | W | Tropical |
| 15 | Heat from lights | $Q_L = P_{\text{lamp}} \cdot F_u \cdot F_{sa}$ | W | Lighting gain |
| 16 | Heat from motors in room | $Q_M = P \cdot (1 - \eta)$ | W | Engine room AC |
| 17 | Ventilation / infiltration load | $Q_v = \dot{m}_v c_p (T_o - T_i)$ | kW | OA load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> This single page of formulas is what a ship's AC designer (e.g., at Wärtsilä, Alfa Laval, Hamworthy) uses to size a self-contained unit like a *Schenker* or *Heinen & Hopman* plant from a one-line heat-load summary.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Air Quantity from Sensible Heat

**Start statement.** A room receives a steady sensible heat gain SH (kW). Conditioned supply air enters at temperature $T_2$ and leaves the room (becoming return air) at $T_3$.

**Mass-flow energy balance:**

$$\text{Energy in by air} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, T_2 \quad \text{(at supply)}$$
$$\text{Energy out by air} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, T_3 \quad \text{(at return)}$$

**Net heat absorbed by air** while passing through the room equals SH:

$$\dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_3 - T_2) = SH$$

**Solve for mass flow:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{SH}{c_p \, (T_3 - T_2)} \;\;[\text{kg/s}]$$

**Convert to volumetric flow** using specific volume $v$ (m³/kg):

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot v = \frac{SH \cdot v}{c_p \, (T_3 - T_2)} \;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Convert seconds to hours** by multiplying by 3600:

$$Q = \frac{SH \cdot v \cdot 3600}{c_p \, (T_3 - T_2)} \;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

**Substitute $SH$ in kW and absorb 1000** (since 1 kW = 1 kJ/s, but we want kJ/h, multiply by 3600):

$$Q = \frac{SH \, [\text{kW}] \times v \times 3600}{c_p \times \Delta T} \;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

With $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K), $v$ = 0.85 m³/kg:

$$Q \approx \frac{SH \times 0.85 \times 3600}{1.005 \times \Delta T} = \frac{3060 \cdot SH}{\Delta T} \;\;[\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, designers use **3060 × SH / ΔT** when SH is in kW and ΔT in K. This is the KTU-board short-cut.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Apparatus Dew Point from Bypass Factor

**Definition restated.** By definition of BF:

$$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$$

**Cross-multiply:**

$$T_2 - T_1 = BF \cdot (T_3 - T_1)$$

**Rearrange to solve for coil surface T₃ (= ADP temperature):**

$$T_3 = T_1 + \frac{T_2 - T_1}{BF}$$

$$\boxed{T_{ADP} = T_1 + \frac{T_2 - T_1}{BF}}$$

A common alternative form is:

$$T_{ADP} = T_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{1 - BF}$$

Both forms are identical.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-mark problem)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Example]**
> A ship's accommodation has the following design data:
> - Inside: 24 °C DB, 50 % RH → enthalpy 47.8 kJ/kg, humidity ratio 0.0094 kg/kg
> - Outside: 35 °C DB, 28 °C WB → enthalpy 85.5 kJ/kg, humidity ratio 0.0184 kg/kg
> - Sensible heat gain = **42 kW**, Latent heat gain = **18 kW**
> - Supply air temperature = **13 °C DB**
> - Ventilation (outside) air = **30 % of total supply**
> - Coil BF = **0.15**
>
> Find: **(a)** Supply air CFM, **(b)** Mixed air condition, **(c)** ADP, **(d)** Total refrigeration tonnage.

### Part (a): Supply air quantity

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{room}} - T_{\text{supply}} = 24 - 13 = 11 \text{ K}$$

Using the SI short-cut:

$$Q_s = \frac{3060 \times SH}{\Delta T} = \frac{3060 \times 42}{11} = 11{,}683 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Convert to CFM:

$$Q_s = 11{,}683 \times 0.5886 = 6{,}876 \;\text{CFM} \;\;\approx \;6{,}900 \;\text{CFM}$$

Check latent requirement:

$$\Delta W = W_{\text{room}} - W_{\text{supply}} \approx 0.0094 - 0.0065 = 0.0029 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

$$Q_l = \frac{LH \cdot v \cdot 3600}{h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W} = \frac{18 \times 0.85 \times 3600}{2430 \times 0.0029} = 7{,}834 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Since **Q_l (7,834) > Q_s (11,683)? No — Qs governs.** Wait, recompute: Q_l = 7,834 < Q_s = 11,683. Hence **sensible governs**.

$$Q_{\text{supply}} = 11{,}683 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;\;(\approx 6{,}900 \;\text{CFM})$$

### Part (b): Mixed air condition

Outside air = 30 % of 11,683 = 3,505 m³/h; Return air = 70 % = 8,178 m³/h.

Mixed temperature:

$$T_m = \frac{0.30 \times 35 + 0.70 \times 24}{1.0} = \frac{10.5 + 16.8}{1.0} = 27.3 \;°\text{C}$$

Mixed humidity ratio (outside 0.0184, return 0.0094):

$$W_m = 0.30 \times 0.0184 + 0.70 \times 0.0094 = 0.00552 + 0.00658 = 0.01210 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

### Part (c): ADP from Bypass Factor

Using the formula derived in §3.2:

$$T_{ADP} = T_1 + \frac{T_2 - T_1}{BF} = 27.3 + \frac{13 - 27.3}{0.15} = 27.3 + \frac{-14.3}{0.15} = 27.3 - 95.3$$

That yields −68 °C, which is impossible. Hence the assumed BF is **too low** for this duty.

**Redesign for BF = 0.15** with $T_1$ = 27.3 °C and target $T_2$ = 13 °C:

$$\text{Required } BF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{ADP} - T_1}$$

For realistic ADP ≈ 7 °C:

$$BF = \frac{13 - 27.3}{7 - 27.3} = \frac{-14.3}{-20.3} = 0.704$$

So a BF of 0.15 is unrealistic. **Conclusion**: choose either a colder supply (e.g., 10 °C) or more coil rows.

### Part (d): Total refrigeration tonnage

$$\text{Total Heat} = SH + LH = 42 + 18 = 60 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\text{Tonnage} = \frac{60}{3.516} = 17.06 \;\text{TR}$$

Add **30 %** for fan heat, duct gain and safety margin:

$$\text{Plant TR} \approx 22 \;\text{TR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight**
> Most students blindly apply BF without checking feasibility. Always verify that $T_{ADP} \ge 5\,°\text{C}$ (to avoid coil frosting) and $T_{ADP} \le T_1$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Each Variable Affects Plant Size

| Variable ↑ | Supply Air Q ↑/↓ | Fan kW ↑/↓ | Coil TR ↑/↓ | Duct Size ↑/↓ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SH | Qs ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| LH | Forces larger Q if Ql > Qs | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ΔT (room–supply) | Q ↓ (good) | ↓ | same | ↓ |
| BF | Q ↓ for given T2 | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Outdoor temperature | SH, ventilation load ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Number of crew | LH ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Lighting level | SH ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Ventilation fraction | SH, LH ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define "Sensible Heat Factor" (SHF). What is its significance in the selection of a cooling coil?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sensible Heat Factor is defined as the ratio of sensible heat to the total heat (sensible + latent) of the conditioned space:

$$SHF = \frac{SH}{SH + LH}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It determines the *slope of the room condition line* on the psychrometric chart.
2. The intersection of this line with the saturation curve gives the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**, which in turn dictates the **chilled-water temperature** and **coil surface area** required.
3. A *high SHF* (e.g., 0.85, dry climates) demands a coil with high sensible capacity; a *low SHF* (e.g., 0.55, tropical ship cabins with 200 crew) demands a coil with high latent capacity.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the "Bypass Factor" of a cooling coil? A coil with BF = 0.15 receives air at 30 °C and leaves it at 14 °C. Calculate the apparatus dew point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Bypass Factor (BF) is the fraction of air that passes through the coil *without* undergoing any temperature change; mathematically,

$$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$$

where $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$ are coil entering, leaving and surface temperatures respectively.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$T_{ADP} = T_1 + \frac{T_2 - T_1}{BF} = 30 + \frac{14 - 30}{0.15} = 30 + \frac{-16}{0.15} = 30 - 106.7$$

This is non-physical. The implication is that for BF = 0.15, an inlet of 30 °C cannot be cooled to 14 °C in a single coil. Either BF must be ≥ 0.5 (a shallow coil), or chilled-water temperature must drop below 5 °C, or two coils in series must be used.

(For exam-credit purposes, the student shows the correct formula and recognises the physical inconsistency.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Air Conditioning Capacity & Air-Flow Sizing for a Ship's Cabin Block

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Modified pattern]**

A naval auxiliary vessel has the following AC design summary:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Inside design | 24 °C DB, 50 % RH |
| Outside design (tropical) | 35 °C DB, 28 °C WB |
| Total Sensible Heat (SH) | 65 kW |
| Total Latent Heat (LH) | 28 kW |
| Supply air temperature | 13 °C DB |
| Outside-air fraction | 25 % |
| Coil Bypass Factor | 0.20 |
| Specific volume of air (average) | 0.86 m³/kg |
| cp of air | 1.02 kJ/(kg·K) |

**Compute the following:**

**(a) [7 marks]** (i) The supply-air quantity in m³/h and CFM, using both sensible and latent heat equations.
(ii) The governing air-flow requirement and the SHF of the system.

**(b) [7 marks]** (iii) The mixed-air temperature.
(iv) The ADP of the cooling coil. Comment on whether the assumed BF = 0.20 is physically attainable.
(v) The required refrigeration tonnage, including a **30 % margin** for fan heat, duct gain and partial-load redundancy.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) (i) Supply Air Quantity

**Sensible basis:**

$$Q_s = \frac{SH \times v \times 3600}{c_p \times \Delta T} = \frac{65 \times 0.86 \times 3600}{1.02 \times (24 - 13)}$$

$$Q_s = \frac{201{,}240}{11.22} = 17{,}934 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

Convert to CFM:

$$Q_s = 17{,}934 \times 0.5886 = 10{,}556 \;\text{CFM}$$

**Latent basis** (need W_room and W_supply from psychrometric chart):

$W_{\text{room}}$ at 24 °C, 50 % RH ≈ **0.0094 kg/kg**
$W_{\text{supply}}$ at 13 °C saturated ≈ **0.0094 (since supply is approx. 95 % RH)} ≈ 0.0086 kg/kg$ *(assume supply leaves coil at 95 % RH)*
$\Delta W = 0.0094 - 0.0086 = 0.0008 \;\text{kg/kg}$ — too small for the high LH.

Better assumption: coil leaves air at saturation of $T_{ADP}$. Iterate: assume $T_{ADP}$ = 8 °C → $W_{ADP}$ ≈ 0.0068 kg/kg. Then $\Delta W = 0.0094 - 0.0068 = 0.0026 \;\text{kg/kg}$.

$$Q_l = \frac{LH \times v \times 3600}{h_{fg} \times \Delta W} = \frac{28 \times 0.86 \times 3600}{2430 \times 0.0026}$$

$$Q_l = \frac{86{,}688}{6.318} = 13{,}722 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 8{,}074 \;\text{CFM}$$

#### Part (a) (ii) Governing flow & SHF

$$Q_{\text{supply}} = \max(Q_s, \; Q_l) = \max(17{,}934, \; 13{,}722) = \mathbf{17{,}934 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}} \;(\mathbf{10{,}556 \;CFM})$$

Sensible heat governs.

$$SHF = \frac{65}{65 + 28} = \frac{65}{93} = \mathbf{0.699}$$

#### Part (b) (iii) Mixed Air Temperature

$$T_m = f_o \cdot T_o + (1 - f_o) \cdot T_r = 0.25 \times 35 + 0.75 \times 24 = 8.75 + 18.0 = \mathbf{26.75 \;°C}$$

#### Part (b) (iv) Apparatus Dew Point

$$T_{ADP} = T_1 + \frac{T_2 - T_1}{BF} = 26.75 + \frac{13 - 26.75}{0.20}$$

$$T_{ADP} = 26.75 + \frac{-13.75}{0.20} = 26.75 - 68.75 = -42 \;°\text{C}$$

**Physical comment (1 mark):** This is unattainable. For BF = 0.20 and $T_1$ = 26.75 °C, the *lowest possible* leaving temperature is when $T_{ADP}$ = chilled-water temperature (≈ 6 °C):

$$T_2^{\min} = T_1 - (1 - BF)(T_1 - T_{ADP}) = 26.75 - 0.80 \times (26.75 - 6) = 26.75 - 16.6 = 10.15 \;°\text{C}$$

So with a 6 °C chilled water the coil can deliver ~10 °C supply air. To achieve 13 °C supply, even BF = 0.20 is acceptable only if chilled water is warmer. **Conclusion:** design is feasible only with a colder chilled-water plant or with **BF increased to ~0.4** (e.g., a 4-row coil instead of 6-row).

#### Part (b) (v) Plant Tonnage

$$\text{Net cooling} = SH + LH = 65 + 28 = 93 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\text{Net TR} = \frac{93}{3.516} = 26.45 \;\text{TR}$$

With 30 % margin:

$$\text{Plant TR} = 1.30 \times 26.45 = \mathbf{34.4 \;\text{TR}} \;\;(\approx \mathbf{35 \;TR})$$

---

### Question B — Duct Sizing & Fan Selection for a Marine AHU

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Modified pattern]**

For the same plant above, the AHU serves a trunk duct of rectangular cross-section **600 mm × 400 mm**, length **30 m**, with two 90° elbows, one balancing damper, and one supply grille (loss coefficient 1.8).

**(a) [7 marks]** (i) Compute the equivalent round duct diameter.
(ii) Determine the air velocity in the trunk.
(iii) Compute the friction loss using the Darcy–Weisbach equation with friction factor f = 0.018 and dynamic pressure at the duct.

**(b) [7 marks]** (iv) Compute the cumulative static pressure loss through elbows, damper and grille.
(v) Calculate the total fan static pressure and the fan motor power assuming fan efficiency 65 % and motor efficiency 90 %.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) (i) Equivalent Diameter

Using the equal-friction formula for rectangular ducts:

$$D_e = 1.30 \cdot \frac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$$

where a = 0.6 m, b = 0.4 m.

$$ab = 0.24, \quad (ab)^{0.625} = 0.24^{0.625}$$

$$\ln(0.24) = -1.427, \quad 0.625 \times (-1.427) = -0.892, \quad e^{-0.892} = 0.410$$

$$a + b = 1.0, \quad (a+b)^{0.25} = 1.0$$

$$D_e = 1.30 \times \frac{0.410}{1.0} = \mathbf{0.533 \;m}$$

#### Part (a) (ii) Velocity in Trunk

Cross-sectional area: $A = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \;\text{m}^2$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{17{,}934}{3600} = 4.98 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$v = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{4.98}{0.24} = \mathbf{20.75 \;\text{m/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Velocity limit warning:** For shipboard trunks the maximum recommended is **9–10 m/s** to keep NC below 45. A 20 m/s trunk is *unacceptable* — the duct must be resized to a larger cross-section.

**Revised duct sizing** for v = 9 m/s: $A = 4.98/9 = 0.553 \;\text{m}^2$, e.g., **900 mm × 650 mm**.

#### Part (a) (iii) Friction Loss (Darcy–Weisbach)

$$\Delta p_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta p_f = 0.018 \times \frac{30}{0.533} \times \frac{1.20 \times 9^2}{2} = 0.018 \times 56.3 \times 48.6$$

$$\Delta p_f = 49.2 \;\text{Pa}$$

#### Part (b) (iv) Local (Fitting) Losses

| Fitting | Quantity | C | Δp = C × ρv²/2 (Pa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90° elbow | 2 | 0.9 each | 2 × 0.9 × 48.6 = 87.5 |
| Balancing damper | 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 × 48.6 = 72.9 |
| Supply grille | 1 | 1.8 | 1.8 × 48.6 = 87.5 |
| **Sub-total** | | | **247.9 Pa** |

#### Part (b) (v) Total Fan SP & Motor kW

Total static pressure (with 10 % allowance for unaccounted fittings):

$$\Delta p_{\text{total}} = 1.10 \times (\Delta p_f + \Delta p_{\text{local}}) = 1.10 \times (49.2 + 247.9) = 326.8 \;\text{Pa}$$

Ideal fan power:

$$P_{\text{fan,ideal}} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta p_{\text{total}} = 4.98 \times 326.8 = 1{,}627 \;\text{W}$$

Brake power (with fan and motor efficiencies):

$$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{P_{\text{fan,ideal}}}{\eta_{\text{fan}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}} = \frac{1{,}627}{0.65 \times 0.90} = 2{,}781 \;\text{W}$$

Select a standard **3.0 kW** marine-grade motor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups:** Mixing kW with TR (1 TR = 3.516 kW). One wrong conversion = 1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting ventilation air:** Using only *room* SH/LH and ignoring the outside-air load. Most common error — **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Inverting the BF formula:** A surprising number of students write $(T_1 - T_2)/(T_1 - T_3)$. Examiners deduct **2 marks**.
> 4. **Using $c_p$ = 1.005 when moist-air value 1.02 is needed** in tropical high-humidity conditions: −1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the 30 % margin** when asked for plant TR: −2 marks.
> 6. **Ignoring duct velocity limits** — saying "design is OK" at 20 m/s is professionally wrong. −2 marks for absence of comment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- [x] **Cooling capacity = SH + LH**; always split into the two.
- [x] **Sensible air flow**: $Q_s = 3060 \cdot SH / \Delta T$  (SH in kW, ΔT in K → Q in m³/h).
- [x] **Latent air flow**: $Q_l = \dot{V} = LH \cdot v \cdot 3600 / (h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W)$.
- [x] **Governing flow** = larger of $Q_s$ and $Q_l$.
- [x] **SHF** = SH / (SH+LH) — slope of room line on psychrometric chart.
- [x] **ADP** = intersection of SHF line with saturation curve → coil surface temperature.
- [x] **BF** = $(T_2 - T_1)/(T_3 - T_1)$; for marine 6-row coil ≈ 0.10–0.15; DX coil ≈ 0.15–0.30.
- [x] **T_ADP formula**: $T_{ADP} = T_1 + (T_2 - T_1)/BF$ — verify result is between 5 °C and $T_1$.
- [x] **Mixed air**: $T_m = f_o T_o + (1 - f_o) T_r$ with $f_o$ = outside-air fraction.
- [x] **Plant TR** = 1.30 × (SH + LH)/3.516 — always include margin for fan, duct and redundancy.
- [x] **Duct velocity**: trunks 6–9 m/s; branches 4–6 m/s; grilles 2.5–4 m/s.
- [x] **Equivalent round duct**: $D_e = 1.30 (ab)^{0.625}/(a+b)^{0.25}$.
- [x] **Fan power**: $P = \dot V \Delta p / (\eta_{fan} \eta_{motor})$.
- [x] **Per-person load (tropical)** ≈ 60 W sensible + 70 W latent; add 14 W per m/s of air movement.
- [x] **Ventilation air** per person = 30 m³/h (IASO) → use in OA fraction calculation.
- [x] **Marine specific**: salt-laden OA → pre-filter + fine filter; low NC noise limits; flame-retardant ducts; fire dampers at bulkhead penetrations.
- [x] **Always convert** CFM ↔ m³/h using 1 CFM = 1.699 m³/h (or 1 m³/h = 0.5886 CFM).
- [x] **Don't forget**: $1.08$ in IP sensible formula and $0.68$ in IP latent formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Systems # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Air Conditioning (AC)** is the simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, air movement, air cleanliness (dust, odor, bacteria), and air distribution** within an enclosed space — typically the accommodation blocks, bridge, galley, server rooms, and machinery control spaces of a marine vessel — to provide a stable, comfortable, and healthy environment for the crew, cargo, and sensitive equipment. The **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE)** Standard 55 defines thermal comfort as *“that condition of mind which expresses satisfaction with the thermal environment.”* For marine applications, **ISO 7547 / IMO Resolution A.772(18)** governs the design conditions for shipboard air conditioning. The four primary functions of any shipboard AC plant are: 1. **Cooling / Heating** — maintain DB temperature between **22 °C and 27 °C** (typically **24 °C DB, 50 % RH** for accommodation). 2. **Humidification / Dehumidification** — maintain relative humidity between **40 % – 60 %**. 3. **Filtration** — remove salt aerosols, soot, oil mist, and biological contaminants using **G3 / G4 pre-filters + F7/F8 fine filters**. 4. **Ventilation / Distribution** — supply **100 % outside air per hour** to accommodation and **6 – 10 air changes per hour (ACH)** for public spaces. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — “The Human Body as an AC Plant” Imagine your body as a ship. The **lungs** are the fresh-air intake fans, the **skin pores** are the cooling coils, the **sweat glands** are the dehumidifiers, and the **bloodstream** is the ductwork carrying conditioned fluid. If any one function fails — say, humidity control — the entire system feels “off,” even if temperature is correct. On a ship: - The **sea** is the ultimate heat sink (analogous to blood returning to the heart). - The **chilled water loop** is the bloodstream. - The **AHU (Air Handling Unit)** is the lungs. - The **thermostat in each cabin** is the brain sensing comfort. This analogy makes it intuitive why a ship cannot use a simple room AC — the **heat gains from sun, engines, galley, and 50+ crew** all converge in one central plant that must reject heat to **sea water at ~28–32 °C** (tropical conditions). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is on the *system architecture* and *component integration* of marine AC — not just the refrigeration cycle (which was Module 2/3). Pay attention to **system selection, duct layouts, redundancy, and zone control** as these are common exam question areas. ## 1.3 Why Air Conditioning on Ships is a *Distinct Engineering Discipline* A ship is unique because: - **Heat sources are dense and asymmetric** — engine room, galley, sun-exposed decks. - **Ambient conditions vary drastically** — polar (−20 °C) to tropical (+45 °C). - **Space is at a premium** — ductwork, AHUs, and chiller rooms must fit between stiffeners. - **Redundancy is mandatory** — SOLAS requires backup for safety-critical ventilation. - **Vibration and motion** — equipment must be resilient to roll/pitch up to **±45° / ±10°** (depending on vessel type). - **Corrosion** — salt-laden air mandates **cupronickel or SS316** coils and epoxy-coated ductwork. These constraints push marine AC toward **centralized chilled-water (CHW) systems** rather than direct-expansion (DX) splits. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Shipboard AC Systems Shipboard AC systems can be classified along **four axes**: ### A. By Refrigerant Delivery Method | System Type | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Direct Expansion (DX)** | Refrigerant evaporates directly in the air-handling coil. | Small vessels, fishing boats, single-cabin ACs | | **Chilled Water (CHW)** | Chiller produces 6–12 °C water; AHUs cool air via water coils. | Large commercial vessels, naval ships, cruise liners | | **Pre-cooled Air Systems** | Central station cools primary air; secondary air mixes via induction units. | Older passenger ships, some naval auxiliaries | ### B. By Air Distribution Topology - **Single Duct (All-Air)** — one supply duct serves all zones; simplest, used in **cargo ships**. - **Dual Duct** — separate hot/cold ducts with mixing boxes; high energy cost, rarely used today except in **luxury cruise liners**. - **Multi-Zone** — AHU serves multiple zones, each with its own damper; common in **modern offshore support vessels (OSVs)**. ### C. By Capacity Range | Class | Cooling Capacity | Vessel | |---|---|---| | Small | < 50 kW | Yachts, small fishing boats | | Medium | 50 – 500 kW | Naval patrol, offshore crew boats | | Large | 500 – 5000 kW | Cruise ships, aircraft carriers | | Mega | > 5000 kW | Icon-class cruise ships (~30 MW total) | ### D. By Energy Source - **Vapor Compression** (dominant) — mechanical chiller. - **Absorption** — uses waste heat from jacket cooling or exhaust; appears on LNG carriers where waste heat is abundant. - **Chilled Beam / Radiant Cooling** — emerging in **green ships**. ## 2.2 The Central Chilled-Water AC Plant — Block Architecture The most common marine configuration follows this architecture: ``` Sea Water → [Box Cooler / Shell & Tube] → [Refrigerant: Evaporator] → [Chilled Water (6–12°C)] → [AHU Coil] → [Supply Air to Cabins] ``` The four key thermodynamic processes are: 1. **Airside** — outside air + return air mixed → filtered → preheated (winter) → cooled/dehumidified → reheated (if needed) → supplied. 2. **Waterside** — chilled water absorbs heat in AHU coil → returns to chiller → rejects heat to seawater in condenser. 3. **Refrigerant cycle** — R-134a, R-407C, R-410A, or increasingly **R-1234ze** (low-GWP). 4. **Sea side** — seawater at 28–32 °C absorbs condenser heat via titanium or cupronickel plate heat exchangers. ## 2.3 Psychrometrics — The Language of AC > [!IMPORTANT] > Every KTU exam problem on AC will use the **psychrometric chart**. You must be fluent in the following eight parameters: | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Ship Value | |---|---|---| | Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT | 24 – 27 °C | | Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT | 18 – 20 °C | | Relative Humidity | RH | 50 % | | Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) | ω | 0.0095 – 0.012 kg/kg dry air | | Enthalpy | h | 50 – 55 kJ/kg | | Specific Volume | v | 0.86 m³/kg | | Dew Point | DPT | 12 – 14 °C | | Air Density | ρ | **1.2 kg/m³** | The four fundamental psychrometric equations you must memorize: ### Equation 1: Humidity Ratio from Saturation Pressure $$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$ where $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor, $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (~101.325 kPa at sea level). ### Equation 2: Relative Humidity $$RH = \frac{P_v}{P_s(DBT)} \cdot 100$$ where $P_s(DBT)$ = saturation pressure at dry-bulb temperature (from steam tables or empirical correlations). ### Equation 3: Moist Air Enthalpy (per kg dry air) $$h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)$$ where $T$ is in °C and $h$ in kJ/kg dry air. ### Equation 4: Wet Bulb Temperature (Carrier's Equation) $$P_s(WBT) - P_v = A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (DBT - WBT)$$ where $A \approx 0.000367$ (psychrometric constant for standard conditions). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — every KTU numerical question is built from one or more of these.** | # | Formula | Use Case | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | Sensible + latent cooling load | kW | | 2 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat only | kW | | 3 | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_2) \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat only | kW | | 4 | $\dot{V} = \frac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required supply airflow | m³/s | | 5 | $SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Factor (0.6–0.8 for cabins) | dimensionless | | 6 | $ADP = f(DBT, RH, SHF)$ | Apparatus Dew Point from psychrometric chart | °C | | 7 | $Bypass\ Factor\ (BF) = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | Coil performance parameter | 0–1 | | 8 | $Contact\ Factor = 1 - BF$ | Effectiveness of coil | 0–1 | | 9 | $U \cdot A = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p}{BF}$ | Coil sizing (Log Mean Temp Diff / LMTD approach) | W/K | | 10 | $EER = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | dimensionless | | 11 | $COP = \frac{Q_e}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (chiller) | dimensionless | | 12 | $Ventilation\ Load = \dot{m}_{oa} \cdot (h_{oa} - h_{rm})$ | Fresh-air enthalpy load | kW | | 13 | $Duct\ Friction\ Loss = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot V^2}{2 \cdot D}$ | Darcy-Weisbach for duct sizing | Pa | | 14 | $ACH = \frac{\dot{V}_{supply}}{V_{room}}$ | Air changes per hour | h⁻¹ | | 15 | $T_{return} = T_{supply} + \frac{Q_{room}}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p}$ | Room heat balance | °C | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** In formulas 1, 2, 3, $\dot{m}_a$ is the **mass flow rate of *dry* air**, not moist air. Always divide the volumetric flow by specific volume: $\dot{m}_a = \dot{V} / v$. ## 2.5 Heat Load Components on a Ship The total cooling load on a vessel's AC system is the sum of **eight** contributions: 1. **Transmission (Conduction) through hull, decks, and bulkheads** — driven by solar gain, sea temperature, engine-room heat soak. 2. **Solar Radiation through windows/scuttles** — can be **300–800 W/m²** on a sunlit side. 3. **Ventilation / Infiltration** — **100 % outside air** is mandatory in accommodation (SOLAS). 4. **Occupants** — **120 W sensible + 50 W latent per person** (light activity, ~24 °C). 5. **Lighting** — **15–25 W/m²** for LED, **30–50 W/m²** for fluorescent. 6. **Electrical equipment** — computers, galley appliances, navigation gear. 7. **Machinery** — galley exhaust hoods, laundry, refrigeration. 8. **Miscellaneous** — piping losses, duct heat gains, safety factor (typically **+10 %**). > [!NOTE] > **ASHRAE Rule of Thumb** for marine accommodation: **120–180 W/m²** of floor area, peaking at **250 W/m²** in tropical galleys. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Enthalpy of Moist Air Starting from first principles, moist air is a binary mixture of **dry air** and **water vapor**. Each kg of dry air carries $\omega$ kg of water vapor. Enthalpy is the sum of the two contributions: $$h = h_{da} + \omega \cdot h_v$$ Using reference states: - $h_{da} = c_{p,da} \cdot T$, where $c_{p,da} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K - $h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot T$, where $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg at 0 °C and $c_{p,v} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K Therefore: $$\boxed{h = 1.005 \cdot T + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T)}$$ This is **Equation 3** in your cheat sheet — used in every cooling-load calculation. ## 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and ADP Consider an air stream entering a cooling coil at state 1 (DBT₁, ω₁) and leaving at state 2 (DBT₂, ω₂). The coil has an **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — the surface temperature of the coil at which the air would leave if it reached saturation. The **Sensible Heat** is the dry-bulb temperature drop: $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$ The **Latent Heat** is the moisture removal: $$Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_2) \cdot h_{fg}$$ The **Total Heat**: $$Q_t = Q_s + Q_l$$ By definition: $$SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t}$$ Geometrically, on the psychrometric chart, SHF is the slope of the line joining **state 1** to **state 2**, relative to the saturation curve. The intersection of this line (extended) with the saturation curve gives the **ADP**: $$ADP = T_1 - \frac{Q_s / (\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p)}{1 - BF}$$ where the **Bypass Factor (BF)** is the ratio of air that does **not** contact the coil to the total air. For marine AHUs, BF typically ranges from **0.05 to 0.20**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern > **Problem (12 Marks Pattern):** > An accommodation block on a passenger ship requires air conditioning. Design conditions: > - Inside: 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH > - Outside (tropical): 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT > - Air quantity: 20 m³/s > - SHF of coil: 0.75 > - Ventilation air: 30 % of supply > - Find: (a) Cooling load in kW, (b) ADP, (c) Chilled water flow at 6 °C inlet / 12 °C outlet. ### Step 1 — Convert Outside Conditions to Humidity Ratio At 35 °C DBT, saturation pressure $P_s(35) \approx 5.628$ kPa. At 28 °C WBT, $P_s(28) \approx 3.783$ kPa. Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. Vapor pressure from wet-bulb equation: $$P_v = P_s(WBT) - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (DBT - WBT)$$ $$P_v = 3.783 - 0.000367 \cdot 101.325 \cdot (35 - 28)$$ $$P_v = 3.783 - 0.260 = 3.523\ \text{kPa}$$ Humidity ratio: $$\omega_o = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.523}{101.325 - 3.523} = 0.0224\ \text{kg/kg dry air}$$ ### Step 2 — Inside Conditions At 24 °C DBT, $P_s(24) = 2.985$ kPa. $$P_v = 0.50 \cdot 2.985 = 1.493\ \text{kPa}$$ $$\omega_i = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.493}{101.325 - 1.493} = 0.00929\ \text{kg/kg dry air}$$ ### Step 3 — Enthalpies Outside: $$h_o = 1.005 \cdot 35 + 0.0224 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 35)$$ $$h_o = 35.175 + 0.0224 \cdot 2566.8$$ $$h_o = 35.175 + 57.50 = 92.67\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ Inside: $$h_i = 1.005 \cdot 24 + 0.00929 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 24)$$ $$h_i = 24.12 + 0.00929 \cdot 2546.1$$ $$h_i = 24.12 + 23.65 = 47.77\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ ### Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate of Air Specific volume of inside air ≈ 0.866 m³/kg. $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{20}{0.866} = 23.09\ \text{kg/s}$$ ### Step 5 — Total Cooling Load Assuming mixed-air (30 % outside + 70 % return) at ideal mixing: $$h_{mix} = 0.30 \cdot 92.67 + 0.70 \cdot 47.77$$ $$h_{mix} = 27.80 + 33.44 = 61.24\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_t = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_{mix} - h_i)$$ $$Q_t = 23.09 \cdot (61.24 - 47.77)$$ $$Q_t = 23.09 \cdot 13.47$$ $$\boxed{Q_t = 311.0\ \text{kW}}$$ ### Step 6 — Apparatus Dew Point Required supply enthalpy: $$h_{supply} = h_i + \frac{Q_s}{\dot{m}_a} = h_i + SHF \cdot (h_{mix} - h_i)$$ $$h_{supply} = 47.77 + 0.75 \cdot (61.24 - 47.77)$$ $$h_{supply} = 47.77 + 10.10 = 57.87\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ Sensible portion of supply temperature drop: $$\Delta T_s = \frac{SHF \cdot Q_t}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p} = \frac{0.75 \cdot 311.0}{23.09 \cdot 1.005} = 10.05\ °C$$ $$T_{supply} = 24 - 10.05 = 13.95\ °C$$ Assuming BF = 0.10: $$ADP = T_{supply} - BF \cdot (T_{mix} - T_{supply})$$ First, mixed DBT: $$T_{mix} = 0.30 \cdot 35 + 0.70 \cdot 24 = 27.3\ °C$$ $$ADP = 13.95 - 0.10 \cdot (27.3 - 13.95) = 13.95 - 1.34 = 12.61\ °C$$ $$\boxed{ADP \approx 12.6\ °C}$$ ### Step 7 — Chilled Water Flow Rate Heat removed by chilled water: $$Q_{cw} = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ $$311.0 = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot 4.186 \cdot (12 - 6)$$ $$\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{311.0}{25.12} = 12.38\ \text{kg/s}$$ In volumetric terms: $$\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{12.38}{1000} = 0.01238\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 44.6\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{cw} \approx 44.6\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Roles | Component | Function | Failure Mode | KTU Exam Tag | |---|---|---|---| | Filter (G3/F7) | Removes dust, salt, soot | Pressure drop ↑, IAQ ↓ | Design load + 10 % | | Pre-heater | Winter preheat to avoid coil freeze | Coil burst | Often asked | | Cooling coil | Sensible + latent cooling | BF rises, ADP shifts | Numericals | | Re-heater | Temperature control (overcooling + reheat) | Wasteful if mis-set | "Should you reheat?" | | Humidifier | Winter humidification | Steam/water carryover | Desiccant systems | | Supply fan | Distributes air | Bearing failure, imbalance | Fan laws | | Return fan | Maintains pressurization | Negative pressure, smoke ingress | SOLAS vent | | Dampers | Zone control | Stuck/failed closed | Duct design | | Ductwork | Air transport | Leakage 5–15 % | Pressure class | ## 3.5 Key Derivation: Duct Friction Loss (Darcy-Weisbach) Pressure drop per meter of duct: $$\Delta P = \frac{f \cdot L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ where: - $f$ = friction factor (Moody chart, ~0.02 for galvanized steel at typical Re) - $L$ = duct length (m) - $D$ = hydraulic diameter (m) - $\rho$ = air density (1.2 kg/m³) - $V$ = air velocity (m/s) For marine rectangular ducts, $D = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$, where $a$ and $b$ are the side dimensions. Equating pressure drop to fan head: $$H_{fan} = \sum \Delta P_{friction} + \sum \Delta P_{fittings} + \Delta P_{coil} + \Delta P_{filter}$$ Typical design velocity in marine ducts: **6–10 m/s** for main trunks, **4–6 m/s** for branches. # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Central Chilled-Water AC System — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outside Air Intake
35 C DB / 28 C WB] --> B[Damper
100 percent Outside Air] B --> C[Pre Filter G3
Salt and Dust Removal] C --> D[Fine Filter F7
IAQ Compliance] D --> E[Mixing Box
OA plus Return Air] E --> F[Pre Heater Coil
Winter Frost Prevention] F --> G[Cooling Coil
ADP 12.6 C, SHF 0.75] G --> H[Reheat Coil
Optional Zone Trim] H --> I[Supply Fan
20 m3 per sec, 800 Pa] I --> J[Main Supply Duct
V 8 m per sec] J --> K[Zone Dampers
Cabin Galley Bridge] K --> L[Cabins and Public Spaces
24 C DB 50 percent RH] L --> M[Return Air Grille] M --> N[Return Fan
Maintains 25 Pa Positive] N --> E O[Chilled Water Plant
6 to 12 C Loop] --> G G --> P[CHW Return Header
12 C to Chiller] P --> Q[Marine Chiller
COP 3.2 to 4.0] Q --> R[Sea Water Condenser
Titanium Plate, 28 C SW] R --> S[Sea Bay Inlet
Sea Chest] S --> T[ overboard Discharge] ``` ## 4.2 Psychrometric Process Path ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Outside Air
35 C DB 22.4 g per kg] --> M[Mixing
30 percent OA plus 70 percent RA] M --> P2[Mixed Air
27.3 C DB 13.6 g per kg] P2 --> P3[Cooling and Dehumidification
SHF 0.75] P3 --> P4[Supply Air
14.0 C DB 9.3 g per kg] P4 --> P5[Room Heat Gains
Sensible and Latent] P5 --> P6[Room Condition
24 C DB 9.3 g per kg] P6 --> M ``` ## 4.3 Component Hierarchy in a Marine AHU ```mermaid flowchart TD AHU[Air Handling Unit AHU Module] --> INTAKE[Intake Section
Damper and Louver] AHU --> FILT[Filter Section
G3 plus F7] AHU --> PREH[Pre Heat Coil
Hot Water] AHU --> COOL[Cooling Coil
Chilled Water 6 to 12 C] AHU --> REH[Reheat Coil
Hot Water] AHU --> FAN[Supply Fan Section
Vane or EC] AHU --> ELIM[Eliminator / Drain Pan
Stainless 304] AHU --> DAMP[Damper Bank
Modulating Actuators] FAN --> DUCT[Discharge Plenum
to Main Duct] ``` ## 4.4 Redundancy Architecture (SOLAS-Compliant) ```mermaid flowchart LR CH1[Chiller 1
60 percent capacity] --> BUS[Common CHW Header] CH2[Chiller 2
60 percent capacity] --> BUS BUS --> PU1[AHU 1
Accommodation] BUS --> PU2[AHU 2
Galley and Laundry] BUS --> PU3[AHU 3
Bridge and ECR] SW1[Sea Water Pump 1] --> CH1 SW2[Sea Water Pump 2] --> CH2 GEN1[Emergency Generator] -.Power Backup.-> CH2 GEN1 -.Power Backup.-> PU3 ``` ## 4.5 Marine AC System Selection Logic Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Vessel Type] --> Q1{Passenger?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Chilled Water with Multi Zone AHUs] Q1 -->|No| Q3{Cargo or Tanker?} Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Central DX or CHW for Accommodation Only] Q3 -->|No| Q5{Navy or Offshore?} Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[CHW with N plus 1 Chiller Redundancy] Q5 -->|No| Q7[Self Contained DX Units] ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List any **six** factors that influence the design of a marine air conditioning system. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** 1. **Vessel type and route** — tropical routes demand higher cooling capacity. 2. **Heat gains** — solar, transmission, occupants, equipment, galley. 3. **Outside air requirements** — SOLAS mandates ~100 % fresh air for accommodation. 4. **Space constraints** — between decks, stiffeners, duct routing. 5. **Redundancy** — SOLAS / Classification society requirements (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's). 6. **Ambient seawater temperature** — dictates condenser sizing. 7. **Vibration and noise** — fan selection, isolation mounts. 8. **Energy efficiency** — EER, COP targets; IMO EEDI/EEXI for new builds. *(Any six well-explained = 3 marks; one-line list = 1–2 marks)* ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** and **Bypass Factor (BF)**. Why is BF important in coil selection? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** - **ADP**: The temperature of the cooling coil surface corresponding to the saturation point on the psychrometric chart where the process line from entering air intersects the saturation curve. It represents the *ideal* leaving air state if 100 % of air contacted the coil. - **BF**: The fraction of supply air that passes through the coil without contacting the cold surface, expressed as $BF = (T_2 - T_{ADP}) / (T_1 - T_{ADP})$. - **Importance**: Lower BF (more rows, higher face velocity) gives better dehumidification but higher pressure drop. BF directly determines coil face area, refrigerant charge, and energy consumption. ## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — **Design-Oriented** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the layout of a **central chilled-water air conditioning plant** for a passenger ship. Label all major components. (7 Marks) > > **(b)** An accommodation space on a vessel has a total cooling load of **250 kW** at design conditions of 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT outside and 24 °C DBT, 50 % RH inside. If the sensible heat factor is 0.70 and the chilled water enters at 6 °C and leaves at 12 °C, calculate: > (i) Mass flow rate of chilled water. > (ii) Supply air temperature, assuming a Bypass Factor of 0.10. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram & Description (7 Marks):** The central chilled-water AC plant on a passenger ship consists of the following subsystems: 1. **Refrigeration Subsystem (Chiller Plant)** — typically two or three screw/centrifugal chillers located in the machinery space, each sized for **60–70 %** of peak load (N+1 redundancy). 2. **Chilled Water (CHW) Distribution** — a closed loop with **6 °C supply / 12 °C return**, with duty + standby circulating pumps. 3. **Sea Water Subsystem** — titanium plate heat exchangers rejecting condenser heat to sea at 28–32 °C; duty + standby sea-water pumps with box coolers. 4. **Air Handling Units (AHUs)** — typically 4–6 modular AHUs serving cabins, galley, public spaces, and the bridge. 5. **Ductwork** — galvanized or SS ducts routed through service corridors; galvanized is preferred inside accommodation, stainless in galley/laundry. 6. **Controls** — DDC with CO₂, temperature, and humidity sensors; BMS integration with class-approved alarm/monitoring. *(Each subsystem 1 mark + 1 mark for the neat block diagram = 7 marks.)* **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $Q_t = 250$ kW, SHF = 0.70 - $T_{cw,in} = 6$ °C, $T_{cw,out} = 12$ °C - $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, BF = 0.10 **(i) Chilled water mass flow:** From $Q_t = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T$: $$\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{Q_t}{c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})} = \frac{250}{4.186 \cdot (12 - 6)} = \frac{250}{25.12}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{cw} = 9.95\ \text{kg/s} \approx 35.8\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}}$$ **(ii) Supply air temperature:** Sensible portion of cooling load: $$Q_s = SHF \cdot Q_t = 0.70 \cdot 250 = 175\ \text{kW}$$ For BF = 0.10 and ADP determined by chilled water mean temperature $\approx 9$ °C: $$BF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$ Assuming mixed air enters at approximately $T_1 = T_{room} + \Delta T$ where $\Delta T = 8$ to 10 °C: Taking $T_1 = 32$ °C (typical mixed condition): $$0.10 = \frac{T_2 - 9}{32 - 9}$$ $$T_2 = 9 + 0.10 \cdot 23 = 11.3\ °C$$ $$\boxed{T_{supply} \approx 11.3\ °C}$$ *(Full marks require showing: identification of ADP ≈ chilled water mean temp, BF equation, substitution. 7 marks total.)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — **Conceptual + Comparison** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]** > **(a)** Compare **central chilled-water systems** and **direct-expansion (DX) systems** for marine air conditioning. Mention at least **five** points of comparison. (7 Marks) > > **(b)** A ship's AHU supplies **15 m³/s** of air at 14 °C DBT, 80 % RH to a cabin zone. The cabin return air is at 26 °C DBT, 50 % RH. If the room heat gains are purely sensible and equal to **80 kW**, calculate the **mass flow rate of supply air** and verify whether the supplied enthalpy is sufficient to absorb the latent and sensible loads. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | # | Parameter | Central CHW | DX System | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Capacity range | Large (50 kW – 30 MW) | Small (< 50 kW) | | 2 | Compressor location | Central chiller room (machinery space) | At each AHU / split unit | | 3 | Maintenance access | Easier (centralized) | Harder (distributed) | | 4 | Refrigerant charge | Low (in chiller only) | High (distributed in duct area) | | 5 | Zonal control | Excellent (water flow valves) | Limited (each DX unit) | | 6 | Energy efficiency (part load) | Better (chiller VFD) | Poorer (cycling losses) | | 7 | Initial cost | Higher | Lower | | 8 | Application | Passenger, navy, OSVs | Yachts, fishing, small boats | *(Each correct point with explanation: 1.5 marks × 5 = 7.5, capped at 7.)* **(b) Numerical Verification (7 Marks):** Specific volume of supply air at 14 °C, 80 % RH ≈ 0.826 m³/kg. $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{15}{0.826} = 18.16\ \text{kg/s}$$ Humidity ratio at supply (14 °C, 80 % RH): - $P_s(14) = 1.599$ kPa - $P_v = 0.80 \cdot 1.599 = 1.279$ kPa - $\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.279}{101.325 - 1.279} = 0.00795$ kg/kg Humidity ratio at return (26 °C, 50 % RH): - $P_s(26) = 3.363$ kPa - $P_v = 0.50 \cdot 3.363 = 1.682$ kPa - $\omega_r = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.682}{101.325 - 1.682} = 0.01051$ kg/kg Enthalpies: $$h_s = 1.005 \cdot 14 + 0.00795 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 14) = 14.07 + 0.00795 \cdot 2527.3 = 14.07 + 20.09 = 34.16\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$h_r = 1.005 \cdot 26 + 0.01051 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 26) = 26.13 + 0.01051 \cdot 2549.9 = 26.13 + 26.80 = 52.93\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ Sensible load check (using supply ΔT = 26 − 14 = 12 °C): $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 18.16 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 12 = 219.0\ \text{kW}$$ This **exceeds** the given 80 kW room load, meaning the supply air is *overcooling* — actual supply temperature should be higher. Recalculating: For $Q_s = 80$ kW with same $\dot{m}_a$: $$\Delta T = \frac{80}{18.16 \cdot 1.005} = 4.38\ °C$$ $$T_{supply} = 26 - 4.38 = 21.62\ °C$$ Latent load (since no latent gains are stated, latent is zero): $$Q_l = 0\ \text{kW}$$ Total load = 80 kW, which equals sensible portion — consistent. $$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 18.16\ \text{kg/s},\ T_{supply} \approx 21.6\ °C,\ \text{system is correctly sized}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting units in mass flow calculations** — always specify kg/s or m³/h explicitly. A correct number without a unit loses 1 mark. > 2. **Mixing up ADP with DPT** — ADP is the *coil surface* temperature, dew point is the *air* temperature at saturation. They are not the same. > 3. **Using moist-air specific heat (1.005)** for water-side calculations — that's a psychrometric convention. Water-side is **4.186 kJ/kg·K**. > 4. **Ignoring the 100 % fresh-air ventilation load** — this is the *dominant* load on most marine AC plants. Examiners love testing whether you add outside-air enthalpy. > 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — every box and arrow must carry a label with flow direction. An unlabeled block diagram earns at most 2 of 7 marks. > 6. **Stating SHF > 1.0 or < 0.0** — physically impossible. SHF is between 0 and 1. If your math gives a negative value, you have an error. > 7. **Forgetting SHF when calculating supply temperature** — many students use total load instead of sensible load, leading to undersized ducts. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air Conditioning Systems (Module 4)** - [ ] **Definition**: AC = control of T, RH, cleanliness, motion, distribution (ASHRAE). - [ ] **Marine design conditions**: 24 °C DB, 50 % RH inside; 35 °C/28 °C WB outside (tropical). - [ ] **System types**: DX vs Chilled Water (CHW); Centralized preferred on large vessels. - [ ] **Architecture**: OA → Filter → Preheat → Cool → Reheat → Fan → Duct → Zone. - [ ] **ASHRAE Standard 55** and **IMO A.772(18)** govern comfort criteria. - [ ] **SOLAS**: 100 % fresh air for accommodation; positive pressure 25 Pa. - [ ] **Psychrometric equations** to memorize: $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$; $\omega = 0.622 P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$. - [ ] **SHF = Q_s / Q_t**; **BF = (T₂ − T_ADP) / (T₁ − T_ADP)**; ADP from psychrometric chart. - [ ] **Heat load components**: Transmission, Solar, Ventilation, Occupants, Lights, Equipment, Machinery. - [ ] **Chilled water loop**: 6 °C supply / 12 °C return; seawater 28–32 °C in condenser. - [ ] **Ventilation rate**: 100 % outside air or **6–10 ACH** in accommodation. - [ ] **Refrigerants**: R-134a legacy; R-1234ze (low-GWP) emerging for marine. - [ ] **Duct design**: 6–10 m/s main, 4–6 m/s branch; galvanized inside, stainless in galley. - [ ] **Redundancy**: N+1 chillers; duty + standby pumps; emergency power per SOLAS. - [ ] **Energy metrics**: COP (chiller) typically 3.0–4.5; EER (room AC) 2.5–3.5. - [ ] **Common exam traps**: units, ADP vs DPT, OA load omission, SHF bounds. - [ ] **Major components**: AHU, chiller, condenser, cooling tower (none on ships — use sea), filters, dampers, ducts, controls. - [ ] **Materials**: cupronickel/SMO/titanium tubes for seawater; SS316 for galley/laundry ducts.

topics.title: HVAC Systems and Components - Single Zone System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HVAC Systems and Components - Single Zone System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
