> **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)** on ships refers to the integrated environmental control system responsible for maintaining **temperature, humidity, air purity, and air motion** within specified comfort or process limits across various compartments such as accommodation blocks, navigation bridge, engine control room (ECR), galleys, and hospital.

A **Single Zone System** is the most fundamental configuration of a centralized air conditioning plant in which a **single Air Handling Unit (AHU)** serves **only one thermal zone** (i.e., a single space, cabin, or compartment) with uniform conditioning requirements. The entire supply air stream is conditioned to identical temperature, humidity, and cleanliness levels because there is no requirement to split the airflow into different sub-zones with varying load profiles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (PENST743 / Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between **Single Zone**, **Multi Zone**, **Dual Duct**, and **Reheat Systems**. The Single Zone System forms the *base architecture* from which all other variants are derived — making it a **CO1 & CO2 high-priority topic**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **single window AC unit installed inside a ship's cabin**:

- There is **one room** → one thermostat on the wall.
- The AC pushes air, cools/dehumidifies it, and returns it back into the same room.
- Whatever setting you choose applies **uniformly** to the entire cabin.

Now scale this idea up to a **centralized chiller-fed AHU** that supplies air through ducts to *one compartment* (say the **Bridge**). That entire architecture — one AHU, one supply duct, one return duct, one thermostat — is the **Single Zone System**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Shipboard Analogy:** The Engine Control Room (ECR) of a tanker is the *quintessential single zone* on board a ship. It has high heat load from instruments, requires positive pressure, and cannot share air with adjacent spaces due to **SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire boundary regulations**. Hence a dedicated AHU is provided — making it a textbook single zone installation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Design indoor temperature (comfort)** | **24 – 26 °C** | °C |
| **Design relative humidity (comfort)** | **50 ± 10 % RH** | % |
| **Specific heat of air (cₚ)** | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** | kJ/kg·K |
| **Density of standard air (ρ)** | **1.2 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| **Specific volume of air (v)** | **0.834 m³/kg** | m³/kg |
| **Latent heat of vaporization (hfg @ 0 °C)** | **2501 kJ/kg** | kJ/kg |
| **Minimum outside air (per person)** | **8 – 10 L/s** | L/s |
| **Air velocity in occupied zone** | **0.1 – 0.3 m/s** | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Shipboard Note:** On **SOLAS-classed vessels**, HVAC duct penetrations through fire-rated bulkheads **must** be fitted with **fire dampers (FCO)** actuated at **74 °C**. This is a recurring question in KTU ship-building examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A shipboard Single Zone HVAC system comprises the following functional blocks, traversed by the supply air in the order shown:

1. **Outside Air (Fresh Air) Intake Louver** — Equipped with a **storm shutter / weather cap** to prevent ingress of sea spray and rain. A **salt filter / mist eliminator** is fitted on board ships to arrest salt-laden moisture that would otherwise corrode the cooling coil fins.

2. **Pre-Filter & Secondary Filter** — G4 grade (coarse) and F7/F9 grade (fine) filters as per **EN 779 / ISO 16890**. On naval vessels, **HEPA H13 filters** are mandated for medical/clean compartments.

3. **Mixing Box / Mixing Plenum** — Where the **return air (RA)** from the conditioned space meets the **outside air (OA)**. The mixture is called **Mixed Air (MA)**.

$$MA = OA + RA$$

4. **Cooling Coil (Chilled Water or Direct Expansion)** — Removes both **sensible heat** (temperature) and **latent heat** (moisture) from the air. Below the coil, a **drain pan** collects condensate, sloped toward a **scupper** discharging overboard or to a **sanitary tank**.

5. **Heating Coil (Hot Water or Electric)** — Engaged in **winter mode** for cold-sea operations (e.g., North Atlantic, Baltic routes). On ships crossing polar waters under the **Polar Code**, redundant heating becomes mandatory.

6. **Humidifier** — Steam manifold or water-spray type for winter humidification when the cold outside air is excessively dry after heating.

7. **Supply Fan / Centrifugal Blower** — Backward-curved, double-inlet type, sized for the calculated **CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute)** with a duct static pressure reserve of **10–15 %**.

8. **Ductwork + Diffusers** — Galvanized steel (G.I.) or aluminium on commercial ships; **stainless steel 316L** in galley/medical zones.

9. **Return Air Grille + Return Duct** — Returns air to the AHU mixing box.

10. **Thermostat / Humidistat (Automatic Controller)** — The single zone signature component — **one controller** governs the entire air stream.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — "The Why & How"

- **Why one zone?** → When a single compartment has **uniform heat generation pattern** (e.g., a server room with evenly distributed racks) and **no exposure to solar/weather variation across sub-areas**, splitting it into multiple zones adds cost without benefit.
- **How is temperature maintained?** → The thermostat modulates either the **chilled water valve** (modulating) or the **DX compressor capacity** (step control / VFD inverter) to maintain the set point.
- **How is humidity controlled?** → The cooling coil is selected for a **Leaving Air Temperature (LAT)** that inherently dehumidifies. In winter, the humidifier adds moisture to the post-heating air.
- **Why on ships?** → Reduces **compartmentalization cost**, simplifies **fire-fighting boundary management**, and the **compact AHU** fits within the constraints of the ship's structural scantlings.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important revision block for KTU ESE questions on Single Zone Systems. Memorize the units — examiners deduct marks for missing units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Room Sensible Heat (RSH)** | $RSH = 1.2 \times CFM \times \Delta T$ | W (or kW) | $\Delta T = T_{room} - T_{supply}$ |
| 2 | **Room Latent Heat (RLH)** | $RLH = 0.68 \times CFM \times \Delta W$ | W (or kW) | $\Delta W = W_{room} - W_{supply}$ in gr/kg |
| 3 | **Room Total Heat (RTH)** | $RTH = RSH + RLH$ | W | Total cooling load |
| 4 | **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** | $SHF = \dfrac{RSH}{RTH}$ | dimensionless | Used for coil selection |
| 5 | **Supply Air Quantity (CFM)** | $CFM = \dfrac{RSH}{1.2 \times \Delta T}$ | m³/min | Engineered for $\Delta T = 8$–$10$ K |
| 6 | **CFM to m³/s conversion** | $1\,CFM = 0.000472\,m^3/s$ | — | $\approx 1.7\,m^3/h$ |
| 7 | **Mass flow of dry air ($\dot{m}$)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{h_{in} - h_{out}}$ | kg/s | Where $h$ = enthalpy in kJ/kg |
| 8 | **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** | From psychrometric chart using coil bypass factor | °C | Key coil selection parameter |
| 9 | **Bypass Factor (BF)** | $BF = \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{ADP}}{T_{in} - T_{ADP}}$ | dimensionless | Typical coil BF = **0.10 – 0.30** |
| 10 | **Effective Temperature** | Combines T, RH, air motion into a single index | — | Used for comfort verification |
| 11 | **Fresh Air Load (Ventilation)** | $Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{OA} \times (h_{OA} - h_{room})$ | W | Depends on route ambient |
| 12 | **Duct Velocity (Recommended)** | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Main ducts: **6–10 m/s**, Branch: **4–6 m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When the problem states "1 TR = 3.517 kW", always write the cooling load in **kW** first, then convert. The reverse causes **dimensional inconsistency** and full-mark deduction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Commercial Shipping (Tanker, Bulker, Container):** Single Zone AHUs are deployed for the **Bridge, ECR, Hospital, and each cabin block individually**. The Bridge AHU is often called the **"Critical Zone Unit"** with redundant twin fan arrangement.
- **Naval / Warship Application:** The **CIC (Combat Information Centre)** and **Operation Room** are individual single zones, each fed by dedicated chillers to meet **shock-grade (MIL-S-901)** vibration standards.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Single Zone units are used for **Driller's Cabin, Mud Logger Room, and Hospital** because of strict contamination and noise isolation requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Cooling Load Equation from First Principles

Consider a conditioned space of volume $V$ supplied with air at mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s) at temperature $T_s$ (°C). The return air leaves at $T_r$ (°C). The space loses heat to walls and gains heat from internal sources, reaching a **steady state**.

**Energy balance on the space:**

$$ \text{Heat gained by space} = \text{Heat removed by supply air} $$

$$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_r - T_s) $$

Where:
- $Q$ = room total sensible load in kW
- $c_p$ = specific heat of air = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** ≈ **1.2 kJ/m³·K** (volumetric form using $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³)

Substituting volumetric flow rate $Q_v$ (m³/s) in place of $\dot{m}/\rho$:

$$ Q = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot Q_v \cdot \Delta T = 1.2 \cdot Q_v \cdot \Delta T \;\; (\text{kW, with } Q_v \text{ in m}^3/\text{s}) $$

**Expressing in CFM** (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM, but the empirical HVAC constant absorbs this):

$$ Q = 1.2 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta T \;\; (\text{kW, with CFM in m}^3/\text{min}) $$

This is the **RSH (Room Sensible Heat)** equation. The factor **1.2** bundles together $\rho$, $c_p$, and the unit conversion.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A ship's Bridge (single zone) has the following design conditions:
> - $T_{room} = 26\,°C$, $RH_{room} = 50\%$
> - $T_{supply} = 16\,°C$, $RH_{supply} = 95\%$
> - Outside air: $T_{OA} = 34\,°C$, $RH_{OA} = 80\%$
> - Volume flow: $CFM = 200\,m^3/min$
> - Outside air fraction: **30 %** of supply
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Room Sensible Heat, (b) Room Latent Heat, (c) Total Cooling Load, (d) SHF, (e) Outside air ventilation load.

**Step 1 — Read psychrometric properties**

From standard psychrometric chart at the given states:

$$ h_{room} = 55.2\;kJ/kg, \quad h_{supply} = 39.3\;kJ/kg, \quad h_{OA} = 91.0\;kJ/kg $$

$$ W_{room} = 10.8\;g/kg, \quad W_{supply} = 11.4\;g/kg, \quad W_{OA} = 24.5\;g/kg $$

**Step 2 — Compute Room Sensible Heat (RSH)**

$$ RSH = 1.2 \times CFM \times (T_{room} - T_{supply}) $$

$$ RSH = 1.2 \times 200 \times (26 - 16) $$

$$ RSH = 1.2 \times 200 \times 10 $$

$$ RSH = 2400\;W = 2.4\;kW $$

**Step 3 — Compute Room Latent Heat (RLH)**

$$ RLH = 0.68 \times CFM \times (W_{room} - W_{supply}) $$

$$ RLH = 0.68 \times 200 \times (10.8 - 11.4) $$

$$ RLH = 0.68 \times 200 \times (-0.6) $$

$$ RLH = -81.6\;W $$

> The **negative sign** indicates the supply air carries **less** moisture than room air → latent cooling is occurring. (Magnitude 81.6 W is the latent heat extracted.)

**Step 4 — Total Room Heat (RTH)**

$$ RTH = RSH + \vert RLH \vert = 2.4 + 0.0816 = 2.4816\;kW $$

**Step 5 — Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**

$$ SHF = \dfrac{RSH}{RTH} = \dfrac{2.4}{2.4816} = 0.967 $$

> A high SHF (≈1) confirms the Bridge is **sensible-dominated** due to instrument heat — the cooling coil selection will be biased toward a **shallow coil** (low dehumidification).

**Step 6 — Outside Air Ventilation Load**

$$ \dot{m}_{OA} = 0.30 \times CFM = 60\;m^3/min = 1.0\;m^3/s $$

$$ Q_{vent} = 1.2 \times CFM_{OA} \times (h_{OA} - h_{room}) \times \dfrac{1}{v} $$

Using $v = 0.834\,m^3/kg$:

$$ \dot{m}_{OA} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.834} = 1.199\;kg/s $$

$$ Q_{vent} = 1.199 \times (91.0 - 55.2) = 1.199 \times 35.8 $$

$$ Q_{vent} = 42.92\;kW $$

> **Critical KTU Insight:** The **fresh air load (42.9 kW)** is **17× larger** than the room load (2.48 kW). This is the unique signature of a ship's Bridge: small volume, but extreme outside air exposure on tropical routes. The plant must be **gross-sized** to handle OA, not just the room load.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Variation of SHF with Application

| Shipboard Zone | Dominant Load | Typical SHF Range | Coil Selection |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bridge / ECR** | Sensible (instruments) | **0.95 – 1.00** | Shallow coil, BF ≈ 0.30 |
| **Accommodation Cabin** | Mixed | **0.70 – 0.85** | Standard coil, BF ≈ 0.20 |
| **Galley** | Latent (cooking steam) | **0.50 – 0.65** | Deep coil, BF ≈ 0.10 |
| **Hospital / OT** | Mixed (sterile) | **0.75 – 0.85** | Deep coil, high efficiency filter |
| **Gymnasium** | Latent (perspiration) | **0.40 – 0.60** | Deep coil with reheat |
| **Battery Room** | Sensible + fumes | **0.95 – 1.00** | Explosion-proof, 100% exhaust |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OA Louver with Storm Shutter] --> B[Pre-Filter G4]
    B --> C[Mixing Box]
    D[Return Air Grille] --> C
    C --> E[Secondary Filter F7]
    E --> F[Cooling Coil DX or Chilled Water]
    F --> G[Drain Pan with Scupper]
    F --> H[Heating Coil Hot Water]
    H --> I[Humidifier Steam Type]
    I --> J[Supply Fan Centrifugal]
    J --> K[Supply Ductwork GI Ducts]
    K --> L[Fire Damper FCO 74 deg C]
    L --> M[Diffuser into Zone]
    M --> N[Conditioned Single Zone]
    N --> D
    O[Thermostat in Zone] --> P[AHU Controller]
    P --> F
    P --> H
    P --> J
```

### 4.2 Functional Sub-System Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Outside_Air_Loop["Outside Air Circuit"]
        OA1[Sea Atmosphere] --> OA2[Salinity Reduction Filter]
        OA2 --> OA3[Louver with Rain Guard]
    end

    subgraph Mixed_Air_Loop["Mixed Air Plenum"]
        MA1[OA Stream] --> MX[Mixing Box]
        MA2[Return Air Stream] --> MX
        MX --> MA3[Filter Bank G4 and F7]
    end

    subgraph Thermal_Loop["Thermal Conditioning Train"]
        TH1[Cooling Coil with TXV] --> TH2[Condensate Drain]
        TH2 --> TH3[Heating Coil]
        TH3 --> TH4[Humidifier]
    end

    subgraph Distribution_Loop["Air Distribution"]
        AD1[Supply Fan] --> AD2[Main Supply Duct]
        AD2 --> AD3[Branch Duct with FCO]
        AD3 --> AD4[Ceiling Diffuser]
    end

    subgraph Control_Loop["Single Zone Control"]
        CT1[Wall Thermostat] --> CT2[PID Controller]
        CT2 --> CT3[Chilled Water Valve Modulating]
        CT2 --> CT4[Hot Water Valve]
        CT2 --> CT5[Fan VFD Speed Control]
    end

    Outside_Air_Loop --> Mixed_Air_Loop
    Mixed_Air_Loop --> Thermal_Loop
    Thermal_Loop --> Distribution_Loop
    Distribution_Loop --> Conditioned_Zone[Conditioned Single Zone]
    Conditioned_Zone --> Control_Loop
    Control_Loop --> Thermal_Loop
```

### 4.3 Process Flow Description

| Stage | Component | Function | KTU Key Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OA Louver | Intake of fresh air | **Ventilation** |
| 2 | Pre-Filter | Remove coarse salt/particles | **Air Purity** |
| 3 | Mixing Box | Recirculate return air for energy saving | **Energy Recovery** |
| 4 | Cooling Coil | Dehumidify and cool | **Sensible + Latent Removal** |
| 5 | Heating Coil | Winter heating | **Heating Mode** |
| 6 | Humidifier | Winter humidification | **Winter Mode** |
| 7 | Supply Fan | Pressurize the system | **Duct Static** |
| 8 | Fire Damper | Fire boundary compliance | **SOLAS Reg. II-2** |
| 9 | Diffuser | Air distribution in zone | **Air Motion** |
| 10 | Thermostat | Close the control loop | **Single Zone Control** |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a "Single Zone" air conditioning system as applied to ships. Give two examples of single zone compartments on board a vessel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Single Zone Air Conditioning System** is a centralized HVAC configuration in which a **single Air Handling Unit (AHU)** supplies conditioned air to **one defined thermal zone** (compartment) with uniform temperature, humidity, and air quality requirements. The system uses **one thermostat** to control the entire air stream, with no subdivision of flow to different sub-zones with varying load profiles.

**Shipboard Examples:**
1. **Navigation Bridge** — High instrument heat load, single crew station, dedicated AHU per SOLAS requirements.
2. **Engine Control Room (ECR)** — Continuous manning, heat from consoles, requires positive pressure and dedicated fire isolation.

> **Marking Note (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for AHU/thermostat clarification, 1 mark for two valid examples).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State the function of a fire damper in a ship's HVAC duct. At what temperature does it actuate as per SOLAS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function:** A **fire damper (FCO — Fire Closure Operated)** is a fusible-link-actuated louver installed at every HVAC duct penetration through a **fire-rated bulkhead or deck (A-class or B-class division)**. In the event of fire on one side, the fusible link melts, the damper closes automatically, and **prevents spread of smoke and fire** through the duct network to adjacent compartments.

**SOLAS Actuation Temperature:** **74 °C** (some class societies also allow 68 °C variants, but SOLAS Chapter II-2 specifies 74 °C as standard).

**Additional function (1 mark):** It also serves as a **smoke barrier** when integrated with the ship's fire detection system.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a single zone air conditioning system** used on board ships. List all major components in the order of airflow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's **Engine Control Room** is conditioned as a single zone. Given:
- $T_{room} = 27\,°C$, $RH_{room} = 55\%$
- $T_{supply} = 14\,°C$
- $W_{room} = 12.5\;g/kg$, $W_{supply} = 10.0\;g/kg$
- Required $\Delta T = 13\;K$

Calculate: **(i)** the supply air quantity in **CFM (m³/min)**, **(ii)** the **Room Sensible Heat (RSH)** in kW, **(iii)** the **Room Latent Heat (RLH)** in kW, and **(iv)** the **Total Room Heat (RTH)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
The AHU draws **Outside Air (OA)** through a louver and pre-filter, mixes it with **Return Air (RA)** in the mixing box, passes the mixture through secondary filters, then through a **cooling coil** (chilled water or DX) where it is dehumidified and cooled. In winter mode, the air then passes through a **heating coil** and **humidifier**. A **centrifugal supply fan** delivers the air through ductwork fitted with **fire dampers** into the single zone, where it enters through **diffusers**. The **return air grille** completes the loop. A **wall-mounted thermostat** modulates the chilled water valve to maintain setpoint.

**Component List in Airflow Order (3 marks):**
1. OA louver with storm shutter
2. Pre-filter (G4)
3. Mixing box
4. Secondary filter (F7)
5. Cooling coil + drain pan
6. Heating coil
7. Humidifier
8. Supply fan
9. Fire damper
10. Diffuser
11. Return air grille → back to mixing box

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Supply Air CFM:**

The room load must be balanced. Using empirical HVAC constant:

$$ CFM = \dfrac{RSH}{1.2 \times \Delta T} $$

From the design $\Delta T$:

$$ CFM = \dfrac{RSH}{1.2 \times 13} = \dfrac{RSH}{15.6} $$

We need the space heat load. Computing from conditions directly using the **mass balance approach**:

$$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{RTH}{h_{room} - h_{supply}} $$

Using the latent data provided:

$$ \Delta W = 12.5 - 10.0 = 2.5\;g/kg $$

Approximating: $h_{room} - h_{supply} \approx 1.005 \times \Delta T + 2.501 \times \Delta W / 1000$

$$ = 1.005 \times 13 + 2.501 \times 0.0025 = 13.065 + 0.00625 \approx 13.07\;kJ/kg $$

If RSH is to be determined first, assume the space load is such that **RSH = 15.6 × CFM** (rearranged). The problem expects students to use:

$$ CFM = \dfrac{\text{Air quantity from CFM formula directly}} $$

Re-reading the problem — $\Delta T$ given is **T_room − T_supply** = 13 K. So if the required air flow matches the design, and using standard HVAC sizing (RSH per typical ECR ≈ 8 kW):

$$ CFM = \dfrac{8 \times 60}{1.2 \times 13 \times 60} = \dfrac{48000}{936} \approx 51.3\;m^3/min $$

**Final (standard answer expected by KTU):**

$$ CFM = \dfrac{8\,kW}{1.2 \times 13\,K} = \dfrac{8}{15.6} = 0.513\,m^3/s = 30.77\,m^3/min $$

(Students should explicitly state RSH = 8 kW assumption OR derive RSH from the latent data if numerical closure is possible.)

**(ii) RSH:**

$$ RSH = 1.2 \times CFM \times \Delta T = 1.2 \times 30.77 \times 13 = 480.0\;W = 0.48\,kW $$

**(iii) RLH:**

$$ RLH = 0.68 \times CFM \times \Delta W = 0.68 \times 30.77 \times 2.5 = 52.31\;W = 0.052\;kW $$

**(iv) RTH:**

$$ RTH = 0.48 + 0.052 = 0.532\;kW $$

**SHF = 0.48/0.532 = 0.902** → Sensible-dominated (consistent with ECR instrument load).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Unit Conversion Error:** $0.68$ constant works when CFM is in **m³/min** and $W$ is in **g/kg**. If students pass $W$ in kg/kg without dividing by 1000, the latent heat becomes **0.052 W → 52 kW** → 1000× error → **0 marks**.
> - **Sign Convention:** Students often get confused with $T_{room} - T_{supply}$ versus $T_{supply} - T_{room}$. Always use **$T_{room} - T_{supply}$** for RSH (positive = cooling).
> - **Forgetting to convert kW → W:** $1.2$ constant expects kW output; mixing units in numerator causes unit mismatch.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a **Single Zone** and a **Multi Zone** air conditioning system. State **three advantages** of the single zone system specifically for shipboard applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's **Galley (kitchen)** is air-conditioned as a single zone. The cooling coil has:
- Entering air: $T_1 = 30\,°C$, $W_1 = 18.0\;g/kg$
- Coil **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** = $10\,°C$
- Coil **Bypass Factor (BF)** = $0.20$

Calculate: **(i)** the **Leaving Air Temperature (LAT)** and **(ii)** the **Leaving Air Humidity Ratio (W₂)** if the coil performs simultaneous cooling and dehumidification with SHF = 0.55. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Differentiation Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Zone | Multi Zone |
|---|---|---|
| Number of AHUs/thermostats | One per system | Multiple per system |
| Cold deck/ hot deck | Single supply air stream | Cold + hot ducts with mixing boxes |
| Zoning capability | None | Each zone can have independent setpoint |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (more dampers, controls) |
| Duct complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Application on ships | Bridge, ECR, Hospital | Accommodation block, public spaces |

**Three Advantages for Ships (3 marks):**
1. **Simplicity of fire isolation** — A single duct trunk per zone simplifies fitting **FCOs at bulkhead penetrations**, easing SOLAS compliance.
2. **Compact and lightweight** — Critical for ship structural design; lower machinery volume and weight penalty.
3. **Easier maintenance with limited crew** — Single AHU per critical space reduces diagnostic complexity for ship's technical staff.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Leaving Air Temperature:**

$$ BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} $$

$$ 0.20 = \dfrac{T_2 - 10}{30 - 10} $$

$$ T_2 - 10 = 0.20 \times 20 = 4 $$

$$ T_2 = 14\,°C $$

**(ii) Leaving Air Humidity Ratio:**

For a cooling coil with simultaneous cooling and dehumidification, the SHF relationship across the coil:

$$ SHF_{coil} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{\dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_2)} $$

Using simplified empirical relation for coil:

$$ \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - ADP} = BF = 0.20 \quad \text{(already used)} $$

For humidity, the **bypass factor applies identically** to the dew-point depression:

$$ BF = \dfrac{W_2 - W_{ADP}}{W_1 - W_{ADP}} $$

At ADP = 10 °C (saturated): $W_{ADP} \approx h_{fg}^{-1} \times P_{sat}(10°C) \approx 7.63\;g/kg$

$$ 0.20 = \dfrac{W_2 - 7.63}{18.0 - 7.63} $$

$$ W_2 - 7.63 = 0.20 \times 10.37 = 2.074 $$

$$ W_2 = 9.70\;g/kg $$

**Verification using SHF (additional insight):**

$$ SHF = 0.55 = \dfrac{c_p (T_1 - T_2)}{c_p (T_1 - T_2) + h_{fg}(W_1 - W_2)} $$

$$ 0.55 = \dfrac{1.005 \times 16}{1.005 \times 16 + 2.501 \times (18.0 - W_2)} $$

$$ 0.55 \times \left[16.08 + 2.501(18 - W_2)\right] = 16.08 $$

$$ 8.844 + 1.376(18 - W_2) = 16.08 $$

$$ 1.376(18 - W_2) = 7.236 $$

$$ 18 - W_2 = 5.26 \Rightarrow W_2 = 12.74\;g/kg $$

> **Note for students:** The two methods (BF and SHF) will not give identical $W_2$ unless the **room SHF equals the coil SHF**. In this problem the SHF of 0.55 is the **room** SHF, and the BF controls the **coil process**. The BF-based $W_2 = 9.70\,g/kg$ is the correct coil leaving condition. The room process then further conditions air from 14 °C / 9.70 g/kg to room setpoint.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> - **Confusing ADP and Room Dew Point:** Many students treat $T_{ADP}$ as the room condition. **ADP is a coil property**, room conditions are determined by the **room sensible heat ratio line intersecting the saturation curve**.
> - **Forgetting BF unit-less check:** If $T_2$ comes out negative or above $T_1$, the BF was applied with wrong direction. $T_2$ must lie **between** $T_{ADP}$ and $T_1$.
> - **Shipboard Specific Mistake:** On ships, galley exhaust is often **independent of return air** (exhaust hood above cooking range). Students sometimes route galley return through AHU — this is **wrong** per maritime hygiene codes; galley return should be **exhausted, not recirculated**, to prevent grease and smoke re-entering the system.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Single Zone HVAC Systems (Module 4 — KTU 2024)**

- ✅ A **Single Zone System** uses **one AHU and one thermostat** serving **one compartment**.
- ✅ Primary shipboard zones: **Bridge, ECR, Hospital, Galley, Server Room, Gym**.
- ✅ **Standard comfort conditions:** 24–26 °C and 50 ± 10 % RH.
- ✅ **Key constants:** $c_p = 1.005\,kJ/kg·K$, $\rho = 1.2\,kg/m^3$, $h_{fg} = 2501\,kJ/kg$.
- ✅ **RSH formula:** $1.2 \times CFM \times \Delta T$ — remember the **1.2** is empirical and unit-sensitive.
- ✅ **RLH formula:** $0.68 \times CFM \times \Delta W$ — with $W$ in **g/kg** and CFM in **m³/min**.
- ✅ **SHF = RSH / RTH** — used to select cooling coil depth.
- ✅ **ADP and Bypass Factor (BF)** are coil-selection tools — BF typically 0.10–0.30.
- ✅ **SOLAS mandates fire dampers at 74 °C** in all duct penetrations of A/B-class divisions.
- ✅ **Galley return air MUST be exhausted, not recirculated** to prevent grease/odor carryover.
- ✅ **Single zone is preferred for shipboard use** due to fire isolation simplicity, lower weight, and easier maintenance.
- ✅ **Ventilation (Outside Air) load** often **dominates** total plant capacity on ships — never size the AHU on room load alone.
- ✅ **Acronyms to remember for KTU exams:** AHU, FCO, ADP, BF, SHF, RSH, RLH, RTH, ECR, CIC, SOLAS, EN 779, ISO 16890, HEPA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** If the exam question says *"explain the single zone system"* — **always draw the airflow path with at least 8 components in sequence**. Examiners award marks for *visual clarity of the schematic*, not just textual description. A neat labelled diagram = **2–3 free marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Zone Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiple Zone Systems

# Multiple Zone Systems in Ship Air Conditioning

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multiple Zone System** is a marine HVAC configuration in which a single central air-handling plant conditions supply air that is subsequently distributed to **two or more independently controlled zones** (e.g., cabins, wheelhouse, galley, engine control room) on a vessel, with each zone maintaining its **own thermostat-set temperature, humidity, and ventilation rate** despite having a common supply source. The defining characteristic is the ability of the system to deliver air at **differing temperatures to different zones simultaneously** from a shared central plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Phrasing):**
> "A multi-zone system is an air-conditioning arrangement in which a single air-handling unit serves multiple zones, each with independent thermostatic control, by varying the temperature (and sometimes volume) of the air supplied to individual zones through zone dampers, reheat coils, or terminal devices."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large restaurant kitchen with one giant oven**, but every chef needs a different dish cooked at a different temperature. The head chef (central plant) prepares a base supply, and then each station (zone) has its own **small booster, damper, or reheat coil** to fine-tune the temperature before it reaches the plate (room). That is exactly what a multi-zone system does aboard a ship — **one central chiller/ahu feeds many compartments**, and each compartment "tunes" its incoming air to match its own load (sun-facing cabins get more cooling, interior cabins get less, the wheelhouse needs fresh air, the galley needs exhaust-heavy ventilation, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ships are uniquely suited to multi-zone systems** because a vessel is a collection of compartments with **vastly differing heat loads** — engine rooms, galleys, refrigerated spaces, bridge, accommodation blocks — yet space, weight, and machinery cost demand a **centralized plant** rather than independent units per room.

### Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Zone**: A space or group of spaces whose temperature is controlled by a **single thermostat** (KTU/IEEE ASHRAE Standard 55 convention).
- **CFM per Zone** (Cubic Feet per Minute): $Q_z$ = volume flow rate supplied to zone $z$.
- **Design Temperature Difference (DTD)**: $\Delta T_{design} = T_{outdoor,design} - T_{indoor,design}$, typically **22 °C to 28 °C** for ship AC.
- **Supply Air Temperature (SAT)**: Usually **13 °C to 16 °C** at AHU outlet for comfort systems.
- **Zone Thermostat Range**: Typically **20 °C to 26 °C** setpoint (IMO/SOLAS recommended comfort band).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ships use multi-zone:** Centralized chilled water generation is **thermally and economically efficient** — one large chiller has a better **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** than ten small ones. Multi-zone distribution lets the ship exploit this efficiency while still giving each compartment individualized comfort.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

**Step 1 — Central Plant Generation**
The central AHU (Air Handling Unit) takes in recirculated + fresh air, filters it, cools it through a cooling coil (chilled water or DX), and supplies it at a **single nominal temperature** (e.g., 13 °C).

**Step 2 — Zone Branching**
The supply duct from the AHU **branches** downstream into multiple sub-ducts, one per zone. Each branch carries the same nominal cold air.

**Step 3 — Zone-Level Modulation**
Each zone has a **terminal device** that adjusts the air delivered to that specific zone. The methods of modulation are:

- **(a) Zone Damper (Single-Duct, blended-air variant):** Mixes cold supply air with warmer return/plenum air using motorised dampers.
- **(b) Reheat Coil:** Passes the cold air over a heating coil (hot water or electric) to raise its temperature for warmer zones.
- **(c) Variable Air Volume (VAV):** Modulates the *volume* of cold air delivered; temperature is constant but quantity varies with load.
- **(d) Induction Unit:** High-velocity primary air induces room air through a secondary coil, giving localized temperature control.

**Step 4 — Thermostatic Feedback**
Each zone thermostat compares the room temperature to the setpoint and sends a signal to the terminal device (damper actuator, reheat valve, VAV box) to **open/close or modulate** accordingly.

**Step 5 — Load Compensation**
Zones with high internal heat gains (galley, sun-deck cabin) get **more cooling** (more cold air or lower reheat). Zones with low gains (interior cabin) get **less cooling**. The central plant sees only the *aggregate* load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Principle:**
> A multi-zone system decouples the **central plant's fixed supply temperature** from the **per-zone load variability** by moving the final temperature adjustment to the **distribution end** (the zone terminal). This is the "Why" of multi-zone design.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_z = \dot{m}_z \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z})$ | Sensible heat gain of zone $z$ | kW |
| $Q_{total} = \sum_{z=1}^{n} Q_z$ | Total cooling load = sum of all zone loads | kW |
| $\dot{V}_{total} = \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{V}_z$ | Total supply air volume = sum of zone airflows | m³/s |
| $T_{supply} = T_{room,avg} - \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{m}_{total} \cdot c_p}$ | Central supply air temperature | °C |
| $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor}}$ | Plant efficiency benchmark | dimensionless |
| $RH = \dfrac{p_{vapor}}{p_{sat}(T)} \times 100$ | Relative humidity used for zone humidity spec | % |
| $\dot{V}_{fresh} = n_{occupants} \times V_{per\,person}$ | Fresh air per occupant (≈ **7.5 L/s per person**, IMO) | L/s |
| $\Delta h_{zone} = h_{room} - h_{supply}$ | Zone enthalpy difference (for total heat gain) | kJ/kg |
| $Q_{latent} = \dot{m} \cdot (W_{room} - W_{supply}) \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat from humidity difference | kW |
| $T_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c c_p T_c + \dot{m}_h c_p T_h}{\dot{m}_c c_p + \dot{m}_h c_p}$ | Mixed air temperature (cold + hot blend) | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for KTU Board Exams:** In your answer scripts, write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value — never use the bare pipe symbol inside a formula that crosses a line, as it can be misread as a bracket.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cruise ships** (e.g., Oasis-class, Queen Mary 2): 2,000+ cabins, each a "zone," fed from a few centralized chilled-water plants using **VAV boxes with reheat**.
- **Naval vessels**: Multi-zone systems allow classified zones (CIC, bridge) to have **redundant independent control** even on a shared plant.
- **Offshore platforms & yachts**: Compact multi-zone AHUs (sometimes single packaged MZ units) provide AC to multiple cabins with **zone-specific thermostats**.
- **Energy savings**: A correctly designed multi-zone system with VAV can save **20–30 % fan energy** compared to constant-volume systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Total Cooling Load of a Multi-Zone System

We begin with the fundamental sensible heat equation for a single zone, then sum across all zones.

**Step 1 — Single Zone Heat Balance**

For any zone $z$, the sensible heat that must be removed is:

$$
Q_{s,z} = \dot{m}_z \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z})
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_z$ = mass flow rate of air supplied to zone $z$ (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of air ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (or **1.02 kJ/kg·K** for moist air, KTU standard)
- $T_{room,z}$ = design room temperature of zone $z$ (°C)
- $T_{supply,z}$ = temperature of air entering zone $z$ (°C)

**Step 2 — Adding All Zones**

The total sensible cooling load is the sum across all $n$ zones:

$$
Q_{s,total} = \sum_{z=1}^{n} Q_{s,z} = \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{m}_z \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z})
$$

**Step 3 — Factoring Out Common $c_p$**

Since $c_p$ is essentially constant for air across typical comfort ranges:

$$
Q_{s,total} = c_p \cdot \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{m}_z \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z})
$$

**Step 4 — Converting Mass Flow to Volume Flow**

Using the ideal gas relation $\dot{m}_z = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_z$ where $\rho \approx$ **1.2 kg/m³** at standard marine conditions (45 °C, 50 % RH per IMO):

$$
Q_{s,total} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{V}_z \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z})
$$

**Step 5 — Total System Form**

The full sensible plus latent load becomes:

$$
Q_{total} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{V}_z \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply,z}) \; + \; \rho \cdot \sum_{z=1}^{n} \dot{V}_z \cdot (W_{room,z} - W_{supply,z}) \cdot h_{fg}
$$

where $h_{fg} \approx$ **2501 kJ/kg** (latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C, KTU value) and $W$ is humidity ratio (kg water/kg dry air).

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic behind this derivation:** Each zone is treated as an independent control volume with its own inlet (supply air) and outlet (room return). The central plant must satisfy the *sum* of all zone demands, but it does so at a single supply temperature — hence the importance of correctly sizing the **terminal device** to bridge the gap between the central supply temp and each zone's required room temperature.

---

### Derivation 2 — Zone Damper Mixing Temperature (Blended-Air Multi-Zone)

This is the most common KTU exam derivation for multi-zone systems.

**Step 1 — Setup**

Cold air from the central AHU at temperature $T_c$ mixes with warmer plenum/return air at $T_h$ via a zone damper. Let the mass flow of cold air be $\dot{m}_c$ and hot (bypass) air be $\dot{m}_h$.

**Step 2 — Energy Balance on Mixing Box**

The enthalpy of the mixture equals the sum of enthalpies of the streams:

$$
(\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h) \cdot c_p \cdot T_{mix} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot T_c + \dot{m}_h \cdot c_p \cdot T_h
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $c_p$**

$$
(\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h) \cdot T_{mix} = \dot{m}_c \cdot T_c + \dot{m}_h \cdot T_h
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_{mix}$**

$$
T_{mix} = \frac{\dot{m}_c \cdot T_c + \dot{m}_h \cdot T_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Mixing Fraction $f$**

Let $f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}$ (fraction of cold air). Then $\dot{m}_h = (1-f)(\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h)$, and:

$$
T_{mix} = f \cdot T_c + (1 - f) \cdot T_h
$$

This is the **key result** examiners love. Note that $T_{mix}$ is a **weighted average** of the two stream temperatures.

**Step 6 — Apply to Zone Thermostat Control**

The zone thermostat varies $f$ (i.e., opens/closes the cold-air damper) so that $T_{mix}$ equals the zone's required supply temperature for that load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The **bypass (hot) air** in a multi-zone blending box is *not* free — it has already been cooled (or partially cooled) by the central plant, so blending it back is **energetically inefficient** compared to reheat or VAV. This is why **VAV is preferred in modern ship AC** despite higher initial cost.

---

### Derivation 3 — VAV System Air Volume Calculation

**Step 1 — Constant Supply Temperature**

In VAV, $T_{supply}$ is fixed. The room temperature $T_{room,z}$ is also held (approximately) constant at setpoint. So:

$$
\Delta T_z = T_{room,z} - T_{supply} = \text{constant} = \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Zone Load Proportional to Volume Flow**

$$
Q_{s,z} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V}_z \cdot \Delta T
$$

Solving for $\dot{V}_z$:

$$
\dot{V}_z = \frac{Q_{s,z}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}
$$

**Step 3 — Total System Volume**

$$
\dot{V}_{total} = \frac{Q_{s,total}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}
$$

This shows the **fan power** (proportional to volume flow cubed via fan laws) is reduced when partial-load conditions lower the total flow — hence the **20–30 % energy savings** of VAV.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Multi-Zone System Types Compared

| System Type | How It Achieves Zone Control | Pros | Cons | Typical Ship Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Duct with Zone Reheat** | Heats cold supply air with reheat coil per zone | Simple, reliable, good humidity control | High reheat energy cost | Older passenger ships |
| **Dual-Duct (Hot & Cold Deck)** | Two ducts: cold + warm; mixing box per zone blends | Fast response, good control | Energy-inefficient, large ducts | Cruise ships (legacy) |
| **Multi-Zone (MZ) Unit** | Single AHU with internal hot/cold dampers per zone | Compact, no duct branches per zone | Limited zones, cross-leakage | Small vessels, yachts |
| **VAV (Variable Air Volume)** | Modulates airflow to each zone; constant SAT | Energy efficient, modern | Duct sealing critical, less stable humidity | Modern naval & offshore |
| **Induction System** | High-velocity primary air induces room air through coil | High induction ratio, good air circulation | Noisy, requires high-pressure duct | Older ocean liners |
| **Fan-Coil Unit (FCU) per Zone** | Independent coil + fan per zone fed by chilled water | Total zone independence | Multiple fans, maintenance | Modern cruise cabin AC |
| **Chilled Beam / Radiant Ceiling** | Chilled water circulates to ceiling panels | Very low noise, draft-free | Slow response, condensation risk | Luxury yachts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Multi-Zone System Architecture (Top-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Central Chilled Water Plant] --> B[Central Air Handling Unit AHU]
    B --> C[Cooling Coil 13 C Supply Air]
    C --> D[Supply Plenum]
    D --> D1[Zone 1 Branch Duct Cabin A]
    D --> D2[Zone 2 Branch Duct Cabin B]
    D --> D3[Zone 3 Branch Duct Wheelhouse]
    D --> D4[Zone 4 Branch Duct Galley]
    D1 --> E1[Zone Damper or VAV Box]
    D2 --> E2[Zone Damper or VAV Box]
    D3 --> E3[Zone Damper or VAV Box]
    D4 --> E4[Zone Damper or VAV Box]
    E1 --> F1[Zone Thermostat Cabin A]
    E2 --> F2[Zone Thermostat Cabin B]
    E3 --> F3[Zone Thermostat Wheelhouse]
    E4 --> F4[Zone Thermostat Galley]
    F1 --> E1
    F2 --> E2
    F3 --> E3
    F4 --> E4
    F1 --> G1[Zone Space Cabin A]
    F2 --> G2[Zone Space Cabin B]
    F3 --> G3[Zone Space Wheelhouse]
    F4 --> G4[Zone Space Galley]
    G1 --> H[Return Air Plenum]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> B
```

### Diagram 2 — Multi-Zone Damper Mixing Box Detail (Blended-Air Variant)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SA[Cold Supply Air 13 C from AHU] --> M1[Cold Air Damper Motorised]
    RA[Warm Return Air from Plenum] --> M2[Hot Bypass Damper Motorised]
    M1 --> MB[Mixing Box Chamber]
    M2 --> MB
    MB --> ZSUP[Mixed Air to Zone 24 C typical]
    TH[Zone Thermostat 22 C Setpoint] --> ACT[Damper Actuator Controller]
    ACT --> M1
    ACT --> M2
```

### Diagram 3 — Reheat-Type Multi-Zone System

```mermaid
graph TD
    AHU[Central AHU 13 C SAT] --> RD[Supply Duct Trunk]
    RD --> RH1[Zone 1 Reheat Coil Hot Water]
    RD --> RH2[Zone 2 Reheat Coil Hot Water]
    RD --> RH3[Zone 3 Reheat Coil Hot Water]
    RH1 --> R1[Zone 1 Room]
    RH2 --> R2[Zone 2 Room]
    RH3 --> R3[Zone 3 Room]
    R1 --> T1[Thermostat 1]
    R2 --> T2[Thermostat 2]
    R3 --> T3[Thermostat 3]
    T1 --> V1[Reheat Valve 1 Modulating]
    T2 --> V2[Reheat Valve 2 Modulating]
    T3 --> V3[Reheat Valve 3 Modulating]
    V1 --> RH1
    V2 --> RH2
    V3 --> RH3
    HW[Hot Water Supply from Boiler or Heat Recovery] --> V1
    HW --> V2
    HW --> V3
    R1 --> RET[Return Air]
    R2 --> RET
    R3 --> RET
    RET --> AHU
```

### Diagram 4 — VAV Multi-Zone System (Modern Ship AC)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CH[Chiller Plant] --> CW[Chilled Water Loop]
    CW --> AHU[AHU with Cooling Coil]
    AHU --> SD[Supply Duct Constant 13 C SAT]
    SD --> VAV1[VAV Box Zone 1 Cabin]
    SD --> VAV2[VAV Box Zone 2 Cabin]
    SD --> VAV3[VAV Box Zone 3 Wheelhouse]
    VAV1 --> Z1[Zone 1]
    VAV2 --> Z2[Zone 2]
    VAV3 --> Z3[Zone 3]
    Z1 --> TH1[Thermostat 1]
    Z2 --> TH2[Thermostat 2]
    Z3 --> TH3[Thermostat 3]
    TH1 --> VAV1
    TH2 --> VAV2
    TH3 --> VAV3
    Z1 --> RA[Return Air]
    Z2 --> RA
    Z3 --> RA
    RA --> MIX[Mixing Box with Fresh Air]
    MIX --> AHU
    FA[Outside Fresh Air] --> MIX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams instead of physical drawings:** Ship multi-zone systems are distributed across many decks — a single physical drawing is impractical. Block diagrams show the **functional relationships** (which thermostat controls which damper, where feedback loops close) that the examiner tests for.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define a multi-zone air-conditioning system. State any two advantages of using a multi-zone system on a passenger ship.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-zone air-conditioning system is one in which a **single central air-handling plant** supplies conditioned air to **two or more zones**, each fitted with its **own thermostat and terminal device** for independent temperature control.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Centralization of plant** reduces machinery space, weight, and capital cost compared to having one independent AC unit per cabin. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Better energy efficiency** at part load because the central chiller operates near its design COP, and zone-level VAV or thermostat control matches supply to demand. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between a single-duct reheat system and a dual-duct system for multi-zone air conditioning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Duct Reheat | Dual-Duct |
|---|---|---|
| Ducts | One supply duct, reheat coil per zone | Two ducts: one cold, one hot |
| Energy | Wastes cooling (then reheats) | Wastes both hot & cold streams |
| Control | Modulates reheat valve | Modulates mixing dampers |
| Duct space | Smaller | Larger (two parallel ducts) |
| Application | Common in mid-size ships | Used in large cruise ships |

*(2 marks for table + 1 mark for stating the key difference: dual-duct blends hot & cold in a terminal box; single-duct reheat uses heating coil.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE full question from each module-section pair.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**
**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working of a **dual-duct multi-zone air-conditioning system** as used on cruise ships. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A ship's accommodation has three zones with the following data:

| Zone | Room Temp (°C) | Sensible Heat Gain (kW) | Latent Heat Gain (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Cabin A) | 24 | 4.0 | 1.0 |
| 2 (Cabin B) | 22 | 3.0 | 0.5 |
| 3 (Wheelhouse) | 23 | 5.0 | 0.8 |

The supply air temperature is **14 °C** for all zones. Take $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³**, $c_p$ = **1.02 kJ/kg·K**, $h_{fg}$ = **2501 kJ/kg**. Calculate the supply air volume flow rate required for **each zone** and the **total**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

A dual-duct multi-zone system has **two parallel supply ducts** running from a central AHU:

- **Cold duct**: Air cooled by the central cooling coil to **~13 °C**.
- **Hot duct**: Air heated by a heating coil (using hot water from waste-heat recovery or boiler) to **~30–35 °C**.

Each zone has a **mixing box (terminal unit)** with two motorised dampers (one on cold duct, one on hot duct), controlled by the zone thermostat. The thermostat modulates the dampers to **blend hot and cold air** to deliver the exact required temperature to that zone. *(3 marks)*

**Block diagram** (to be drawn by student — key elements: central AHU, cold deck, hot deck, zone mixing boxes, thermostats, supply diffusers, return air): *(2 marks)*

**Working principle**: As zone load increases (e.g., sun hits a starboard cabin), the thermostat calls for more cooling → cold-damper opens more, hot-damper closes → cooler air enters. As load decreases → hot-damper opens, cold-damper closes. *(1 mark)*

**Suitability for ships**: A single central plant, distributed through two trunks, services many cabins — ideal for large passenger vessels. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat equation per zone:**

$$
\dot{V}_z = \frac{Q_{s,z}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{room,z} - T_{supply})}
$$

**Step 2 — Zone 1 (Cabin A):**

$$
\dot{V}_1 = \frac{4.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times (24 - 14)} = \frac{4.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times 10}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_1 = \frac{4.0}{12.24} = 0.3268 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \approx 0.327 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Zone 2 (Cabin B):**

$$
\dot{V}_2 = \frac{3.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times (22 - 14)} = \frac{3.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times 8}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_2 = \frac{3.0}{9.792} = 0.3064 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \approx 0.306 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Zone 3 (Wheelhouse):**

$$
\dot{V}_3 = \frac{5.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times (23 - 14)} = \frac{5.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times 9}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_3 = \frac{5.0}{11.016} = 0.4540 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \approx 0.454 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Volume Flow:**

$$
\dot{V}_{total} = \dot{V}_1 + \dot{V}_2 + \dot{V}_3 = 0.327 + 0.306 + 0.454
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{total} = 1.087 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check using total load:**

$Q_{s,total} = 4.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 12.0$ kW. Average $\Delta T = (10+8+9)/3 = 9$ °C.

$$
\dot{V}_{total} = \frac{12.0}{1.2 \times 1.02 \times 9} = \frac{12.0}{11.016} = 1.089 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

(Small discrepancy due to averaging — both answers are acceptable.) *(1 mark for unit consistency and final statement)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to use **moist air $c_p$ = 1.02 kJ/kg·K** (not 1.005) costs 1 mark.
> - Confusing $\Delta T$ direction (using $T_{supply} - T_{room}$ gives **negative** flow — instant fail).
> - Omitting units in the final answer (m³/s) loses 0.5 mark.
> - Not summing the zones for total flow loses 0.5 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**(a)** Explain the principle of **Variable Air Volume (VAV) multi-zone system** and list **three advantages** over a constant-air-volume reheat system for ship application. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A central AHU supplies air at **13 °C** to two zones. Zone 1 requires supply air at **17 °C** and Zone 2 requires supply air at **20 °C**. The cold-air temperature from the AHU is **13 °C**, and the hot bypass air available is at **28 °C**. Using the blending relation, calculate the **mass-flow fractions** $f$ of cold air required for each zone. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

In a VAV system, the **supply air temperature** is held constant by the central AHU, but the **volume of air** delivered to each zone is varied by a VAV terminal box (a motorized damper) controlled by the zone thermostat. *(2 marks)*

When the zone load drops (e.g., at night, or in a shaded cabin), the thermostat throttles the VAV damper, **reducing airflow**. When load rises, the damper opens, **increasing airflow**. The room temperature remains at setpoint because the air temperature is constant and the flow changes. *(2 marks)*

**Three advantages over reheat:**
1. **Energy saving** — fan power follows the cube of flow (fan affinity law: $P \propto \dot{V}^3$), so reducing flow at part load saves **20–30 % fan energy**. *(1 mark)*
2. **No reheat energy waste** — unlike reheat systems, VAV does not first cool and then reheat, avoiding double energy use. *(1 mark)*
3. **Better part-load efficiency** of central chiller (cooler return water, better COP). *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Use the mixing equation:**

$$
T_{mix} = f \cdot T_c + (1 - f) \cdot T_h
$$

Solving for $f$:

$$
f = \frac{T_h - T_{mix}}{T_h - T_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Zone 1 (Target $T_{mix} = 17$ °C):**

$$
f_1 = \frac{28 - 17}{28 - 13} = \frac{11}{15} = 0.7333
$$

So Zone 1 needs **73.3 % cold air** and **26.7 % hot bypass air**. *(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Zone 2 (Target $T_{mix} = 20$ °C):**

$$
f_2 = \frac{28 - 20}{28 - 13} = \frac{8}{15} = 0.5333
$$

So Zone 2 needs **53.3 % cold air** and **46.7 % hot bypass air**. *(3 marks)*

**Step 4 — Sanity check:** Zone 2 has a *higher* required $T_{mix}$ (warmer air), so it logically needs *less* cold air — confirmed ($f_2 < f_1$). *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Mixing up numerator and denominator (giving $f > 1$ or $f < 0$) — physical impossibility, full 0.
> - Not stating the percentage of cold air vs hot air — partial mark loss.
> - Failing to identify that the bypass air was *already cooled* by the central plant, hence the **energy penalty** of dual-duct/blending systems (1 conceptual mark).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Night:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Multi-zone = single central plant + multiple independently controlled zones.
- ✅ **Zone = one thermostat** (ASHRAE/IMO convention).
- ✅ **Types to memorize:** Single-duct reheat, Dual-duct (hot/cold deck), MZ unit, VAV, Induction, FCU.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $Q_{s,z} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V}_z \cdot \Delta T_z$ (per zone).
- ✅ **Total load** = sum of zone loads (linear superposition).
- ✅ **Mixing equation:** $T_{mix} = f \cdot T_c + (1-f) \cdot T_h$ — must memorize.
- ✅ **Solve for $f$:** $f = (T_h - T_{mix})/(T_h - T_c)$.
- ✅ **Constants:** $c_p$ = **1.02 kJ/kg·K** (moist air), $\rho$ = **1.2 kg/m³**, $h_{fg}$ = **2501 kJ/kg**.
- ✅ **VAV advantage:** Fan power $\propto \dot{V}^3$ → big energy savings.
- ✅ **Reheat disadvantage:** Wastes cooling energy already delivered.
- ✅ **Dual-duct disadvantage:** Both streams are conditioned; bypass hot air is energy penalty.
- ✅ **Ship-specific note:** Galley, wheelhouse, engine room have very different loads → multi-zone is **mandatory** on most vessels.
- ✅ **Fresh air per person:** ≈ **7.5 L/s** (IMO/SOLAS) — often a follow-up part-(c) question.
- ✅ **Typical SAT:** **13–16 °C**, room setpoint **22–24 °C**, design DTD **22–28 °C**.
- ✅ **Modern preference:** VAV + chilled-water FCUs in cabins (cruise ships).
- ✅ **Block diagram must show:** AHU → supply duct → branches → zone terminals → thermostats → feedback loop → return.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! Anchor your study around the *mixing equation* and the *per-zone load formula* — they appear in nearly every multi-zone problem.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminal Reheat System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Terminal Reheat System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 · Terminal Reheat System

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Terminal Reheat System** is a centralized all-air air-conditioning arrangement in which a single primary air-handling unit (AHU) cools and dehumidifies the supply air to a low dew-point condition, after which the air is distributed through a common duct to multiple zones, each of which is equipped with an individual **terminal reheat coil** that raises the air temperature to the local space requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST743 Module 4):**
> *"A terminal reheat system is one in which the air is cooled below the desired room temperature in a central plant to dehumidify it, and then each zone has a local reheat coil to warm the air to the desired temperature — providing simultaneous control of temperature and humidity in multi-zone applications typical aboard ships."*

The system is formally classified as a **centralized single-duct, constant-volume, all-air system with zone-level reheat**. It is widely specified in:
- Passenger ship cabins (variable solar/occupancy loads)
- Naval vessel sick bays and operating theatres
- Reefer ship container cooling systems where zone-specific humidity control is mandatory
- LNG carrier accommodation blocks

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dew-Point Bakery"

Imagine a large bakery that bakes three different cakes simultaneously: a chocolate cake (needs a cold oven), a sponge cake (needs a warm oven), and a soufflé (needs a very warm oven). Instead of buying three separate ovens, the bakery:
1. **Chills all dough to the same low temperature** in one giant walk-in cooler (this is the central cooling/dehumidification coil).
2. **Sends each chilled dough to its dedicated table**, where a small **personal heating lamp** warms it back up to the exact temperature needed (this is the terminal reheat coil).

The bakery doesn't waste energy reheating the *entire* walk-in cooler for every cake — it only reheats *exactly what each cake needs*. The **central unit handles the heavy lifting (dehumidification)**, and the **local units handle the fine-tuning (temperature)**. That is precisely how a terminal reheat system works aboard a ship, where cabins on the sun-side and shade-side, or galley vs. cabin, have very different loads but share a central chilled-water/air plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Terminal"?**
> The word **terminal** denotes that the reheat coil is located at the *end* (terminus) of the supply duct — right before the air enters the conditioned space — rather than at the central plant.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following standard engineering metrics are associated with the terminal reheat system and appear frequently in KTU examinations:

- **ADP (Apparatus Dew Point):** The temperature of the cooling coil surface at which moisture begins to condense out of the airstream. Typical design value: **10 °C – 13 °C** for shipboard comfort systems.
- **Bypass Factor (BF):** The fraction of air that escapes the cooling coil without contacting its surface. Typical value: **0.05 – 0.30**.
- **Reheat Factor (RHF):** The ratio of reheat coil load to the gross cooling coil load; a measure of energy waste.
- **SHF (Sensible Heat Factor):** Used to determine ADP and BF from room conditions.
- **CFM / kg/s:** Standard airflow units (cubic feet per minute or kg of dry air per second).

> [!TIP]
> Remember: in terminal reheat, the **central AHU handles latent load (dehumidification)** while the **terminal coils handle only sensible load (zone temperature trim)**. This division of duties is the *philosophical core* of the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A terminal reheat system operates in **three sequential thermodynamic stages** that form a continuous psychrometric cycle:

### Stage 1 — Mixed Air (Return + Outside)
- Warm, humid return air from conditioned zones mixes with required fraction of fresh outside air (typically **10–25 %** for shipboard systems, higher for galley/mess decks).
- State: **State 1 — Mixed air condition** at temperature $T_1$ and humidity ratio $W_1$.

### Stage 2 — Central Cooling & Dehumidification
- Mixed air passes over the central chilled-water or direct-expansion (DX) cooling coil.
- Coil surface temperature (ADP) is below the dew point of the air, causing moisture to condense.
- Air leaves the coil at **State 2 — Supply air condition** at temperature $T_2$ (low) and lower $W_2$ (drier).
- This stage removes **both sensible AND latent** heat from the air.

### Stage 3 — Terminal Reheat (Per Zone)
- The dehumidified, cool supply air is ducted to each zone.
- In each zone, a **terminal reheat coil** (hot water, steam, or electric) adds sensible heat.
- Air leaves the terminal coil at **State 3 — Room supply condition** at temperature $T_3$ (designed for that specific zone).
- Only **sensible heat** is added back; humidity ratio remains $W_2$.

### Stage 4 — Room Absorption
- Supply air absorbs room sensible and latent loads, reaching **State 4 — Room return condition** at $T_4$, $W_4$.
- This air returns to mix with outside air, closing the cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:**
> The fundamental thermodynamic insight is that the *rejected* sensible heat from the central cooling coil is *partially* compensated by the heat added at the terminal reheat coil. This means the chiller does **net less work** than in a single-zone system, but **wasteful reheat still occurs** because the energy quality is degraded (high-grade cooling rejected, low-grade heat added).

## 2.2 Components of a Typical Shipboard Terminal Reheat System

| # | Component | Function | Typical Shipboard Specification |
|---|-----------|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Mixing Box / Plenum | Mix return and outside air, with dampers | Galvanized steel, marine-grade |
| 2 | Pre-filter & Bag Filter | Remove salt spray, lint, soot | EU3 / EU7 grades (G4 / F7) |
| 3 | Central Cooling Coil | Cool and dehumidify mixed air | Chilled water 6 °C in / 12 °C out |
| 4 | Supply Fan | Distribute air to zones | Centrifugal, marine-class |
| 5 | Main Supply Duct | Carry air to zone terminals | Insulated steel / GI |
| 6 | Terminal Reheat Coil | Sensibly heat supply air per zone | Hot water 80 °C / Steam / Electric |
| 7 | Zone Damper (optional) | Balance airflow to zone | Manual or motorized |
| 8 | Zone Thermostat | Modulate reheat valve / coil | Pneumatic or electronic (DDC) |
| 9 | Humidity Sensor (central) | Monitor supply dew point | Capacitive polymer sensor |
| 10 | Chilled Water Pump & Chiller | Provide central cooling | Marine-grade centrifugal chiller |

## 2.3 Psychrometric Process Path — The Classic 4-State Cycle

The complete thermodynamic path on a psychrometric chart is:

$$\text{State 4 (Room)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{State 1 (Mixed)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{State 2 (After Central Coil)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{State 3 (After Terminal Reheat)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{State 4 (Room)}$$

The processes are:
- **4 → 1**: Adiabatic mixing of return + outside air (straight line on chart).
- **1 → 2**: Cooling + dehumidification (down and to the left, crossing saturation curve).
- **2 → 3**: Sensible reheat (horizontal line — constant $W$).
- **3 → 4**: Room sensible + latent heat gain (diagonal line up-right).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU high-yield** — they appear with high frequency in Module 4 university exam questions. Memorize them in **SI units** (kJ/kg, kg/s, °C, kPa).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3)$ | Sensible heat absorbed in room (zone load) | kW |
| 2 | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_4 - W_3)$ | Latent heat absorbed in room | kW |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_l$ | Total room heat gain | kW |
| 4 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Factor (room process slope) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{ADP} = T_1 - \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - BF}$ | Coil ADP from total load and bypass factor | °C |
| 6 | $\text{BF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | Bypass factor definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{coil,gross} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$ | Gross cooling coil capacity (before reheat) | kW |
| 8 | $Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$ | Terminal reheat coil capacity per zone | kW |
| 9 | $\text{RHF} = \dfrac{Q_{reheat}}{Q_{coil,gross}}$ | Reheat Factor (energy waste indicator) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3)} = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.005 \cdot \Delta T_s}$ | Required supply airflow rate | kg/s |
| 11 | $\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{1.2}$ | Volumetric flow (ρₐ ≈ 1.2 kg/m³ at sea level) | m³/s |
| 12 | $\text{COP}_{system} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling,net}}{W_{compressor} + W_{reheat}}$ | System-level COP (penalty for reheat) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $h = c_p T + W \cdot (2501 + 1.88 T)$ | Moist air specific enthalpy (approx.) | kJ/kg dry air |
| 14 | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{p_{atm} - p_v}$ | Humidity ratio from partial vapor pressure | kg water/kg dry air |

**Standard Constant Values (Bold for Emphasis):**
- $c_p$ (specific heat of dry air) = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C) = **2501 kJ/kg**
- $\rho_a$ (density of standard air) = **1.2 kg/m³**
- $0.622$ (ratio of molecular weights of water to air) = **0.622**

## 2.5 Why This System is Used in Ships — Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiners love this "real-world utility" angle. Memorize 2–3 reasons for shipboard application.**

1. **Variable solar gain across the hull:** A ship sailing through tropical waters has cabins on the sun-side receiving 5–8× more radiant heat than the shade-side cabins. Terminal reheat lets each cabin trim its temperature independently.
2. **Multi-zone humidity control:** Critical for reefer ships carrying sensitive cargo (pharmaceuticals, chocolates, films) where the *entire* cargo hold must be at a precise dew point, regardless of zone temperature.
3. **Crew comfort vs. machinery space:** The bridge, ECR (engine control room), galley, and cabins all have different SHF values. Central dehumidification + zone reheat elegantly serves them all.
4. **IMO/SOLAS compliance:** Centralized chilled water plants with terminal reheat enable single-point maintenance and satisfy classification society rules for fire-safe, vibration-resistant ducting.
5. **Energy recovery potential:** The reheat coil can be fed by **waste heat from jacket cooling water or exhaust gas economizers** — turning a thermodynamic loss into useful heating in the accommodation block.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bypass Factor from Room and Supply Conditions

The **Bypass Factor (BF)** is the single most important parameter in cooling-coil design. Its derivation follows directly from the linear nature of air cooling along a straight line on the psychrometric chart between the entering air and the apparatus dew point (ADP).

### Step 1 — Define the cooling coil as a black box

When air enters a cooling coil at $T_1$ and leaves at $T_2$, the air that *actually* contacts the coil surface is cooled to $T_{ADP}$, while the air that *bypasses* (slips past without contact) remains at $T_1$. The leaving air is a mixture:

$$T_2 = (1 - BF) \cdot T_{ADP} + BF \cdot T_1$$

**Explanation of step:** This is a simple mass-weighted temperature balance. If a fraction $BF$ of the air stays at $T_1$ and the remaining $(1 - BF)$ reaches the cold coil surface at $T_{ADP}$, the outlet temperature is the weighted average.

### Step 2 — Solve for BF

Rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= T_{ADP} - BF \cdot T_{ADP} + BF \cdot T_1 \\
T_2 - T_{ADP} &= BF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP}) \\
BF &= \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of step:** Standard algebraic isolation of BF. This is the KTU "must-memorize" form.

### Step 3 — Numerical Illustration

Suppose the entering mixed air is at $T_1 = 30$ °C, leaving supply air is at $T_2 = 14$ °C, and coil ADP is $T_{ADP} = 10$ °C. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
BF &= \dfrac{14 - 10}{30 - 10} \\
&= \dfrac{4}{20} \\
&= 0.20 \quad \text{(i.e., 20\% of air bypasses the coil)}
\end{aligned}$$

A BF of **0.20** is typical for a well-designed 4-row chilled-water coil in a shipboard AHU.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Air Mass Flow Rate for a Given Zone

Given a zone sensible heat load $Q_s$ and a design supply-to-room temperature difference $\Delta T_s = T_4 - T_3$, the required dry-air mass flow rate is:

### Step 1 — Start from sensible heat definition

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3)$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{m}_a$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \dfrac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_s} \\
&= \dfrac{Q_s}{1.005 \cdot \Delta T_s} \quad \text{(in kW, kg/s, °C)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example

A ship's officer cabin has:
- Sensible heat gain $Q_s = 4.5$ kW
- Design $\Delta T_s = T_4 - T_3 = 25 - 14 = 11$ °C

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \dfrac{4.5}{1.005 \cdot 11} \\
&= \dfrac{4.5}{11.055} \\
&= 0.407 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to volumetric flow:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_a &= \dfrac{0.407}{1.2} \\
&= 0.339 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 1220 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \quad (\approx 717 \text{ CFM})
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Gross Cooling Coil Capacity and Reheat Factor

### Step 1 — Gross cooling coil load

The central cooling coil must remove all the heat from $T_1$ down to $T_2$ (no zone reheat considered):

$$Q_{coil,gross} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

### Step 2 — Net useful cooling

After reheat, the room only sees the air entering at $T_3$ and leaving at $T_4$:

$$Q_{net,room} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3)$$

### Step 3 — Reheat coil capacity

The terminal reheat coil adds back the heat rejected by the central coil between $T_2$ and $T_3$:

$$Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$

### Step 4 — Reheat Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RHF} &= \dfrac{Q_{reheat}}{Q_{coil,gross}} \\
&= \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of step:** This non-dimensional ratio directly measures how much of the "expensive" central cooling is "wasted" on reheat. An RHF > 0.5 indicates a poorly designed system.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

Take: $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_2 = 14$ °C, $T_3 = 18$ °C:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RHF} &= \dfrac{18 - 14}{30 - 14} \\
&= \dfrac{4}{16} \\
&= 0.25 \quad \text{(25% of central cooling is wasted — acceptable)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Apparatus Dew Point from SHF and Bypass Factor

This is a critical KTU design problem — given room conditions and a known coil BF, find the required supply temperature.

### Step 1 — Sensible Heat Factor defines room process slope

$$\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$$

### Step 2 — On the psychrometric chart, draw the room process line from $T_3$ to $T_4$

The room process line slope on a $T$–$W$ chart is given by $\dfrac{c_p}{h_{fg} \cdot \text{SHF}}$. At ADP, $W = W_{ADP}$ (saturation).

### Step 3 — Find ADP temperature

The standard KTU closed-form result is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ADP} &= T_1 - \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{1 - BF}
\end{aligned}$$

But since $T_2$ is unknown, use the room process line. The bypass factor line from $(T_1, W_1)$ to $(T_2, W_2)$ extended meets the saturation curve at $T_{ADP}$. The numerical solution typically requires iterative chart work.

### Step 4 — Practical KTU-style formula (commonly used)

For exam problems, the relation between room SHR, BF, and ADP is given by the SHF-BF chart, but a useful analytical shortcut for *given* BF is:

$$T_{ADP} = T_4 - \dfrac{(T_4 - T_3)}{\text{SHF}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - BF} \cdot \text{SHF}$$

A simpler, exam-friendly derivation uses the equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{room} &= T_4 - T_3 \\
\Delta T_{coil} &= T_1 - T_2 = (1 - BF)(T_1 - T_{ADP}) \\
T_{ADP} &= T_1 - \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{1 - BF}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

If $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_2 = 14$ °C, BF = 0.2:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ADP} &= 30 - \dfrac{30 - 14}{1 - 0.2} \\
&= 30 - \dfrac{16}{0.8} \\
&= 30 - 20 \\
&= 10 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the assumed ADP from §3.1, confirming our design.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Common KTU Exam Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula Used |
|--------------|-------|------|------------------|
| Air quantity calculation | $Q_s$, $\Delta T_s$ | $\dot{m}_a$, $\dot{V}_a$ | $\dot{m}_a = Q_s / (c_p \cdot \Delta T_s)$ |
| BF determination | $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_{ADP}$ | BF | $BF = (T_2 - T_{ADP})/(T_1 - T_{ADP})$ |
| ADP from BF | $T_1$, $T_2$, BF | $T_{ADP}$ | $T_{ADP} = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)/(1-BF)$ |
| Reheat coil capacity | $\dot{m}_a$, $T_2$, $T_3$ | $Q_{reheat}$ | $Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$ |
| Reheat factor | $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$ | RHF | $RHF = (T_3 - T_2)/(T_1 - T_2)$ |
| Enthalpy at state | $T$, $W$ | $h$ | $h = c_p T + W(2501 + 1.88 T)$ |
| Humidity ratio | $p_v$, $p_{atm}$ | $W$ | $W = 0.622 \cdot p_v/(p_{atm} - p_v)$ |

## 4.2 Psychrometric Process Path on T–W Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the four-state psychrometric cycle of the terminal reheat process, annotated with the controlling equations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["State 4: Room Condition T4, W4"]
    P2["State 1: Mixed Air T1, W1"]
    P3["State 2: After Central Cooling Coil T2, W2"]
    P4["State 3: After Terminal Reheat Coil T3, W3"]
    P1 -- 4 to 1 Adiabatic Mixing --> P2
    P2 -- 1 to 2 Cooling and Dehumidification ADP reached --> P3
    P3 -- 2 to 3 Sensible Reheat constant W --> P4
    P4 -- 3 to 4 Room Sensible and Latent Gain --> P1
    P2 -.SHF line from T4 T3 extended to ADP.-> P5["ADP on Saturation Curve at T ADP"]
    P3 -.Bypass factor line from T1 through T2 to ADP.-> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** The colour convention is purely functional. Trace air flow from the *mixed air plenum* → *central cooling coil* → *supply fan* → *main duct* → *terminal reheat coil* (one per zone) → *zone diffuser* → *room* → *return grille* → *back to plenum*. Heat (hot water/steam) flows from the boiler plant to each terminal reheat coil in parallel, controlled by zone thermostats.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define a Terminal Reheat Air Conditioning System. List its two main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A terminal reheat system is a centralized all-air HVAC arrangement in which air is cooled and dehumidified in a central air-handling unit (AHU) to a low dew-point condition, and then distributed to multiple zones where each zone has a local reheat coil to raise the air temperature to the desired room condition. The two main components are:
1. **Central cooling coil** in the AHU — performs dehumidification.
2. **Terminal reheat coil** in each zone — performs sensible heating to zone requirement.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each component = 3 marks)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define "Reheat Factor". What is its typical acceptable range in a well-designed terminal reheat system?**

**Model Answer:**
The Reheat Factor (RHF) is the ratio of the heat added by the terminal reheat coil to the gross cooling coil capacity:

$$\text{RHF} = \dfrac{Q_{reheat}}{Q_{coil,gross}} = \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$

A well-designed shipboard system should have an RHF in the range of **0.10 to 0.30** (i.e., 10–30 % of the central cooling is wasted as reheat). Values above 0.50 indicate poor energy design.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark acceptable range = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 4, Model Paper]
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a terminal reheat air conditioning system as used in shipboard applications. Clearly identify the four psychrometric states and label the controlling components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A central AHU on a passenger ship handles **12,000 m³/h** of mixed air at **32 °C DBT, 60 % RH** (sea-level atmospheric pressure 101.325 kPa). The air leaves the central cooling coil at **14 °C saturated**. The supply air is then reheated in the cabin terminal coil to **20 °C** before being delivered to the cabin at **24 °C DBT**. Calculate:
   (i) The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) and the Bypass Factor (BF) of the central coil.
   (ii) The mass flow rate of dry air.
   (iii) The gross cooling coil capacity and the terminal reheat coil capacity.
   (iv) The Reheat Factor (RHF) and comment on its acceptability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B-A

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**

A terminal reheat system consists of a central air-handling unit (AHU) housing filters, a chilled-water cooling coil, and a supply fan, connected via insulated ducts to multiple terminal units. Each terminal unit serves a separate zone (cabin, galley, ECR, etc.) and contains its own reheat coil.

The four psychrometric states are:
- **State 4** (Room condition): $T_4 = 24$ °C, $W_4 = W_3 + \Delta W_{room}$
- **State 1** (Mixed air): $T_1 = 32$ °C, $W_1$ corresponding to 60 % RH at 32 °C
- **State 2** (After central coil): $T_2 = 14$ °C, $W_2 = W_s(14°C)$
- **State 3** (After terminal reheat): $T_3 = 20$ °C, $W_3 = W_2$

Working: Return air from zones mixes with outside air → cooled/dehumidified centrally → distributed to zones → each zone reheat coil adds sensible heat to match zone load → air enters room at $T_3$ → absorbs sensible + latent load → returns as $T_4$. *(7 marks: 2 for sketch description, 2 for naming states, 2 for explanation, 1 for shipboard relevance)*

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $\dot{V} = 12{,}000$ m³/h, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³

**(i) ADP and BF:**

The supply air leaves at $T_2 = 14$ °C saturated. Therefore the **ADP of the coil lies between $T_2$ and the coldest coil surface temperature**. In the absence of further coil data, we take the ADP = $T_2$ for a perfectly saturated leaving condition with negligible superheat, OR the standard KTU assumption when $T_2$ is given as "saturated" is that $T_{ADP} = T_2 = 14$ °C (this is the conservative design choice).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ADP} &= 14 \text{ °C} \\
BF &= \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \dfrac{14 - 14}{32 - 14} = \dfrac{0}{18} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

*Note: In real shipboard practice BF = 0.05–0.30; a BF of 0 indicates a theoretically infinite-row coil. For examination purposes, if BF is not specified separately, the leaving saturated condition implies BF = 0 and ADP = $T_2$.*

**(ii) Mass flow rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a &= \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a = \dfrac{12{,}000}{3600} \cdot 1.2 \\
&= 3.333 \cdot 1.2 \\
&= 4.0 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Cooling and reheat capacities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{coil,gross} &= \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) \\
&= 4.0 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (32 - 14) \\
&= 4.0 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 18 \\
&= 72.36 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{reheat} &= \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2) \\
&= 4.0 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (20 - 14) \\
&= 4.0 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 6 \\
&= 24.12 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Reheat Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RHF} &= \dfrac{T_3 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{6}{18} = 0.333
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** An RHF of **0.333 (33.3 %)** is on the higher side of acceptable; ideally it should be below **0.30**. This indicates that about one-third of the central chiller's work is being rejected at the terminal reheat coil — a moderate energy penalty. For improved efficiency, the designer should consider lowering $T_2$ slightly or raising $T_3$ for less-loaded zones (i.e., using Variable Air Volume — VAV — instead of constant volume).

*(7 marks: 2 for BF/ADP, 1 for mass flow, 2 for Q's, 1 for RHF, 1 for comment)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Module 4, Model Paper]
