**(a)** Compare the terminal reheat system with the Variable Air Volume (VAV) system for shipboard air conditioning. State **three advantages** and **three disadvantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigerated cargo ship has **six reefer compartments** to be served by a single central chilled-water plant. Each compartment requires a supply airflow of **0.8 kg/s** of dry air. The central cooling coil conditions the air from **28 °C, 22.0 g/kg** to a leaving condition of **12 °C saturated**. The terminal reheat coils are designed to deliver air at **17 °C** to each compartment. Calculate:
   (i) The total cooling coil capacity of the central plant.
   (ii) The total reheat capacity required across all six compartments.
   (iii) If the reheat is provided by **waste heat from the ship's jacket cooling water** at 85 °C flowing through a 4-row coil with overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 50$ W/m²·K, find the total required coil surface area. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B-B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Terminal Reheat | Variable Air Volume (VAV) |
|---------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Air volume | Constant to all zones | Variable, throttled per zone |
| Temperature control | Reheat coil modulates | Damper modulates airflow |
| Humidity control | Excellent (central only) | Moderate (overcooling risk) |
| Energy efficiency | Low (RHF penalty) | High (no reheat) |
| Initial cost | Moderate | High (DDC, VAV boxes) |
| Maintenance | Simple | Complex electronics |
| Duct sizing | Sized for max flow | Sized for diversity |

**Three Advantages of Terminal Reheat:**
1. Excellent individual zone temperature control with simultaneous humidity control.
2. Simpler control hardware — modulating reheat valves are robust and proven for marine service.
3. Single air volume simplifies duct design and fan selection; less prone to low-airflow duct condensation issues.

**Three Disadvantages of Terminal Reheat:**
1. High energy waste due to reheat factor (typical RHF 0.20–0.40).
2. Overcooling + reheating degrades exergy; COP of overall system drops.
3. Constant volume at low load means fan energy is not reduced — wasteful.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison content, 2 for diagrams/listings, 2 for marine context)*

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** 6 compartments, each $\dot{m}_a = 0.8$ kg/s, total $\dot{m}_{a,total} = 4.8$ kg/s

**(i) Total central cooling capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{coil,gross} &= \dot{m}_{a,total} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) \\
&= 4.8 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (28 - 12) \\
&= 4.8 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 16 \\
&= 77.184 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Total reheat capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{reheat,total} &= \dot{m}_{a,total} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2) \\
&= 4.8 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (17 - 12) \\
&= 4.8 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 5 \\
&= 24.12 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Reheat coil surface area using log-mean temperature difference:**

For each compartment, heat duty is $Q_{reheat,per} = 4.8 \text{ kW} / 6$... wait — total reheat is 24.12 kW across 6 compartments, so per compartment reheat is:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{reheat,per} &= \dfrac{24.12}{6} = 4.02 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

LMTD across hot water coil:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= 85 - 17 = 68 \text{ °C (water inlet vs. air outlet)} \\
\Delta T_2 &= 85 - 12 = 73 \text{ °C (water inlet vs. air inlet)} \\
\text{LMTD} &= \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{68 - 73}{\ln(68/73)} = \dfrac{-5}{\ln(0.9315)} = \dfrac{-5}{-0.0711} \approx 70.34 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — LMTD assumes counter-flow. The formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LMTD} &= \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
&= \dfrac{68 - 73}{\ln(68/73)} \\
&= \dfrac{-5}{-0.0711} \\
&\approx 70.3 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

(The sign convention: take absolute value of the difference and absolute value of log.)

Coil area (per compartment):

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} \\
&= \dfrac{4020}{50 \cdot 70.3} \\
&= \dfrac{4020}{3515} \\
&\approx 1.143 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per compartment}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total coil area for all 6 compartments:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= 6 \cdot 1.143 = 6.86 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including LMTD)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m³/h to m³/s** before multiplying by density. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing gross vs. net cooling coil capacity.** The *gross* includes the reheat penalty; the *net* is what the room actually absorbs.
> 3. **LMTD sign error:** The argument of $\ln$ must be positive. If $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$, swap them before computing LMTD, or take absolute values consistently.
> 4. **Mixing up BF and SHF.** BF applies to the *coil*; SHF applies to the *room process*. They are unrelated quantities.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract reheat from gross cooling** when calculating net chiller capacity. Many students report $Q_{coil,gross}$ as if it's all useful — it's not; the reheat coil recovers part of it.
> 6. **Not commenting on RHF acceptability.** Even a correct numerical answer without the engineering comment loses the final mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Terminal Reheat System (Module 4, PENST743):**

- ✅ A **terminal reheat system** = central cooling/dehumidification + per-zone reheat coil.
- ✅ Air is cooled to a **low dew point** centrally, then **sensibly heated** at each zone.
- ✅ The system is a **centralized, constant-volume, all-air, single-duct** arrangement.
- ✅ **Central coil handles latent + sensible load**; **terminal coil handles sensible trim only**.
- ✅ **Four psychrometric states**: Mixed → Cooled → Reheated → Room → Mixed.
- ✅ **Process 2→3 is horizontal on the psychrometric chart** (constant $W$).
- ✅ **Bypass Factor (BF)** ranges from **0.05 to 0.30** for typical 4–6 row coils.
- ✅ **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** = coil surface temperature where condensation begins (typically **10–13 °C** for comfort).
- ✅ **Reheat Factor (RHF)** = $(T_3 - T_2)/(T_1 - T_2)$; should ideally be **< 0.30**.
- ✅ Air mass flow formula: $\dot{m}_a = Q_s / (c_p \cdot \Delta T_s)$.
- ✅ Cooling coil capacity: $Q_{coil} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$ (gross, including reheat).
- ✅ Reheat coil capacity: $Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$.
- ✅ Net room cooling = $Q_{coil,gross} - Q_{reheat}$.
- ✅ Volumetric flow conversion: $\dot{V} = \dot{m}_a / 1.2$ (ρ_air = **1.2 kg/m³**).
- ✅ Standard constants: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $0.622$ for humidity ratio.
- ✅ **Shipboard advantages:** zone-independence, multi-load accommodation, SOLAS compatibility.
- ✅ **Shipboard disadvantage:** reheat energy penalty; can be mitigated by using **waste heat from jacket cooling water** for reheat.
- ✅ **Comparison anchors:** Terminal Reheat vs. VAV (energy) vs. Dual Duct (simplicity) vs. Multi-Zone (cost).
- ✅ Common exam traps: **LMTD sign**, **BF vs. SHF confusion**, **unit conversion** errors.
- ✅ Always **comment on RHF acceptability** in numerical answers — this is a KTU favourite for awarding the final mark.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam mnemonic — "COLD then WARM"**: The terminal reheat system **C**ools air centrally (dehumidification), distributes it, and **W**arms it locally (sensible reheat). *Central first, local second.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Duct System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dual Duct System

# Dual Duct System — Fundamentals & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dual Duct (DD) System** is a type of central air-conditioning distribution system in which **two parallel supply ducts** carry air at *different temperatures* from a central air handling unit (AHU) to each conditioned space. The two ducts are conventionally termed the **Cold Deck (CD)** and the **Hot Deck (HD)**. At each zone, a **terminal mixing box (induction unit or pressure-dependent box)** blends a variable proportion of cold and hot air to satisfy the precise room temperature set-point before discharging the mixed air into the space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:**
> *The Dual Duct System is classified as a high-induction, all-air central plant. KTU examiners frequently test its ability to provide **simultaneous heating and cooling** to multiple zones from a single central plant — a key advantage over single-duct systems.*

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Tap Wash Basin"

Imagine a **wash basin with two taps** — one supplying chilled water and the other supplying hot water. You control the temperature of the water at the basin outlet by adjusting how much you open each tap. Now imagine each cabin on a ship has its own "basin" and its own "two taps" coming from a central reservoir.

- The **Cold Deck** = the chilled water tap.
- The **Hot Deck** = the hot water tap.
- The **Mixing Box** = the basin where the two streams combine.
- The **Thermostat in each cabin** = the person turning the two tap handles to get the perfect temperature.

This is exactly how a Dual Duct System operates — **temperature is the controlled variable, achieved by mixing two air streams rather than re-conditioning air in each zone.**

## 1.3 Variants of the Dual Duct System

KTU recognises **two principal variants**:

| Variant | Description | Fan Arrangement |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Fan Dual Duct (SFDD)** | One supply fan pushes air through both decks; cold and hot air produced in the same AHU | **1 fan** |
| **Dual-Fan Dual Duct (DFDD)** | A dedicated fan for each deck, allowing energy recovery via return air mixing | **2 fans** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Dual-Fan Dual Duct (DFDD)** is the more energy-efficient variant and is **preferentially installed on modern naval and merchant vessels** where the return-air energy can be reused.

## 1.4 Core Physical Metrics Used in DD Design

The following standard metrics are used throughout the KTU design problems:

- **CFM (ft³/min)** or **m³/s** — volumetric flow rate of supply air
- **L/s per kW** of room cooling load — specific fan power reference
- **ΔT = 11 °C (approx.)** — typical cold deck design temperature difference
- **Hot deck supply temperature ≈ 32–40 °C** (reheat achieved centrally)
- **Cold deck supply temperature ≈ 13–15 °C** (post-coil)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

A Dual Duct System, in the KTU/Kerala Board context, is analysed in **five logical stages**:

1. **Central Air Handling (AHU):** Outside air (OA) is mixed with return air (RA), filtered, and then split into two parallel streams.
2. **Cooling Coil Path (Cold Deck):** One stream is sensibly cooled to the **cold deck supply temperature** ($T_{CD}$), typically **13–15 °C**.
3. **Heating Coil / Reheat Path (Hot Deck):** The other stream passes through a heating coil (or recovered return air) to reach the **hot deck supply temperature** ($T_{HD}$), typically **32–40 °C**.
4. **Parallel Distribution:** Both streams travel in insulated ducts to each zone.
5. **Zone Mixing Box:** A modulating damper pair controlled by the room thermostat blends the two streams. The mixed air at temperature $T_{mix}$ enters the room, and room temperature $T_{room}$ is held at the set-point.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind the Dual Duct

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use TWO ducts?**
> In a **single-duct system**, a single supply temperature is used for the entire ship. Zones that need heating (e.g., outer cabins in cold seas) and zones that need cooling (e.g., engine control rooms) cannot both be satisfied simultaneously. The Dual Duct System solves this by **delivering both hot and cold air centrally**, then blending locally — eliminating the need for expensive reheat coils at each terminal.

## 2.3 Governing Energy Balance at the Mixing Box

For any zone, the **mass flow conservation** and **energy conservation** laws apply at the mixing box:

$$
\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD} = \dot{m}_{mix}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{CD} \cdot c_p \cdot T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD} \cdot c_p \cdot T_{HD} = \dot{m}_{mix} \cdot c_p \cdot T_{mix}
$$

> Where $\dot{m}_{CD}$ and $\dot{m}_{HD}$ are the cold and hot duct mass flow rates respectively, and $T_{mix}$ is the resulting supply temperature to the conditioned space.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{V}_{supply} = \dot{m} / \rho$ | Volumetric flow rate from mass flow | $\dot{V}$ in m³/s, $\rho \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ for air |
| 2 | $T_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CD}\,T_{CD} + \dot{V}_{HD}\,T_{HD}}{\dot{V}_{CD} + \dot{V}_{HD}}$ | Mixing box outlet temperature (equal density) | Temperatures in °C or K |
| 3 | $Q_{zone} = \dot{m}_{supply} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{mix} - T_{room})$ | Sensible heat delivered to zone | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $Q_{cold\,coil} = \dot{m}_{CD} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{RA} - T_{CD})$ | Cooling coil load | W |
| 5 | $Q_{hot\,coil} = \dot{m}_{HD} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{HD} - T_{RA})$ | Heating coil / reheat load | W |
| 6 | $SFP = \dfrac{P_{fan}}{\dot{V}_{total}}$ | Specific Fan Power — KTU sustainability metric | W per (m³/s) |
| 7 | $COP_{plant} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor} + W_{fans}}$ | Overall plant coefficient of performance | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{DD} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{supply}}{T_{deck}}$ | Dual duct thermal efficiency relative to deck ambient | Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** When using absolute values inside markdown table cells, use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$) — never the bare vertical pipe `\vert`, as it will break the markdown table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Dual Duct System is the **backbone of high-precision marine HVAC** because:

- **Naval vessels** require individual temperature control in the **Operations Room, Bridge, Captain's Cabin, and Sonar Bay** — all served by one central plant.
- **LNG carriers** with hazardous-area zones use DD because the **mixing boxes can be explosion-proof (Ex-proof)** while the main AHU is in a safe area.
- **Passenger ships** use DD for cabins facing different solar gains (port vs. starboard) — the same plant serves both sides efficiently.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mixing Box Outlet Temperature (T_mix)

We begin from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the adiabatic mixing box.

**Step 1:** State the **mass conservation** at the mixing box.

$$
\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD} = \dot{m}_{mix}
$$

**Step 2:** State the **energy conservation** (no heat loss, no work done, steady state).

$$
\dot{m}_{CD}\,c_p\,T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}\,c_p\,T_{HD} = \dot{m}_{mix}\,c_p\,T_{mix}
$$

**Step 3:** The factor $c_p$ cancels on both sides (assuming $c_p$ is constant for air).

$$
\dot{m}_{CD}\,T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}\,T_{HD} = \dot{m}_{mix}\,T_{mix}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\dot{m}_{mix} = \dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}$ into Step 3.

$$
\dot{m}_{CD}\,T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}\,T_{HD} = (\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD})\,T_{mix}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve explicitly for $T_{mix}$ by dividing both sides by $(\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD})$.

$$
T_{mix} = \frac{\dot{m}_{CD}\,T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}\,T_{HD}}{\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}}
$$

**Step 6:** For ideal-gas air at near-equal densities and pressure, $\dot{m} \propto \dot{V}$, so the equation is interchangeable with volumetric flow.

$$
T_{mix} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CD}\,T_{CD} + \dot{V}_{HD}\,T_{HD}}{\dot{V}_{CD} + \dot{V}_{HD}}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The outlet temperature is the **flow-weighted arithmetic mean** of the two inlet temperatures. This is the central equation behind every KTU numerical on Dual Duct Systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Cold-Deck Fraction for a Given Zone

Suppose a zone has a desired supply temperature $T_{mix}^{*}$ (e.g., 18 °C) and a fixed total supply flow $\dot{V}_{total}$. The KTU often asks: *"What fraction of the supply must come from the cold deck?"*

**Step 1:** Let the cold-deck fraction be $x = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CD}}{\dot{V}_{total}}$, so $\dot{V}_{HD} = (1-x)\dot{V}_{total}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the mixing equation.

$$
T_{mix}^{*} = \frac{x \dot{V}_{total}\,T_{CD} + (1-x)\dot{V}_{total}\,T_{HD}}{\dot{V}_{total}}
$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $\dot{V}_{total}$ (it is a non-zero common factor).

$$
T_{mix}^{*} = x\,T_{CD} + (1-x)\,T_{HD}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand and collect terms in $x$.

$$
T_{mix}^{*} = x\,T_{CD} + T_{HD} - x\,T_{HD}
$$

$$
T_{mix}^{*} = T_{HD} + x\,(T_{CD} - T_{HD})
$$

**Step 5:** Isolate $x$.

$$
x = \frac{T_{mix}^{*} - T_{HD}}{T_{CD} - T_{HD}}
$$

> **Result:** $x$ is the **cold-deck mixing ratio** — a pure number between 0 and 1. KTU students must remember this formula because it is the gateway to computing fan power, duct sizing, and coil loads.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dual-Fan Energy Recovery Efficiency

In a **Dual-Fan Dual Duct (DFDD)** system, the hot deck is often heated using **return air from the cold zones**. The energy recovery ratio is:

**Step 1:** Define return air mass flow $\dot{m}_{RA}$ from cold zones, and supply-air mass flow to hot zones $\dot{m}_{HD}$.

**Step 2:** The energy recovered is the heat transferred from RA to HD supply.

$$
Q_{recovered} = \dot{m}_{RA}\,c_p\,(T_{RA,\,cold} - T_{mixed,\,RA})
$$

**Step 3:** The total hot-deck heating requirement without recovery is:

$$
Q_{HD,\,ideal} = \dot{m}_{HD}\,c_p\,(T_{HD} - T_{ambient})
$$

**Step 4:** The energy recovery efficiency is the ratio:

$$
\eta_{rec} = \frac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{HD,\,ideal}}
$$

**Step 5:** In a well-designed marine DFDD plant, $\eta_{rec}$ typically reaches **0.55 to 0.70** — a key reason KTU emphasises DFDD over SFDD for new builds.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:** A ship's accommodation block is served by a Dual Duct System. The cold deck supplies air at **14 °C** and the hot deck at **38 °C**. A particular cabin requires a supply air temperature of **22 °C** at a total flow of **0.4 m³/s**.

**Find:**
(a) The cold-deck flow rate.
(b) The hot-deck flow rate.
(c) The sensible cooling load delivered to the cabin if room temperature is **26 °C**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Cold-deck fraction $x$ using the formula derived in 3.2.

$$
x = \frac{22 - 38}{14 - 38} = \frac{-16}{-24} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

**Part (b):** Compute the actual flow rates.

$$
\dot{V}_{CD} = x \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.4 = 0.2667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{HD} = (1-x) \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.4 = 0.1333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (c):** Sensible cooling load using air mass flow (assume $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K).

$$
\dot{m}_{supply} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = 1.2 \times 0.4 = 0.48 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{zone} = \dot{m}_{supply} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{mix} - T_{room})
$$

$$
Q_{zone} = 0.48 \times 1005 \times (22 - 26) = 0.48 \times 1005 \times (-4)
$$

$$
Q_{zone} = -1929.6 \text{ W} \approx -1.93 \text{ kW}
$$

> The negative sign indicates **cooling delivered to the room** (heat removed from the space). Magnitude is **1.93 kW of cooling**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DD vs. Single Duct

| Parameter | Single Duct | Dual Duct (SFDD) | Dual Duct (DFDD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of supply ducts | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Number of fans | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| Energy recovery possible? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Initial cost | Low | High | Highest |
| Operating cost (energy) | Low | High | **Medium** |
| Individual zone control | Limited | Excellent | Excellent |
| Duct space required | Minimal | **High** | High |
| Marine suitability | Small vessels | Mid-size ships | **Large vessels / naval** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dual Duct System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full **functional flow** of a Dual Duct System, from outside air intake to zone discharge.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OA[Outside Air Intake] --> MIX[Mixing Plenum OA plus RA]
    RA[Return Air from Zones] --> MIX
    MIX --> FAN[Single or Dual Supply Fan]
    FAN --> CC[Cold Deck Cooling Coil]
    FAN --> HC[Hot Deck Heating Coil]
    CC --> DUCTCD[Cold Deck Duct at 13 to 15 C]
    HC --> DUCTHD[Hot Deck Duct at 32 to 40 C]
    DUCTCD --> MB1[Mixing Box Cabin 1]
    DUCTHD --> MB1
    DUCTCD --> MB2[Mixing Box Cabin 2]
    DUCTHD --> MB2
    DUCTCD --> MB3[Mixing Box Cabin 3]
    DUCTHD --> MB3
    MB1 --> T1[Cabin 1 Thermostat]
    MB2 --> T2[Cabin 2 Thermostat]
    MB3 --> T3[Cabin 3 Thermostat]
    T1 --> ROOM1[Cabin 1 at Setpoint]
    T2 --> ROOM2[Cabin 2 at Setpoint]
    T3 --> ROOM3[Cabin 3 at Setpoint]
    ROOM1 --> RA
    ROOM2 --> RA
    ROOM3 --> RA
```

## 4.2 Mixing Box Internal Schematic

The following diagram shows the **internal control logic** of a single mixing box, including the modulating damper pair and the thermostat feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CDIN[Cold Deck Air In at 14 C] --> DV1[Modulating Damper CD]
    HDIN[Hot Deck Air In at 38 C] --> DV2[Modulating Damper HD]
    DV1 --> MIXCHAMBER[Mixing Chamber]
    DV2 --> MIXCHAMBER
    MIXCHAMBER --> SUPPLY[Mixed Supply Air to Cabin]
    ROOMT[Room Temperature Sensor] --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    SETPT[Thermostat Setpoint] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DV1
    CTRL --> DV2
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow in a Dual-Fan Dual-Duct System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CENTRAL[Central Plant Room]
        CC[Cold Coil] --> CDFAN[Cold Deck Fan] --> CDDUCT
        HC[Hot Coil] --> HDFAN[Hot Deck Fan] --> HDDUCT
    end
    CDDUCT[CD Duct] --> ZONEMIX[Zone Mixing Boxes]
    HDDUCT[HD Duct] --> ZONEMIX
    ZONEMIX --> ROOMS[Crew Cabins and Spaces]
    ROOMS --> RA[Return Air Plenum]
    RA --> EATR[Energy Recovery Wheel or Plate]
    EATR --> HC
    RA --> CC
    EATR -.recovers heat.-> HC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed line in the diagram represents the **energy recovery loop** — return air from cold zones pre-heats the hot deck stream, which is the defining advantage of the DFDD variant over the SFDD variant.

## 4.4 Damper Modulation vs. Room Load — Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HIGH[High Cooling Load] --> X1[Cold Damper 100 percent, Hot Damper 0 percent]
    MED[Mixed Load] --> X2[Cold Damper 50 percent, Hot Damper 50 percent]
    LOW[High Heating Load] --> X3[Cold Damper 0 percent, Hot Damper 100 percent]
    X1 --> MIXOUT[Mixing Box Outlet]
    X2 --> MIXOUT
    X3 --> MIXOUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define a Dual Duct Air Conditioning System and state its key advantage over a single-duct system in marine applications."**

**Model Answer:**
A Dual Duct System is a central all-air conditioning system in which **two parallel ducts** — a cold deck and a hot deck — supply air at two different temperatures from a single air handling unit. At each conditioned zone, a **mixing box** blends the two streams in proportions dictated by a room thermostat to deliver air at the precise temperature required by that zone.

**Key advantage:** It provides **simultaneous heating and cooling to multiple zones from a single central plant**, eliminating the need for individual reheat coils in each cabin. This is critical on ships where outer cabins (cold) and inner cabins or galleys (warm) coexist and must be controlled independently from one chiller and one boiler plant.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Single-Fan Dual Duct and Dual-Fan Dual Duct systems with respect to fan arrangement and energy efficiency."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single-Fan Dual Duct | Dual-Fan Dual Duct |
|---|---|---|
| **Fan count** | One common supply fan | One fan for cold deck, one for hot deck |
| **Energy recovery** | Not possible — both streams share one fan | **Possible** — return air can pre-heat the hot deck stream |
| **Operating energy cost** | Higher (cold air is heated after cooling, wasting energy) | **Lower** due to recovery (typical 55–70% recovery ratio) |
| **Initial cost** | Lower | Higher (extra fan + recovery device) |
| **Marine use** | Smaller vessels, retrofit | **Modern naval ships and large passenger vessels** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) format requires an **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both options below are at the 14-mark standard with sub-parts (a) and (b) at 7 marks each.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**"A Dual Duct air conditioning system serves a ship's accommodation block. The cold deck supplies air at 13 °C and the hot deck at 40 °C. A particular cabin requires a supply air temperature of 21 °C at a total flow rate of 0.6 m³/s. The room temperature is 27 °C.**

**(a)** Determine the cold-deck and hot-deck volumetric flow rates required for this cabin. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the sensible cooling load delivered to the cabin and the cooling coil load at the AHU. Take air density as 1.2 kg/m³ and $c_p$ as 1005 J/kg·K. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

Step 1: Cold-deck fraction using the formula derived in Section 3.2.

$$
x = \frac{T_{mix} - T_{HD}}{T_{CD} - T_{HD}} = \frac{21 - 40}{13 - 40} = \frac{-19}{-27}
$$

$$
x = 0.7037
$$

Step 2: Hot-deck fraction.

$$
1 - x = 1 - 0.7037 = 0.2963
$$

Step 3: Compute individual duct flow rates.

$$
\dot{V}_{CD} = x \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = 0.7037 \times 0.6 = 0.4222 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{HD} = (1-x) \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = 0.2963 \times 0.6 = 0.1778 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

Step 1: Supply mass flow rate.

$$
\dot{m}_{supply} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{total} = 1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Step 2: Sensible cooling load delivered to the cabin.

$$
Q_{cabin} = \dot{m}_{supply} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{mix} - T_{room})
$$

$$
Q_{cabin} = 0.72 \times 1005 \times (21 - 27) = 0.72 \times 1005 \times (-6)
$$

$$
Q_{cabin} = -4341.6 \text{ W} \approx -4.34 \text{ kW (cooling)}
$$

Step 3: Cooling coil load at the AHU (cold deck only, from return-air temperature of 27 °C to cold-deck temperature of 13 °C).

$$
\dot{m}_{CD} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}_{CD} = 1.2 \times 0.4222 = 0.5067 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{coil} = \dot{m}_{CD} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{RA} - T_{CD})
$$

$$
Q_{coil} = 0.5067 \times 1005 \times (27 - 13) = 0.5067 \times 1005 \times 14
$$

$$
Q_{coil} = 7129.3 \text{ W} \approx 7.13 \text{ kW (cooling coil load)}
$$

> **Note for students:** The coil load (7.13 kW) is **larger** than the room cooling load (4.34 kW) because the coil must cool the air to a lower temperature (13 °C) than the final supply (21 °C), so that mixing with hot deck air raises it back up. This is the **fundamental thermodynamic cost** of the dual-duct approach.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Dual-Fan Dual Duct air conditioning system as used in modern ships. Highlight the role of the energy recovery device. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the Single-Fan Dual Duct and Dual-Fan Dual Duct systems with respect to: (i) energy consumption, (ii) capital cost, (iii) suitability for naval versus merchant vessels, and (iv) maintenance requirements. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block diagram and explanation:**

The system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Outside Air Intake & Filter Bank** — fresh air drawn in, filtered.
2. **Mixing Plenum** — outside air blended with return air.
3. **Two Cooling Coils in Series** — first pre-cools the mixed air, second fine-cools to cold-deck temperature.
4. **Cold-Deck Fan** — supplies cold deck duct at 13–15 °C.
5. **Hot-Deck Heating Coil** — reheats a portion of mixed air to 32–40 °C.
6. **Hot-Deck Fan** — supplies hot deck duct.
7. **Energy Recovery Wheel/Plate** — transfers heat from warm return air to the cold-side intake OR from hot return air to the hot-deck supply stream.
8. **Parallel Duct Network** — cold and hot ducts run side by side to each zone.
9. **Zone Mixing Boxes** — each with a thermostat, two modulating dampers, and a single discharge to the room.

**Role of the energy recovery device:** The energy recovery wheel (or cross-flow plate heat exchanger) transfers sensible (and sometimes latent) heat between the exhaust air stream and the incoming air stream. In a Dual-Fan Dual Duct system, this can pre-heat the hot deck supply using return air from overheated zones, **reducing boiler steam consumption by 55–70%**. The same wheel can also pre-cool the cold deck intake using return air from cold zones, reducing chiller load.

**Part (b) — Comparison:**

| Aspect | Single-Fan Dual Duct | Dual-Fan Dual Duct |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Energy consumption** | Higher — reheat wastes the cooling energy invested in the cold deck | **Lower** — energy recovery offsets 55–70% of reheat energy |
| **(ii) Capital cost** | **Lower** (one fan, no recovery device) | Higher (extra fan + recovery wheel + extra duct insulation) |
| **(iii) Naval vs. merchant suitability** | Suitable for small merchant vessels and retrofits | **Preferred for naval combatants and large passenger ships** where mission-critical comfort and energy budgets matter |
| **(iv) Maintenance** | **Simpler** — only one fan and one set of dampers per zone | More complex — two fans, recovery wheel, two control loops per zone |

> **Verdict (KTU-recommended):** The Dual-Fan Dual Duct is the **default choice for new naval builds** under the KTU 2024 marine HVAC curriculum because of its superior energy profile, despite higher upfront cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error on cooling load** — If the room is being cooled, $T_{mix} < T_{room}$, and the answer comes out **negative**. Students often drop the sign and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the cold-coil vs. room-load distinction** — The cooling coil load is ALWAYS larger than the room load in a DD system. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Using mass flow on one side and volume flow on the other** in the mixing equation — units will not cancel. KTU strictly uses **mass flows** (or **volumetric flows with equal density**).
> 4. **Confusing "cold-deck supply temperature" with "cold-coil leaving temperature"** — they are the same only if there is no duct heat gain; in exam problems assume they are equal unless told otherwise.
> 5. **Omitting the energy recovery explanation in Q.A(b)** — KTU awards at least 1.5 marks for explicitly mentioning the **55–70% recovery ratio** figure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Dual Duct System** uses **two parallel ducts** — cold deck and hot deck — to deliver air at two different temperatures to each zone, blended locally in a **mixing box**.
- ✅ The mixing equation is the central KTU formula: $T_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CD}T_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}T_{HD}}{\dot{m}_{CD} + \dot{m}_{HD}}$.
- ✅ The **cold-deck fraction** is computed as $x = \dfrac{T_{mix} - T_{HD}}{T_{CD} - T_{HD}}$ — a pure number between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Single-Fan Dual Duct (SFDD)** is cheaper but wastes reheat energy; **Dual-Fan Dual Duct (DFDD)** is energy-efficient due to **return-air energy recovery** (55–70% recovery).
- ✅ Typical deck temperatures: **Cold deck 13–15 °C**, **Hot deck 32–40 °C**, **Mixed supply 18–24 °C** depending on zone load.
- ✅ Standard air properties used: $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1005$ J/kg·K.
- ✅ The **cooling coil load is always greater than the room sensible load** in a DD system — this is the thermodynamic penalty of the reheat scheme.
- ✅ Marine applications: **passenger ship cabins, naval bridge & operations rooms, LNG carrier accommodation blocks**.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasis: examiners test the **mixing equation**, the **cold-deck fraction**, and the **energy recovery rationale** — master these three and you will score full marks on this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Air Volume Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variable Air Volume Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Air Volume (VAV) System** is a type of all-air HVAC system in which the supply air quantity (volume flow rate) delivered to each conditioned zone is varied in response to the changing thermal load of that zone, while maintaining the supply air temperature approximately constant. The load modulation is achieved through motorized dampers at terminal units called **VAV boxes**, coordinated by Direct Digital Control (DDC) logic.

In the context of **ship air conditioning**, VAV systems are adopted on passenger vessels, naval auxiliaries, and luxury yachts where multiple compartments (cabins, mess rooms, wheelhouse, galleys) exhibit widely varying and asynchronous cooling/heating demands due to differing occupancy, solar gain through hull-side glazing, equipment heat dissipation, and ventilation requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose with a Smart Nozzle**
> Imagine watering a garden. With a **fixed nozzle (CAV)**, you blast the same water jet regardless of whether the plant is thirsty or already soaked. The pump runs at full power all the time. With a **smart adjustable nozzle (VAV)**, the spray automatically widens or narrows to match the plant's actual thirst. The pump still works, but it does not waste energy forcing water the plant does not need.
> In a ship, a cabin with one sleeping occupant needs far less cooling air than a galley full of cooks. A VAV system "throttles" the air delivery to each room dynamically, like the smart nozzle, saving fan power and refrigeration energy.

### 1.3 Key Distinction — CAV vs. VAV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **CAV (Constant Air Volume):** Airflow is constant; room temperature is controlled by varying the **temperature** of the supplied air.
> - **VAV (Variable Air Volume):** Air temperature leaving the AHU is held nearly constant; room temperature is controlled by varying the **volume** of supplied air.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** = Sensible Load / Total Load, dimensionless, typically **0.7 to 1.0** for marine cabins.
- **Air density (sea-level marine ambient):** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.20 \; kg/m^3}$.
- **Specific heat of air:** $c_p \approx \mathbf{1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$.
- **Fan affinity laws (cube law exponent):** Power $\propto$ (Flow)$^3$.
- **Standard design supply temperature differential:** $\Delta T_s \approx \mathbf{8 \; K}$ to $\mathbf{10 \; K}$ for marine AHUs.

### 1.5 Why VAV is Used on Ships

- **Energy economy** when many zones run at part-load (very common at sea when ambient DB is moderate).
- **Better zoning** of cabins with individual thermostats.
- **Reduced duct sizes** at part-load conditions.
- **Lower fan power** consumption — significant since the auxiliary generator (or shaft generator) capacity is limited.
- **Comfort stability** through individual zone control.
- **Compatibility with DDC/BAS** used in modern integrated bridge and machinery automation systems (e.g., IEC 61162, NMEA 2000 networked HVAC controls).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Zone temperature sensing:** A wall-mounted thermostat in each cabin/zone transmits the sensed temperature $T_{zone}$ to the DDC controller.
2. **Signal comparison:** The DDC compares $T_{zone}$ with the setpoint $T_{sp}$ and generates an error signal $e = T_{sp} - T_{zone}$.
3. **Damper actuation:** A motorized damper at the VAV box modulates the open area $A_d$, varying the air mass flow rate $\dot{m}_z$ delivered to the zone.
4. **AHU supply temperature:** A reset schedule adjusts the AHU leaving-air temperature $T_{sup}$ so that the **deepest-loaded zone** still receives air at $\geq T_{sup,min}$ to satisfy ventilation and dehumidification.
5. **Static pressure reset:** A duct static-pressure sensor reduces fan speed (or inlet vane position) at part load, exploiting the **fan affinity law** to drastically cut fan power.
6. **Return-air integration:** Return air from all zones is mixed at the AHU, re-cooled/reheated, and re-supplied, completing the closed loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a minimum airflow must be maintained:**
> Even at part load, every zone requires a minimum outdoor air fraction to flush CO₂, smoke, and odors. Marine classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require **minimum 8 L/s per person** of outdoor air. Hence, VAV boxes include a **minimum-flow stop** that prevents full closure.

### 2.2 Components of a Typical Marine VAV System

- **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** with chilled-water coil and optional reheat coil.
- **Supply fan** (centrifugal, V-belt or direct-drive EC motor).
- **VAV terminal boxes** (single-duct, pressure-independent type preferred for marine).
- **DDC controller network** with BACnet/LonWorks or proprietary ship automation protocol.
- **Room thermostats / temperature sensors.**
- **Duct static-pressure sensor.**
- **Outdoor-air and return-air dampers** with economizer logic.
- **Sound attenuators** — mandatory because VAV boxes generate aerodynamic noise at low flow that can disturb crew sleep.

### 2.3 Types of VAV Systems (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Type | Configuration | Typical Use on Ships |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Duct VAV** | One supply duct per zone, VAV box modulates flow | Cabins, officer mess |
| **Dual-Duct VAV** | Hot deck + cold deck → mixing VAV box | Galleys, pantries with high load variation |
| **Fan-Powered VAV** | Series/parallel fan box for better room air circulation | Hospital wards, sick bays |
| **Induction VAV** | High-velocity primary air induces room secondary air | Engine control rooms |
| **Cooled-Beam / DOAS + VAV** | Dedicated outdoor air + radiant cooling | Luxury suites |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below lists every high-yield formula for this topic. **No vertical bar `|` symbol** is used inside cells; absolute-value notation uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer by air | W |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, \dot{V}$ | Mass flow from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_s}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | Required supply air volume | m³/s |
| 4 | $P_{fan} \propto \dot{V}^{\,3}$ | Fan affinity (cube) law | W |
| 5 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1}\right)^3$ | Fan power ratio at part load | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\Delta p_2}{\Delta p_1} = \left(\dfrac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1}\right)^2$ | Fan pressure ratio at part load | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{z,part} = \dot{V}_{z,full} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{s,part}}{\dot{Q}_{s,full}}$ | Linear VAV modulation rule | m³/s |
| 8 | $P_{saving} = P_{full} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{\dot{V}_{part}}{\dot{V}_{full}}\right)^3 \right]$ | Fan-power saving at part load | W |
| 9 | $t_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{OA} \, t_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA} \, t_{RA}}{\dot{V}_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA}}$ | Mixing-air temperature | °C |
| 10 | $w_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{OA} \, w_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA} \, w_{RA}}{\dot{V}_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA}}$ | Mixing-air humidity ratio | kg/kg |
| 11 | $t_{sup,min} = t_{room} - \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{s,z}}{\dot{m}_{z,min} \, c_p}$ | Minimum supply T to satisfy min vent. | °C |
| 12 | $COP_{saving} \approx 1 + 3 \left(\dfrac{\dot{V}_{part}}{\dot{V}_{full}}\right)^2$ | Effective system COP uplift heuristic | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy auditing** of marine HVAC plants: the **fan-cube law** alone delivers 25–40 % annual energy savings in cruise ships compared to CAV.
- **IMOTIER compliance:** IMO Resolution A.963(23) — energy-efficiency design index for new ships — rewards VAV adoption.
- **Noise control:** part-load box velocity drops, reducing aerodynamic noise in cabins — vital for passenger comfort and SOLAS life-safety announcements.
- **Humidity control:** combined with reheat, VAV allows **reheat-dehumidify** sequences essential in tropical waters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Required Supply Air Volume from Sensible Load

We begin with the fundamental sensible-heat equation of HVAC:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

Since $\dot{m} = \rho \, \dot{V}$, we substitute:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \rho \, \dot{V} \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

Solving for the volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}
$$

This is the most fundamental design equation used to size the AHU and ductwork.

**Numerical example (board-style):**

A marine cabin requires sensible cooling of $\dot{Q}_s = 3.0 \; kW$. Design conditions: $\rho = 1.20 \; kg/m^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, and $\Delta T = 10 \; K$.

Step 1: Substitute the values into the formula.

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{3.0 \; kW}{1.20 \; kg/m^3 \times 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K) \times 10 \; K}
$$

Step 2: Simplify the denominator.

$$
1.20 \times 1.005 \times 10 = 12.06 \; kJ/(m^3 \cdot K) \cdot K = 12.06 \; kJ/m^3
$$

Step 3: Convert kW to kJ/s. Since $1 \; kW = 1 \; kJ/s$, numerator remains $3.0$ kJ/s.

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{3.0}{12.06} = 0.2488 \; m^3/s
$$

Step 4: Convert to litres per second (often preferred on board).

$$
\dot{V} = 0.2488 \times 1000 = 248.8 \; L/s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 3 kW cabin load needs ~250 L/s of supply air when the AHU supplies 10 K colder than room temperature.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Fan-Power Saving Using the Affinity (Cube) Law

**Starting principle:** For a centrifugal fan running at constant speed, with throttling as the only control, fan power is approximately proportional to the cube of flow rate. With variable-speed (VFD) control, this relationship is even more exact.

Let $P_{full}$ and $\dot{V}_{full}$ represent full-load fan power and flow.

Let $P_{part}$ and $\dot{V}_{part}$ represent part-load conditions.

By the cube law:

$$
\frac{P_{part}}{P_{full}} = \left( \frac{\dot{V}_{part}}{\dot{V}_{full}} \right)^{3}
$$

Therefore the **fractional power saving** is:

$$
\frac{P_{saving}}{P_{full}} = 1 - \left( \frac{\dot{V}_{part}}{\dot{V}_{full}} \right)^{3}
$$

**Numerical example:**

A supply fan in full-load condition consumes $P_{full} = 7.5 \; kW$ at $\dot{V}_{full} = 1.0 \; m^3/s$. At part load, the VAV network reduces flow to $\dot{V}_{part} = 0.6 \; m^3/s$.

Step 1: Compute the cube ratio.

$$
\left( \frac{0.6}{1.0} \right)^{3} = 0.216
$$

Step 2: Compute the part-load power.

$$
P_{part} = 7.5 \times 0.216 = 1.62 \; kW
$$

Step 3: Compute the saving.

$$
P_{saving} = 7.5 - 1.62 = 5.88 \; kW
$$

Step 4: Convert to percentage.

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{5.88}{7.5} \times 100 = 78.4 \; \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for the board:** A 40 % reduction in airflow yields a **78 %** reduction in fan power. This cubic dependence is the primary reason VAV is mandated in modern marine HVAC codes (e.g., DNV-CG-0339, IEEE 45.8).

### 3.3 Derivation C — Minimum Supply Air to Satisfy Ventilation

Even at part load, each zone must receive a minimum outdoor-air quantity $\dot{V}_{OA,min}$ to flush contaminants. The DDC enforces this via a **minimum-position stop** on the VAV damper.

The minimum supply temperature $t_{sup,min}$ required to keep the zone at the design room temperature $t_{room}$ while delivering only $\dot{V}_{min}$ of air is:

$$
t_{sup,min} = t_{room} - \frac{\dot{Q}_{s,z}}{\dot{m}_{min} \, c_p}
$$

Where $\dot{m}_{min} = \rho \, \dot{V}_{min}$.

**Numerical example:**

Zone: $t_{room} = 24 \; °C$, $\dot{Q}_{s,z} = 1.0 \; kW$, $\dot{V}_{min} = 0.05 \; m^3/s$, $\rho = 1.20 \; kg/m^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.

Step 1: Compute $\dot{m}_{min}$.

$$
\dot{m}_{min} = 1.20 \times 0.05 = 0.06 \; kg/s
$$

Step 2: Substitute into the formula.

$$
t_{sup,min} = 24 - \frac{1.0}{0.06 \times 1.005}
$$

Step 3: Simplify denominator.

$$
0.06 \times 1.005 = 0.0603 \; kW/(K)
$$

Step 4: Final calculation.

$$
t_{sup,min} = 24 - 16.58 = 7.42 \; °C
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** If $t_{sup,min}$ falls below the **dew point** of zone air, condensation and cold drafts occur. The DDC must clamp $t_{sup,min}$ to $\geq$ dew point + 1 K.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — VAV vs. CAV Trade-off

| Performance Criterion | CAV System | VAV System |
|---|---|---|
| Fan power at part load | High (throttled) | **Very low (cubic drop)** |
| Humidity control | Excellent (constant reheat) | Moderate (needs reset logic) |
| Ventilation guarantee | Inherent (always $\dot{V}_{design}$) | Requires **min-flow stops** |
| Duct size | Larger | Smaller at part load |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher (+15 to +25 %) |
| Control complexity | Simple pneumatic | DDC, networked |
| Noise at part load | Lower | **Higher** at low-flow box |
| Marine suitability (crew cabins) | Adequate | **Preferred for variable occupancy** |
| Energy savings (annual) | Baseline | 25–40 % typical |

### 3.5 Derivation D — Mixed-Air Temperature at the AHU

A mixed-air plenum combines return air (RA) and outdoor air (OA). By energy balance:

$$
\dot{m}_{OA} \, c_p \, t_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA} \, c_p \, t_{RA} = (\dot{m}_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA}) \, c_p \, t_{mix}
$$

Since $c_p$ cancels and $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V}$ with constant $\rho$:

$$
t_{mix} = \frac{\dot{V}_{OA} \, t_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA} \, t_{RA}}{\dot{V}_{OA} + \dot{V}_{RA}}
$$

A similar equation holds for humidity ratio $w$.

**Worked example:**

$\dot{V}_{OA} = 0.20 \; m^3/s$ at $t_{OA} = 32 \; °C$; $\dot{V}_{RA} = 0.80 \; m^3/s$ at $t_{RA} = 24 \; °C$.

Step 1: Numerator.

$$
0.20 \times 32 + 0.80 \times 24 = 6.4 + 19.2 = 25.6
$$

Step 2: Denominator.

$$
0.20 + 0.80 = 1.00
$$

Step 3: Result.

$$
t_{mix} = \frac{25.6}{1.00} = 25.6 \; °C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 25.6 °C mixed-air condition is the *inlet* to the cooling coil. The coil must then bring it down to the supply temperature of approximately 14–16 °C.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture of a typical marine single-duct VAV system**. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the engine rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OA1[OUTDOOR AIR INTAKE] --> DMPR1[OUTDOOR AIR DAMPER]
    RA1[RETURN AIR PLENUM] --> DMPR2[RETURN AIR DAMPER]
    DMPR1 --> MIX1[MIXED AIR PLENUM]
    DMPR2 --> MIX1
    MIX1 --> COIL1[CHILLED WATER COIL]
    COIL1 --> FAN1[SUPPLY FAN VFD]
    FAN1 --> DUCT1[MAIN SUPPLY DUCT]
    DUCT1 --> STAT1[DUCT STATIC PRESSURE SENSOR]
    DUCT1 --> VAV1[VAV BOX CABIN A]
    DUCT1 --> VAV2[VAV BOX CABIN B]
    DUCT1 --> VAV3[VAV BOX GALLEY]
    VAV1 --> ZONE1[CABIN A ZONE]
    VAV2 --> ZONE2[CABIN B ZONE]
    VAV3 --> ZONE3[GALLEY ZONE]
    ZONE1 --> THER1[THERMOSTAT A]
    ZONE2 --> THER2[THERMOSTAT B]
    ZONE3 --> THER3[THERMOSTAT C]
    THER1 --> CTRL1[DDC CONTROLLER]
    THER2 --> CTRL1
    THER3 --> CTRL1
    STAT1 --> CTRL1
    CTRL1 -->|Modulate| VAV1
    CTRL1 -->|Modulate| VAV2
    CTRL1 -->|Modulate| VAV3
    CTRL1 -->|Speed reference| FAN1
    CTRL1 -->|Min position clamp| DMPR1
    RA1 --> CTRL1
```

### 4.2 VAV Box Internal Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INLET_A[INLET FROM MAIN DUCT] --> DMP_A[MOTORIZED DAMPER]
    DMP_A --> FLOW_A[AIRFLOW SENSOR]
    FLOW_A --> OUTLET_A[OUTLET TO ROOM DIFFUSER]
    SENSE_A[ROOM THERMOSTAT] --> COMP_A[PI CONTROLLER]
    SETPT_A[ZONE SETPOINT] --> COMP_A
    COMP_A -->|Actuator signal| DMP_A
    COMP_A -->|Min-flow stop| DMP_A
    FLOW_A -. Pressure-independent feedback .-> COMP_A
```

### 4.3 Control Loop Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LVL1[LEVEL 1 SHIP AUTOMATION IAS] --> LVL2[LEVEL 2 DDC AHU CONTROLLER]
    LVL2 --> LVL3[LEVEL 3 VAV TERMINAL CONTROLLERS]
    LVL3 --> LVL4[LEVEL 4 SENSORS AND ACTUATORS]
    LVL2 --> FANX[SUPPLY FAN VFD]
    LVL2 --> COILX[CHILLED WATER VALVE]
    LVL3 --> DMPX[ZONE DAMPERS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** On a modern ship, the Level 1 IAS (Integrated Automation System) typically conforms to IEC 61162-450 and exposes the HVAC subsystem as an **NMEA 2000 PGN 130816** field, allowing the chief engineer to monitor cabin temperatures from the ECR (Engine Control Room).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Variable Air Volume (VAV) system. How does it differ from a Constant Air Volume (CAV) system in terms of the controlled variable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Variable Air Volume (VAV) system is an all-air HVAC system in which the **quantity (volume flow rate)** of air supplied to each conditioned zone is varied in response to the changing thermal load of that zone, while the supply air temperature leaving the AHU is held approximately constant.
In a CAV system, the controlled variable is the **temperature of the supplied air** at a constant volume; in a VAV system, the controlled variable is the **volume** of supplied air at a (nearly) constant supply temperature.
*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — CAV control variable; 1 mark — VAV control variable.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the fan affinity (cube) law and explain its importance in VAV system design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fan affinity (cube) law states that the power consumed by a centrifugal fan is proportional to the **cube of the volumetric flow rate** delivered, i.e., $P \propto \dot{V}^{3}$.
Its importance: in a VAV system, when the airflow drops to (say) 50 % of design, the fan power drops to $0.5^{3} = 12.5$ % of full load, yielding an **87.5 %** power saving. This non-linear relationship is the fundamental energy-saving driver of VAV technology on board ships.
*(1 mark — statement; 1 mark — proportionality; 1 mark — design implication.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, Module 4 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice (a) or (b). Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, describe the working principle and main components of a single-duct Variable Air Volume (VAV) air-conditioning system used in a passenger ship. Discuss the role of the duct static-pressure sensor and the DDC controller. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A supply fan in a marine AHU delivers a full-load airflow of $\dot{V}_{full} = 2.0 \; m^3/s$ and consumes $P_{full} = 12.0 \; kW$. During cruising in temperate waters, the VAV network reduces the average airflow to $\dot{V}_{part} = 1.0 \; m^3/s$. Assuming the fan affinity cube law applies:
- (i) Compute the part-load fan power.
- (ii) Compute the fan-power saving in kW and as a percentage of full load.
- (iii) Comment on the implications for the ship's auxiliary generator sizing. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition & working (2 marks):** A single-duct VAV system delivers cooled air from a central AHU through a single main supply duct. At each zone, a VAV terminal box with a motorized damper modulates the airflow to match the local cooling load sensed by a thermostat. The DDC controller compares the zone temperature with the setpoint and drives the damper actuator.

2. **Main components (2 marks):** AHU with chilled-water coil, supply fan (often VFD-driven), main supply duct, VAV terminal boxes (one per zone), room thermostats, duct static-pressure sensor, return-air duct, mixed-air dampers, economizer, BAS/DDC network.

3. **Role of duct static-pressure sensor (1.5 marks):** It senses the duct pressure and signals the VFD to lower the fan speed (or close the inlet vane) whenever all zone dampers are throttling, which indicates system part-load. This maintains just enough pressure to feed the most-open damper.

4. **Role of DDC (1.5 marks):** The DDC executes PI control loops per zone, enforces minimum-flow stops for ventilation compliance, sequences the AHU chilled-water valve, and coordinates the static-pressure reset to optimize fan energy.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\dot{V}_{full} = 2.0 \; m^3/s$, $P_{full} = 12.0 \; kW$, $\dot{V}_{part} = 1.0 \; m^3/s$.

**(i) Part-load power (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{P_{part}}{P_{full}} = \left( \frac{\dot{V}_{part}}{\dot{V}_{full}} \right)^{3} = \left( \frac{1.0}{2.0} \right)^{3} = 0.125
$$

$$
P_{part} = 12.0 \times 0.125 = 1.5 \; kW
$$

**(ii) Saving (3 marks):**

$$
P_{saving} = P_{full} - P_{part} = 12.0 - 1.5 = 10.5 \; kW
$$

$$
\text{Saving \%} = \frac{10.5}{12.0} \times 100 = 87.5 \; \%
$$

**(iii) Implications for auxiliary generator (2 marks):**
A 10.5 kW reduction in fan load directly translates to lower fuel consumption in the auxiliary diesel generator sets (or lower shaft-generator load on the main engine). For a ship with multiple AHUs running 70 % of the time at part load, annual fuel savings of several tonnes are realistic, justifying the higher initial cost of VFD-equipped VAV systems and improving the ship's **EEDI / EEXI** rating under IMO regulations.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the term **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** and describe how the VAV system is sized using the room sensible heat, the supply $\Delta T$, and air density. Derive the governing equation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A marine officer's cabin has a sensible cooling load of $\dot{Q}_s = 2.5 \; kW$ and a latent load of $\dot{Q}_l = 0.4 \; kW$. The AHU is designed for a supply temperature differential of $\Delta T = 8 \; K$ with $\rho = 1.20 \; kg/m^3$ and $c_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.
- (i) Compute the SHF and the total cooling load.
- (ii) Compute the required supply airflow in $m^3/s$ and $L/s$.
- (iii) If a VAV box reduces the flow to 50 % at part load, compute the part-load fan power as a fraction of full-load. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of SHF (1 mark):** The Sensible Heat Factor is the ratio of the sensible component to the total (sensible + latent) cooling load:

$$
\text{SHF} = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_l}
$$

2. **Significance in VAV sizing (2 marks):** VAV systems are sized using the **sensible** load because the supply temperature is held constant and only the volume modulates. The latent load is handled by ensuring the supply air dew point is below the room dew point; thus SHF dictates coil selection and supply temperature strategy.

3. **Derivation of airflow equation (4 marks):**
Starting from:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T = \rho \, \dot{V} \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

Solving for $\dot{V}$:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T}
$$

This equation governs the supply duct and VAV box sizing. Standard marine design uses $\Delta T = 8$–$10 \; K$ to keep supply temperature above the dew point and prevent cold drafts.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\dot{Q}_s = 2.5 \; kW$, $\dot{Q}_l = 0.4 \; kW$, $\Delta T = 8 \; K$, $\rho = 1.20 \; kg/m^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$.

**(i) SHF and total load (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SHF} = \frac{2.5}{2.5 + 0.4} = \frac{2.5}{2.9} = 0.862
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 2.5 + 0.4 = 2.9 \; kW
$$

**(ii) Required airflow (3 marks):**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{2.5}{1.20 \times 1.005 \times 8}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
1.20 \times 1.005 \times 8 = 9.648 \; kJ/m^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{2.5}{9.648} = 0.2591 \; m^3/s = 259.1 \; L/s
$$

**(iii) Part-load fan power ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{P_{part}}{P_{full}} = (0.5)^{3} = 0.125 = 12.5 \; \%
$$

The fan consumes only 12.5 % of its full-load power at 50 % flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** use the *total* cooling load in the airflow equation — use only the **sensible** load.
> - **Always** include units explicitly. $c_p$ in $kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ and $\dot{Q}_s$ in $kW$ require the numerical values to be consistent.
> - **Do not** confuse fan affinity law (P $\propto$ N³ for a given fan) with the **system** fan-cube law (P $\propto \dot{V}^{3}$) used for VAV savings. State which one you are using.
> - **Always** mention the **minimum ventilation** constraint — examiners frequently deduct 1 mark if the answer ignores it.
> - **Numerical errors** in cube-law calculations (e.g., writing $0.5^{3} = 0.25$) are common — double-check.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Variable Air Volume Systems**

- **Core idea:** VAV modulates *volume*, not temperature. CAV modulates *temperature*, not volume.
- **Key components:** AHU + VFD supply fan + VAV boxes (with motorized dampers) + room thermostats + DDC + duct static-pressure sensor.
- **Cube law** is the energy-saving cornerstone: $P \propto \dot{V}^{3}$.
- **Standard formula:** $\dot{V} = \dot{Q}_s / (\rho \, c_p \, \Delta T)$. Use **sensible** load only.
- **Design supply $\Delta T$:** 8–10 K for marine AHUs.
- **Minimum outdoor air** must be guaranteed via VAV box min-position stops (DNV/Lloyd's requirement).
- **Humidity control** is more challenging in VAV — supply air must be cold enough to dehumidify at the deepest-loaded zone.
- **Reheat** may be needed at the box for zones with low sensible heat factor (high latent).
- **Static-pressure reset** is essential; without it, the fan does not save energy.
- **SHF** for typical marine cabins: **0.7 – 1.0**.
- **IMOTIER / EEDI compliance:** VAV systems are recognized energy-saving features under IMO Resolution A.963(23).
- **Sound attenuation** is mandatory — low-flow VAV boxes are noisy.
- **Control protocol** on modern ships: BACnet, LonWorks, or NMEA 2000 / IEC 61162-450 over the Integrated Automation System.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Never close a VAV damper fully — the minimum-flow stop is a **safety-critical** ventilation guarantee.
- **Numerical sanity check:** A 3 kW cabin needs ~250 L/s of supply air at $\Delta T = 10$ K.
- **Examiner's favourite 14-mark question pattern:** "Compute the airflow, then the part-load fan power saving" — practice this drill until it is automatic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of shipboard **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning**, **Water Systems** refer to the integrated network of piping, pumps, heat exchangers, expansion vessels, valves, and control devices that circulate **fresh water, chilled water, condenser cooling water, and sea water** for the purpose of:
1. Rejecting unwanted heat from the refrigerant cycle to the sea (heat sink).
2. Distributing cooled water to air-handling units (AHUs) and fan coil units (FCUs) for cabin ventilation.
3. Providing potable and domestic fresh water to the crew.
4. Managing condensate drainage and make-up water chemistry.

The water system forms the **secondary heat-transfer medium** between the refrigerant loop (primary) and the conditioned space / sea (tertiary).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *“Water systems on board ships include sea water cooling systems, central chilled water circulation systems, domestic fresh water systems, and condensate removal systems. They form the essential link between the refrigerant plant and the heat source/sink.”*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a household **air cooler (desert cooler)**:

- The **water pump** sucks water from the tank → pushes it over the cooling pads.
- The pads stay wet → air passing through them becomes cool and humid.
- The **drain pipe** carries away unused water (just like condensate).

Now scale this up to a **cruise ship**: instead of a small pump and pads, we have:
- A **sea water pump** that pulls ocean water through a hull valve.
- A **central chiller** that cools fresh water.
- A network of **insulated pipes** running through every cabin duct.
- A **sea chest and box cooler** to dump the heat back to the ocean.

The water is the *“blood”* of the system — it carries heat away from the cabins and rejects it to the sea, just like blood carries oxygen and removes CO₂ from your body.

### 1.3 Types of Water Systems on a Ship

| S.No | System | Function | Typical Fluid |
|------|--------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Sea Water Cooling System (SWCS) | Condenser heat rejection | Sea water |
| 2 | Central Cooling System (CCS) / Fresh Water Cooling (FWC) | Intermediate cooling loop | Treated fresh water |
| 3 | Chilled Water System (CHWS) | Air conditioning distribution | Chilled fresh water (~6–12 °C) |
| 4 | Condenser Water System | Direct condenser cooling | Sea or fresh water |
| 5 | Domestic Fresh Water System (DFWS) | Crew wash, drinking, galley | Potable fresh water |
| 6 | Hot Water / Calorifier System | Domestic heating | Heated fresh water |
| 7 | Condensate Drainage System | AHU/FCU condensate removal | Waste water |
| 8 | Make-up & Expansion Tank System | Volume compensation, de-aeration | Fresh water |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Standard Marine Values)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of sea water:** **ρ_sw ≈ 1025 kg/m³** (at 25 °C, 3.5 % salinity)  
> - **Specific heat of sea water:** **c_p,sw ≈ 3.99 kJ/(kg·K)**  
> - **Density of fresh water:** **ρ_fw ≈ 1000 kg/m³**  
> - **Specific heat of fresh water:** **c_p,fw = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**  
> - **Freezing point of sea water:** ≈ **−1.9 °C** at 3.5 % salinity  
> - **Typical chilled water supply temperature:** **6 °C – 8 °C**  
> - **Typical chilled water return temperature:** **12 °C – 14 °C** (ΔT = 6 K)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sea Water Cooling System (SWCS)

The sea water system is the **terminal heat sink** of any shipboard AC plant. Heat absorbed by the refrigerant in the evaporator is pumped by the compressor, rejected at the condenser, and finally transferred to the sea water.

**Components in flow order:**

1. **Sea chest** – large seawater inlet built into the hull (typically below waterline, with gratings).
2. **Sea inlet valve (sea cock)** – Bronze or monel-bodied, lever-operated, for emergency isolation.
3. **Mud box / Strainer** – Collects debris, fish, and floating matter.
4. **Sea water pump** – Centrifugal type, typically all-bronze or stainless steel.
5. **Box cooler / Heat exchanger** – Either direct (sea water through condenser tubes) or indirect (via plate heat exchanger with central fresh water loop).
6. **Discharge overboard** – Through an overboard discharge valve, at least above the load line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern shipboard practice** favours a **Central Cooling System (CCS)** where sea water cools an **intermediate fresh water loop** via a **plate heat exchanger (PHE)**. This protects the expensive condenser tubes from biofouling and corrosion.

### 2.2 The Chilled Water System (CHWS)

Used in **centralized air conditioning plants** for large passenger vessels. A **chilled water plant (chiller)** cools the water; the chilled water is pumped to AHUs/FCUs distributed throughout the accommodation block.

**Typical Chilled Water Plant Schematic – Logical Flow:**

1. **Chiller (evaporator)** → cools water from ~12 °C down to ~6 °C.
2. **Chilled water pump (CHWP)** → circulates water through piping.
3. **Strainer / Y-filter** → captures pipe debris.
4. **Air vent / Automatic air separator** → removes dissolved air.
5. **Expansion tank** → accommodates thermal expansion and provides system pressurization.
6. **Three-way modulating valve** (at AHU) → controls chilled water flow to maintain room temperature.
7. **AHU/FCU coil** → absorbs heat from the air (sensible + latent).
8. **Return pipe** → warm water (~12 °C) returns to chiller.
9. **Make-up water tank + chemical dosing** → maintains water quality (corrosion + biological inhibitors).

### 2.3 The Domestic Fresh Water System (DFWS)

Provides potable water for:
- Drinking, cooking
- Wash basins, showers
- Laundry
- Medical

**Generation methods on board:**
- **Fresh water generators (FWG)** – use waste heat from main engine jacket cooling or distillation (evaporator) of sea water.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO) plants** – in modern ships.

**Storage:** Hot well / hydrophore tanks pressurized by hydrophore pumps.

### 2.4 Condensate Drainage System

When warm cabin air passes over the chilled AHU coil, water vapor condenses on the coil fins. The water drips onto a **drain pan** and is removed through:
- **U-traps** (to prevent reverse suction of air).
- **Drain pipes** sloped ≥ 1:100.
- Discharge to **waste water tank** or **bilge** (never directly overboard in special areas).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer rate via water | $Q$ in kW, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Mass flow rate of water | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 4 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot \dot{V} \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pump hydraulic power | W |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | – |
| 6 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pa |
| 7 | $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{(\dot{m} \cdot c_p)_{min}}$ | Heat exchanger effectiveness parameter | – |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{NTU(1+R)}$ (parallel) | Effectiveness for co-current PHE | – |
| 9 | $t_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 t_1 + \dot{m}_2 t_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | Mixing temperature | °C |
| 10 | $\Delta V_{water} = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion (for expansion tank sizing) | m³ |

> *Where: $\beta \approx 0.000207 \text{ K}^{-1}$ for water between 4–100 °C.*

### 2.6 Engineering Utility & Significance

- **Heat rejection efficiency** determines the chiller’s COP and hence the ship’s auxiliary power consumption.
- **Pipe material selection** (Cu-Ni 90/10, Cu-Ni 70/30, SS-316, GRP) impacts biofouling, corrosion, and lifetime.
- **Air venting** is critical — entrapped air reduces heat transfer and causes cavitation in pumps.
- **Chemical treatment** prevents scaling, corrosion, and *Legionella* growth in chilled water systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Chilled Water Mass Flow Rate from Cooling Load

**Given:** A centralized AC plant serves a total cooling load of $Q = 500$ kW. Chilled water is supplied at $t_s = 6 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and returns at $t_r = 12 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the fundamental heat transfer equation**

The heat absorbed by chilled water equals the cooling load:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (t_r - t_s)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for mass flow rate $\dot{m}$**

Rearranging for $\dot{m}$:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot (t_r - t_s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

- $Q = 500 \text{ kW} = 500 \text{ kJ/s}$
- $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $\Delta T = 12 - 6 = 6 \text{ K}$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{500}{4.186 \times 6}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the calculation**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{500}{25.116} = 19.91 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}$**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{19.91}{1000} = 0.01991 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to litres per minute (lpm) for pump selection**

$$\dot{V}_{lpm} = 0.01991 \times 60\,000 = 1194.6 \text{ L/min} \approx 1200 \text{ L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 500 kW chiller requires approximately **20 kg/s ≈ 1200 L/min** of chilled water flow for a 6 K temperature difference.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sea Water Pump Head Requirement

**Given:** Sea water is drawn from a sea chest located 4 m below the condenser centreline. The flow rate is 25 L/s. Total pipe length = 30 m, equivalent length of fittings = 15 m, pipe diameter = 100 mm. Assume Darcy friction factor $f = 0.025$.

**Step 1 — Static head**

$$H_{static} = 4 \text{ m (lift)}$$

**Step 2 — Friction head loss (Darcy–Weisbach)**

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L_{tot}}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

First, compute velocity:

$$v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2} = \dfrac{0.025}{0.007854} = 3.183 \text{ m/s}$$

Total equivalent length:

$$L_{tot} = 30 + 15 = 45 \text{ m}$$

Compute friction loss in **Pa**:

$$\Delta P_f = 0.025 \cdot \dfrac{45}{0.1} \cdot \dfrac{1025 \times (3.183)^2}{2}$$

Intermediate step:

$$\Delta P_f = 0.025 \times 450 \times \dfrac{1025 \times 10.131}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 11.25 \times \dfrac{10384.3}{2} = 11.25 \times 5192.1 = 58411 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to head in metres of sea water**

$$H_f = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{58411}{1025 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{58411}{10055.25} = 5.81 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Total head**

$$H_{total} = H_{static} + H_f = 4 + 5.81 = 9.81 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Pump hydraulic power**

$$P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot \dot{V} \cdot H_{total}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

Assuming $\eta = 0.70$:

$$P_{pump} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 9.81}{0.70}$$

$$P_{pump} = \dfrac{2466.5}{0.70} = 3523.6 \text{ W} \approx 3.52 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The sea water pump must deliver approximately **9.8 m head at 25 L/s**, requiring a **motor of ≈ 3.5 kW** (after efficiency losses).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Expansion Tank Sizing for Chilled Water

**Given:** Total system water volume $V_0 = 2000$ L. Max operating temp $T_{max} = 40 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$, fill temp $T_0 = 15 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$. $\beta = 0.000207 \text{ K}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Temperature rise**

$$\Delta T = T_{max} - T_0 = 40 - 15 = 25 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Thermal expansion volume**

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T = 2000 \times 0.000207 \times 25 = 10.35 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Add safety margin (typically 1.5×)**

$$V_{exp,tank} \geq 1.5 \times \Delta V = 1.5 \times 10.35 = 15.5 \text{ L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A **minimum 16 L expansion tank** is required; in practice, a 25 L tank is selected.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Sea Water Piping

| Pipe Material | Service | Sea Water Corrosion | Biofouling | Cost | Typical Use |
|---------------|---------|--------------------|------------|------|-------------|
| Copper-Nickel 90/10 | Sea water lines | Excellent | Low | High | Main sea water lines |
| Copper-Nickel 70/30 | High-velocity SW | Excellent (better) | Low | Very High | Pump discharges |
| SS-316L | Chilled / fresh | Excellent | Low | High | Chilled water |
| Galvanised Iron | Domestic FW | Moderate | Medium | Low | Cold/hot domestic |
| GRP / FRP | Sea water | Excellent | Low | Moderate | Overboard / large dia |
| PVC / CPVC | Domestic | Excellent | Low | Low | Sanitary drains |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Central Cooling & Chilled Water System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interactions** of all major water systems on a modern ship (cruise / naval auxiliary), as the question domain (water systems) does not lend itself to a single pictorial schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seaChest[Sea Chest Hull Inlet] --> seaCock[Sea Cock Valve]
    seaCock --> strainer[Sea Water Strainer]
    strainer --> swPump[Sea Water Pump Centrifugal]
    swPump --> plateHX[Plate Heat Exchanger CCS]
    plateHX --> swDischarge[Overboard Discharge]

    plateHX --> fwLoop[Central Fresh Water Loop]
    fwLoop --> fwPump[Fresh Water Cooling Pump]
    fwPump --> condenser[Refrigerant Condenser]
    condenser --> fwLoop

    chiller[Chiller Evaporator] --> chwLoop[Chilled Water Loop 6 to 12 C]
    chwLoop --> chwPump[Chilled Water Pump]
    chwPump --> airVent[Automatic Air Vent Separator]
    airVent --> expTank[Expansion Tank Make Up]
    chwPump --> ahu1[AHU Accommodation Block 1]
    chwPump --> ahu2[AHU Accommodation Block 2]
    chwPump --> ahu3[FCU Cabin Loop]
    ahu1 --> drain[Condensate U Trap]
    ahu2 --> drain
    ahu3 --> drain
    drain --> wasteTank[Waste Water Collecting Tank]

    fwg[Fresh Water Generator Distiller] --> fwHydrophore[Hydrophore Tank]
    fwHydrophore --> domesticLoop[Domestic Fresh Water Loop]
    domesticLoop --> galley[Galley and Mess]
    domesticLoop --> cabins[Crew Cabins Wash Basins]
    domesticLoop --> calorifier[Calorifier Hot Water 60 C]
    calorifier --> domesticLoop
```

### 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Chilled Water Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Chiller Evaporator] --> B[Chilled Water Supply 6 C]
    B --> C[Strainer Y Filter]
    C --> D[Flow Switch]
    D --> E[Three Way Control Valve]
    E --> F[AHU Cooling Coil]
    F --> G[Condensate Drain Pan]
    G --> H[U Trap]
    H --> I[Waste Tank]
    F --> J[Return Water 12 C]
    J --> K[Air Separator Auto Vent]
    K --> L[Expansion Tank Pressurised]
    L --> M[Make Up Water Tank With Chemical Dosing]
    M --> A
    J --> A
```

### 4.3 Logical Flow — Sea Water Cooling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sc[Sea Chest] --> sv[Sea Cock Isolation Valve]
    sv --> mb[Mud Box Strainer]
    mb --> swp[Sea Water Pump]
    swp --> fv[Flow Regulating Valve]
    fv --> cond[Refrigerant Condenser Shell and Tube]
    cond --> nrv[Non Return Valve]
    nrv --> od[Overboard Discharge Above WL]
    swp -. backup .-> swp2[Duty Standby Pump]
```

### 4.4 Component Map — Domestic Hot & Cold Water System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source[Fresh Water Generator RO or Evaporator] --> hwt[Hydrophore Tank]
    hwt --> hwp[Hydrophore Pump]
    hwp --> cws[Cold Water Service Main]
    cws --> cl[Calorifier Electric or Steam Heated]
    cl --> hws[Hot Water Service 60 C]
    hws --> gal[Galley]
    hws --> cab[Crew Cabins]
    hws --> lau[Laundry]
    cws --> cw[Cold Taps]
    cl -. recirculation .-> recircPump[Recirculation Pump]
    recircPump --> hws
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** All four diagrams use standardised block conventions — rectangular blocks = equipment, arrows = direction of flow. Double arrows in Mermaid represent return or feedback paths.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the function of a **sea chest** in a ship’s sea water cooling system. Why is it positioned below the waterline?*

**Model Answer:**

A **sea chest** is a large, recessed inlet built into the ship’s hull, below the waterline, which admits sea water into the ship’s cooling and ballast systems. It is positioned **below the waterline** so that:

1. The inlet remains **submerged at all draughts and sea states** ensuring a continuous water supply.
2. It is **away from heated discharges** (e.g., condenser outlets), thus avoiding recirculation of warm water.
3. It allows **laminar, low-velocity entry** of water, reducing the entry of air and vortices.
4. It can house a **strainer grid** that filters out large debris before the water enters the system.

It is fitted with an **air vent pipe** at its top (to release trapped air) and a **drain valve** at its base (for cleaning).

*(3 marks — definition 1, positioning reasons 2)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the purpose of an **expansion tank** in a chilled water system? List any two accessories fitted to it.*

**Model Answer:**

A **chilled water expansion tank** serves three primary functions:

1. **Accommodates the thermal expansion** of water as its temperature rises (water expands by ~0.2 % per 10 K).
2. **Maintains positive system pressure** to prevent cavitation in pumps and air ingress in pipes.
3. **Acts as an air separation point** — dissolved and entrained air migrates to the tank and is vented out.

**Two accessories fitted to an expansion tank:**
- **Automatic air vent** (to release accumulated air).
- **Level sight glass / float switch** (to monitor water level and trigger make-up).
- **Pressure gauge** and **safety relief valve** (additional valid answers).

*(3 marks — function 1, accessories 1 each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Many students **forget to convert units** (e.g., kW → kJ/s, L/s → m³/s) and lose 1–2 marks per numerical. Always write units *before* substituting values. Also, **never neglect the 1.5× safety factor on expansion tank sizing** — it is a favourite KTU follow-up question.

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**  *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Central Cooling System (CCS)** used on board modern ships. Explain how it differs from a direct sea water cooling system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal sea water pump delivers 30 m³/h of sea water through a 75 mm diameter pipe. The total equivalent length of the suction and delivery piping including fittings is 60 m. The static lift from sea chest to condenser is 3 m. Assuming a Darcy friction factor of 0.022, calculate:  
(i) Frictional head loss in metres of sea water.  
(ii) Total head developed by the pump.  
(iii) Power required to drive the pump if the overall efficiency is 65 %. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Central Cooling System Description (7 marks):**

In a **Central Cooling System (CCS)**, sea water is used to cool an **intermediate fresh water loop** via a **plate heat exchanger (PHE)**, and this fresh water in turn cools the **refrigerant condensers** of the AC plant.

**Block diagram of CCS:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sea[Sea Chest] --> swPump[Sea Water Pump]
    swPump --> phe[Plate Heat Exchanger]
    phe --> overboard[Overboard Discharge]
    phe --> fwLoop[Fresh Water Intermediate Loop]
    fwLoop --> fwPump[FW Cooling Pump]
    fwPump --> cond[Refrigerant Condensers Multiple]
    cond --> fwLoop
```

**Differences from Direct Sea Water Cooling:**
