| Parameter | Direct SW Cooling | Central Cooling System |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Sea water in condenser | Yes | No |
| Condenser material | Cu-Ni / Ti / Al-Br | Carbon steel (cheaper) |
| Biofouling risk | High | Low (only in PHE) |
| Maintenance cost | High | Low |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher (extra PHE + pump) |
| Temperature control | Less stable | More stable |

*(Full 7 marks: diagram 3, comparison 3, explanation 1)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\dot{V} = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 30/3600 = 0.00833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $D = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $L_{tot} = 60 \text{ m}$
- $H_{static} = 3 \text{ m}$
- $f = 0.022$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\eta = 0.65$

**Step 1 — Pipe cross-sectional area**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocity in the pipe**

$$v = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{A} = \dfrac{0.00833}{0.004418} = 1.886 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Friction head loss in metres of sea water**

$$H_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L_{tot}}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$$

$$H_f = 0.022 \cdot \dfrac{60}{0.075} \cdot \dfrac{(1.886)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$H_f = 0.022 \times 800 \times \dfrac{3.557}{19.62}$$

$$H_f = 17.6 \times 0.1813 = 3.19 \text{ m of sea water}$$

**Step 4 — Total head**

$$H_{total} = H_{static} + H_f = 3 + 3.19 = 6.19 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Pump power**

$$P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot \dot{V} \cdot H_{total}}{\eta}$$

$$P = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.00833 \times 6.19}{0.65}$$

Numerator:

$$1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25$$
$$10055.25 \times 0.00833 = 83.76$$
$$83.76 \times 6.19 = 518.4 \text{ W}$$

Dividing by efficiency:

$$P = \dfrac{518.4}{0.65} = 797.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.80 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $H_f = 3.19 \text{ m}$
- (ii) $H_{total} = 6.19 \text{ m}$
- (iii) $P = 0.80 \text{ kW}$ (≈ 1.1 hp motor to be selected)

*(7 marks: 2+2+3 distributed across parts (i), (ii), (iii))*

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**  *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain, with a sketch, the layout of a **closed-loop chilled water distribution system** on a passenger ship. List any four accessories associated with the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The air conditioning plant of a ship has a total cooling load of 300 kW. The chilled water is supplied at 7 °C and returns at 13 °C. Calculate:  
(i) The mass flow rate of chilled water required.  
(ii) The volumetric flow rate in L/min.  
(iii) If the return water is mixed with 20 % of fresh chilled water (bypass) at 7 °C to give a final supply temperature of 9 °C, find the required mass of bypass water. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Chilled Water Distribution Layout (7 marks):**

A closed-loop chilled water system on a passenger ship comprises a primary chiller plant, secondary distribution loop, and terminal units (AHUs/FCUs). A detailed block arrangement:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ch[Chiller Plant] -->|6-8 C Supply| chwp[Chilled Water Pumps Duty and Standby]
    chwp --> st[Strainers and Isolation Valves]
    st --> rv[Pressure Regulating Valves]
    rv --> ring[Ring Main Pipe Circuit Accommodation]
    ring --> ahu1[AHU Deck A]
    ring --> ahu2[AHU Deck B]
    ring --> ahu3[AHU Deck C]
    ring --> ahu4[FCU Cabins]
    ahu1 --> ret[Return Pipe 12-14 C]
    ahu2 --> ret
    ahu3 --> ret
    ahu4 --> ret
    ret --> av[Air Vent and Separator]
    av --> exp[Expansion Tank With Make Up]
    exp --> ch
```

**Four accessories:**
1. **Strainers / Y-filters** at pump discharge.
2. **Automatic air vents** at high points.
3. **Pressure gauges and thermometers** at strategic points.
4. **Three-way modulating valves** at each AHU.
5. *(Optional)* **Flow switches** for pump cut-off, **chemical dosing pot**, **drain valves**.

*(7 marks: diagram 4, accessories list 2, description 1)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $Q = 300 \text{ kW}$
- $t_s = 7 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $t_r = 13 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**(i) Mass flow rate of chilled water:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p (t_r - t_s)} = \dfrac{300}{4.186 \times (13-7)} = \dfrac{300}{25.116} = 11.94 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{11.94}{1000} = 0.01194 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{V}_{lpm} = 0.01194 \times 60\,000 = 716.4 \text{ L/min}$$

**(iii) Bypass mixing calculation:**

The chiller supplies water at $7 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Return water at $13 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ mixes with bypass at $7 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to give supply at $9 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Let $\dot{m}_b$ = bypass flow, $\dot{m}_r$ = return flow. Total flow = $\dot{m}_b + \dot{m}_r$.

Given that **20 %** is bypass → $\dot{m}_b = 0.20 \cdot \dot{m}_{total}$ and $\dot{m}_r = 0.80 \cdot \dot{m}_{total}$.

Mixing equation:

$$t_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_b \cdot t_b + \dot{m}_r \cdot t_r}{\dot{m}_b + \dot{m}_r}$$

Substituting $t_{mix} = 9 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $t_b = 7 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $t_r = 13 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$9 = \dfrac{0.20 \cdot 7 + 0.80 \cdot 13}{1.00}$$

$$9 = 1.4 + 10.4 = 11.8 \,^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{(NOT equal to 9 °C)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation:** The 20 % bypass assumption is **inconsistent with a 9 °C supply**. The correct procedure is to **find the bypass fraction $x$** that yields 9 °C.

**Re-solving for $x$:**

$$9 = x(7) + (1-x)(13) = 13 - 6x$$

$$6x = 13 - 9 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 0.667$$

**Required bypass mass flow:**

$$\dot{m}_b = x \cdot \dot{m}_{total} = 0.667 \times 11.94 = 7.96 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Verification of supply temperature:**

$$t_s = (0.667)(7) + (0.333)(13) = 4.67 + 4.33 = 9.00 \,^{\circ}\text{C} \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\dot{m} = 11.94 \text{ kg/s}$
- (ii) $\dot{V} = 716.4 \text{ L/min}$
- (iii) $\dot{m}_b = 7.96 \text{ kg/s}$ (66.7 % bypass)

*(7 marks: 2+2+3 with proper unit conversions and interpretation)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Water Systems (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Sea chest** must be below the waterline with an air vent and drain.
- ✅ **Sea cock** is the mandatory isolation valve before any sea water pipe enters the ship.
- ✅ **Central Cooling System (CCS)** uses an intermediate fresh water loop cooled by sea water in a **plate heat exchanger** — preferred for modern ships.
- ✅ **Chilled water** temperatures: **Supply 6–8 °C, Return 12–14 °C** with ΔT ≈ 6 K.
- ✅ **Sea water properties:** $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p \approx 3.99 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- ✅ **Fresh water properties:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- ✅ **Q = ṁ · c_p · ΔT** is the master equation for any water-side heat transfer numerical.
- ✅ **Pump power** $P = \rho g \dot{V} H / \eta$ — always include efficiency (0.6–0.75 typical).
- ✅ **Expansion tank** is sized using $\Delta V = V_0 \beta \Delta T$ **plus a 1.5× safety factor**.
- ✅ **Three-way valve** modulates chilled water flow to maintain cabin temperature.
- ✅ **U-trap** in condensate drain prevents reverse airflow.
- ✅ **Pipe material for sea water:** Cu-Ni 90/10 (main), Cu-Ni 70/30 (high velocity), GRP (large diameter).
- ✅ **Pipe material for chilled water:** SS-316L or carbon steel with internal coating.
- ✅ **Domestic water** comes from **fresh water generators (evaporators or RO)** and is stored in **hydrophore tanks**.
- ✅ **Calorifier** heats domestic water using **steam coils or electric heaters** to ~60 °C.
- ✅ **Make-up water** + **chemical dosing** prevents corrosion, scaling, and bacterial growth.
- ✅ **Always include** an **automatic air vent** at the highest point of the chilled water loop.
- ✅ **Valuable examiner tip:** When mixing two water streams, always apply the **energy balance** $t_{mix} = (\dot{m}_1 t_1 + \dot{m}_2 t_2) / (\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2)$ and *verify* the result.
- ✅ **Condensate** must be discharged to a **waste water tank** or **bilge**, never directly overboard in sensitive zones (e.g., near potable water intakes).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unitary Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unitary Systems

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Unitary System** in refrigeration and air conditioning is defined as a *factory-assembled, factory-tested, and self-contained (or matched-split) refrigeration unit* in which the **compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator** are integrated within a single cabinet or are split between two matched, interconnectable sections. Per ASHRAE and KTU 2024 conventions, the entire refrigeration circuit is pre-charged with refrigerant, pre-wired, and pre-piped at the manufacturing plant, requiring only electrical and ductwork connections at the installation site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A unitary system is a **factory-made, single-package or split-package** refrigerant-based cooling/heating system in which the refrigeration cycle components (compressor + condenser + metering device + evaporator) are housed as a single coordinated assembly. Capacity range typically spans **0.5 TR to 100 TR** (1.75 kW to 350 kW) for marine and commercial applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

Imagine buying a microwave oven versus assembling a stove, a generator, and a cookware from scratch. The microwave is a **unitary device** — everything is sealed inside one box, you just plug it in. A unitary AC works the same way: instead of an engineer on board the ship having to braze copper pipes, evacuate a system, charge refrigerant, and wire individual components, the entire refrigeration loop arrives as a **plug-and-cool module**.

For a ship, this is a game-changer. Crew is limited, space is tight, vibration is constant, and the marine environment is corrosive. A unitary unit is **dropped into a cabin, bolted down, power-cabled, duct-connected, and switched on** — minimal shipyard work, maximum reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipboard Reality:** Most cabin ACs on passenger ships, naval vessels, and offshore platforms are unitary. The **central chilled-water plant** only generates cold water; the **unitary terminal units (fancoil units, chilled-water air handlers)** distribute the cooling into each zone.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics

| Metric | Standard Range / Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | **0.5 TR to 100 TR** (1.75–350 kW) | Cabinet to large packaged units |
| **Refrigerants** | **R-134a, R-407C, R-410A, R-22** | HFC/HCFC based |
| **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)** | **2.8 – 3.6** | Higher is better |
| **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** | **2.5 – 4.0** | Cooling-mode ratio |
| **Power Supply (Marine)** | **440 V, 3-phase, 60 Hz** | Standard ship mains |
| **Operating Ambient** | **+5 °C to +52 °C** | Marine ambient envelope |
| **Sound Level** | **35 – 65 dB(A)** | Cabin-grade to plant-room grade |
| **Vibration Tolerance** | **Up to 4 g RMS** | Per IACS E10 / IEC 60068 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Unitary System

A unitary system, whether a window AC, split AC, packaged AC, or marine self-contained unit, contains these **five functional blocks**:

1. **Compressor (Heart)** — Reciprocating, scroll, or rotary. In modern unitary units, **scroll compressors** dominate due to lower vibration, fewer moving parts, and superior part-load efficiency. In marine plant rooms, **semi-hermetic screw or scroll** is preferred.

2. **Condenser (Heat Rejector)** — Air-cooled (most common in unitary), water-cooled (for marine where seawater cooling is available), or evaporatively cooled.

3. **Expansion Device (Throttle)** — **Capillary tube** (small units < 3 TR) or **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV/TEV)** (larger unitary and packaged units).

4. **Evaporator (Cooling Coil)** — Direct-expansion (DX) coil with fin-and-tube construction. For chilled-water unitary terminal units, this is replaced by a **water coil** and the refrigerant cycle lives in a central chiller.

5. **Controls & Safety Chain** — Thermostat, high/low pressure cutouts, oil-pressure switch, anti-frost thermostat, overload relay, and (marine) **vibration-isolated mountings**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Unitary"?** The word "unitary" comes from *unity* — a **single, undivided, self-contained** functional unit. ASHRAE classifies equipment as "unitary" if it incorporates **at least the evaporator, compressor, and condenser** in one factory assembly, even if the air-handling and refrigeration sections are in separate casings (as in a split system).

## 2.2 KTU Classification of Unitary Systems

The KTU 2024 scheme, following ASHRAE Standard 15 and 16, classifies unitary systems as:

### A. By Physical Configuration
- **Monoblock / Window Unit** — All components in one cabinet, mounted on a wall or window ledge. Capacities 0.5–3 TR.
- **Mini-Split (Single/Multi)** — Two units: indoor (evaporator + fan) and outdoor (compressor + condenser), connected by refrigerant lines and electrical cable. Capacities 0.75–5 TR per indoor unit, multi-systems up to 10+ indoor units on one outdoor.
- **Ductable Split** — Indoor unit discharges into a small duct network. 1.5–6 TR. Very common in **ship cabins** where ducting runs through ceiling voids.
- **Packaged Terminal Air Conditioner (PTAC)** — Similar to window, installed through a wall sleeve. Common in **hotel-style ship cabins**.
- **Packaged Unit (Roof-Top or Floor-Mounted)** — 5–100 TR, fully weatherproof, used for **ship's galley, mess, machinery control rooms**.
- **Self-Contained Marine Unit** — Specially ruggedized, vibration-isolated, with **cupro-nickel or titanium** seawater condenser for direct seawater cooling.

### B. By Refrigeration Cycle Location
- **Direct-Expansion (DX) Unitary** — Refrigerant circulates in the conditioned space. Faster response, lower cost. Used in **cabin ACs, wheelhouse ACs**.
- **Chilled-Water (Hydronic) Terminal Unitary** — Air handler with chilled-water coil. Connected to a **central marine chiller**. Used in **passenger ships, naval vessels** with large cooling loads.

### C. By Function
- **Cooling-Only Unitary**
- **Heat Pump Unitary** — Reverses refrigerant flow for heating.
- **Heat Recovery Unitary** — Extracts heat from condenser for hot water.

## 2.3 The Reverse-Cycle (Heat Pump) Principle

In **heat pump unitary systems**, a **4-way reversing valve** swaps the function of the indoor and outdoor coils:
- **Cooling Mode:** Indoor coil = evaporator, outdoor coil = condenser.
- **Heating Mode:** Indoor coil = condenser, outdoor coil = evaporator.

This is highly relevant for ships operating in cold waters (North Atlantic, Arctic) where the same unitary unit must both **cool in tropical waters and heat in polar waters**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat capacity of conditioned air | kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | Cooling capacity (DX coil) | kW |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Chilled-water coil capacity | kW |
| 4 | $\text{EER} = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{W_{in}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | (BTU/h)/W |
| 5 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{heat}}{W_{in}}$ | Heat pump COP for heating | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{\rho_{air} \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required air-flow rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $\eta_{sensible} = \dfrac{T_{room} - T_{supply}}{T_{room} - T_{outside}}$ | Effective Room Sensible Heat Ratio (RSHR) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{duct} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Frictional duct pressure drop | Pa |
| 10 | $Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_L$ | Total load = Sensible + Latent | kW |
| 11 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{Q_e}{\text{COP}}$ | Compressor power from cooling load | kW |
| 12 | $T_{dew,coil} \approx T_{room} - \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\text{By-pass factor}}$ | Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) relation | °C |
| 13 | $T_{eq,coil} = \text{BPF} \cdot T_{outside} + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot T_{room}$ | Effective coil surface temperature | °C |
| 14 | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{pw} \cdot (T_{sw,out} - T_{sw,in})}$ | Seawater flow for marine condenser | kg/s |
| 15 | $P_{room} = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{1.2} \cdot 0.6$ | Rough ventilation air per person | L/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` (vertical bar) in tables!** All absolute-value and conditional expressions must use `\vert` or `\mid`. The formula $T_{dew} \vert_{coil}$ is rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.5 Why Unitary Systems are Dominant in Marine HVAC

- **Modularity:** Each cabin / zone has an independent unit — fault in one cabin doesn't shut down the entire system.
- **Maintainability:** Defective unit is removed, repaired on board, and replaced. No central plant shutdown.
- **Vibration isolation:** Each unit is mounted on **rubber/neoprene anti-vibration pads**, isolating compressor vibration from the ship's hull.
- **Solas / Class compliance:** Independent systems meet redundant cooling requirements for **machinery control rooms, navigation bridge, and radio rooms**.
- **Zonal temperature control:** Each cabin has its own thermostat → passenger comfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Modern cruise ships use **hybrid systems** — a central chiller plant (chilled water) feeding **hundreds of fan-coil units (FCUs)**. Each FCU is a "unitary" terminal device. Cargo ships and naval vessels, however, still favor **direct-expansion unitary self-contained units** for crew cabins and operational spaces due to their simplicity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sizing a Marine Cabin Unitary AC

A ship's captain's cabin has the following design data:
- Cabin volume: $V_{cabin} = 60 \, m^3$
- Design outside temperature (Red Sea): $T_{out} = 45 \, °C$ DB, $28 \, °C$ WB
- Design inside temperature: $T_{in} = 24 \, °C$ DB, $50 \,\%$ RH
- Occupancy: 1 person (light office work)
- Glass area: $1.5 \, m^2$, U-value $5.8 \, W/m^2K$
- External wall area: $8 \, m^2$, U-value $2.5 \, W/m^2K$
- Internal heat loads: lighting 100 W, equipment 50 W, person 75 W sensible + 55 W latent

**Step 1 — Calculate Sensible Heat from Conduction (Fabric).**

$$
Q_{s,fabric} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

$$
Q_{s,fabric} = 2.5 \cdot 8 \cdot (45 - 24) = 2.5 \cdot 8 \cdot 21 = 420 \, W
$$

$$
Q_{s,glass} = 5.8 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 21 = 182.7 \, W
$$

**Step 2 — Sensible Heat from Internal Sources.**

$$
Q_{s,internal} = Q_{light} + Q_{equip} + Q_{person,s} = 100 + 50 + 75 = 225 \, W
$$

**Step 3 — Latent Heat from Internal Sources.**

$$
Q_{L,internal} = Q_{person,L} = 55 \, W
$$

**Step 4 — Infiltration / Ventilation Load (assume 1.5 ACH).**

$$
\dot{V}_{inf} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot V_{cabin}}{3600} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot 60}{3600} = 0.025 \, m^3/s
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{inf} = 0.025 \cdot 1.2 = 0.03 \, kg/s
$$

For air-side sensible heat from infiltration:

$$
Q_{s,inf} = \dot{m}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 0.03 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 21 = 0.633 \, W
$$

(For tightness, often ignored in modern ships, but kept for completeness.)

**Step 5 — Total Sensible Heat.**

$$
Q_{s,total} = 420 + 182.7 + 225 + 0.633 \approx 828.3 \, W
$$

**Step 6 — Total Latent Heat.**

$$
Q_{L,total} = 55 \, W \quad (\text{ventilation latent is small for 1.5 ACH})
$$

**Step 7 — Total Cooling Load.**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{s,total} + Q_{L,total} = 828.3 + 55 = 883.3 \, W \approx 0.25 \, TR
$$

> **Selection:** A unitary unit of **0.5 TR (1.75 kW)** is chosen (50% safety margin for solar gain through portholes, which were neglected, and for any future load changes).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coil Air-Side Performance (Bypass Factor Method)

A unitary DX coil has:
- Entering air: $T_1 = 28 \, °C$ DB, $W_1 = 14 \, g/kg$
- Leaving air: $T_2 = 14 \, °C$ DB
- Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) temperature: $T_{ADP} = 8 \, °C$
- Air mass flow: $\dot{m}_a = 0.5 \, kg/s$

**Step 1 — Determine Bypass Factor (BF).**

$$
BF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}
$$

$$
BF = \dfrac{14 - 8}{28 - 8} = \dfrac{6}{20} = 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Coil Surface Temperature.**

$$
T_{eq} = BF \cdot T_1 + (1 - BF) \cdot T_{ADP}
$$

$$
T_{eq} = 0.30 \cdot 28 + 0.70 \cdot 8 = 8.4 + 5.6 = 14.0 \, °C
$$

**Step 3 — Sensible Cooling Capacity.**

$$
Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 0.5 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (28 - 14)
$$

$$
Q_s = 0.5 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 14 = 7.035 \, kW
$$

**Step 4 — Contact Factor (= 1 - BF).**

$$
CF = 1 - BF = 0.70
$$

Higher CF = more efficient coil. For unitary units, **CF ≥ 0.75 is the KTU benchmark**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Pump Performance in Cold-Water Heating

A heat-pump unitary system on a ship operates in heating mode at $-5 \, °C$ outside. Data:
- Heating capacity: $Q_h = 8.0 \, kW$
- Power input: $W = 2.5 \, kW$
- Indoor temperature: $22 \, °C$

**Step 1 — COP of Heat Pump.**

$$
COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_h}{W} = \dfrac{8.0}{2.5} = 3.2
$$

**Step 2 — Useful Heat per kWh of Electricity.**

$$
\text{Useful heat} = COP_{HP} \cdot 1 \, kWh = 3.2 \, kWh
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Electric Resistance Heater.**

Electric heater: $\eta = 1.0$, produces 1 kWh heat per 1 kWh electricity.

**Heat pump is 3.2× more efficient** than a resistive heater. KTU 2024 emphasizes this in marine applications where **diesel generator electricity is expensive**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Seawater-Cooled Marine Unitary Condenser

A marine unitary AC has:
- Total heat rejection: $Q_{cond} = Q_{cool} + W_{comp} = 7.5 + 2.2 = 9.7 \, kW$
- Seawater inlet: $T_{sw,in} = 30 \, °C$ (warm tropical waters)
- Allowed seawater temperature rise: $\Delta T_{sw} = 5 \, °C$

**Step 1 — Seawater Mass Flow Required.**

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} = \dfrac{9.7}{4.186 \cdot 5}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{9.7}{20.93} = 0.4635 \, kg/s
$$

**Step 2 — Seawater Volumetric Flow.**

$$
\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{0.4635}{1025} = 4.52 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/s \approx 0.45 \, m^3/h
$$

> **Design check:** This is small enough for a self-contained cupro-nickel tube-in-shell condenser. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Unitary vs Central Plant

| Parameter | DX Unitary | Chilled-Water Unitary (FCU) | Central Plant Only |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerant in space** | Yes | No (water only) | Yes (chiller room) |
| **Initial Cost (small ship)** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Initial Cost (large ship)** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Zonal Control** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (single point) |
| **Maintenance** | Per-cabin swap | Per-FCU swap | Specialist crew needed |
| **Energy Efficiency (full load)** | 2.8–3.4 EER | 3.0–4.0 EER (chiller + FCU) | 3.5–5.5 EER (chiller) |
| **Space Required** | Multiple cabinets | Central plant + small FCUs | Central plant + AHUs |
| **Vulnerability** | Each unit independent | Plant single point of failure | Plant single point of failure |
| **Best for Marine** | Crew cabins, bridge | Passenger cabins, galley | Machinery cooling, provisions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Unitary System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply 440V 3ph 60Hz] --> B[Control Panel and Thermostat]
    B --> C[Compressor Scroll or Reciprocating]
    C --> D[Hot Discharge Gas]
    D --> E[Condenser Air Cooled or Seawater Cooled]
    E --> F[Subcooled Liquid Refrigerant]
    F --> G[Filter Drier]
    G --> H[Expansion Device TXV or Capillary]
    H --> I[Low Pressure Two Phase Mix]
    I --> J[Evaporator DX Coil or Chilled Water Coil]
    J --> K[Cold Air to Cabin]
    J --> L[Suction Line]
    L --> M[Accumulator]
    M --> C

    N[Cabin Return Air] --> O[Return Air Filter]
    O --> J

    P[Safety Chain] --> C
    P --> B
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Unitary Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[UNITARY SYSTEMS] --> B[Single Package Monoblock]
    A --> C[Split System]
    A --> D[Multi Split System]
    A --> E[Packaged Unit]

    B --> B1[Window AC]
    B --> B2[Portable AC]
    B --> B3[Marine Self Contained Seawater Cooled]

    C --> C1[Mini Split Wall Mounted]
    C --> C2[Ductable Split]
    C --> C3[Ceiling Cassette]
    C --> C4[Floor Ceiling Convertible]

    D --> D1[One Outdoor Many Indoors]
    D --> D2[VRF VRV Systems]

    E --> E1[Roof Top Package]
    E --> E2[Floor Mounted Package]
    E --> E3[Marine Plant Room Package]
```

## 4.3 Heat Pump Reverse-Cycle Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COOLING
        C1[Compressor] --> C2[Indoor Coil as Condenser]
        C2 --> C3[Reversing Valve 4 Way]
        C3 --> C4[Outdoor Coil as Evaporator]
        C4 --> C5[Expansion Device]
        C5 --> C3
        C3 --> C1
    end

    subgraph HEATING
        H1[Compressor] --> H2[Indoor Coil as Condenser]
        H2 --> H3[Reversing Valve 4 Way]
        H3 --> H4[Outdoor Coil as Evaporator]
        H4 --> H5[Expansion Device]
        H5 --> H3
        H3 --> H1
    end
```

## 4.4 Airflow Path Inside a Ductable Split Unit (Marine Cabin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cabin Return Air Grill] --> B[Return Air Plenum]
    B --> C[Washable Filter Class G3]
    C --> D[Evaporator DX Coil 4 Row]
    D --> E[Centrifugal Fan Forward Curved]
    E --> F[Supply Plenum]
    F --> G[Supply Duct in Ceiling Void]
    G --> H[Cabin Supply Diffuser]
    D --> I[Drain Pan with Marine Grade Trap]
    I --> J[Condensate Drain to Scupper]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a unitary system in refrigeration and air conditioning. List the major components integrated inside it.**

**Model Answer:**
A unitary system is a factory-assembled, self-contained refrigeration unit in which the major components — **compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator** — are integrated into a single cabinet or matched split sections. Auxiliary components include controls, fans, filters, and a safety chain. The unit arrives pre-charged with refrigerant, pre-wired, and pre-piped, requiring only electrical and ductwork connections at site. **[3 marks]**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between a monoblock unitary AC and a split unitary AC? Give one marine application each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **monoblock** has all four components in a single cabinet, typically installed in a window or wall sleeve; one marine example is a **wheelhouse window AC**. A **split** has two sections: indoor (evaporator + fan) and outdoor (compressor + condenser), connected by refrigerant lines; a marine example is a **cabin ductable split AC** with the outdoor unit in the ship's deck house. **[3 marks]**

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two questions. Students answer either the full OR group of two alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **window-type unitary air conditioner**. List its advantages and limitations for marine applications. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A unitary split AC in a ship's officer cabin has a cooling capacity of **3.5 kW** with an EER of **3.0**. Calculate:
- (i) Power input
- (ii) Heat rejected at the condenser
- (iii) Mass flow of R-134a in the cycle, given enthalpies $h_1 = 245 \, kJ/kg$ at compressor inlet, $h_2 = 275 \, kJ/kg$ at compressor outlet, $h_3 = 105 \, kJ/kg$ at condenser outlet, $h_4 = h_3$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of Window AC — Schematic Description:**
The window AC has all four components in a single cabinet divided into two sections: an **indoor (evaporator) side** and an **outdoor (condenser) side**, separated by an insulated partition. A **single double-shaft centrifugal fan** (sometimes with two fans) draws indoor air through a filter over the evaporator coil, cooling it, and discharges the cool air into the room. The same fan (or a second one) draws outside air over the condenser coil, rejecting heat to the atmosphere. A **hermetically sealed reciprocating or rotary compressor** circulates R-134a / R-410A through the cycle. A **capillary tube** serves as the expansion device. A **slinger ring** on the fan motor shaft throws condensate onto the condenser coil for evaporative cooling.

**Advantages for marine use:** low cost, easy installation, factory-sealed refrigerant circuit (no on-board charging), compact. **Limitations:** high noise, lower efficiency than split units, single-phase (less suitable for high-capacity shipboard needs), no zonal control. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Calculations:**

(i) Power input:
$$
W = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{EER} = \dfrac{3.5}{3.0} = 1.167 \, kW
$$

(ii) Heat rejected at condenser:
$$
Q_{cond} = Q_{cool} + W = 3.5 + 1.167 = 4.667 \, kW
$$

(iii) Mass flow of refrigerant:
$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_{cool}}{h_1 - h_4}
$$

Given $h_4 = h_3 = 105 \, kJ/kg$:
$$
\dot{m}_{ref} = \dfrac{3.5}{245 - 105} = \dfrac{3.5}{140} = 0.025 \, kg/s = 1.5 \, kg/min
$$

**[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **heat pump unitary system** with a neat sketch of the 4-way reversing valve. Compare its performance in cooling and heating modes for a ship operating in tropical and polar waters. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A marine self-contained unitary AC uses **seawater-cooled cupro-nickel condenser** to reject **12 kW** of heat. Seawater enters at **28 °C** and leaves at **33 °C**. Calculate:
- (i) Seawater mass flow rate
- (ii) Seawater volumetric flow rate
- (iii) Pipe diameter required if velocity is limited to **2 m/s**

Take $c_{pw} = 4.186 \, kJ/kg \cdot K$ and $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, kg/m^3$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Heat Pump Working Principle:**
A heat pump unitary system uses a **4-way reversing valve** at the compressor discharge to switch between cooling and heating modes. In **cooling mode**, the indoor coil acts as the evaporator (absorbs heat from cabin) and the outdoor coil acts as the condenser (rejects heat to ambient). In **heating mode**, the reversing valve re-routes hot discharge gas to the indoor coil, making it the **condenser** that heats the cabin, while the outdoor coil becomes the **evaporator**, absorbing heat from cold ambient air/water.

For a ship: in **tropical waters** (e.g., Persian Gulf, 40 °C ambient), the heat pump primarily runs in cooling mode but the high condensing temperature reduces COP. In **polar waters** (e.g., Arctic, -20 °C ambient), the system runs in heating mode, and the heat pump's COP remains acceptable (>2.0) as long as the outdoor coil does not ice up severely. The same unitary unit thus serves year-round. **[7 marks]**

**(b) Calculations:**

(i) Seawater mass flow rate:
$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} = \dfrac{12}{4.186 \cdot (33-28)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \dfrac{12}{4.186 \cdot 5} = \dfrac{12}{20.93} = 0.5734 \, kg/s
$$

(ii) Seawater volumetric flow rate:
$$
\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{0.5734}{1025} = 5.595 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/s
$$

(iii) Pipe diameter (continuity equation $\dot{V} = A \cdot V$):
$$
A = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{sw}}{V_{sw}} = \dfrac{5.595 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 2.798 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \Rightarrow D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 2.798 \times 10^{-4}}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{3.562 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01887 \, m
$$

$$
D \approx 19 \, mm \Rightarrow \text{Use 3/4" nominal pipe (DN20)}
$$

**[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the latent load** — students often compute only sensible heat and skip the latent heat from occupants, infiltration, and equipment. KTU 2024 strictly awards marks for both sensible and latent heat calculation.
> 2. **Wrong sign for condenser heat** — $Q_{cond} = Q_{cool} + W_{comp}$, NOT $Q_{cond} = Q_{cool} - W_{comp}$. The compressor adds work to the refrigerant.
> 3. **Mixing EER and COP** — EER uses BTU/h for $Q$ and W for $W$, while COP is dimensionless (using same units). Don't write $COP = 3.0$ when the problem says EER = 3.0.
> 4. **Seawater density** — fresh water is 1000 kg/m³; seawater is **1025 kg/m³**. Wrong density loses marks in numerical problems.
> 5. **Bypass Factor (BF) interpretation** — students confuse BF and Contact Factor (CF). Remember: $\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BF}$. Lower BF = better coil.
> 6. **Heat pump indoor/outdoor coil terminology** — students forget that in heating mode, the indoor coil becomes the **condenser**, not the evaporator. Reverse carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Unitary system = factory-sealed refrigeration unit** with compressor + condenser + expansion device + evaporator.
- **Two main families:** (1) **Monoblock / Window** (single cabinet) and (2) **Split** (indoor + outdoor sections).
- **Refrigerants used in modern unitary units:** R-134a, R-410A, R-407C (HCFC R-22 phased out under Montreal Protocol).
- **Marine-specific unitary units** use **seawater-cooled cupro-nickel condensers** and **vibration-isolated mountings** (4-pad rubber isolators).
- **Cooling capacity sizing** must include sensible + latent + ventilation + fabric + internal loads, with a **20–30% safety margin**.
- **EER** = $Q_{cool} / W$ in (BTU/h)/W; **COP** = $Q_{cool}/W$ in same units (dimensionless).
- **Heat pump unitary** uses a 4-way reversing valve to switch between cooling and heating. **COP_HP > 3** is typical; much better than electric resistance heating.
- **Bypass Factor (BF)** of a coil is the fraction of air that **misses the coil**; **Contact Factor = 1 − BF**; high CF (≥ 0.75) is desirable.
- **Packaged units (5–100 TR)** are used for ship's galley, mess halls, and machinery spaces; **split units** for cabins.
- **Cruise ships** use **central chiller + fan-coil units (FCUs)** — FCUs are unitary **terminal devices**.
- **Cargo / naval vessels** prefer **DX unitary self-contained units** for redundancy and zonal control.
- **IACS E10 / IEC 60068** specifies vibration, shock, and tilt requirements for marine unitary equipment.
- **Marine ambient design:** typically **+45 °C DB / +28 °C WB outside** and **+30 °C / 80% RH inside** for a **24 °C / 50% RH cabin**.
- **Refrigerant charge** in a unitary unit is **factory-sealed** — never recharged at sea unless under class-approved gas-recovery procedure.
- **Safety chain** includes HP/LP cutouts, oil-pressure switch, anti-frost thermostat, and (for marine) **flow switch on seawater circuit**.
- **Common exam tip:** always draw the **T-s or P-h diagram** alongside any numerical problem on unitary systems for full marks.

> **Key takeaway:** Unitary systems are the **workhorse of marine HVAC** — modular, maintainable, vibration-tolerant, and class-approved. Master their classification, sizing equations, and heat-pump variant for KTU 2024 success. ⚓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Hold Dehumidification Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST743 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING OF SHIPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cargo Hold Dehumidification Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cargo Hold Dehumidification System (CHDS):** A marine engineering installation comprising dehumidifiers, air ducts, fans, sensors, and control units designed to **reduce and maintain the moisture content (humidity ratio) of air** inside a ship's cargo compartments to a level at which condensation on cargo, ship's structure, or hatch covers is prevented throughout the voyage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a CHDS is treated as a **specialty HVAC subsystem** that combines principles of **psychrometrics**, **low-temperature heat transfer**, and **desiccant / refrigeration thermodynamics** to provide **moisture control** rather than primary temperature control.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the cargo hold as a **closed cool box that breathes through steel walls**. On a cold North Atlantic voyage, the outer steel plates become very cold (say 5 °C). If the air inside is warm and humid (like in the tropics, say 30 °C and 80 % RH), the moment that humid air touches the cold steel, water "sweats" out — exactly like a cold bottle of water on a hot day. This is **Ship Sweat**. Conversely, if cold cargo is loaded into a warm humid hold, moisture condenses on the cargo itself — this is **Cargo Sweat**. A dehumidification system is like a **silent moisture-sucking machine** that dries the air before the journey so neither sweat forms.

### 1.3 Why Dehumidification is Mandatory on Ships

| Problem | Root Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship Sweat** | Inner hull plate below dew point of hold air | Water drips onto cargo, damages it |
| **Cargo Sweat** | Cold cargo surface below dew point of hold air | Surface dampening, caking, rusting |
| **Mould & Mildew** | RH > 70 % on hygroscopic cargo (grain, cocoa) | Biological degradation |
| **Corrosion** | Sustained RH > 60 % on steel | Structural deterioration |
| **Caking of powders** | Moisture migration in bulk cargo (cement, sugar) | Cargo loss, claim disputes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Standard: The hold air **dew point** must always be kept **below the coldest surface temperature** the air can contact (hatch covers, deck plates, tank tops, bulkheads). A common marine target is to maintain hold RH at **50 % ± 5 %** and dew point at or below the lowest anticipated steel temperature.

### 1.4 Critical Physical Quantities

- **Relative Humidity (RH, φ):** ratio of actual water-vapour partial pressure to saturation pressure at the same temperature. Unitless, expressed as **%**.
- **Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity (ω):** mass of water vapour per kg of dry air — **kg water/kg da**.
- **Dew Point Temperature (Tdp):** temperature at which condensation begins when air is cooled at constant pressure — **°C**.
- **Wet Bulb Temperature (Twb):** adiabatic saturation temperature — **°C**.
- **Specific Enthalpy of moist air (h):** **kJ/kg da**.
- **Universal Gas Constant for Water Vapour: Rv = 0.4615 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C: hfg = 2501 kJ/kg**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Marine Dehumidifier

The system operates on **two fundamental physics routes** to remove water vapour from air:

1. **Condensation Route (Mechanical / Refrigeration):** Cool the air below its dew point so water condenses out as liquid. The dry cold air is then re-heated to a temperature acceptable for the cargo.
2. **Adsorption / Absorption Route (Desiccant):** Pass the air over a hygroscopic material (silica gel, lithium chloride, molecular sieve) that physically or chemically binds water vapour, releasing dry air. The desiccant is regenerated by hot air.

**Why two routes?** Condensation fails when the air is already cold (e.g., 8 °C with 80 % RH) because cooling further is energy-inefficient. Desiccants work at any temperature — they are **temperature-independent** moisture removers.

### 2.2 Working Logic — Stepwise

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** RH and temperature sensors in each hold send feedback to the controller.
- **Step 2 — Comparison:** Controller compares measured RH with the **set-point RH (typically 50 %)**.
- **Step 3 — Actuation:** If RH > set-point, the dehumidifier is energised; supply fan and return fan start.
- **Step 4 — Moisture Removal:** Air is drawn from the hold → passed through the dehumidifier → moisture is removed → dry air is re-supplied through ducts.
- **Step 5 — Heat Rejection (for condensation type):** Latent heat of condensation plus sensible heat is rejected through a refrigerant condenser to sea water.
- **Step 6 — Regeneration (for desiccant type):** A separate regeneration air stream is heated (often by electric heater or steam coil) and passed through the desiccant wheel to drive off absorbed moisture, which is then exhausted to the weather deck.
- **Step 7 — Recirculation & Mixing:** Recirculated dry air mixes with make-up ventilation air, keeping hold RH within band.

### 2.3 Heat and Moisture Sources in a Cargo Hold

A dehumidifier must overcome the following gains:

- **Hull Heat Transmission:** Q = U × A × ΔT
- **Solar Gain** through exposed deck plating (top-side wing tanks)
- **Respiration Heat** from hygroscopic cargo (grain, fruit, tobacco)
- **Infiltration / Ventilation Air** moisture
- **Bilge water evaporation**
- **Internal sources** (lighting, electrical panels, crew)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio (ω) | $\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | P = total pressure (Pa or kPa) |
| 2 | Relative Humidity (φ) | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_s(T)} \times 100$ | % | $P_s$ from steam tables or Antoine eq. |
| 3 | Specific Enthalpy (h) | $h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | kJ/kg da | T in °C |
| 4 | Dew Point | $P_{v}(T_{dp}) = P_v$; solve for $T_{dp}$ | °C | Numerically or psychrometric chart |
| 5 | Sensible Heat (Q_s) | $Q_s = \dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | kW | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s da, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K |
| 6 | Latent Heat (Q_l) | $Q_l = \dot{m}\,h_{fg}\,\Delta\omega$ | kW | $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg |
| 7 | Total Heat | $Q_t = \dot{m}\,\Delta h$ | kW | sensible + latent |
| 8 | Hull Heat Gain | $Q_{hull} = U\,A\,(T_{sea} - T_{hold})$ | kW | U in kW/m²·K, A in m² |
| 9 | Moisture Removal Rate | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_{da}\,( \omega_{in} - \omega_{out} )$ | kg/s | dehumidifier capacity |
| 10 | Dew Point from RH & T | $T_{dp} \approx \dfrac{b\,\gamma(T,RH)}{a - \gamma(T,RH)}$, with $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{aT}{b+T}$ | °C | Magnus–Tetens approximation |
| 11 | Air Density (moist) | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_d\,T_k\,(1 + 1.6078\,\omega)}$ | kg/m³ | $R_d = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K |
| 12 | Specific Volume | $v = R_d\,T_k\,(1 + 1.6078\,\omega)/P$ | m³/kg da | psychrometric inversion |
| 13 | Dew Point Margin (Design) | $\Delta T_{dp} = T_{coldest\_surface} - T_{dp,air}$ | K | MUST be > 0 to avoid sweat |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Sweat Design Rule (KTU-Standard):** At every instant during the voyage, $T_{dp,\,air} \le T_{steel,\,min} - 1\,K$ (a 1 K safety margin is customary).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CHDS is essential for: reefer-deck cargo holds, grain carriers, Ro-Ro car decks, containerised hygroscopic goods, munitions, cocoa, coffee, paper, electronics, and pharmaceutical shipments. The European standard **EN 12255** and **IMO CSS Code** indirectly govern hold atmospheric conditions for safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Humidity Ratio (Specific Humidity)

Start with the **definition** of humidity ratio:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{m_v}{m_a}
$$

where $m_v$ = mass of water vapour and $m_a$ = mass of dry air in the same volume V.

Using the **ideal gas law** for both vapours:

$$
P_v V = m_v R_v T_k \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_v = \dfrac{P_v V}{R_v T_k}
$$

$$
P_a V = m_a R_a T_k \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_a = \dfrac{P_a V}{R_a T_k}
$$

Dividing:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{m_v}{m_a} = \dfrac{P_v / R_v}{P_a / R_a}
$$

Since $P_a = P - P_v$ (Dalton's law), and $R_a / R_v = 0.622$ (because $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K and $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K):

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}
$$

This is the **master equation** used in every psychrometric calculation for marine AC and dehumidification.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Dew Point Condition

At the dew point, **saturation occurs** with the same partial pressure of water vapour:

$$
P_v = P_s(T_{dp})
$$

For the Magnus–Tetens approximation (valid from 0 to 60 °C):

$$
P_s(T) = 0.61078 \times \exp\!\left( \dfrac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3} \right) \quad \text{[kPa]}
$$

Hence $T_{dp}$ satisfies:

$$
0.61078 \times \exp\!\left( \dfrac{17.27\,T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 237.3} \right) = P_v = \phi \cdot P_s(T_{db})
$$

This implicit equation is solved numerically. For exam purposes, given $T_{db}$ and $\phi$, the magnitude of $T_{dp}$ can be read directly from a psychrometric chart or by the **simplified** form:

$$
T_{dp} \approx T_{db} - \dfrac{100 - \phi}{5}
$$

(useful when $\phi$ is between 50 % and 100 %).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A cargo hold contains air at **35 °C dry-bulb, 28 °C wet-bulb**, and barometric pressure **101.325 kPa**. The hold is to be maintained at **50 % RH** using a refrigeration dehumidifier. Calculate:

1. Specific humidity of the hold air.
2. Dew point of hold air.
3. Humidity ratio of air leaving the dehumidifier.
4. Moisture removal rate if the dry-air mass flow rate is **2.5 kg/s**.
5. Latent heat load on the refrigerant coil.

**Step 1 — Saturation Pressure at 35 °C (Antoine/Magnus):**

$$
P_s(35) = 0.61078 \times \exp\!\left( \dfrac{17.27 \times 35}{35 + 237.3} \right)
$$

$$
P_s(35) = 0.61078 \times \exp(2.218) = 0.61078 \times 9.194 \approx 5.615 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Vapour Pressure from Wet-Bulb (28 °C):**

At $T_{wb} = 28\,°C$, $P_s(28) = 0.61078 \times \exp(1.815) \approx 3.778$ kPa.

Using the psychrometric wet-bulb relation (in kPa):

$$
P_v = P_s(T_{wb}) - (P - P_s(T_{wb} \times 1.005\,(T_{db}-T_{wb})/h_{fg})
$$

(For a quick KTU exam, take $P_v \approx 3.0$ kPa — read from psychrometric chart.)

**Step 3 — Specific Humidity:**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.0}{101.325 - 3.0} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.0}{98.325}
$$

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times 0.03051 = 0.01898 \text{ kg/kg da} \approx 0.019 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 4 — Dew Point:**

We need $T_{dp}$ such that $P_s(T_{dp}) = 3.0$ kPa.

Iterating: at $T = 24\,°C$, $P_s(24) = 0.61078 \times \exp(1.589) = 2.985$ kPa. 

$$
\therefore T_{dp} \approx 24.05\,°C
$$

**Step 5 — Target Condition (50 % RH at 35 °C):**

$$
P_{v,\text{target}} = 0.50 \times 5.615 = 2.8075 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\omega_{out} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.8075}{101.325 - 2.8075} = 0.622 \times 0.02850 = 0.01773 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 6 — Moisture Removal Rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = 2.5 \times (0.01898 - 0.01773) = 2.5 \times 0.00125
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w = 0.003125 \text{ kg/s} = 11.25 \text{ kg/hour}
$$

**Step 7 — Latent Heat Load:**

$$
Q_l = \dot{m}_{da} \times h_{fg} \times \Delta\omega = 2.5 \times 2501 \times 0.00125
$$

$$
Q_l = 7.815 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This latent load must be added to the **sensible cooling load** (from the re-heat temperature drop) to size the **evaporator capacity** of the refrigerant plant — a classic KTU numerical-type question.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — System Selection

| Parameter | Refrigeration Dehumidifier | Desiccant Wheel | Liquid Desiccant (LiCl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Condense below dew point | Adsorb on silica gel | Absorb in hygroscopic solution |
| Effective at low T (≤ 10 °C) | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Energy source | Electrical (compressor) | Heat (regeneration) + electrical | Heat (regen) + electrical |
| Outlet air temperature | Cold (needs reheat) | Higher than inlet | Higher than inlet |
| RH achievable | 70 – 90 % | 5 – 30 % | 10 – 40 % |
| Corrosion risk | None | None | High (chloride attack) |
| Typical marine use | Chilled cargo holds | General cargo, grain | Limited |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Wheel life ~3–5 yr | Solution management |
| **Cost per kg water removed** | Medium | Low at scale | Low at scale |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Marine CHDS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HoldAirReturn] --> B[ReturnFanSU1]
    B --> C[FilterScreenF1]
    C --> D[CoolingCoilCU1]
    D --> E[CondensateDrainCD1]
    D --> F[ReheatCoilRH1]
    F --> G[SupplyFanSU2]
    G --> H[MainDuctMD1]
    H --> I[BranchDuct1BD1]
    H --> J[BranchDuct2BD2]
    I --> K[Hold1CompartmentH1]
    J --> L[Hold2CompartmentH2]
    K --> M[ReturnPathRP1]
    L --> N[ReturnPathRP2]
    M --> A
    N --> A
    O[RHsensorSH1] --> P[ControllerPLC1]
    Q[TsensorST1] --> P
    P --> D
    P --> F
    P --> B
    P --> G
    P --> R[AlarmAnnunciatorAA1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Hold air is drawn in (A → B), filtered (C), cooled below dew point (D) so that water condenses and drains out (E), reheated to avoid overcooling the cargo (F), then supplied back into the holds via the duct network (G → H → I/J → K/L). Sensors (O, Q) feed the PLC (P), which modulates the cooling coil, reheat coil, and fans. The annunciator (R) raises alarms on RH/ temperature out-of-band.

### 4.2 Psychrometric Process Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point1OutsideAir\n35C 80RH] --> M1[MixingSection]
    P2[Point2RecirculatedAir\n30C 70RH] --> M1
    M1 --> P3[Point3MixedAir\n33C 75RH]
    P3 --> CO[CoolingCoilDewPoint]
    CO --> P4[Point4SaturatedAir\n10C 100RH]
    P4 --> RH[ReheatCoil]
    RH --> P5[Point5SupplyAir\n22C 50RH]
    P5 --> HLD[CargoHold]
    HLD --> P2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cooling coil **must** cool the air down to the **apparatus dew point (ADP)** so that on leaving the coil the air is saturated (Point 4). The subsequent reheat then brings it back up to the desired supply temperature, now at the target RH. This is the **standard psychrometric signature** of every marine refrigeration-dehumidifier.

### 4.3 Desiccant Wheel CHDS — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ProcessAirFan] --> DW[DesiccantWheelDS1]
    DW --> A2[ProcessAirOutletToHold]
    R1[RegenAirFan] --> HX[RegenHeaterRG1]
    HX --> DW
    DW --> R2[RegenAirExhaustToWeather]
    S1[RHTempSensor] --> CTRL[ControllerCR1]
    CTRL --> DW
    CTRL --> HX
    CTRL --> A1
    CTRL --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The desiccant wheel rotates slowly (typically 6–20 rph) between two airstreams: a **process stream** that dries the hold air, and a **regeneration stream** that is heated to strip moisture off the wheel and exhaust it overboard. Only one wheel + heater combination is required per hold, making it compact and energy-efficient for low-temperature cargo holds.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *dew point temperature* of moist air. Why is it the most critical parameter for the design of a cargo hold dehumidification system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Dew point temperature ($T_{dp}$) of moist air is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure, i.e., the temperature at which condensation of water vapour just begins.
- **(1 mark)** It is the *true indicator* of the absolute moisture content of the air, independent of dry-bulb temperature.
- **(1 mark)** In cargo hold design, the air dew point must always be kept **below the coldest surface** the air can contact (steel plates, hatch covers). If $T_{dp} > T_{surface}$, condensation forms — leading to ship sweat, cargo sweat, mould, and corrosion. Hence $T_{dp}$ governs the size, type, and rating of the dehumidification plant.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Distinguish between **Ship Sweat** and **Cargo Sweat**. State the conditions under which each occurs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1.5 marks) Ship Sweat** occurs when the **outer hull plates become colder than the dew point of the air inside the hold**, e.g., when a vessel moves from warm tropical waters into cold northern seas. Moisture condenses on the **inner surface of the hull** and drips onto the cargo. Prevention: keep hold air dry so $T_{dp} < T_{hull}$.
- **(1.5 marks) Cargo Sweat** occurs when **cold cargo is loaded into a hold that is warm and humid**, so that the cargo surface temperature lies below the air dew point. Moisture condenses **on the cargo** itself. Prevention: pre-cool/dry the hold **before** loading, or pre-warm the cargo so $T_{cargo} > T_{dp}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. Most students **forget to include sensible re-heat load** on the refrigerant coil when sizing a refrigeration-type dehumidifier — you lose 2–3 marks if you only quote latent load.
> 2. In psychrometric numericals, students often **mix up total enthalpy with sensible enthalpy**. Always write the full expression: $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$.
> 3. Units! Steam-table $P_s$ is in **kPa**; do not confuse with Pa when using the humidity ratio formula.
> 4. When discussing system selection, always mention **cargo temperature range** — it is the deciding factor between refrigeration and desiccant types.

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **mechanical (refrigeration) cargo hold dehumidification system** on a ship. Clearly state the role of the cooling coil, reheat coil, condensate drain, supply and return fans, and PLC. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cargo hold contains 8 000 m³ of air at 30 °C DBT, 75 % RH, and barometric pressure 101.325 kPa. The dehumidifier is required to bring it to 50 % RH. Calculate:
- (i) Initial and final humidity ratios.
- (ii) Initial and final dew points.
- (iii) Total mass of water to be removed.
- (iv) Latent heat to be extracted.
- (v) Re-heat coil load to raise supply air from coil outlet (10 °C, 100 % RH) to 25 °C supply. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **(1 mark)** Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes): return air → return fan → filter → cooling coil (condensation) → condensate drain → reheat coil → supply fan → main duct → branch ducts → cargo hold → return path.
- **(1 mark) Cooling coil** cools air below dew point; water condenses and drains.
- **(1 mark) Reheat coil** raises the dry air temperature so it does not chill the cargo below its safe limit and to control RH.
- **(1 mark) Supply and return fans** maintain closed-loop circulation; supply fan overcomes duct resistance, return fan maintains negative pressure balance to prevent short-circuiting.
- **(1 mark) PLC** receives RH and T sensor signals, modulates the cooling coil valve position, the reheat coil, and the fan speeds (often VFD).
- **(1 mark) Sensors & Alarms** — RH/T sensors in each hold, high-humidity and low-temperature alarms to bridge.
- **(1 mark) Condensate drain** is piped to bilge; a U-trap prevents reverse air flow.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Humidity ratios:**

At 30 °C, $P_s(30) = 0.61078 \times \exp(17.27 \times 30 / (30 + 237.3)) = 0.61078 \times \exp(2.029) = 0.61078 \times 7.612 = 4.246$ kPa.

$$
P_{v,1} = 0.75 \times 4.246 = 3.185 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.185}{101.325 - 3.185} = 0.622 \times 0.03245 = 0.0202 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

$$
P_{v,2} = 0.50 \times 4.246 = 2.123 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\omega_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.123}{101.325 - 2.123} = 0.622 \times 0.02140 = 0.01331 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

**(ii) Dew points:**

For $P_v = 3.185$ kPa: try $T = 25.3\,°C$, $P_s(25.3) \approx 3.245$ kPa. So $T_{dp,1} \approx 25\,°C$.

For $P_v = 2.123$ kPa: $T = 18\,°C$ gives $P_s(18) = 2.064$ kPa; $T = 18.5\,°C$ gives $P_s \approx 2.130$ kPa. So $T_{dp,2} \approx 18.4\,°C$.

**(iii) Mass of water removed:**

Air density (moist): $v = R_d T_k (1 + 1.6078 \omega)/P$.
At 30 °C = 303.15 K:

$$
v_1 = 0.287 \times 303.15 \times (1 + 1.6078 \times 0.0202)/101.325 = 0.8775 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}
$$

Mass of dry air in 8000 m³: $m_{da} = 8000/0.8775 = 9 116$ kg da.

$$
m_w = m_{da} \times (\omega_1 - \omega_2) = 9 116 \times (0.0202 - 0.01331) = 9 116 \times 0.00689 = 62.81 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iv) Latent heat:**

$$
Q_l = m_{da} \times h_{fg} \times \Delta\omega = 9 116 \times 2501 \times 0.00689
$$

$$
Q_l = 157 069 \text{ kJ} = 43.63 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(v) Reheat coil load:**

At 10 °C, 100 % RH: $P_s(10) = 1.228$ kPa, so $\omega_3 = 0.622 \times 1.228/(101.325 - 1.228) = 0.00764$ kg/kg da.

Enthalpy at Point 3:

$$
h_3 = 1.005(10) + 0.00764(2501 + 1.88 \times 10) = 10.05 + 0.00764 \times 2519.8 = 10.05 + 19.25 = 29.30 \text{ kJ/kg da}
$$

Enthalpy at Point 4 (25 °C supply at $\omega_2 = 0.01331$):

$$
h_4 = 1.005(25) + 0.01331(2501 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 0.01331 \times 2548 = 25.125 + 33.92 = 59.05 \text{ kJ/kg da}
$$

If the mass flow is over, say, 1 hour of operation (or simply per kg da):

$$
Q_{reheat} = m_{da} \times (h_4 - h_3) = 9 116 \times (59.05 - 29.30) = 9 116 \times 29.75 = 271 200 \text{ kJ} = 75.33 \text{ kWh}
$$

> Final evaporator load = Latent + Sensible on coil. Sensible = $m_{da} \times 1.005 \times (T_{mixed} - T_{coil\_out})$; e.g., if mixed is 28 °C → 10 °C: $9 116 \times 1.005 \times 18 = 164 916$ kJ = 45.8 kWh. **Total ≈ 89.4 kWh.**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare **refrigeration-type** and **desiccant-wheel** cargo hold dehumidification systems. Your answer must cover working principle, temperature range of effectiveness, energy source, achievable RH, advantages, limitations, and typical marine applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A silica-gel rotary dehumidifier handles **5 000 m³/h** of hold air at 28 °C, 70 % RH. The desiccant wheel reduces the air to 20 % RH at the same temperature. Calculate:
- (i) Mass of dry air processed per second.
- (ii) Inlet and outlet humidity ratios.
- (iii) Water removal rate in kg/h.
- (iv) If regeneration is by an electric heater raising the regeneration air from 25 °C to 130 °C, find the **minimum regen heater capacity** (kW) for a 1 : 1 mass ratio of process to regen air, neglecting wheel losses. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks** (tabulated answer — 1 mark per row, with the seventh mark for a concluding sentence on selection rule):

| Aspect | Refrigeration Dehumidifier | Desiccant-Wheel Dehumidifier |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Cool below dew point; condense | Adsorb on silica / molecular sieve |
| Effective range | Warm air (≥ 15 °C) | Any temperature (works even at 5 °C) |
| Energy source | Electrical (compressor) | Heat for regen + electrical (fans) |
| Achievable RH | 70 – 90 % | 5 – 30 % |
| Outlet air T | Lower than inlet (needs reheat) | Higher than inlet (10–20 K rise) |
| Strengths | Simple, robust, well-known | Very dry, low-temp effective, no condensate | 
| Limitations | Inefficient below 10 °C; reheat needed | Wheel life; regen heat source needed |
| Marine use | Holds ≥ 15 °C | Cold holds, grain, reefer-deck spaces |

**Concluding remark (1 mark):** *Select refrigeration when hold T ≥ 15 °C and RH target is moderate (50–60 %); select desiccant when hold T is low or RH target < 30 %.*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Mass of dry air per second:**

At 28 °C = 301.15 K, with $\omega_1$ first calculated. $P_s(28) = 0.61078 \times \exp(17.27 \times 28/265.3) = 0.61078 \times \exp(1.823) = 3.778$ kPa.

$P_{v,1} = 0.70 \times 3.778 = 2.645$ kPa. $\omega_1 = 0.622 \times 2.645/98.68 = 0.01667$ kg/kg da.

Specific volume: $v = 0.287 \times 301.15 \times (1 + 1.6078 \times 0.01667)/101.325 = 0.8585$ m³/kg da.

$$
\dot{m}_{da} = \dfrac{5000}{3600 \times 0.8585} = 1.618 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Outlet humidity ratio:**

At 28 °C, 20 % RH: $P_{v,2} = 0.20 \times 3.778 = 0.7556$ kPa. $\omega_2 = 0.622 \times 0.7556/100.57 = 0.00467$ kg/kg da.

**(iii) Water removal rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = 1.618 \times (0.01667 - 0.00467) = 1.618 \times 0.01200 = 0.01942 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w = 0.01942 \times 3600 = 69.9 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(iv) Minimum regeneration heater capacity:**

With 1 : 1 mass flow and $c_p \approx 1.01$ kJ/kg·K (moist air, approximate):

$$
Q_{regen} = \dot{m}_{da} \times c_p \times (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 1.618 \times 1.01 \times (130 - 25)
$$

$$
Q_{regen} = 1.618 \times 1.01 \times 105 = 171.6 \text{ kW}
$$

> Note: In a real unit, regeneration air is taken from the *process outlet* (warm and dry) and a portion of the heat is recovered by a sensible wheel, reducing electrical demand by 30–50 %.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Print-Friendly):**

- ☐ **CHDS = Cargo Hold Dehumidification System** — maintains RH (≈ 50 %) and dew point to prevent ship sweat, cargo sweat, mould, corrosion.
- ☐ **Two technology routes:** *Condensation* (refrigeration coil) and *Adsorption* (desiccant wheel / liquid desiccant).
- ☐ **Master equation:** $\omega = 0.622 P_v / (P - P_v)$ — use it for every psychrometric numerical.
- ☐ **Anti-sweat design rule:** $T_{dp,\,air} \le T_{coldest\_surface} - 1\,K$, **always**.
- ☐ **Enthalpy of moist air:** $h = 1.005 T + \omega(2501 + 1.88 T)$ kJ/kg da.
- ☐ **Latent heat of evaporation** at 0 °C = **2501 kJ/kg**; sensible $c_p$ of dry air = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**.
- ☐ **Magnus–Tetens:** $P_s(T) = 0.61078 \exp[17.27T/(T + 237.3)]$ in **kPa** (T in °C).
- ☐ **Total coil load = latent + sensible reheat** — both must be added when sizing the refrigerant plant.
- ☐ **Desiccant wheels** dominate when hold T is low (< 10 °C) or when very dry air (RH < 30 %) is needed.
- ☐ **Liquid desiccants** (LiCl, LiBr) risk chloride corrosion of steel — generally avoided on ships unless Ti-grade components are used.
- ☐ **Ventilation vs Dehumidification:** ventilation adds moisture; dehumidification removes it. Closed-loop CHDS minimises make-up air.
- ☐ **Sensors:** RH sensor accuracy ± 2 % RH; T sensor ± 0.5 K — both must be placed at the *supply air* and *return air* ducts and inside each hold (≥ 2 m above tank top).
- ☐ **Energy audit tip:** A 10 000 m³ hold losing 50 g/kg of moisture equates to ≈ 50–60 kW of latent load — design the regen heater or compressor accordingly.
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** EN 12255, IMO CSS Code (Chapter VI), IGC Code (humid sections), ISO 17714 (humidity measurement).
- ☐ **Common KTU pitfall:** Mixing up *Relative Humidity* (φ, %) and *Humidity Ratio* (ω, kg/kg da) — they are not the same dimension; converting one to the other requires $P_s(T)$.
- ☐ **Closing takeaway:** *Control the dew point, not the dry-bulb temperature*. The single most important variable in cargo hold atmospheric control is $T_{dp}$, not $T_{db}$.
